title: The Quantitative Social Scientist
text:
“Expertise is still the result of rigorous engagement with information.”
This is a reference you grow into, not a course you finish. It walks the road from basic probability to the academic rigour of causal inference and the messy realities of field and client work — always intuition first, examples second, and mathematics used in a common-sense way to explain principles and deviations, with the full derivations kept in an appendix you can reach for but never have to wade through.
It is self-contained — every idea, with a runnable figure, lives on the page — and built on a curated library of canonical sources (Wooldridge, Angrist & Pischke, Cunningham, Gerber & Green, Ross, ISLR, Meadows, Barabási, Mitchell, and more), with explicit per-source attribution throughout. See How to use this.

title: The Quantitative Social Scientist
text:
The road
One rising arc, from “what is chance?” to “what causes what?” — undergraduate to graduate, intuition first. The spine climbs to causal inference as its summit; machine learning runs as a separate, bridged track you are pointed to when a predictive question shows up.


The ascent

Overture — How Research Works orientationWhy we do any of this: questions, measurement, designs, ethics.
Foundations: Probability & Data basicThe grammar of uncertainty; describing real data.
Inference undergradSampling, the CLT, estimation, testing, Bayesian inference.
Statistical Modelling: Regression undergradOLS, the regression workhorse, diagnostics, limited outcomes.




To the summit

Predictive Modelling graduateThe computational turn: bias–variance, cross-validation, time series, the debates.
The Summit: Causal Inference graduatePotential outcomes, RCTs, the “Furious Five”, DAGs, synthetic control.
Doing Research in the World appliedMeasurement, communicating uncertainty, the tyranny of metrics.



Side tracks

ML Track — learning, regularisation, trees, heterogeneity. Bridged from the spine.
Thinking in Dataframes — the R/tidyverse data-science workflow.
Complexity Science — systems thinking, network analysis & the dynamics of emergence.
Appendix · Mathematics — the proofs, on demand.
Appendix · Computing — R-first, with Python alongside.

title: The Quantitative Social Scientist
text:
How to read an article
Every entry follows the same shape, so you always know where to stand:

In one sentence — the intuition kernel.
Why it matters / where it sits — the use case before the formula.
Intuition — the idea in plain language, with examples.
Worked examples — drawn from the source library.
The math, where it earns its place — minimal, inline, in service of the idea.
Common pitfalls — how this gets misused in practice.
From the sources — exactly which book says what.
Appendix — the full mathematics, for when you want the proof of thought.

Start with the flagship entry to see the style in full: Sampling distributions & the Central Limit Theorem →

title: Support Vector Machines
text: A support vector machine classifies with a single geometric idea: find the boundary that sits as far as possible from the nearest points of either class — the widest “street” between them. Only the points on the street edge (the support vectors) matter; the rest are irrelevant. A soft margin lets a few points trespass for robustness, and the kernel trick bends the straight boundary into curves while keeping the computation linear. It is one of the most elegant ideas in machine learning.

title: Support Vector Machines
text:
Why it matters
SVMs were the dominant classifier of the 2000s and remain a sharp tool — especially when predictors vastly outnumber observations, the p>n regime where they shine. (James et al. 2021) They’re also conceptually important: the margin formalises “a confident boundary,” and the kernel trick shows how to get non-linearity for free — both ideas recur far beyond SVMs. This is the geometric counterpart to the probabilistic logistic regression you already know.

title: Support Vector Machines
text:
The maximal margin classifier
Start with the separable case. A hyperplane in p dimensions is a flat (p{-}1)-dimensional surface \beta_0+\beta_1X_1+\dots+\beta_pX_p=0; its sign classifies a point. (James et al. 2021) If the classes are linearly separable, infinitely many separating hyperplanes exist — so which? The maximal margin classifier picks the one with the largest margin: the greatest perpendicular distance to the nearest training points. (James et al. 2021) The widest street is the most confident, most generalisable cut.
The points lying exactly on the margin are the support vectors — and they alone define the boundary. Move any other point and nothing changes (as long as it stays off the street); move a support vector and the boundary shifts. (James et al. 2021) But the pure maximal margin classifier is brittle: it needs perfect separability, and it’s hypersensitive — a single new point can swing the hyperplane wildly, overfitting. (James et al. 2021) The fix is to allow a few violations.

title: Support Vector Machines
text:
The support vector classifier (soft margin)
The support vector classifier relaxes the rule: let some points sit inside the margin, or even on the wrong side, via slack variables \epsilon_i. (James et al. 2021)

\epsilon_i=0: point is on the correct side, outside the margin.
\epsilon_i>0: inside the margin (a violation).
\epsilon_i>1: on the wrong side of the hyperplane (misclassified).

A budget C caps the total violation, \sum_i\epsilon_i\le C — and C is a bias–variance dial chosen by cross-validation: (James et al. 2021)

Small budget → narrow margins, few support vectors → low bias, high variance (the boundary hugs the data).
Large budget → wide margins, many support vectors → high bias, low variance (a stable, smooth boundary).

Crucially, only the support vectors (points on or violating the margin) shape the classifier — making SVMs robust to far-away outliers, unlike methods that fit every point. (James et al. 2021)

title: Support Vector Machines
section: The kernel trick: curved boundaries, linear math
text:
The kernel trick: curved boundaries, linear math
Real boundaries are often non-linear. The SVM’s move: enlarge the feature space so a linear boundary in the bigger space becomes a curved one back in the original. (James et al. 2021) The genius is that the classifier depends on the data only through inner products \langle x_i,x_{i'}\rangle — so replace that inner product with a kernel K(x_i,x_{i'}), a measure of similarity that implicitly computes the inner product in a high- (even infinite-) dimensional space without ever building the features. (James et al. 2021) This is the kernel trick: (James et al. 2021)

Polynomial kernel K(x_i,x_{i'})=(1+\sum_j x_{ij}x_{i'j})^d — boundaries up to degree d.
Radial (RBF) kernel K(x_i,x_{i'})=\exp(-\gamma\sum_j (x_{ij}-x_{i'j})^2) — and because the exponential decays with distance, only nearby training points influence a test point’s prediction, giving the radial kernel a local character (a cousin of KNN). (James et al. 2021)

The figure shows both ends: a linear margin with its support vectors, and an RBF boundary curving around non-separable classes.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)

# ---- Left: linearly separable, illustrate margin + support vectors ----
A = rng.normal([-1.6, 0], 0.55, (25, 2))
B = rng.normal([1.6, 0], 0.55, (25, 2))
w = np.array([1.0, 0.0])                      # separating direction (vertical street)
# margin half-width = distance from boundary (x=0) to nearest point each side
mA = -A[:, 0].max(); mB = B[:, 0].min(); m = min(mA, mB)
svA = A[np.argmax(A[:, 0])]; svB = B[np.argmin(B[:, 0])]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.8, 3.9))
axL.scatter(A[:, 0], A[:, 1], s=18, color="#1f3a5f", label="class −1")
axL.scatter(B[:, 0], B[:, 1], s=18, color="#1f8a4c", label="class +1")
axL.axvline(0, color="#111", lw=2.0)
axL.axvline(-m, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--"); axL.axvline(m, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--")
for sv in (svA, svB):
    axL.scatter(*sv, s=120, facecolors="none", edgecolors="#c0392b", lw=2, zorder=5)
axL.text(0, 1.9, "margin", ha="center", color="#555", fontsize=8)
axL.text(svB[0]+0.1, svB[1], "support\nvectors", color="#c0392b", fontsize=7.5)
axL.set_title("Maximal margin (linear)"); axL.legend(fontsize=7, frameon=False, loc="lower left")
axL.set_xlim(-3.4, 3.4); axL.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.4)

# ---- Right: RBF kernel decision function (sum of Gaussians), non-separable ----
t = rng.uniform(0, 2*np.pi, 60); r = rng.normal(0.6, 0.12, 60)
inner = np.c_[r*np.cos(t), r*np.sin(t)]           # class +1: inner ring
t2 = rng.uniform(0, 2*np.pi, 60); r2 = rng.normal(1.8, 0.18, 60)
outer = np.c_[r2*np.cos(t2), r2*np.sin(t2)]       # class -1: outer ring
Xk = np.vstack([inner, outer]); yk = np.r_[np.ones(60), -np.ones(60)]
gamma = 1.2
def decision(grid):
    d2 = ((grid[:, None, :] - Xk[None, :, :])**2).sum(2)
    return (np.exp(-gamma*d2) * yk).sum(1)
gx, gy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-2.6, 2.6, 120), np.linspace(-2.6, 2.6, 120))
Z = decision(np.c_[gx.ravel(), gy.ravel()]).reshape(gx.shape)
axR.contourf(gx, gy, Z, levels=[-1e9, 0, 1e9], colors=["#dfeae5", "#e7ecf2"], alpha=0.6)
axR.contour(gx, gy, Z, levels=[0], colors="#111", linewidths=2.0)
axR.scatter(inner[:, 0], inner[:, 1], s=14, color="#1f8a4c")
axR.scatter(outer[:, 0], outer[:, 1], s=14, color="#1f3a5f")
axR.set_title("Radial (RBF) kernel — non-linear"); axR.set_xticks([]); axR.set_yticks([])

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Left: a linear support-vector classifier. The boundary (solid) maximises the margin (dashed ‘street’); the support vectors (circled) are the points on the margin edges that alone determine it. Right: a radial (RBF) kernel SVM on classes that no straight line can separate — the kernel bends the boundary into a closed curve, and its distance-decay makes it local. Same underlying machine, linear vs kernelised.

title: Support Vector Machines
text:
SVM vs logistic regression
The SVM isn’t as alien to your toolkit as it looks. The support vector classifier minimises a “loss + penalty” with the hinge loss \max[0,\,1-y_i f(x_i)] — and the hinge loss behaves much like the logistic loss, so the two methods often give similar results. (James et al. 2021) The differences are characterful: (James et al. 2021)

SVM gives a hard classification — its loss is exactly zero for points comfortably on the right side, so far-away points are ignored entirely.
Logistic regression gives probabilities — useful when you need calibrated risk, not just a label.

For multiclass problems SVMs use one-vs-one (fit \binom{K}{2} pairwise SVMs, majority vote of the “duels”) or one-vs-all (fit K class-vs-rest SVMs, assign to the largest margin). (James et al. 2021)






NoteISL’s two SVM datasets




Heart disease. The support vector classifier did slightly better than LDA; with a radial kernel, raising \gamma improved training accuracy but overfit — \gamma=10^{-1} looked near-perfect in training yet was worst on the test set, a vivid bias–variance lesson. (James et al. 2021)
Khan gene expression (p=2{,}308 genes \gg n). The classes were perfectly linearly separable — because with so many features a separating hyperplane almost always exists — so a linear kernel sufficed; added kernel flexibility would only overfit. (James et al. 2021) In high dimensions, keep it linear.

title: Support Vector Machines
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using the hard maximal margin. It demands perfect separability and is hypersensitive; use the soft-margin classifier and tune C. (James et al. 2021)
Not tuning C and \gamma by CV. Both control flexibility; the Heart example shows large \gamma overfits badly. (James et al. 2021)
Forgetting to scale features. Kernels measure distance, so unstandardised predictors let the largest-scale variable dominate — the same scaling rule as KNN. (James et al. 2021)
Reaching for a fancy kernel in high dimensions. When p\gg n data are often already separable; a linear kernel is usually best. (James et al. 2021)
Wanting probabilities from a raw SVM. It outputs a hard label; use logistic regression (or post-hoc calibration) when you need risk. (James et al. 2021)

title: Support Vector Machines
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 9) — the maximal margin classifier (separating hyperplane, margin, support vectors, brittleness); the support vector classifier (slack \epsilon_i, budget C, the bias–variance of C, only support vectors matter); the SVM with kernels — the kernel trick (inner products), polynomial (1+\langle x,x'\rangle)^d and radial \exp(-\gamma\|x-x'\|^2) kernels and the radial kernel’s locality; hinge vs logistic loss, hard label vs probabilities; one-vs-one / one-vs-all multiclass; the Heart (\gamma overfitting) and Khan gene-expression (p\gg n, linearly separable) examples.

title: Support Vector Machines
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The soft-margin optimisation. Maximise the margin M subject to correct-side constraints with slack: 
\max_{\beta,\,\epsilon}\ M \quad\text{s.t.}\quad
\|\beta\|=1,\;\; y_i(\beta_0+\beta^\top x_i)\ge M(1-\epsilon_i),\;\; \epsilon_i\ge0,\;\; \sum_i\epsilon_i\le C.
 Its dual depends on the data only through inner products \langle x_i,x_{i'}\rangle, and the solution f(x)=\beta_0+\sum_{i\in\mathcal S}\alpha_i\langle x,x_i\rangle sums over the support set \mathcal S (the \alpha_i are nonzero only there). Replacing \langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle with K(\cdot,\cdot) gives the kernel SVM, f(x)=\beta_0+\sum_{i\in\mathcal S}\alpha_i K(x,x_i) — non-linear, but still a weighted vote of similarities to the support vectors.
Hinge loss = SVM. The classifier minimises \sum_i \max[0,\,1-y_i f(x_i)] + \lambda\|\beta\|^2, a loss + ridge penalty. The hinge is exactly zero once y_i f(x_i)\ge1 (correct and confident), which is why only margin-violating points — the support vectors — enter the solution, and why the SVM gives a hard label rather than a probability.
Why RBF is local and infinite-dimensional. \exp(-\gamma\|x-x'\|^2) is the inner product of an infinite Taylor-feature expansion, yet computable in closed form. Its decay means K(x,x_i)\to0 for distant x_i, so the prediction at x is dominated by nearby support vectors — large \gamma ⇒ very local ⇒ wiggly ⇒ overfitting (the Heart lesson).

title: Support Vector Machines
text:
Connections

Builds on: classification (the task and KNN’s locality), logistic regression (the loss comparison), bias–variance (C, \gamma), and regularization (the ridge penalty in the hinge form).
Leads to: neural networks (another route to non-linear boundaries, via learned features rather than fixed kernels).
Bridges back: an SVM is a pure predictive classifier — a boundary, not an explanation; its weights are not effects.

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text: You now have the full predictive toolkit. This closing entry is about using it well as a social scientist — the disciplined workflow, the awkward truth that social data is usually small and noisy, the genuine jobs ML does superbly, and the boundary it must never cross. The whole track converges on one sentence: machine learning answers “what is Y given X?”, not “what happens to Y if I change D?” Keep those questions apart and ML is a powerful ally; conflate them and a model that predicts beautifully can recommend something disastrous.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text:
Why it matters
ML is a magnificent shovel for digging through data — but, as Judea Pearl puts it, “you are smarter than your data: data do not understand causes and effects; humans do.” (Jacobucci et al. 2023) The master of metrics uses the shovel without ever mistaking the hole for the foundation of the house. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) This entry consolidates the workflow lessons scattered across Part IV, confronts the realities of social data, and draws the firm bridge back to the causal summit that the whole book defends.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text:
The workflow
Everything rests on a disciplined pipeline that guards against overfitting — fitting the sample’s noise instead of the signal: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Train / validation / test. Fit parameters on train, choose models and tune hyperparameters on validation, and reserve test for a single, once-and-for-all honest assessment on truly unseen data. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Avoid data leakage. The cardinal sin is selecting or tuning on the same data you report on — “optimism bias.” The fix is nested cross-validation: tune in an inner loop, assess in an outer loop, so the reported number never saw the tuning. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Cross-fitting (for causal applications) ensures the nuisance models don’t contaminate the treatment-effect estimate — the discipline double ML is built on. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

This is the cross-validation entry’s “choices inside the loop” rule, scaled up to a whole project.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text:
The realities of social data
Industry ML lives on millions of rows. Social science usually doesn’t — and that changes everything. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Small n, wide p. Psychology and sociology datasets are often small with many correlated predictors — exactly where flexible methods overfit. The flexibility trap is “beating the data until it confesses.” (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
No Free Lunch. There is no single best algorithm for all problems. On noisy tabular social data, simple regularized models (Lasso/Ridge, well-tuned linear models) frequently match or beat black-box learners — with interpretability thrown in. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
The replication ethic. Because patterns in wide data can appear by chance, “an ounce of replication is worth more than a ton of inferential statistics.” (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

The lesson echoes the bias–variance trade-off: match the flexibility to the data you actually have, not the data you wish you had.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
section: What ML is genuinely good for
text:
What ML is genuinely good for
Used for the right jobs, ML adds real value to social research: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Prediction-policy problems. When the payoff comes from an accurate forecast to target a scarce resource — flagging students at risk of dropout, defendants’ recidivism risk, patients’ health risk — to direct interventions. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Measurement (text-as-data). Turning unstructured text or images into numbers with embeddings/transformers, creating variables that then feed ordinary causal models. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Exploratory pattern-finding. Tree-based methods surface interactions and nonlinearities you didn’t pre-specify — hypotheses to test later. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Heterogeneous treatment effects. Causal forests estimate the CATE — for whom a treatment works — the explicit ML-for-causation bridge. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Notice the pattern: ML supplies predictions and features; the causal question is answered by a design.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
section: The central discipline: prediction is not explanation
text:
The central discipline: prediction is not explanation
Here is the line the entire book defends. Accurate prediction does not imply causal understanding. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Two different questions: (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

Prediction: what is the most likely Y given X? (ML’s home turf.)
Causation: what happens to Y if I intervene and change D? (needs a research design.)

A model can predict superbly by leaning on markers rather than causes — and a policy built on it can be harmful. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) The figure sorts problems into the two camps.


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import FancyBboxPatch

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.6, 4.2))
ax.axis("off")

def box(x, w, title, items, color):
    ax.add_patch(FancyBboxPatch((x, 0.1), w, 0.78, boxstyle="round,pad=0.02",
                                fc=color, ec="#333", lw=1.2, alpha=0.18))
    ax.text(x + w/2, 0.82, title, ha="center", fontsize=10, fontweight="bold")
    for i, it in enumerate(items):
        ax.text(x + 0.04, 0.66 - i*0.11, it, ha="left", fontsize=8.2)

box(0.04, 0.44, "PREDICTION problem\n“what is Y given X?”", [
    "• target a scarce resource",
    "  (dropout, recidivism, health risk)",
    "• measurement / text-as-data",
    "• forecasting demand",
    "• exploratory pattern-finding",
    "→ ML shines"], "#1f8a4c")

box(0.52, 0.44, "CAUSAL problem\n“what if we change D?”", [
    "• does the program work?",
    "• should we treat this person?",
    "• the effect of a policy / price",
    "• who benefits most (CATE)",
    "→ needs a research DESIGN",
    "  (ML only as a tool within it)"], "#1f3a5f")

ax.annotate("conflating these\nis the fatal error", xy=(0.5, 0.5), xytext=(0.5, -0.02),
            ha="center", fontsize=8.5, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#c0392b", lw=0))
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(-0.1, 0.95)
ax.set_title("Match the tool to the question")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two kinds of question, two kinds of tool. If your decision only needs to know who or what (target the scarce resource, measure a variable, forecast demand), it’s a PREDICTION problem and ML shines. If your decision requires the EFFECT of changing something (does the program work? should we treat this person?), it’s a CAUSAL problem — and no amount of predictive accuracy substitutes for a design. The fatal error is treating a causal question as a prediction problem.











NoteThe asthma–pneumonia trap



The classic cautionary tale: a model predicting death from pneumonia learned that asthmatic patients had lower risk — and was, as a prediction, correct. The reason was causal and inverted: asthmatics were rushed to the ICU and treated more aggressively, which lowered their death rate. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) A policy reading the model literally — treat asthmatics less urgently because they’re low-risk — would be lethal. The model captured a marker shaped by treatment, not a cause. This is collider/selection logic wearing a machine- learning coat, and the reason feature importance ≠ causal effect: importance tells you what helped the algorithm reduce error in this dataset, never “the causes.” (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text:
The ethical dimension
One last duty, pointing to Part VI: a deployed model encodes and amplifies the prejudices in its training data, so fairness checks across subgroups are mandatory — acting on a biased model produces systemic injustice at scale. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Predictive power without fairness auditing is not neutral; it is harm, automated.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Tuning and reporting on the same data. Leakage inflates every number; use nested CV, keep the test set sacred. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
A flexible model on small wide data. It overfits; prefer regularized/simple models and replicate. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Treating a causal question as a prediction problem. “Should we change D?” needs a design, not a better classifier. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Reading feature importance as a list of causes. It ranks predictive contribution in one dataset, not effects. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Deploying without a fairness audit. Average accuracy hides subgroup harm; check it. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, ML for Social & Behavioral Research — the train/validation/test and nested-CV workflow, data leakage, cross-fitting; small-n/wide-p realities, the flexibility trap, No Free Lunch, the replication ethic; appropriate uses (prediction-policy, text-as-data, exploration, CATE); prediction ≠ explanation, markers vs causes, the asthma–pneumonia trap, feature importance ≠ causal effect; fairness/bias; Pearl (“you are smarter than your data”) and the shovel/foundation framing.
James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL — the predictive workflow and out-of-sample assessment underlying it all.
Angrist & Pischke — the prediction vs causal question distinction and the primacy of design.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text:
Appendix · the boundary, formally

Why a great predictor can mislead a policy. ML targets \mathbb E[Y\mid X=x] — the observational conditional mean. A policy that intervenes needs \mathbb E[Y\mid \operatorname{do}(D=d)] — the interventional mean. These coincide only under unconfoundedness. In the asthma case, \Pr(\text{death}\mid \text{asthma}) is genuinely low, but \Pr(\text{death}\mid \operatorname{do}(\text{de-prioritise asthmatics})) is high — because the observed association ran through aggressive treatment, a path the intervention destroys. The model estimated the first; the policy needed the second.
Nested CV. Let the inner loop pick hyperparameters \hat\lambda(\mathcal D_{\text{in}}) by CV on a training fold, and the outer loop evaluate the whole procedure (including that selection) on a held-out fold. The outer estimate is unbiased for the generalisation error of the pipeline, whereas a single CV that both tunes and reports is optimistic by roughly the spread of the inner search — the multiplicity of trying many \lambda.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home
text:
Connections

Builds on: the whole predictive Part IV (workflow, bias–variance, interpretability) and the ML Track entries; bridges to causal ML (HTE/double ML) and the credibility ethics of Part VI.
Closes: the ML Track, and the manual — returning the predictive culture to the service of, and subordinate to, the causal and ethical questions that motivate social science.
The last word: ML is a superb shovel. It is never the foundation. Prediction is not explanation; importance is not cause.
crumbs: ML for Social Science: Workflow & the Bridge Home

title: ML Track (separate)
text:
Machine learning is kept deliberately separate from the causal main track, and bridged to it only where it earns a place. The reason is the argument made in Predictive Modelling: ML is, by default, the predictive culture. Pretending its tools answer causal questions is one of the most common and costly confusions in applied work.
That said, the frontier of social science increasingly uses ML in service of causal goals — estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity, handling high-dimensional controls — and this track follows that bridge carefully.
The foundations of predictive modelling — the bias–variance trade-off, cross-validation, regularization, trees/forests/boosting, and classification — live in Part IV. This track goes deeper and broader: the flexible supervised methods Part IV deferred, plus the unsupervised half of machine learning the main spine never needed. Supervised learning predicts a label Y from features X; unsupervised learning finds structure in X with no label at all.
