title: ML Track (separate)
text:
The bridge back
Every entry here points back to the main spine: a prediction is not an effect, and a feature importance is not a cause. The ML track sharpens your tools without loosening your causal discipline.

title: Gaussian Processes
text: Every model so far fit the parameters of a fixed shape — a line, a tree, a network. A Gaussian process (GP) is different: it places a probability distribution directly over functions, then conditions on the data to get a posterior over functions. The reward is the most honest uncertainty in machine learning — the predictive band pinches tight where you have data and fans out where you don’t — all in closed form, with the smoothness assumptions made explicit through a kernel.

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
Why it matters
GPs are the Bayesian, nonparametric answer to regression: small-data, uncertainty-critical problems — Bayesian optimisation, spatial statistics, calibration, active learning — where knowing what you don’t know matters as much as the point prediction. They also tie a bow on the ML track: a GP is the Bayesian generalisation of the kernel methods behind SVMs, upgraded from point estimates to full predictive distributions.

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
A distribution over functions
Where ordinary regression estimates a fixed parameter vector \theta, a GP defines a distribution over functions (Murphy 2012). Its defining property: any finite set of function values \{f(x_1),\dots,f(x_N)\} has a joint multivariate Gaussian distribution (Murphy 2012). Because it assumes no fixed functional form (not linear, not polynomial), it is nonparametric — its effective complexity grows with the data, letting it capture almost any smooth relationship (Murphy 2012).

title: Gaussian Processes
section: The two ingredients: mean and kernel
text:
The two ingredients: mean and kernel
A GP is fully specified by two functions (Murphy 2012):

Mean function m(x) = \mathbb{E}[f(x)] — the prior expected value; in practice usually set to 0, since the covariance carries the modelling load (Murphy 2012).
Covariance / kernel function k(x,x') — which encodes the central assumption that nearby inputs have correlated outputs (Murphy 2012).

The default is the squared-exponential (RBF) kernel (Murphy 2012):

k(x,x') = \sigma_f^2 \exp\!\Big(-\frac{(x-x')^2}{2\ell^2}\Big).

Its hyperparameters are interpretable: the length-scale \ell controls smoothness — large \ell gives slowly-varying functions, small \ell lets them be “wiggly” — and the signal variance \sigma_f^2 sets the vertical scale (Murphy 2012).

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
GP regression: the predictive posterior
We observe noisy data y = f(x) + \epsilon, \epsilon\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_y^2), which adds a “nugget” to the covariance: K_y = K + \sigma_y^2 I (Murphy 2012). Conditioning the joint Gaussian prior on the data gives a closed-form Gaussian predictive distribution at a new point x_*, \mathcal N(\mu_*,\sigma_*^2), with (Murphy 2012):

\mu_* = k_*^\top K_y^{-1}\,y,
\qquad
\sigma_*^2 = k(x_*,x_*) - k_*^\top K_y^{-1} k_* ,

where k_* is the vector of kernel values between x_* and the training inputs. The mean is a weighted sum of the observed y’s; the variance is the prior variance minus what the data explains. The signature behaviour: predictive variance is small near the training data and grows as you move into unexplored regions — calibrated uncertainty, for free (Murphy 2012).


Show the GP code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
def rbf(a, b, ell=1.0, sf=1.0):
    d = a[:, None] - b[None, :]
    return sf**2 * np.exp(-0.5 * (d/ell)**2)

# training data from a smooth latent function + noise (note the gap on the right)
Xtr = np.array([-4.0, -3.0, -1.5, -0.5, 0.5, 1.5, 2.0])
ytr = np.sin(Xtr) + 0.3*Xtr + rng.normal(0, 0.15, Xtr.size)
ell, sf, sy = 1.0, 1.0, 0.15

Xte = np.linspace(-6, 6, 250)
Ky = rbf(Xtr, Xtr, ell, sf) + sy**2 * np.eye(Xtr.size)
Ks = rbf(Xte, Xtr, ell, sf)
Kss = rbf(Xte, Xte, ell, sf)
Ky_inv = np.linalg.inv(Ky)

mu = Ks @ Ky_inv @ ytr
cov = Kss - Ks @ Ky_inv @ Ks.T
sd = np.sqrt(np.clip(np.diag(cov), 0, None))

# three posterior function samples (jitter for numerical PSD)
L = np.linalg.cholesky(cov + 1e-9*np.eye(Xte.size))
samples = mu[:, None] + L @ rng.normal(size=(Xte.size, 3))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.6, 4.3))
ax.fill_between(Xte, mu - 2*sd, mu + 2*sd, color="#1f4e79", alpha=0.15,
                label="±2 SD predictive band")
for j in range(3):
    ax.plot(Xte, samples[:, j], lw=0.9, alpha=0.7,
            color=["#c0392b", "#1f8a4c", "#d98a29"][j])
ax.plot(Xte, mu, color="#1f4e79", lw=2.4, label="posterior mean")
ax.plot(Xtr, ytr, "o", color="black", ms=6, label="observations")
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("f(x)")
ax.set_title("GP regression: uncertainty pinches at data, fans out away from it")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left"); ax.set_ylim(-4, 4)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Gaussian-process regression, implemented by hand with an RBF kernel. Black dots are 7 noisy observations. The blue line is the GP posterior mean; the shaded band is ±2 predictive standard deviations; thin coloured curves are three functions drawn from the posterior (a GP is a distribution over functions, so you can sample whole functions). The band pinches tight at the data and fans out between and beyond the points — including the wide extrapolation region on the right where there is no data. That growing uncertainty is the GP’s defining virtue: it knows where it doesn’t know.

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
Learning the hyperparameters
The hyperparameters \theta=(\ell,\sigma_f,\sigma_y) are learned by maximising the log marginal likelihood (Murphy 2012):

\log p(y\mid X,\theta) = \underbrace{-\tfrac12\,y^\top K_y^{-1} y}_{\text{data fit}}
\ \underbrace{-\ \tfrac12\log|K_y|}_{\text{complexity penalty}}\ -\ \tfrac{N}{2}\log 2\pi .

This is automatic Occam’s razor: the data-fit term rewards models that pass through the points, while the complexity term -\tfrac12\log|K_y| rewards simpler, smoother functions (smaller |K_y|). The marginal likelihood balances them and prevents overfitting without a separate validation set (Murphy 2012) — the Bayesian model-comparison logic of WAIC/evidence, here in closed form.

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
Kernels, and the O(N^3) wall
GPs are the Bayesian generalisation of kernel methods like SVMs — but where an SVM gives a point prediction, a GP gives a well-calibrated probability distribution (Murphy 2012). The price is computational (Murphy 2012):

The bottleneck. Every fit requires inverting (or Cholesky-decomposing) the N\times N kernel matrix — O(N^3) time and O(N^2) memory (Murphy 2012). GPs are a small-/medium-data method.
Scaling up. Sparse approximations use M \ll N inducing points to summarise the data, cutting cost to O(M^2 N) — linear in N (Murphy 2012).

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Trusting the kernel blindly. The kernel is your prior; an RBF assumes smooth, stationary functions — wrong for jumps, periodicity, or trends. Choose (or compose) kernels deliberately. (Murphy 2012)
Throwing a GP at big data. O(N^3) makes exact GPs infeasible beyond a few thousand points; use sparse / inducing-point methods. (Murphy 2012)
Ignoring the noise term \sigma_y^2. Too small and the GP interpolates noise; too large and it oversmooths. Learn it via the marginal likelihood. (Murphy 2012)
Over-reading extrapolation. Far from data the posterior reverts to the prior mean with a wide band — that’s honest, not informative; don’t trust the mean out there. (Murphy 2012)
Numerical instability. K_y can be near-singular; add a small jitter to the diagonal and use Cholesky rather than a raw inverse. (Murphy 2012)

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Murphy, Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective — the GP as a distribution over functions with joint Gaussianity of any finite set, Bayesian nonparametric; the mean and kernel ingredients, the squared-exponential/RBF kernel and the length-scale smoothness control; GP regression with the noise nugget K_y=K+\sigma_y^2 I and the closed-form predictive mean k_*^\top K_y^{-1}y and variance k(x_*,x_*)-k_*^\top K_y^{-1}k_*, with uncertainty growing away from data; marginal-likelihood hyperparameter learning and its automatic Occam’s razor (data-fit vs complexity); the kernel-method/SVM connection; and the O(N^3) bottleneck with inducing-point sparse approximations.

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Conditioning a joint Gaussian (the whole trick). Stack training outputs y and a test value f_*. Under the GP prior they are jointly Gaussian,

\begin{bmatrix} y \\ f_* \end{bmatrix} \sim
\mathcal N\!\left(0,\ \begin{bmatrix} K_y & k_* \\ k_*^\top & k(x_*,x_*) \end{bmatrix}\right).

The standard Gaussian conditioning formula p(f_*\mid y) then gives the predictive mean \mu_* = k_*^\top K_y^{-1} y and variance \sigma_*^2 = k(x_*,x_*) - k_*^\top K_y^{-1} k_* directly — no optimisation, no sampling. All of GP regression is one application of “condition the joint Normal.”
Why the variance ignores y. \sigma_*^2 depends only on the inputs X, x_* (through the kernel), not the observed y — the GP’s uncertainty is set by where you have data, not what value it took. Hence the band’s shape is fixed by the sampling design.
The complexity penalty. -\tfrac12\log|K_y| is large (more negative) when K_y has big eigenvalues — i.e. when the kernel permits very flexible functions. Maximising the marginal likelihood thus trades fit against this volume term, the GP’s built-in Occam factor.

title: Gaussian Processes
text:
Connections

Builds on: support vector machines and the kernel trick (GPs are their Bayesian cousin), common distributions (the multivariate Normal), and regularization (the complexity penalty).
Echoes: Bayesian model comparison — the marginal likelihood is the same automatic-Occam machinery.
Contrasts with: trees & forests — non-smooth, scalable, but without the GP’s calibrated, closed-form uncertainty.

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text: Random forests build many trees in parallel and average them. Boosting turns the idea inside out: grow trees sequentially, each new (weak) tree fitting the part the current ensemble still gets wrong, and add it in scaled by a small learning rate. Learning slowly from residuals — via gradient descent in the space of functions — is what makes gradient boosting and its turbocharged cousin XGBoost the methods that win tabular-data competitions.

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
Why it matters
On structured/tabular social-science data, gradient-boosted trees are routinely the most accurate off-the-shelf predictor — ahead of deep nets and forests. Understanding why (and how to tune the three knobs without overfitting) is essential ML literacy, and it cements the bias–variance lesson: forests cut variance, boosting cuts bias by learning slowly.

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
Sequential, not parallel
The contrast with bagging is the whole idea (James et al. 2021):

Bagging fits many independent, high-variance trees on bootstrap samples and averages them — parallel variance reduction.
Boosting fits trees sequentially, each “grown using information from previously grown trees” — no bootstrap; each tree is fit to a modified version of the data (the current errors) (James et al. 2021).

By adding many weak learners (high-bias, low-variance — often shallow trees) scaled by a small learning rate, the ensemble “learns slowly,” which typically yields superior predictive performance (James et al. 2021).

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
section: AdaBoost: focus on the hard cases
text:
AdaBoost: focus on the hard cases
AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) was the first big success (James et al. 2021). For classification, it weights every observation; after each round it increases the weights of the misclassified points, forcing the next weak learner to concentrate on the hard cases (James et al. 2021). The final prediction is a weighted majority vote, with more accurate learners given more influence (\alpha_m) (James et al. 2021). Statistically, AdaBoost minimises an exponential loss — which makes it sensitive to outliers/noise (it penalises misclassification exponentially), a weakness modern methods improve on (James et al. 2021).

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
section: Gradient boosting: gradient descent in function space
text:
Gradient boosting: gradient descent in function space
Gradient boosting generalises the idea as numerical optimisation: minimise a loss by adding a function at each step (James et al. 2021). Each new tree is fit to the negative gradient of the loss at the current predictions — and for squared-error loss the negative gradient is simply the residuals (James et al. 2021). The algorithm (ISL Alg. 8.2) (James et al. 2021; Jacobucci et al. 2023):

Initialise \hat f(x)=0 and residuals r_i=y_i. For b = 1,\dots,B: (a) fit a shallow tree \hat f^b (with d splits) to the residuals (X,r); (b) update \hat f(x) \leftarrow \hat f(x) + \lambda\,\hat f^b(x); (c) update residuals r_i \leftarrow r_i - \lambda\,\hat f^b(x_i). Output \hat f(x) = \sum_{b=1}^{B}\lambda\,\hat f^b(x).



Show the code (gradient boosting with stumps, by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
f_true = lambda x: np.sin(2*x) + 0.5*x
n = 200
x = rng.uniform(0, 6, n); y = f_true(x) + rng.normal(0, 0.5, n)
xt = rng.uniform(0, 6, n); yt = f_true(xt) + rng.normal(0, 0.5, n)
grid = np.linspace(0, 6, 300)

def fit_stump(x, r):
    o = np.argsort(x); xs, rs = x[o], r[o]
    csum = np.cumsum(rs); tot = csum[-1]; m = len(rs)
    k = np.arange(1, m)
    lsum, rsum = csum[:-1], tot - csum[:-1]
    gain = lsum**2 / k + rsum**2 / (m - k)         # RSS reduction
    b = np.argmax(gain)
    return (xs[b] + xs[b+1]) / 2, lsum[b]/k[b], rsum[b]/(m-k[b])

def pred(stump, x):
    thr, cL, cR = stump
    return np.where(x <= thr, cL, cR)

# Left panel: staged fits at lambda = 0.1
lam = 0.1; B = 200; f = np.zeros(n); snaps = {1, 10, 50, 200}; curves = {}
fg = np.zeros(len(grid))
for b in range(1, B+1):
    s = fit_stump(x, y - f); f += lam * pred(s, x); fg += lam * pred(s, grid)
    if b in snaps: curves[b] = fg.copy()

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 4.0))
axL.scatter(x, y, s=8, color="#cdd6df")
axL.plot(grid, f_true(grid), color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4, label="true f(x)")
for b, c in sorted(curves.items()):
    axL.plot(grid, c, lw=1.4, alpha=0.9, label=f"B = {b}")
axL.set_title("Staged fit: learning slowly from residuals"); axL.set_xlabel("x"); axL.set_ylabel("y")
axL.legend(fontsize=7.5, frameon=False)

# Right panel: train/test error vs B for two learning rates
axR.axhline(0, color="none")
for lam, col in [(0.1, "#1f4e79"), (0.5, "#c0392b")]:
    f = np.zeros(n); fte = np.zeros(n); tr, te = [], []
    for b in range(300):
        s = fit_stump(x, y - f); f += lam*pred(s, x); fte += lam*pred(s, xt)
        tr.append(np.mean((y-f)**2)); te.append(np.mean((yt-fte)**2))
    axR.plot(tr, color=col, lw=1.5, ls="--", alpha=0.7)
    axR.plot(te, color=col, lw=2.0, label=f"test, λ={lam}")
axR.set_title("Boosting can overfit (test error, solid)"); axR.set_xlabel("number of trees B")
axR.set_ylabel("MSE"); axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False); axR.set_ylim(0, 1.2)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Gradient boosting built by hand from decision stumps (depth-1 trees), λ = 0.1. Left: the staged ensemble fit after 1, 10, 50, and 200 stumps — each stump fits the current residuals, so the piecewise-constant ensemble ‘learns slowly,’ creeping toward the true curve (green). Right: train vs test error against the number of trees B for two learning rates. With λ = 0.1 (blue) test error keeps falling then flattens; with the aggressive λ = 0.5 (red) it bottoms out early and then rises — boosting, unlike bagging, can overfit if B is too large. Small learning rate + many trees + cross-validated stopping is the recipe.

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
The three knobs
Gradient boosting is tuned by three hyperparameters (James et al. 2021):

Number of trees B. Unlike bagging, boosting can overfit if B is too large (though usually slowly) — so B must be chosen, typically by cross-validation (James et al. 2021).
Learning rate / shrinkage \lambda. A small positive number (often 0.01–0.1). Smaller \lambda needs a larger B but generalises better (James et al. 2021).
Interaction depth d. The number of splits per tree; d=1 (stumps) gives an additive model (each term one variable), and larger d allows higher-order interactions (James et al. 2021).

The recurring recipe: small \lambda, many trees, modest depth, CV-chosen B.

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
XGBoost: the competition workhorse
XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) is the efficient, regularised implementation that dominates tabular-data competitions (James et al. 2021; Bruce et al. 2020):

Regularisation — its objective penalises tree complexity, curbing overfitting (Bruce et al. 2020).
Second-order (Newton) updates — uses both the gradient and the Hessian of the loss, not just the first derivative (Bruce et al. 2020).
Stochastic features — row and column subsampling decorrelate trees and speed training (Bruce et al. 2020).
Early stopping — halt once validation performance stops improving (Bruce et al. 2020).

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
Reading a boosted model
Because the ensemble is hundreds of trees, you can’t draw it — so you interpret it indirectly (James et al. 2021):

Variable importance — total reduction in RSS (or Gini) from splits on each predictor, averaged over all B trees (James et al. 2021).
Partial-dependence plots — the marginal effect of one or two predictors after integrating out the rest (James et al. 2021) (see interpretability & ML).

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Letting B run unbounded. Boosting overfits with too many trees — cross-validate B / use early stopping (bagging never has this problem). (James et al. 2021)
Too-large a learning rate. Aggressive \lambda fits noise fast; prefer small \lambda with more trees. (James et al. 2021)
Deep trees by default. Large d models high-order interactions you may not have data for; start with stumps/shallow trees. (James et al. 2021)
AdaBoost on noisy data. Exponential loss chases outliers; gradient boosting with a robust loss (or XGBoost regularisation) is safer. (James et al. 2021)
Trusting a single importance number. Importances are split-based and correlated-feature-sensitive; corroborate with partial-dependence/SHAP. (James et al. 2021)

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL — boosting as sequential (vs bagging’s parallel) slow learning; AdaBoost (reweighting hard cases, weighted vote, exponential loss, outlier sensitivity); gradient boosting as fitting the negative gradient/residuals with the three knobs (B, \lambda, d/stumps), and that boosting can overfit in B; variable importance and partial-dependence plots.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — XGBoost: complexity regularisation, second-order (Newton) updates, row/column subsampling, early stopping, and its dominance on tabular data.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang — the gradient-boosting algorithm statement (Alg. 2) corroborating ISL.

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Functional gradient descent. To minimise \sum_i L(y_i, F(x_i)) over functions F, take a step in the steepest-descent direction in function space: the negative gradient at observation i is g_i = -\big[\partial L(y_i,F(x_i))/\partial F(x_i)\big]. Boosting fits a tree \hat f^b to the g_i and updates F \leftarrow F + \lambda \hat f^b. For squared loss L=\tfrac12(y-F)^2, g_i = y_i - F(x_i) — the residual — recovering the residual-fitting algorithm in the figure.
AdaBoost = forward stagewise on exponential loss. Minimising \sum_i \exp(-y_i F(x_i)) (y_i\in\{-1,1\}) one term at a time yields exactly AdaBoost’s reweighting w_i \leftarrow w_i\exp(\alpha_m\mathbf 1\{y_i\ne
G_m(x_i)\}) and learner weight \alpha_m = \tfrac12\log\frac{1-\text{err}_m}{\text{err}_m} — the statistical view that demystified the algorithm.
XGBoost’s second-order objective. At stage t XGBoost approximates the loss by a 2nd-order Taylor expansion, \sum_i [g_i f_t(x_i) + \tfrac12 h_i f_t(x_i)^2] + \Omega(f_t), with gradient g_i, Hessian h_i, and complexity penalty \Omega(f)=\gamma T + \tfrac12\lambda\|w\|^2 (number of leaves T, leaf weights w). The closed-form optimal leaf weight w_j^* = -\frac{\sum_{i\in j} g_i}{\sum_{i\in j} h_i+\lambda} is what makes it fast and regularised.

title: Boosting: AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting & XGBoost
text:
Connections

Builds on: trees, forests & boosting (the intro this deepens), the bias–variance trade-off (boosting attacks bias), and cross-validation (tuning B, \lambda, d).
Relates to: regularization (shrinkage \lambda and XGBoost’s penalty) and interpretability & ML (importance, PDPs, SHAP).
Contrasts with: Gaussian processes and neural networks — the other top predictors, with different inductive biases.

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text: Programming is a “cross-cutting tool” used in every part of an analysis, and writing functions is the single biggest multiplier of your reach. (Wickham et al. 2023) The rule is mechanical: once you’ve copied and pasted a block more than twice, wrap it in a function. The payoff is a name that explains the intent, one place to fix a bug, and the elimination of the copy-paste errors that silently corrupt results.

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Why it matters
Manual, repeated code is where analyses rot: you update a variable in three of the four pasted copies and ship a wrong number. Functions turn that fragility into reliability, and they let you build a personal vocabulary of operations tailored to your data. The authors call writing functions “one of the most effective ways to improve your reach as a data scientist.” (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
The DRY rule
Don’t Repeat Yourself. The trigger is concrete: write a function “whenever you have copied and pasted a block of code more than twice” (i.e. you’re looking at three copies). (Wickham et al. 2023) Three benefits follow: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Evocative naming — a function name reveals intent (rescale01() says what it does; the inlined arithmetic doesn’t), making code readable.
Centralised maintenance — fix a bug or change a requirement in one place, not four.
Accuracy — no more copy-paste slips, like renaming a variable in one spot and forgetting the others.

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
The three steps
To write a function you decide three things: (Wickham et al. 2023)

A name (rescale01 <- function(...)).
The arguments — the inputs, listed inside function().
The body — the code in the { } block.

# Three pasted copies of "rescale a column to 0..1" become one named function:
rescale01 <- function(x) {
  rng <- range(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  (x - rng[1]) / (rng[2] - rng[1])
}

rescale01(c(0, 5, 10))   # 0.0 0.5 1.0
Now a bug in the rescaling logic is fixed once, and mutate(across(everything(), rescale01)) applies it everywhere.

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Vector functions vs data-frame functions
Most functions you write take and return vectors (like rescale01 above). (Wickham et al. 2023) You can also write functions that operate on whole data frames in the dplyr/ggplot2 style — but the authors are candid that, at this edition’s writing, the tooling for that (tidy evaluation) was “idiosyncratic and immature,” with a cleaner approach still under development. (Wickham et al. 2023) (That cleaner approach is the { } “embracing” syntax; treat data-frame functions as a more advanced step once vector functions are second nature.)

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Conditions
Inside a function, if runs code conditionally on a test. (Wickham et al. 2023) One caution the book stresses: the condition must be a single TRUE/FALSE — a vector of logicals triggers a warning, and an NA throws an error. (Wickham et al. 2023) (For element-wise choices across a column, use the vectorised if_else/case_when instead of if.)

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Living with three pasted copies. That’s the DRY trigger; the fourth edit will miss a copy and create a silent bug. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Cryptic function names. A function’s value is the intent its name conveys; f2() helps no one. (Wickham et al. 2023)
if on a vector. if wants one logical; a vector warns, an NA errors. Use if_else/case_when for element-wise logic. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Reaching for data-frame functions too early. Tidy-evaluation plumbing is fiddly; master vector functions first. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — programming as a “cross-cutting tool”; the DRY rule (function after copy-pasting “more than twice”) and the three benefits (evocative naming, centralised maintenance, accuracy); the three steps (name, arguments, body) with the rescale01() example; vector vs data-frame functions (tidy evaluation “idiosyncratic and immature” at the time); and if conditions requiring a single logical (vector → warning, NA → error).

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Connections

Builds on: the grammar of manipulation and logical vectors & summaries (the operations you’ll wrap).
Leads to: iteration — once an operation is a function, you apply it to many inputs with map()/across().
Underwrites: every reproducible analysis — named, tested functions are what make a Quarto report trustworthy.

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text: Most people think of plots as a menu — bar chart, scatter, line — and pick one. R for Data Science teaches something deeper and more generative: every plot is a mapping from variables in a tidy table to visual properties (position, colour, size, shape), assembled from interchangeable layers. Learn the grammar and you stop memorising chart types and start composing exactly the picture your question needs.

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Why it matters
Visualisation is the step that can surprise you — the one tool that shows you what you didn’t expect. (Wickham et al. 2023) But a menu of fixed chart types is a cage: it can only draw the pictures someone pre-built. ggplot2’s grammar of graphics replaces the menu with a small set of composable pieces, so the space of plots you can build is effectively unlimited — and, more importantly, each plot becomes a precise statement about which variable controls which visual channel. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
A plot is a mapping
The core mental shift: a plot is a mapping of variables to aesthetics. An aesthetic is any visual property of the marks on the page — their x-position, y-position, colour, size, shape, transparency. You build a graph by saying which column drives which aesthetic. (Wickham et al. 2023)
library(tidyverse)

ggplot(data = mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class))
Read it as a sentence: take mpg, add a layer of points, and map engine size to the x-axis, highway mileage to the y-axis, and vehicle class to colour. Move class from color to size and the same data tells a different visual story — the mapping is the choice, not the chart type. (Wickham et al. 2023)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 60
x = rng.uniform(1.5, 6.5, n)
grp = rng.integers(0, 3, n)                 # a categorical third variable
y = 35 - 3.0 * x + grp * 2.5 + rng.normal(0, 1.5, n)

palette = np.array(["#1f3a5f", "#3f8f6f", "#9a6a2f"])
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.6))

# map group -> COLOR
axL.scatter(x, y, c=palette[grp], s=34, alpha=0.85)
axL.set_title("group → color", fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold", color="#1f3a5f")
axL.set_xlabel("engine size"); axL.set_ylabel("mileage")

# map group -> SIZE
axR.scatter(x, y, s=(grp + 1) * 45, facecolor="none", edgecolor="#1f3a5f", lw=1.4, alpha=0.85)
axR.set_title("group → size", fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold", color="#9a6a2f")
axR.set_xlabel("engine size"); axR.set_ylabel("mileage")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A plot as a mapping from a variable to an aesthetic. Same x–y points; the third variable (group) is mapped to COLOR on the left and to SIZE on the right. You don’t pick a ‘chart type’ — you choose which column drives which visual channel. ggplot then handles scaling: assigning each value of the variable its own level of the aesthetic automatically.





Once you map a variable to an aesthetic, ggplot2 handles scaling for you — it “automatically assigns a unique level of the aesthetic (e.g. a specific shade of blue) to each unique value of the variable,” and draws the legend. (Wickham et al. 2023) You declare the mapping; the system works out the colours, sizes, and key.

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Layers all the way down
The grammar’s reach comes from its claim that the pieces are universal. The authors state it precisely: “you can uniquely describe any plot as a combination of a dataset, a geom, a set of mappings, a stat, a position adjustment, a coordinate system, and a faceting scheme.” (Wickham et al. 2023)

geom — the kind of mark (points, lines, bars, boxplots).
mappings — which variable drives which aesthetic.
stat — a transformation applied before drawing (a histogram’s geom is a bar, but its stat bins and counts).
position, coordinate system, facets — how marks are arranged, the space they live in, and how the plot is split into small multiples by a variable.

You add layers with +, the visual cousin of the pipe: each layer composes onto the last, so a complex figure is built up from simple, legible parts rather than configured through one monstrous function call. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Setting an aesthetic when you meant to map it. color = "blue" inside aes() doesn’t paint the points blue — it creates a constant variable called “blue” and maps it, producing a red legend titled “blue.” To set a constant, put it outside aes(). Mapping vs setting is the classic confusion. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Mapping a continuous variable to a discrete aesthetic (e.g. shape), which ggplot can’t sensibly do — match the variable’s type to the aesthetic’s. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Overloading aesthetics. Driving colour and size and shape from three different variables at once usually buries the signal. Each aesthetic is a channel; too many channels is noise. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Reaching for a chart type instead of a mapping. Ask “which variable should control position? colour?” — not “should this be a bar or a line?” The mapping is the real decision. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science — ggplot2 as the grammar of graphics; a plot as a mapping of variables to aesthetics (position, colour, size, shape) via aes(); scaling as the automatic assignment of a unique aesthetic level to each value, plus the legend; and the claim that any plot is “a combination of a dataset, a geom, a set of mappings, a stat, a position adjustment, a coordinate system, and a faceting scheme,” composed in layers with +.

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Connections

Builds on: the workflow (the visualize station) and tidy data (aesthetics map columns, so the table must be tidy).
Pairs with: exploratory data analysis — the statistical purpose of the plots this grammar builds.
Echoes: the grammar of manipulation — same composable, layer-by-layer philosophy, applied to pictures instead of tables.

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text: A surprising share of wrangling is text surgery: a column called new_sp_m014 that secretly holds three variables, a date trapped in a sentence, a category spelled four different ways. Two tools handle almost all of it — the stringr package for clean, consistent string operations, and regular expressions, a “terse language” for describing patterns. Master them and “unstructured” data stops being a wall.

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Why it matters
Strings are where messy reality leaks into the dataframe. Real datasets are full of “unstructured or semi-structured data” and “odd variable codes” that must be parsed before they mean anything. (Wickham et al. 2023) The tidy shape you need often lives inside a text column, and getting it out is a string problem. This is among the least glamorous and most decisive skills in the craft.

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
String basics
A string is text in quotes — single ' or double ", with no behavioural difference, though the authors recommend double quotes as the default. (Wickham et al. 2023) The wrinkle is escapes: to put a literal quote inside a double-quoted string you escape it with a backslash, "\""; to put a literal backslash you double it, "\\". (Wickham et al. 2023)
library(tidyverse)

x <- "She said \"hi\" and left.\nThen \\ appeared."
x              # printed form SHOWS the escapes: "She said \"hi\"...
writeLines(x)  # writeLines() shows the ACTUAL content (the quotes, newline, backslash)
Because the printed representation shows the escapes, use writeLines() to see what a string really contains. (Wickham et al. 2023) Common special characters: \n (newline), \t (tab), and Unicode escapes like "µ" for µ. (Wickham et al. 2023)






NoteUnder the hood: the global string pool



R stores text efficiently through a “global string pool” — each unique string is held in memory once, and every later use is just a pointer to it. (Wickham et al. 2023) So a million-row column with five distinct labels costs about five strings of memory, not a million. (This is also why factors are cheap.)

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
stringr: the str_ verbs
Base R has string functions, but stringr is preferred for consistent, guessable names — they all start with str_ and take the string as the first argument (so they pipe). (Wickham et al. 2023) The core set:



Function
What it does




str_length()
number of characters


str_c()
combine strings — vectorised, recycles shorter vectors; collapse= joins a vector into one


str_sub()
extract by position (inclusive); negative counts from the end (-3, -1 = last three)


str_detect()
logical: does the pattern match? (pairs with filter())


str_count()
how many matches — matches never overlap ("aba" hits "abababa" twice, not three times)


str_extract() / _all()
pull out the matched text (_all returns a list; simplify=TRUE → matrix)


str_replace() / _all()
substitute matches (a named vector does several replacements at once)


str_split()
break a string into pieces on a pattern



fruits <- c("apple", "banana", "pear")
str_detect(fruits, "an")          # FALSE  TRUE FALSE
str_replace_all("2024-01-31", c("-" = "/"))   # "2024/01/31"
str_c("id", 1:3, sep = "_")       # "id_1" "id_2" "id_3"  (vectorised + recycled)

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Regular expressions
A regular expression describes a pattern, not a literal string — the engine behind str_detect, str_extract, and friends. (Wickham et al. 2023) The pieces:

Literals match themselves; the . (dot) is a wildcard for any character except a newline. To match a literal dot you escape it: the regex is \., which in an R string is written "\\." (the double-backslash trips everyone up at first). (Wickham et al. 2023)
Anchors: ^ matches the start, $ the end. The book’s mnemonic: “if you begin with power (^), you end up with money ($).” (Wickham et al. 2023)
Character classes: \d any digit, \s whitespace, [abc] any of a/b/c, [^abc] anything but. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Quantifiers (repetition): ? 0-or-1, + 1-or-more, * 0-or-more, {n} exactly n, {n,m} between n and m. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Alternation & groups: | is “or” (red|blue); parentheses () make groups, referenced later by backreferences \1, \2. (Wickham et al. 2023)

str_detect(c("grey", "gray"), "gr(e|a)y")   # TRUE TRUE  — one pattern, both spellings
str_extract("Order #4821 shipped", "\\d+")  # "4821"     — one or more digits
str_detect(words, "^p")                      # words starting with p






WarningDon’t reach for one giant regex



The authors quote Jamie Zawinski’s famous warning: “Some people, when confronted with a problem, think ‘I know, I’ll use regular expressions.’ Now they have two problems.” (Wickham et al. 2023) The fix is to break it down — a “series of simpler regexps”, or several str_detect() calls combined with logical operators (!, &, |) — rather than one unreadable monster. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
section: The payoff: parsing codes into tidy columns
text:
The payoff: parsing codes into tidy columns
Strings and tidying meet in the book’s WHO tuberculosis case study. A column named new_sp_m014 secretly encodes three variables: the diagnosis method (sp), the gender (m), and the age group (014). (Wickham et al. 2023) The cleaning recipe is pure string work: first str_replace() fixes an inconsistency ("newrel" → "new_rel") so the fields line up, then separate() splits the code into its constituent columns. (Wickham et al. 2023) tidyr::extract() does the same job straight from regex groups, pulling each () capture into its own column. This is the moment a string column becomes a tidy table.

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting the double escape. A literal dot is \. in regex but "\\." in an R string; one backslash isn’t enough. The same trap bites \d ("\\d"). (Wickham et al. 2023)
Expecting . to match a newline. The wildcard skips newlines by default — multi-line text needs care. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Assuming matches can overlap. str_count counts non-overlapping matches; "aba" in "abababa" is 2, not 3. (Wickham et al. 2023)
One regex to rule them all. Unreadable and unmaintainable. Compose simpler patterns and logical operators instead. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Reading a printed string literally. The console shows escapes; use writeLines() to see the true content before you debug a pattern. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — strings (quotes, escapes, writeLines(), special characters, the global string pool); stringr’s str_ verbs (str_length/c/sub/detect/count/extract/replace/split, vectorisation & recycling, non-overlapping counts); regular expressions as a “terse language” (literals & ., anchors ^ $, character classes, quantifiers, alternation, groups & backreferences, the double-escape); the Zawinski “two problems” warning and “series of simpler regexps”; and the WHO new_sp_m014 parsing case study (str_replace → separate/extract).

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Connections

Builds on: the grammar of manipulation (str_detect inside filter, str_replace inside mutate) and tidy data (parsing codes into columns).
Leads to: factors (once text is clean, categoricals get explicit levels) and importing data (parsing is where raw text becomes typed columns).
Reappears in: the data-science workflow — text cleaning is a large, unglamorous slice of wrangling.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text: Data almost never arrives as a single tidy table. It comes as several — survey responses in one, respondent demographics in another, region codes in a third — linked by shared identifiers. Thinking relationally means seeing those keys and knowing how to join on them. Get joins right and scattered tables become one analysis-ready frame; get them wrong and you silently drop or duplicate observations.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Why it matters
The transform station usually begins by assembling the data, because the information you need is spread across tables — “data analysis rarely involves a single data frame.” (Wickham et al. 2023) A flight has a carrier code; the carrier’s name lives in a separate airlines table. To plot delay by airline name, you must join them. The relational mindset — keys connecting tables — is the difference between an analyst who can only use pre-merged data and one who can build the dataset the question actually requires.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Keys: how tables connect
Tables are linked by keys — variables that identify observations: (Wickham et al. 2023)

A primary key uniquely identifies each observation in its own table (e.g. carrier code in the airlines table — one row per carrier).
A foreign key is that same variable appearing in another table, where it points back to the first (e.g. carrier in the flights table — many rows per carrier).

A primary key in one table matched to a foreign key in another is a relation. Verifying that your primary key really is unique — exactly one row per value — is the unglamorous check that prevents silent duplication later. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Joins: combining on a key
A join uses the key to bring columns from one table into another, matching rows where the keys agree. R for Data Science pictures a match as a dot at the intersection of a row from each table. (Wickham et al. 2023) The mutating joins add variables from one table to another, and they differ only in which unmatched rows they keep: (Wickham et al. 2023)

inner_join() — keep only rows that match in both tables. Unmatched rows are dropped from both.
left_join() — keep all rows of the left table, filling NA where the right has no match.
right_join() — keep all rows of the right table.
full_join() — keep all rows of both, NA wherever either side is missing.



Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.4))

def draw_join(ax, title, rows, title_color):
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold", color=title_color)
    ax.axis("off"); ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
    ax.text(0.28, 0.92, "key  val_x  val_y", fontsize=8, style="italic", ha="center")
    for i, (k, vx, vy, present) in enumerate(rows):
        y = 0.76 - i * 0.18
        color = "#e7f0e9" if present else "#f0e0e0"
        ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.10, y), 0.55, 0.14, fc=color, ec="#1f3a5f", lw=1.1))
        ax.text(0.37, y + 0.07, f"{k}      {vx}        {vy}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9)

# inner: only keys 1,2
draw_join(axL, "inner_join (matches only)",
          [("1", "x1", "y1", True), ("2", "x2", "y2", True)], "#1f3a5f")
axL.text(0.37, 0.04, "rows 3 and 4 dropped", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#9a6a2f", style="italic")

# left: keep all of x (1,2,3), NA for 3
draw_join(axR, "left_join (keep all of x)",
          [("1", "x1", "y1", True), ("2", "x2", "y2", True), ("3", "x3", "NA", False)], "#3f8f6f")
axR.text(0.37, 0.04, "key 3 kept, y filled NA", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#3f8f6f", style="italic")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two joins on a shared key. Left table x (keys 1,2,3) and right table y (keys 1,2,4). An INNER join keeps only keys present in both (1,2) — row 3 and row 4 are dropped. A LEFT join keeps every row of x (1,2,3), filling NA where y has no match (key 3); it never loses an original observation, which is why it’s the recommended default.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Why left_join is the default
Wickham & Grolemund give a clear rule of thumb: the left join should be your default. (Wickham et al. 2023) The reason is conservatism — it “preserves all the original observations” in your primary table even when the lookup table has no match. You started with a table of flights; a left join onto airline names keeps every flight, attaching the name where it exists and NA where it doesn’t. An inner join, by contrast, would silently delete any flight whose carrier code was missing from the airlines table — quietly changing your sample. When the left table is your analysis sample, left_join is what keeps that sample intact. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using inner_join as a habit. It drops unmatched rows from both sides; if the left table is your sample, that’s silent, non-random attrition. Default to left_join and check what didn’t match. (Wickham et al. 2023)
A non-unique “primary” key. If the key repeats in the table you thought was one-row-per-key, the join duplicates rows — your dataset quietly grows and every later count is wrong. Verify uniqueness first. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Mismatched key types or codings. "01" ≠ 1, "USA" ≠ "US"; keys must match exactly to join. Reconcile types and coding before joining. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Not inspecting NAs after a left join. The NAs a left join introduces are information — they tell you which rows had no match. Ignoring them hides a data problem. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science — relational data because “data analysis rarely involves a single data frame”; keys (primary uniquely identifies an observation in its own table; foreign points to it from another); a match pictured as a dot at the intersection; the mutating joins (inner_join keeps only matches; left_join/right_join/full_join keep unmatched rows from one or both sides); and the recommendation that left_join be the default because it preserves all original observations.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Connections

Builds on: tidy data (each table tidy, linked by keys) and the grammar of manipulation (joins are verbs that act across tables).
Pairs with: the workflow — assembling tables is usually the first move of transform.
Underwrites: any analysis whose variables come from multiple sources — merging treatment assignment, outcomes, and covariates into the single tidy frame a model or causal design requires.

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text: The dplyr verbs are only as good as the expressions you feed them. filter() needs a logical test; mutate() needs a numeric transformation; summarize() needs a summary. This entry is the toolbox of those small operations — comparisons and boolean logic, numeric helpers, and aggregation functions — plus the two traps that bite everyone: floating-point equality and the & / && distinction.

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Logical vectors
A logical vector holds only TRUE, FALSE, or NA. (Wickham et al. 2023) You make one with the comparison operators — >, >=, <, <=, == (equal), != (not equal) — and combine logicals with boolean algebra: & (and), | (or), ! (not). (Wickham et al. 2023) A handy shorthand for a chain of “or”s is x %in% y, which is TRUE wherever x is one of the values in y.






WarningThe floating-point trap



Never use == on numbers with decimals. Computers use finite precision, so “every number you see is an approximation” — and sqrt(2)^2 == 2 actually evaluates to FALSE. (Wickham et al. 2023) Use dplyr::near(), which ignores tiny differences. This bug is silent and ruins filters and joins.








Note& vs && — vectorised vs short-circuit



Use & and | for vectorised logic — element by element, the kind filter() needs. Use && and || only for a single TRUE/FALSE in conditional execution (an if statement); they short-circuit, stopping the moment the answer is settled (&& quits at the first FALSE). (Wickham et al. 2023) Putting && inside filter() is a classic mistake.


For conditional values, if_else() handles a two-way choice (a test, a “yes” value, a “no” value), and case_when() handles multi-way choices without a tangle of nested ifs: (Wickham et al. 2023)
flights |>
  mutate(status = case_when(
    arr_delay < 0  ~ "early",
    arr_delay == 0 ~ "on time",
    arr_delay > 0  ~ "late",
    .default       = "unknown"        # catches NA and anything unmatched
  ))

Logicals are numbers in disguise
The most useful trick: in a numeric context FALSE becomes 0 and TRUE becomes 1. (Wickham et al. 2023) So inside summarize(), sum() of a logical counts the TRUEs and mean() of a logical is the proportion of TRUEs — no extra recoding:
flights |>
  group_by(dest) |>
  summarize(
    n_late    = sum(arr_delay > 0, na.rm = TRUE),   # count of late flights
    prop_late = mean(arr_delay > 0, na.rm = TRUE)   # share that were late
  )

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Numeric transformations
The numeric helpers that show up constantly: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Modular arithmetic — %/% (integer division) and %% (remainder) split an integer apart (turn 1517 into 15 hours and 17 minutes).
Offsets — lead() and lag() refer to the next/previous value, essential for running differences and change-over-time.
Ranking — min_rank() (ties share a rank: 1, 2, 2, 4), with desc() to rank from the top; cousins row_number(), dense_rank(), percent_rank().
Binning — cut() “discretizes continuous variables” into labelled groups (turn income into brackets).
Cumulative aggregates — cumsum(), cumprod(), cummin(), cummax(), and dplyr’s cummean().

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Summary functions
Summaries collapse a column to one number per group: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Counts: n() (group size), n_distinct(x) (number of unique values).
Location & spread: median(), IQR() (interquartile range), min(), max() — the robust pair when data is skewed.

These are the building blocks that, with group_by(), implement split–apply–combine.

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




== on decimals. Floating-point approximation makes exact equality unreliable; use near(). (Wickham et al. 2023)
&&/|| in vectorised code. They return a single value and short-circuit; inside filter()/mutate() use &/|. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Forgetting na.rm = TRUE. A single NA makes sum/mean/median return NA (next entry); decide how missing is handled before aggregating. (Wickham et al. 2023)
case_when() with no default. Unmatched rows become NA; add .default (or an explicit catch-all) unless you mean that. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — logical vectors (comparisons, the floating-point trap and near(), boolean &/|/!, %in%, & vs && vectorised vs short-circuit, if_else/case_when, and TRUE/FALSE as 1/0 so sum counts and mean is a proportion); numbers (%/%/%%, lead/lag, min_rank & ranking family, cut binning, cumulative aggregates); and summary functions (n, n_distinct, median, IQR, min/max).

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Connections

Builds on: the grammar of manipulation (these expressions are what the verbs consume).
Leads to: missing values (why na.rm matters) and exploratory visualization (summaries become plots).
Mirrors: Part I · location, spread & shape — the same statistics, in dataframe-native form.

title: Thinking in Dataframes (R)
text: Every other Part of this book is about a method — a way of turning data into a claim. This track is about the step that comes before any method and never appears in the results section: getting the data into a shape you can think with, and then bending it to your questions. Experienced researchers spend the majority of their time here, and the fluent ones share a mental model — the dataframe as the unit of thought, and a small grammar of operations on it. This track teaches that craft end to end, following Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel & Garrett Grolemund’s R for Data Science (2e). (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Thinking in Dataframes (R)
text:
Why a separate track
The dataframe craft is orthogonal to the statistical ascent. Probability, inference, regression, and causal inference are a rising arc of ideas; tidying and transforming data is a skill you apply at every rung of that arc. Folding it into the spine would interrupt the argument; leaving it out would pretend the data arrives analysis-ready. So it lives here — a companion you reach for the moment you face a real, messy table. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Thinking in Dataframes (R)
text:
The whole game
R for Data Science organises everything around one picture of the data-science workflow — import → tidy → (transform ⇄ visualise ⇄ model) → communicate. (Wickham et al. 2023) This track walks that whole picture, one craft at a time.
