title: Thinking in Dataframes (R)
section: The bridge to the rest of the book
text:
The bridge to the rest of the book
Tidy data is what every model in this encyclopedia silently assumes: a regression expects one row per observation and one column per variable; an exploratory plot is a mapping from tidy columns to visual properties; a causal design lives or dies on whether treatment, outcome, and covariates are cleanly separated columns. Learn to think in dataframes and the rest of the book gets a great deal easier — and reproducible. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text: Once data is tidy, nearly everything you do to it is one of a few verbs: pick rows, pick columns, make new columns, sort, summarise — and, crucially, do any of these group-by-group. Chain them with the pipe and a transformation stops being a tangle of nested functions and becomes a readable sequence of actions. This is the grammar that turns “wrangling” from a fight into a sentence.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
Why it matters
The transform station of the workflow is where you spend much of your day, and without structure it degenerates into bespoke, unreadable code. Wickham & Grolemund’s dplyr imposes a grammar: a small, consistent set of verbs that each take a data frame and return a data frame, so they compose without friction. (Wickham et al. 2023) Learn five verbs plus one grouping operator and you can express the overwhelming majority of real data transformations.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
The verbs
Each verb does one thing, takes a data frame as its first argument, and returns a modified data frame: (Wickham et al. 2023)



Verb
What it does




filter()
Pick observations (rows) by their values


select()
Pick variables (columns) by their names


mutate()
Create new variables as functions of existing ones


arrange()
Reorder the rows


summarize()
Collapse many values down to a single summary



Because each verb’s input and output are the same kind of object — a data frame — they snap together like LEGO. The connective tissue is the pipe.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
section: The pipe: code that reads as then
text:
The pipe: code that reads as then
The pipe, written |> and pronounced “then,” takes the result on its left and feeds it as the first argument on its right. (Wickham et al. 2023) It lets you write a transformation as the sequence of actions you’d say out loud:
library(tidyverse)

flights |>
  filter(dest == "IAH") |>            # keep flights to Houston, THEN
  group_by(year, month, day) |>       # for each day, THEN
  summarize(avg_delay = mean(arr_delay, na.rm = TRUE)) |>  # average the delay, THEN
  arrange(desc(avg_delay))            # sort worst-first
Read it top to bottom: take flights, then keep Houston-bound ones, then group by day, then average the delay, then sort. The authors stress that the pipe “focuses on verbs, not nouns,” making code “easier for a human to consume.” (Wickham et al. 2023)






NoteTwo analogies for why the pipe wins




The Dagwood-sandwich problem. Without the pipe, the same logic nests inside-out: arrange(summarize(group_by(filter(flights, ...), ...), ...), ...). You must read it from the innermost call outward — a towering, unreadable sandwich. The pipe flattens it into reading order. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Little Bunny Foo Foo. Wickham & Grolemund tell the children’s rhyme to make the point: a story of hopping, then scooping up the field mice, then bopping them on the head reads naturally as a sequence — but written as nested functions (bop(scoop(hop(foo_foo)))) or as a clutter of named intermediates (foo_foo_1, foo_foo_2, …) it becomes hard to follow. The pipe tells the story in order and spares you inventing “unimportant names” for intermediate objects. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
Split–apply–combine
The most powerful move is summarize() paired with group_by(). Alone, group_by() changes nothing visible — it just changes the scope of the verbs that follow, from “the whole table” to “group-by-group.” (Wickham et al. 2023) That single switch implements the split–apply–combine strategy:


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(8.8, 3.2))
for ax in axes:
    ax.axis("off"); ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)

def cell(ax, x, y, text, color):
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x, y), 0.26, 0.13, fc=color, ec="#1f3a5f", lw=1.1))
    ax.text(x + 0.13, y + 0.065, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8.5)

# SPLIT
axes[0].set_title("split", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", color="#1f3a5f")
for i, (g, v, c) in enumerate([("A", 3, "#dce7f0"), ("A", 5, "#dce7f0"),
                               ("B", 8, "#e7f0e9"), ("B", 6, "#e7f0e9")]):
    cell(axes[0], 0.36, 0.78 - i*0.17, f"{g}   {v}", c)
axes[0].text(0.49, 0.97, "key  value", ha="center", fontsize=8, style="italic")

# APPLY
axes[1].set_title("apply (mean)", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", color="#3f8f6f")
cell(axes[1], 0.36, 0.62, "A → mean", "#dce7f0")
cell(axes[1], 0.36, 0.30, "B → mean", "#e7f0e9")

# COMBINE
axes[2].set_title("combine", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", color="#9a6a2f")
cell(axes[2], 0.36, 0.58, "A   4.0", "#dce7f0")
cell(axes[2], 0.36, 0.41, "B   7.0", "#e7f0e9")
axes[2].text(0.49, 0.78, "one row per group", ha="center", fontsize=8, style="italic")

# arrows between panels
for x in (0.0, 0.5):
    fig.add_artist(plt.Line2D([0.33 + x*0.0, 0.36 + x*0.0], [0.5, 0.5], lw=0))
fig.text(0.345, 0.5, "→", fontsize=20, ha="center", va="center", color="#5a6b7a")
fig.text(0.675, 0.5, "→", fontsize=20, ha="center", va="center", color="#5a6b7a")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Split–apply–combine, the engine behind group_by() |> summarize(). SPLIT the tidy table into groups by a key (here, group A/B); APPLY a summary (the mean) within each group; COMBINE the per-group answers back into one tidy table — one row per group. group_by() sets the scope; summarize() does the collapsing.





Split the data into groups by a key, apply a function (a summary) to each group, and combine the results back into a single data frame — one row per group. (Wickham et al. 2023) This one pattern answers an enormous share of real questions: average delay per airline, total sales per region, recidivism per prior-conviction count. It is the dataframe-native expression of “compare groups,” and it reappears the moment you reach comparing groups in inference.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting na.rm in a summary. A single missing value makes mean() return NA; summaries silently collapse to nothing. Decide what missing means before you aggregate. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Leaving data grouped. A group_by() persists; verbs downstream keep operating per-group when you meant the whole table. ungroup() when the grouped work is done. (Wickham et al. 2023)
mutate vs summarize confusion. mutate() keeps every row (adds a column); summarize() collapses rows (one per group). Reach for the wrong one and your table is the wrong length. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Nesting instead of piping. The Dagwood sandwich is still legal R; it’s just unreadable. If you’re counting parentheses, reach for the pipe. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science — the dplyr grammar and its core verbs (filter, select, mutate, arrange, summarize); group_by() as a change of scope and the split–apply–combine strategy; the pipe (|>, “then”) as code that “focuses on verbs, not nouns”; the Dagwood-sandwich problem of nested calls and the Little Bunny Foo Foo analogy for avoiding “unimportant names” for intermediate objects.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
Connections

Builds on: tidy data (the verbs assume one row per observation) and the workflow (this is the transform station).
Leads to: the grammar of graphics (the same composable, layered thinking, applied to plots) and relational joins (verbs across tables).
Reappears as: comparing groups and every per-group summary in applied work — split–apply–combine is “compare groups” in dataframe form.

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text: Before any model, you interrogate the data. R for Data Science frames exploratory data analysis (EDA) not as a fixed procedure but as an iterative cycle and a “state of mind”: ask a question, answer it with a plot or a summary, let the answer breed the next question. (Wickham et al. 2023) Two questions are always worth asking — about variation within a variable and covariation between variables — and a handful of plots answer them.

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Why it matters
This is the visualize loop of the workflow, the step that can surprise you. The authors are explicit that EDA is “fundamentally a creative process”: at the start you don’t know what’s hidden, so the goal is to “generate a large quantity of questions” and investigate “every idea that occurs to you” before honing in. (Wickham et al. 2023) It is the dataframe-native version of Part I’s exploratory data analysis — and, as Tukey said (quoted in the book), “the simple graph has brought more information to the data analyst’s mind than any other device.” (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
The EDA cycle
A three-step loop you spin many times: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Generate questions about your data.
Search for answers by visualizing, transforming, and modelling.
Refine the questions, or spawn new ones, from what you learn.

There are no rules for which questions — but two types are “always useful”: (Wickham et al. 2023)

What type of variation occurs within my variables? What type of covariation occurs between my variables?

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Variation: looking within one variable
Variation is the tendency of a variable’s values to change from measurement to measurement; you see it by plotting the distribution. (Wickham et al. 2023)

Categorical → a bar chart (geom_bar()): bar height is the count per category.
Continuous → a histogram (geom_histogram()): counts within bins.

What to look for: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Typical values — the tall bars (common) vs short bars (rare). Why is each common or rare?
Clusters / subgroups — separated humps suggest distinct subpopulations. How are within-cluster cases alike, and how do clusters differ?
Outliers — points that don’t fit. In big data they hide; the authors zoom the y-axis with coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 50)) to expose the rare bins.



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
# Two subgroups + a few outliers — the kind of structure a histogram reveals
a = rng.normal(20, 3, 600)
b = rng.normal(38, 4, 400)
outliers = np.array([70, 72, 75, 78, 80])
x = np.concatenate([a, b, outliers])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.hist(x, bins=40, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85, edgecolor="white")
ax.annotate("two clusters →\nlikely two subgroups", xy=(29, 40), fontsize=8.5, color="#3f8f6f")
ax.annotate("outliers", xy=(74, 8), xytext=(60, 35), fontsize=8.5, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"))
ax.set_xlabel("value"); ax.set_ylabel("count")
ax.set_title("What does the distribution look like?")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Variation within one continuous variable. A histogram exposes what a mean and SD hide: here, two clusters (a bimodal distribution — evidence of two subgroups) plus a handful of outliers far to the right. R for Data Science’s EDA habit is to SEE the distribution first and let its shape — typical values, clusters, outliers — generate the next question.





Handling unusual values: the authors don’t recommend silently dropping outlier rows. Instead, replace the suspect value with NA and keep the row: mutate(y = ifelse(y < 3 | y > 20, NA, y)). (Wickham et al. 2023) That preserves the rest of the observation and makes the decision explicit (see missing values).

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Covariation: looking between variables
Covariation is the tendency of two variables to move together. (Wickham et al. 2023) The right plot depends on the pair of types:

Categorical × continuous. Overlaid frequency polygons (geom_freqpoly()), or — to compare groups of very different sizes — map y = after_stat(density) so shapes are comparable. A boxplot (geom_boxplot()) gives a compact median/spread summary; if the categories have no natural order, reorder() them by the value you’re plotting (aes(x = reorder(class, hwy, FUN = median), y = hwy)) to reveal the trend. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Two categorical. geom_count() (circle size = count per combination), or count() then geom_tile() with a fill aesthetic — a heatmap of the cross-tabulation. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Two continuous. The scatterplot (geom_point()). When points pile up (overplotting), bin into geom_bin2d() (rectangles) or geom_hex() (hexagons), or slice one variable with cut_width() and boxplot each slice. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating EDA as a checklist. It’s a creative, iterative search; mechanical plotting misses the surprising question. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Dropping outliers reflexively. Investigate first; if you must remove, set to NA (keep the row) and say so. A dropped outlier can be the most interesting finding. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Comparing raw counts across unequal groups. Use density so shape, not size, drives the comparison. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Overplotting hiding the pattern. Thousands of overlapping points look like a blob; bin (geom_bin2d/geom_hex) to recover the density. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Leaving categories in alphabetical order. reorder() (or fct_reorder) turns a jumbled boxplot into a readable ranking. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — EDA as an iterative cycle / “state of mind” / “creative process” (generate → answer → refine; “generate a large quantity of questions”; the Tukey quote); the two always-useful questions (variation within, covariation between); variation via bar charts (geom_bar) and histograms (geom_histogram), reading typical values, clusters/subgroups, and outliers (coord_cartesian zoom; replace with NA rather than drop); and covariation — categorical×continuous (geom_freqpoly, density, geom_boxplot, reorder), two categorical (geom_count, count + geom_tile), two continuous (geom_point, geom_bin2d/geom_hex, cut_width).

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Connections

Builds on: the grammar of graphics (these are its geoms in action) and the data-science workflow (the visualize loop).
Mirrors: Part I · exploratory data analysis — the same Tukey-rooted discipline, here in tidyverse form.
Leads to: modelling — EDA’s covariation questions become the hypotheses a model tests.

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text: The CSV is the easy case. Real social-science data lives in Excel workbooks with merged cells, SQL databases too big to load, JSON APIs that return deeply nested lists, and web pages with no download button at all. R for Data Science gives a consistent escape hatch for each — and the same destination every time: a tidy data frame you can transform and model.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Why it matters
Importing a flat file is the start; the data you actually need is often locked in a spreadsheet, a database, or behind an API. Knowing how to liberate it — and where each source’s traps lie — is what separates an analyst who can only use pre-packaged datasets from one who can assemble the dataset a question requires. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Spreadsheets
Use the readxl package: read_excel() reads both .xls and .xlsx, and excel_sheets() lists the sheet names in a workbook so you can pick the one you want. (Wickham et al. 2023) For Google Sheets, the parallel package is googlesheets4. Spreadsheets carry spreadsheet-specific hazards — formatting as data, multiple header rows, merged cells — so expect to combine read_excel() with skip, explicit col_names, and a tidy-up pass.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Databases
For data too large to hold in memory, the authors recommend DBI: open a connection to a backend (RSQLite, RMySQL, Postgres…) and run SQL queries that return results as a data frame. (Wickham et al. 2023) The key idea — vital for large data — is that you don’t pull everything into memory; you query only what you need. (Wickham et al. 2023)
The tidyverse makes this seamless via dbplyr: write ordinary dplyr verbs against a database table and they are translated to SQL and run on the database server, with only the (small) result collected back into R.
library(dbplyr)
con <- DBI::dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "company.sqlite")

tbl(con, "transactions") |>           # a remote table, not in memory
  filter(year == 2024) |>             # these verbs become SQL...
  group_by(region) |>
  summarize(revenue = sum(amount)) |>
  collect()                            # ...and only the summary is pulled into R
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Hierarchical / JSON data
APIs and config files return hierarchical data — deeply nested lists. Read it with jsonlite (JSON) or xml2 (XML), which lands in R as a nested list, and then “rectangle” it: tidyr::unnest() (and unnest_longer()/unnest_wider()) expands nested columns back into a regular data frame where each row is one observation. (Wickham et al. 2023) Rectangling is the JSON-era equivalent of tidying.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Web scraping
When there’s no file at all, rvest scrapes the page itself: read_html() loads it, CSS selectors in html_elements() pick out the parts you want, and html_table() turns an HTML <table> straight into a data frame. (Wickham et al. 2023)






WarningScrape responsibly



Scraping pulls data someone else published, often without an API. Before scraping at scale, check the site’s terms and robots.txt, identify yourself, rate-limit your requests, and prefer an official API or data download when one exists. Treat a website’s data as someone else’s property and server — the same ethics that govern research integrity and data handling apply here.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Loading a giant table into memory. With databases, filter and summarise on the server (dbplyr → SQL); collect() only the result. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Trusting a spreadsheet’s layout. Merged cells, multi-row headers, and “formatting as data” need skip, explicit col_names, and cleanup. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Stopping at the nested list. JSON isn’t analysis-ready until you rectangle it with unnest_*() into one-row-per-observation. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Scraping without checking permission. Respect terms/robots.txt, rate-limit, and prefer an API; a scraper is a guest on someone’s server.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — spreadsheets (readxl::read_excel, excel_sheets, googlesheets4); databases via DBI running SQL and returning data frames, the principle of querying only what you need (and dbplyr translating dplyr to SQL); hierarchical/JSON data (jsonlite/xml2 → nested list → tidyr::unnest() “rectangling”); and web scraping with rvest (read_html, html_elements + CSS selectors, html_table).
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Connections

Builds on: importing data (the flat-file case) and tidy data (rectangling is tidying for nested data).
Pairs with: the grammar of manipulation (dbplyr runs the same verbs against SQL) and iteration (scrape/parse many pages).
Respects: research integrity & data ethics — scraped and queried data still carries provenance and consent obligations.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text: The same systems that work so well also blindside us — booms turn to busts, fixes backfire, far-off variables erupt. Meadows argues this isn’t bad luck: our “linear” upbringing blinds us to the characteristic behaviour of systems (Meadows 2008). This entry catalogues the cognitive blind spots — confusing events for structure, trusting flawed mental maps, deciding rationally on local information, and missing nonlinearities, delays, and boundaries — that turn ordinary systems into surprises.

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
Why it matters
Most analysis stops where surprise begins. We explain a recession by blaming a leader (an event), model the world with a tidy linear regression (a flawed mental map), and optimise our own corner rationally (local information) — and then the system does something none of that predicted. Naming these blind spots is the first defence against them, and the bridge from the systems mindset to the formal dynamics of the rest of the track.

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
Events vs behaviour vs structure
Meadows’ central distinction. Most attention stops at events — the visible, one-off happenings. Below them is behaviour: the pattern over time. Below that is structure: the stock-flow-feedback arrangement that generates the behaviour (Meadows 2008). Her metaphor is the Slinky: you see the event of a hand releasing it, but the bouncing behaviour comes from the Slinky’s own internal structure, not the hand (Meadows 2008). Analysis that stops at events — “blame this leader for the recession” — misleads, because the ups and downs are inherent in the system’s structure (Meadows 2008). (This is exactly why a time series is read for its behaviour over time, not its latest data point.)

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
Mental models are flawed maps
We never engage the world directly — only through mental models, which are always simplified, incomplete maps (Meadows 2008). Because those models tend to be linear and feedback-blind, they fail to predict the side effects of our actions (Meadows 2008). The map is not the territory, and the map most of us carry omits the loops.

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
Bounded rationality
Drawing on Herbert Simon, Meadows notes that people make “rational” decisions on the limited, local information available to them (Meadows 2008). The trap: locally rational choices routinely produce disastrous system-level outcomes, because the actors cannot see the full feedback structure they are embedded in (Meadows 2008). No one need be foolish or malicious for the system to fail — bounded rationality is enough. (It is the same mechanism behind the tragedy of the commons.)

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
Nonlinearities and delays
Two structural features defeat linear intuition (Meadows 2008):

Nonlinearities — in a system, “the whole is different from the sum of its parts.” A small change can produce a disproportionately large (or tiny) effect depending on the system’s state (Meadows 2008).
Delays — feedback can only affect future behaviour; it cannot correct the behaviour that drove it fast enough (Meadows 2008). These lags make systems overshoot their goals, producing oscillations or collapse (Meadows 2008).



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

G, T, S0 = 100.0, 120, 40.0
def sim(delay, alpha=0.4):
    S = np.full(T, S0)
    for t in range(1, T):
        perceived = S[t-1-delay] if t-1-delay >= 0 else S0   # act on (possibly stale) info
        S[t] = S[t-1] + alpha*(G - perceived)
    return S

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.2))
ax.axhline(G, color="#888", ls="--", lw=1.2, label="goal")
ax.plot(sim(0), color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, label="no delay → smooth convergence")
ax.plot(sim(7), color="#c0392b", lw=2.0, label="7-step delay → overshoot & oscillation")
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("stock")
ax.set_title("A delay in the feedback turns convergence into boom-and-bust")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Why delays make systems oscillate, simulated. Both systems try to raise a stock from 40 to its goal of 100 with the same correction strength. The no-delay system (green) acts on the current gap and converges smoothly. The delayed system (red) acts on information that is 7 steps stale — so it keeps correcting after the goal is reached, overshoots, then over-corrects the other way, oscillating around the goal. The behaviour (boom-bust cycling) is created entirely by the delay in the structure, not by any external shock — exactly Meadows’ point about why systems surprise us.

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
section: Where do you draw the boundary?
text:
Where do you draw the boundary?
Finally, Meadows reminds us there are no separate systems in the real world — only “clouds” of interconnectedness (Meadows 2008). Where a researcher draws the boundary of a system is a subjective decision that determines what is counted and what is ignored — and the surprises come from the variables left outside (Meadows 2008). The boundary is a modelling choice, not a fact of nature, and every model’s blind spots live just past its edge.

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Stopping at events. Explaining a pattern by its latest event (“blame the leader”) ignores the structure that generates it. Look for behaviour-over-time, then structure. (Meadows 2008)
Trusting a linear mental model. Feedback-blind maps miss side effects; expect the loop you didn’t draw. (Meadows 2008)
Assuming local rationality aggregates. Sensible local choices can produce terrible system outcomes — design for the feedback actors can’t see. (Meadows 2008)
Ignoring delays. Acting on stale information guarantees overshoot and oscillation; account for the lag before you crank up the correction. (Meadows 2008)
Forgetting the boundary is a choice. The variables you exclude are where the surprises come from; test whether your boundary is hiding the mechanism. (Meadows 2008)

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Meadows, Thinking in Systems — the events / behaviour / structure hierarchy and the Slinky; mental models as flawed, feedback-blind maps; bounded rationality (Simon) and locally-rational / globally-bad outcomes; nonlinearities (“the whole is different from the sum”) and delays causing overshoot/oscillation; and system boundaries as subjective (“clouds of interconnectedness”) choices whose excluded variables produce the surprises.

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
Appendix · the dynamics

The delay-oscillation model. A balancing loop adjusting a stock to goal G is S_{t+1}=S_t+\alpha\,(G - S_{t-d}), acting on information delayed by d steps. With d=0 it is a standard first-order convergence, S_t\to G monotonically for 0<\alpha<1. With a delay d>0, the correction applied at t is based on a gap that may already be closed — so the system keeps pushing, overshoots, then reverses. The characteristic equation gains complex roots once \alpha d is large enough, and the solution becomes an oscillation; large \alpha d makes it undamped or divergent. The practical moral: in the presence of delay, a stronger correction makes oscillation worse, not better — a deeply counter-intuitive result and a classic source of policy surprise (the inventory “bullwhip,” boom-bust construction cycles).
Nonlinearity, formally. Linear means the response to a sum of inputs is the sum of the responses. A system with a state-dependent gain — \Delta S = f(S)\,x with f varying in S — violates this: the same push x yields a different effect at different states S, so small causes can have large effects (and vice versa) near thresholds. Most of the systems traps live on these nonlinear thresholds.

title: Why Systems Surprise Us
text:
Connections

Builds on: thinking in systems (stocks, flows, feedback — the structure beneath the behaviour) and why systems work.
Explains: the systems traps (each is a structural surprise) and connects to time-series oscillation and structural breaks.
Leads to: the Dynamics cluster — nonlinear dynamics & chaos, where delays and nonlinearities become the formal study of unpredictability.

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text: Systems thinking explained why complex systems resist simple fixes. This entry is the practitioner’s map: Meadows’ twelve leverage points — ranked places to intervene, from the weak (tweak a number) to the world-changing (shift a paradigm) — and her catalogue of systems traps, the structural archetypes (tragedy of the commons, drift to low performance, seeking the wrong goal) that turn good intentions into predictable failure. For a quantitative scientist who builds the numbers and metrics systems run on, this is the map of how good numbers go wrong.

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text:
Why it matters
The metrics, incentives, and policies a quantitative researcher helps design are interventions in systems — and most interventions push the wrong lever, or the right lever the wrong way (Meadows 2008). Knowing the leverage hierarchy tells you why a tax tweak rarely fixes anything while a changed goal transforms everything; knowing the traps tells you why your well-measured KPI quietly corrupts the thing it measures. This is the systems-level companion to the tyranny of metrics.

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text:
The twelve leverage points
Meadows defines a leverage point as a place where “a small shift in one thing can produce big changes in everything” — and warns that people intuitively find them, then push in the wrong direction, worsening the problem (Meadows 2008). Ranked from weakest to most powerful (the easy-to-change ones are weak; the hard-to-change ones are transformative) (Meadows 2008):








#
Leverage point
Why it sits here




12
Numbers — parameters, constants, subsidies, tax rates
The focus of 99% of debate, yet rarely changes system behaviour


11
Buffer sizes — stabilizing stocks
Bigger buffers add stability but slow response


10
Stock-and-flow structures — physical layout
High leverage but slow/expensive to rebuild


9
Lengths of delays
Too-long delays cause overshoot and collapse


8
Strength of balancing (negative) feedback loops
Goal-seeking self-correction (a thermostat, a law)


7
Gain of reinforcing (positive) feedback loops
Slowing runaway loops often beats strengthening balancing ones


6
Structure of information flows — who can see what
Adding a missing feedback link can change behaviour cheaply


5
Rules — incentives, punishments, constraints
Rules reshape interconnections, radically altering behaviour


4
Power to self-organize — add/change structure
The deepest resilience: a system that can rewrite itself


3
Goals of the system
The “least obvious” but decisive driver — everything realigns to the goal


2
Paradigm / mindset the system arises from
Shared assumptions; hardest to change, most powerful


1
Power to transcend paradigms
Holding no paradigm as final — choosing the most useful one









TipMeadows’ punchline



We obsess over the numbers (point 12 — tax rates, budgets) precisely because they’re easy to argue about, and ignore the goals and paradigms (points 3–1) that actually run the system. A famous illustration: a visible electric meter (an information flow, point 6) cut household use more than any price change — the missing feedback link, not the parameter, was the lever (Meadows 2008).

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text:
The systems traps
Traps are structures that reliably produce undesirable behaviour — and each has a characteristic way out (Meadows 2008):

Policy resistance (fixes that fail). Actors with different goals pull in different directions; effort cancels and the status quo persists. Way out: find a common goal that meets everyone’s underlying need, rather than win the tug-of-war (Meadows 2008).
Tragedy of the commons. Users of a shared, limited resource act on individual benefit and deplete it for all. Way out: feedback — educate users to see the consequence, or regulate via property rights / mutual agreement (Meadows 2008).
Drift to low performance (eroding goals). A system lets its standard slide toward recent bad performance, a reinforcing race to the bottom. Way out: hold standards absolute, or peg them to the best past performance, not the last (Meadows 2008).
Escalation. Two actors try to out-do each other (arms race, price war) — a runaway reinforcing loop. Way out: one side unilaterally de-escalates, or both agree a ceiling (Meadows 2008).
Success to the successful. Winners are rewarded with the means to win again, until competitors are eliminated. Way out: periodically level the field (antitrust, diversification) (Meadows 2008).
Shifting the burden to the intervenor (addiction). Leaning on an outside fix lets the system’s own self-correction atrophy, breeding dependence. Way out: invest in the internal, long-term capacity, not the short-term patch (Meadows 2008).
Rule beating. Actors obey the letter while subverting the intent (“juking the stats”). Way out: design rules that reward the intent, or fix the goal (Meadows 2008).
Seeking the wrong goal. The goal is set as a proxy measure (GDP, test scores) rather than the real outcome, so the system delivers the measure and fails the intent. Way out: make goals and measures reflect real welfare (Meadows 2008).



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

K, r, T = 100.0, 0.4, 70

def simulate(open_access):
    R = 80.0; e = 0.12 if open_access else 0.20
    traj = []
    for t in range(T):
        traj.append(R)
        harvest = e * R
        if open_access:
            e *= 1.06            # effort grows each period (reinforcing, ignores the stock)
        R = R + r*R*(1 - R/K) - harvest
        R = max(R, 0.0)
    return np.array(traj)

open_traj = simulate(True)
managed_traj = simulate(False)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.2))
ax.plot(open_traj, color="#c0392b", lw=2.4, label="open access (effort keeps growing) → collapse")
ax.plot(managed_traj, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4, label="feedback management (sustainable effort)")
ax.axhline(0, color="#999", lw=0.8)
ax.fill_between(range(T), 0, open_traj, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.06)
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("shared resource stock")
ax.set_title("Tragedy of the commons: structure, not effort, decides the outcome")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
ax.set_ylim(0, 100)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The tragedy of the commons, simulated as a difference equation. A shared resource regrows logistically. Under OPEN ACCESS (red), each user’s harvesting effort grows every period — a reinforcing loop with no feedback from the dwindling stock — so extraction outruns regrowth and the resource collapses. Under FEEDBACK MANAGEMENT (green), effort is held at a sustainable level (a balancing loop), and the stock settles at a healthy equilibrium. Same resource, same biology; the difference is purely the system’s structure — exactly Meadows’ point that structure, not effort or blame, drives the trap.

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text:
The quantitative scientist’s takeaway
Two of Meadows’ ideas land squarely on this manual’s themes:

“Seeking the wrong goal” is Goodhart’s law. When you optimise a proxy (test scores, citations, GDP) the system produces the proxy and abandons the intent — the metric you built becomes the trap (Meadows 2008). The fix is leverage point #3: change the goal, not the number.
Information flows (#6) are cheap, powerful leverage. As a data scientist you often are the information flow — the dashboard, the released estimate, the visible meter. Adding the missing feedback link can move a system more than any parameter you could tune (Meadows 2008).

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Tweaking parameters and calling it reform. Numbers (point 12) are the weakest lever; if behaviour won’t budge, look to rules, goals, and paradigms. (Meadows 2008)
Pushing the right lever the wrong way. Strengthening a reinforcing loop you meant to damp, or adding a buffer that just slows response — intuition often inverts the sign. (Meadows 2008)
Optimising a proxy goal. “Seeking the wrong goal” + “rule beating” guarantee the metric improves while the mission decays. (Meadows 2008)
Becoming the intervenor. Repeated external fixes erode the system’s own capacity (addiction); build internal feedback instead. (Meadows 2008)
Blaming actors for a structural trap. The tragedy of the commons isn’t bad people — it’s a missing feedback loop; fix the structure. (Meadows 2008)

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Meadows, Thinking in Systems: A Primer — the twelve leverage points in increasing power (numbers → buffers → structures → delays → balancing loops → reinforcing loops → information flows → rules → self-organization → goals → paradigms → transcending paradigms), the insight that easy parameters are weak while goals/paradigms are strong, and that people push leverage points the wrong way; the visible electric meter (information-flow) example; and the systems traps — policy resistance, tragedy of the commons, drift to low performance, escalation, success to the successful, shifting the burden (addiction), rule beating, and seeking the wrong goal — each with its way out.

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text:
Appendix · the dynamics

The commons model. The stock follows logistic regrowth minus harvest, R_{t+1} = R_t + rR_t(1-R_t/K) - e_t R_t. With constant effort e<r there is a stable equilibrium R^\* = K(1-e/r) — the managed (green) case settles there. Under open access the effort grows geometrically, e_{t+1}=1.06\,e_t; once e_t exceeds the maximum regrowth rate r, net change is negative for all R and the stock collapses to zero. The reinforcing loop on effort (more boats → visible profit → more boats), uncoupled from the falling stock, is the whole trap; adding a balancing link from R back to e is the “way out.”
Why goals dominate (leverage #3). In control terms, a feedback system drives its state toward its setpoint; change a parameter and you nudge the trajectory, change the setpoint (goal) and the entire loop re-aims. That is the formal reason a changed goal out-leverages any changed number.

title: Systems Traps & Leverage Points
text:
Connections

Builds on: systems thinking (stocks, flows, feedback loops — the vocabulary these leverage points and traps are written in).
Deepens: the tyranny of metrics — “seeking the wrong goal” and “rule beating” are Goodhart’s law as systems archetypes.
Feeds: from evidence to decision and the limits of quantification — choosing where to intervene, not just what to measure.

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text: Classical epidemiology assumes everyone mixes with everyone. Real contagion — viral, informational, behavioural — moves node by node along the edges of a contact network, so structure is destiny. The hubs are superspreaders that abolish the usual safety threshold; weak ties carry novelty between communities; and behaviours spread by a different rule than germs. This entry — the capstone of the Network Analysis sub-track — is where everything before it pays off.

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
Why it matters
Epidemics, viral marketing, the diffusion of innovations, the cascade of a protest or a bank run — all are spreading processes on networks, and the network determines who to vaccinate, who to seed, and why some good ideas never catch on. It also connects straight back to causal inference: peer effects and contagion are why SUTVA (no interference between units) so often fails in social data.

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
section: Epidemic models, run on a graph
text:
Epidemic models, run on a graph
The compartmental models, but evolving node by node with transmission along edges at probability \beta (Barabási 2016; Menczer et al. 2020):

SI — susceptible → infected, and infected forever. The simplest baseline.
SIS — no immunity (a cold): infected nodes recover back to susceptible and can be reinfected.
SIR — permanent immunity or removal: susceptible → infectious → removed (recovered/dead, no longer transmitting).

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
Hubs and the missing threshold
Network topology sets the speed and reach (Barabási 2016):

Hubs are superspreaders. A high-degree node is infected early (many exposures) and, once infected, rebroadcasts to enormous numbers of others; many shortest paths run through hubs, shrinking the distance the contagion must travel (Barabási 2016).
No epidemic threshold on scale-free networks (Pastor-Satorras & Vespignani). In a random network a disease must be “contagious enough” to persist; on a scale-free network the hubs let a disease linger and spread no matter how low its infectiousness (Barabási 2016). The usual safety margin simply vanishes.

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
Simple vs complex contagion
Crucially, information and behaviour spread by different rules (Menczer et al. 2020):

Simple contagion — germs and simple information (a rumour) transmit on a single contact (Menczer et al. 2020).
Complex contagion — behaviours, norms, and technology adoption need multiple reinforcing contacts: a node adopts only when a threshold fraction q of its neighbours already has (Granovetter threshold models, the Watts global-cascade model) (Menczer et al. 2020).

This flips the value of network structure (Menczer et al. 2020): the long-range shortcuts that make a small world great for simple contagion are ineffective for complex contagion, which needs social reinforcement and so spreads better through densely clustered ties where neighbours share friends (Menczer et al. 2020). Tight communities can block a cascade — why some innovations never diffuse.

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
The strength of weak ties
Granovetter’s classic insight (Menczer et al. 2020): strong ties (close friends) are redundant — they sit inside the same dense cluster, all knowing each other. Weak ties (acquaintances) act as local bridges between communities, and so are the main conduits of novel information — job leads, news from distant parts of the network (Menczer et al. 2020). The counterintuitive moral: for reach and novelty, your acquaintances matter more than your friends.


Show the figure code (BA network + SIR by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
N, m = 1500, 2
# grow a Barabási–Albert network
deg = np.zeros(N, int); repeated = []; nbrs = [set() for _ in range(N)]
for i in range(m+1):
    for j in range(i+1, m+1):
        nbrs[i].add(j); nbrs[j].add(i); deg[i]+=1; deg[j]+=1; repeated += [i, j]
for new in range(m+1, N):
    tg = set()
    while len(tg) < m: tg.add(repeated[rng.integers(len(repeated))])
    for t in tg:
        nbrs[new].add(t); nbrs[t].add(new); deg[new]+=1; deg[t]+=1; repeated += [new, t]
adj = [np.fromiter(s, int) for s in nbrs]

def sir(immune, beta=0.06, gamma=0.12, T=60):
    state = np.zeros(N, int)                  # 0=S 1=I 2=R
    state[list(immune)] = 2
    ever = np.zeros(N, bool)
    seed = rng.choice(np.where(state == 0)[0]); state[seed] = 1; ever[seed] = True
    curve = []
    for _ in range(T):
        inf = np.where(state == 1)[0]
        ns = state.copy()
        for u in inf:
            for v in adj[u]:
                if state[v] == 0 and rng.random() < beta: ns[v] = 1; ever[v] = True
            if rng.random() < gamma: ns[u] = 2
        state = ns; curve.append(ever.mean())
    return np.array(curve)

nv = int(0.08*N)
rand_immune = set(rng.choice(N, nv, replace=False))
hub_immune = set(np.argsort(deg)[-nv:])
none = np.mean([sir(set()) for _ in range(5)], axis=0)
randc = np.mean([sir(rand_immune) for _ in range(5)], axis=0)
hubc = np.mean([sir(hub_immune) for _ in range(5)], axis=0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.plot(none, color="#c0392b", lw=2.2, label="no immunisation")
ax.plot(randc, color="#d98a29", lw=2.2, label="random 8% immunised")
ax.plot(hubc, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4, label="top-8% HUBS immunised")
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("cumulative fraction infected")
ax.set_title("Vaccinate the hubs: same budget, the epidemic collapses")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="center right"); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Why you vaccinate the hubs, simulated. An SIR epidemic runs on a scale-free network (grown by preferential attachment). With NO immunisation (red) the outbreak infects most of the network. Immunising 8% of nodes at RANDOM (orange) barely helps — a random node is almost always low-degree, so the hubs survive and keep spreading. Immunising the SAME 8%, but chosen as the highest-degree HUBS (green), collapses the epidemic to a small cluster: removing the superspreaders shatters the transmission backbone. Same vaccine budget, opposite outcome — structure, not quantity, decides.

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
Practical implications
The structure points straight at policy (Barabási 2016; Menczer et al. 2020):

Targeted immunisation. Vaccinating a small fraction of hubs fragments the network and stops a pandemic far more effectively than random vaccination (Barabási 2016) — the figure’s lesson.
Seeding diffusion. In marketing, target influentials with access to easily-influenced others to seed a cascade; but for complex contagion, seed clustered communities for reinforcement (Menczer et al. 2020).
Why innovations stall. Densely closed communities can block cascades — a diagnosis for failed diffusion (Menczer et al. 2020).

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Assuming well-mixing. Network structure changes the threshold, the speed, and who matters; the mean-field SIR can badly mislead. (Barabási 2016)
Treating behaviour like a virus. Adoption is complex contagion (needs reinforcement); long-range shortcuts that spread rumours fast may fail to spread behaviour. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Random vaccination on a scale-free network. It wastes doses on low-degree nodes and misses the hubs doing the spreading. (Barabási 2016)
Ignoring interference in causal studies. Contagion violates SUTVA — one unit’s treatment spills to its neighbours. (Menczer et al. 2020)

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Barabási, Network Science — SI/SIS/SIR on networks with edge transmission; hubs as superspreaders; the absence of an epidemic threshold on scale-free networks (Pastor-Satorras & Vespignani); and targeted (hub) immunisation.
Menczer, Fortunato & Davis, A First Course in Network Science — simple vs complex contagion (Granovetter thresholds, Watts cascades; clusters vs shortcuts), the strength of weak ties (bridges, novel information), and seeding/diffusion implications.

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

SIR on a network. Each infected node u infects each susceptible neighbour independently with probability \beta per step and recovers with probability \gamma — exactly the loop in the code. The mean-field epidemic threshold is \lambda_c=\beta/\gamma \sim \langle k\rangle/\langle k^2\rangle: an outbreak grows only if \lambda>\lambda_c. On scale-free networks \langle k^2\rangle\to\infty, so \lambda_c\to0 — no threshold. Targeted immunisation works by removing the high-k nodes that make \langle k^2\rangle explode, restoring a finite threshold.
Complex-contagion threshold. In Watts’ model a node with fraction \phi of adopting neighbours adopts iff \phi\ge q. A global cascade requires a connected “vulnerable” cluster (nodes with low enough threshold relative to degree); too few or too many connections both suppress cascades, so there is a cascade window in (q,\langle k\rangle) — unlike simple contagion, more connectivity is not always more spread.
Why weak ties carry novelty. A strong tie sits in a triangle (its endpoints share neighbours), so its information is redundant. A weak tie that is a local bridge is the only short path between two clusters, so it is the unique conduit for whatever one cluster knows and the other doesn’t.

title: Spreading on Networks: Contagion & Diffusion
text:
Connections

Builds on: scale-free networks (hubs, no threshold), small worlds (shortcuts speed simple contagion), and community structure (clusters reinforce complex contagion).
Connects to: Erdős–Rényi percolation (the \langle k\rangle=1 threshold is the epidemic threshold) and causal interference / SUTVA.
Leads to: the Dynamics cluster — agent-based models make these spreading rules into full simulations of social behaviour.

title: Self-Organised Criticality
text: Drop sand onto a table, one grain at a time. At first the pile just grows. But once its slope reaches the angle of repose, something remarkable happens: the pile stops getting steeper and instead sheds sand in avalanches — most tiny, a few enormous, and every size in between. The pile has tuned itself to a critical state, with no one adjusting anything. Per Bak called this self-organised criticality, and it is the leading candidate for why power laws are so common: a system poised at criticality responds to a small nudge with a cascade whose size is unpredictable and scale-free.

title: Self-Organised Criticality
text:
Why it matters
Self-organised criticality (SOC) reframes how we think about big events. In a critical system, the large avalanche is not caused by a large shock — the trigger is the same single grain that usually does nothing. The size of the response is a property of the system’s state, not the size of the cause (Mitchell 2009). That idea reaches directly into the social world: market crashes, mass extinctions, traffic jams, cascading blackouts, and viral fads can all be the rare giant avalanche of a system that spends most of its time quiet. It dissolves the instinct to hunt for a proportionate cause behind every catastrophe — sometimes the cause is ordinary and the system was primed.

title: Self-Organised Criticality
section: The critical state: between order and randomness
text:
The critical state: between order and randomness
Most systems we understand sit at one of two extremes. An ordered system is frozen and predictable — perturb it and the disturbance dies out locally. A disordered (chaotic) system is all noise — perturb it and the effect is random and uncorrelated. The critical state is the razor’s edge between them: the unique point where disturbances are correlated across all scales, so a local nudge can stay local or propagate across the whole system (Mitchell 2009).
Physicists already knew critical points exist — water poised exactly between liquid and gas shows fluctuations at every scale. But that requires fine-tuning the temperature to a precise value. Per Bak’s startling claim was that many systems reach this critical point on their own, as an attractor of their ordinary dynamics — no tuning required. Hence self-organised criticality (Mitchell 2009).

title: Self-Organised Criticality
text:
The sandpile model
The canonical model is the abelian sandpile (Bak–Tang–Wiesenfeld). The rules could not be simpler:

Each cell of a grid holds some grains of sand.
Drop one grain on a random cell.
Whenever a cell holds 4 or more grains, it topples: it sheds 4 grains, one to each of its four neighbours. Grains that fall off the edge of the table are lost.
Toppling can push a neighbour over 4, so one drop can set off a chain reaction — an avalanche. Count the topplings; that is the avalanche size.

Run it and the pile self-organises: it climbs to the critical slope and stays there, balanced by avalanches. Crucially, the distribution of avalanche sizes is a power law — there is no typical avalanche, just as there is no typical earthquake (Mitchell 2009). The figure builds a real sandpile by hand and measures its avalanches.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
N = 50
z = np.zeros((N, N), dtype=np.int64)

def drop(i, j):
    """Add a grain at (i,j) and topple to stability; return avalanche size."""
    z[i, j] += 1
    size = 0
    while True:
        over = z >= 4
        n = int(over.sum())
        if n == 0:
            break
        size += n
        z[over] -= 4
        # each toppling cell sends one grain to each neighbour; edges spill off
        z[1:, :]  += over[:-1, :]    # to the cell below
        z[:-1, :] += over[1:, :]     # to the cell above
        z[:, 1:]  += over[:, :-1]    # to the right
        z[:, :-1] += over[:, 1:]     # to the left
    return size

# warm-up: let the pile self-organise to the critical state (record nothing)
for _ in range(20000):
    drop(rng.integers(N), rng.integers(N))

snapshot = z.copy()

# measure avalanche sizes in the critical state
sizes = np.fromiter(
    (drop(rng.integers(N), rng.integers(N)) for _ in range(40000)),
    dtype=np.int64,
)
sizes = sizes[sizes > 0]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.3))

im = axL.imshow(snapshot, cmap="magma", vmin=0, vmax=3)
axL.set_title("Critical configuration (grains per cell)")
axL.set_xticks([]); axL.set_yticks([])
fig.colorbar(im, ax=axL, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04, ticks=[0, 1, 2, 3])

# log-binned avalanche-size distribution
bins = np.logspace(0, np.log10(sizes.max() + 1), 22)
hist, edges = np.histogram(sizes, bins=bins, density=True)
centres = np.sqrt(edges[:-1] * edges[1:])
m = hist > 0
axR.loglog(centres[m], hist[m], "o", color="#1f4e79", ms=5, label="avalanche sizes")
# reference power-law slope for the eye
ref_x = centres[m]
axR.loglog(ref_x, hist[m][0] * (ref_x / ref_x[0]) ** (-1.1),
           "--", color="#c0392b", lw=1, label="slope ≈ −1.1 (power law)")
axR.set_xlabel("avalanche size  s"); axR.set_ylabel("p(s)")
axR.set_title("Avalanche sizes: scale-free")
axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A self-organised critical state, built by hand. LEFT: a snapshot of a 50×50 abelian sandpile after it has self-organised — each cell holds 0–3 grains (toppling at 4), a balanced critical configuration that the dynamics reach and maintain on their own, with no tuning. RIGHT: the distribution of avalanche sizes (number of topplings per dropped grain), on log-log axes, measured over tens of thousands of drops. It is a near-straight line — a POWER LAW spanning several decades. Most drops do nothing; a few set off system-spanning cascades; every size in between occurs. The same single grain causes both. That is the signature of criticality: scale-free response.

title: Self-Organised Criticality
text:
Tipping points and early warnings
SOC supplies a mechanism for tipping points — the moments when a system flips abruptly from one regime to another (a lake turning turbid, a market crashing, an ice sheet collapsing). As a system drifts toward such a critical transition, it shows a detectable fingerprint called critical slowing down: the stabilising feedbacks that pull it back from disturbances weaken, so it takes longer and longer to recover from small perturbations, and its fluctuations grow more correlated (Mitchell 2009). This slowing recovery — measurable as rising autocorrelation and variance in a time series — has been proposed as a generic early-warning signal of approaching tipping points in ecosystems, climate, and financial markets (Mitchell 2009).
The promise is real but so is the difficulty: the signal is noisy, it can appear without a transition following, and a transition can arrive with little warning. SOC is a powerful lens, not a crystal ball.

title: Self-Organised Criticality
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Demanding a big cause for a big event. In a critical system the trigger of the giant avalanche is the same ordinary grain that usually does nothing — looking for a proportionate cause is a category error. (Mitchell 2009)
Assuming criticality must be tuned. The whole point of SOC is that the critical state is an attractor — the system finds it without anyone setting a dial. (Mitchell 2009)
Treating early-warning signals as guarantees. Critical slowing down is a statistical tendency, not a deterministic alarm; false positives and silent transitions both occur. (Mitchell 2009)
Over-extending the sandpile. SOC is a compelling candidate mechanism for many power laws, not a proof that any particular one is self-organised critical — that still needs the data. (Mitchell 2009)

title: Self-Organised Criticality
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — Per Bak’s self-organised criticality; the system that evolves without tuning to a critical state between order and randomness; the sandpile model and its power-law avalanche-size distribution; and the link to tipping points / critical transitions with critical slowing down as an early-warning signal across ecosystems, climate, and markets.

title: Self-Organised Criticality
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The abelian sandpile (BTW). On a grid, each site i holds height z_i. The toppling rule is: if z_i \ge z_c (here z_c=4, the number of neighbours), then z_i \to z_i - 4 and each neighbour gains one grain. Grains at the boundary leave the system (open boundary), which is what lets the pile reach a stationary critical state rather than filling up. The model is “abelian” because the final stable configuration is independent of the order in which unstable sites are toppled — a deep property that makes the dynamics well-defined.
Why a power law. At the critical state the system has no characteristic length: the spatial extent of toppling activity is correlated across all scales (a diverging correlation length, exactly as at an equilibrium critical point). The avalanche-size distribution then takes the scale-free form 
P(s) \sim s^{-\tau} f(s/s_{\max}),
 a power law with exponent \tau (near 1 for the 2-D BTW pile) cut off only by the finite system size s_{\max}. See power laws & scaling for the statistics of such distributions.
Critical slowing down. Near a critical transition the dominant eigenvalue of the system’s linearised recovery dynamics approaches 1 (discrete time) or 0 (continuous time). The recovery rate \to 0, so the return time after a perturbation diverges; in observable terms the lag-1 autocorrelation \to 1 and the variance rises. These are the statistical early-warning indicators.

title: Self-Organised Criticality
text:
Connections

Builds on: nonlinear dynamics & chaos (the order–chaos axis SOC sits between) and power laws & scaling (the distribution SOC produces).
Mechanism for: emergence & self-organisation — criticality is how a system organises itself without a controller.
Reaches into: spreading on networks (cascades and tipping in connected populations) and the study of systemic risk and fragility.

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text: Before you can say a real network is special, you need a baseline of pure randomness. The Erdős–Rényi (ER) model supplies it: take N nodes and connect each pair independently with probability p. ER gets one thing profoundly right — a giant component that snaps into existence at a critical average degree — and two things wrong (no hubs, no clustering), and those failures are exactly what motivated the small-world and scale-free models that followed.

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
Why it matters
ER is the null model of network science — the “what would this look like by chance?” against which real structure is judged (it underlies the modularity null). And its phase transition is a first, clean example of emergence: a global property (system-wide connectivity) appearing abruptly from a smooth change in a local parameter — the theme of the whole Dynamics cluster.

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
The G(N,p) model
Start with N isolated nodes; connect each of the \binom{N}{2} possible pairs with fixed probability p (Barabási 2016). Larger p means more edges; the average degree is \langle k\rangle = p(N-1).

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
Degree distribution: Poisson and peaked
For large sparse ER graphs the degree distribution is Poisson (Barabási 2016):

p_k = e^{-\langle k\rangle}\frac{\langle k\rangle^{k}}{k!}.

It is peaked at \langle k\rangle — most nodes have roughly the same degree, so the average degree is a genuine characteristic scale of the network (Barabási 2016). (Hold that thought: the scale-free entry is named for the absence of such a scale.)

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
The giant-component phase transition
ER’s signature result: macroscopic connectivity depends sharply on \langle k\rangle (Barabási 2016). A giant component — one cluster holding a finite fraction of all nodes — emerges suddenly when \langle k\rangle > 1; below that threshold the graph is a dust of tiny isolated pieces (Barabási 2016).


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import deque

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
N = 600
kbars = np.linspace(0.1, 4.0, 26)

def giant_fraction(kbar):
    p = kbar/(N-1)
    U = rng.random((N, N)); A = (U < p); A = np.triu(A, 1); A = A | A.T
    adj = [np.where(A[i])[0] for i in range(N)]
    seen = np.zeros(N, bool); largest = 0
    for s in range(N):
        if not seen[s]:
            q = deque([s]); seen[s] = True; cnt = 0
            while q:
                u = q.popleft(); cnt += 1
                for v in adj[u]:
                    if not seen[v]: seen[v] = True; q.append(v)
            largest = max(largest, cnt)
    return largest/N

frac = [giant_fraction(kb) for kb in kbars]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.plot(kbars, frac, "o-", color="#1f4e79", lw=2, ms=4)
ax.axvline(1.0, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.6)
ax.text(1.05, 0.08, "critical ⟨k⟩ = 1", color="#c0392b", fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("average degree ⟨k⟩"); ax.set_ylabel("giant-component size (fraction of N)")
ax.set_title("Connectivity appears all at once: the ER phase transition")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The Erdős–Rényi giant-component phase transition, simulated by hand. For each average degree ⟨k⟩ we build a random graph (each edge an independent coin flip), find the largest connected component with BFS, and plot its size as a fraction of the network. Below ⟨k⟩ = 1 (red line) the graph is fragmented — the largest piece is a vanishing fraction. Right at ⟨k⟩ = 1 a giant component abruptly appears and then rapidly engulfs almost everyone. One smooth knob (the average number of friends), one sharp qualitative change: a phase transition, and a textbook case of emergence.

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
Short paths
Random graphs have very short distances: the average path length scales as \langle d\rangle \approx \ln N/\ln\langle k\rangle (Barabási 2016) — logarithmically in network size, so even billions of nodes sit a few hops apart. This part ER gets right, and it is half of the “small world.”

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
section: Why ER fails on real networks
text:
Why ER fails on real networks
The other half it gets wrong (Barabási 2016):

No hubs. The Poisson tail decays faster than exponentially (\sim 1/k!), so a node with vastly more links than average is essentially forbidden — yet real networks (the Internet, the web, citation graphs) are built around hubs (Barabási 2016).
Low clustering. ER’s clustering coefficient is just C = p (Barabási 2016) — vanishingly small in a large sparse network, whereas real social networks have high clustering (triadic closure). Your friends being friends is not a coincidence ER can produce.

These two failures are precisely the gaps filled by Watts–Strogatz (clustering) and Barabási–Albert (hubs).

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using ER as a model of a real network. It is a null model, not a description — real networks have hubs and clustering it can’t produce. (Barabási 2016)
Expecting connectivity to scale smoothly. Below \langle k\rangle=1 adding edges barely connects anything; cross the threshold and the network integrates abruptly. (Barabási 2016)
Reading the average degree as typical in real data. It is typical in ER (Poisson), but meaningless for the heavy-tailed degree distributions of real networks. (Barabási 2016)

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Barabási, Network Science — the G(N,p) model; the Poisson degree distribution with characteristic scale \langle k\rangle; the giant-component phase transition at \langle k\rangle=1; the logarithmic average path length \ln N/\ln\langle k\rangle; and ER’s failures — no hubs (1/k! tail) and low clustering (C=p) — motivating small-world and scale-free models.

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Poisson degree distribution. Each node has N-1 potential neighbours, each present with probability p, so degree is Binomial(N-1,p). For large N, small p, fixed \langle k\rangle=p(N-1), the Binomial converges to Poisson(\langle k\rangle) — hence the 1/k! tail that forbids hubs.
The \langle k\rangle = 1 threshold (branching intuition). Follow a random edge: the expected number of new neighbours it leads to is \langle k\rangle. If \langle k\rangle<1 the branching process dies out (small components); if \langle k\rangle>1 it survives with positive probability, seeding a component that grows with N — the giant component. The transition at \langle k\rangle=1 is the percolation threshold, the same mathematics as epidemic R_0=1 (spreading on networks).
Clustering C=p. Two neighbours of a node are themselves connected with probability exactly p (edges are independent), so the expected local clustering is p — independent of degree, and tiny when p\sim
\langle k\rangle/N.

title: Random Graphs: The Erdős–Rényi Model
text:
Connections

Builds on: network structure & metrics (components, clustering, path length) and common distributions (Binomial → Poisson).
Contrasts with: Watts–Strogatz (adds the missing clustering) and scale-free networks (adds the missing hubs).
Echoes: the epidemic threshold (percolation = the \langle k\rangle=1 transition) and emergence (a phase transition from a smooth parameter).

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text: Take the simplest nonlinear rule for a population — x_{n+1} = r\,x_n(1-x_n) — and turn one knob. At low r the population settles to a steady value; raise r and it splits into a 2-cycle, then 4, then 8, then chaos: aperiodic, never-repeating, and exponentially sensitive to its starting point. The system is fully deterministic — no randomness anywhere — yet long-range prediction is impossible in principle. That is the revolution of chaos theory, and it bounds what any model of a complex system can forecast.

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
Why it matters
Chaos sets a hard ceiling on prediction for weather, ecosystems, heart rhythms, and economies (Strogatz 2015). It dissolves the comforting equation deterministic = predictable: you can know the exact rule and still not know the future, because you can never know the present to infinite precision. For a forecaster or modeller, knowing which regime a system is in — stable, periodic, or chaotic — is the difference between a useful forecast and false confidence.

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
Dynamical systems and attractors
A dynamical system evolves over time by a fixed rule — a differential equation (continuous time) or an iterated map (discrete) (Strogatz 2015). Its states live in phase space, and over time the system often settles onto an attractor (Strogatz 2015). Stable fixed points (sinks) pull nearby states back after a disturbance; unstable ones (sources) repel (Strogatz 2015). The question chaos answers is: what happens when the attractor is neither a point nor a cycle?

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
section: The logistic map: a road to chaos
text:
The logistic map: a road to chaos
The canonical example — simple rule, astounding complexity (Mitchell 2009; Strogatz 2015):

x_{n+1} = r\,x_n(1-x_n),

with x_n the population as a fraction of carrying capacity and r the growth rate. As r rises (Mitchell 2009):

small r → a stable fixed point (one steady value);
higher r → period-doubling: oscillation between 2 values, then 4, then 8, … each split a bifurcation;
the cascade culminates in chaos at r \approx 3.5699 (Mitchell 2009).



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Bifurcation diagram (vectorised over r)
rs = np.linspace(2.5, 4.0, 2000)
x = np.full_like(rs, 0.5)
for _ in range(400): x = rs*x*(1-x)            # discard transient
pts_r, pts_x = [], []
for _ in range(160):
    x = rs*x*(1-x); pts_r.append(rs.copy()); pts_x.append(x.copy())
pts_r = np.concatenate(pts_r); pts_x = np.concatenate(pts_x)

# Sensitive dependence at r = 3.9
r = 3.9; a = np.empty(60); b = np.empty(60); a[0], b[0] = 0.5, 0.5 + 1e-9
for n in range(59):
    a[n+1] = r*a[n]*(1-a[n]); b[n+1] = r*b[n]*(1-b[n])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.3), gridspec_kw={"width_ratios":[1.5,1]})
axL.plot(pts_r, pts_x, ",k", alpha=0.25)
axL.axvline(3.5699, color="#c0392b", lw=1, ls="--"); axL.text(3.58, 0.05, "onset of chaos", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axL.set_xlabel("growth rate  r"); axL.set_ylabel("long-run population  x"); axL.set_title("Bifurcation diagram: period-doubling → chaos")

axR.plot(a, "o-", color="#1f4e79", ms=3, lw=1, label="x₀ = 0.5")
axR.plot(b, "o-", color="#c0392b", ms=3, lw=1, label="x₀ = 0.5 + 10⁻⁹")
axR.set_xlabel("step n"); axR.set_ylabel("x"); axR.set_title("Sensitive dependence (r = 3.9)")
axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The logistic map’s route to chaos, computed by hand. Left: the bifurcation diagram — for each growth rate r we iterate x_{n+1}=r·x_n(1−x_n), discard the transient, and plot the long-run values. A single stable value splits into 2, then 4, then 8 (the period-doubling cascade), then dissolves into chaos around r≈3.57 — a smear of aperiodic values, punctuated by white ‘windows’ of order. Right: sensitive dependence at r=3.9. Two trajectories start 10⁻⁹ apart (indistinguishable) and track perfectly… until the tiny gap, amplified exponentially, explodes — and they diverge completely. Deterministic, yet unpredictable: the butterfly effect.





A stunning twist: Feigenbaum found the bifurcations arrive at a universal rate — the gaps shrink by a constant factor \delta \approx 4.6692 for a huge class of maps (Mitchell 2009). The road to chaos has the same shape across utterly different systems.

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
Chaos and sensitive dependence
Chaos is aperiodic long-term behaviour in a deterministic system with sensitive dependence on initial conditions (Strogatz 2015). Sensitive dependence — the “butterfly effect” — means two trajectories from nearly identical states diverge exponentially fast, so any measurement error, however tiny, makes long-term prediction vastly inaccurate (Mitchell 2009). The Lyapunov exponent \lambda measures that divergence rate; a positive \lambda is the signature of chaos (Strogatz 2015). Crucially, chaos is deterministic, not random: the rule is exact with no random inputs — it only appears random because we can’t know the start to infinite precision (Mitchell 2009).

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
Strange attractors
A bounded chaotic system that settles onto neither a point nor a cycle lands on a strange attractor — e.g. the butterfly-shaped Lorenz attractor, a fractal with fine structure at every scale (Strogatz 2015). Its geometry comes from stretching (magnifying microscopic differences) and folding (keeping trajectories bounded), like kneading dough (Strogatz 2015).

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
The limits of prediction
The practical payload (Strogatz 2015):

A prediction horizon. Because errors grow exponentially, forecasts fail beyond a finite time — about a week for weather (Strogatz 2015).
Precision barely helps. A million-fold improvement in initial-measurement accuracy extends the horizon only slightly (Strogatz 2015).
Predict statistics, not specifics. You can forecast the climate (statistical properties) even when the weather (the specific path) is fundamentally obscured (Strogatz 2015).

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Equating deterministic with predictable. A known exact rule can still be unforecastable; chaos is the reason. (Mitchell 2009)
Calling noisy data ‘chaotic’. Chaos is low-dimensional determinism, not randomness; the claim requires evidence (a positive Lyapunov exponent, a strange attractor), not just irregularity. (Strogatz 2015)
Chasing long-range point forecasts. Past the prediction horizon, report distributions/statistics, not a single trajectory. (Strogatz 2015)
Assuming more data fixes it. Exponential error growth means precision yields only logarithmic horizon gains. (Strogatz 2015)

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos — dynamical systems, phase space, fixed points/attractors and stability; chaos as aperiodic deterministic behaviour with sensitive dependence; the Lyapunov exponent; strange attractors (Lorenz, stretch-and-fold, fractal); and the prediction horizon / futility-of-precision implications.
Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — the logistic map x_{n+1}=rx_n(1-x_n), the period-doubling cascade and bifurcation diagram, the onset of chaos at r\approx3.57, the Feigenbaum universal constant, and that chaos is deterministic, not random.

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Logistic-map stability. The map f(x)=rx(1-x) has a nonzero fixed point x^*=1-1/r, stable while |f'(x^*)|=|2-r|<1, i.e. 1<r<3. At r=3, |f'|=1 and the fixed point loses stability — the first period-doubling bifurcation, where a stable 2-cycle is born. Successive doublings occur at r_1=3,\,r_2\approx3.449,\,r_3\approx3.544,\dots, accumulating at r_\infty\approx3.5699.
Feigenbaum universality. The ratios \delta_k=(r_k-r_{k-1})/(r_{k+1}-r_k) converge to \delta=4.66920\ldots for any smooth unimodal map — the route to chaos is universal, independent of the specific equation, which is why a single toy map illuminates real systems.
Lyapunov exponent. \lambda=\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac1N\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\ln|f'(x_n)|. A small initial separation \delta_0 grows as \delta_n\approx\delta_0 e^{\lambda n}; \lambda>0 ⇒ chaos. The prediction horizon scales as \sim\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(1/\delta_0) — logarithmic in precision, hence the futility of buying accuracy.

title: Nonlinear Dynamics & Chaos
text:
Connections

Builds on: why systems surprise us (nonlinearities and delays — chaos is their extreme) and time-series fundamentals (deterministic chaos vs stochastic noise).
Leads to: power laws & criticality (systems poised at the edge) and the edge of chaos (computation between order and randomness).
Bounds: every forecasting claim — the prediction horizon is a property of the system, not the method.

title: Complexity Science
text:
Most of this manual works reductively: isolate a relationship, hold other things constant, estimate a clean effect. Complexity science is the counter-discipline. It studies systems where the interesting behaviour — a market crash, a traffic jam, a flock, a norm cascade, an epidemic — is emergent: it lives in the interactions, not in any component, and it changes over time as the parts adapt. For a social scientist, that is most of the interesting world.
New here? Start with What is complexity science? — the orientation that synthesises the whole track in one reading (and one runnable figure).
This track assembles complexity science from three disciplines, each with a distinct, practical job.
The arc is structure → measurement → dynamics: the mindset tells you to look at the whole, networks let you measure its connective tissue, and complexity theory explains how it moves.

title: Complexity Science
section: Where this fits in the rest of QSS
text:
Where this fits in the rest of QSS
Complexity science is not a detour — it reuses and extends the manual’s machinery:

Networks are graphs you’ve already met: the DAGs of causal inference and the path diagrams of SEM are directed graphs; relational data is a graph in tables.
Dynamics rests on simulation: the Monte Carlo and agent-based methods here are the same simulation mindset, pointed at emergence.
Systems thinking sharpens field judgement: it deepens the tyranny of metrics (Goodhart as a feedback loop) and from evidence to decision.

title: Complexity Science
text:
Sources

Donella Meadows, Thinking in Systems: A Primer — the systems-thinking pillar.
Menczer, Fortunato & Davis, A First Course in Network Science — network algorithms and metrics.
Albert-László Barabási, Network Science — random, small-world, and scale-free network structure.
Melanie Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — the synthesis of emergence, chaos, power laws, and adaptation.
Hiroki Sayama, Introduction to the Modeling and Analysis of Complex Systems — cellular automata and agent-based models.
Miller & Page, Complex Adaptive Systems — heterogeneous adaptive agents and emergent macro behaviour.
Steven Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos — dynamical systems, attractors, and the mathematics of chaos.
Epstein & Axtell, Growing Artificial Societies — the Sugarscape agent-based model.

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text: A single ant is nearly mindless; an ant colony forages with collective intelligence. A neuron is not conscious; a brain is. Emergence is this leap — macroscopic pattern and behaviour arising from many simple parts following local rules, not present in or predictable from any part. Its social-science emblem is Schelling’s segregation model, where a mild individual preference produces stark collective segregation that no one intended — the defining lesson of the whole Dynamics cluster.
