title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
Why it matters
Emergence is why social aggregates can’t be read off individual intentions. Segregation without racists, bank runs without panic, traffic jams without crashes, inequality without villainy — the macro pattern is “disconnected from its origins” (Mitchell 2009). For a quantitative social scientist this is a standing warning: the representative-agent shortcut and the assumption that group outcomes mirror individual preferences can both be exactly wrong.

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
Emergence: “more is different”
Emergence occurs when large numbers of simple entities — each following local rules with no central leader — produce macroscopic patterns not present in, or predictable from, the parts (Mitchell 2009; Miller and Page 2007). The slogan is “more is different”: aggregate behaviour is “disconnected from its origins” (Mitchell 2009). The canonical examples (Mitchell 2009; Miller and Page 2007):

Ant colonies — coherent foraging fronts from simple chemical signals, no commander.
Brains — intelligence and consciousness from interactions of non-conscious neurons.
Markets — stable global prices from the chaotic, heterogeneous efforts of independent traders.
Traffic jams & segregation — collective patterns that appear even when no individual intends them.

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
Self-organisation
Self-organisation is the dynamical process by which a system spontaneously forms structure and order over time without a central controller or blueprint (Mitchell 2009). It is “collective intelligence” arising from local feedback — fireflies flashing in unison, the mutually sustaining structure of an economy (Mitchell 2009). (It is also Meadows’ high leverage point: a system that can build its own structure can adapt to almost anything.)

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
Schelling: mild preferences, stark segregation
The classic social-science demonstration (Miller and Page 2007). Agents of two types sit on a grid and move if the fraction of same-type neighbours falls below a threshold. The shock is how little intolerance it takes: agents content to be a minority — happy with, say, just a third of neighbours like themselves — still unravel an integrated city into stark segregation (Miller and Page 2007). It is the purest example of an “unintended collective outcome”: local preferences no one would call extreme aggregate into a macro pattern no individual sought (Miller and Page 2007).


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
L, empty_frac, thresh = 50, 0.10, 0.35
cells = rng.choice([0, 1, 2], size=(L, L), p=[empty_frac, (1-empty_frac)/2, (1-empty_frac)/2])
grid0 = cells.copy()

def same_frac(g, i, j):
    t = g[i, j]
    if t == 0: return 1.0
    s = o = 0
    for di in (-1, 0, 1):
        for dj in (-1, 0, 1):
            if di == 0 and dj == 0: continue
            v = g[(i+di) % L, (j+dj) % L]
            if v != 0:
                o += 1; s += (v == t)
    return 1.0 if o == 0 else s/o

def seg_index(g):
    vals = [same_frac(g, i, j) for i in range(L) for j in range(L) if g[i, j] != 0]
    return np.mean(vals)

s0 = seg_index(cells)
for _ in range(40):
    moved = False
    empties = list(zip(*np.where(cells == 0)))
    order = rng.permutation(L*L)
    for idx in order:
        i, j = idx // L, idx % L
        if cells[i, j] != 0 and same_frac(cells, i, j) < thresh and empties:
            e = empties.pop(rng.integers(len(empties)))
            cells[e] = cells[i, j]; empties.append((i, j)); cells[i, j] = 0
            moved = True
    if not moved: break
s1 = seg_index(cells)

cmap = plt.matplotlib.colors.ListedColormap(["white", "#1f4e79", "#d98a29"])
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 4.4))
axL.imshow(grid0, cmap=cmap, vmin=0, vmax=2); axL.set_title(f"start: mixed (segregation {s0:.2f})"); axL.axis("off")
axR.imshow(cells, cmap=cmap, vmin=0, vmax=2); axR.set_title(f"end: segregated (segregation {s1:.2f})"); axR.axis("off")
fig.suptitle("Schelling: a 35% same-type preference produces near-total segregation", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Schelling’s segregation model, simulated by hand. Two groups (blue, orange) and empty cells (white) start randomly mixed (left). Each agent is ‘happy’ only if at least 35% of its occupied neighbours are the same type; unhappy agents move to a random empty cell, repeated until everyone is content (right). The result: near-total segregation — even though every agent was willing to live in a neighbourhood that is 65% the other type. The segregation index (mean same-type-neighbour fraction) jumps from ~0.5 (random) to ~0.8+. No agent wanted this; the structure emerged from mild local preferences. That gap — between micromotives and macrobehaviour — is emergence.

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
The lesson for social science
Schelling crystallises the danger emergence poses to naïve inference: the macro pattern is not the sum of the micro intentions. You cannot conclude from observed segregation that individuals are extremely prejudiced, nor design policy as if fixing preferences linearly fixes the outcome — the mapping from micromotives to macrobehaviour is nonlinear and emergent. This is the conceptual root of the ecological fallacy and of why agent-based models exist.

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading intentions off outcomes. Stark segregation does not imply extreme preferences (Schelling); the aggregate can betray the individuals. (Miller and Page 2007)
Assuming the representative agent. Averaging individuals away erases exactly the heterogeneity and interaction that generate the emergent pattern. (Miller and Page 2007)
Expecting macro to track micro linearly. Small changes in local rules/thresholds can flip the global outcome (tipping). (Miller and Page 2007)
“Emergence” as mysticism. It is not magic — it is the concrete, simulable consequence of local rules; if you can’t grow it, treat the claim with suspicion. (Mitchell 2009)

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — emergence (“more is different”, macro disconnected from parts; ants, brains, markets, traffic); self-organisation as spontaneous order without a controller (fireflies, economies).
Miller & Page, Complex Adaptive Systems — the Schelling segregation model as the canonical social emergence: mild same-type preference → stark, unintended segregation; macro ≠ sum of micro intentions.

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Micromotives → macrobehaviour. Let \tau be each agent’s tolerance threshold (minimum same-type fraction). For small \tau a fully mixed configuration is unstable: any local fluctuation that raises one type’s local share makes the other type’s agents unhappy, they leave, raising the share further — a reinforcing loop that drives the neighbourhood to near-purity. The integrated state is a knife-edge; the segregated states are the stable attractors. The model has a tipping structure: above a critical \tau the mixed state can persist; below it, segregation is essentially inevitable — yet the critical \tau is far lower than intuition expects (≈ 1/3, not 1/2).
Why prediction is hard. Emergent outcomes are typically path-dependent (history matters) and computationally irreducible — for many systems there is no shortcut to the macro state except to run the micro dynamics, which is exactly why simulation/ABM is the native method.

title: Emergence & Self-Organisation
text:
Connections

Builds on: systems thinking (feedback loops drive self-organisation) and why systems work (self-organisation as resilience).
Leads to: agent-based modelling (the method that generates emergence) and cellular automata (emergence in its starkest, rule-based form).
Relates to: homophily & community structure (Schelling on a network) and the ecological fallacy / limits of quantification.

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
text: A market has no manager, yet it allocates resources. An immune system has no commander, yet it learns to recognise new pathogens. An ant colony has no architect, yet it builds. These are complex adaptive systems (CAS): large populations of agents that adapt — changing their behaviour in response to feedback from the environment and from each other — with no central control. Out of this local adaptation, coherent and intelligent-looking macro behaviour emerges that no single agent designed or could predict. The system as a whole adapts, even though adaptation lives only in the parts.

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
text:
Why it matters
Most of the systems social scientists actually care about — economies, organisations, cultures, ecosystems — are complex adaptive systems, and they break the assumptions of the tidy models we reach for first. Their agents are heterogeneous, not identical; they learn and change strategy, so yesterday’s regularity dissolves; and the “fitness” of a strategy depends on what everyone else is doing, so the ground keeps moving (Miller and Page 2007). CAS thinking gives you the right default expectations — perpetual novelty, adaptation, emergent order — and warns you off the wrong ones (a fixed equilibrium reached by a representative rational agent).

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
section: What makes a system complex and adaptive
text:
What makes a system complex and adaptive
John Holland’s definition: a CAS is a large network of heterogeneous agents interacting without central control, each adapting its behaviour based on feedback to do better in its environment (Mitchell 2009; Miller and Page 2007). From that local adaptation, emergent macro behaviour arises — global patterns not present in, and not predictable from, any individual part (Miller and Page 2007). Holland highlighted recurring ingredients: aggregation (agents combine into higher-level structures), nonlinearity (the whole is not the sum), flows of resources and information through networks, and diversity (a continuously renewed variety of agent types). The defining move beyond a merely complex system is adaptation: the agents are not fixed automata but learners.

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
section: Fitness landscapes: a map for adaptation
text:
Fitness landscapes: a map for adaptation
The dominant metaphor for adaptation is the fitness landscape (Mitchell 2009). Picture every possible strategy laid out on a plain, with height = fitness (payoff, reproductive success, profit). Adaptation becomes hill-climbing: an agent tweaks its strategy and moves toward higher ground.
The shape of the landscape decides how hard this is:

A smooth landscape has a single peak — easy. Keep climbing and you arrive at the global best.
A rugged landscape — created by nonlinear interactions among the parts of a strategy — has many peaks and valleys (Mitchell 2009). Now hill-climbing is treacherous: you can climb diligently and still get stuck on a local optimum, a hill that is higher than everything nearby but far short of the true summit.

This is the exploration–exploitation trade-off in its purest form: exploit the good peak you’re on, or explore — risk going downhill — in search of a higher one (Mitchell 2009). Every adaptive agent, from a firm choosing a strategy to an algorithm fitting a model, lives on this knife-edge.

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
section: Genetic algorithms: evolution as a search strategy
text:
Genetic algorithms: evolution as a search strategy
Holland turned the metaphor into a method. A genetic algorithm (GA) searches a rugged landscape the way evolution does — with a population, not a lone climber, so many regions are explored at once (Mitchell 2009). The recipe:

Population. Start with a set of random candidate solutions, each encoded as a string (a “genome”).
Selection. Evaluate each candidate’s fitness; pick “parents” with probability rising in fitness (fitness-proportional selection) — the better solutions reproduce more.
Crossover. Pair parents and recombine their genomes — swap segments — so good pieces from different solutions can combine.
Mutation. Randomly flip a few bits, injecting the diversity that lets the search escape local optima.
Repeat over many generations; the population marches uphill.

The figure runs a real genetic algorithm, by hand, on a deliberately rugged landscape.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)

# rugged fitness landscape on [0,1]: one global + two local peaks (+ ripples)
def fitness_x(x):
    return (np.exp(-30 * (x - 0.70) ** 2)          # global peak
            + 0.65 * np.exp(-40 * (x - 0.30) ** 2)  # local peak
            + 0.50 * np.exp(-70 * (x - 0.48) ** 2)  # local peak
            + 0.03 * np.sin(40 * x) ** 2)

L = 16                      # bits per genome
P = 40                      # population size
G = 40                      # generations
pc, pm = 0.8, 0.02          # crossover, per-bit mutation rates
denom = 2 ** L - 1

def decode(pop):            # binary rows -> x in [0,1]
    weights = 2 ** np.arange(L)[::-1]
    return (pop @ weights) / denom

pop = rng.integers(0, 2, size=(P, L))
gen0_x = decode(pop)
best_hist, mean_hist = [], []

for g in range(G):
    fit = fitness_x(decode(pop))
    best_hist.append(fit.max()); mean_hist.append(fit.mean())
    # fitness-proportional (roulette) selection of parents
    probs = fit / fit.sum()
    idx = rng.choice(P, size=(P, 2), p=probs)
    new = np.empty_like(pop)
    for k, (a, b) in enumerate(idx):
        child = pop[a].copy()
        if rng.random() < pc:                       # single-point crossover
            cut = rng.integers(1, L)
            child[cut:] = pop[b, cut:]
        flip = rng.random(L) < pm                   # mutation
        child[flip] ^= 1
        new[k] = child
    # elitism: carry the best solution forward unchanged
    new[0] = pop[fit.argmax()]
    pop = new

genG_x = decode(pop)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.3))

grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 800)
axL.plot(grid, fitness_x(grid), color="#999999", lw=1.5, label="fitness landscape")
axL.scatter(gen0_x, fitness_x(gen0_x) + 0.02, color="#d97706", s=18, zorder=3, label="generation 0")
axL.scatter(genG_x, fitness_x(genG_x) - 0.02, color="#1f4e79", s=18, zorder=3, label="generation 40")
axL.axvline(0.70, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=0.8)
axL.text(0.70, 0.05, " global peak", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axL.set_xlabel("strategy  x"); axL.set_ylabel("fitness")
axL.set_title("A population crossing the valleys")
axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")

axR.plot(best_hist, color="#1f4e79", lw=2, label="best fitness")
axR.plot(mean_hist, color="#d97706", lw=2, label="mean fitness")
axR.set_xlabel("generation"); axR.set_ylabel("fitness")
axR.set_title("Adaptation over generations")
axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower right")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A genetic algorithm climbing a rugged fitness landscape, by hand. LEFT: the landscape (grey curve) has a tall GLOBAL peak near x≈0.70 plus two lower LOCAL peaks — exactly the trap that defeats a lone hill-climber. Generation 0 (orange) is a random population scattered everywhere; after 40 generations of selection + crossover + mutation the population (blue) has concentrated on the global peak. RIGHT: best and mean fitness per generation. The population’s best solution finds the global optimum within a handful of generations and the whole population follows — evolutionary search, not gradient following, is what lets it cross the valleys a hill-climber cannot.

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
section: Co-evolution: the landscape that fights back
text:
Co-evolution: the landscape that fights back
The deepest twist is that in a real CAS, fitness is endogenous — how well your strategy does depends on the strategies of everyone else (Miller and Page 2007). Predators adapt to prey, which adapt right back; firms react to competitors who react in turn. So the landscape is not a fixed terrain you climb but a co-evolving surface that deforms as you and everyone else move — a Red Queen world where you must keep adapting just to hold your position. This is why CAS rarely settle into a static equilibrium and instead show perpetual novelty, arms races, and waves of innovation.

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Assuming a single equilibrium. A CAS with co-evolving, adapting agents typically has no fixed resting point; modelling it as one representative agent at equilibrium throws away its essential behaviour. (Miller and Page 2007)
Forgetting local optima. Pure exploitation (greedy hill-climbing) gets stuck; without exploration — mutation, search, experimentation — a system fossilises on a mediocre peak. (Mitchell 2009)
Designing for control. There is no central controller to program; you shape a CAS through incentives, feedback, and the rules of interaction, not by issuing commands. (Miller and Page 2007)
Reading GAs as optimisers with guarantees. A genetic algorithm is a robust heuristic search of a rugged space, not a method that provably returns the global optimum. (Mitchell 2009)

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — Holland’s CAS; the fitness-landscape metaphor, hill-climbing, rugged vs smooth, local optima, and the exploration–exploitation trade-off; genetic algorithms (selection, crossover, mutation) as evolutionary search.
Miller & Page, Complex Adaptive Systems — the agent-based, no-central-control definition; emergent macro behaviour from local adaptation; heterogeneity, flows, and diversity; and co-evolution with endogenous, shifting fitness.

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Hill-climbing and gradients. On a smooth landscape f, local adaptation is gradient ascent x_{t+1}=x_t+\eta\,\nabla f(x_t), which converges to a local maximum (where \nabla f=0 and the Hessian is negative-definite). On a rugged landscape there are many such points, and which one you reach depends entirely on the start — hence the global-vs-local problem.
Why a population helps. A single climber samples one basin of attraction. A population of P climbers samples up to P basins in parallel; crossover then recombines partial solutions across basins — a move no local gradient step can make. This is the intuition behind Holland’s schema theorem: short, high-fitness building blocks (schemas) receive exponentially increasing trials over generations, so a GA implicitly searches many strategy-combinations at once (“implicit parallelism”).
NK landscapes. Kauffman’s NK model tunes ruggedness with one knob: N genes, each contributing to fitness depending on K others. K=0 gives a single smooth peak; raising K increases the epistatic interactions and the number of local optima, interpolating from Mount-Fuji-smooth to fully rugged. It is the standard formalisation of “how hard is this adaptive search.”

title: Complex Adaptive Systems
text:
Connections

Builds on: agent-based modelling (the computational tool for simulating adaptive agents) and emergence & self-organisation (the macro order CAS produce).
Relates to: systems thinking (CAS are the formal cousin of Meadows’ adaptive, self-organising systems) and the exploration–exploitation trade-off that recurs across learning.
Leads to: cellular automata — the simplest systems in which simple local rules generate open-ended complexity.

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text: Ask “who is the most important node?” and the network gives different answers depending on what you mean. Degree rewards popularity; betweenness rewards brokerage; closeness rewards reach; eigenvector and PageRank reward prestige. A low-degree person who is the only bridge between two communities is nearly invisible to one measure and dominant in another — and knowing which to use is the whole skill.
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
Why it matters
“Who should we vaccinate / target / monitor / promote?” is a centrality question, and choosing the wrong measure points you at the wrong node. The broker who controls information flow, the hub everyone follows, and the node that reaches everyone fastest are different people — centrality makes the distinction precise.
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
Degree centrality — popularity
The simplest measure: the number of connections, normalised by the maximum possible (Menczer et al. 2020):

c_D(i) = \frac{k_i}{N-1}, \qquad k_i = \sum_j A_{ij}.

It captures popularity, activity, exposure — the hubs of a social network, the major airports of a flight network (Menczer et al. 2020). Local and cheap, but blind to where in the structure those edges sit.
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
Betweenness centrality — brokerage
Betweenness measures how often a node sits on the shortest paths between other pairs (Menczer et al. 2020):

b_i = \sum_{h\ne j\ne i} \frac{\sigma_{hj}(i)}{\sigma_{hj}},

where \sigma_{hj} is the number of shortest paths from h to j and \sigma_{hj}(i) how many pass through i. It quantifies brokerage, gatekeeping, control of flow — the bridges between communities (Menczer et al. 2020). Its signature case: a low-degree individual who is the only link between two social circles (spanning a structural hole) has very high betweenness, because all communication between the groups must pass through them (Menczer et al. 2020). It is computed from BFS all-pairs shortest paths (Menczer et al. 2020).
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
Closeness centrality — reach
Closeness is the inverse of the total distance to everyone else (Menczer et al. 2020):

g_i = \frac{1}{\sum_{j\ne i}\ell_{ij}}.

High closeness means a node can reach the whole network in few hops — the most efficient seed for spreading a rumour or diffusing information fast (Menczer et al. 2020).
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
Eigenvector centrality — prestige by association
Degree counts neighbours; eigenvector centrality weights them by their importance — “a node is important if its neighbours are important” (Menczer et al. 2020). An endorsement from a high-status node counts more than one from the periphery; an academic is influential for being cited by the highly cited (Menczer et al. 2020). Formally it is the leading eigenvector of the adjacency matrix:

A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v} \quad(\lambda = \text{largest eigenvalue}),

and c_E(i) = v_i (Menczer et al. 2020).
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
PageRank — the directed, random-walk variant
PageRank (Google’s founders) adapts eigenvector centrality to directed networks via a random walker: importance is the long-run probability the walker is at a node (Barabási 2016). The fix that makes it work is a damping factor \alpha (≈0.85): with probability \alpha the walker follows an out-link, and with probability 1-\alpha it teleports to a random node — which handles dangling nodes (no out-links) that would otherwise trap the walker and drain importance (Barabási 2016). The transition operator is T = \alpha A D^{-1} + \tfrac{1-\alpha}{n}J, and PageRank is its stationary distribution (Barabási 2016). A page can have high degree but low PageRank if its links come only from unimportant pages (Barabási 2016).
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
section: When centralities disagree — and how to choose
text:
When centralities disagree — and how to choose


Show the figure code (betweenness via Brandes; eigenvector via power iteration — by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import deque

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
A = np.zeros((15, 15), int)
def connect(nodes, p):
    for a in range(len(nodes)):
        for b in range(a+1, len(nodes)):
            if rng.random() < p: A[nodes[a], nodes[b]] = A[nodes[b], nodes[a]] = 1
cluA, cluB, bridge = list(range(0, 7)), list(range(7, 14)), 14
connect(cluA, 0.6); connect(cluB, 0.6)
for u in (cluA[0], cluA[1]): A[u, cluA[(cluA.index(u)+1)]] = A[cluA[(cluA.index(u)+1)], u] = 1  # ensure connected
A[bridge, 3] = A[3, bridge] = 1; A[bridge, 10] = A[10, bridge] = 1   # the ONLY A-B link
N = 15; adj = [np.where(A[i])[0] for i in range(N)]

# degree
deg = A.sum(1).astype(float)

# betweenness via Brandes (unweighted)
btw = np.zeros(N)
for s in range(N):
    S = []; P = [[] for _ in range(N)]; sigma = np.zeros(N); sigma[s] = 1
    d = np.full(N, -1); d[s] = 0; Q = deque([s])
    while Q:
        v = Q.popleft(); S.append(v)
        for w in adj[v]:
            if d[w] < 0: d[w] = d[v]+1; Q.append(w)
            if d[w] == d[v]+1: sigma[w] += sigma[v]; P[w].append(v)
    delta = np.zeros(N)
    while S:
        w = S.pop()
        for v in P[w]: delta[v] += (sigma[v]/sigma[w])*(1+delta[w])
        if w != s: btw[w] += delta[w]
btw /= 2

# eigenvector centrality via power iteration
v = np.ones(N)
for _ in range(200):
    v = A @ v; v /= np.linalg.norm(v)
eig = v

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10.2, 3.8))
ang = {}; posA = np.c_[0.2+0.12*np.cos(np.linspace(0,2*np.pi,7,endpoint=False)),
                       0.5+0.22*np.sin(np.linspace(0,2*np.pi,7,endpoint=False))]
posB = np.c_[0.8+0.12*np.cos(np.linspace(0,2*np.pi,7,endpoint=False)),
             0.5+0.22*np.sin(np.linspace(0,2*np.pi,7,endpoint=False))]
pos = np.vstack([posA, posB, [[0.5, 0.5]]])
for ax, score, name in zip(axes, [deg, btw, eig], ["degree", "betweenness", "eigenvector"]):
    for i in range(N):
        for j in adj[i]:
            if j > i: ax.plot(*zip(pos[i], pos[j]), color="#dadee2", lw=0.7, zorder=1)
    s = 60 + 1400*(score/score.max())
    ax.scatter(pos[:,0], pos[:,1], s=s, color="#1f4e79", zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(*pos[bridge], s=60+1400*(score[bridge]/score.max()), facecolor="#1f4e79",
               edgecolor="#c0392b", linewidth=2.5, zorder=3)
    ax.set_title(name); ax.axis("off"); ax.set_xlim(0,1); ax.set_ylim(0.15,0.85)
fig.suptitle("Same network, three notions of 'important' — watch the bridge node (red outline)", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The same network scored by three centralities (node size ∝ score), computed by hand. Two dense communities are joined by a single BRIDGE node (red outline). Degree (left): the bridge is tiny — it has only two connections. Betweenness (middle): the bridge is the LARGEST node — every shortest path between the communities runs through it (Brandes’ algorithm). Eigenvector (right): the bridge shrinks again and the well-connected core of each community lights up — prestige by association. Same node, three verdicts. Choosing the centrality is choosing the definition of ‘important.’





The measures disagree because they capture different things (Barabási 2016). The selection rule (Menczer et al. 2020; Barabási 2016):








Use…
…to find
Example




Degree
local popularity / activity
most-followed account


Betweenness
chokepoints / flow controllers
the broker between cliques


Closeness
fast reach / accessibility
best seed for diffusion


Eigenvector / PageRank
global prestige / ranking
most-cited-by-the-cited; web ranking
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Defaulting to degree. It misses brokers and prestige; the most structurally important node often isn’t the most connected. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Betweenness on disconnected graphs. Undefined across components and dominated by a few bridges; compute within the giant component and interpret carefully. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Eigenvector centrality on directed/disconnected graphs. It can concentrate on one component or fail to converge — use PageRank (with damping) for directed networks. (Barabási 2016)
Forgetting normalisation. Raw betweenness scales with N^2; normalise before comparing networks of different size. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Equating high degree with influence. A hub linked only by unimportant nodes has low PageRank — quantity isn’t quality. (Barabási 2016)
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Menczer, Fortunato & Davis, A First Course in Network Science — degree c_D=k_i/(N-1); betweenness b_i=\sum \sigma_{hj}(i)/\sigma_{hj} (brokerage / structural holes, via BFS); closeness g_i=1/\sum_j\ell_{ij}; eigenvector centrality as the leading eigenvector of A; and that the measures disagree, with a selection rule by research question.
Barabási, Network Science — PageRank as the damped random-walk variant for directed graphs (T=\alpha AD^{-1}+\tfrac{1-\alpha}{n}J), handling dangling nodes, and high-degree-but-low-PageRank.
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Brandes’ algorithm (the code). Naïvely, betweenness needs all shortest paths through each node — O(N^3). Brandes computes it in O(NL) by, for each source s: a BFS that records the shortest-path counts \sigma_s(v) and predecessors, then a reverse-BFS accumulation of the dependency \delta_s(v)=\sum_{w:\,v\in\text{pred}(w)} \tfrac{\sigma_s(v)}{\sigma_s(w)}(1+\delta_s(w)). Summing \delta_s(v) over all sources s gives b_v (halved for undirected graphs) — exactly the loop in the figure.
Eigenvector centrality and power iteration. A\mathbf v=\lambda\mathbf v for the largest \lambda; by Perron–Frobenius, for a connected graph the leading eigenvector is positive, so v_i\ge0 are valid scores. Power iteration — repeatedly \mathbf v\leftarrow A\mathbf v/\|A\mathbf v\| — converges to it because the dominant eigendirection grows fastest. PageRank is the same idea on the stochastic operator T, whose stationary distribution (leading eigenvector, \lambda=1) is guaranteed unique by the teleportation term.
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network
text:
Connections

Builds on: network structure & metrics (betweenness/closeness use BFS distances) and graphs & the adjacency matrix (eigenvector/PageRank are spectral).
Leads to: community detection (centrality finds nodes; communities find groups) and spreading on networks (central nodes are the superspreaders).
Echoes: the leading eigenvector (PCA/factor analysis) — the same spectral idea in a different guise.
crumbs: Centrality: Who Matters in a Network

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text: Emergence says macro patterns arise from micro rules. Agent-based modelling (ABM) is the method that produces them: simulate a population of autonomous, heterogeneous agents following simple behavioural rules and interacting locally, then watch what collective structure emerges. It is “generative” social science — Epstein’s dictum, “if you can’t grow it, you don’t understand it” — and it reaches the heterogeneity, adaptation, and out-of-equilibrium dynamics that equation-based models can’t.

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text:
Why it matters
ABM is a third mode of doing science alongside induction (find patterns in data) and deduction (prove theorems): you grow a phenomenon and study the conditions that generate it. For social science — full of heterogeneous, adapting, interacting people who are rarely in equilibrium — it reaches questions that regression and closed-form game theory cannot, and it makes the otherwise-vague claim “this macro pattern could arise from these micro rules” into a runnable demonstration.

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text:
What ABM is
A bottom-up computational method: simulate many autonomous, heterogeneous agents interacting locally with each other and an environment, and observe the macro patterns that arise (Epstein and Axtell 1996; Miller and Page 2007). Epstein & Axtell call it generative social science: the test of an explanation is whether you can grow the phenomenon from agent rules — “if you can’t grow it, you don’t understand it” (Epstein and Axtell 1996).

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text:
Sugarscape: growing a society
The foundational example is Sugarscape (Epstein and Axtell 1996). Agents live on a landscape of renewable “sugar” and follow one simple rule: look within your vision, move to the cell with the most sugar, and eat (Epstein and Axtell 1996). From that almost trivial rule, a “vast panoply” of macro phenomena emerges: highly skewed wealth inequality (Pareto distributions), seasonal migration, the formation of cultural “tribes,” and even trade and epidemics (Epstein and Axtell 1996) — none of it written into the agents, all of it grown.


Show the figure code (Sugarscape by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
G = 40
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.arange(G), np.arange(G))
def peak(cx, cy, s): return 4*np.exp(-((xx-cx)**2+(yy-cy)**2)/(2*s**2))
cap = np.maximum(peak(11, 11, 6), peak(28, 28, 6))      # two sugar mountains
sugar = cap.copy()

n = 220
pos = rng.integers(0, G, (n, 2))
vision = rng.integers(1, 7, n)
metab = rng.integers(1, 4, n)
wealth = rng.integers(5, 25, n).astype(float)

for step in range(80):
    sugar = np.minimum(sugar + 1.0, cap)                # regrow
    for a in rng.permutation(n):
        i, j = pos[a]; bi, bj, bs = i, j, sugar[i, j]
        for di, dj in ((-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)):
            for d in range(1, vision[a]+1):
                ni, nj = (i+di*d) % G, (j+dj*d) % G
                if sugar[ni, nj] > bs: bs, bi, bj = sugar[ni, nj], ni, nj
        pos[a] = (bi, bj)
        wealth[a] += sugar[bi, bj] - metab[a]; sugar[bi, bj] = 0.0
        wealth[a] = max(wealth[a], 0.0)

def gini(w):
    w = np.sort(w); k = np.arange(1, len(w)+1)
    return (2*np.sum(k*w)/(len(w)*np.sum(w))) - (len(w)+1)/len(w)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.2))
axL.imshow(sugar, cmap="YlOrBr", origin="lower")
axL.scatter(pos[:,1], pos[:,0], s=10, color="#c0392b")
axL.set_title("the Sugarscape (agents cluster at the peaks)"); axL.axis("off")
axR.hist(wealth, bins=30, color="#1f4e79", alpha=0.85)
axR.set_title(f"emergent wealth distribution  (Gini = {gini(wealth):.2f})")
axR.set_xlabel("wealth"); axR.set_ylabel("# of agents")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A Sugarscape, simulated by hand. Left: a landscape with two sugar-rich peaks (yellow) and 220 agents (red) who each step move toward the most sugar within their vision and eat it, paying a metabolic cost — agents differ in vision and metabolism. They migrate to and cluster around the peaks. Right: the emergent wealth distribution after the run — highly skewed (a few rich, a long tail of poor), with a high Gini coefficient. No inequality was programmed in; identical rules applied to agents with slightly different endowments and a little luck of position generate a Pareto-like distribution. That is generative social science: grow the macro fact from the micro rule.

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text:
Why ABM complements equation-based models
Traditional neoclassical / equation-based models lean on a representative agent (homogeneity) and assume equilibrium. ABM relaxes both (Miller and Page 2007; Epstein and Axtell 1996):

Heterogeneity. Agents can differ in traits, endowments, vision, metabolism — diversity that often makes mathematical models intractable but is trivial in a simulation (Epstein and Axtell 1996).
Adaptation. Agents can learn and change their behaviour from feedback, so you can study how systems evolve (Miller and Page 2007).
Out-of-equilibrium dynamics. Where closed-form tools “purify” a model down to static rest points, ABM follows the transient paths and perpetual motion that real social life usually exhibits (Epstein and Axtell 1996).

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Confusing ‘can grow it’ with ‘is true’. Generating a pattern shows it’s sufficient, not that it’s the real mechanism — many rule-sets can grow the same macro fact. (Epstein and Axtell 1996)
Parameter overload. ABMs have many knobs; without disciplined calibration and sensitivity analysis they can be made to show almost anything. (Miller and Page 2007)
Skipping validation. Verification (the code does what you meant) is not validation (it matches the world); do both. (Miller and Page 2007)
Over-reading a single run. Stochastic ABMs need many runs and distributions of outcomes, not one pretty picture. (Miller and Page 2007)
Mistaking richness for rigour. A baroque model is harder to understand, not more credible; prefer the simplest model that grows the phenomenon. (Miller and Page 2007)

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Epstein & Axtell, Growing Artificial Societies — ABM as generative social science (“if you can’t grow it, you don’t understand it”); Sugarscape (the simple harvest rule generating emergent wealth inequality / Pareto distributions, migration, tribes, trade, epidemics); heterogeneity and out-of-equilibrium dynamics.
Miller & Page, Complex Adaptive Systems — ABM as a bottom-up method; agent heterogeneity and adaptation; the contrast with representative-agent, equilibrium models; verification vs validation.
Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — agent models as the route to studying emergence computationally.

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The Sugarscape movement rule. Each agent with vision v inspects the cells along the four axes up to distance v, moves to the unoccupied cell of maximum sugar (nearest in case of ties), harvests it, and pays metabolism m: w \leftarrow w + s_{\text{cell}} - m. Death occurs if w<0 (here we clamp at 0). The landscape regrows toward capacity each step. The code implements exactly this.
Why inequality emerges. With identical rules but heterogeneous vision and metabolism, agents who start near a peak or see farther accumulate faster; the renewable-but-finite sugar means their gains are others’ losses, and cumulative advantage (preferential attachment’s cousin) amplifies small initial differences into a heavy-tailed wealth distribution. Inequality is emergent, not assumed — a structural fact about the interaction, not a statement about the agents’ fairness.
Gini. The figure reports G = \frac{2\sum_k k\,w_{(k)}}{n\sum_k w_{(k)}} - \frac{n+1}{n} on the sorted wealths w_{(k)} — 0 is perfect equality, 1 is total concentration.

title: Agent-Based Modelling
text:
Connections

Builds on: emergence & self-organisation (the patterns ABM grows) and Monte Carlo simulation (its computational engine).
Generates: power-law / Pareto distributions (Sugarscape wealth) and runs naturally on networks (agents interacting along edges).
Complements: randomized experiments and regression — a generative third method beside data-induction and math-deduction.

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text: The Overture’s ethics covered protecting subjects — consent, the IRB, do-no-harm. This entry covers protecting the science and the public: don’t fabricate or falsify, disclose who funded you, claim only the authorship you earned — and, now that data is scraped from billions of people and models are deployed on millions, honour privacy and check for the biases your model will otherwise amplify. Integrity is not a compliance form; it is the precondition for the whole enterprise being worth anything.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Why it matters
Every technical safeguard in this Part — preregistration, honest reporting — assumes a researcher acting in good faith. This entry is about the good faith itself, and its institutional pressures. (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016) It matters because the failures here are not subtle statistical slips but betrayals of trust — faked data that kills clinical trials, hidden funding that warps public health, a deployed model that quietly discriminates. The quantitative researcher wields real power; integrity is what makes that power legitimate.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Scientific misconduct: FFP
The gravest breaches are FFP — Fabrication (making up data), Falsification (manipulating data or materials), and Plagiarism (appropriating others’ work). (Reinhart 2015) These are not honest QRPs; they are fraud.






NoteThe Anil Potti case



A Duke researcher whose cancer-genomics work promised personalised treatment — until it emerged that Potti had falsified both his data and his résumé. (Reinhart 2015) The fallout: papers retracted, clinical trials already under way cancelled, and a company built on the technology closed. Uncovering it took biostatisticians Baggerly and Coombes an estimated 2,000 hours of forensic reconstruction — possible only because they fought for the data and code. (Reinhart 2015) The case is why open science is an integrity tool, not just a convenience: fabrication hides where no one can re-run the analysis.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
section: Conflicts of interest and funding bias
text:
Conflicts of interest and funding bias
Money bends findings — sometimes subtly, sometimes by contract — so disclosure of funding and conflicts is a baseline duty. (Bryman 2016) The corpus’s cases: (Bryman 2016; Reinhart 2015)

Food industry. Agreements between researchers and Coca-Cola were found to let the company review and comment on research before publication and included early-termination clauses that could kill a study producing unfavourable results — pre-loading the published record toward favourable findings. (Bryman 2016)
Pharma. The Reboxetine case: published trials suggested Pfizer’s antidepressant worked, while three times as much unpublished data showed it didn’t — selective publication as a tool of funding bias. (Reinhart 2015)
Tobacco (and a sobering aside that statistics itself grew up entangled with the eugenics movement) — a long history of industry shaping which questions get asked and which answers get out. (Reinhart 2015)

The figure shows the aggregate footprint: when a funder controls design, review, and what gets published, the literature tilts even if no single study is fraudulent.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
n = 600
# True effect is mildly unfavourable to the funder (centered slightly negative).
truth = -0.1
independent = rng.normal(truth, 1.0, n)
# Industry: favourable-design shift + selective publication (drop the most unfavourable).
industry_raw = rng.normal(truth + 0.6, 1.0, n)
industry = industry_raw[industry_raw > np.quantile(industry_raw, 0.35)]  # file-drawer the bad ones

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
bins = np.linspace(-4, 4, 40)
ax.hist(independent, bins=bins, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.7, density=True, label="independently funded")
ax.hist(industry, bins=bins, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.55, density=True, label="industry funded (published)")
ax.axvline(truth, color="#1f8a4c", ls="--", lw=1.8)
ax.text(truth - 0.1, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.92, "truth", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=9, ha="right")
ax.axvline(np.mean(industry), color="#c0392b", ls=":", lw=1.4)
ax.set_xlabel("finding:  unfavourable  ←→  favourable to funder")
ax.set_ylabel("density"); ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_title("Who pays can tilt what gets published")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Funding bias in the published record. Each study’s finding lands on a scale from ‘unfavourable’ to ‘favourable’ to the funder’s interest. Independent studies (grey) scatter around the truth. But when an industry sponsor can shape the design, review results pre-publication, and halt unfavourable trials (red), the published distribution shifts toward favourable — not necessarily through fraud in any one study, but through selection at every stage. The reader sees only the published tilt.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Perverse incentives and authorship
Misconduct doesn’t happen in a vacuum — it grows in an incentive structure. The “frenzied quest” for publications, where tenure, grants, and prestige hang on a long list of top-journal papers, creates relentless pressure to cut corners or torture the data into significance. (Reinhart 2015) The same novelty premium that starves replication rewards the splashy and the fast.
Authorship is where credit and responsibility meet, and it has its own ethics: (Bryman 2016)

Authorship should reflect a substantial contribution. A statistician who shapes the analysis is a research partner deserving co-authorship, not unpaid technical support.
Honorary authorship (a senior name added for clout, having done little) and ghost authorship (the real authors omitted — common in industry-funded trials, to hide the sponsor’s hand) are both violations. (Bryman 2016)
The fear of being scooped pushes researchers to hoard data until they’ve wrung every paper from it — in tension with the openness science needs.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
section: Data ethics in the age of harvested data
text:
Data ethics in the age of harvested data
Classic research ethics assumed you collected the data from consenting subjects. Big data breaks that assumption: much research now uses harvested data — social media, administrative records — generated with no research in mind. (Bryman 2016) New duties follow: (Bryman 2016)

The right to erasure. If someone deletes a post, immortalising it in a paper bypasses their right to withdraw and can expose them to real harm. Harvested ≠ free to use forever.
Consent friction. Users technically “agree” via Terms of Service they never read and don’t understand — a thin substitute for informed consent.
Anonymisation and re-identification. Truly anonymising data is hard and gets harder as outside datasets grow (re-identification risk rises with time); proper scrubbing causes real delays — up to 20 months for some Facebook data. (Bryman 2016) “It’s public” is not an ethics argument.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Algorithmic fairness before deployment
The book’s causal and predictive ML tools don’t sit in a notebook — they get deployed, and a model is only as fair as the data that trained it. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Models learn the historical biases in their training data — the documented gender and racial bias in word embeddings is the textbook case — and then amplify them at scale. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Acting on a biased model in a clinical or policy setting produces systemic injustice, efficiently. The obligation is concrete: fairness checks before deployment — verify the model performs (and estimates effects) accurately across subgroups, not just on average. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) A model that is right on average and wrong for a protected group is not “mostly right”; it is discriminatory. This is the tyranny of metrics with the stakes of real lives — and the reason interpretability and fairness auditing are ethical, not optional.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating QRPs and fraud as a spectrum you can drift along. Honest flexibility and FFP differ in kind; never falsify, full stop. (Reinhart 2015)
Non-disclosure of funding/conflicts. Even good-faith work needs the funding source on the table; contracts that gate publication are a red flag. (Bryman 2016)
Honorary or ghost authorship. Credit must track contribution; hidden authors hide hidden interests. (Bryman 2016)
“It’s public data, so consent isn’t needed.” Harvested data carries privacy duties — the right to erasure, re-identification risk, and meaningful (not ToS) consent. (Bryman 2016)
Deploying a model without a fairness audit. Average accuracy can mask severe subgroup harm; check performance across protected groups before go-live. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — FFP and the Anil Potti misconduct case (falsified data/résumé; retractions; cancelled trials; Baggerly–Coombes ~2,000 hours); funding bias and selective publication (Reboxetine); the publish-or-perish incentive driving corner-cutting.
Bryman, Social Research Methods — conflicts of interest / funding bias (Coca-Cola review/termination clauses); authorship ethics (substantial contribution; statisticians as partners; honorary and ghost authorship); big-data ethics — harvested data, the right to erasure, ToS-consent friction, anonymisation/re-identification (Facebook ~20 months).
Chernozhukov / Jacobucci — algorithmic fairness: ML encoding/amplifying gender and racial bias (word embeddings), the harm of deploying biased models, and fairness checks across subgroups before deployment.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Appendix · two formal notes

Funding bias as selection. Let a study’s finding be \theta=\theta_0+\varepsilon around a truth \theta_0. A sponsor’s influence acts as (i) a design shift \theta_0\to\theta_0+\delta (favourable framing) and (ii) selective publication \Pr(\text{publish})=\pi(\theta) increasing in favourability. The published mean is \mathbb E[\theta\mid\text{published}] = \theta_0+\delta+\operatorname{Cov}(\theta,\pi)/\mathbb E[\pi] > \theta_0 — biased upward even if every individual study is internally valid. The distortion lives in the selection, which is why registered reports (publication independent of result) are the structural fix.
Fairness across subgroups. A model can satisfy small overall error while badly failing a group g: \mathbb E[L]=\sum_g \Pr(g)\,\mathbb E[L\mid g] is dominated by large groups, so a small \Pr(g) lets \mathbb E[L\mid g] be large undetected. Fairness auditing reports the per-group losses (or per-group treatment-effect accuracy) rather than the aggregate — the same lesson as the rare-class accuracy paradox, now an ethical mandate.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Connections

Builds on: ethics & human subjects (consent/IRB, here extended past it), questionable research practices (the honest-vs-fraud line; funding-driven selective publication), open science (transparency as an integrity tool), and causal ML (fairness in deployment).
Leads to: from evidence to decision (whose biases and incentives shape how numbers are used) and the limits of quantification.
Underwrites: the trust that makes every other entry’s rigour worth having.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text: Questionable research practices flourish in the dark — in analytic choices no one sees and null results no one publishes. Open science is the architecture that turns the lights on: preregister the plan before the data arrive, share the data and code so anyone can re-run them, and replicate to see if the effect was ever real. None of it requires trusting the researcher’s virtue; it changes the system so honesty is the path of least resistance.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Why it matters
Every fix in the last entry was something an individual must remember to do. Open science makes integrity structural — baked into how studies are registered, reviewed, and published — so it survives ordinary human incentives. (Reinhart 2015) It is the infrastructure of trust, and the reason this very book is built as a reproducible document. It does not make weak studies strong, but it makes all studies checkable — the precondition for science to self-correct.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Two kinds of “it holds up”
The vocabulary matters because the two are routinely confused: (Reinhart 2015)

Reproducibility (computational). An independent analyst, given your same data and same code, gets the same numbers. This is a floor, not a ceiling — and shockingly often unmet. If code and data were posted, a colleague could simply read the code instead of spending thousands of hours reconstructing your steps. (Reinhart 2015)
Replicability (empirical). A new study, with new data and subjects but the same design, finds the same effect. Replication is called the heart of science — yet is rarely done, because it is laborious and brings little professional reward. (Reinhart 2015)

A finding can be perfectly reproducible (the code runs) and utterly un-replicable (the effect was noise). You need both.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
section: Preregistration: closing the garden of forking paths
text:
Preregistration: closing the garden of forking paths
The most direct antidote to researcher degrees of freedom: preregistration — committing to the hypothesis and the full analysis plan before seeing the data. (Reinhart 2015) It works by drawing a bright line: (Reinhart 2015)

Confirmatory research tests a priori hypotheses with the pre-specified analysis — these p-values mean what they claim.
Exploratory research searches the data for patterns — legitimate and valuable, but its findings are hypotheses to test later, not confirmed results.

By fixing the outliers, covariates, and outcome in advance, preregistration closes the garden of forking paths: you can no longer (even unconsciously) pick the analysis most flattering to your hypothesis. (Reinhart 2015) It doesn’t forbid exploration — it forbids disguising exploration as confirmation.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Registered reports: defeating publication bias
Preregistration fixes the analysis; registered reports fix the publication incentive. (Reinhart 2015) Here the journal peer-reviews the study design before any data are collected and, if the design is sound, grants in-principle acceptance — a promise to publish regardless of whether the results are significant. (Reinhart 2015) This strikes directly at publication bias / the file drawer: when null results are guaranteed a home, the literature stops being a biased sample of only the “eye-catching” findings. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Replication: direct and conceptual
Replication is the system’s error-correction, in two forms: (Reinhart 2015)

Direct replication recreates the original conditions as precisely as possible — did this specific finding hold?
Conceptual replication tests the same idea with different settings, subjects, or measures — does the underlying effect generalise?

A failure to replicate suggests the original was a fluke (noise — the winner’s curse made visible) or depended on unmeasured context. (Reinhart 2015) The figure shows the pattern the Reproducibility Project found across psychology: replication effects are systematically smaller than the originals, and many vanish — exactly what truth inflation predicts.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(12)
k = 100
# True effects are mostly small; originals are significance-selected (inflated upward).
true = np.abs(rng.normal(0.2, 0.15, k))
sig_thresh = 0.25
orig = true + np.abs(rng.normal(0.25, 0.12, k))   # winner's curse: originals overshoot
repl = true + rng.normal(0, 0.10, k)              # replications scatter around the truth

failed = repl < sig_thresh
frac_fail = failed.mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.4))
lim = 0, max(orig.max(), repl.max()) * 1.05
ax.axhspan(lim[0], sig_thresh, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.07)
ax.plot(lim, lim, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.4, label="equal effect (45°)")
ax.scatter(orig[~failed], repl[~failed], s=22, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.7, label="replicated")
ax.scatter(orig[failed], repl[failed], s=22, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.7,
           label=f"failed to replicate ({frac_fail*100:.0f}%)")
ax.axhline(sig_thresh, color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, ls=":")
ax.text(lim[1]*0.6, sig_thresh*0.4, "replication not significant", color="#b03a2e", fontsize=8)
ax.set_xlabel("original published effect size")
ax.set_ylabel("replication effect size")
ax.set_title("Replications shrink toward the truth")
ax.set_xlim(lim); ax.set_ylim(lim)
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Original vs replication effect sizes (Reproducibility-Project style). Each point is a study: its originally published effect (x) against the effect found when independently repeated (y). Almost all points fall below the 45° line — replications are smaller — and many land below the significance threshold (shaded), i.e. fail to replicate. This is truth inflation at the scale of a literature: published effects are systematically exaggerated, and replication is what reveals it.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Data, code, and reproducible workflows
The practical machinery of openness: (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016)

Open data, code, and materials — post everything, so verification is reading the code, not archaeology. A study by Wicherts found that, despite a requirement to share, nearly three- quarters of authors refused when asked — and refusers were more likely to have made statistical errors. (Reinhart 2015) Reluctance to share is itself a signal.
Literate programming. Tools like Sweave and Quarto embed the analysis code inside the document, so every table and figure is regenerated from raw data — no copy-paste. (This encyclopedia is built that way: each figure is executable code, not a pasted image.) (Reinhart 2015)
Version control. Recording every change to the analysis means you can always reconstruct how a specific number was produced. (Reinhart 2015)
Permanent repositories — GenBank, Dryad, Figshare — give data and code citable, lasting homes. (Reinhart 2015)







WarningWhen the code is the bug



Openness catches errors that peer review never could. Biostatisticians Baggerly and Coombes spent roughly 2,000 hours trying to reproduce a Duke cancer-genomics study (Potti) and uncovered a mess — mislabelled data, duplicated samples, columns shifted by one — that invalidated research already guiding clinical trials. (Reinhart 2015) And Reinhart’s plain warning: manually copying data into spreadsheets is “tremendously error-prone,” and Excel’s regression is often numerically ill-behaved. A result no one can re-run is a result no one has truly checked. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
The incentives, and the limits
Why isn’t all science already open? Because the incentives fight it: (Reinhart 2015)

The novelty premium. Careers reward novel, groundbreaking results in prestige journals; there is little glory in replicating others or cleaning code for public release. (Reinhart 2015)
Fear of being scooped. Researchers worry that sharing hard-won data lets competitors beat them to the next finding. (Reinhart 2015)

And the honest caveat: open science is necessary but not sufficient. Transparency cannot rescue a study that is underpowered or built on a flawed premise — it just lets everyone see that it is. (Reinhart 2015) It makes science checkable; it doesn’t make it correct.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Confusing reproducibility with replicability. Code that runs is not an effect that’s real; you need both. (Reinhart 2015)
Presenting exploratory findings as confirmatory. Without preregistration the distinction blurs and p-values lose their meaning; label which is which. (Reinhart 2015)
“Data available on request.” Wicherts shows requests are routinely refused; deposit data and code in a real repository. (Reinhart 2015)
Spreadsheet pipelines. Copy-paste and Excel formulas are error-prone and unauditable; use scripted, version-controlled, literate workflows. (Reinhart 2015)
Expecting openness to fix bad design. It reveals problems; it doesn’t cure underpowered or ill-conceived studies. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — reproducibility (computational) vs replicability; preregistration (confirmatory vs exploratory; closing the garden of forking paths); registered reports and in-principle acceptance vs publication bias; direct vs conceptual replication; open data/code, literate programming (Sweave/Quarto), version control, repositories; incentive problems (novelty premium, scooping) and “necessary but not sufficient”; the Reproducibility Project, Wicherts (¾ refused to share; refusers more error-prone), and the Duke/Potti genomics debacle (Baggerly & Coombes, ~2,000 hours).
Bryman, Social Research Methods — replication norms and open materials/data sharing as part of credible research practice.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
section: Appendix · why preregistration restores the p-value
text:
Appendix · why preregistration restores the p-value

The forking-paths inflation, formally. Suppose the data are consistent with K analysis paths, each a valid test of level \alpha, and the analyst (consciously or not) reports the most significant. The realised false-positive rate is 
\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \Pr\Big(\min_{k\le K} p_k < \alpha\Big) \approx 1-(1-\alpha)^{K} \gg \alpha,
 the same multiplicity math as explicit multiple comparisons — even when only one test is reported — because the choice of path was data-dependent. Preregistration fixes K=1 in advance, restoring \alpha_{\text{eff}}=\alpha. It is multiplicity control applied to the decisions you’d otherwise make implicitly.
Registered reports and selection. Let publication probability be \pi(\text{sig}). Ordinary publishing has \pi(\text{significant})\gg\pi(\text{null}), so the published effect distribution is the truth conditioned on significance — inflated (the winner’s curse). In-principle acceptance sets \pi independent of the result, so the published distribution is unbiased for the truth. Replication then samples that unbiased process and regresses estimates toward the real effect — the shrinkage in the figure.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Connections

Builds on: questionable research practices (the failures this fixes structurally), the replication crisis, and power (what openness can’t fix).
Leads to: communicating findings (transparency in the write-up) and from evidence to decision (trusting a checkable literature).
Embodied in: this manual itself — every figure is executable, reproducible code under version control.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text: This manual has spent six parts making you formidable with numbers. It ends by insisting that the numbers are not enough. Not everything that counts can be counted, and not everything that can be counted counts. The hardest thing a quantitative researcher learns is where measurement stops — where context, meaning, and tacit experience take over, and where a confident decimal is a fake veil of objectivity. The destination of the whole journey is not the model. It is judgment: rigour and humility, held in the same hand.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Why it matters
Everything before this taught the power of quantification. Muller and Bryman supply the boundary: measurement is the servant of experience, never its master. (Muller 2018; Bryman 2016) This is not a retreat from rigour — it is its completion. The analyst who can’t say where the numbers fail will over-trust them precisely where they mislead. The closing skill is knowing the edge of your instrument.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
What numbers cannot capture
The fundamental limit: not everything important is measurable. What is countable is not always what is worth counting, and an over-reliance on metrics yields “distorted knowledge” that looks solid and is actually deceptive. (Muller 2018) Numbers struggle with the things that often matter most: (Muller 2018)

Intangibles — reputation, trust, morale, motivation, cooperation, the quality of a relationship.
The “feel for the whole.” Judgment needs a talent for synthesis — grasping the particularities of a situation and the way things hang together — which no indicator supplies. (Muller 2018)
Tacit knowledge. Drawing on Michael Oakeshott, Muller separates abstract “recipe knowledge” (conveyable in formulas) from practical knowledge earned through experience, which cannot be written as a general rule. (Muller 2018) The expert clinician, teacher, or detective knows more than they can say — and more than any dataset records.

The adage Muller cites is the whole entry in one line: “Not everything that can be counted counts, and not everything that counts can be counted.” (Muller 2018)


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Circle

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.4))
ax.add_patch(Circle((0.38, 0.5), 0.30, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.20))
ax.add_patch(Circle((0.62, 0.5), 0.30, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.20))
ax.text(0.38, 0.86, "MEASURABLE", ha="center", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
ax.text(0.62, 0.86, "IMPORTANT", ha="center", color="#1f7a44", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")

ax.text(0.22, 0.5, "measurable\nbut trivial\n(metric fixation\nover-weights this)",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8, color="#1f3a5f")
ax.text(0.78, 0.5, "important but\nunmeasurable\n(trust, meaning,\ntacit skill)",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8, color="#1f7a44")
ax.text(0.50, 0.5, "good\nquantitative\nwork", ha="center", va="center",
        fontsize=9, color="#111", fontweight="bold")

ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0.1, 1.0); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("Not everything that counts can be counted")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: What gets measured vs what matters. Good quantitative work lives in the overlap — things both important and measurable. But two regions sit outside it: the measurable-but-trivial (which metric fixation over-weights precisely because it is easy to count) and the important-but-unmeasurable (trust, meaning, tacit skill — which numbers silently drop). The discipline is to keep the whole picture in view, not just the part that fits in a spreadsheet.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
section: Mixed methods: numbers and meaning together
text:
Mixed methods: numbers and meaning together
If numbers miss meaning, the answer is not to abandon them but to complement them. Bryman argues the rigid quantitative/qualitative divide is unhelpful: mixed-methods research gives a fuller account by combining their strengths. (Bryman 2016)

Completeness. Qualitative work fills the gaps numbers leave — the reasons, rationalisations, and arguments behind observed behaviour. (Bryman 2016)
Meaning and process. Where quantitative research gives a static snapshot, qualitative work captures process — how events unfold and how participants interpret their world. (Bryman 2016)
Triangulation. Cross-checking findings across methods (a survey and focus groups) raises the credibility of a conclusion: agreement from independent angles is hard to fake. (Bryman 2016)

The regression coefficient tells you that education raises wages; the interview tells you how a particular person’s schooling changed their life. A mature study often needs both.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
section: The epistemology: how quantifying can distort
text:
The epistemology: how quantifying can distort
Quantification can warp reality through the very act of organising it: (Bryman 2016; Muller 2018)

Abstraction from the particular. Making things comparable through standardisation strips them of context, history, and meaning — the price of a clean variable. (Muller 2018)
Spurious precision. A numerical result wears an appearance of certainty and objectivity it may not have earned, peeling away the caveats and ambiguities that belong with it. (Muller 2018) A reported “3.7%” feels more authoritative than “roughly a thirtieth,” though they may say the same uncertain thing.
Reification. Treating a constructed measure as a real, fixed thing — a static view of social life, as if structures existed independently of the people who make them. (Bryman 2016)
False objectivity. Muller’s sharpest warning: metric enthusiasts seek mechanically universalizable procedures because they distrust judgment — not realising the metrics themselves are products of choices and observational bias. The objectivity is partly a pose. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Muller’s verdict: complement, not substitute
The “tyranny” of metrics is what happens when numbers replace judgment instead of informing it. Muller’s constructive conclusion gives measurement a proper, humble role: (Muller 2018)

Complement judgment — use metrics as internal, diagnostic tools to spot outliers and refine practice, not as high-stakes cudgels.
No silver bullet — there is no substitute for actually knowing your subject and your organisation, an unquantifiable skill earned by experience.
The beginning of wisdom is recognising the limits of the possible: not every problem yields to measurement, and “everything that can be measured can be improved” is a fallacy. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
section: The path of the practitioner: rigour and humility
text:
The path of the practitioner: rigour and humility
So we end where the causal summit pointed: the master walks toward knowledge with, in Angrist and Pischke’s phrase, “confidence as well as humility.” (Angrist and Pischke 2015)

Rigour — deploy the most powerful tools available to reach conclusions that can survive cross-examination. Everything in Parts I–V is in service of this.
Humility — acknowledge the messiness of real research and be transparent about mistakes, rather than hiding behind a fake veil of objectivity. (Reinhart 2015)

The master skill is judgment: knowing when the data gives a clear signal and when it is merely a quagmire of quantification. (Muller 2018) Isaiah Berlin said it with finality: “To demand or preach mechanical precision, even in principle, in a field incapable of it is to be blind and to mislead others.” (Muller 2018) That is the last thing this book has to teach — and the reason the quantitative social scientist must be more than a calculator.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Mistaking the measurable for the important. The easy-to-count crowds out the things that matter; keep the unmeasured in view. (Muller 2018)
Spurious precision. A decimal point is not certainty; carry the caveats the number was built on. (Muller 2018)
Reifying constructs. A standardised measure is a useful abstraction, not the thing itself; don’t forget the people behind the variable. (Bryman 2016)
Treating qualitative work as soft or optional. It captures meaning, process, and context numbers can’t; triangulate. (Bryman 2016)
Hiding behind objectivity. Judgment is unavoidable; name it openly rather than pretending the metric decided. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Muller, The Tyranny of Metrics — measurable ≠ important and “distorted knowledge”; the “feel for the whole” and tacit knowledge (Oakeshott, recipe vs practical); the “not everything that counts” dictum; spurious precision, false objectivity; metrics as complement not substitute, no silver bullet, the “improvement” fallacy; judgment as the master skill; Berlin on mechanical precision.
Bryman, Social Research Methods — mixed methods (completeness, meaning/process, triangulation); the quant/qual divide as unhelpful; reification and the “static view of social life.”
Angrist & Pischke; Reinhart — the practitioner’s stance of rigour and humility (“confidence as well as humility”; conclusions that survive cross-examination; transparency over a “fake veil of objectivity”).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Appendix · why precision can mislead

Spurious precision, quantified. A report of “3.74\%” implies resolution to 0.01, yet if the estimate carries a standard error of, say, 1.5 points, the honest statement is “3.7\%\ \pm\ 3” — the last two digits are noise. Reporting more significant figures than the uncertainty supports manufactures false confidence; the number of meaningful digits is set by the confidence interval, not the software’s default. This is the formal core of “spurious precision”: the display claims information the data don’t contain.
Reification and construct validity. A latent construct (ability, well-being, “school quality”) is operationalised as a measured proxy M. Reification is forgetting the gap M\ne construct — treating M as the thing rather than a noisy, choice-laden indicator of it. The validity of every downstream inference is bounded by how well M stands in for what you actually care about — a question no amount of M-precision can answer.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Connections

Builds on: the tyranny of metrics (its constructive other half), concepts & measurement (validity and the construct/proxy gap), and from evidence to decision (judgment in action).
Closes: the entire manual — from what probability is to the wisdom to know what the numbers can’t say.
Hands off to: the ML Track for those extending the predictive toolkit — carrying this humility with them.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text: The academy stops at the estimate; the world begins there. A coefficient with a confidence interval is an input to a decision, never the decision itself — because acting requires what the number leaves out: how big the effect is, how common the case, what each kind of mistake costs, who bears the risk, and what you should do before the evidence is complete. Evidence informs the choice; it cannot make it. The last competence this book teaches is wearing both hats — analyst and decider — at once.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
Why it matters
This is the subtitle of the whole module: the client who needs an answer by Friday. Evidence-based practice says decisions should rest on the best available research — a genuine advance over hunch and authority. (Bryman 2016) But evidence is partial, contested, and value-laden, and a number never speaks for itself. The skill of translating an estimate into a defensible action — explicit about costs, risks, and uncertainty — is what separates a technician from someone who can be trusted with a question.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
Evidence-based policy, and its limits
The ideal: ground decisions in rigorous research, synthesised through systematic reviews. (Bryman 2016) The limits are just as important to state: (Muller 2018; Bryman 2016)

Evidence is partial — and can deceive. What is measurable is not what is important; leaning only on metrics yields “distorted knowledge” that looks solid. (Muller 2018)
Context-dependence. “What works once may not work again” — David Kilcullen’s lesson from counterinsurgency, where human actors adapt to whatever you do (as students adapt to a tested curriculum). (Muller 2018) External validity is not guaranteed.
The is/ought gap. Evidence describes how the world is; it cannot dictate what a society ought to value. Isaiah Berlin’s point that judgment needs a “talent for synthesis” and a “feel for the whole” names exactly the part no metric supplies. (Muller 2018)

Evidence narrows the argument and disciplines it; it does not end it.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
section: Translating an estimate into a decision
text:
Translating an estimate into a decision
Four lenses turn a “best guess” into an action: (Reinhart 2015; Muller 2018)

Effect size, not just significance. Decision-makers need how big, not is it p<0.05. A trivial effect can be significant in a large sample and still not worth the investment. (Reinhart 2015)
Base rates. A low base rate flips the calculus: screen for a rare condition and even an accurate test yields more false alarms than true cases, inviting harmful over-intervention. (Reinhart 2015)
The asymmetry of errors. A Type I and a Type II error rarely cost the same. An administrator weighing a $900,000 reading curriculum fears a Type I error (paying for something no better than the status quo); a traffic-safety regulator fears a Type II error (missing a real hazard — measured in preventable deaths). (Reinhart 2015) The same p-value warrants different decisions depending on which error you can least afford.
Expected value. Weight each outcome by its probability and its payoff — the logic even of Pascal’s Wager, where a large enough stake dominates a small probability. (Reinhart 2015)

The figure puts these together: the right choice depends not just on how likely the intervention works but on the cost of being wrong — and the break-even probability moves with it.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)            # probability the intervention truly works
benefit = 100                          # value if it works

# Expected net value of adopting = p*benefit - (1-p)*cost_of_wrong_adoption
cost_cheap = 40                        # a cheap mistake
cost_costly = 400                      # a costly/harmful mistake
ev_cheap = p * benefit - (1 - p) * cost_cheap
ev_costly = p * benefit - (1 - p) * cost_costly

be_cheap = cost_cheap / (benefit + cost_cheap)
be_costly = cost_costly / (benefit + cost_costly)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.axhline(0, color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.2)
ax.plot(p, ev_cheap, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4, label="cheap to be wrong")
ax.plot(p, ev_costly, color="#c0392b", lw=2.4, label="costly/harmful to be wrong")
for be, c in [(be_cheap, "#1f3a5f"), (be_costly, "#c0392b")]:
    ax.axvline(be, color=c, ls=":", lw=1.4)
    ax.text(be + 0.01, -120, f"act if p > {be:.2f}", color=c, fontsize=8, rotation=90, va="bottom")
ax.fill_between(p, ev_cheap, 0, where=ev_cheap > 0, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.06)
ax.set_xlabel("probability the intervention truly works")
ax.set_ylabel("expected net value of adopting")
ax.set_title("The same evidence, different decisions — set by the cost of error")
