What matters is AR(2): it should not be significant — a significant AR(2) means the level errors are themselves serially correlated, so your lag-2 instruments are invalid and you need deeper lags (Li 2023).
Sargan / Hansen J test of overidentifying restrictions — null: all instruments are valid (uncorrelated with the error). A rejection flags misspecification or invalid instruments (Li 2023).

title: Dynamic Panels & GMM (Arellano–Bond)
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Running FE with a lagged dependent variable in a short panel. Nickell bias is severe and doesn’t vanish in N — use Arellano–Bond/Blundell–Bond. (Li 2023)
Ignoring the OLS–FE bracket. If your GMM estimate falls outside the (upward) OLS and (downward) FE estimates, something is wrong. (Li 2023)
Instrument proliferation. A near-perfect Hansen p-value (≈1.00) usually means too many instruments, not a great model — collapse them. (Li 2023)
Skipping AR(2) and Hansen. Both are required to defend the instruments; a significant AR(2) invalidates the lag-2 moment conditions. (Li 2023)
Difference GMM on a persistent series. Weak instruments bias it; switch to system GMM. (Li 2023)

title: Dynamic Panels & GMM (Arellano–Bond)
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Dong Li, Panel Data Econometrics (with Wooldridge) — the Nickell / dynamic-panel bias (LDV correlated with the within-transformed error; O(1/T), not vanishing in N); Arellano–Bond difference GMM (first-difference out the FE, instrument the lagged difference with deeper lagged levels); Blundell–Bond system GMM for persistent series; the instrument-proliferation problem and collapsing/limiting instruments; and the diagnostics — AR(1)/AR(2) serial-correlation tests (expect AR(1), fear AR(2)) and the Sargan/Hansen J test of overidentifying restrictions.

title: Dynamic Panels & GMM (Arellano–Bond)
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why FE is biased here. The within estimator regresses \dot y_{it} on \dot y_{i,t-1} where \dot{} denotes deviation from the unit mean. Since \bar y_i (hence \dot y_{i,t-1}) depends on the whole history of u_i, \mathbb{E}[\dot y_{i,t-1}\dot u_{it}] \ne 0 — specifically it is negative, of order -\tfrac{1}{T}\cdot\frac{1+\delta}{\,\cdots}, which is why the FE estimate is biased downward and the bias is O(1/T).
Arellano–Bond moment conditions. With \Delta v_{it}=v_{it}-v_{i,t-1}, the levels y_{i,t-s} (s\ge2) satisfy \mathbb{E}[y_{i,t-s}\,\Delta v_{it}]=0. Stacking these for all valid s and t gives the moment vector \mathbb{E}[Z_i'\Delta v_i]=0; the (two-step, efficient) GMM estimator minimises \big(\tfrac1N\sum Z_i'\Delta v_i\big)' \widehat W \big(\tfrac1N\sum Z_i'\Delta v_i\big) with optimal weight \widehat W=\big(\tfrac1N\sum Z_i'\Delta\hat v_i\Delta\hat v_i'Z_i\big)^{-1}.
The Hansen J. At the GMM optimum, J = N\,\bar g'\widehat W\bar g \sim \chi^2_{(\#\text{moments}-\#\text{params})} under the null that all moments hold; it is the GMM generalisation of the Sargan overidentification test — identical logic to the IV J-test.

title: Dynamic Panels & GMM (Arellano–Bond)
text:
Connections

Builds on: panel & fixed effects (the static estimator this fixes for dynamics) and instrumental variables (GMM is many-instrument IV; the Hansen J is the overidentification test).
Relates to: time-series fundamentals (persistence and unit roots — a \delta near 1 is the dynamic-panel analogue) and staggered DiD (another modern fix to naïve panel estimation).
Cautioned by: weak instruments — the same disease as in IV, here from too-deep or too-many lags.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text: When a policy hits one group and not another, you can’t just compare the two — they differed to begin with. And you can’t just track the treated group over time — everything else moved too. Difference-in- differences does both subtractions at once: the treated group’s change minus the control group’s change. The first difference erases fixed group gaps; the second erases shocks common to all. What survives is the treatment effect — if the groups would have moved in parallel anyway.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Why it matters
RD needs a sharp cutoff; IV needs a clever instrument. DiD needs only a policy change, a comparison group, and data before and after — which is why it is the most widely used quasi-experimental design in economics and policy. (Cunningham 2021) It exploits a different kind of as-good-as-random variation: the timing of a treatment that some units get and others don’t. And it is the design where the field has most recently discovered it was doing things wrong — making this entry both a how-to and a cautionary tale. (Baker et al. 2025)

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
The canonical 2×2
The simplest DiD has two groups — treated (D=1) and control (D=0) — and two periods, before and after. The estimator double-differences four means: (Baker et al. 2025) 
\hat\delta_{DD}=\big(\bar Y_{\text{treat,post}}-\bar Y_{\text{treat,pre}}\big)
-\big(\bar Y_{\text{ctrl,post}}-\bar Y_{\text{ctrl,pre}}\big).
 Read the two subtractions: (Baker et al. 2025)

The first difference (post − pre, within each group) wipes out every time-invariant group trait — the treated group can be richer, sicker, bigger; if it doesn’t change, it cancels.
The second difference (treated change − control change) wipes out any shock common to both groups in that period — a recession, a seasonal swing, a nationwide trend.

Two confounding layers removed by arithmetic, no controls required. What remains is attributed to the treatment.






NoteCard & Krueger and the minimum wage



The study that made DiD famous — and overturned a textbook prediction. In 1992 New Jersey raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05; neighbouring Pennsylvania held at $4.25. Card & Krueger surveyed ~400 fast-food restaurants before and after, measuring full-time-equivalent employment: (Cunningham 2021)




Pre
Post
Change




New Jersey (treated)
20.44
21.03
+0.59


Pennsylvania (control)
23.33
21.17
−2.16



DiD = (+0.59)-(-2.16) = \mathbf{+2.76}. Employment fell in Pennsylvania (a common regional shock) but rose relative to it in New Jersey — the opposite of the competitive prediction that a higher minimum wage destroys jobs. Note the logic: NJ’s raw +0.59 alone would be misleading; only by netting out PA’s -2.16 regional slump does the policy’s relative effect appear. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
The one assumption: parallel trends
DiD’s entire credibility rests on parallel trends: absent the treatment, the treated and control groups would have moved in parallel. (Baker et al. 2025) The control group’s change is used as the treated group’s missing counterfactual change — valid only if their paths would have stayed parallel. Two things to internalise: (Baker et al. 2025)

It is fundamentally untestable. It concerns the treated group’s unobserved no-treatment path after the intervention — a counterfactual we never see.
Pre-trends are the evidence. If the groups tracked each other before treatment, it’s more plausible they’d have continued. So researchers plot the pre-period and check for parallel paths — necessary support, not proof. (Levels may differ wildly; it is the slopes that must match.)



Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = [0, 1]                       # pre, post
nj = [20.44, 21.03]             # treated
pa = [23.33, 21.17]             # control
nj_cf = [20.44, 20.44 + (pa[1] - pa[0])]   # counterfactual: NJ + PA's change
did = (nj[1] - nj[0]) - (pa[1] - pa[0])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.plot(t, nj, "o-", color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4, label="New Jersey (treated)")
ax.plot(t, pa, "o-", color="#9aa0a6", lw=2.4, label="Pennsylvania (control)")
ax.plot(t, nj_cf, "o--", color="#c0392b", lw=2.0, label="NJ counterfactual (parallel)")
ax.annotate("", xy=(1, nj[1]), xytext=(1, nj_cf[1]),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color="#c0392b", lw=2.0))
ax.text(1.02, (nj[1] + nj_cf[1]) / 2, f"DiD = {did:+.2f}", color="#c0392b", fontsize=10, va="center")
ax.set_xticks(t); ax.set_xticklabels(["pre (Feb 1992)", "post (Nov 1992)"])
ax.set_ylabel("FTE employment per restaurant")
ax.set_title("Each group its own control: subtract the common shock")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower left")
ax.set_xlim(-0.15, 1.35)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Difference-in-differences (Card–Krueger). New Jersey (treated) and Pennsylvania (control) start at different employment levels — DiD allows that. Pennsylvania’s change (−2.16) estimates the common shock both states faced; applying it to New Jersey gives the counterfactual (dashed) — where NJ would have landed with no minimum-wage hike. The gap between NJ’s actual post value and that counterfactual is the DiD effect, +2.76.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
TWFE and the event study
In practice DiD is run as a two-way fixed effects (TWFE) regression, which generalises the four means to many units and periods: (Baker et al. 2025) 
Y_{it}=\eta_i+\theta_t+\beta D_{it}+\epsilon_{it}.
 The unit fixed effects \eta_i absorb all time-invariant differences between units (the first difference); the time fixed effects \theta_t absorb shocks common to all units in a period (the second difference); \beta is the DiD effect. (Baker et al. 2025) It is the panel/fixed-effects machinery of the next entry — DiD is a fixed-effects design.
The event study enriches this by replacing the single D_{it} with dummies for periods relative to treatment: (Baker et al. 2025) 
Y_{it}=\eta_i+\theta_t+\sum_{\tau=-q}^{m}\delta_\tau D_{i,\tau}+\epsilon_{it}.
 The leads (\tau<0) should be near zero — a visual parallel-trends check; the lags (\tau\ge0) trace how the effect grows, fades, or plateaus after treatment. (Baker et al. 2025) An event study is the single most persuasive DiD graphic: flat before, a clear break after.

title: Difference-in-Differences
section: The DiD revolution: when TWFE lies
text:
The DiD revolution: when TWFE lies
Here is the cautionary tale, and why this entry cites a 2020s practitioner’s guide. When units are treated at different times (staggered adoption), the trusty TWFE regression can become biased — even sign-flipped. (Baker et al. 2025)

Forbidden comparisons. With staggering, TWFE implicitly uses already-treated units as controls for later-treated ones. If effects change over time (dynamic effects), those already-treated “controls” are themselves still moving because of their own treatment, contaminating the comparison. (Baker et al. 2025)
Goodman-Bacon decomposition. TWFE’s \beta is a weighted average of all possible 2×2 DiDs — and in staggered designs some weights can be negative. The estimate can come out the wrong sign even if every unit’s true effect is positive. (Baker et al. 2025)
New estimators. “Forward-engineered” methods fix it by using only clean controls: Callaway–Sant’Anna (compare each treated cohort only to never-treated or not-yet-treated units), Sun–Abraham (a saturated cohort-by-time interaction), and de Chaisemartin–D’Haultfœuille (robust when treatment switches on and off). (Baker et al. 2025)

The lesson is humbling and current: a method everyone trusted for decades was quietly mis-estimating staggered rollouts, and the corpus’s most recent source exists to set it right.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Asserting parallel trends from a good story. It’s untestable; show pre-trends / event-study leads near zero, and reason about confounders that could break parallelism. (Baker et al. 2025)
A control group on its own trajectory. If the control was already diverging (a differential trend), the second difference doesn’t cancel it and DiD is biased. (Baker et al. 2025)
Default TWFE under staggered adoption. Negative weights and forbidden comparisons can flip the sign; use Callaway–Sant’Anna / Sun–Abraham / dCDH and report a Goodman-Bacon decomposition. (Baker et al. 2025)
A shock that hits only one group. Parallel trends fails if something else changed for the treated group at the same time (a concurrent policy). (Cunningham 2021)
Anticipation. If units change behaviour before treatment (expecting it), pre-periods are contaminated and leads aren’t a clean test. (Baker et al. 2025)

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






DiD Practitioner’s Guide (Baker, Callaway, Cunningham, Goodman-Bacon, Sant’Anna) — the 2×2 and double-differencing; parallel trends, its untestability, and pre-trend/event-study evidence; TWFE Y_{it}=\eta_i+\theta_t+\beta D_{it}+\epsilon_{it} and the event-study leads/lags; the staggered-adoption problem — forbidden comparisons, the Goodman-Bacon decomposition and negative weights, and the new Callaway–Sant’Anna / Sun–Abraham / de Chaisemartin– D’Haultfœuille estimators.
Cunningham, The Mixtape — DiD intuition and the Card–Krueger minimum-wage study (NJ 20.44\to21.03, PA 23.33\to21.17, DiD =+2.76).
Angrist & Pischke — DiD as a fixed-effects regression within the credibility-revolution toolkit.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why double-differencing identifies the effect. Write potential outcomes with additive group and time components: Y_{it}(0)=\gamma_i+\lambda_t+\varepsilon_{it}, and Y_{it}(1)=Y_{it}(0)+\delta. Parallel trends is the statement that \lambda_t is common across groups (the no-treatment trend is the same). Taking each group’s post−pre difference removes \gamma_i; subtracting the control’s difference removes the common \lambda_{\text{post}}-\lambda_{\text{pre}}, leaving exactly \delta. The assumption is precisely “the \lambda_t path is shared,” which is why differing levels \gamma_i are harmless but differing trends are fatal.
TWFE recovers the 2×2. In the saturated two-period, two-group case, the OLS coefficient on D_{it} in Y_{it}=\eta_i+\theta_t+\beta D_{it}+\epsilon_{it} equals \hat\delta_{DD} exactly — the unit dummies are the first difference, the time dummies the second.
Goodman-Bacon weights. With staggered timing, \hat\beta_{TWFE}=\sum_k s_k\,\hat\beta_k over all 2×2 sub-experiments k, with weights s_k proportional to subsample size and treatment variance. Comparisons that use already-treated units as controls can receive negative s_k when those controls’ effects are still evolving — so a positive-everywhere effect can aggregate to a negative \hat\beta_{TWFE}. The new estimators restrict attention to clean (never- / not-yet-treated) controls, forcing non-negative weighting.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Connections

Builds on: panel & fixed effects (TWFE is its two-way special case), potential outcomes (parallel trends as an assumption about counterfactual changes), and regression FE intuition.
Leads to: synthetic control — what to do when no single control group has parallel trends, by building a weighted one.
Reappears in: modern applied policy work throughout Part VI, where staggered rollouts are the norm and the new estimators are now standard.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text: The whole of Part V chased a single number: an average effect. But “never cross a river that is on average four feet deep.” Causal machine learning estimates the effect as a function of who you are — the conditional average treatment effect \tau(x) — using the flexibility of ML without inheriting its bias. The trick is to estimate the effect, not the outcome, and to protect that estimate from overfitting with orthogonalisation and cross-fitting. It closes the loop: the predictive culture, disciplined back into the service of cause.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Why it matters
An ATE can hide everything that matters for policy: a drug that helps the young and harms the old can average to “no effect.” (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Modern social science wants heterogeneous treatment effects — for whom does this work? — and the obvious idea, “throw a random forest at it,” fails in a way worth understanding deeply. This entry is where Part IV’s machinery — regularization, cross-validation, forests, the bias–variance trade — is repurposed for causation, and where the double/debiased ML preview is finally cashed out. It is the natural summit of the summit: the predictive and causal cultures, reconciled.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
From ATE to CATE
The target shifts from a scalar to a function — the conditional average treatment effect: 
\tau(x)=\mathbb E\big[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X=x\big].
 \tau(x) is the effect for units with characteristics x — the engine of personalised policy, letting you move from a uniform rollout to targeting whoever benefits most. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Everything from potential outcomes still holds; we’ve simply stopped averaging away the variation we now want to see.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
section: Why naive ML is biased for effects
text:
Why naive ML is biased for effects
The tempting move — fit a flexible ML model of Y on (D,X) and read off D’s influence — is biased, for a reason that is subtle and important: (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

Regularization bias. ML methods are built to minimise prediction error for Y, so they regularize — shrinking coefficients, pruning splits — toward whatever predicts the outcome best. When the treatment effect is small relative to other predictors, the algorithm cheerfully shrinks the treatment’s role toward zero, biasing the effect. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Effect ≠ prediction. Estimating an effect is fundamentally harder than predicting an outcome: it targets the difference between two counterfactual states, only one of which is ever observed. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) A model superb at predicting Y can be terrible at isolating \partial
Y/\partial D.

The fix is to stop estimating the outcome and start estimating the effect, with the nuisances partialled out.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Double/debiased machine learning
Double machine learning (DML), due to Chernozhukov and coauthors, lets you use any flexible ML for the hard prediction parts while keeping valid inference on the causal parameter. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Three moves:

Estimate two nuisance functions with ML: an outcome model g_0(X)=\mathbb E[Y\mid X] and a treatment/propensity model m_0(X)=\mathbb E[D\mid X]. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Orthogonalise (partial out). Residualise both Y and D on X — regress the Y-residual on the D-residual. This is Frisch–Waugh–Lovell with ML doing the conditioning, and it yields a Neyman-orthogonal moment: the effect estimate is first-order insensitive to small errors in g_0 and m_0. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) You can plug in imperfect ML nuisances and the errors don’t spill into the treatment effect — the “debiasing.”
Cross-fit. Estimate the nuisances on one fold and the effect on a held-out fold, so no unit’s own outcome contaminates its own nuisance prediction — removing overfitting bias. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) (Exactly the cross-validation discipline, now load-bearing for inference, not just tuning.)

Orthogonality kills the regularization bias; cross-fitting kills the overfitting bias. Together they let ML serve identification instead of sabotaging it. The figure shows the payoff DML and causal forests deliver — recovering an effect that varies with x.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(21)
n = 6000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
D = rng.integers(0, 2, n)                  # randomized treatment (clean identification)
tau_true = 0.5 + 3.0 * x                   # effect grows with x (heterogeneous)
y = 1.0 + 2.0 * x + tau_true * D + rng.normal(0, 1.0, n)

# Honest binned CATE: within each x-bin, treated mean - control mean (+ 95% CI).
edges = np.linspace(0, 1, 11)
centers, est, lo, hi = [], [], [], []
for a, b in zip(edges[:-1], edges[1:]):
    m = (x >= a) & (x < b)
    yt, yc = y[m & (D == 1)], y[m & (D == 0)]
    d = yt.mean() - yc.mean()
    se = np.sqrt(yt.var(ddof=1)/yt.size + yc.var(ddof=1)/yc.size)
    centers.append((a+b)/2); est.append(d); lo.append(d-1.96*se); hi.append(d+1.96*se)
centers, est = np.array(centers), np.array(est)
ate = (y[D==1].mean() - y[D==0].mean())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
xs = np.linspace(0, 1, 100)
ax.plot(xs, 0.5 + 3.0*xs, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4, label="true CATE  τ(x)")
ax.errorbar(centers, est, yerr=[est-np.array(lo), np.array(hi)-est], fmt="o",
            color="#1f3a5f", ms=5, capsize=3, label="honest binned estimate")
ax.axhline(ate, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.6, label=f"ATE = {ate:.2f} (hides heterogeneity)")
ax.set_xlabel("covariate x"); ax.set_ylabel("treatment effect")
ax.set_title("Who benefits? The effect as a function of x")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Heterogeneous treatment effects. The true CATE τ(x) (green) rises with a covariate x — the treatment helps high-x units far more than low-x units, a pattern the ATE (grey dashed) completely hides. An honest, binned effect estimator (blue points, with 95% intervals) recovers τ(x) by comparing treated and control outcomes within strata of x — the intuition behind causal forests, which split to maximise effect heterogeneity and estimate honestly for valid intervals.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Causal forests and honesty
To estimate \tau(x) flexibly, causal forests (generalized random forests) adapt random forests with two changes: (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

Causal splitting. Instead of splitting to reduce prediction error (RSS), trees split to maximise the heterogeneity of the estimated treatment effect across the children — actively hunting for the covariates along which the effect varies. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Honesty. A tree is honest if it uses one subsample to choose the splits and a separate subsample to estimate the effect in each leaf. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) This separation is what makes the leaf estimates statistically valid — it earns trustworthy confidence intervals on \tau(x), instead of the overconfident ones you’d get from estimating effects in the very leaves you carved to maximise them.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Policy learning
The point of knowing \tau(x) is often to act. Policy learning turns CATEs into an assignment rule \pi(x). (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) The unconstrained optimum is simple — treat everyone with a positive predicted effect: 
\pi^*(x)=\mathbf 1\{\tau(x)\ge 0\}.
 Framed as a classification problem, it predicts the sign of the effect, weighted by its magnitude — misclassifying a large positive effect costs more than fumbling a near-zero one (cost-sensitive classification). (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) This is where causal inference meets decision-making: not “what is the effect?” but “whom should we treat?”

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
section: The cautions: ML is not a license
text:
The cautions: ML is not a license
The most important sentence in the chapter: machine learning does not fix identification. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Every method here delivers causal answers only if the identifying assumptions still hold — conditional exogeneity (unconfoundedness) and overlap 0<\Pr(D=1\mid X)<1. DML and causal forests make you efficient and flexible under those assumptions; they cannot conjure them. Throwing a forest at confounded data yields a beautifully estimated biased effect. Two further risks: (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

Fairness. ML models absorb the prejudices in their training data (gender and racial bias in text embeddings, biased historical decisions) — a Part IV worry with higher stakes when the output assigns treatment.
Interpretability ≠ mechanism. Tools like SHAP or distilling a forest into a shallow tree explain how the model guesses, not the causal mechanism of the world. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Naive ML on the outcome. Regularization shrinks small treatment effects toward zero. Orthogonalise (DML) — estimate the effect, not Y. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Skipping cross-fitting. Using a unit’s own data to fit its nuisance reintroduces overfitting bias into the effect. Split the folds. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Dishonest forests. Choosing splits and estimating effects on the same data gives invalid, overconfident intervals on \tau(x). Use honest estimation. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Believing ML earns identification. Unconfoundedness and overlap are still required; ML buys flexibility, not exogeneity. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Acting on a biased CATE. A policy rule is only as causal as the \tau(x) beneath it — garbage identification in, harmful targeting out. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Chernozhukov et al., Applied Causal Inference Powered by ML and AI — the CATE \tau(x)=\mathbb E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X=x] and the “four-feet-deep river”; regularization bias from naive outcome-prediction ML; double/debiased ML — nuisances g_0(X),m_0(X), Neyman orthogonality, cross-fitting; causal forests / GRF with causal splitting and honest estimation for valid CIs; policy learning \pi^*(x)=\mathbf 1\{\tau(x)\ge0\} as cost-sensitive classification; and the cautions — ML doesn’t fix identification (needs unconfoundedness + overlap), fairness, and interpretability-vs-mechanism.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, ML for Social & Behavioral Research — heterogeneous effects for social science, encoded bias/fairness, and interpretability tools (SHAP, distillation) describing the model, not the mechanism.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The orthogonalised (partialling-out) estimator. In the partially linear model Y=\theta D+g_0(X)+\varepsilon, D=m_0(X)+v, define residuals \tilde Y=Y-\mathbb E[Y\mid X] and \tilde D=D-m_0(X). The DML estimate solves the Neyman-orthogonal moment 
\hat\theta=\frac{\frac1n\sum_i \tilde D_i\,\tilde Y_i}{\frac1n\sum_i \tilde D_i^2},
 each nuisance fit on a separate fold. Orthogonality means \partial_{\eta}\mathbb E[\psi(W;\theta,\eta)]=0 at the truth: small errors \hat g-g_0, \hat m-m_0 enter the estimating equation only at second order, so \hat\theta stays \sqrt n-consistent and asymptotically normal even though the nuisances converge slowly. This is FWL made robust to ML noise.
Honest CATE intervals. Split the data: use sample \mathcal I_1 to grow the tree (choose splits maximising \sum_{\text{children}} n_c\,\hat\tau_c^2, effect heterogeneity), and the independent sample \mathcal I_2 to estimate each leaf’s \hat\tau(x). Because the leaf was not chosen using \mathcal I_2’s outcomes, the leaf estimate is (asymptotically) unbiased with a usable variance — the formal basis for confidence intervals on \tau(x).
Policy value. A rule \pi earns welfare V(\pi)=\mathbb E[\,Y(\pi(X))\,]=\mathbb E[\,\pi(X)\tau(X)\,]+\text{const}, maximised pointwise by \pi^*(x)=\mathbf 1\{\tau(x)\ge0\} — treat exactly where the effect is positive; the regret of any rule is \mathbb E[(\pi^*-\pi)\tau], which weights mistakes by effect size.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Connections

Builds on: interpretability & ML (the DML/causal- forest preview, now full), regularization and cross-validation (the bias they cause and cure here), FWL partialling-out, and matching/overlap (the identification ML still needs).
Closes: the summit — from the fundamental problem to estimating who benefits and whom to treat.
Leads to: combining & triangulating designs (doubly robust estimation is the stacking of the propensity and outcome models seen here), then sensitivity analysis & external validity (how fragile and how generalisable any of these estimates are), and onward to Part VI · doing research in the world, where these estimates meet measurement, incentives, fairness, and the tyranny of metrics.

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text: ARIMA models one series from its own past. But macro and social systems are webs: output, inflation, and interest rates each move the others. Vector autoregression (VAR) treats them symmetrically — every variable is regressed on the lagged values of all variables, none privileged as exogenous. From that one democratic system come three tools: Granger causality (does the past of X help predict Y?), impulse responses (how does a shock propagate?), and variance decomposition (which shocks drive which series?).
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
Why it matters
Old large-scale macro models hard-wired which variables were “causes” and which “effects” — restrictions Christopher Sims (1980) attacked as “incredible,” lacking real theoretical basis (Enders 2014). His atheoretical alternative: treat every variable as endogenous and let the data speak. The VAR became the workhorse of empirical macro and a staple of political science and finance — and its innovation accounting (impulse responses + variance decomposition) is how researchers read dynamic cause-and-effect within a system without imposing a dubious structural story.
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
The VAR(p) system
A VAR of order p models a vector of n variables \mathbf{x}_t as a linear function of its own p lags — each equation containing the lags of every variable (Enders 2014). The crux is the distinction between two forms:
Structural (primitive) VAR — includes contemporaneous effects among variables, with pure, uncorrelated structural shocks \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t:

B\mathbf{x}_t = \Gamma_0 + \Gamma_1 \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \cdots + \Gamma_p \mathbf{x}_{t-p} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t .

Reduced-form VAR — premultiply by B^{-1} to get the estimable system:

\mathbf{x}_t = A_0 + \sum_{i=1}^p A_i\,\mathbf{x}_{t-i} + \mathbf{e}_t .

The catch: the reduced-form errors \mathbf{e}_t = B^{-1}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t are composites of the structural shocks, so they are correlated across equations and have no direct economic interpretation (Enders 2014). That correlation is the whole identification problem (below).
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
Estimation and lag length
Because every equation has the identical set of regressors (the same lags of all variables), OLS equation-by-equation is consistent and asymptotically efficient — no system estimator needed (Enders 2014). The real choice is the lag length p: too few causes misspecification, too many wastes degrees of freedom (Enders 2014). Pick p to minimise an information criterion, \text{AIC} = T\ln|\Sigma| + 2N or \text{SBC} = T\ln|\Sigma| + N\ln T (with N total parameters), or test nested lag lengths with a likelihood-ratio statistic (T-c)\,(\ln|\Sigma_r| - \ln|\Sigma_u|), which is \chi^2 (Enders 2014).
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
section: Granger causality: predictive precedence, not cause
text:
Granger causality: predictive precedence, not cause
In a VAR, Granger causality measures predictive precedence, not philosophical causation (Enders 2014). Variable y Granger-causes z if past values of y help predict z beyond what z’s own past predicts (Enders 2014). You test it with an F-test that the coefficients on all lags of y in the z-equation are jointly zero; block exogeneity is the multivariate version, asking whether a block of variables helps predict the rest (Enders 2014).






Warning‘Granger-causes’ is a loaded name



It means temporal predictability, full stop. y can Granger-cause z with no causal link — both driven by a third variable, or by anticipation (stock prices “Granger-cause” earnings because they price in the future). Granger causality is necessary-ish for, but very far from sufficient for, the causal claims of Part V. (Enders 2014)
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
Impulse-response functions
The impulse-response function (IRF) traces the effect of a one-time shock to one innovation on the current and future values of all variables (Enders 2014). It comes from the vector moving-average (VMA) representation — the VAR rewritten as an infinite sum of past shocks:

\mathbf{x}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\Phi_i\,\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{t-i}.

The matrices \Phi_i are the impulse responses: element (j,k) of \Phi_i is the response of variable j, i periods after a shock to variable k. Plotting them shows how a shock propagates through the system and decays (provided the VAR is stationary) (Enders 2014).
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
Identification: why the ordering matters
Here is the subtlety. Because the reduced-form residuals \mathbf{e}_t are correlated, a “shock to one equation” can’t be cleanly attributed to one variable — you must impose identifying restrictions (Enders 2014). The standard device is the Choleski (Cholesky) decomposition: a recursive ordering that makes the contemporaneous-effects matrix triangular, imposing exactly (n^2-n)/2 restrictions (Enders 2014). Its meaning: the variable ordered first affects all others contemporaneously, but is itself moved by the others only with a lag (Enders 2014).
The consequence is unavoidable: when residuals are highly correlated, changing the ordering changes the impulse responses and variance decompositions — the Sims–Bernanke concern (Enders 2014). The ordering is an economic assumption, not a technicality.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

A = np.array([[0.5, 0.3],            # VAR(1) coefficient matrix (stable)
              [0.2, 0.4]])
Sigma = np.array([[1.0, 0.8],        # reduced-form residual covariance (corr 0.8)
                  [0.8, 1.0]])
H = 12

def orth_irf(A, Sigma, order):
    """Orthogonalized IRF to a 1-SD shock in the variable placed FIRST under `order`."""
    P = np.eye(2)[order]                       # permutation to chosen order
    Sig_o = P @ Sigma @ P.T
    L = np.linalg.cholesky(Sig_o)              # lower-triangular factor
    shock = P.T @ (L @ np.array([1.0, 0.0]))   # 1-SD shock to first-ordered var, back to original axes
    resp = np.empty((H, 2)); cur = shock.copy()
    for h in range(H):
        resp[h] = cur
        cur = A @ cur                          # Phi_i = A^i applied to the shock
    return resp

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.8), sharey=True)
for ax, order, lab in [(axes[0], [0, 1], "Ordering X → Y"),
                       (axes[1], [1, 0], "Ordering Y → X")]:
    r = orth_irf(A, Sigma, order)
    ax.axhline(0, color="#999", lw=0.8)
    ax.plot(range(H), r[:, 0], "o-", color="#1f4e79", lw=2, ms=4, label="response of X")
    ax.plot(range(H), r[:, 1], "s-", color="#c0392b", lw=2, ms=4, label="response of Y")
    ax.set_title(lab, fontsize=10); ax.set_xlabel("horizon (periods after shock)")
    ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
axes[0].set_ylabel("response to a 1-SD shock")
fig.suptitle("Same VAR, two Cholesky orderings → different impulse responses", fontsize=11.5)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Orthogonalized impulse responses from a 2-variable VAR(1), computed by hand, under two Cholesky orderings of the same system (residual correlation 0.8). Each panel shows both variables’ response to a one-standard-deviation shock to the variable ordered FIRST. Note the contemporaneous (h=0) response of the second variable: it is forced to react in one ordering and forced to zero in the other. Same data, same VAR — different causal story, purely from the ordering. This is the Sims–Bernanke warning made visible.
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
Variance decomposition
The forecast-error variance decomposition (FEVD) is the IRF’s complement: it partitions the variance of each variable’s forecast error into the share attributable to each structural shock (Enders 2014). It answers “what % of the movement in z comes from z’s own shocks versus shocks to other variables?” Typically a variable explains most of its own variance at short horizons, with other variables’ shocks growing more important at longer horizons as effects diffuse through the system (Enders 2014). IRFs and FEVD together are Sims’s “innovation accounting” (Enders 2014).
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading Granger causality as causation. It is predictive precedence; a significant F-test is not a causal license. (Enders 2014)
Ignoring the Cholesky ordering. Highly correlated residuals make IRFs/FEVD ordering-dependent; justify the ordering or report robustness across orderings. (Enders 2014)
Running a VAR on non-stationary levels. If the series are I(1) and cointegrated, you need a VECM, not a plain VAR; if I(1) and not cointegrated, model the differences. (Enders 2014)
Over-lagging. Each extra lag costs n^2 parameters; let AIC/SBC and LR tests discipline p. (Enders 2014)
Over-interpreting structural shocks from a reduced form. Reduced-form residuals have no economic meaning until identified. (Enders 2014)
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Enders, Applied Econometric Time Series — Sims’s “incredible restrictions” critique and the atheoretical VAR; the structural vs reduced-form systems and why reduced-form errors are correlated composites; OLS equation-by-equation estimation and lag-length selection (AIC/SBC, the LR statistic); Granger causality as predictive precedence with the joint F-test and block exogeneity; impulse responses from the VMA representation \mathbf{x}_t=\mu+\sum\Phi_i\epsilon_{t-i}; the identification problem, the Choleski recursive ordering with (n^2-n)/2 restrictions, and why ordering matters (Sims–Bernanke); the FEVD and “innovation accounting.”
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

From VAR to VMA. Write the VAR(1) \mathbf{x}_t = A\mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \mathbf{e}_t (demeaned). Back- substituting, \mathbf{x}_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} A^i \mathbf{e}_{t-i}, so the reduced-form impulse matrices are A^i. Stationarity requires every eigenvalue of A to lie inside the unit circle, which makes A^i \to 0 and the responses decay. A VAR(p) is recast as a VAR(1) in companion form to apply the same result.
Orthogonalizing the shocks. With reduced-form covariance \Sigma = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{e}_t\mathbf{e}_t'], the Cholesky factor L (lower-triangular, LL'=\Sigma) defines orthogonal shocks \mathbf{u}_t = L^{-1}\mathbf{e}_t with identity covariance. The orthogonalized IRF to a unit shock in the k-th orthogonal innovation is \Phi_i L\,\mathbf{s}_k (with \mathbf{s}_k the k-th unit vector). Triangularity is the recursive assumption — variable 1 reacts to none of the others contemporaneously, and so on — which is exactly why a different ordering yields a different L and different responses.
Counting restrictions. Identifying n^2 structural parameters from the n(n+1)/2 distinct elements of \Sigma requires (n^2 - n)/2 extra restrictions; a recursive (triangular) B supplies exactly that many, which is why Cholesky just identifies the system.
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses
text:
Connections

Builds on: ARIMA (the univariate AR generalised to a system), unit roots (VARs need stationary inputs), and multiple regression (each equation is an OLS).
Leads to: cointegration & error-correction — the VECM is a VAR for cointegrated I(1) series.
Contrasts with: causal inference — Granger causality is predictive, not the structural causality of potential outcomes and DAGs.
crumbs: Vector Autoregression: VAR, Granger & Impulse Responses

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text: A decision tree asks a series of yes/no questions and reads off an answer — the most interpretable model there is. It is also dangerously high-variance: redraw the data and the tree reshuffles. The fix is to grow hundreds of trees and average them. That move — random forests and boosting — sacrifices the flowchart for some of the best prediction accuracy available.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Why it matters
Trees are the other great route (besides regularization) to managing the bias–variance trade-off, and they handle something linear models struggle with: non-linearities and interactions you never specified. (James et al. 2021) A single tree is a gift for communication — you can show it to a policymaker. Its ensembles are a gift for accuracy — forests and boosting are among the strongest off-the-shelf predictors on tabular social data. (Bruce et al. 2020) The journey from one tree to a forest is also the cleanest illustration of why averaging decorrelated models is the deepest trick in predictive modelling.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: Decision trees: a flowchart fit to data
text:
Decision trees: a flowchart fit to data
A tree predicts by recursive binary splitting — a greedy, top-down carve-up of the predictor space. (James et al. 2021) Start at the root (all observations); find the single predictor and cutpoint whose split most improves fit; repeat inside each branch; stop at terminal nodes (leaves), where the prediction is just the mean (regression) or majority class (classification) of the observations there. (James et al. 2021) How is “best split” judged? Regression trees minimise RSS; classification trees maximise node purity via the Gini index or cross-entropy (better than raw error rate for growing the tree). (James et al. 2021)






NoteISL’s baseball-salary tree



Predicting log-salary from a player’s career stats, a small 3-leaf tree uses just Years and Hits: players with < 4.5 years experience average about $165,174; experienced players with ≥ 118 hits average $845,346. (James et al. 2021) Two questions, a clear story — “pay rises with experience, then with productivity” — readable by anyone. Trees are even easier to explain than linear regression, and arguably mirror how humans actually decide. (James et al. 2021)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: The flaw: high variance, and pruning
text:
The flaw: high variance, and pruning
A single tree’s fatal weakness: it is high-variance and overfits easily. (James et al. 2021) Grow it deep enough and it carves the data into perfectly pure leaves — memorising the noise — and a slightly different sample yields a wholly different tree. Pruning (cost-complexity / weakest-link pruning) controls this: grow a large tree, then cut it back with a penalty \alpha on the number of leaves, choosing \alpha by cross-validation. (James et al. 2021) But pruning only mitigates the variance — the real cure is to stop relying on one tree.

See it: one jagged tree vs a smooth forest
Below, a single deep tree (orange) fits the data as a wild step function — over-reacting to every wiggle of noise. A forest of many trees, each grown on a bootstrap resample and then averaged (blue), smooths those steps into a stable estimate of the signal. Same data, the variance averaged away.


Show the figure code (a minimal regression tree + bagging)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

def fit_tree(x, y, depth, max_depth, min_leaf=3):
    if depth >= max_depth or x.size < 2 * min_leaf:
        m = y.mean();  return lambda xq: np.full(xq.shape, m)
    order = np.argsort(x); xs = x[order]
    best = (np.inf, None)
    for i in range(min_leaf, x.size - min_leaf + 1):
        t = 0.5 * (xs[i-1] + xs[i])
        l, r = y[x <= t], y[x > t]
        if l.size and r.size:
            sse = ((l - l.mean())**2).sum() + ((r - r.mean())**2).sum()
            if sse < best[0]: best = (sse, t)
    t = best[1]
    if t is None:
        m = y.mean();  return lambda xq: np.full(xq.shape, m)
    L = fit_tree(x[x <= t], y[x <= t], depth+1, max_depth)
    R = fit_tree(x[x > t],  y[x > t],  depth+1, max_depth)
    return lambda xq: np.where(xq <= t, L(xq), R(xq))

n = 70
x = np.sort(rng.uniform(0, 10, n))
signal = lambda x: np.sin(0.9 * x) + 0.25 * x
y = signal(x) + rng.normal(0, 0.45, n)
grid = np.linspace(0, 10, 400)

single = fit_tree(x, y, 0, max_depth=10)(grid)               # one deep tree
B = 150
forest = np.zeros_like(grid)
for _ in range(B):
    idx = rng.integers(0, n, n)                               # bootstrap resample
    forest += fit_tree(x[idx], y[idx], 0, max_depth=10)(grid)
forest /= B

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=16, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6, zorder=2)
ax.plot(grid, signal(grid), color="#7a7f87", lw=1.6, ls="--", label="true signal")
ax.plot(grid, single, color="#d98a29", lw=1.6, label="single deep tree (overfit)")
ax.plot(grid, forest, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6, label=f"forest: average of {B} trees")
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("One tree is jagged; a forest is smooth")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A single deep regression tree (orange) overfits — a jagged step function chasing the noise, the hallmark of high variance. A forest (blue), averaging 150 trees each grown on a bootstrap resample, smooths the steps into a stable fit close to the true signal (grey). Averaging decorrelated trees is what tames the variance.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Bagging: average away the variance
Bagging (bootstrap aggregating) is the general cure for any high-variance method. (James et al. 2021) Draw B bootstrap resamples, grow a deep (unpruned) tree on each, and average their predictions (or take a majority vote). Averaging many noisy-but-unbiased estimates slashes variance without adding bias — the law of large numbers applied to models. A free bonus: out-of-bag (OOB) error. Each tree omits about a third of the data (the observations not drawn into its bootstrap), so predicting each point with only the trees that didn’t see it gives a built-in test-error estimate — no separate validation set needed. (James et al. 2021)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Random forests: decorrelate, then average
Bagging has a subtle weakness, and random forests fix it. (James et al. 2021) If one predictor is very strong, every bagged tree splits on it first — so the trees are highly correlated, and averaging correlated things barely reduces variance (the same correlation penalty as in LOOCV). The fix is elegantly simple: at each split, allow only a random subset of m predictors to be considered. (James et al. 2021) This forces trees to use different variables, decorrelating them, so their average is far more stable. The standard choice is m \approx \sqrt{p}. (James et al. 2021) That one tweak — randomly handicapping each split — is what makes a random forest beat plain bagging.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: Boosting: learn slowly from your mistakes
text:
Boosting: learn slowly from your mistakes
Boosting takes the opposite philosophy to bagging’s parallel averaging: it grows trees sequentially, each new tree fitting the residuals (the errors) of the current ensemble. (James et al. 2021) The model learns slowly, concentrating on the cases it has been getting wrong. Three tuning knobs: the number of trees B (too many can overfit, but slowly), the shrinkage / learning rate \lambda (how fast it learns — typically 0.01 or 0.001), and the tree depth d (often tiny — even stumps, d=1, suffice). (James et al. 2021) Well-tuned boosting is frequently the most accurate method on structured/tabular data.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: Peeking into the black box: variable importance
text:
Peeking into the black box: variable importance
Ensembles buy accuracy by sacrificing the single tree’s interpretability — there’s no one flowchart to read. (James et al. 2021) Variable importance measures partly reopen the box: (James et al. 2021)

Gini / RSS importance — total up how much each predictor’s splits reduced RSS (regression) or the Gini index (classification), averaged over all trees.
Permutation importance — randomly shuffle a predictor’s values and measure how much accuracy drops; a big drop means the model relied on it.







NoteISL’s Heart data



For predicting heart disease, the most important predictors surfaced by a forest were Thal, Ca, and ChestPain. (James et al. 2021) Importance gives a ranking — useful and suggestive — but, as the next entry stresses, a ranking is not a causal effect. (James et al. 2021)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: Trees vs linear models: which wins?
text:
Trees vs linear models: which wins?
It depends entirely on the true data-generating process. (James et al. 2021)

Linear/logistic models win when the relationship is approximately linear — OLS exploits that structure efficiently, and trees only approximate a line with clumsy steps.
Trees and forests win when the truth is highly non-linear with many interactions the researcher never specified — they discover that structure automatically. (James et al. 2021)

A useful division of labour: single trees for communication and exploration; forests and boosting for maximum predictive power. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Trusting a single deep tree. It’s high-variance and overfit; a different sample gives a different tree. Prune it, or move to an ensemble. (James et al. 2021)
Expecting a forest to be interpretable. Averaging hundreds of trees destroys the flowchart. Use variable importance / partial dependence, and accept you’ve traded transparency for accuracy. (James et al. 2021)
Reading variable importance as a causal effect. It measures predictive contribution, not cause; a strong predictor may be a proxy or a collider. (James et al. 2021)
Skipping tuning on boosting. Too high a learning rate or too-deep trees overfit; boosting needs CV on its knobs. (James et al. 2021)
Using trees where a line would do. On near-linear data, regression beats trees and stays interpretable. Match the method to the structure. (James et al. 2021)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — recursive binary splitting, leaves and the mean/majority prediction, RSS vs Gini/entropy splits; tree interpretability; high variance and cost-complexity pruning; the Hitters salary tree (Years/Hits; $165,174 and $845,346); bagging and OOB error; random forests, decorrelation, and m\approx\sqrt p; boosting (sequential, residual-fitting, B/\lambda/d, stumps); Gini and permutation variable importance; the Heart data (Thal, Ca, ChestPain); the trees-vs-linear verdict.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — trees and ensembles in the applied data-science idiom; single trees for communication, ensembles for predictive power.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Split criteria. A regression split sends the data into regions R_1,R_2 to minimise \sum_{i\in R_1}(y_i-\bar y_{R_1})^2 + \sum_{i\in R_2}(y_i-\bar y_{R_2})^2. A classification split minimises a purity measure — the Gini index G=\sum_k \hat p_k(1-\hat p_k) or cross-entropy D=-\sum_k \hat p_k\log\hat p_k — both small when one class dominates a node.
Cost-complexity pruning. For a large tree T_0, choose the subtree T\subseteq T_0 minimising \sum_{m=1}^{|T|}\sum_{i\in R_m}(y_i-\bar y_{R_m})^2 + \alpha|T|, where |T| is the number of leaves and \alpha\ge 0 trades fit against size. Increasing \alpha yields a nested sequence of subtrees; CV picks \alpha.
Why decorrelation reduces variance. The average of B identically-distributed trees each with variance \sigma^2 and pairwise correlation \rho has variance 
\rho\,\sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{B}\,\sigma^2 .
 As B\to\infty the second term vanishes, but the first, \rho\sigma^2, does not — so bagging is capped by how correlated the trees are. Random forests shrink \rho (by restricting splits to m random predictors), pushing the floor down — the exact mechanism behind their gain. (Same correlated-average algebra as the Moulton/clustering and LOOCV results.)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Connections

Builds on: the bias–variance trade-off (a high-variance method tamed by averaging), the bootstrap (bagging’s engine), and the LLN (why averaging many models works).
Leads to: interpretability & ML for social science (variable importance and partial dependence as the price of ensembles), and causal forests for heterogeneous treatment effects.
Contrasts with: regression — linear structure vs learned non-linear partitions; the right choice depends on the truth.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text: OLS is unbiased — and when predictors are many or collinear, that unbiasedness comes at a ruinous price in variance. Regularization makes a trade the bias–variance logic predicts will pay: shrink the coefficients toward zero, accept a little bias, and buy a large drop in variance. The Lasso goes further, setting some coefficients exactly to zero — doing variable selection automatically.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Why it matters
The estimation entry’s shrinkage estimator — biased but lower-MSE — was a curiosity there. Here it becomes a workhorse. When the number of predictors p is large relative to n, OLS overfits badly; when p > n, OLS has no unique solution at all. (James et al. 2021) Regularization rescues both, and the Lasso throws in a bonus social scientists prize: interpretability, by zeroing out the variables that don’t earn their place. (James et al. 2021) This is also the on-ramp to high-dimensional and causal ML, where regularised estimates of many nuisance parameters are routine.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
The idea: penalise big coefficients
Both methods keep OLS’s goal — minimise the residual sum of squares — but add a penalty on the size of the coefficients, so the fit must now balance accuracy against coefficient magnitude. (James et al. 2021) A tuning parameter \lambda \ge 0 sets the exchange rate:

\lambda = 0: no penalty — you get plain OLS.
\lambda \to \infty: the penalty dominates — all coefficients are crushed toward zero (the null model).

In between, \lambda is a bias–variance dial: as it rises, variance falls (the model is less sensitive to the training sample) and bias rises (it assumes coefficients are small). The test error is U-shaped in \lambda, and you pick the bottom by cross-validation. (James et al. 2021)

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Ridge regression (\ell_2)
Ridge adds the sum of squared coefficients: (James et al. 2021)

\min_{\beta}\ \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n\Big(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_j \beta_j x_{ij}\Big)^2}_{\text{RSS}}
\;+\; \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 .

The \ell_2 penalty shrinks every coefficient toward zero but never to zero (except in the limit \lambda=\infty). (James et al. 2021) So Ridge keeps all predictors — it tames their magnitudes rather than dropping any. Two practical notes: Ridge is not scale-invariant (the penalty treats all \beta_j alike, so a variable measured in dollars is penalised differently from one in years), so you must standardise predictors to mean 0, SD 1 before fitting; and Ridge is especially good under multicollinearity, spreading weight smoothly across correlated predictors instead of letting OLS swing them wildly. (James et al. 2021)

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
The Lasso (\ell_1)
The Lasso changes one thing — squared coefficients become absolute values: (James et al. 2021)

\min_{\beta}\ \text{RSS} \;+\; \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| .

That small change has a dramatic effect: for large enough \lambda, the \ell_1 penalty forces some coefficients to be exactly zero. (James et al. 2021) The Lasso therefore performs automatic variable selection, yielding a sparse model — only the predictors that earn their keep survive — which is far easier for a social scientist to interpret than Ridge’s dense vector of small-but-nonzero coefficients. (James et al. 2021)

Why Lasso zeroes coefficients but Ridge doesn’t
The reason is geometry, and it is beautiful. Each method can be written as “minimise RSS subject to a budget on coefficient size.” The budget’s shape is everything: (James et al. 2021)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bhat = np.array([1.9, 1.1])                         # OLS estimate
A = np.array([[1.0, 0.5], [0.5, 1.3]])              # RSS contour shape
g1, g2 = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-0.5, 3, 400), np.linspace(-0.5, 2.6, 400))
Z = (A[0,0]*(g1-bhat[0])**2 + 2*A[0,1]*(g1-bhat[0])*(g2-bhat[1]) + A[1,1]*(g2-bhat[1])**2)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4.4))
for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.contour(g1, g2, Z, levels=8, colors="#c0392b", linewidths=0.8, alpha=0.8)
    ax.scatter(*bhat, color="#c0392b", zorder=5); ax.text(bhat[0]+0.06, bhat[1]+0.05, "β̂", color="#c0392b")
    ax.axhline(0, color="#999", lw=0.7); ax.axvline(0, color="#999", lw=0.7)
    ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 3); ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 2.6); ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xlabel("β₁"); ax.set_ylabel("β₂")

s = 1.15
axL.add_patch(plt.Polygon([[s,0],[0,s],[-s,0],[0,-s]], closed=True,
              facecolor="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.3, edgecolor="#1f3a5f", lw=2))
axL.scatter(0, s, color="#1f8a4c", zorder=6, s=60)
axL.text(0.06, s+0.06, "solution: β₁ = 0", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
axL.set_title("Lasso (ℓ₁): diamond → hits a corner")

axR.add_patch(plt.Circle((0,0), 1.15, facecolor="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.3, edgecolor="#1f3a5f", lw=2))
ang = 0.62
axR.scatter(1.15*np.cos(ang), 1.15*np.sin(ang), color="#1f8a4c", zorder=6, s=60)
axR.text(0.55, 0.78, "both β ≠ 0", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
axR.set_title("Ridge (ℓ₂): circle → no corner")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Why Lasso selects and Ridge doesn’t (ISL’s picture). Red ellipses are RSS contours around the OLS estimate β̂; the blue region is the coefficient budget. Left (Lasso, ℓ1): the budget is a diamond with corners on the axes, so the growing ellipse first touches it at a corner — where β₁ = 0. Right (Ridge, ℓ2): the budget is a smooth circle with no corners, so the contact point has both coefficients non-zero.





The diamond has corners sitting exactly on the axes (where a coefficient is zero), and the expanding RSS ellipse tends to touch the budget at one of those corners — zeroing that coefficient. The circle is smooth and corner-free, so contact almost always lands where both coefficients are non-zero. (James et al. 2021) Sparsity is a consequence of the \ell_1 ball’s pointy shape.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Choosing \lambda, and which method wins
Pick \lambda by k-fold cross-validation: score a grid of \lambda values, take the one minimising CV error (or the one-standard-error rule’s most parsimonious model within one SE). (James et al. 2021)






NoteISL’s Credit data and the verdict



On the Credit data (predicting card balance), the Ridge paths show Rating, Limit, Income, Student all shrinking smoothly toward zero but staying non-zero; the Lasso paths show variables switching on one at a time as \lambda falls — Rating first, then Student, Limit, finally Income — a readable ranking of importance, with the rest pinned at zero. (James et al. 2021)
Neither method universally wins. In a sparse world (a few predictors really matter) the Lasso excels; in a dense world (many small effects) Ridge does. So: Lasso for interpretability and selection, Ridge for robustness under multicollinearity. (James et al. 2021) (The elastic net blends both.)

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting to standardise. Ridge and Lasso are scale-dependent; an unstandardised variable is penalised by its units, not its importance. Standardise predictors first. (James et al. 2021)
Reading shrunken coefficients as unbiased effects. Regularization deliberately biases coefficients toward zero — they are tuned for prediction, not for an honest causal magnitude. Don’t interpret a Lasso coefficient like an OLS one. (James et al. 2021)
Treating Lasso’s selection as the “true model.” Which variables survive depends on \lambda, the sample, and correlations among predictors; a dropped variable isn’t proven irrelevant, and with correlated predictors the Lasso picks one somewhat arbitrarily. (James et al. 2021)
Choosing \lambda by training error. Training error falls as \lambda\to 0 (toward OLS); only cross-validated error reveals the right amount of shrinkage. (James et al. 2021)
Using Ridge when you wanted selection (or Lasso under heavy collinearity). Match the tool to the world — sparse vs dense, correlated vs not. (James et al. 2021)

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — the rationale for shrinkage (prediction accuracy when p is large or p>n; interpretability); Ridge’s objective \text{RSS}+\lambda\sum\beta_j^2, the role of \lambda, no-exact-zeros, and the standardisation requirement; the Lasso’s objective \text{RSS}+\lambda\sum|\beta_j| and exact-zero variable selection / sparsity; the diamond-vs-circle geometry; bias-up/variance-down as \lambda grows; choosing \lambda by CV with the one-SE rule; the Credit-data coefficient paths and the sparse-vs-dense simulation verdict (Lasso for selection, Ridge for correlated predictors).

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Ridge has a closed form. In matrix form, minimising \lVert y-X\beta\rVert^2+\lambda\lVert\beta\rVert^2 gives

\hat\beta^{\text{ridge}} = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^\top y .

The added \lambda I makes X^\top X+\lambda I invertible even when X^\top X is singular (e.g. p>n) — which is precisely why Ridge works where OLS fails. As \lambda\to\infty, \hat\beta^{\text{ridge}}\to 0.
The constrained form. Each penalty is equivalent (by Lagrangian duality) to minimising RSS subject to a budget: Ridge to \sum\beta_j^2\le s, Lasso to \sum|\beta_j|\le s, with a one-to-one \lambda\leftrightarrow s. The geometry of those budget sets — the smooth \ell_2 ball vs the cornered \ell_1 ball — is what the figure draws, and is the exact reason for (no) sparsity.
Soft-thresholding (orthonormal case). When the predictors are orthonormal, the Lasso solution is explicit: \hat\beta_j^{\text{lasso}} = \operatorname{sign}(\hat\beta_j)\,(|\hat\beta_j|-\lambda/2)_+ — each OLS coefficient is pulled toward zero by \lambda/2 and clipped to exactly zero once it’s smaller than that. Ridge instead scales each by 1/(1+\lambda) — shrinking proportionally, never to zero. That contrast — subtract-and-clip vs scale — is the algebra behind the geometry.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Connections

Builds on: the bias–variance trade-off (regularization buys variance reduction with bias), the shrinkage estimator (lower MSE via bias), cross-validation (how \lambda is chosen), and OLS / Gauss–Markov (the unbiased baseline being traded away).
Leads to: trees & forests (another route to low variance, via averaging) and high-dimensional causal ML (Lasso for selecting controls; double/debiased ML).
Contrasts with: logistic regression, which the Lasso also regularises for high-dimensional classification.

title: IV · The Computational Turn: Predictive Modelling
text:
This is the hinge of the manual: where statistical thinking (estimate an effect, defend it, quantify uncertainty) meets computational thinking (build something that predicts well on data it has never seen). It is the on-ramp to the causal summit — you meet cross-validation, regularization, and the bias–variance trade-off here, because modern causal inference uses these tools — and it gathers the field’s running debates in one place.
There are two distinct goals people pursue with the same regression output, and conflating them is the source of endless confusion in both academia and industry:

Where this leads

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text: The bias–variance sweet spot is invisible: it lives in test error, and you don’t have infinite test data. Cross-validation is the trick that manufactures honest test error from a limited sample — by repeatedly hiding part of the data, fitting on the rest, and scoring on what was hidden. It is how every flexibility dial in this chapter actually gets set.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
Why it matters
The previous two entries established the goal — minimise error on unseen data — and warned that training error is a liar, falling toward zero as you overfit. (James et al. 2021) But if you can’t trust training error and don’t have a giant separate test set, how do you choose a model’s flexibility, or a regularization penalty, or which variables to keep? Cross-validation is the answer, and it is the most important procedure in predictive modelling: the disciplined way to estimate test error and tune a model without fooling yourself. Get its one golden rule wrong and every result downstream is optimistic fiction.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
The sacred test set
The cleanest discipline splits data three ways: (James et al. 2021)

Training set — fit the model’s parameters.
Validation set — tune its complexity (flexibility, penalty, which variables).
Test set — sacred: held out, untouched, never seen by the algorithm until a single, final, once-and-for-all evaluation.

The test set exists to give an honest estimate of real-world performance, and it only stays honest if you never peek. (James et al. 2021) The cardinal sin is data leakage — letting information from the validation or test data seep into training. A classic leak: standardising the whole dataset before splitting, so the training fold has secretly “seen” the held-out data’s mean and scale. The result is an artificially optimistic score that collapses in production. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
section: The validation-set approach (and why it’s not enough)
text:
The validation-set approach (and why it’s not enough)
The simplest estimate of test error: randomly split the data once into a training half and a validation (hold-out) half, fit on the first, score on the second. (James et al. 2021)






NoteISL’s Auto example



Predicting a car’s gas mileage from horsepower, ISL splits 392 observations into 196 train / 196 validation. A linear fit gives a validation MSE of 23.30; adding a quadratic term drops it to 18.90 — clear evidence the relationship curves. (James et al. 2021)


But the single-split approach has two flaws: (James et al. 2021)

High variability. The estimate swings depending on which observations land in which half — ISL shows ten different random splits producing ten different error curves.
Wasted data. Training on only half the sample, models perform worse than they would on the full data, so the validation approach overestimates the true test error.

Cross-validation fixes both by reusing every observation for both roles.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
k-fold cross-validation
The modern standard. Split the data into k roughly-equal folds; hold out one fold as validation, train on the other k-1, score on the held-out fold; rotate so each fold is the validation set exactly once; average the k scores. (James et al. 2021)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

k = 5
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.2))
for row in range(k):                       # one round per row
    for col in range(k):                   # the k folds
        val = (col == row)
        ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((col, k - 1 - row), 0.96, 0.96,
                     facecolor="#d98a29" if val else "#1f3a5f",
                     edgecolor="white", lw=2))
    ax.text(-0.25, k - 1 - row + 0.48, f"round {row+1}", ha="right", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((k + 0.4, k - 1.5), 0.5, 0.5, facecolor="#1f3a5f"))
ax.text(k + 1.05, k - 1.25, "train", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((k + 0.4, k - 2.3), 0.5, 0.5, facecolor="#d98a29"))
ax.text(k + 1.05, k - 2.05, "validate", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlim(-1.5, k + 2.5); ax.set_ylim(-0.3, k + 0.3)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("5-fold cross-validation: every fold is held out once")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: 5-fold cross-validation. The data is split into 5 folds. In each of 5 rounds, one fold (orange) is held out for validation while the model trains on the other four (blue). Every observation is used for validation exactly once; the 5 validation scores are averaged into one honest test-error estimate. No single split decides the answer.





Why k = 5 or 10? They are the standard compromise. (James et al. 2021) The extreme case k = n is leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) — fit on n-1 points, test on the one left out, repeat n times. LOOCV has very low bias (each fit uses almost all the data) and is perfectly stable (no random splits), but two costs push practitioners toward 5 or 10 folds: (James et al. 2021)

Computation — LOOCV fits the model n times (though OLS has a magic closed-form shortcut).
Higher variance of the estimate — a subtle point. LOOCV’s n models are trained on nearly identical data, so their errors are highly correlated, and averaging strongly-correlated quantities yields a higher-variance average than averaging the 5 or 10 less-correlated estimates of k-fold. So 10-fold often estimates test error better than LOOCV. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
section: Using CV to tune the model
text:
Using CV to tune the model
This is what cross-validation is for: pick the flexibility that minimises estimated test error. (James et al. 2021) Compute the CV error across a grid of the tuning knob — polynomial degree, the regularization penalty \lambda, tree depth — and find the bottom of the U-shaped curve.






TipThe one-standard-error rule



When the CV curve is flat near its minimum, the lowest point is barely better than its neighbours and is itself noisy. The one-standard-error rule says: compute the standard error of the CV estimates, and pick the simplest (most parsimonious) model whose CV error is within one standard error of the minimum. (James et al. 2021) You give up a sliver of estimated accuracy for a simpler, more robust model — a principled lean toward parsimony.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
section: The golden rule: keep choices inside the loop
text:
The golden rule: keep choices inside the loop
The single most common — and most damaging — mistake. Any data-dependent choice must be made inside the cross-validation loop. (James et al. 2021) If you use CV to select variables, you must redo the variable selection separately within each training fold, so the validation fold stays genuinely unseen.
The tempting shortcut — screen all the variables (or standardise, or impute) on the whole dataset first, then cross-validate the chosen model — lets the model peek at the answers. The validation folds influenced the choices being “validated,” so the CV score is over-optimistic and won’t generalise. (James et al. 2021) If a decision touches the data, it goes inside the loop. No exceptions.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Pre-processing before splitting. Standardising, imputing, or selecting variables on the full dataset leaks the held-out data into training. Fit every data-dependent step on the training fold only. (James et al. 2021)
Touching the test set more than once. The moment you use the test set to make a choice, it becomes a validation set and stops being an honest estimate. Hold it sacred for the final number. (James et al. 2021)
Trusting a single train/validation split. Its estimate is high-variance and pessimistic; use k-fold to average over splits and use all the data. (James et al. 2021)
Assuming LOOCV is best because it has lowest bias. Its highly-correlated fits give a higher-variance estimate than 10-fold; the standard k=5 or 10 usually wins. (James et al. 2021)
Reporting the CV error of the selected model as its test error. Having chosen the model to minimise CV error, that minimum is itself optimistic — the honest number comes from the untouched test set.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — training error underestimates test error; the train/validation/test split and the sacred test set; the validation-set approach (Auto data, 196/196 split, linear MSE 23.30 vs quadratic 18.90) and its two drawbacks; LOOCV (procedure, low bias, stability, the OLS shortcut); k-fold CV with k=5,10 and the correlation argument for its lower-variance estimate; CV for tuning flexibility/\lambda and the one-standard-error rule; the rule that data-dependent choices belong inside the CV loop.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the applied train/test idiom and data leakage; the exam-cheating intuition for evaluating on training data.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The k-fold estimate. Partition the indices into folds C_1,\dots,C_k of size \approx n/k. Let \hat f^{-\kappa} be the model fit with fold \kappa removed. Then

\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{\kappa=1}^{k} \text{MSE}_\kappa,
\qquad \text{MSE}_\kappa = \frac{1}{|C_\kappa|}\sum_{i\in C_\kappa}\big(y_i - \hat f^{-\kappa}(x_i)\big)^2 .

LOOCV is the case k=n, |C_\kappa|=1.
LOOCV’s magic shortcut (OLS). For linear models fit by least squares, LOOCV needs only one fit:

\text{CV}_{(n)} = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{y_i-\hat y_i}{1-h_i}\right)^2,

where \hat y_i is the full-sample fitted value and h_i is observation i’s leverage. The leverage inflates each residual by exactly the amount that point pulled the fit toward itself — recovering the leave-one-out error without refitting.
Why LOOCV has higher variance. The estimate is a mean of n fold-errors. For correlated terms, \operatorname{Var}(\bar T) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\big(1+(n-1)\bar\rho\big) — the same clustering inflation (Moulton) logic. LOOCV’s fits are almost identical, so \bar\rho is large and the average is high-variance; k-fold’s fits overlap less, lowering \bar\rho and the variance of the estimate.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
Connections

Builds on: the bias–variance trade-off (CV estimates the test error whose minimum is the sweet spot), explaining vs predicting (out-of-sample is the only honest scorecard), and the bootstrap / resampling (CV is a resampling method).
Leads to: regularization (CV chooses the penalty \lambda) and trees & forests (CV tunes depth and the number of trees).
Reappears in: causal machine learning, where cross-fitting (sample-splitting) is what keeps ML-estimated nuisances from biasing the treatment effect.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text: Half of supervised learning predicts a label, not a number: voted or not, defaulted or not, relapsed or not. The squared-error machinery of bias–variance still governs the trade-offs, but the scoreboard changes. A single accuracy number lies when one class is rare; the honest picture is a confusion matrix and the sensitivity–specificity trade it encodes. And just as regression had an irreducible-error floor, classification has the Bayes error rate — the best any classifier could ever do.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Why it matters
Everything so far measured a predictor by mean squared error — a regression loss. But the outcomes social scientists most often predict are categorical: a yes/no, a class, a diagnosis. (James et al. 2021) Classification is arguably more common than regression in applied social science — predicting loan defaults, vote choice, dropout, recidivism, clinical diagnosis — and it changes two things at once: the loss you minimise and the metrics you report. (Bruce et al. 2020) The modelling philosophy is unchanged — fit on a training set, judge on unseen data, tune flexibility by the bias–variance trade — but you need a new vocabulary to say what “good” means when there is no residual to square.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
section: The Bayes classifier: the floor for labels
text:
The Bayes classifier: the floor for labels
Regression had irreducible error \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon) — a floor no model beats. (James et al. 2021) Classification has the exact analog: the Bayes classifier. Given the predictors X=x_0, it assigns the class with the highest conditional probability \Pr(Y=j \mid X=x_0) — for two classes, predict 1 whenever \Pr(Y=1\mid X=x_0) > 0.5. (James et al. 2021) This rule is unbeatable: no classifier can have a lower expected error rate.
Its error — the Bayes error rate — is the floor, and it is usually above zero because the classes overlap in the population: at the same x_0 you sometimes see a 1 and sometimes a 0, so even perfect knowledge of the probabilities cannot separate them. (James et al. 2021) This is the lesson to carry: just as no model predicts noise, no classifier achieves 100% accuracy when classes overlap — and chasing it means overfitting. The catch is that the Bayes rule needs the true \Pr(Y\mid X), which we never have; real methods estimate it (as logistic regression does directly) or approximate the rule by looking at nearby points — which is exactly what K-nearest neighbours does.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
K-nearest neighbours: the simplest learner
