K-nearest neighbours (KNN) is the canonical non-parametric classifier and the natural first method to meet. (James et al. 2021) It makes no assumption about the shape of the boundary. To classify a new point x_0: find the K training observations closest to it, and take a majority vote of their labels. (James et al. 2021) That is the whole algorithm — it estimates the Bayes rule locally, using the neighbourhood’s class proportions as a stand-in for \Pr(Y=j\mid X=x_0).
KNN is worth knowing not because it always wins but because it makes two universal lessons concrete:

K is a flexibility dial. K=1 is maximally flexible — every point gets its nearest neighbour’s label, carving a jagged, overfit boundary (low bias, high variance). A large K averages over a wide neighbourhood, giving a smooth, rigid boundary (high bias, low variance). (James et al. 2021) The bias–variance U-curve reappears, now indexed by 1/K.
Scaling is not optional. Because KNN measures distance, a predictor on a large numeric scale (income in dollars) swamps one on a small scale (years of age) — the large-scale variable silently dominates “closeness.” You must standardise the predictors first so each contributes comparably. (James et al. 2021; Bruce et al. 2020) (This distance-sensitivity is why KNN degrades in high dimensions, where “nearest” neighbours are not actually near — the curse of dimensionality.)
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
section: The confusion matrix: the heart of classification metrics
text:
The confusion matrix: the heart of classification metrics
You cannot summarise a classifier with one number. Start from the confusion matrix — for a binary outcome, the 2\times2 table cross-tabulating predictions against truth: (Bruce et al. 2020)




Actual 1 (positive)
Actual 0 (negative)




Predicted 1
True Positive (TP)
False Positive (FP)


Predicted 0
False Negative (FN)
True Negative (TN)



Every metric is a ratio drawn from this table. The reason it matters — and the reason raw accuracy (\text{TP}+\text{TN})/n is dangerous — is the rare-class problem (the accuracy paradox). (Bruce et al. 2020) If only 1% of students attempt self-harm, a model that predicts “never” for everyone is 99% accurate and completely useless: it never flags the cases you built it to catch. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Accuracy hides the failure; the confusion matrix exposes it.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Sensitivity, specificity, precision
The table yields the metrics that actually matter, each answering a different question: (Bruce et al. 2020)

Sensitivity (= recall, the true-positive rate) =\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}} — of the actual positives, what fraction did we catch? How good are we at finding the 1s?
Specificity (the true-negative rate) =\dfrac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN}+\text{FP}} — of the actual negatives, what fraction did we correctly clear?
Precision (positive predictive value) =\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}} — when we predict positive, how often are we right?

Which one rules depends on the costs of the two errors, and in social science they are rarely equal. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Missing a high-risk student (a false negative) may be far costlier than offering unneeded tutoring to a safe one (a false positive) — so you would prize sensitivity even at the price of precision. A spam filter inverts the costs: a false positive (a real email lost to the spam folder) hurts more, so you prize precision. The metric is a policy choice, not a statistical default.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
section: The threshold and the ROC curve
text:
The threshold and the ROC curve
Here is the move that ties it together. A classifier like logistic regression outputs a probability, and turning that into a label requires a cutoff. The default 0.5 is just the Bayes rule under equal costs — but you can move it. (Bruce et al. 2020) Lower the threshold and you flag more positives: sensitivity rises, but more false alarms drop specificity. Raise it and the reverse. Sensitivity and specificity are on a see-saw, and the threshold is the fulcrum.
Plot that entire trade-off and you get the ROC curve (Receiver Operating Characteristic): the true-positive rate against the false-positive rate (1-\text{specificity}) as the threshold sweeps from high to low. (Bruce et al. 2020) A useless (coin-flip) classifier rides the 45° diagonal; a good one bows toward the top-left corner (high sensitivity and high specificity at once). The single summary is the AUC — the Area Under the Curve: 0.5 is no better than chance, 1.0 is perfect separation. (Bruce et al. 2020) AUC has a clean interpretation — the probability that the model scores a randomly chosen positive above a randomly chosen negative — and, crucially, it judges the model across all thresholds at once, so you compare classifiers before committing to an operating point.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 2000
# Latent scores: positives shifted up, but the classes OVERLAP (Bayes error > 0).
neg = rng.normal(0.0, 1.0, n)
pos = rng.normal(1.6, 1.0, n)
scores = np.concatenate([neg, pos])
labels = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])

# Sweep thresholds high -> low, build the ROC by confusion-matrix counts.
thr = np.linspace(scores.max() + 0.5, scores.min() - 0.5, 250)
tpr = np.array([(pos >= t).mean() for t in thr])   # sensitivity
fpr = np.array([(neg >= t).mean() for t in thr])   # 1 - specificity
auc = np.sum(np.diff(fpr) * (tpr[:-1] + tpr[1:]) / 2)  # area under ROC (trapezoid)

t0 = 0.8                                            # one illustrative cutoff
tpr0, fpr0 = (pos >= t0).mean(), (neg >= t0).mean()

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.6, 3.7))

# Left: overlapping score distributions with a threshold.
axL.hist(neg, bins=40, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.7, label="actual 0")
axL.hist(pos, bins=40, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.6, label="actual 1")
axL.axvline(t0, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.6)
axL.text(t0 + 0.1, axL.get_ylim()[1] * 0.9, "threshold", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axL.set_xlabel("model score"); axL.set_ylabel("count")
axL.set_title("Overlapping scores"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)

# Right: the ROC curve.
axR.plot(fpr, tpr, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2)
axR.fill_between(fpr, tpr, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.10)
axR.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.3)
axR.plot(fpr0, tpr0, "o", color="#c0392b", ms=7)
axR.text(fpr0 + 0.03, tpr0 - 0.08, "this cutoff", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axR.text(0.55, 0.25, f"AUC = {auc:.2f}", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
axR.set_xlabel("false-positive rate (1 − specificity)")
axR.set_ylabel("true-positive rate (sensitivity)")
axR.set_title("ROC curve"); axR.set_xlim(0, 1); axR.set_ylim(0, 1)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: From overlapping scores to the ROC curve. Left: a classifier outputs higher scores for positives (green) than negatives (grey), but the distributions overlap, so no single cutoff separates them perfectly — that overlap is the Bayes error made visible. Sliding the threshold (dashed line) trades false negatives for false positives. Right: sweeping every threshold traces the ROC curve; the area under it (AUC) summarises the model’s separating power across all cutoffs at once. The diagonal is a coin flip.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Validation: same loop, different scoreboard
Does any of the cross-validation machinery change? The process does not: you still split train/validation/test, still use k-fold CV to tune flexibility, and still treat the test set as sacred. (James et al. 2021) Two things differ: (Bruce et al. 2020)

The metric. Replace MSE with a classification loss — the error rate, or better, a probability-aware loss like log-loss (cross-entropy), which penalises confident mistakes more than hesitant ones and rewards well-calibrated probabilities.
The threshold is itself a tuning choice. Pick the operating point to match your misclassification costs — and, like every data-driven choice, do it inside the CV loop, never on the test set, or you inherit the same optimism/leakage that inflates every other accuracy estimate. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting accuracy on imbalanced data. With a rare positive, “95% accurate” can mean “never finds the thing.” Always show the confusion matrix and the sensitivity for the class you care about. (Bruce et al. 2020; Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Treating 0.5 as sacred. The cutoff is a policy lever set by the relative cost of false negatives vs false positives — not a fixed constant. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Forgetting to scale before KNN (or any distance method). Unstandardised predictors let the largest-scale variable dictate “nearness,” silently. (James et al. 2021)
Chasing zero error. When classes overlap, the Bayes error rate is a hard floor; a classifier near it is finished, not deficient. Pushing past it fits noise. (James et al. 2021)
Tuning the threshold on the test set. That is leakage by another name. Choose it inside cross-validation. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — the classification setting and the error rate; the Bayes classifier (predict the most probable class) and the Bayes error rate as the irreducible floor created by overlapping classes; KNN (majority vote of the K nearest points), K as a flexibility/bias–variance dial, the need to standardise, and the curse of dimensionality.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the confusion matrix as the heart of classification metrics; sensitivity/recall, specificity, precision; the rare-class (accuracy) problem; thresholds, the ROC curve, and AUC.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, Machine Learning for Social & Behavioral Research — why the costs of false positives vs false negatives are unequal in social science, the accuracy paradox on rare outcomes, log-loss, and keeping the threshold/metric choices inside the validation loop.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The Bayes rule and its error. For classes j, the Bayes classifier is 
\hat C_{\text{Bayes}}(x_0) = \arg\max_{j}\ \Pr(Y=j \mid X=x_0).
 At a point x_0 its error is 1-\max_j \Pr(Y=j\mid X=x_0), and the overall Bayes error rate is the average of this over the distribution of X: 
1 - \mathbb{E}_X\!\Big[\max_j \Pr(Y=j\mid X)\Big].
 It is zero only if some class has probability 1 everywhere — i.e. the classes never overlap. KNN estimates the inner probability by the local frequency \hat\Pr(Y=j\mid X=x_0)=\frac1K\sum_{i\in \mathcal N_K(x_0)} \mathbb 1(y_i=j) over the K nearest neighbours \mathcal N_K(x_0), then applies the same \arg\max. As n\to\infty with K growing slowly, KNN’s error approaches the Bayes rate.
Log-loss (cross-entropy). For predicted probabilities \hat p_i=\hat\Pr(Y_i=1\mid X_i) and labels y_i\in\{0,1\}, 
\text{log-loss} = -\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \big[y_i\ln \hat p_i + (1-y_i)\ln(1-\hat p_i)\big].
 It is minimised (at 0) only by confident, correct probabilities and explodes when the model is confidently wrong (\hat p_i\to0 while y_i=1). This is exactly the negative log-likelihood that logistic regression maximises — the bridge from the explanatory model to the predictive scoreboard.
AUC as a probability. The area under the ROC equals \Pr\big(\hat s(X^+) > \hat s(X^-)\big) — the chance the model scores a random positive above a random negative — which is the Mann–Whitney U statistic rescaled. A coin flip gives 0.5; perfect ranking gives 1.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Connections

Builds on: bias–variance (the Bayes error rate is the classification floor, K is its flexibility dial) and logistic regression (the probability model whose output a threshold turns into a label).
Leads to: cross-validation (the same tuning loop, with a classification metric) and trees & forests (classifiers split on Gini/entropy, scored by these same metrics).
Reappears in: interpretability & ML and the prediction problems of Part V, where propensity scores are themselves classifiers whose calibration matters.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text: Once a series is stationary, forecasting becomes tractable. The ARIMA family says the future is a blend of two memories — the series’ own past values (AR) and its past shocks (MA) — applied to a differenced (I) series. The Box–Jenkins method is the disciplined cycle for choosing, fitting, and checking such a model, and its forecasts come with one unavoidable honesty: the further ahead you look, the less you know.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Why it matters
Forecasting is the explicit goal for vast swaths of applied time-series work — central banks, firms, governments all need a number for next quarter. (Enders 2014) ARIMA models are the classical workhorse, and even where modern ML competes, the ARIMA concepts — autoregression, the memory of shocks, differencing, and widening forecast uncertainty — remain the vocabulary. This entry turns the stationarity foundations into actual predictions, and brings the bias–variance and parsimony themes of the chapter to bear on choosing a model.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
section: The building blocks: AR, MA, ARMA
text:
The building blocks: AR, MA, ARMA
Autoregressive — AR(p). Model today as a regression on its own recent past: (Enders 2014)

y_t = a_0 + a_1 y_{t-1} + a_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + a_p y_{t-p} + \epsilon_t .

The AR(1) case y_t = a_0 + a_1 y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t depends only on yesterday. It is stationary iff |a_1| < 1; at a_1 = 1 it is the random walk. (Enders 2014) An AR process has infinite memory — a shock propagates forward through the chain of lagged y’s, decaying as a_1^h.
Moving average — MA(q). Model today as a weighted sum of recent shocks: (Enders 2014)

y_t = \epsilon_t + \beta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \cdots + \beta_q \epsilon_{t-q} .

The contrast with AR is the key intuition: MA has finite memory — a shock \epsilon_t affects the series for exactly q periods and then vanishes completely. (Enders 2014)
ARMA(p,q) combines both, often fitting a series with fewer total parameters than a pure AR or MA would need — the parsimony principle at work: (Enders 2014)

y_t = a_0 + \sum_{i=1}^p a_i\,y_{t-i} + \epsilon_t + \sum_{i=1}^q \beta_i\,\epsilon_{t-i}.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
ARIMA: adding the “I” for integrated
Most economic series are non-stationary in levels — they trend. (Enders 2014) The I in ARIMA handles that: a series is integrated of order d (I(d)) if it must be differenced d times to become stationary. So ARIMA(p,d,q) = difference the series d times (usually d = 1 for economic data), then fit an ARMA(p,q) to the stationary result \Delta^d y_t. (Enders 2014) The whole pipeline: difference to stationarity, model the dependence, forecast, then un-difference.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
The Box–Jenkins method
Box and Jenkins gave the field its disciplined, three-stage iterative cycle for finding the right model. (Enders 2014)
Stage 1 — Identification. On the stationary series, read the ACF and partial ACF (PACF) to propose tentative orders p and q. The PACF at lag s is the correlation between y_t and y_{t-s} after removing the linear influence of the intervening lags y_{t-1},\dots,y_{t-s+1} — it isolates the direct dependence at lag s, whereas the ordinary ACF blends direct and indirect channels together. The signature patterns: (Enders 2014)








Process
ACF
PACF




AR(p)
decays geometrically (smoothly or oscillating)
cuts off after lag p


MA(q)
cuts off after lag q
decays geometrically


ARMA(p,q)
decays (after lag q)
decays (after lag p)



The mnemonic: the PACF fingerprints AR order; the ACF fingerprints MA order.
Stage 2 — Estimation. Fit the tentative model by maximum likelihood or non-linear least squares. Enders stresses the principle of parsimony — the simplest adequate model, to avoid overfitting the sample’s idiosyncrasies — and compares candidates with the AIC and BIC/SBC information criteria (lower is better; both reward fit and penalise extra parameters). (Enders 2014)
Stage 3 — Diagnostic checking. A good model leaves residuals that are white noise — no structure left to exploit. The Ljung–Box Q-test checks whether a batch of residual autocorrelations is jointly non-zero; if it is, the model is inadequate and you loop back to Stage 1. (Enders 2014)

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
section: Forecasting: mean reversion and widening intervals
text:
Forecasting: mean reversion and widening intervals
With a fitted model, point forecasts come from iterating the conditional expectation forward — for AR(1), the one-step forecast is \mathbb{E}_t[y_{t+1}] = a_0 + a_1 y_t, then feed it back in for two steps, and so on. (Enders 2014) Two properties define how those forecasts behave:

Mean reversion. For any stationary ARMA model, the forecast \mathbb{E}_t[y_{t+j}] converges to the series’ unconditional mean as the horizon j grows. (Enders 2014) Far enough out, your best guess is simply “the long-run average” — the dependence that made the near future predictable has decayed away.
Widening intervals. Forecasts are (approximately) unbiased but less certain the further out you look: each additional period adds a new unknown shock \epsilon_{t+j} to the accumulated error, so the forecast-error variance grows with the horizon. (Enders 2014)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
a1, mu, sigma = 0.7, 10.0, 1.0
a0 = mu * (1 - a1)
T = 80
y = np.empty(T); y[0] = mu
for t in range(1, T):
    y[t] = a0 + a1 * y[t-1] + rng.normal(0, sigma)

H = 30
fc = np.empty(H); last = y[-1]
for h in range(H):
    last = a0 + a1 * last
    fc[h] = last
# forecast-error variance at horizon h+1: sigma^2 * (1-a1^{2(h+1)})/(1-a1^2)
var_h = sigma**2 * (1 - a1**(2*(np.arange(1, H+1)))) / (1 - a1**2)
band = 1.96 * np.sqrt(var_h)

t_hist = np.arange(T); t_fut = np.arange(T, T + H)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.5, 4.0))
ax.plot(t_hist, y, color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.4, label="observed series")
ax.axhline(mu, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.3, label="unconditional mean")
ax.plot(t_fut, fc, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, label="point forecast")
ax.fill_between(t_fut, fc - band, fc + band, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.18,
                label="95% forecast interval")
ax.axvline(T - 0.5, color="#c0392b", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("AR(1) forecast: mean reversion + widening uncertainty")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Forecasting a stationary AR(1) series (a₁ = 0.7, mean 10). The point forecast (blue) reverts toward the unconditional mean (grey dashed) as the horizon extends, and the 95% forecast interval (shaded) widens with each step — each future period adds a fresh unknown shock. For a stationary series the band widens toward a constant (the unconditional spread); for a random walk it would grow without bound.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Honesty about the method
Enders is candid that Box–Jenkins is “a blend of skill, art, and perseverance,” not a mechanical science. (Enders 2014) The ambiguities are real: in his interest-rate-spread analysis, AIC and BIC disagree — one favouring an AR(7), the other an AR(6) — and only judgment resolves it. (Enders 2014) You must also watch for common factors — AR and MA terms that algebraically cancel. Writing L for the lag operator, which shifts a series back one period (Ly_t=y_{t-1}, so L^k y_t = y_{t-k}), a model such as (1-0.5L)y_t = (1-0.5L)\epsilon_t carries the same factor on both sides; it cancels to plain white noise y_t=\epsilon_t, signalling an over-parameterised model that a simpler one would capture. (Enders 2014) The discipline is the cycle; the craft is the choices within it.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Fitting ARMA to a non-stationary series. Difference to stationarity first (the “I”); modelling trending levels invites spurious results. (Enders 2014)
Over-fitting with too many lags. A high-order model fits the sample’s noise and forecasts worse. Favour parsimony; let AIC/BIC and the test of residual whiteness discipline p and q. (Enders 2014)
Trusting long-horizon point forecasts. Stationary forecasts revert to the mean, so far-out predictions carry little information beyond the average — and their intervals are wide. Report the interval, not just the point. (Enders 2014)
Skipping diagnostic checking. If the residuals aren’t white noise (Ljung–Box rejects), the model is missing structure — respecify, don’t forecast. (Enders 2014)
Ignoring the common-factor trap. Cancelling AR and MA roots mean you’ve over-parameterised; simplify. (Enders 2014)

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Enders, Applied Econometric Time Series — the AR(p) model and the AR(1) stationarity condition |a_1|<1; the MA(q) model and its finite memory; ARMA parsimony; ARIMA(p,d,q) and the integrated/differencing component; the Box–Jenkins three stages (ACF/PACF identification with the cut-off/decay rules, ML/NLS estimation, parsimony and AIC/BIC, diagnostic checking with Ljung–Box); forecasting by iterated conditional expectation, mean reversion to the unconditional mean, and forecast-error variance growing with the horizon; the “skill, art, and perseverance” caveat, the interest-rate-spread AIC-vs-BIC ambiguity, and the common-factor problem.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

AR(1) moments. For |a_1|<1, taking expectations of y_t=a_0+a_1y_{t-1}+\epsilon_t gives the unconditional mean \mu = a_0/(1-a_1), and the variance \gamma_0=\sigma^2/(1-a_1^2). The ACF is \rho_s = a_1^{\,s} — geometric decay, the AR fingerprint.
Why intervals widen (AR(1)). Write y_{t+h}-\mu = a_1^h(y_t-\mu) + \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} a_1^{i}\epsilon_{t+h-i}. The conditional forecast is \mathbb{E}_t[y_{t+h}] = \mu + a_1^h(y_t-\mu) (→ \mu as h\to\infty: mean reversion), and the forecast-error variance is

\operatorname{Var}_t(y_{t+h}) = \sigma^2\sum_{i=0}^{h-1} a_1^{2i} = \sigma^2\,\frac{1-a_1^{2h}}{1-a_1^2},

which increases in h and, for |a_1|<1, converges to the unconditional variance \sigma^2/(1-a_1^2) — so the band widens toward a constant. For a unit root (a_1=1) the sum is h\sigma^2, growing without bound (\sqrt h intervals) — the forecasting signature of non-stationarity.
Information criteria. \text{AIC} = -2\ln L + 2k and \text{BIC} = -2\ln L + k\ln n for k parameters and likelihood L. BIC’s heavier penalty (\ln n > 2 for n>7) favours smaller models — the usual source of the AIC-vs-BIC disagreement Enders flags.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Connections

Builds on: time-series fundamentals (stationarity, the ACF, differencing, white noise), maximum likelihood (estimation), and cross-validation / parsimony (AIC/BIC as the model-selection analogue).
Closes: Part IV. You now have both the predictive paradigm (bias–variance, CV, regularization, ensembles, interpretability) and the dedicated time-series toolkit.
Leads to: Part V · Causal Inference — including time-series causal designs (difference-in-differences, event studies, synthetic control) that rest on these foundations.

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text: Factor analysis recovers latent constructs from indicators; regression relates variables you can measure. Structural equation modeling (SEM) fuses them: a measurement model that builds latent constructs from observed items, plus a structural model of regression-like paths among those latents. The reward is twofold — you can estimate relationships between unobservable constructs while explicitly modelling the measurement error, and you get the gold-standard framework for mediation.

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text:
Why it matters
Ordinary regression assumes your variables are measured perfectly — and measurement error attenuates coefficients when they aren’t. SEM is the comprehensive framework that models “complex systems of relationships simultaneously,” (Jacobucci et al. 2023) separating the construct from its noisy measurement so the error doesn’t bias the relationships you care about. It is the backbone of psychometrics, much of psychology and sociology, and modern measurement-aware modelling.

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text:
Two models in one
SEM is the union of two pieces: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

The measurement model — a CFA that defines each latent construct from its observed indicators (the loadings).
The structural model — the regression-like paths among the latent variables (and any observed ones): the substantive hypotheses about what affects what.

Conventionally drawn as a path diagram: circles/ellipses are latent variables, squares/rectangles are observed indicators, and arrows are directed effects. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Ellipse, FancyArrowPatch

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.4, 4.2))
ax.set_xlim(0, 10); ax.set_ylim(0, 6); ax.axis("off")

def latent(x, y, label):
    ax.add_patch(Ellipse((x, y), 1.7, 1.0, fc="#dce7f0", ec="#1f3a5f", lw=1.6))
    ax.text(x, y, label, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9.5, fontweight="bold")

def observed(x, y, label):
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x-0.55, y-0.32), 1.1, 0.64, fc="#e7f0e9", ec="#3f8f6f", lw=1.3))
    ax.text(x, y, label, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8)

def arrow(p, q, color="#5a6b7a", style="-|>"):
    ax.add_patch(FancyArrowPatch(p, q, arrowstyle=style, mutation_scale=12, color=color, lw=1.6))

# Left latent (SES) with 3 indicators
latent(2.2, 3, "SES\n(latent)")
for i, yy in enumerate([4.6, 3.0, 1.4]):
    observed(0.4, yy, ["income", "educ", "occup"][i]); arrow((2.0, 3+ (yy-3)*0.55), (0.95, yy))
# Right latent (Wellbeing) with 3 indicators
latent(7.8, 3, "Wellbeing\n(latent)")
for i, yy in enumerate([4.6, 3.0, 1.4]):
    observed(9.6, yy, ["health", "mood", "satis"][i]); arrow((8.0, 3+(yy-3)*0.55), (9.05, yy))
# Structural path between latents
arrow((3.1, 3), (6.9, 3), color="#c0392b")
ax.text(5.0, 3.3, "structural path", ha="center", fontsize=9, color="#c0392b", fontweight="bold")
ax.text(5.0, 5.6, "measurement model (loadings) + structural model (path among latents)",
        ha="center", fontsize=8.5, style="italic", color="#1f3a5f")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: An SEM path diagram. Two latent constructs (ellipses) — each measured by three observed indicators (squares) via factor loadings — are connected by a structural path (the hypothesis: ‘Socioeconomic status → Wellbeing’). The measurement model is the loadings; the structural model is the path between latents. SEM estimates both at once, and the indicators’ unique variance absorbs measurement error so it doesn’t bias the structural path.

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text:
Judging the model: fit indices
Because SEM tries to reproduce the entire observed covariance matrix, fit is judged by several indices: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Chi-square — tests for “perfect” fit (and is famously sensitive to sample size).
CFI / TLI (Comparative / Tucker–Lewis) — values > 0.95 indicate good fit.
RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation) — values < 0.05 suggest good fit.

No single index is decisive; you read them together with theory and the residuals.

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text:
Why SEM is powerful
Two capabilities set it apart: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

It models measurement error explicitly, as each indicator’s “unique variance” — so the error stays out of the construct, preventing it from biasing the relationships between latents. This is the principled fix for the attenuation ordinary regression suffers.
It is the gold standard for mediation, decomposing an effect into direct and indirect paths within one unified system — though the causal cautions of the DAG chapter still apply (a fitted path is not a verified cause).

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text:
The modern ML bridge
Jacobucci et al. integrate machine learning into SEM for “theory-guided exploration” of complex data: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Regularized SEM (RegSEM) — adds Lasso/Ridge penalties to the SEM estimator, shrinking inessential paths toward zero for automated variable selection and less overfitting, so models generalise better. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Exploratory SEM (ESEM) — a hybrid using an EFA-style measurement model (allowing cross-loadings) within a structural SEM. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
SEM Trees & Forests — pair decision trees/forests with SEM to find heterogeneity: subgroups where the model’s parameters (factor means, paths) differ, with forest-based variable importance. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Their stance is the ML-track refrain: “you are smarter than your data” — these tools integrate algorithms, data, and theory, not replace it. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading a fitted path as causal. SEM estimates the paths you drew; a good fit doesn’t validate the causal structure — the DAG cautions hold. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Chasing fit indices. Tweaking a model until CFI > 0.95 on one dataset is overfitting; cross-validate or pre-register the structure. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Ignoring the measurement model. Poor indicators make the structural paths meaningless — garbage constructs in, garbage relationships out. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Over-trusting mediation. Decomposing direct/indirect effects assumes the mediator is unconfounded — a strong, separate assumption. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, ML for Social and Behavioral Research — SEM as measurement model (CFA) + structural model (paths among latents); path diagrams (circles = latent, squares = observed); fit indices (chi-square, CFI/TLI > 0.95, RMSEA < 0.05); modelling measurement error as unique variance and SEM as the mediation gold standard; and the ML bridge — RegSEM (Lasso/Ridge penalties), ESEM, and SEM trees/forests for heterogeneity — “you are smarter than your data.”

title: Structural Equation Modeling
text:
Connections

Builds on: factor analysis (the measurement model), multiple regression (the structural paths), and measurement error (what it fixes).
Bridges to: regularization and trees/forests via RegSEM and SEM trees, and to the ML track.
Cautioned by: DAGs & mediation — a path is not a proven cause.

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text: The Gauss–Markov assumptions are rarely exactly true. The good news: the most common violations — non-constant error variance, clustered data, correlated regressors — usually leave the coefficients unbiased and damage only the standard errors. So diagnostics is mostly about detecting those violations and repairing the inference, not throwing out the model. This entry is the practitioner’s checklist.

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Why it matters
A regression can be unbiased and still lie to you — not through its coefficients, but through its standard errors. (Wooldridge 2019) If the SEs are wrong, every t-test, p-value, and confidence interval built on them is wrong, and you’ll claim significance you haven’t earned (or miss effects that are real). The reassuring theme of this entry: heteroskedasticity, clustering, and multicollinearity do not bias OLS — they distort precision — so the fixes are about honest uncertainty, and they’re cheap. Knowing which problems threaten the coefficients (functional-form misspecification, influential outliers) versus only the standard errors is the core diagnostic skill.

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Heteroskedasticity
Heteroskedasticity is non-constant error variance: \operatorname{Var}(u\mid x) changes with the regressors, violating MLR.5. (Wooldridge 2019) It is the rule, not the exception, in social data — spending varies more among rich households than poor ones, test scores more in big schools than small.
Consequences. OLS stays unbiased and consistent — the coefficients are fine. But OLS is no longer BLUE, and the usual standard-error formulas are invalid, so t-tests and intervals are unreliable even in large samples. (Wooldridge 2019)
Detection. (Wooldridge 2019; Ashley 2012)

Graphically: plot residuals against fitted values and look for a fan or cone — scatter that widens (or narrows) as the fitted value grows.
Breusch–Pagan test: regress the squared residuals \hat u^2 on the regressors; a significant F/LM statistic rejects homoskedasticity.
White test: the same idea with squares and cross-products added (a popular shortcut regresses \hat u^2 on \hat y and \hat y^2).



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(6)
n = 300
x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
fitted = 2 + 1.2 * x

resid_homo = rng.normal(0, 1.5, n)                 # constant variance
resid_hetero = rng.normal(0, 0.3 + 0.45 * x)       # variance grows with x (fan)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.6), sharey=True)
for ax, resid, title in [(axL, resid_homo, "Homoskedastic: even band"),
                         (axR, resid_hetero, "Heteroskedastic: a widening fan")]:
    ax.scatter(fitted, resid, s=12, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.55)
    ax.axhline(0, color="#c0392b", lw=1.3)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("fitted value  ŷ")
axL.set_ylabel("residual  û")
fig.suptitle("Residuals vs fitted — the first plot to draw", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The canonical diagnostic: residuals vs fitted values. Left (homoskedastic) — an even band of constant width; the usual standard errors are valid. Right (heteroskedastic) — a fan that widens with the fitted value; OLS is still unbiased, but the usual standard errors are wrong and you need robust ones.





The fix. Use heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors (White / Huber–White / “robust” SEs), valid in large samples whatever the form of the heteroskedasticity. (Wooldridge 2019) They cost nothing and change only the SEs, not the coefficients — which is why they are now the default in much professional research: when in doubt, report robust SEs. (Wooldridge 2019) (Ashley’s example: a regression of state death rates on marriage rates shows far wider scatter for populous states — severe heteroskedasticity that invalidates the naive SEs. (Ashley 2012))

Weighted and generalized least squares
Robust SEs correct the inference but leave OLS no longer efficient — under heteroskedasticity OLS is unbiased but not BLUE. The classical alternative restores efficiency: if you know (or can model) how the error variance changes, weighted least squares (WLS) down-weights the noisy, high-variance observations and up-weights the precise ones. (Wooldridge 2019) WLS minimises a weighted sum of squared residuals, with each observation weighted by the inverse of its error variance 1/\sigma_i^2 — so a point with twice the variance counts half as much. It is the special case of generalized least squares (GLS) for a diagonal (uncorrelated-but-unequal) error structure, and under the assumed variance form it is BLUE. In practice \sigma_i^2 is unknown and must be estimated first (model the variance, then weight), giving feasible GLS (FGLS). (Wooldridge 2019)
The modern trade-off: WLS/GLS is more efficient if you get the variance model right, but inconsistent for the SEs if you get it wrong; robust SEs on plain OLS are less efficient but safe. Today most applied researchers prefer OLS-with-robust-SEs for its robustness, reserving WLS/GLS for cases where the variance structure is genuinely known (e.g., grouped/averaged data, where the variance scales with group size). (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Clustered standard errors
A cousin of heteroskedasticity. When observations fall into clusters — students within classrooms, repeated measurements on the same person, households within villages — the errors are correlated within each cluster because they share unobserved influences (the same teacher, the same person). (Ashley 2012) Ordinary SEs assume independence, so they come out too small, producing over-optimistic t-statistics and false significance. (Ashley 2012)
The damage is quantified by the Moulton factor, 1 + (n-1)\rho_e, where \rho_e is the intra-cluster correlation and n the cluster size. (Ashley 2012) The fix is cluster-robust standard errors, which allow arbitrary correlation within clusters while assuming independence across them.






NoteHow big can the clustering inflation be? (STAR)



In the Tennessee STAR class-size experiment, test scores were correlated within classrooms at \rho_e = 0.31. With realistic class sizes the Moulton factor was about 7 — meaning the honest standard errors were roughly \sqrt{7} \approx \mathbf{2.65} times the naive ones. Ignore clustering and you’d overstate your precision by a factor of nearly three. (Ashley 2012)

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Multicollinearity
Multicollinearity is high correlation among regressors. (Wooldridge 2019) Its symptoms are distinctive: large standard errors, coefficients that swing wildly with small data changes, and the tell-tale combination of a high R^2 with few individually significant t-statistics (the model explains a lot collectively, but can’t credit any single variable). (Wooldridge 2019)
Detect it with the variance inflation factor, \text{VIF}_j = 1/(1 - R_j^2), where R_j^2 comes from regressing x_j on the other regressors; a common rule of thumb flags \text{VIF} > 10. (Wooldridge 2019) This is exactly the (1-R_j^2) term in the coefficient-variance formula — multicollinearity is variance inflation, literally.






TipWhy it usually isn’t a crisis



Multicollinearity is a data problem, not an assumption violation — Arthur Goldberger mockingly called it “micronumerosity” (not enough independent information), the same nuisance as a small sample. (Wooldridge 2019) OLS stays unbiased; you just can’t separate the tangled variables precisely. The practical verdict: if your coefficient of interest is still significant despite the collinearity, you don’t need to worry — and “dropping a collinear control” can reintroduce omitted-variable bias, which is worse. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Outliers, leverage, and influence
OLS minimises squared residuals, so it is acutely sensitive to extreme points. (Ashley 2012) Three distinct ideas: (Ashley 2012)

Outlier: an observation whose actual y is far from its fitted \hat y (large studentized residual).
Leverage: an observation with an extreme value of x — it has the potential to swing the slope, like a long lever arm.
Influence: an observation whose removal materially changes the estimates. Cook’s distance combines residual and leverage into one influence score.

A high-leverage outlier is the dangerous combination — one point can tilt the whole line.






NoteNevada and the bridal chapels



In 1980 Census data, Nevada is a wild outlier on marriage rates (its wedding-chapel industry). In a death-on-marriage regression, including Nevada pulled the slope from 0.89 down to 0.82 — a single state moving the headline coefficient. (Ashley 2012) Always check whether one point is driving your result.

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Functional-form misspecification
If you fit a line to a relationship that’s really a curve — omitting a needed log or quadratic — the estimates are biased and inconsistent. (Wooldridge 2019) The Ramsey RESET test is a general check: (Wooldridge 2019)

Estimate your model and get the fitted values \hat y.
Re-estimate with powers of the fitted values (\hat y^2, \hat y^3) added as regressors.
F-test their joint significance. If significant, the original specification is rejected — you’ve missed a nonlinearity.

A caveat Wooldridge stresses: RESET tests functional form only, not omitted variables that happen to be uncorrelated with the included regressors. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating heteroskedasticity/clustering as bias. They corrupt standard errors, not coefficients. The fix is robust/clustered SEs, not a different estimator. (Wooldridge 2019)
Failing to cluster grouped data. Independent-looking SEs on clustered data can overstate precision several-fold (STAR’s ×2.65). Cluster at the level of treatment assignment. (Ashley 2012)
Panicking about multicollinearity. If your key coefficient is significant, collinearity among controls is harmless. Don’t drop a needed control just to lower a VIF — that risks OVB. (Wooldridge 2019)
Ignoring influential points — or deleting them reflexively. Check leverage and Cook’s distance; investigate an influential point (is it an error? a real but special case?) before keeping or dropping it. (Ashley 2012)
Skipping the residual plot. The single residuals-vs-fitted plot reveals heteroskedasticity and functional-form problems in seconds. Draw it every time.

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — heteroskedasticity (unbiased but invalid SEs; Breusch–Pagan and White tests; robust SEs as default); multicollinearity, the VIF =1/(1-R_j^2), “micronumerosity,” and why it’s usually benign; the Ramsey RESET test for functional form.
Ashley, Fundamentals of Applied Econometrics — the residuals-vs-fitted fan; the death-rate / marriage-rate heteroskedasticity and the Nevada outlier (slope 0.89\to0.82); clustered standard errors, the Moulton factor, and the STAR example (\rho_e=0.31, factor \approx7, \times2.65); leverage, influence, and Cook’s distance.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the data-science framing of influential observations and residual diagnostics.

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Robust (White) variance. OLS gives \hat\beta=\beta+(X'X)^{-1}X'u, so \operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta\mid X)=(X'X)^{-1}\big(X'\,\Omega\,X\big)(X'X)^{-1} with \Omega=\operatorname{diag}(\sigma_i^2). Under homoskedasticity \Omega=\sigma^2 I and this collapses to the usual \sigma^2(X'X)^{-1}. The White estimator replaces the unknown \sigma_i^2 with the squared residuals \hat u_i^2: \widehat{\operatorname{Var}}=(X'X)^{-1}\big(\sum_i \hat u_i^2 x_i
x_i'\big)(X'X)^{-1} — consistent for any heteroskedasticity, no model of it required.
Cluster-robust variance. Replace the middle “meat” with a sum over clusters g: \sum_g X_g'\,\hat u_g \hat u_g'\,X_g, allowing arbitrary within-cluster correlation. The Moulton factor 1+(n-1)\rho_e is the ratio of the correct variance to the naive one in the simple equicorrelated case — at \rho_e=0.31, n\approx 20, it is \approx 7.
VIF and the variance formula. From \operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta_j)=\sigma^2/[\text{SST}_j(1-R_j^2)], the factor 1/(1-R_j^2)=\text{VIF}_j is exactly how much the variance is inflated relative to an orthogonal regressor. R_j^2=0.9\Rightarrow\text{VIF}=10\Rightarrow SE inflated by \sqrt{10}\approx3.2.

title: Diagnostics & Robust Inference
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Gauss–Markov assumptions (MLR.5 and MLR.3 are what these diagnostics check), inference for coefficients (the SEs being repaired), and messy real data (outliers and the long tail, now in a model).
Leads to: limited dependent variables (where heteroskedasticity is built in, motivating logit/probit) and what regression can’t tell you (diagnostics fix SEs, but not the bias from a bad design).
Reused throughout: robust and clustered SEs are standard in every causal study in Part V.

title: Quantile Regression
text: OLS estimates the conditional mean. Yet the question is often distributional: does schooling raise the wages of the lowest earners or the highest? Does a training programme cut inequality or just lift the average? Quantile regression answers these by fitting a line to any percentile of the outcome — the median, the 10th, the 90th — revealing effects that differ across the distribution and that the mean conceals entirely.

title: Quantile Regression
text:
Why it matters
Modelling only the mean can hide changes in inequality (Angrist and Pischke 2009). A program might leave average earnings unchanged while compressing or stretching the distribution — and only a tool that looks at the tails can see it. Quantile regression is also robust to outliers and uniquely suited to censored (top-coded) data, making it a staple of labour economics and any field where the shape of an outcome, not just its centre, is the point.

title: Quantile Regression
text:
Beyond the conditional mean
OLS is the “foundation” of econometrics but models only the conditional mean (Angrist and Pischke 2009; Wooldridge 2019). The distributional questions it can’t answer:

How does education affect the 10th percentile (lowest earners) versus the 90th percentile (highest)? (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Does a policy change earnings inequality as well as average earnings? (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

title: Quantile Regression
text:
Median regression (LAD) and robustness
The most famous special case is median regression at \tau=0.5, also called least absolute deviations (LAD) — it estimates the conditional median rather than the mean (Wooldridge 2019). Its great virtue is robustness: OLS squares each residual, giving “increasing importance to large residuals,” so a single outlier can yank the fit; LAD’s objective is linear on either side of zero, so it is far more resilient to outlying observations (Wooldridge 2019).

title: Quantile Regression
section: The general \tau-th quantile and the check function
text:
The general \tau-th quantile and the check function
Quantile regression generalises LAD to any \tau\in(0,1) by minimising an asymmetric absolute loss — the check (or pinball) function (Angrist and Pischke 2009):

\hat\beta_\tau = \arg\min_b \ \sum_i \rho_\tau\!\big(y_i - x_i'b\big),
\qquad
\rho_\tau(u) = \big(\tau - \mathbf 1\{u\le 0\}\big)\,u .

So \rho_\tau weights positive residuals by \tau and negative residuals by 1-\tau. That asymmetry is exactly what “picks out conditional quantiles” — to target the 90th percentile you penalise under-prediction nine times as heavily as over-prediction, pushing the fit up until 90% of points lie below it (Angrist and Pischke 2009). A quantile coefficient describes an effect on the distribution, not on an individual: if a training program raises the 0.10 quantile of earnings, “those who are poor in the regime with training are less poor than the poor would be without it” (Angrist and Pischke 2009).

title: Quantile Regression
section: Effects that change across the distribution
text:
Effects that change across the distribution
The payoff is seeing how the slope itself varies with \tau — the conditional quantile function (CQF), the quantile analogue of the conditional expectation function (Angrist and Pischke 2009):

Under a pure location shift (homoskedastic errors), the slopes are identical across all quantiles — quantile regression tells you nothing new (Angrist and Pischke 2009).
Under heteroskedasticity — e.g. wage variance growing with education — the quantile coefficients “fan out” (Angrist and Pischke 2009). Angrist & Pischke’s census example: in 2000 the returns to schooling were asymmetric, rising sharply in the upper tail relative to the lower (Angrist and Pischke 2009).



Show the code (IRLS quantile regression by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
n = 500
x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
y = 2.0 + 1.0*x + (0.4 + 0.5*x) * rng.normal(size=n)   # spread grows with x
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), x])

def qreg(X, y, tau, iters=200):
    """tau-th quantile regression by iteratively reweighted least squares."""
    beta = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)[0]
    for _ in range(iters):
        r = y - X @ beta
        w = np.where(r >= 0, tau, 1 - tau) / np.maximum(np.abs(r), 1e-4)
        XtW = X.T * w
        beta = np.linalg.solve(XtW @ X, XtW @ y)
    return beta

ols = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)[0]
xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 50); Xs = np.column_stack([np.ones(50), xs])
taus = [0.1, 0.5, 0.9]; colors = ["#c0392b", "#1f8a4c", "#1f4e79"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.3))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=8, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.4)
for tau, c in zip(taus, colors):
    b = qreg(X, y, tau)
    ax.plot(xs, Xs @ b, color=c, lw=2.2, label=f"τ = {tau}  (slope {b[1]:.2f})")
ax.plot(xs, Xs @ ols, color="black", lw=2.0, ls="--", label=f"OLS mean (slope {ols[1]:.2f})")
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("Quantile lines fan out: the effect is bigger at the top")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Quantile regression fit by hand (IRLS) on heteroskedastic data — the spread of y grows with x. OLS (black dashed) tracks the conditional mean and reports one slope. The τ = 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 quantile lines (computed by minimising the check function) FAN OUT: the 0.9 line is steeper than the 0.1 line because the upper tail rises faster than the lower. One mean slope would report a single ‘effect of x’; quantile regression shows the effect is larger at the top of the distribution than the bottom — the heterogeneity OLS averages away.

title: Quantile Regression
text:
Inference and cautions

Inference via the bootstrap. Analytic standard errors are awkward — they depend on the density of the residual at zero — so practitioners routinely use the bootstrap for standard errors and confidence intervals (Angrist and Pischke 2009).
Conditional vs unconditional. It is “surprisingly difficult” to go from conditional quantile effects (for units with given X) to marginal population-quantile effects; linear conditional quantiles do not imply a simple linear population quantile (Angrist and Pischke 2009).
Quantile crossing. Linear quantile fits are an MMSE approximation to the CQF, so estimated lines for different \tau can cross — a predicted lower quantile exceeding a higher one — for some X (Angrist and Pischke 2009).
Censoring — a superpower. Because the median is unaffected by changes above it, quantile regression can recover the true relationship even when the upper tail is top-coded/hidden (Angrist and Pischke 2009).

title: Quantile Regression
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading a quantile effect as an individual effect. It describes the distribution (the poor are less poor), not a fixed person moving up. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Using OLS SEs. Quantile inference needs the bootstrap (or kernel density of residuals); plug-in OLS errors are wrong. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Ignoring quantile crossing. If lines cross in your X range, the linear specification is straining; consider non-crossing methods or rethink the model. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Expecting insight under homoskedasticity. If errors are a pure location shift, every quantile slope is the same as OLS — quantile regression adds nothing. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Confusing conditional and unconditional quantiles. A policy’s effect on the conditional 10th percentile is not its effect on the population 10th percentile. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

title: Quantile Regression
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Angrist & Pischke, Mostly Harmless Econometrics / Mastering ’Metrics — quantile regression beyond the mean; the check/pinball function \rho_\tau(u)=(\tau-\mathbf 1\{u\le0\})u and asymmetric weighting; the conditional quantile function; slopes that fan out under heteroskedasticity (the schooling upper-tail example); the distributional (“the poor are less poor”) interpretation; bootstrap inference; the conditional-vs-unconditional difficulty; quantile crossing; and the censoring advantage.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — LAD / median regression at \tau=0.5 and its robustness to outliers versus OLS’s residual-squaring.

title: Quantile Regression
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why the check function targets the \tau-quantile. Minimising \mathbb{E}[\rho_\tau(Y-q)] over a scalar q has first-order condition \tau - \Pr(Y\le q) = 0, i.e. \Pr(Y\le q)=\tau — exactly the definition of the \tau-quantile. The regression version replaces the constant q with x'\beta_\tau, so x'\hat\beta_\tau estimates the conditional \tau-quantile.
IRLS (the code). The check loss \sum_i \rho_\tau(r_i) equals \sum_i w_i r_i^2 with w_i = \rho_\tau(r_i)/r_i^2 = \big[\tau\mathbf 1\{r_i>0\}+(1-\tau)\mathbf 1\{r_i\le0\}\big]/|r_i|. Iterating weighted least squares with these weights (clipping |r_i| away from 0) converges to \hat\beta_\tau — a by-hand solver standing in for the linear-programming routines production code uses.
Robustness of LAD. The influence of a point on an OLS fit grows linearly with its residual (from the squared loss derivative 2r); for LAD the derivative is \operatorname{sign}(r), bounded — so an outlier far from the line exerts no more pull than one just past it. That bounded influence is the formal meaning of “resilient to outliers.”

title: Quantile Regression
text:
Connections

Builds on: simple and multiple regression (the mean model it generalises) and the bootstrap (its inference).
Relates to: regression diagnostics and heteroskedasticity & GLS — heteroskedasticity is precisely when quantile slopes fan out and become interesting.
Echoes: causal machine learning — both look past the average effect to where in the distribution (or for whom) the effect lands.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text: A huge share of social data is nested: pupils within classrooms within schools, patients within hospitals, repeated measures within a person. Observations in the same group share unmeasured influences, so they are not independent — and ordinary regression, blind to this, reports standard errors that are far too small. Mixed-effects (multilevel, hierarchical) models fix it by splitting effects into a fixed part shared by all and a random part that varies by group — and, as a bonus, they let data-poor groups borrow strength from data-rich ones.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Why it matters
OLS assumes independent observations. (Li 2023) Clustered data breaks that assumption: students in the same class share a teacher and environment, making them more alike than students in other classes. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) The consequence is precise but subtle — OLS stays unbiased, but it is inefficient and its standard errors are wrong, usually badly underestimated, because your effective sample size is closer to the number of clusters than the number of individuals. (Li 2023) Report those SEs and you will claim significance you haven’t earned. Mixed models are the principled response — and the natural model whenever the clustering that diagnostics only patched is something you’d rather understand.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Fixed and random effects
A mixed model literally mixes two kinds of coefficient: (Li 2023)

Fixed effects are the same for every unit — the average return to education across the whole population.
Random effects are allowed to vary across clusters, drawn from a distribution the model estimates.

Two flavours cover most needs: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Random intercepts — each group gets its own baseline. Graphically: parallel lines, one per school, shifted up or down. (Some schools start higher.)
Random slopes — the effect of a predictor varies by group: the return to education might differ for every person, so each group’s line gets its own tilt.


y_{ij} = \underbrace{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{ij}}_{\text{fixed}} + \underbrace{u_{j}}_{\text{random intercept}} + \varepsilon_{ij},
\qquad u_j \sim \mathcal N(0,\sigma_u^2),\ \ \varepsilon_{ij}\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2).

Here i indexes individuals, j their group; u_j is the group’s random departure from the common intercept.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Partial pooling: the big idea
The conceptual heart, and what makes multilevel models more than “regression with cluster dummies.” There’s a spectrum of how to treat groups: (Lambert 2018)

Complete pooling — ignore groups, fit one global mean. Simple, but “too simplistic,” and it reports “insufficient uncertainty.” (Lambert 2018)
No pooling — a separate estimate per group (a dummy per school). It overfits, especially for small groups, where a handful of noisy observations produce a wild estimate. (Lambert 2018)
Partial pooling (the multilevel model) — assume the group parameters share a common distribution, u_j \sim \mathcal N(\mu, \sigma_u^2), and estimate that distribution and the groups together. (Lambert 2018)

The payoff of partial pooling is shrinkage: each group’s estimate is pulled toward the overall mean, and small or extreme groups shrink most — they have the least information of their own, so they lean hardest on the crowd. (Lambert 2018) This is how data-poor groups “borrow statistical strength” from data-rich ones, (Jacobucci et al. 2023) trading a little bias for a large cut in variance — exactly the bias–variance bargain of regularization, here arising naturally from the grouping.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
grand = 50.0          # true global mean
tau   = 8.0           # between-group SD (true spread of group means)
sigma = 12.0          # within-group SD (noise per observation)

n_groups = 30
sizes = rng.integers(2, 60, n_groups)                 # group sizes vary a lot
true_means = grand + rng.normal(0, tau, n_groups)
raw_means = true_means + rng.normal(0, sigma / np.sqrt(sizes))   # noisy group means

# Partial-pooling (shrinkage) weight: reliability of each group's own mean
#   w_j = tau^2 / (tau^2 + sigma^2/n_j)  -> small groups get small w (shrink more)
w = tau**2 / (tau**2 + sigma**2 / sizes)
pooled = w * raw_means + (1 - w) * grand

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.2))
ax.axhline(grand, color="#9a6a2f", ls="--", lw=1.4, label="complete pooling (global mean)")
for s, r, p in zip(sizes, raw_means, pooled):
    ax.annotate("", xy=(s, p), xytext=(s, r),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.0))
ax.scatter(sizes, raw_means, s=22, color="#c0392b", label="raw group mean (no pooling)", zorder=3)
ax.scatter(sizes, pooled, s=22, color="#1f3a5f", label="partial-pooled estimate", zorder=3)
ax.set_xlabel("group size $n_j$"); ax.set_ylabel("estimated group mean")
ax.set_title("Small, noisy groups shrink toward the global mean")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Partial pooling and shrinkage. Each point is a group’s raw mean (no pooling), plotted against group size; the dashed line is the global mean (complete pooling). The partial-pooled estimates (arrows’ heads) pull every group toward the global mean — and SMALL groups (left) shrink far more than large ones (right), because they carry less information of their own. Multilevel modelling is this compromise, done optimally.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Variance components and the ICC
Because the model separates group-level from individual-level randomness, it decomposes the total variance into a between-group part \sigma_u^2 and a within-group part \sigma_e^2. (Li 2023) Their ratio is the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC): 
\rho = \frac{\sigma_u^2}{\sigma_u^2 + \sigma_e^2},
 the proportion of total variation attributable to the grouping. (Li 2023) A high \rho means group members behave very similarly — so sampling more individuals from the same group adds little new information (and ignoring the clustering would most badly mislead your SEs). (Li 2023) The ICC is both a substantive finding (“how much is about the school?”) and a diagnostic for how much clustering matters.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
section: Random effects vs fixed effects: the Hausman choice
text:
Random effects vs fixed effects: the Hausman choice
Econometrics frames the same structure as a choice of estimator, and it comes with a famous test: (Li 2023)

The random-effects (RE) estimator is more efficient — it estimates the variance of the group intercepts rather than N-1 separate dummy coefficients. But it is consistent only if the group effect is uncorrelated with the regressors. (Li 2023)
The fixed-effects (FE) estimator (the panel workhorse) makes no such assumption — it sweeps the group effect away by within-group differencing — at the cost of efficiency and of dropping anything time-invariant.

The Hausman test decides between them: it compares the FE and RE estimates, and if their difference is systematic (significant), the RE exogeneity assumption has failed and you should trust the FE within-group variation instead. (Li 2023) In causal language: RE is a modelling convenience; FE is the one that defends against a confounded grouping.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
section: The Bayesian view: hierarchy all the way up
text:
The Bayesian view: hierarchy all the way up
Mixed models are most natural in the Bayesian framework, because a hierarchy is just a prior extended to multiple levels. (Lambert 2018) Instead of fixing the group distribution’s parameters, you give them their own hyperpriors and infer them from the data — the data tell you how much groups vary, and therefore how much to shrink. (Lambert 2018) The reward is honest uncertainty: the hierarchical posterior reflects the true uncertainty at every level — individual, classroom, school — rather than a single point estimate that pretends the group means were known. (Lambert 2018) Partial pooling, from this angle, isn’t a trick; it’s what Bayes does when you admit the groups are exchangeable draws from a population.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
The social-science bridge






NoteMixed models in the wild




Education. Pupils nested in classes nested in schools — the canonical case; school quality and state policy induce exactly the dependence multilevel models are built for (SAT/test-score studies). (Lambert 2018; Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Tennessee STAR. The class-size experiment is the standard cautionary tale: randomisation was at the classroom level, so honest inference must account for clustering there. (Li 2023)
Labour economics. Wage equations where unobserved ability is a person-level effect u_i — the same object panel fixed effects sweep away. (Wooldridge 2019; Li 2023)
Repeated measures / growth. Rat growth curves, spinal bone-mineral density across ages and ethnicities — measures within an individual, modelled with random effects per subject. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Ignoring clustering entirely. OLS SEs on nested data are “usually significantly underestimated” — the effective n is the number of clusters. Cluster, or model the levels. (Li 2023)
No pooling on small groups. A dummy per group overfits tiny groups into noise; partial pooling shrinks them sensibly. (Lambert 2018)
Using RE without checking exogeneity. RE is inconsistent if the group effect correlates with a regressor; run the Hausman test and fall back to FE when it rejects. (Li 2023)
Forgetting random slopes. If an effect plausibly varies by group, a random-intercept-only model understates uncertainty in that effect. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Reading shrinkage as a bug. A shrunken small-group estimate is more accurate, not censored — it’s the bias–variance trade working for you. (Lambert 2018)

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Dong Li, Panel Data Econometrics — clustered data breaks OLS independence (unbiased but inefficient, underestimated SEs, effective n ≈ number of clusters); variance components and the ICC \rho=\sigma_u^2/(\sigma_u^2+\sigma_e^2); the RE estimator (efficient; consistent only under exogeneity) and the Hausman test (FE vs RE); ability as a person-level effect; Tennessee STAR clustering.
Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — complete / no / partial pooling; shrinkage toward the mean; hierarchical models as priors extended over levels with hyperpriors; honest multi-level uncertainty.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, ML for Social and Behavioral Research — multilevel structure (students in classes/schools), random intercepts/slopes, borrowing statistical strength; repeated-measures/growth examples.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — unobserved individual effects in wage equations; the link to fixed-effects panel estimation.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The variance-components model. With y_{ij}=\mathbf x_{ij}^\top\beta + u_j + \varepsilon_{ij}, u_j\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_u^2), \varepsilon_{ij}\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2), two observations in the same group j have covariance \operatorname{Cov}(y_{ij},y_{i'j})=\sigma_u^2, so their correlation is \rho=\sigma_u^2/(\sigma_u^2+\sigma_e^2) — the ICC. OLS ignores this nonzero off-diagonal covariance, which is precisely why its SEs are wrong; GLS/REML weights by it.
Shrinkage, exactly. The partial-pooled estimate of group j’s mean is the precision-weighted average of its own mean \bar y_j and the grand mean \mu: 
\hat\theta_j = w_j\,\bar y_j + (1-w_j)\,\mu,\qquad
w_j=\frac{\sigma_u^2}{\sigma_u^2+\sigma_e^2/n_j}.
 w_j\to 1 for large n_j (trust the group) and w_j\to 0 for small n_j (trust the crowd) — the algebra of “small groups shrink most,” and the curve drawn in the figure.
RE vs FE. FE estimates \beta from within-group deviations y_{ij}-\bar y_j, eliminating u_j and so needing no assumption about \operatorname{Cov}(u_j,\mathbf x). RE (FGLS) uses both within- and between-group variation, gaining efficiency but requiring \operatorname{Cov}(u_j,\mathbf x)=0. Hausman compares \hat\beta_{FE}-\hat\beta_{RE} to its variance; a large gap rejects RE.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Connections

Builds on: multiple regression, diagnostics & clustered SEs (the patch this entry upgrades to a model), and the Normal/variance machinery.
Bridges to: panel data & fixed effects (FE is the causal sibling; Hausman chooses) and Bayesian machinery (hierarchical priors).
Echoes: regularization — shrinkage as variance reduction, here earned from the grouping rather than imposed by a penalty.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text: A regression coefficient is a statistic, so it has a sampling distribution: run the study again and it would land somewhere slightly different. Everything in this entry — the standard error, the t-test, the confidence interval, the F-test — is the Part II inference machinery attached to \hat\beta. The discipline is the same: never report a coefficient without its uncertainty.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Why it matters
The printout says the effect of education on log-wage is 0.092. Is that real, or could a fresh sample have produced 0? You answer with the coefficient’s standard error and the tests built on it. (Wooldridge 2019) This is where regression stops being description and becomes inference: every “significant effect,” every “we cannot reject,” every error bar in an empirical paper is one of the procedures below. And the statistical-vs-practical distinction returns with a vengeance — with enough data, everything is significant.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
section: The sampling distribution of a coefficient
text:
The sampling distribution of a coefficient
To do exact inference at any sample size, Wooldridge adds one assumption to the Gauss–Markov five: (Wooldridge 2019)

MLR.6 (Normality): the error u is independent of the regressors and distributed \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2).

MLR.1–MLR.6 are the Classical Linear Model. Under them, each OLS estimator is Normally distributed around the truth: (Wooldridge 2019)

\hat\beta_j \sim \mathcal{N}\!\big(\beta_j,\ \operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta_j)\big).

(Even without MLR.6, the CLT delivers approximate Normality in large samples — which is why regression inference works so widely.) The standard error \operatorname{se}(\hat\beta_j) is the estimated standard deviation of this distribution, built from the variance formula with \sigma^2 replaced by its estimate \hat\sigma^2 (the SER squared). (Wooldridge 2019) The standard error is the whole ball game: it is what converts a point estimate into a claim.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
section: Testing a single coefficient: the t-test
text:
Testing a single coefficient: the t-test
To ask “could the true effect be zero?”, test H_0:\beta_j = 0 with the t-statistic — the estimate measured in standard errors: (Wooldridge 2019)

t = \frac{\hat\beta_j}{\operatorname{se}(\hat\beta_j)}.

If |t| exceeds the critical value from the t-distribution with n-k-1 degrees of freedom, reject H_0 — the coefficient is statistically significant. (Wooldridge 2019) The companion confidence interval gives the range of values you can’t reject: (Wooldridge 2019)

\hat\beta_j \pm c\cdot\operatorname{se}(\hat\beta_j),

with c \approx 2 for 95%. The interval and the test are duals: the coefficient is significant at 5% exactly when its 95% interval excludes zero.






NoteWooldridge’s WAGE1 t-test



In the log-wage regression (n=526), the education coefficient is \hat\beta_{\text{educ}} = 0.092 with \operatorname{se} = 0.007. So t = 0.092/0.007 \approx 13.1 — vastly beyond any critical value. Education is overwhelmingly statistically significant: a sampling fluke essentially could not produce a t of 13. (Wooldridge 2019) (The 95% interval, 0.092 \pm 2(0.007) = [0.078, 0.106], nowhere near zero.)



Significance is estimate ÷ standard error
The t-statistic makes vivid that significance is not about the size of an effect but about its size relative to its noise. Below, two coefficients have the identical estimate (0.5); only their standard errors differ. The precise one is clearly significant; the noisy one can’t be distinguished from zero — same number, opposite verdict.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

est = 0.5
xs = np.linspace(-1.0, 2.0, 600)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.7))
for se, color, label in [(0.10, "#1f3a5f", "se = 0.10  (t = 5.0, significant)"),
                         (0.35, "#d98a29", "se = 0.35  (t = 1.4, not significant)")]:
    nd = NormalDist(est, se)
    ax.plot(xs, [nd.pdf(v) for v in xs], color=color, lw=2.0, label=label)
    lo, hi = est - 1.96 * se, est + 1.96 * se
    y = -0.25 if se == 0.10 else -0.5
    ax.errorbar(est, y, xerr=1.96 * se, fmt="o", color=color, capsize=4, lw=2)
ax.axvline(0, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.6)
ax.text(0.02, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.85, "H₀: β = 0", color="#c0392b", fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticks([]); ax.set_xlabel("coefficient value")
ax.set_title("Same estimate (0.5), different precision, different verdict")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Significance = estimate ÷ standard error. Both coefficients are estimated at 0.5. The precise estimate (blue, se = 0.10, t = 5) has a 95% interval far from zero — significant. The noisy estimate (orange, se = 0.35, t = 1.4) has an interval straddling zero — not significant. The point estimate is the same; the standard error decides.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
section: Testing several coefficients at once: the F-test
text:
Testing several coefficients at once: the F-test
Some questions involve groups of coefficients: “do any of these four neighbourhood variables matter?”, “should this block of regressors be in the model at all?” Testing them one t-test at a time invites the multiple-comparisons problem; the F-test asks the joint question in one shot. (Wooldridge 2019) It compares a restricted model (the suspect coefficients forced to zero) against the unrestricted model (all included), via their residual sums of squares: (Wooldridge 2019)

F = \frac{(\text{SSR}_r - \text{SSR}_{ur})/q}{\text{SSR}_{ur}/(n-k-1)},

where q is the number of restrictions. The logic is pure ANOVA: does dropping those regressors make the fit significantly worse? A special case — the test of overall significance — restricts all slopes to zero (H_0:\beta_1=\cdots=\beta_k=0), asking whether the model explains anything at all. (Wooldridge 2019) (This F is what reproduces the ANOVA F-test when the regressors are group dummies — regression contains ANOVA.)

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Statistical vs practical significance — again
Wooldridge hammers the distinction, because regression makes it so easy to confuse: (Wooldridge 2019)

Statistical significance (the t-stat / p-value) asks whether the effect is distinguishable from zero — a question about noise.
Practical (economic) significance (the magnitude of \hat\beta_j) asks whether the effect is big enough to matter — a question about substance.

With a huge sample, a trivially small coefficient can be “highly significant” (t enormous, effect meaningless); with a small sample, a large, important effect can be “insignificant.” (Wooldridge 2019) Always report and interpret the coefficient’s size in real units, not just its stars. A significant 0.2%-per-year effect and a significant 8%-per-year effect are both “significant” and worlds apart.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Prediction: two kinds of interval
Inference isn’t only about coefficients — often the goal is to predict y for a new case with regressor values x_0. The point prediction is just the fitted value, \hat y_0 = \hat\beta_0 +
\hat\beta_1 x_{0}+\cdots. (Wooldridge 2019) But “how uncertain is that prediction?” has two different answers, and confusing them is a classic error:

A confidence interval for the mean, E[y\mid x_0] — the average outcome for everyone with characteristics x_0. Its only uncertainty comes from the estimated coefficients, so it is narrow and shrinks toward zero as n\to\infty.
A prediction interval for a single new individual y_0 — that person’s actual outcome. It carries the coefficient uncertainty plus the irreducible scatter of individuals around the line, the error variance \sigma^2. So it is much wider and does not vanish as n grows: even with infinite data and a perfectly known line, individuals still scatter by \sigma. (Wooldridge 2019)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
n = 60
x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
sigma = 2.5
y = 1.0 + 0.8 * x + rng.normal(0, sigma, n)

xbar, Sxx = x.mean(), np.sum((x - x.mean()) ** 2)
b1 = np.cov(x, y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(x)
b0 = y.mean() - b1 * xbar
resid = y - (b0 + b1 * x)
s = np.sqrt(np.sum(resid ** 2) / (n - 2))            # estimated sigma
tcrit = 1.96

xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
se_mean = s * np.sqrt(1 / n + (xs - xbar) ** 2 / Sxx)        # SE of the mean prediction
se_pred = s * np.sqrt(1 + 1 / n + (xs - xbar) ** 2 / Sxx)    # + 1 for an individual
fit = b0 + b1 * xs

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=16, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6, zorder=2)
ax.fill_between(xs, fit - tcrit * se_pred, fit + tcrit * se_pred,
                color="#d98a29", alpha=0.18, label="95% prediction interval (an individual)")
ax.fill_between(xs, fit - tcrit * se_mean, fit + tcrit * se_mean,
                color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.35, label="95% confidence interval (the mean)")
ax.plot(xs, fit, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2)
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("Confidence interval (mean) vs prediction interval (individual)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 2: Two intervals, two questions. The inner blue band is the 95% confidence interval for the mean outcome E[y|x] — narrow, and narrowest near the centre of the data. The outer band is the 95% prediction interval for a single new individual: far wider because it adds the irreducible person-to-person scatter (σ), which no amount of data removes. Predicting an average is easy; predicting a person is not.





The practical upshot: a regression can pin down an average tightly while still being nearly useless for forecasting any single outcome — the prediction band is wide because people are not their average. (For log-outcome models, predicting the level needs a retransformation correction, since \exp of the predicted log under-predicts the mean — the Jensen gap again.)

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading stars instead of magnitudes. Significance is not importance. A tiny, precisely-estimated effect is significant and may be useless; report the size and a confidence interval. (Wooldridge 2019)
Mining many t-tests for the significant one. Each coefficient at 5% has a 1-in-20 false-positive rate; with many regressors, use the F-test for joint questions and pre-specify hypotheses. (Wooldridge 2019)
Trusting standard errors when MLR.5 fails. Heteroskedasticity or clustering makes the printed SEs wrong (the coefficients are still fine). Use robust/clustered SEs. (Wooldridge 2019)
Interpreting a wide interval around zero as ‘no effect.’ It may mean underpowered, not null — the power lesson, in regression dress.
Forgetting inference assumes a valid model. A perfect t-statistic on an endogenous coefficient precisely estimates a biased number. Inference quantifies sampling noise, not specification error.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — MLR.6 normality and the Classical Linear Model; the Normal sampling distribution of \hat\beta_j and the standard error; the t-statistic t=\hat\beta_j/\operatorname{se}(\hat\beta_j), the n-k-1 degrees of freedom, and confidence intervals; the WAGE1 example (\hat\beta_{\text{educ}}=0.092, \operatorname{se}=0.007, t\approx13.1); the F-test for joint/exclusion restrictions and overall significance; the statistical-vs-practical-significance distinction and the large-sample caution.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

From Normal to t. Under the CLM, (\hat\beta_j-\beta_j)/\operatorname{sd}(\hat\beta_j) is exactly standard Normal. Replacing the unknown \sigma in \operatorname{sd} with the estimate \hat\sigma inflates the tails to a t distribution with n-k-1 degrees of freedom — the same \sigma-is-estimated penalty as the one-sample t-test, now with k+1 parameters consumed instead of one.
The F as a fit comparison. Adding regressors can only lower (never raise) the SSR, so \text{SSR}_r \ge \text{SSR}_{ur}.
