The numerator (\text{SSR}_r-\text{SSR}_{ur})/q is the average improvement per restriction; the denominator \text{SSR}_{ur}/(n-k-1) estimates \sigma^2. Under H_0 both estimate the same noise, so F\approx1; a real contribution makes F large. F\sim F_{q,\,n-k-1} under the null.
t and F coincide for one restriction. For q=1, F = t^2 exactly — the joint test of a single coefficient is just the square of its t-test.
An R²-form of the overall F. Dividing through by SST, the overall-significance F equals \dfrac{R^2/k}{(1-R^2)/(n-k-1)} — large when the model explains a big share of variance relative to its degrees of freedom.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Gauss–Markov assumptions (the variance formula behind the SE; MLR.6 for exact normality), estimation (a coefficient is an estimator with a sampling distribution), and confidence intervals & testing (the t/F machinery, applied to \hat\beta).
Leads to: functional form (interpreting the coefficients you’re now testing) and diagnostics (when the standard errors are wrong and how to fix them).
Reappears in: every causal estimate, each reported with a standard error, a t-stat, and an interval generated exactly this way.

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
text: The Gauss–Markov ideal assumes constant error variance. Real data rarely oblige — spending varies more among the rich, test scores more in big schools. Under this heteroskedasticity, OLS coefficients stay unbiased, but the usual standard errors are biased, so your t- and F-tests are invalid. The fixes split into two philosophies: robust standard errors that work without knowing the variance pattern, and (feasible) GLS that models it to recover efficiency.

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
text:
Why it matters
Heteroskedasticity is the rule, not the exception, in cross-sectional social data — and its danger is subtle: nothing looks wrong (the point estimates are fine), but the inference silently misleads, and the problem does not vanish as the sample grows (Wooldridge 2019). Knowing how to detect it and which correction to use is basic hygiene for any regression you’ll defend.

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
section: What it is, and what it costs
text:
What it is, and what it costs
Heteroskedasticity means the error variance, conditional on the regressors, is not constant: \operatorname{Var}(u\mid X)=\sigma^2(X) depends on X (Wooldridge 2019). The consequences are specific (Wooldridge 2019):

Still unbiased and consistent. These properties need only \mathbb{E}[u\mid X]=0, not constant variance — so OLS gets the right answer on average (Wooldridge 2019).
No longer BLUE / efficient. OLS loses its minimum-variance crown among linear unbiased estimators (Wooldridge 2019).
Invalid inference. The usual SE formulas are biased, so t, F, and confidence intervals are wrong — and stay wrong even in large samples (Wooldridge 2019).

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
section: Robust standard errors: the agnostic fix
text:
Robust standard errors: the agnostic fix
The everyday response is heteroskedasticity-robust (White / Huber–Eicker) standard errors (Wooldridge 2019). They adjust the SEs to be valid in large samples regardless of the form of the heteroskedasticity — without your having to model \operatorname{Var}(u\mid X) at all (Wooldridge 2019). This is why “robust SEs” is the modern default. One caveat from Wooldridge: in small samples robust SEs can be more misleading than conventional ones, because they carry higher sampling variability themselves (Wooldridge 2019).


Show the code (OLS + White robust SE by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
beta1_true = 1.0
n, S = 60, 3000

def fit(x, y):
    X = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(x)), x])
    XtX_inv = np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X)
    b = XtX_inv @ X.T @ y
    e = y - X @ b
    # classical SE
    s2 = (e @ e) / (len(x) - 2)
    se_cl = np.sqrt((s2 * XtX_inv)[1, 1])
    # White (HC0) robust SE: sandwich
    meat = X.T @ (e[:, None]**2 * X)
    Vrob = XtX_inv @ meat @ XtX_inv
    se_rob = np.sqrt(Vrob[1, 1])
    return b[1], se_cl, se_rob

cover_cl = cover_rob = 0
for _ in range(S):
    x = rng.uniform(0, 5, n)
    y = 1.0 + beta1_true * x + (0.3 + 0.8 * x) * rng.normal(size=n)   # heteroskedastic
    b1, se_cl, se_rob = fit(x, y)
    cover_cl += abs(b1 - beta1_true) <= 1.96 * se_cl
    cover_rob += abs(b1 - beta1_true) <= 1.96 * se_rob
cover_cl, cover_rob = 100*cover_cl/S, 100*cover_rob/S

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.8), gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [1.3, 1]})
xd = rng.uniform(0, 5, n); yd = 1.0 + xd + (0.3 + 0.8*xd)*rng.normal(size=n)
axL.scatter(xd, yd, s=12, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6)
xs = np.linspace(0, 5, 30); axL.plot(xs, 1+xs, color="#1f4e79", lw=2)
axL.set_title("Heteroskedastic data (spread grows with x)"); axL.set_xlabel("x"); axL.set_ylabel("y")

bars = axR.bar(["classical\nOLS SE", "robust\n(White) SE"], [cover_cl, cover_rob],
               color=["#c0392b", "#1f8a4c"])
axR.axhline(95, color="#444", ls="--", lw=1.3); axR.text(1.5, 95.4, "95% target", fontsize=8, ha="right")
axR.set_ylim(80, 100); axR.set_ylabel("actual CI coverage (%)")
for b, v in zip(bars, [cover_cl, cover_rob]):
    axR.text(b.get_x()+b.get_width()/2, v+0.3, f"{v:.0f}%", ha="center", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold")
axR.set_title("Robust SEs restore correct coverage")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Why robust SEs matter, by Monte Carlo (3000 simulated datasets, n=60, true slope = 1). The errors are heteroskedastic — their spread grows with x (left panel shows one dataset). Right: the actual coverage of nominal 95% confidence intervals for the slope. Classical OLS standard errors are biased, so their intervals cover the truth only ~88% of the time — you’d reject true nulls far too often. White/Huber–Eicker robust SEs (computed here by hand as the sandwich estimator) restore coverage to ~95%. Same point estimate; only the standard error — and therefore every test — differs.

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
text:
Formal tests
Two standard tests of the null H_0: homoskedasticity (Wooldridge 2019):

Breusch–Pagan. Regress the squared OLS residuals on the regressors: \hat u^2 = \delta_0 + \delta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \delta_k x_k + v. The null is \delta_1=\cdots=\delta_k=0 (variance unrelated to X); a significant F/LM rejects it (Wooldridge 2019).
White test. More general — regress \hat u^2 on the regressors, their squares, and all cross-products. A parsimonious version uses just the fitted values and their square, \hat u^2 = \delta_0 + \delta_1\hat y + \delta_2\hat y^2 + \text{error} (Wooldridge 2019).

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
section: WLS and feasible GLS: model the variance
text:
WLS and feasible GLS: model the variance
When you can model the variance, generalized least squares (GLS) restores efficiency (Wooldridge 2019):

Weighted least squares (WLS). If \operatorname{Var}(u_i\mid x_i)=\sigma^2 h_i, divide every variable (including the intercept) by \sqrt{h_i} (Wooldridge 2019). This down-weights high-variance observations, making the transformed errors homoskedastic and the estimator BLUE (Wooldridge 2019).
Feasible GLS (FGLS). When h_i is unknown, estimate it — typically model the variance as an exponential (to stay positive): regress \log(\hat u^2) on the regressors, exponentiate the fitted values to get \hat h_i = \exp(\hat g_i), and use those as WLS weights (Wooldridge 2019).







TipThe efficiency–robustness trade-off



FGLS is asymptotically more efficient than OLS if the variance model is correct. But if the variance model is wrong, WLS can be less efficient than OLS — so OLS with robust SEs is the “safer,” more interpretable default (Wooldridge 2019). Reach for FGLS when you genuinely know the variance form (or efficiency is critical); otherwise, robust SEs.

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
text:
The time-series cousin: serial correlation
In time series, errors are often serially correlated — today’s error related to yesterday’s (Wooldridge 2019). The parallels to heteroskedasticity are close: with strictly exogenous regressors OLS stays unbiased/consistent but inefficient, and the usual SEs and tests are invalid (Wooldridge 2019). Detection and cures (Wooldridge 2019; Ashley 2012):

Durbin–Watson — the classic AR(1) test; ≈ 2 means no correlation, near 0 means strong positive. Often inconclusive, and invalid with a lagged dependent variable (Wooldridge 2019).
Breusch–Godfrey (LM) — more general: regress OLS residuals on the regressors and lagged residuals \hat u_{t-1},\dots,\hat u_{t-q} (Wooldridge 2019).
Newey–West (HAC) SEs — robust to both heteroskedasticity and arbitrary serial correlation in large samples (Wooldridge 2019).
Cochrane–Orcutt / Prais–Winsten — FGLS via a quasi-differencing transform that removes AR(1), given an estimate of \rho (Wooldridge 2019).
Dynamic respecification. Ashley’s emphasis: serial correlation is often a symptom of missing dynamics — adding lagged terms can “cure” it by specifying the model properly (Ashley 2012).

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Trusting default OLS SEs on cross-sectional data. Assume heteroskedasticity; use robust SEs by default. (Wooldridge 2019)
Using robust SEs in a tiny sample. They have high variability themselves and can mislead at small n. (Wooldridge 2019)
Applying WLS with a wrong variance model. You can end up less efficient than plain OLS — and biased if the weights are endogenous. (Wooldridge 2019)
Treating serial correlation as just a SE problem. It often signals a misspecified dynamic model; fix the specification, don’t only patch the SEs. (Ashley 2012)
Reading Durbin–Watson with a lagged dependent variable. It’s invalid there — use Breusch–Godfrey. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — heteroskedasticity as \operatorname{Var}(u\mid X)=\sigma^2(X); OLS unbiased/consistent but not BLUE, with biased SEs that don’t vanish in large samples; White/Huber–Eicker robust SEs and the small-sample caveat; the Breusch–Pagan and White tests; WLS (1/\sqrt{h_i} weighting → BLUE) and FGLS (log-variance model, exponentiate), with the efficiency-vs-robustness trade-off; serial correlation, Durbin–Watson, Breusch–Godfrey, Newey–West HAC, and Cochrane–Orcutt/Prais–Winsten.
Ashley, Fundamentals of Applied Econometrics — serial correlation as a symptom of missing dynamics and dynamic respecification as the cure.

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The sandwich (White) estimator. OLS gives \hat\beta=(X'X)^{-1}X'y with \operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta\mid X)=(X'X)^{-1}\big(X'\,\Omega\,X\big)(X'X)^{-1}, where \Omega=\operatorname{diag}(\sigma_i^2). Under homoskedasticity \Omega=\sigma^2 I and this collapses to \sigma^2(X'X)^{-1}. White’s insight: you don’t need each \sigma_i^2 — replace the unknown “meat” X'\Omega X=\sum_i \sigma_i^2 x_i x_i' with \sum_i \hat u_i^2\, x_i x_i' (HC0), which is consistent for it. That is exactly the meat = X.T @ (e**2 * X) line in the code; the bread is (X'X)^{-1} on either side.
Why WLS is BLUE. Dividing through by \sqrt{h_i} gives errors u_i/\sqrt{h_i} with variance \sigma^2 h_i/h_i=\sigma^2 — constant. Gauss–Markov now applies to the transformed model, so WLS is BLUE. Equivalently, GLS is \hat\beta_{\text{GLS}}=(X'\Omega^{-1}X)^{-1}X'\Omega^{-1}y, the variance-weighted normal equations.
Newey–West. Generalises the sandwich meat to include lagged cross-products \sum_{\ell} w_\ell \sum_t \hat u_t \hat u_{t-\ell}(x_t x_{t-\ell}' + x_{t-\ell}x_t') with Bartlett weights w_\ell tapering to zero — making the SE robust to serial correlation up to the chosen lag.

title: Heteroskedasticity, Robust Errors & GLS
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Gauss–Markov assumptions (homoskedasticity is the one that fails here), regression diagnostics (detecting it), and inference for regression (the tests it invalidates).
Relates to: quantile regression (heteroskedasticity is exactly when quantile slopes fan out) and time-series fundamentals (serial correlation’s home).
Generalises in: mixed-effects models and clustered standard errors — other departures from i.i.d. errors handled by modelling or robustifying.

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text: OLS always produces a line. Whether that line tells the truth depends on a short list of assumptions. Four of them make OLS unbiased; a fifth makes it efficient; together they make it BLUE — the best linear unbiased estimator. One assumption, exogeneity, carries the entire causal burden, and it is the one that fails most often.

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text:
Why it matters
You have seen OLS fit a line and read off coefficients. But “the computer printed 0.54” is not the same as “0.54 is the truth.” Between them sit the Gauss–Markov assumptions — the conditions under which the OLS estimator is unbiased and as precise as any linear unbiased rule can be. (Wooldridge 2019) Knowing them is not pedantry: it is how you diagnose when to believe a regression and, just as importantly, why a coefficient might be wrong. The assumptions also name the threats — omitted variables, simultaneity, measurement error — that the rest of econometrics and all of causal inference exist to address.

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text:
The five assumptions
Wooldridge’s assumptions for multiple regression on cross-sectional data: (Wooldridge 2019)









Assumption
What it says




MLR.1
Linear in parameters
The population model is y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k + u.


MLR.2
Random sampling
The n observations are a random sample from that population.


MLR.3
No perfect collinearity
No regressor is constant, and none is an exact linear combination of the others.


MLR.4
Zero conditional mean (exogeneity)
E[u \mid x_1,\dots,x_k] = 0 — the unobservables average to zero at every combination of the regressors.


MLR.5
Homoskedasticity
\operatorname{Var}(u \mid x_1,\dots,x_k) = \sigma^2 — the error variance is constant across the regressors.



A word on each of the load-bearing two. MLR.3 doesn’t forbid correlated regressors — only perfectly collinear ones (you can’t separate the effects of “years of education” and “months of education”). MLR.4 is the heart of everything: it says x carries no information about the unobserved u, so the part of y that moves with x is genuinely x’s doing and not some lurking factor’s. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text:
What each assumption buys
The division of labour is clean and worth memorising: (Wooldridge 2019)

MLR.1–MLR.4 give unbiasedness: E[\hat\beta_j] = \beta_j. Across many random samples, the OLS estimate averages to the true parameter. Note that homoskedasticity is not needed for unbiasedness — a heteroskedastic model still has unbiased coefficients.
MLR.5 buys the usual variance formula: homoskedasticity is what makes the standard-error formulas your software prints (and the tests built on them) valid. Without it the coefficients are still unbiased but the standard errors are wrong — the problem robust standard errors fix.

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
section: The Gauss–Markov theorem: OLS is BLUE
text:
The Gauss–Markov theorem: OLS is BLUE
Put MLR.1–MLR.5 together and you get the central justification for OLS. (Wooldridge 2019)

Gauss–Markov theorem. Under MLR.1–MLR.5, OLS is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE).

Unpack the acronym: (Wooldridge 2019)

Linear — the estimator is a linear function of the outcomes y.
Unbiased — it’s right on average, E[\hat\beta_j] = \beta_j.
Best — among all linear unbiased estimators, OLS has the smallest variance. No other linear unbiased rule is more precise.

So OLS is not an arbitrary choice; it is provably the most efficient option in its class. The picture makes “Best” concrete: a rival linear-unbiased estimator (here, a simple grouping estimator) is also centred on the truth, but it scatters far more than OLS.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
true_slope, n, reps = 1.0, 40, 5000
ols, grouping = np.empty(reps), np.empty(reps)

for r in range(reps):
    x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
    y = 2 + true_slope * x + rng.normal(0, 3, n)
    # OLS slope
    ols[r] = np.cov(x, y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(x)
    # rival: split at median x, slope through the two group means (linear & unbiased)
    hi = x >= np.median(x)
    grouping[r] = (y[hi].mean() - y[~hi].mean()) / (x[hi].mean() - x[~hi].mean())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.7))
bins = np.linspace(-0.5, 2.5, 70)
ax.hist(grouping, bins=bins, density=True, color="#d98a29", alpha=0.7,
        label=f"Grouping estimator (SD {grouping.std():.2f})")
ax.hist(ols, bins=bins, density=True, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.8,
        label=f"OLS (SD {ols.std():.2f})")
ax.axvline(true_slope, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.8, label="true slope = 1")
ax.set_yticks([]); ax.set_xlabel("estimated slope")
ax.set_title("Both unbiased — OLS is the minimum-variance (Best) one")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: OLS is BLUE. Across 5,000 simulated samples (true slope = 1), both OLS (blue) and a rival linear, unbiased ‘grouping’ estimator (orange) centre on the truth — both are unbiased. But OLS is far tighter: among linear unbiased estimators, none has smaller variance. That minimum-variance property is the ‘Best’ in BLUE.

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
section: Exogeneity: the assumption that does the causal work
text:
Exogeneity: the assumption that does the causal work
If you remember one thing, remember this: MLR.4 (exogeneity) is the assumption that licenses a causal reading, and it is the most fragile. (Wooldridge 2019) When E[u\mid x]\neq 0, the regressor is endogenous: it carries information about the unobservables, so the slope mixes x’s true effect with whatever lurks in u. Three classic ways it fails: (Wooldridge 2019)

Omitted variables. A relevant factor left out of the model and correlated with a regressor — ability in a wage-on-education regression. Its effect contaminates the education coefficient (the OVB of the last entry). (Wooldridge 2019)
Simultaneity. x causes y and y causes x — price and quantity in a market, where supply and demand determine each other. (Wooldridge 2019)
Measurement error. The recorded x is a noisy proxy for the true one, which (as the next-but-one entry shows) typically attenuates the coefficient toward zero. (Wooldridge 2019)

Crucially, no amount of data fixes an exogeneity failure — a bigger sample just pins down the wrong number more precisely. This is exactly why Part V builds an entire arsenal (instruments, experiments, discontinuities) around defending or engineering exogeneity.

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text:
What inflates a coefficient’s variance
Even when OLS is BLUE, “best” can still be imprecise. The variance of an OLS slope is (Wooldridge 2019)

\operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta_j) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\text{SST}_j\,(1 - R_j^2)},

and reading off the three knobs tells you what hurts precision:

Error variance \sigma^2 (numerator): more unexplained noise → fuzzier estimates. Good controls reduce it.
Regressor variation \text{SST}_j: little spread in x_j → hard to see its effect. You can’t estimate the slope of a line from points that barely move horizontally.
Multicollinearity R_j^2: how well the other regressors explain x_j. As R_j^2 \to 1 the denominator \to 0 and the variance explodes — when two regressors move together, OLS struggles to apportion credit between them (diagnostics).

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Believing a regression because it ran. OLS always produces numbers; they’re only trustworthy if the assumptions — above all exogeneity — hold. The software cannot check MLR.4 for you. (Wooldridge 2019)
Thinking more data cures bias. Endogeneity bias does not shrink with n. Precision improves; accuracy doesn’t. Only a better design or a credible identification argument helps. (Wooldridge 2019)
Treating heteroskedasticity as a threat to unbiasedness. It isn’t — MLR.5 affects standard errors, not the coefficients. Use robust SEs, don’t panic about bias. (Wooldridge 2019)
Confusing ‘no perfect collinearity’ with ‘no correlation.’ Correlated regressors are fine and normal; only exact linear dependence is forbidden. High (not perfect) correlation just inflates variance. (Wooldridge 2019)
Forgetting BLUE is conditional. “Best” holds within linear unbiased estimators and under the assumptions. Drop exogeneity and OLS isn’t even unbiased; allow nonlinear or biased estimators and something else (e.g. ridge) can beat it on MSE.

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the five MLR assumptions (linearity, random sampling, no perfect collinearity, zero conditional mean / exogeneity, homoskedasticity); MLR.1–4 ⟹ unbiasedness while MLR.5 underwrites the usual variance formula; the Gauss–Markov theorem and the meaning of BLUE (linear, unbiased, minimum-variance); exogeneity as the causal workhorse and its three failure modes (omitted variables, simultaneity, measurement error); the slope-variance formula \sigma^2/[\text{SST}_j(1-R_j^2)] and its three drivers.

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Unbiasedness from exogeneity. Write \hat\beta_1 = \beta_1 + \sum_i w_i u_i where w_i = \tilde x_{1i}/\sum_j \tilde x_{1j}^2 (the partialling-out weights). Taking the conditional expectation and using MLR.4, E[u_i\mid X]=0, gives E[\hat\beta_1\mid X]=\beta_1 — unbiased, regardless of the variance structure. Homoskedasticity (MLR.5) is never used here.
The variance formula. Under MLR.1–MLR.5, \operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta_j\mid X) = \sigma^2/\sum_i \tilde x_{ji}^2. Since \sum_i \tilde x_{ji}^2 = \text{SST}_j(1-R_j^2) — total variation in x_j times the fraction not explained by the other regressors — this is the printed formula. Heteroskedasticity breaks it: the correct variance becomes \sum_i w_i^2\sigma_i^2, which is what the White/robust estimator estimates directly.
Gauss–Markov, sketched. Any linear estimator is \sum_i a_i y_i; unbiasedness imposes linear constraints on the a_i. Minimising \operatorname{Var}=\sigma^2\sum_i a_i^2 subject to those constraints (Lagrange) yields exactly the OLS weights a_i=w_i. Hence OLS attains the minimum variance among linear unbiased estimators — the content of “Best.”

title: The Gauss–Markov Assumptions & BLUE
text:
Connections

Builds on: multiple regression (the model these assumptions govern), estimation (bias, variance, efficiency, BLUE — first met there), and conditional expectation (exogeneity makes the regression line the CEF).
Leads to: inference for coefficients (which adds normality, MLR.6, for exact tests), diagnostics (detecting and fixing MLR.5 and collinearity), and regression and causality (exogeneity as the threat).
Motivates: the causal-inference summit, whose every method is a strategy for making exogeneity credible.

title: Factor Analysis: Measuring the Unseen
text: The constructs social science cares about — intelligence, depression, political ideology, trust — are latent: unobservable directly. What you can observe is a battery of items that all tap the same underlying thing, and so move together. Factor analysis turns that logic around: from the correlations among many observed indicators, it recovers the few latent factors that explain them — giving you a principled measurement of a concept you can only see indirectly.

title: Factor Analysis: Measuring the Unseen
text:
Why it matters
Measurement is where studies are won or lost, and most important constructs are latent. Factor analysis is the statistical formalization of measuring them: it assumes the correlation among observed variables is the result of their shared relationship with common factors, and estimates those factors. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) It is how a 20-item depression scale becomes one “depression” score, and the foundation of psychometrics, attitude research, and the SEM of the next entry.

title: Factor Analysis: Measuring the Unseen
section: The model: manifest items, latent factors
text:
The model: manifest items, latent factors
The core picture: a few unobserved latent factors drive many observed manifest variables (survey items, test questions). (Jacobucci et al. 2023) The strength of each link is a factor loading — “the regression coefficient from a latent variable to an observed indicator.” (Jacobucci et al. 2023) A large loading means the item is a strong, representative measure of the construct; a near-zero loading means it taps something else. Read the loadings and you learn which items measure what.

title: Factor Analysis: Measuring the Unseen
text:
Exploratory vs confirmatory
The same machinery runs in two modes, depending on whether you’re discovering or testing: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Exploratory factor analysis (EFA) — discovery-oriented, with few assumptions: every manifest variable is allowed to load on every factor, and you let the data reveal the structure. The central question is how many factors? — answered with eigenvalues and a scree plot (often via parallel analysis), looking for the “elbow” where additional factors stop explaining meaningful variance. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) — theory-testing: you specify a hypothesized structure (which items load on which factors), impose constraints (forcing other loadings to zero), and test whether that model fits the observed data. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Eigenvalues: 3 strong factors, then a scree of noise
eig = np.array([4.6, 2.9, 1.8, 0.55, 0.42, 0.33, 0.28, 0.22, 0.18, 0.14])
factors = np.arange(1, len(eig) + 1)
random_line = np.linspace(1.1, 0.3, len(eig))    # parallel-analysis reference

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.plot(factors, eig, "o-", color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2, ms=6, label="observed eigenvalues")
ax.plot(factors, random_line, "s--", color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.4, ms=4, label="parallel analysis (random data)")
ax.axvline(3.5, color="#c0392b", ls=":", lw=1.5)
ax.annotate("the elbow → keep 3 factors", xy=(3, 1.8), xytext=(4.2, 3.0), fontsize=9, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"))
ax.set_xlabel("factor number"); ax.set_ylabel("eigenvalue (variance explained)")
ax.set_title("Scree plot: how many latent factors?")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A scree plot, the workhorse of EFA. Each point is the variance (eigenvalue) explained by successive factors, in descending order. The ‘elbow’ — here after the 3rd factor — marks where extra factors stop capturing real shared structure and start fitting noise. Parallel analysis refines this by comparing against eigenvalues from random data (dashed): keep factors above the random line.

title: Factor Analysis: Measuring the Unseen
text:
Factor analysis is not PCA
The two are constantly confused but answer different questions: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

PCA models the total variance of the variables to achieve dimension reduction — it’s a variance-maximising rotation, agnostic about causes.
Factor analysis models only the shared variance among variables to identify latent constructs — it posits that a real, unobserved factor causes the correlations.

PCA asks “how do I compress these variables?”; factor analysis asks “what hidden thing makes them correlate?” Use FA when you believe in an underlying construct; PCA when you just want fewer dimensions.

title: Factor Analysis: Measuring the Unseen
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating PCA components as factors. They optimise different things; a PCA component is not a latent construct. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Over-extracting factors. Reading too many factors off the scree fits noise; use the elbow / parallel analysis, not “eigenvalue > 1” alone. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Naming a factor from weak loadings. A construct’s meaning comes from the items that load strongly on it; don’t over-interpret small loadings. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Confirming with the data you explored. EFA on a dataset then CFA on the same data isn’t a test; CFA needs fresh data or strong prior theory. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Factor Analysis: Measuring the Unseen
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, ML for Social and Behavioral Research — latent factors driving manifest items (correlations as shared-factor effects); factor loadings as latent→indicator regression coefficients; EFA (free loadings; eigenvalues / scree / parallel analysis / the “elbow”) vs CFA (constrained, hypothesis-testing); and factor analysis vs PCA (shared vs total variance; constructs vs dimension reduction).
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — latent constructs and measurement of the unobservable.

title: Factor Analysis: Measuring the Unseen
text:
Connections

Builds on: concepts & measurement (latent constructs) and covariance & correlation (the correlations it explains).
Leads to: structural equation modeling — CFA is SEM’s measurement model.
Contrast with: PCA & unsupervised learning (dimension reduction vs latent constructs).

title: The Bootstrap
text: Classical inference needs a formula for the sampling distribution — and for anything past a mean, those formulas get ugly or don’t exist. The bootstrap sidesteps the algebra entirely: treat your sample as a stand-in for the population, resample it with replacement thousands of times, recompute your statistic each time, and read the standard error and confidence interval straight off the spread of those replicates. It works for the median, a correlation, a complicated machine-learning estimator — any statistic — which is why it’s an indispensable, one-size-fits-all tool.

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Why it matters
For a mean, the CLT hands you a standard error. For a median, a correlation, a ratio, or a tangled multi-step estimator, the analytic formula is hard or nonexistent. (Bruce et al. 2020) The bootstrap is the “one size fits all” answer: a computer-intensive method that replaces a derivation with brute-force resampling, giving honest standard errors and confidence intervals for statistics you could never tackle by hand. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: The Bootstrap
section: The core idea: resample with replacement
text:
The core idea: resample with replacement
You only have one sample — so make it work harder. Treat the observed sample of size n as a “hypothetical population,” and draw a new sample of size n from it, with replacement — a bootstrap sample. (Bruce et al. 2020) Recompute your statistic on that bootstrap sample. Repeat B times (typically 1,000 or 10,000), and the collection of bootstrap replicates approximates the statistic’s sampling distribution. (Bruce et al. 2020) Bruce & Bruce’s image: “imagine replicating the original sample thousands or millions of times so that you have a hypothetical population that embodies all the knowledge from your original sample.” (Bruce et al. 2020)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
sample = rng.exponential(scale=4.0, size=80)        # one skewed sample
B = 5000
boot_medians = np.array([np.median(rng.choice(sample, size=len(sample), replace=True))
                         for _ in range(B)])

se_boot = boot_medians.std()
lo, hi = np.percentile(boot_medians, [2.5, 97.5])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.hist(boot_medians, bins=40, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85, edgecolor="white")
ax.axvline(np.median(sample), color="#3f8f6f", lw=2.2, label=f"sample median = {np.median(sample):.2f}")
ax.axvline(lo, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.8)
ax.axvline(hi, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.8, label=f"95% percentile CI [{lo:.2f}, {hi:.2f}]")
ax.text(0.02, 0.9, f"bootstrap SE = {se_boot:.2f}", transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=10,
        color="#1f3a5f", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("median of a bootstrap resample"); ax.set_ylabel("frequency")
ax.set_title("5,000 bootstrap medians ≈ the sampling distribution")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The bootstrap distribution of a median. From one skewed sample (n=80), we resample with replacement 5,000 times and recompute the median each time. The histogram of those 5,000 bootstrap medians IS the (approximate) sampling distribution — for a statistic with no easy formula. Its standard deviation is the bootstrap standard error; its 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles (red) are a 95% percentile confidence interval.

title: The Bootstrap
text:
The plug-in principle
Why is resampling your data legitimate? The plug-in principle: in the absence of the true population distribution, use the empirical distribution of the sample — which puts probability 1/n on each observed value — as a stand-in. (Bruce et al. 2020) For large n this empirical distribution is “an arbitrarily good approximation to the actual distribution,” (Ashley 2012) and the intuition is Dayal’s: “if the sample is like the population, then many samples from the sample are representative of many samples from the population.” (Bruce et al. 2020) Resampling the sample mimics resampling the population.

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Standard errors and confidence intervals
Two outputs fall straight out of the replicates: (Bruce et al. 2020)

Bootstrap standard error — just the standard deviation of the bootstrap replicates. It “sums up the variability in the sampling distribution,” and shines for complex estimators with no analytic SE. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Percentile confidence interval — the most intuitive CI: sort the B replicates and trim 2.5% from each end; the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles are your 95% interval. (Bruce et al. 2020) No Normality assumed — if the bootstrap distribution is skewed, the interval is too.
Bias-corrected (BCa) — because the bootstrap can reveal asymmetry and bias the percentile method ignores, the BCa interval adjusts for both, at the cost of more replicates ($$10,000) to stabilise. (Wooldridge 2019; James et al. 2021)

title: The Bootstrap
section: When it works — and when it fails
text:
When it works — and when it fails
The bootstrap is powerful but “not a panacea.” (Ashley 2012) Know its boundaries: (Ashley 2012)

Works for “standard” statistics — means, variances, regression coefficients, correlations.
Small samples. It “does not compensate for a small sample size.” If n is tiny (say 10), the empirical distribution is a poor stand-in for the population and the bootstrap fails. (Ashley 2012)
Sample extremes. It is “unlikely to be satisfactory” for maxima, minima, or the range — statistics driven by the tails, which a resample can’t reproduce. (Ashley 2012)
Dependent data. A naive bootstrap “fails to replicate the dependence” in time-series or clustered data. The fix is the block bootstrap — resample whole blocks (or clusters) to preserve the internal structure. (Ashley 2012)

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Bootstrapping a tiny sample. Garbage in, garbage out: the method can’t manufacture information the sample doesn’t contain. (Ashley 2012)
Bootstrapping the max/min/range. Tail-driven statistics break the plug-in approximation; use extreme-value theory instead. (Ashley 2012)
Ignoring dependence. Time-series or clustered data need a block bootstrap; the naive version understates uncertainty. (Ashley 2012)
Too few replicates for a CI. A stable percentile interval (and especially BCa) needs thousands of resamples, not a few hundred. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: The Bootstrap
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the bootstrap as resampling with replacement to build a “hypothetical population”; $B=$1,000–10,000 replicates; the plug-in / empirical distribution; the bootstrap standard error (SD of replicates) and the percentile CI (trim 2.5% each end); the “one size fits all” virtue for any statistic.
Ashley, Fundamentals of Applied Econometrics — the plug-in justification (“arbitrarily good approximation”); the boundary conditions — small samples, extremes, and dependent data (the block bootstrap fix); “not a panacea.”
Wooldridge / James et al. (ISL) — bias-corrected (BCa) intervals and replicate counts; bootstrapping regression statistics. Grinstead & Snell — the bootstrap as a Monte Carlo technique (“poor man’s” posterior; “spinning gold from straw”).

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Connections

Builds on: sampling distributions & the CLT (what it approximates), estimation (the SE it produces), and Monte Carlo (the bootstrap is a Monte Carlo on the empirical distribution).
Powers: confidence intervals without formulas and the bootstrap hypothesis test.
Reappears in: ML (bagging is bootstrap aggregating; uncertainty for complex estimators).

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text: Every statistic you compute is a random variable in disguise: run the study again and you’d get a slightly different number. The sampling distribution is the set of numbers you could have gotten, and the Central Limit Theorem tells you that — for averages — that set is almost always a bell curve.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Why it matters
You run a survey, compute an average, and report it. But you only ran the survey once. Had a different 1,200 people answered the phone, your average would have come out a little different. So the real question behind every empirical claim is: how much would my answer have bounced around if chance had dealt a different sample?
That bounce is not a nuisance to apologize for — it is the thing inference is about. Margins of error, confidence intervals, p-values, standard errors: every one of them is a statement about a sampling distribution. Get this idea and the rest of statistics stops being a bag of formulas and becomes one question asked many ways.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Intuition
There are two different distributions that beginners constantly confuse, and keeping them apart is most of the battle. (Bruce et al. 2020)

A data distribution describes the individual values in one dataset — the heights of the people in your sample, say. Plot a histogram of your data and that’s what you see.
A sampling distribution describes how a summary of the data — the mean, a proportion, a treatment effect — would vary across many hypothetical datasets drawn the same way.

The leap is this: a statistic is a function of random data, so it is itself random. The sample mean \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i is a sum of random variables, and a sum of random variables has its own probability distribution. (Ross 2018) Grinstead & Snell make this concrete by simulation: keep drawing samples, compute the mean each time, and watch those means scatter into a shape of their own. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) In an experiment, Gerber & Green give the cleanest gloss — the sampling distribution is “the collection of estimates that could have been generated by every possible random assignment.” That is exactly what lets you ask whether your result was a lucky draw. (Gerber and Green 2012)

Watch it happen
The most convincing argument here is not a proof — it’s a picture. Start with a population that is emphatically not bell-shaped (a heavily skewed one), draw samples, and plot the distribution of the sample mean as the sample size grows.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# A strongly right-skewed "population": an exponential distribution.
# (Think: income, donation sizes, time-to-event — common in social data.)
pop_mean = 1.0
population = rng.exponential(scale=pop_mean, size=200_000)

sample_sizes = [2, 10, 30]
n_reps = 50_000

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(11, 3.1))

# Panel 0: the raw population
axes[0].hist(population, bins=60, range=(0, 6), color="#9a6a2f", alpha=0.85)
axes[0].set_title("Population\n(raw data, skewed)", fontsize=10)
axes[0].set_yticks([])

# Panels 1-3: sampling distribution of the mean at increasing n
for ax, n in zip(axes[1:], sample_sizes):
    means = population[rng.integers(0, population.size, size=(n_reps, n))].mean(axis=1)
    ax.hist(means, bins=60, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85, density=True)
    ax.set_title(f"Mean of n = {n}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_yticks([])
    # overlay the Normal the CLT predicts: mean = pop_mean, sd = pop_sd / sqrt(n)
    pop_sd = population.std()
    xs = np.linspace(means.min(), means.max(), 200)
    se = pop_sd / np.sqrt(n)
    normal = np.exp(-0.5 * ((xs - pop_mean) / se) ** 2) / (se * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
    ax.plot(xs, normal, color="#b5446e", lw=1.8)

fig.suptitle("The sample mean forgets the shape it came from", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A skewed population (left) versus the sampling distribution of its mean at n = 2, 10, and 30. The raw data is lopsided, yet the averages march toward a bell.





The leftmost panel is the world as it actually is: lopsided. Yet by the time we average just 30 observations, the distribution of that average is almost perfectly Normal (the pink curve is the bell the theorem predicts). The sample mean forgets the shape it came from. That forgetting is the Central Limit Theorem.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
In plain language: if you add up or average enough independent random pieces, the result is approximately Normal — no matter what distribution the pieces came from. Francis Galton called it the “supreme law of unreason”: order emerging reliably out of individual chaos. (Grinstead and Snell 1997)
Stated carefully: let X_1, X_2, \dots be independent and identically distributed with finite mean \mu and finite variance \sigma^2. Then as n \to \infty, the standardized sample mean converges in distribution to a standard Normal:

Z_n \;=\; \frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}(0, 1).

Two things deserve emphasis, because they are where the theorem is most often misremembered:

What it needs: independence, and a finite mean and variance. It does not need the data to be Normal — that’s the whole point. A version even allows the pieces to have different distributions, as long as no single one dominates the sum. (Ross 2018)
How fast: remarkably fast for symmetric data — a fair die’s average looks bell-shaped by n \approx 10. The famous “n \ge 30” is only a rule of thumb, and a bad one for heavily skewed data, which can need far more. (Bruce et al. 2020; Reinhart 2015)







NoteThe LeBron James of statistics (Wheelan)



Charles Wheelan calls the CLT “the LeBron James of statistics” — a near-“miracle” that is the power source for almost all inference. (Wheelan 2013) His intuition pump: a bus of marathon runners goes missing, and you find a broken-down bus whose passengers average over 220 pounds. Without checking a single ID you radio in — “I think it’s the wrong bus. Keep looking” — because a random sample of 60 marathoners being that heavy is astronomically unlikely. (The bus was bound for the International Festival of Sausage.) (Wheelan 2013) That reflex is the CLT: the average of a sample has a predictable, bell-shaped spread, so an average far in its tail signals the sample didn’t come from the population you assumed — “consistent with” it, or an “aberration.” The crescendo, in Wheelan’s word: it holds even when the underlying population is not Normal (household income, say). (Wheelan 2013)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Standard deviation vs standard error
This is the second great confusion, and it costs people dearly. (Reinhart 2015)

Standard deviation (\sigma) measures the spread of individual data points. How much do people differ from the average person? That’s the SD.
Standard error (SE) measures the spread of a statistic across hypothetical samples. It is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution. How much would my estimate bounce around if I redid the study? That’s the SE. (Bruce et al. 2020)

They are linked by the single most useful formula in applied statistics:

\text{SE}(\bar{X}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.

The \sqrt{n} is doing quiet, brutal work. Because uncertainty shrinks with the square root of the sample size, to halve your standard error you must quadruple your sample. This is why precision is expensive, and why a poll of 1,200 is “good enough” while a poll of 4,800 is only twice as good. (Bruce et al. 2020)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Worked examples
An astronomer measures a distance. (Ross 2018) Atmospheric distortion and human error make any single telescope reading noisy. So the astronomer takes n independent readings and averages them. By the CLT, that average is approximately Normal around the true distance — and its spread, the SE, shrinks like \sigma/\sqrt{n}. More readings, sharper answer.
A pollster samples a nation. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) The CLT is why a sample of roughly 1,200 people pins down a national preference to about a \pm 3\% margin — and, strikingly, why that barely depends on whether the country has 100,000 or 100 million people. It’s the sample size that drives precision, not the population size.
Female village councils in India. (Gerber and Green 2012) When researchers asked whether reserving council-head seats for women shifted budget priorities, they used the sampling distribution of the estimated effect to ask: is the spending difference we observed bigger than what random assignment alone could have produced? That comparison is the inference.
The RAND Health Insurance Experiment. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Before trusting any treatment effect, the researchers used standard errors to check that groups assigned to different insurance plans were balanced at baseline — comparable starting points, within the bounds of chance.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Thinking your data should be Normal. The CLT is about the sampling distribution of a statistic, not your raw observations. Social-science data is routinely skewed and long-tailed (income, donations, durations). Forcing a Normal model onto raw data underestimates extreme “black swan” values. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Trusting n \ge 30 blindly. For strongly skewed data, t-tests and Normal approximations can be badly off even at moderate n — Reinhart’s example is charitable donations, where a few huge gifts wreck the approximation. (Reinhart 2015)
Eyeballing overlapping error bars. Two confidence intervals can overlap and the difference can still be significant. “Your eyeball is not a well-defined statistical procedure” — run the actual test. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating the bootstrap as a cure for small samples. Resampling your own data reveals the variability already present; it does not manufacture information you never collected. It frees you from assuming Normality, not from needing enough data. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The gambler’s fallacy and optional stopping. A run of heads does not make tails “due” — the Law of Large Numbers governs the long run, not short streaks. And peeking at your data repeatedly and stopping the moment p < 0.05 massively inflates false positives. (Grinstead and Snell 1997; Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the sample mean as a random variable; the formal CLT; the astronomer example.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — CLT via simulation; the polling example; the Law of Large Numbers and gambler’s fallacy.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — data vs sampling distribution; SD vs SE; the bootstrap; the “raw data isn’t Normal” warning.
Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — small-sample failures; overlapping error bars; optional-stopping inflation of false positives.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — the sampling distribution as “every possible random assignment”; the India village-council study.
Angrist & Pischke, Mostly Harmless Econometrics — standard errors for baseline balance in the RAND experiment.
Wheelan, Naked Statistics — the CLT as “the LeBron James of statistics” and the marathon-bus intuition for why a sample mean far in the tail signals the wrong population.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Mean and variance of the sample mean. Let X_1,\dots,X_n be i.i.d. with \mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu and \operatorname{Var}(X_i)=\sigma^2. Then by linearity of expectation,

\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu,

so the sample mean is unbiased. For the variance, independence makes the cross terms vanish:

\operatorname{Var}(\bar{X})
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\,(n\sigma^2)
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.

Taking the square root gives the standard error, \text{SE}(\bar X)=\sigma/\sqrt{n}, and with it the \sqrt{n} rule.
The Central Limit Theorem (Lindeberg–Lévy form). Define the standardized mean

Z_n = \frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n} = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu).

Then for every real z,

\lim_{n\to\infty} \Pr(Z_n \le z) = \Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-t^2/2}\,dt .

Sketch of why. Work with the characteristic function \varphi(t)=\mathbb{E}[e^{itZ_n}]. Writing Y_i=(X_i-\mu)/\sigma (mean 0, variance 1) and using independence,

\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \left[\varphi_Y\!\left(\tfrac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)\right]^{n}.

A second-order Taylor expansion gives \varphi_Y(s) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{2}s^2 + o(s^2), so

\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \left[1 - \frac{t^2}{2n} + o\!\left(\tfrac{1}{n}\right)\right]^{n}
\;\longrightarrow\; e^{-t^2/2},

which is the characteristic function of the standard Normal. Lévy’s continuity theorem then delivers convergence in distribution. The finite-variance assumption is exactly what makes the quadratic term — and nothing higher — survive in the limit; when variance is infinite (heavy tails), the limit is a different, non-Normal stable law, which is the deep reason the CLT can fail.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Connections

Builds on: random variables, expectation and variance (the \sigma/\sqrt{n} standard error), the Normal distribution, and the Law of Large Numbers (which gives the destination the CLT then puts a shape around).
Leads to: confidence intervals and hypothesis testing, where the sampling distribution becomes the engine of inference.
Reappears in: the standard errors attached to every regression coefficient and every causal estimate.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text: Every test in the last few entries leaned on an assumption — usually Normality, or a large enough sample for the CLT to rescue you. Real social data is skewed, outlier-ridden, ordinal, and often small. Resampling and rank-based tests throw the assumptions out: they build the null distribution from your own data by shuffling it, or sidestep distribution entirely by working with ranks. Often the most honest p-value is one you simulated.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Why it matters
The t-test, ANOVA, and the Normal-approximation proportion tests all assume something about the shape of the data or the size of the sample. (Bruce et al. 2020) When income is log-skewed, when a few outliers dominate, when responses are 1–5 Likert ratings, or when n is simply too small for the CLT to kick in, those assumptions fail — and the p-values they produce can’t be trusted. (Bruce et al. 2020; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
Two families fix this. Resampling tests (permutation and bootstrap) manufacture the null distribution directly from the observed data, assuming almost nothing. Rank-based non-parametric tests discard the raw numbers for their ranks, immunising against skew and outliers. Together they are the assumption-light backbone of modern data analysis — and the engine behind every A/B test.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
The permutation test
The permutation (or randomization) test is the most intuitive idea in all of inference. (Bruce et al. 2020) Its logic: if the two groups are really the same, the labels are arbitrary. “Page A” versus “Page B” is meaningless under the null, so you can shuffle the labels and the data shouldn’t care. Do that thousands of times, and you see how big a group difference chance alone manufactures — then ask where your real difference falls in that pile. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The procedure: (Bruce et al. 2020)

Combine both groups into one pooled dataset.
Shuffle and deal out new groups of the original sizes.
Recompute the statistic (e.g. difference in means) on the shuffled groups.
Repeat thousands of times to build the permutation distribution — the null, made from your own data.
The p-value is the fraction of shuffles whose difference equals or exceeds the one you actually observed.

Because the null distribution is built from the data, the test needs almost no distributional assumptions — no Normality, no minimum sample size. (Bruce et al. 2020)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
# Two groups of skewed session times (lognormal); B a bit higher than A.
group_a = rng.lognormal(4.7, 0.6, 21)
group_b = rng.lognormal(4.9, 0.6, 15)
obs_diff = group_b.mean() - group_a.mean()

pooled = np.concatenate([group_a, group_b])
nA = len(group_a)
n_perm = 10_000
perm_diffs = np.empty(n_perm)
for i in range(n_perm):
    rng.shuffle(pooled)
    perm_diffs[i] = pooled[nA:].mean() - pooled[:nA].mean()

p_val = np.mean(np.abs(perm_diffs) >= abs(obs_diff))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.7))
ax.hist(perm_diffs, bins=60, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.8)
ax.axvline(obs_diff, color="#c0392b", lw=2.0)
ax.axvline(-abs(obs_diff), color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, ls=":")
ax.text(obs_diff, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.9, f"  observed = {obs_diff:.1f}s",
        color="#c0392b", fontsize=9)
ax.set_title(f"Permutation null distribution — p ≈ {p_val:.3f}")
ax.set_xlabel("difference in group means under shuffled labels")
ax.set_yticks([])
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A permutation test in action. The grey histogram is the null distribution of the difference in group means, built by shuffling the combined data 10,000 times. The red line is the actually-observed difference. The p-value is the share of shuffles at least as extreme (shaded) — here a middling value, so the observed gap is well within what chance produces.











NoteBruce & Bruce’s web-stickiness test



A company asked whether Page A or Page B held visitors longer. Page A: 21 visitors, mean 126.33 seconds; Page B: 15 visitors, mean 162.00 seconds — an observed gap of 35.67 seconds. They pooled all 36 session times, reshuffled them into groups of 21 and 15 1,000 times, and found a difference that large arose by chance about 12.4% of the time (p = 0.124). The verdict: not significant — the 35.67-second lead can’t be distinguished from luck. No Normality was ever assumed. (Bruce et al. 2020)


Full vs randomized. An exhaustive permutation test enumerates every possible relabeling for an exact p-value — feasible only for small samples. For anything larger, a randomized test uses many random shuffles (10,000, say) and lands at a near-identical answer. (Bruce et al. 2020) The figure above is the randomized kind.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
A/B testing: the framework underneath
An A/B test is just a randomized experiment in digital dress: group A gets the control, group B the new treatment, subjects are randomly assigned, and you compare a pre-chosen metric. (Bruce et al. 2020) Two disciplines make it trustworthy:

Randomization ensures any group difference is due to the treatment or to chance — never to systematic bias in who got what. (Bruce et al. 2020) (The same logic that powers causal inference.)
A single, pre-specified test statistic (conversion rate, click rate, session time) chosen before the test — so you can’t fish through metrics for one that’s significant. (Bruce et al. 2020)

And how do you analyse the result? Usually with exactly the permutation and bootstrap machinery of this entry — letting the data gauge chance variation without leaning on a formula. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
The bootstrap hypothesis test
The bootstrap you met building confidence intervals can also test. (Bruce et al. 2020) The twist: to test a null, first transform the data so the null is true — e.g. shift the two groups to share a common mean — then resample with replacement and see how often a resampled difference reaches your observed one. (Bruce et al. 2020) The contrast with the CI is worth fixing:

A bootstrap confidence interval measures a statistic’s variability to locate the true parameter.
A bootstrap hypothesis test computes the probability of your result if the null held.

Same resampling engine, aimed at two different questions. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Rank-based non-parametric tests
The classical distribution-free alternatives predate the computer: replace each value by its rank (1st, 2nd, 3rd…) and test on the ranks. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) Ranks are immune to skew and outliers — the largest value is just “rank n,” however extreme — and they work for inherently ordinal data like Likert scales. Each parametric test has a rank twin:



Parametric test
Rank-based (distribution-free) twin
Compares




Two-sample t-test
Mann–Whitney U / Wilcoxon rank-sum
two independent groups


Paired t-test
Wilcoxon signed-rank
before/after, matched pairs


One-way ANOVA
Kruskal–Wallis
three or more groups



Reach for them when the data is skewed, outlier-heavy, ordinal (Likert), or so small that Normality can’t be checked. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) They ask a subtly different question — whether one group tends to rank higher than another — but for most purposes they answer “are these groups different?” without the parametric baggage.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
section: Multi-arm bandits: testing that optimises as it goes
text:
Multi-arm bandits: testing that optimises as it goes
A fixed A/B test runs to completion before you act — exposing many users to the losing version meanwhile. Multi-arm bandit algorithms instead learn in real time, shifting traffic toward whichever “arm” is winning while still occasionally exploring the rest (Bruce et al. 2020). The aim changes from “cleanly measure the difference” to “maximise payoff during the experiment.” That whole story — epsilon-greedy, Thompson sampling, regret, and the trade-off against clean inference — has its own dedicated entry: A/B testing & multi-armed bandits.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Defaulting to the t-test on ugly data. Heavy skew, wild outliers, or tiny n break the parametric assumptions; a permutation or rank test is the honest call. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Too few permutations. A handful of shuffles gives a noisy p-value. Use thousands (10,000 is common) so the permutation distribution is smooth. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Forgetting to impose the null in a bootstrap test. Unlike a bootstrap CI, a bootstrap test requires shifting the data so the null holds first — skip it and you’re not testing anything. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Choosing the metric after seeing the data. A/B tests demand a pre-specified statistic; picking the winning metric post-hoc is p-hacking. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Treating a rank test as identical to its parametric twin. Rank tests compare typical ranks, not means; with very different group shapes they answer a slightly different question. Know what you’re claiming. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the permutation/randomization test logic and procedure; the web-stickiness example (Page A n=21 mean 126.33; Page B n=15 mean 162.00; observed 35.67s; p=0.124 over 1,000 shuffles); full vs randomized permutation; the A/B testing framework (control/treatment, randomization, pre-specified metric) analysed by resampling; the bootstrap hypothesis test and how it differs from the bootstrap CI; multi-arm bandits and epsilon-greedy.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative & Statistical Research Methods — rank-based non-parametric tests as distribution-free alternatives: Mann–Whitney/Wilcoxon rank-sum, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Kruskal–Wallis, and when to use them (skew, outliers, ordinal data, small samples).

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The permutation p-value. Let t_{\text{obs}} be the observed statistic and t_1,\dots,t_M the values from M random relabelings. The (two-sided) p-value is

\hat p = \frac{1 + \#\{m : |t_m| \ge |t_{\text{obs}}|\}}{1 + M},

the “+1” counting the observed arrangement itself (so \hat p is never exactly 0 and the test is valid). Its justification is exchangeability: under the null, every relabeling of the pooled data is equally likely, so t_{\text{obs}} is just one draw from the permutation distribution.
Why it needs no Normality. The reference distribution is the empirical distribution of t over relabelings, not a theoretical t or F curve. Nothing about the population shape is assumed — only that, under H_0, the labels are exchangeable.
Bootstrap test vs CI. For two groups, recentre each to a common mean (impose H_0: \tilde x_i = x_i - \bar x_g + \bar x_{\text{pool}}), resample each group with replacement B times, and compute the proportion of |\bar x_B^{*1}-\bar x_B^{*2}| \ge |t_{\text{obs}}|. Contrast with the percentile CI, which resamples the un-shifted data and reads off the 2.5th/97.5th percentiles — same engine, null imposed in the test but not in the interval.
Rank-sum sketch. Mann–Whitney pools the two groups, ranks all N values, and sums the ranks W in one group. Under H_0, W has mean n_1(N+1)/2 and a known variance, giving a Normal approximation for large samples and exact tables for small ones — all from ranks, no raw-value distribution required.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing & p-values (the null/p-value logic it reimplements by simulation), confidence intervals (the bootstrap, here turned into a test), and the Law of Large Numbers (why thousands of shuffles converge on the true permutation distribution).
Sits beside: ANOVA, chi-square & proportions — the parametric tests these methods replace when assumptions fail.
Leads to: A/B testing & multi-armed bandits — the applied experimentation entry — and to randomized experiments in the causal summit, where randomization plus resampling is the cleanest inference there is.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text: A single test at \alpha=0.05 risks a false positive 5% of the time. Run twenty and the odds of at least one fluke “significant” leap to 64%; run a hundred and it’s a near-certain 99%. This is the multiple-comparisons problem, and it quietly invalidates a huge amount of published research. The fixes trade off two philosophies: control the chance of any false positive (Bonferroni), or control the fraction of your discoveries that are false (the false-discovery rate).

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Why it matters
The logic is almost tautological: the more questions you ask of a dataset, the more likely you are to find a “significant” answer purely by chance. (Reinhart 2015) Modern research asks many questions — dozens of outcomes, subgroups, model specifications — so the nominal 5% error rate is a fiction unless you account for the multiplicity. This is one of the central engines of the replication crisis, and spotting it is a core part of reading any study critically.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
The arithmetic of luck
If you run m independent tests of true nulls at level \alpha, the chance of at least one false positive is 1-(1-\alpha)^m: (Reinhart 2015)

m=20 at \alpha=0.05: 1-0.95^{20}\approx \mathbf{0.64} — a 64% chance of a spurious “finding.”
m=100: \approx \mathbf{0.99}.



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

m = np.arange(1, 101)
p_any = 1 - (1 - 0.05)**m
# Under Bonferroni each test uses alpha/m, so familywise stays ~ 1-(1-0.05/m)^m -> ~0.05
p_bonf = 1 - (1 - 0.05/m)**m

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.plot(m, p_any, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6, label="uncorrected: P(≥1 false positive)")
ax.plot(m, p_bonf, color="#c0392b", lw=2.4, label="Bonferroni (familywise ≈ 0.05)")
ax.axhline(0.05, color="#9aa0a6", ls=":", lw=1.2)
for mm in (20, 100):
    ax.plot(mm, 1-(1-0.05)**mm, "o", color="#1f3a5f")
    ax.annotate(f"{1-(1-0.05)**mm:.0%}", (mm, 1-(1-0.05)**mm), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(-28, -2), fontsize=9, color="#1f3a5f")
ax.set_xlabel("number of tests m"); ax.set_ylabel("P(at least one false positive)")
ax.set_title("Run enough tests and a 'significant' fluke is nearly guaranteed")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="center right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The 5% promise erodes fast. With every null true, the probability of at least one ‘significant’ result by chance, 1−(1−0.05)^m, climbs to 64% by 20 tests and 99% by 100. Bonferroni (red) holds the familywise error at 5% by testing each hypothesis at the far stricter α/m — restoring the guarantee at the cost of power.











NoteGreen jelly beans, and the garden of forking paths



Reinhart’s favourite illustration is the xkcd cartoon: scientists find “no link” between acne and 19 colours of jelly bean, then a link with green ones (p<0.05) — splashed as a headline. They tested 20 colours, so one lucky anomaly was nearly guaranteed. (Reinhart 2015) The subtler cousin is the garden of forking paths: even without running 20 explicit tests, a chain of individually defensible choices — which covariates to include, which outliers to drop — lets different analysts reach different “significant” conclusions from the same data. The multiplicity is hidden in the analysis decisions. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
section: Fix 1 — control the familywise error rate (Bonferroni)
text:
Fix 1 — control the familywise error rate (Bonferroni)
The familywise error rate (FWER) is the probability of making at least one Type I error across the whole family of tests. (Reinhart 2015) The Bonferroni correction controls it by brute force: test each hypothesis at \alpha' = \alpha/m. (Reinhart 2015) For 20 tests at the 0.05 level, you only reject when p<0.0025. It works — the familywise error stays near 5% — but it is severely conservative: dividing \alpha by m slashes power, so you miss many real effects. (Reinhart 2015) (Holm’s step-down procedure is a uniformly more powerful way to control the same FWER.)

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Fix 2 — control the false-discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg)
When you’re screening thousands of hypotheses (genomics, A/B tests, brain imaging), FWER control is too strict — you’d find nothing. The false-discovery rate (FDR) changes the target: instead of “never make a single false positive,” it controls the expected fraction of your declared discoveries that are false. (Reinhart 2015) The Benjamini–Hochberg procedure sorts the p-values and applies a sliding threshold, guaranteeing that, on average, no more than a chosen fraction q (say 10%) of your “discoveries” are flukes. (Reinhart 2015) It is far less conservative than Bonferroni — you accept a known, small rate of false discoveries in exchange for catching many more true effects. The right tool depends on the cost of a false positive: FWER when one wrong call is disastrous, FDR when you’re generating leads to follow up.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
section: The link to p-hacking and replication
text:
The link to p-hacking and replication
Multiplicity is the mechanism behind p-hacking — selectively reporting, or “dredging” the data, until something clears p<0.05. (Reinhart 2015) Unreported multiplicity is why so many findings fail to replicate: the pharmaceutical company Bayer could fully replicate only 14 of 67 highly-cited studies. (Reinhart 2015) Pre-registration and correction for multiple comparisons are the structural antidotes — the subject of the replication-crisis and open-science entries.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting the one significant result out of many. Twenty outcomes, one “hit” — that’s the jelly-bean fallacy. Correct for all the tests you ran, not just the winner. (Reinhart 2015)
Hidden multiplicity in flexible analysis. The garden of forking paths inflates false positives even with a single reported test; pre-register the analysis to close it. (Reinhart 2015)
Defaulting to Bonferroni on huge screens. It’s so conservative you’ll miss real effects; use FDR/BH when generating hypotheses. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating FDR like FWER. FDR allows a controlled fraction of false discoveries — appropriate for screening, not for a single high-stakes confirmatory test. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — multiple comparisons as “the more questions you ask, the more likely a significant answer by chance”; the 1-(1-\alpha)^m arithmetic (64% at 20, 99% at 100); the green jelly beans (xkcd) and the garden of forking paths; FWER and the Bonferroni (\alpha/m) correction and its power cost; the false-discovery rate and Benjamini–Hochberg sliding threshold; p-hacking/dredging and the Bayer 14/67 replication failure.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — multiple comparisons in the data-science / A/B-testing idiom and FDR for large-scale screening.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing (the single-test \alpha this multiplies) and statistical power (what corrections cost).
Leads to: the replication crisis and questionable research practices (p-hacking, the garden of forking paths) and their fix in open science.
Reappears in: large-scale ML/feature screening and any study with many outcomes or subgroups.

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text: Basic Metropolis proved you can sample a posterior without its impossible integral. But a blind random walk is slow — it crawls through high-dimensional, correlated posteriors. Three refinements fix that: Metropolis–Hastings (handle asymmetric, constrained proposals), Gibbs (exploit conditional structure for rejection-free moves), and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (use the gradient to make long, accurate jumps). Knowing how each works tells you why Stan and PyMC behave as they do — and which diagnostic to trust.
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
Why it matters
When you fit a Bayesian model in Stan, PyMC, or brms, you are running HMC/NUTS, and the warnings it prints — divergences, low effective sample size, R-hat — are meaningless unless you understand the machine producing them. This entry is the working knowledge behind those warnings: the algorithms, their failure modes, and the diagnostics that catch them.
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
Metropolis–Hastings: asymmetric proposals
Basic Metropolis requires a symmetric proposal — the chance of proposing A\to B equals B\to A (Lambert 2018). That breaks for constrained parameters: a symmetric Normal step near zero will keep proposing impossible negative values for a standard deviation \sigma > 0 (Lambert 2018). Metropolis–Hastings (MH) generalises to asymmetric proposals by adding a Hastings correction to the acceptance ratio (Lambert 2018):

r = \underbrace{\frac{p(\theta_{t+1}\mid\text{data})}{p(\theta_t\mid\text{data})}}_{\text{posterior ratio}}
\times
\underbrace{\frac{J(\theta_t\mid\theta_{t+1})}{J(\theta_{t+1}\mid\theta_t)}}_{\text{Hastings (q-) ratio}} .

The second factor compensates for a proposal that itself favours some states over others — without it, the chain would converge to the wrong distribution (Lambert 2018). (When J is symmetric the q-ratio is 1 and MH collapses to Metropolis.)
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
Gibbs sampling: the conditional workhorse
Gibbs sampling exploits a model’s structure: instead of proposing a joint move, it samples each parameter in turn from its full conditional distribution given the current value of all the others, p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, \text{data}) (Lambert 2018). Its signature property: no rejection step — the acceptance probability is always 1, because each draw comes straight from the true conditional (Lambert 2018). Every iteration moves.
The cost: Gibbs is only possible when you can derive and sample those full conditionals — which usually requires conjugacy (Lambert 2018). And it has a sharp geometric weakness: when parameters are highly correlated (a sharp “diagonal ridge”), Gibbs — restricted to axis-aligned, horizontal-and-vertical moves — is forced into tiny steps and explores agonisingly slowly (Lambert 2018).


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def gibbs_bivariate(rho, n, rng):
    sd = np.sqrt(1 - rho**2)
    pts = np.empty((2*n + 1, 2)); x, y = -2.5, 2.5
    pts[0] = (x, y)
    for i in range(n):
        x = rho * y + sd * rng.normal();  pts[2*i+1] = (x, y)   # update x | y
        y = rho * x + sd * rng.normal();  pts[2*i+2] = (x, y)   # update y | x
    return pts

def density_grid(rho):
    g = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 120)
    X, Y = np.meshgrid(g, g)
    Z = np.exp(-0.5/(1-rho**2) * (X**2 - 2*rho*X*Y + Y**2))
    return X, Y, Z

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))
for ax, rho in zip(axes, [0.4, 0.95]):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
    X, Y, Z = density_grid(rho)
    ax.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=5, colors="#9aa0a6", linewidths=0.8)
    pts = gibbs_bivariate(rho, 40, rng)
    ax.plot(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], color="#1f4e79", lw=0.8, alpha=0.9)
    ax.plot(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], "o", color="#c0392b", ms=2.5)
    ax.set_title(f"ρ = {rho}  →  {'fast mixing' if rho < 0.5 else 'trapped on the ridge'}",
                 fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("θ₁"); ax.set_ylabel("θ₂"); ax.set_xlim(-3.5, 3.5); ax.set_ylim(-3.5, 3.5)
fig.suptitle("Gibbs sampling: axis-aligned steps stall on a correlated posterior", fontsize=11.5)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Gibbs sampling a correlated bivariate Normal, by hand, via its full conditionals x|y ~ N(ρy, 1−ρ²) and y|x ~ N(ρx, 1−ρ²). Each move is purely horizontal then purely vertical (the axis-aligned stair-step). Left (ρ=0.4): the conditionals are wide, the sampler strides across the distribution and mixes fast. Right (ρ=0.95): the posterior is a thin diagonal ridge, the conditionals are narrow, and the sampler is trapped taking minuscule steps along it — Gibbs’ notorious slow mixing under high correlation.
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
section: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo: a directed walk
text:
Hamiltonian Monte Carlo: a directed walk
HMC abandons the random walk for a directed one driven by the gradient of the log-posterior (Lambert 2018). Lambert’s sledge analogy: picture a sledge sliding on a frictionless surface in negative-log-posterior space (Lambert 2018).

Gradient (gravity). The slope pulls the sledge toward troughs in NLP space — the peaks of the posterior (Lambert 2018).
Momentum (the shove). A random kick (momentum drawn from a multivariate Normal) gives the sledge energy to climb out of a basin and traverse the whole landscape, rather than settling at the mode (Lambert 2018).

Because it uses local geometry to propose distant moves with high acceptance, HMC explores high-dimensional posteriors “orders of magnitude faster” than random-walk Metropolis or Gibbs (Lambert 2018). Two practicalities:

NUTS & Stan. HMC needs a trajectory length T; too long and the sledge does a U-turn, retracing its path. The No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) picks T adaptively at each step, and is the default engine in Stan (Lambert 2018).
Divergences. When the discrete leapfrog approximation of the trajectory fails to track the posterior’s true curvature — typically in high-curvature “neck” regions — the path diverges. A reported divergence flags a region the sampler cannot explore properly, signalling potential bias (Lambert 2018). Divergences are not cosmetic; they are a correctness warning.
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
section: Diagnostics that matter: autocorrelation, ESS, thinning
text:
Diagnostics that matter: autocorrelation, ESS, thinning
MCMC draws are dependent, and that dependence is the quantity to police (Lambert 2018):

Autocorrelation. How correlated a draw is with its own past; high autocorrelation means the chain lingers and each draw carries little new information (Lambert 2018).
Effective sample size (ESS). The number of independent samples that would carry the same information as your autocorrelated draws — almost always far below the nominal count (Lambert 2018). 4,000 draws with high autocorrelation might be worth an ESS of 200. ESS, not the raw draw count, governs your Monte Carlo precision.
Thinning. Keeping every n-th draw to cut autocorrelation. It saves memory but Lambert (and Murphy) stress it does not add information — a thinned chain is not a better chain, just a smaller one (Lambert 2018).
