\hat R (Gelman–Rubin). Compares within-chain to between-chain variance across multiple chains; trust convergence only when \hat R \approx 1 for every parameter (Lambert 2018).
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
Choosing a sampler

crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting the Hastings correction with an asymmetric proposal. The chain converges to the wrong distribution — a silent, serious bug. (Lambert 2018)
Using Gibbs on highly correlated parameters. It mixes painfully slowly; reparameterise or switch to HMC. (Lambert 2018)
Ignoring divergences in Stan. They flag regions the sampler can’t reach — the posterior estimates may be biased, not just noisy. (Lambert 2018)
Trusting the raw draw count. Report ESS; 10,000 autocorrelated draws can carry the information of a few hundred. (Lambert 2018)
Thinning to ‘improve’ a chain. It discards information; only thin to save storage, never to fix mixing. (Lambert 2018)
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — Metropolis–Hastings and the Hastings correction (q-ratio) for asymmetric/constrained proposals; Gibbs sampling from full conditionals, the always-accept property, its conjugacy requirement and correlation weakness (axis-aligned steps on a diagonal ridge); HMC via the frictionless-sledge / gradient / momentum analogy, its high-dimensional efficiency, NUTS as Stan’s default and the U-turn problem, and divergences as curvature-failure warnings; autocorrelation, effective sample size, thinning (saves memory, not information), and \hat R\approx 1.
Murphy, Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective — corroborating treatment of HMC and the ESS / thinning diagnostics.
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why the Hastings correction restores the target. For the chain to have posterior \pi as its stationary distribution it suffices that detailed balance holds: \pi(\theta)\,T(\theta\to\theta') = \pi(\theta')\,T(\theta'\to\theta), where the transition is T(\theta\to\theta') = J(\theta'\mid\theta)\,\alpha(\theta\to\theta'). Substituting the MH acceptance \alpha = \min\!\big(1,\ \tfrac{\pi(\theta')J(\theta\mid\theta')}{\pi(\theta)J(\theta'\mid\theta)}\big) makes both sides equal — the q-ratio is exactly what cancels the proposal’s asymmetry. Drop it and detailed balance fails, so the chain targets a different distribution.
Gibbs is MH with acceptance 1. Take the proposal for component i to be its full conditional, J(\theta_i'\mid\theta) = p(\theta_i'\mid\theta_{-i}). The MH ratio then algebraically reduces to 1 — every proposal is accepted. Gibbs is the special case where the proposal is perfect.
HMC in one breath. Augment \theta with momentum \mathbf{p}\sim\mathcal N(0,M) and define the Hamiltonian H(\theta,\mathbf{p}) = -\log\pi(\theta) + \tfrac12\mathbf{p}^\top M^{-1}\mathbf{p} (potential + kinetic energy). Simulating Hamilton’s equations with the leapfrog integrator conserves H approximately, so the proposal at the trajectory’s end has near-1 acceptance; the small leapfrog error is corrected by a Metropolis step. Divergences are leapfrog blow-ups where curvature is too sharp for the step size — the diagnostic that the discretisation broke.
Monte Carlo error with ESS. A posterior-mean estimate has standard error \approx \sigma_{\text{post}}/\sqrt{\text{ESS}}, not /\sqrt{M}. With autocorrelation \rho_k, \text{ESS} = M / (1 + 2\sum_{k\ge1}\rho_k) — which is why a sticky chain’s effective size collapses.
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
Connections

Builds on: credible intervals & computation (basic Metropolis and R-hat), the Bayesian machinery (conjugacy that makes Gibbs possible), and the LLN (why samples approximate the posterior).
Powers: hierarchical Bayesian models — whose funnels are exactly the high-curvature regions HMC was built for — and Bayesian model comparison.
Relates to: maximum likelihood (HMC needs the same log-density gradients) and the Monte Carlo foundations.
crumbs: MCMC in Depth: Metropolis–Hastings, Gibbs & Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

title: II · Inference
text:
Inference is the discipline of saying something about a population you cannot fully see, using a sample you can, while being honest about the uncertainty. Every tool here is a way of reading the sampling distribution — the idea that turns a single number into a claim.
This part is built as a stand-alone textbook on inference, in two sub-chapters. The first walks the dominant classical (frequentist) paradigm end to end — from drawing a sample to bounding an estimate to testing a claim, and on to the ways the whole edifice gets abused. The second is a dedicated treatment of Bayesian inference, so you leave genuinely bilingual rather than with a footnote.

title: II · Inference
text:
A recurring theme
This part carries the manual’s critical conscience: Statistics Done Wrong sits beside every test, because knowing how inference is misused (p-hacking, optional stopping, underpowered studies) is part of knowing how to use it — the thread that the replication-crisis entry gathers into one rigorous account.

title: II · Inference
text:
Where this leads
Inference is the grammar that Part III · Regression speaks: a regression coefficient is an estimate, and everything you learn here about uncertainty attaches to it.

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text: Data come in groups: students in schools, patients in hospitals, repeated measures on people. You can pool everything (one estimate for all — blind to real group differences) or pool nothing (a separate estimate per group — hopeless for small groups). Hierarchical models take the middle path: group parameters are drawn from a shared population distribution, so each group borrows strength from the rest and is shrunk toward the overall mean — by an amount the data itself decides. This is the Bayesian engine behind mixed-effects models.

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text:
Why it matters
The single most common structure in social data is groups within a population, and the naïve analyses both fail. Hierarchical modelling is the principled fix — and it is everywhere modern Bayesian work lives: multilevel regression, meta-analysis, small-area estimation, MRP for polling. It also unifies three ideas you’ve met separately: random effects, regularization, and the Bayesian treatment of priors.

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text:
The spectrum of pooling
Facing grouped data, you stand between two extremes (Lambert 2018):

Complete pooling — ignore the groups, fit one global parameter for all observations. It uses the full sample but produces too little uncertainty and often misrepresents the data-generating process by erasing real group variation (Lambert 2018).
No pooling (heterogeneous) — fit a separate, independent model per group. It respects group differences but overfits, and is “terrible” for small groups, whose scant data give wild, noisy estimates (Lambert 2018).

The hierarchical model bridges the gap: group-specific parameters that are nonetheless tied together through a common population distribution (Lambert 2018).

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text:
Partial pooling and shrinkage
Because the groups share a population distribution, their estimates are partially pooled — each borrows statistical strength from the others and is pulled toward the grand mean \bar\mu (Lambert 2018). The shrinkage rule is the beautiful part (Lambert 2018):

Groups with small samples or noisy data are shrunk more toward the population average; large, data-rich groups stay close to their raw empirical means.

And the model learns how much to pool from the between-group variance \bar\sigma (Lambert 2018): if \bar\sigma is estimated small, it moves toward complete pooling; if large, toward no pooling. The data picks the point on the spectrum.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
K = 12
mu_pop, tau = 50.0, 6.0           # population mean and between-group SD
sigma = 12.0                      # within-group SD
true_mu = rng.normal(mu_pop, tau, K)
n_j = rng.choice([3, 5, 8, 15, 40, 120], size=K)     # very unequal group sizes

ybar = np.array([rng.normal(true_mu[j], sigma/np.sqrt(n_j[j])) for j in range(K)])  # no-pool est
se2 = sigma**2 / n_j              # sampling variance of each group mean
grand = np.average(ybar, weights=1/se2)
# partial-pooling (Normal-Normal) estimate: precision-weighted blend of ybar_j and grand mean
shrink = se2 / (se2 + tau**2)     # 0 = trust data, 1 = full shrink to grand mean
partial = (1 - shrink) * ybar + shrink * grand

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.4))
for j in range(K):
    lw = 0.5 + 2.5 * (n_j[j] / n_j.max())
    ax.plot([0, 1], [ybar[j], partial[j]], "-", color="#1f4e79", lw=lw, alpha=0.7)
    ax.plot(0, ybar[j], "o", color="#c0392b", ms=4)
    ax.plot(1, partial[j], "o", color="#1f8a4c", ms=4)
    ax.text(-0.04, ybar[j], f"n={n_j[j]}", ha="right", va="center", fontsize=7, color="#555")
ax.axhline(grand, color="#444", ls="--", lw=1.2)
ax.text(1.02, grand, "grand mean", va="center", fontsize=8.5, color="#444")
ax.set_xticks([0, 1]); ax.set_xticklabels(["no pooling\n(raw group mean)", "partial pooling\n(hierarchical)"])
ax.set_xlim(-0.25, 1.35); ax.set_ylabel("group estimate")
ax.set_title("Shrinkage: small, noisy groups are pulled hardest toward the mean")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Partial pooling as shrinkage. Twelve groups, each observed with a different sample size. Left points: the no-pooling estimates (raw group means) — scattered widely, the small-n groups flung furthest by noise. Right points: the partial-pooling (hierarchical) estimates, each pulled toward the grand mean (dashed). The pull is selective: thin lines (small, noisy groups) shrink a lot; thick lines (large groups) barely move. The model borrows strength where data is scarce and trusts it where data is plentiful — exactly the shrinkage rule, computed by hand.

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
section: A worked example: English school scores
text:
A worked example: English school scores
Lambert’s case is GCSE scores across 3,458 English schools (Lambert 2018). A pooled model gives a single effect for, say, poverty (free school meals) — but that’s “highly suspect” because regions differ in quality. A heterogeneous model gives a separate effect for each of the 152 Local Education Authorities (LEAs) — but the estimates are “extreme” for LEAs containing only a few schools. The hierarchical model draws each LEA’s parameter from an England-level distribution, yielding LEA estimates shifted toward the grand mean with lower uncertainty, because each LEA now uses information from all the others (Lambert 2018).

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
section: Connections to mixed models and regularization
text:
Connections to mixed models and regularization
Two bridges make this less exotic than it looks (Lambert 2018):

Frequentist mixed/random-effects models are the direct counterpart. The difference is philosophical: frequentists treat group effects as random and population effects as fixed constants; Bayesians treat all parameters as random variables with distributions (Lambert 2018). (See mixed-effects models.)
Regularization. Shrinkage is regularization: like Lasso/Ridge, the hierarchical prior prevents overfitting by reining in extreme parameter values unsupported by the population-level data (Lambert 2018). A multilevel model is a self-tuning regulariser.

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text:
The funnel and non-centered parameterization
Hierarchical models bite back when sampled. The posterior has a “funnel” — high curvature where the between-group SD \bar\sigma is small (Lambert 2018):

When \bar\sigma is small, the \mu_j are squeezed tight, demanding tiny leapfrog steps; when it is large, the posterior is flat, demanding long steps. A single HMC step size can’t serve both, producing divergences (Lambert 2018).
Non-centered parameterization fixes it: introduce a standardized helper \mu_{\text{raw},j}\sim
\text{Normal}(0,1) and set \mu_j = \bar\mu + \mu_{\text{raw},j}\cdot\bar\sigma. This decouples the group parameters from the population parameters, flattening the geometry so the sampler explores it without divergences (Lambert 2018).

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Defaulting to complete or no pooling. Both are usually wrong; the hierarchical middle is the honest model for grouped data. (Lambert 2018)
Fitting the centered parameterization with little data per group. You’ll hit the funnel and divergences — switch to the non-centered form. (Lambert 2018)
Over-tight hyperpriors on \bar\sigma. They pre-decide the amount of pooling; use a weakly-informative half-Cauchy/half-Normal and let the data speak. (Lambert 2018)
Forgetting that shrinkage is intentional. A small group’s estimate should be pulled toward the mean — that’s the model protecting you from noise, not a bug. (Lambert 2018)
Ignoring exchangeability. If groups aren’t exchangeable (a known covariate orders them), model that covariate rather than assuming a common prior. (Lambert 2018)

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — the complete-pooling vs no-pooling spectrum and why each fails; the hierarchical structure (likelihood → group-level population distribution → hyperprior) with hyperparameters learned from data; exchangeability; partial pooling and the shrinkage rule (small/noisy groups shrunk more) with the between-group variance controlling the amount of pooling; the English-schools / LEA worked example; the links to frequentist mixed models and regularization; and the funnel pathology with the non-centered parameterization \mu_j=\bar\mu+\mu_{\text{raw},j}\bar\sigma fix.

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The shrinkage estimate, derived. With group means \bar x_j of sampling variance \sigma_j^2 = \sigma^2/n_j and a group-level prior \mu_j\sim\mathcal N(\bar\mu,\tau^2), the Normal–Normal posterior mean is the precision-weighted blend

\hat\mu_j = (1-B_j)\,\bar x_j + B_j\,\bar\mu,
\qquad B_j = \frac{\sigma_j^2}{\sigma_j^2 + \tau^2}.

B_j is the shrinkage factor: as n_j\to\infty, \sigma_j^2\to 0, B_j\to 0 — no shrinkage (trust the data); as n_j\to 0 or \tau^2\to 0, B_j\to 1 — full shrinkage to the grand mean. The code computes exactly this B_j. The hierarchical model estimates \tau^2 jointly, which is how it learns the pooling.
Why non-centering flattens the funnel. In the centered form, \mu_j\mid\bar\sigma has SD \bar\sigma, so the joint density of (\mu_j,\log\bar\sigma) narrows to a cusp as \bar\sigma\to0 — Neal’s funnel. Writing \mu_j=\bar\mu+\bar\sigma\,z_j with z_j\sim\mathcal N(0,1) makes z_j a priori independent of \bar\sigma, so the sampled geometry is a product of well-scaled standard Normals — no cusp, constant curvature, one step size suffices.
Stein’s insight. That a shrunk estimate beats the raw per-group means in total expected error is the Bayesian face of the James–Stein phenomenon: pooling across \ge 3 groups dominates the obvious estimator. Hierarchical shrinkage is Stein estimation with the pooling strength learned from data.

title: Hierarchical & Multilevel Bayesian Models
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Bayesian machinery (priors and Normal–Normal updating) and MCMC in depth (HMC, funnels, divergences — the sampling challenges here).
Twins with: mixed-effects & multilevel models (the frequentist counterpart) and regularization (shrinkage as a penalty).
Leads to: Bayesian model comparison — choosing among hierarchical specifications by predictive accuracy.

title: Effect Sizes
text: A p-value answers “is there an effect?”; it says nothing about how big. And size is what decides whether a result matters — whether a drug is worth taking, a program worth funding. The effect size supplies that magnitude, on a scale that doesn’t inflate just because your sample is large. Cohen’s d, R^2, an odds ratio: each answers the question significance can’t. Reporting it is now the expected standard, not a courtesy.

title: Effect Sizes
text:
Why it matters
A p-value is a “measure of surprise,” not of magnitude. (Reinhart 2015) And surprise is cheap when n is huge: a gigantic sample will flag “extremely tiny but relatively unimportant differences” as p<0.05. (Reinhart 2015) The statistical-vs-practical-significance trap is exactly this confusion. Effect size provides the “so what?” — the size of the relationship, independent of sample size. (Reinhart 2015) It is the number a decision actually depends on.

title: Effect Sizes
text:
Standardized effect sizes
The most useful effect sizes are standardized, so they compare across studies and instruments: (Reinhart 2015)

Cohen’s d — the standardized mean difference between two groups, in pooled-SD units: 
d = \frac{\bar X_{\text{treatment}} - \bar X_{\text{control}}}{SD_{\text{pooled}}}.
 The rough conventions: 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large. (Reinhart 2015) A d of 0.8 means the average treated unit is 0.8 SDs above the average control — a gap you’d notice.
Correlation r and R^2 — “variance-accounted-for” effect sizes. (Reinhart 2015) R^2 runs 0 to 1 and reports the proportion of variation in the outcome explained, tying directly to regression’s goodness of fit.
Odds ratios / risk ratios — for binary outcomes, exponentiating a logistic coefficient gives an odds ratio, the relative change in the odds of the outcome (a hazard ratio plays the same role for durations). (Reinhart 2015)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-4, 5, 400)
def normal(x, mu): return np.exp(-0.5*(x-mu)**2) / np.sqrt(2*np.pi)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.6, 3.2), sharey=True)
for ax, d, lab in zip(axes, [0.2, 0.5, 0.8], ["small", "medium", "large"]):
    ax.fill_between(x, normal(x, 0), color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.7, label="control")
    ax.fill_between(x, normal(x, d), color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.5, label="treatment")
    ax.set_title(f"d = {d}  ({lab})", fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_yticks([]); ax.set_xlabel("outcome (SD units)")
axes[0].legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.suptitle("Cohen's d: the standardized distance between two groups", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: What Cohen’s d looks like. Two group distributions (control grey, treatment navy) separated by d = 0.2, 0.5, and 0.8 pooled standard deviations. ‘Small’ (d=0.2) is heavy overlap — a real but subtle shift; ‘large’ (d=0.8) is a visibly different distribution. The effect size translates a mean difference into a picture of how distinguishable the groups are, no matter the sample size.

title: Effect Sizes
section: Statistical vs practical significance, made concrete
text:
Statistical vs practical significance, made concrete
The two can point opposite ways: (Reinhart 2015)

Significant but trivial. Reinhart’s example: a “banana” that raises SAT scores by 0.9 points is statistically significant in a massive sample and practically meaningless. (Reinhart 2015) Big n + tiny effect = a real-but-pointless result.
Large but non-significant. A big effect in a small sample may fail the 0.05 test yet be important and worth following up — the effect size flags it where the p-value buries it. (Reinhart 2015)

This is why the effect size, with its confidence interval, tells the honest story: the CI shows the range of magnitudes the data are consistent with, not just a pass/fail verdict.

title: Effect Sizes
text:
The reporting reform
Because p-values are “like mosquitoes” — persistent despite their well-known flaws — journals increasingly require reporting effect sizes and confidence intervals alongside (or instead of) bare significance. (Reinhart 2015) The American Psychological Association has urged this since 1994, so results are read in their “situational context” rather than as a yes/no significance decision. (Reinhart 2015) It’s the same reform Kaplan and the modern critics press: lead with the magnitude.

title: Effect Sizes
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting only the p-value. It hides the magnitude; a reader can’t judge importance without the effect size and its CI. (Reinhart 2015)
Reading significance as size. A tiny p in a large sample can mean a trivial effect; check d / R^2. (Reinhart 2015)
Dismissing a non-significant large effect. In a small sample it may simply lack power; the effect size says it’s worth a bigger study. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating Cohen’s benchmarks as laws. 0.2/0.5/0.8 are rules of thumb; “large” in one field is “small” in another — interpret in context. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Effect Sizes
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — p as a “measure of surprise,” not magnitude; big-n detecting “tiny unimportant differences”; Cohen’s d (standardized mean difference; 0.2/0.5/0.8) and r/R^2 as “variance-accounted-for” effect sizes; the banana / 0.9-SAT-point example of statistical-but-not-practical significance (and the reverse); p-values “like mosquitoes” and the journal/APA (1994) push to report effect sizes and confidence intervals in “situational context.”
Bruce & Bruce / Martin & Bridgmon — effect size vs statistical significance; odds ratios and variance-explained measures in applied modelling.

title: Effect Sizes
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing (the significance it complements) and confidence intervals (the range it travels with).
Pairs with: statistical power (power is computed for a target effect size) and regression R^2 / odds & hazard ratios.
Drives: the reporting reforms of communicating findings and open science.

title: Confidence Intervals
text: A single estimate is a best guess with no error bars. A confidence interval wraps that guess in a range of plausible values — and carries a guarantee, though a subtler one than nearly everyone believes. The “95%” is a property of the procedure, not of the interval sitting in front of you.

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
Why it matters
“Support is 52%.” “The effect was 0.3 points.” A point estimate delivered alone is quietly dishonest: it tells you the best guess but says nothing about how close that guess is likely to be to the truth. (Bruce et al. 2020) Two studies can both report “52%” — one a careful national survey, one a classroom poll of thirty — and the bare number hides the chasm between them.
The confidence interval closes that gap. It is the natural sequel to estimation: take the point estimate and attach to it the standard error the sampling distribution gives you, and you get not a guess but a range of defensible guesses. Confidence intervals are also, not coincidentally, the single most misinterpreted object in quantitative research — so getting their logic exactly right is worth real effort.

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
What a confidence interval is
The recipe is short:

\text{point estimate} \;\pm\; \text{margin of error},
\qquad \text{margin of error} = (\text{critical value}) \times (\text{standard error}).

The standard error measures how much the estimate bounces from sample to sample; the critical value says how many standard errors you must reach out to capture your chosen share of that bounce. For a 95% interval the critical value is about 2 (more precisely 1.96 for the Normal). (Bruce et al. 2020) So a 95% interval reaches out roughly two standard errors on each side of the estimate — wide enough that, across repeated studies, the net it casts lands over the true value 95% of the time.

title: Confidence Intervals
section: The interpretation almost everyone gets wrong
text:
The interpretation almost everyone gets wrong
This is the crux, and it is worth slowing down. (Reinhart 2015)






ImportantThe two readings



Correct (frequentist). “If I repeated this whole sampling procedure many times, computing a fresh 95% interval each time, about 95% of those intervals would contain the true parameter.” The procedure has a 95% hit rate. (Bruce et al. 2020; Grinstead and Snell 1997)
Wrong (and seductive). “There is a 95% probability that the true value lies in this interval, [5.6\%,\, 9.4\%].” (Reinhart 2015)


Why is the second wrong? Because in the frequentist world the parameter is a fixed constant, not a random variable. (Reinhart 2015) Once you have computed your interval, [5.6\%, 9.4\%] are just two numbers, and the truth is a third fixed number. The truth is either between them or it isn’t — there is no probability left to assign to this particular, already-decided case. The randomness lived in the sampling, which is now over. The 95% describes the long-run reliability of the net, not the odds for the one cast you happen to be holding.
(This is precisely the door through which Bayesian inference will later walk: if you want to say “95% probability the parameter is in here,” you need a credible interval, which requires treating the parameter as random and supplying a prior. The two intervals answer different questions — a theme we develop fully in the Bayesian sub-chapter.)

Watch the procedure work
The cleanest way to feel the correct interpretation is to do the repeating that the definition imagines. Below, the true population proportion is fixed at 0.54 (an election too close to call). We run 100 independent polls of n = 1{,}000, build a 95% interval from each, and stack them up. The truth doesn’t move — the intervals do, and about five of them miss.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
p_true, n, n_polls = 0.54, 1000, 100

phat = rng.binomial(n, p_true, size=n_polls) / n
se   = np.sqrt(phat * (1 - phat) / n)
lo, hi = phat - 1.96 * se, phat + 1.96 * se
covers = (lo <= p_true) & (hi >= p_true)
n_miss = int((~covers).sum())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.4))
for i in range(n_polls):
    color = "#1f3a5f" if covers[i] else "#c0392b"
    ax.plot([lo[i], hi[i]], [i, i], color=color, lw=1.3, alpha=0.9)
ax.axvline(p_true, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.8)
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("Estimated support")
ax.set_title(f"{n_miss} of 100 intervals miss the truth — that is what '95% confidence' means")
ax.text(p_true + 0.004, 2, "true value 0.54", color="#b5446e", fontsize=9, rotation=90, va="bottom")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: 100 independent 95% confidence intervals for a true proportion of 0.54 (dashed line), each from a fresh poll of n = 1,000. The intervals jump around; the truth is fixed. Red intervals miss — and there are about five of them, because the procedure is right 95% of the time, not 100%.





Stare at the red intervals. Nothing went wrong in those polls — they followed the identical, correct procedure. They simply drew an unlucky sample, as roughly 5% of honest studies always will. That is the entire content of “95% confidence”: not a probability about your one interval, but a batting average of the method.

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
Building one: means and proportions
For a mean, use the sample mean and its standard error s/\sqrt n:

\bar y \;\pm\; c\cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt n},

where c is the critical value. (Wooldridge 2019) Wooldridge’s worked case: across 20 manufacturing firms, a 95% interval for the change in scrap rates came out as [-2.28,\,-0.02] — the whole interval sits below zero, so the data point to a genuine decrease in waste (barely). (Wooldridge 2019)
For a proportion, the standard error is \sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}:

\hat p \;\pm\; z\cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat p(1-\hat p)}{n}}.

This is the machinery behind every poll’s “margin of error.” (Grinstead and Snell 1997) To pin a proportion to a \pm 3\% margin (a 6-point-wide interval) you need about n = 1{,}111 respondents — which is why national polls cluster around a thousand. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) And the long-run promise checks out: in a simulation of 10,000 polls drawn from a true support of 0.54, about 9,648 (≈96%) of the computed intervals landed within 3% of the truth. (Grinstead and Snell 1997)

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
When \sigma is unknown: the t-distribution
The formulas above quietly assumed we knew the population spread \sigma. We almost never do — we estimate it with the sample standard deviation s, and that estimate is itself uncertain, especially in small samples. (Bruce et al. 2020) Plugging a noisy s into the formula and using the Normal critical value would understate our uncertainty.
The fix is the t-distribution, worked out by W. S. Gosset writing as “Student” while a brewery forbade him publishing under his own name. (Bruce et al. 2020) The t looks like the Normal but with fatter tails: to capture 95% of its area you must reach further out than 1.96, and that extra reach is precisely the penalty for not knowing \sigma. Its exact shape is set by the degrees of freedom, usually n-1 for a single mean — the same degree of freedom spent estimating the mean in the variance formula. As n grows, s pins down \sigma ever more tightly, the tails thin, and the t-distribution melts into the Normal: for n past ~30 the two are practically identical, which is why “t vs z” only matters for small samples. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Confidence Intervals
section: The bootstrap: a confidence interval with no formula
text:
The bootstrap: a confidence interval with no formula
What if there is no clean algebra for your statistic’s standard error — a median, a trimmed mean, a ratio? The bootstrap sidesteps the math entirely. (Bruce et al. 2020) Treat your sample as a mini-population and draw new samples from it, with replacement, each the same size n. Compute your statistic on each resample; after, say, 1,000 repetitions you have an empirical distribution of the statistic. For a 95% interval, simply trim the lowest and highest 2.5% — the remaining range is your interval. (Bruce et al. 2020)






TipWhat the bootstrap can and cannot do



It is superb for statistics with no tidy formula (medians, trimmed means) and frees you from assuming Normality. But it does not manufacture information: it reveals how much your existing sample would vary if redrawn from a similar population. It cannot rescue a small or biased sample — resampling 30 unrepresentative people 1,000 times just gives you 1,000 views of the same 30 people. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
Width: the anatomy of precision
The width of an interval is your precision, and three things drive it. (Bruce et al. 2020)

Sample size. Width shrinks like 1/\sqrt n, so — the recurring tax of the \sqrt n — to halve an interval you must quadruple the sample. (Grinstead and Snell 1997)
Confidence level. Demanding more confidence (99% vs 95%) forces a wider interval; to be surer of catching the truth you must cast a bigger net.
Variability. A noisier population (larger \sigma) yields wider, less precise intervals.

You can buy precision, but the exchange rate is brutal and fixed.

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
The duality with hypothesis testing
Confidence intervals and significance tests are two faces of one coin. (Wooldridge 2019) The rule:

If a hypothesised value falls outside the 95% confidence interval, you would reject that hypothesis at the 5% level — and vice versa.

The most-used special case: if a treatment effect’s 95% interval excludes zero, the effect is “statistically significant” at 5%; if it includes zero, it isn’t. (Wooldridge 2019) Wooldridge’s scrap-rate interval [-2.28, -0.02] excludes zero (just), so the decrease is significant at 5%. The interval does everything a test does and more — it shows the whole range of values you can’t reject, not merely a yes/no verdict — which is why many statisticians now urge reporting intervals over bare p-values. This duality is the bridge to the next entry.

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




“95% probability the truth is in this interval.” The single most common error. The procedure has a 95% hit rate; the fixed truth is either in your one interval or it isn’t. Want the probabilistic reading? That requires a Bayesian credible interval. (Reinhart 2015)
Eyeballing overlap between two intervals. It is not true that overlapping 95% intervals mean “no significant difference.” Two intervals can overlap and the difference still be significant — comparing two groups needs a test built for the difference, not a glance at their separate bars. “Your eyeball is not a well-defined statistical procedure.” (Reinhart 2015)
Using z when you should use t. With small n and an estimated \sigma, the Normal critical value gives intervals that are too narrow — falsely precise. Use t until n is comfortably large. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Treating the bootstrap as a small-sample cure. It quantifies the variability already in your data; it cannot create data you never collected or undo selection bias. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Confusing interval width with bias. A tight interval around a biased estimate is confidently wrong. Width measures precision (variance), not accuracy (bias) — both must be right.

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — point estimate ± margin of error; the critical-value-near-2 construction; the t-distribution, Gosset/“Student”, fatter tails and degrees of freedom; the bootstrap (resample with replacement, trim 2.5%) and its limits; the drivers of interval width.
Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — the correct-vs-wrong interpretation and why the wrong one is wrong (fixed parameter); the overlapping-error-bars fallacy; “your eyeball is not a statistical procedure.”
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the proportion interval and polling margin of error; n = 1{,}111 for \pm 3\%; the 10,000-poll coverage simulation (≈96% within 3% at p = 0.54).
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the mean interval \bar y \pm c\cdot s/\sqrt n; the 20-firm scrap-rate interval [-2.28,-0.02]; the duality between intervals and tests (excludes zero ⇔ significant).

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Where the mean interval comes from (the pivot). By the CLT, \bar X \approx \mathcal N(\mu, \sigma^2/n), so the standardised quantity Z = (\bar X - \mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n) is approximately standard Normal. Since \Pr(-1.96 \le Z \le 1.96) = 0.95, substitute and rearrange the inequality to isolate \mu:

\Pr\!\left(\bar X - 1.96\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n} \;\le\; \mu \;\le\; \bar X + 1.96\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}\right) = 0.95 .

The randomness is in the endpoints \bar X \pm 1.96\,\sigma/\sqrt n, not in \mu — which is exactly why the probability statement is about the interval, not the fixed \mu.
The t statistic. When \sigma is replaced by the sample s, the standardised quantity is no longer Normal but follows Student’s t with n-1 degrees of freedom:

T = \frac{\bar X - \mu}{s/\sqrt n} \sim t_{\,n-1}.

The interval becomes \bar X \pm t_{n-1,\,0.975}\, s/\sqrt n, with the t critical value exceeding 1.96 (e.g., 2.26 at n=10, df =9) and shrinking toward 1.96 as n\to\infty.
The proportion sample size. The margin is z\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}, worst-case at p = 0.5 where \sqrt{p(1-p)} = 0.5. Using the round critical value z \approx 2 gives the handy conservative rule \text{margin} \approx 2 \cdot 0.5/\sqrt n = 1/\sqrt n. Setting 1/\sqrt n = 0.03 yields

n = \frac{1}{(0.03)^2} \approx 1{,}111,

the figure the source quotes. (The slightly sharper z = 1.96 gives n \approx 1{,}068.) Either way: a \pm 3\% poll needs roughly a thousand respondents, and N never appears.
Bootstrap percentile interval. Draw B resamples of size n with replacement; compute \hat\theta^{*}_1,\dots,\hat\theta^{*}_B. The percentile 95% interval is \big[\hat\theta^{*}_{(0.025)},\, \hat\theta^{*}_{(0.975)}\big] — the empirical 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the resampled estimates. No Normality assumed; the data’s own shape drives the interval.

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
Connections

Builds on: estimation (a CI is a point estimate plus its standard error), sampling distributions & the CLT (the Normal/​t shape that sets the critical value), and the Law of Large Numbers (why the bootstrap and bigger n both work).
Leads to: hypothesis testing & p-values (the dual of the interval) and statistical power (how wide your interval will be before you collect data).
Reappears as: the credible interval in the Bayesian sub-chapter (the interval that does license “95% probability the truth is in here”), and the standard-error bands on every regression and causal estimate.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text: The t-test compares two group means. Real studies rarely stop at two, and rarely have means at all — they have three or more groups, categorical outcomes, binary conversions. This entry stocks the rest of the toolkit: ANOVA for many means, chi-square for categories, and proportion tests for rates — each answering “are these groups really different, or is it noise?”

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Why it matters
A t-test handles exactly two groups of numeric data. But you will constantly face questions it can’t: Do these four web layouts differ in engagement? Are click-throughs independent of which headline a visitor saw? Does price A convert better than price B? Each needs a different test, matched to the number of groups and the type of outcome. Knowing which test when — and why you can’t just paper over the gap with repeated t-tests — is core inferential literacy, and the sources treat all three as essential. (Bruce et al. 2020; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
section: First, the trap: why not just run many t-tests?
text:
First, the trap: why not just run many t-tests?
The tempting move with four groups is to t-test every pair. But four groups means six pairwise tests, and each carries its own 5% false-positive risk. (Bruce et al. 2020) As the replication-crisis entry showed, running many tests makes at least one spurious “hit” nearly inevitable — the multiple-comparisons problem in miniature. The fix is an omnibus test: a single test asking “is there any difference among the groups?” before you go hunting for which pair. (Bruce et al. 2020) That omnibus test is ANOVA.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
ANOVA and the F-test
Analysis of variance compares three or more group means with one test, and its engine is a beautiful idea: detect differences in means by comparing two kinds of variance. (Bruce et al. 2020)

Between-group variance — how far the group means scatter around the overall grand mean. This is the “signal”: real differences push group means apart.
Within-group variance — how much individual observations scatter inside their own group. This is the “noise”: natural variability that has nothing to do with the groups.

The F-statistic is their ratio: (Bruce et al. 2020; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

F = \frac{\text{between-group variance}}{\text{within-group variance}}
= \frac{\text{MS}_{\text{treatment}}}{\text{MS}_{\text{error}}}.

When the groups are truly identical, signal and noise are the same thing and F \approx 1. A large F says the group means differ by more than the within-group noise can explain, and the F-distribution converts it to a p-value. (Bruce et al. 2020) This rests on the ANOVA decomposition — total variation splits cleanly, exactly the law of total variance made into a test: (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

\underbrace{\text{SS}_{\text{total}}}_{\text{all variation}} =
\underbrace{\text{SS}_{\text{between}}}_{\text{explained by group}} +
\underbrace{\text{SS}_{\text{within}}}_{\text{residual noise}},

with degrees of freedom K-1 between (for K groups) and N-K within. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

Seeing the F-ratio
F is a signal-to-noise ratio, so the same set of group means can be clearly significant or clearly not — it all depends on the within-group scatter. Below, three groups sit at identical means in both panels; only the noise differs.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
means = np.array([168.0, 176.0, 184.0])
n = 30

def f_stat(groups):
    allx = np.concatenate(groups)
    grand = allx.mean()
    k, N = len(groups), len(allx)
    ss_between = sum(len(g) * (g.mean() - grand) ** 2 for g in groups)
    ss_within  = sum(((g - g.mean()) ** 2).sum() for g in groups)
    return (ss_between / (k - 1)) / (ss_within / (N - k))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.8), sharey=True)
for ax, sd, label in zip(axes, [14.0, 3.5], ["large noise", "small noise"]):
    groups = [rng.normal(m, sd, n) for m in means]
    F = f_stat(groups)
    for i, g in enumerate(groups):
        ax.scatter(np.full(n, i) + rng.uniform(-0.08, 0.08, n), g,
                   s=12, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6)
        ax.plot([i - 0.25, i + 0.25], [g.mean()] * 2, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.5)
    ax.axhline(np.concatenate(groups).mean(), color="#7a7f87", ls="--", lw=1.2)
    ax.set_title(f"{label}:  F = {F:.1f}", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xticks([0, 1, 2]); ax.set_xticklabels(["Group 1", "Group 2", "Group 3"])
axes[0].set_ylabel("outcome")
fig.suptitle("Same means, different noise — F is signal ÷ noise", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: ANOVA is signal ÷ noise. Both panels have the same three group means (dashed grey = grand mean). Left: large within-group scatter swamps the gaps between means — F is near 1, not significant. Right: tight groups make the same mean-gaps leap out — a large F, clearly significant. The means didn’t change; the noise did.











NoteBruce & Bruce’s web-page stickiness test



A company compared four page layouts by how long visitors stayed (seconds), 5 visitors per page (N=20). The group means were Page 1: 172, Page 2: 185, Page 3: 176, Page 4: 162, around a grand mean of 173.75. ANOVA gave F = 2.74 on (3, 16) degrees of freedom, with p = 0.0776. Since p > 0.05, the differences are not significant — the spread among those four means is within what chance alone would produce. The eye-catching 23-second gap between the best and worst page is, statistically, noise. (Bruce et al. 2020)


If the omnibus F is significant, you follow up with post-hoc tests like Tukey’s Honestly Significant Difference to find which pairs differ — and, crucially, only after a significant F, which keeps the multiple-comparison error rate in check. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
section: The chi-square test: are categories related?
text:
The chi-square test: are categories related?
When the data are counts in categories, not means, the workhorse is the chi-square test. It asks whether the observed frequencies in a contingency table depart from what you’d expect if the variables were independent. (Bruce et al. 2020) The logic is observed vs expected: compute the counts you’d see under “no relationship,” then measure the total discrepancy: (Bruce et al. 2020)

\chi^2 = \sum_{\text{cells}} \frac{(O - E)^2}{E},
\qquad \text{df} = (\text{rows}-1)\times(\text{columns}-1).

Each cell contributes its squared surprise, scaled by what was expected; a large total means the data stray far from independence. (Bruce et al. 2020)






NoteBruce & Bruce’s headline test



Three headlines (A, B, C) were each shown to 1,000 visitors; clicks were 14, 8, 12 (total 34). Under the null that all headlines convert equally, the expected rate is 34/3000 \approx 1.13\%, so each headline’s expected clicks are \approx \mathbf{11.33}. The statistic works out to \chi^2 = 1.666 on \text{df} = (3-1)(2-1) = 2. That value sits well inside the range of ordinary chance variation: the headlines do not differ significantly. The 14-vs-8 gap is the kind of wobble random clicks produce. (Bruce et al. 2020)


The same machinery does goodness of fit — testing whether one categorical variable matches a hypothesised distribution (are digits uniform? are arrivals as forecast?). And when counts are tiny (expected cells around 5 or fewer), the chi-square approximation breaks down; switch to Fisher’s exact test, which computes the exact probability of the observed table. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Tests for proportions
The most common metric in applied and data-science work is a binary proportion — converted or not, clicked or not, turned out to vote or not. (Bruce et al. 2020) To compare two such rates, the two-proportion z-test uses the Normal approximation to the binomial. (For two groups this is algebraically equivalent to a 2\times2 chi-square — same question, two dialects.)






NoteBruce & Bruce’s price test



An e-commerce experiment compared two prices. Price A converted 200 of 23,739 (0.842%); Price B 182 of 22,588 (0.806%). Despite 45,000+ visitors, the two-proportion test returned p = 0.3498 — not significant. The lesson is sharp: with very low base rates, statistical power comes from the number of conversions, not the number of exposures. Tens of thousands of visitors can still leave you underpowered if almost none convert. (Bruce et al. 2020) (A direct echo of the power entry.)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Which test when?



Outcome type
Two groups
Three or more groups




Numeric (means)
two-sample t-test
ANOVA / F-test (then Tukey HSD)


Categorical (counts)
2\times2 chi-square (or Fisher’s exact if small)
chi-square on the full table


Binary (proportion)
two-proportion z-test
chi-square across groups



Pick by the shape of your outcome and the number of groups; the question — “real difference or noise?” — is always the same.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Running pairwise t-tests instead of ANOVA. Many comparisons inflate the false-positive rate; use the omnibus F first, post-hoc tests only after. (Bruce et al. 2020; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
Mining post-hoc pairs after a non-significant F. If the omnibus test is null, hunting for a “significant pair” is p-hacking. The gate exists for a reason. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
Using chi-square with tiny expected counts. Below ~5 per cell the approximation misleads — switch to Fisher’s exact. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Confusing exposures with conversions. A huge sample with a rare outcome can still be underpowered; precision tracks the count of events, not of trials. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Reporting a significant F as the whole story. ANOVA says some group differs, not which or by how much. Follow up with post-hoc tests and effect sizes. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the multiplicity problem motivating ANOVA; the F-test as between- over within-group variance; the web-page-stickiness example (means 172/185/176/162; F=2.74, p=0.0776, not significant); the chi-square observed-vs-expected logic and statistic; the headline-click example (14/8/12; expected 11.33; \chi^2=1.666, df 2, not significant); goodness-of-fit and Fisher’s exact for small counts; the two-proportion test and the e-commerce price example (p=0.3498 despite 45,000+ trials).
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative & Statistical Research Methods — the ANOVA sum-of-squares decomposition and degrees of freedom (K-1, N-K); post-hoc Tukey HSD and the rule to run it only after a significant omnibus F.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The one-way ANOVA F-statistic. For K groups of sizes n_j with group means \bar y_j, grand mean \bar y, and total size N:

\text{SS}_{\text{between}} = \sum_{j=1}^K n_j(\bar y_j - \bar y)^2, \qquad
\text{SS}_{\text{within}} = \sum_{j=1}^K \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} (y_{ij} - \bar y_j)^2,


F = \frac{\text{SS}_{\text{between}}/(K-1)}{\text{SS}_{\text{within}}/(N-K)}
= \frac{\text{MS}_{\text{between}}}{\text{MS}_{\text{within}}} \;\sim\; F_{K-1,\,N-K}\ \text{under } H_0.

Under the null (all group means equal) both mean squares estimate the same \sigma^2, so F\approx1; real differences inflate only the numerator. This is the total-variance decomposition \operatorname{Var}(Y) =
\operatorname{Var}(E[Y\mid g]) + E[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid g)] turned into a test of the between-group term.
Chi-square expected counts. For a contingency table, the expected count under independence is E_{ij} = (\text{row}_i\,\text{total})(\text{col}_j\,\text{total})/N. The headline test: expected = 1000 \times 34/3000 = 11.33 clicks each, and \chi^2 = \frac{(14-11.33)^2}{11.33} + \frac{(8-11.33)^2}{11.33} + \frac{(12-11.33)^2}{11.33} on the clicks, plus the matching non-click cells, \approx 1.67 — small for df =2.
Two-proportion z-test. With pooled proportion \hat p = (x_1+x_2)/(n_1+n_2),

z = \frac{\hat p_1 - \hat p_2}{\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)\left(\tfrac{1}{n_1}+\tfrac{1}{n_2}\right)}}.

Squaring this z gives exactly the 2\times2 chi-square statistic — the two tests are one.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing & p-values (the null/alternative logic and the t-test it generalises), the law of total variance (ANOVA’s between/within split), and the common distributions (the F, chi-square, and Normal-approximation-to-binomial).
Sits beside: resampling & distribution-free tests — the assumption-light alternatives when ANOVA’s normality fails.
Leads to: regression, which contains ANOVA as a special case (regression on group dummies reproduces the F-test) and extends it to continuous predictors and controls.

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
text: A posterior is an answer conditional on the model being true. Before you trust it you must ask does the model fit? (checking) and, among rivals, which predicts best? (comparison). Bayesian practice answers the first with posterior predictive checks — simulate fake data and see if it looks real — and the second with out-of-sample predictive accuracy (LOO-CV, WAIC), not with the seductive-but-treacherous Bayes factor.

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
text:
Why it matters
The most expensive Bayesian mistakes come from skipping these steps: trusting a model that never fit, or selecting among models with a tool (Bayes factors) that quietly lets the prior drive the verdict. This entry gives the working checklist — what to run, what each catches, and what to distrust — closing the Bayesian sub-track with the discipline that keeps it honest.

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
section: Posterior predictive checks — your best friend
text:
Posterior predictive checks — your best friend
The posterior predictive distribution generates the data you might collect tomorrow if the data-generating process holds (Lambert 2018). You sample it in two steps (Lambert 2018):

Draw a parameter from the posterior, \theta^{(i)} \sim p(\theta\mid\text{data}).
Draw a new dataset from the likelihood, x'^{(i)} \sim p(x'\mid\theta^{(i)}).

The logic is disarmingly simple: if the model fits well, simulated data should be indistinguishable from the real data (Lambert 2018). You compare them graphically, or via test statistics — the minimum, maximum, or proportion of zeros are common choices (Lambert 2018). The Bayesian p-value is the fraction of simulated datasets whose statistic is as extreme or more extreme than the observed one; near 0.5 is healthy, near 0 or 1 signals misfit (Lambert 2018). Lambert calls PPCs your “best friend” because they are superb at “hinting at a model’s inadequacies” (Lambert 2018). (The prior predictive distribution is the same idea using prior draws — the data your model can generate before seeing any.) (Lambert 2018)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
n = 200
# observed data: zero-inflated counts (40% structural zeros, else Poisson(3))
structural_zero = rng.random(n) < 0.40
y_obs = np.where(structural_zero, 0, rng.poisson(3, n))

lam = y_obs.mean()                       # fit a plain Poisson matched to the mean
S = 2000
reps = rng.poisson(lam, size=(S, n))     # replicated datasets from the fitted model

prop_zero_rep = (reps == 0).mean(axis=1)
mean_rep = reps.mean(axis=1)
prop_zero_obs = (y_obs == 0).mean()
mean_obs = y_obs.mean()
p_zeros = np.mean(prop_zero_rep >= prop_zero_obs)
p_mean = np.mean(mean_rep >= mean_obs)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.hist(prop_zero_rep, bins=30, color="#cdd6df", edgecolor="white")
axL.axvline(prop_zero_obs, color="#c0392b", lw=2.4)
axL.set_title(f"T = proportion of zeros\nBayesian p ≈ {p_zeros:.2f}  → MISFIT", fontsize=10)
axL.set_xlabel("proportion of zeros in replicated data")
axL.text(prop_zero_obs-0.01, axL.get_ylim()[1]*0.6, "observed", color="#c0392b",
         ha="right", fontsize=8.5, rotation=90)

axR.hist(mean_rep, bins=30, color="#cdd6df", edgecolor="white")
axR.axvline(mean_obs, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4)
axR.set_title(f"T = mean\nBayesian p ≈ {p_mean:.2f}  → looks fine", fontsize=10)
axR.set_xlabel("mean of replicated data")
fig.suptitle("Posterior predictive check: the right statistic exposes the misfit", fontsize=11.5)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A posterior predictive check that catches a misfit the mean would hide. Observed data: 200 counts that are zero-inflated (many structural zeros). We fit a plain Poisson matched to the observed mean, then simulate 2000 replicated datasets. Left: the test statistic ‘proportion of zeros’ — the observed value (red) sits far in the tail of the replicated distribution (Bayesian p ≈ 0): the Poisson cannot make enough zeros, so it’s badly misspecified. Right: the test statistic ‘mean’ — observed sits dead-centre (p ≈ 0.5), because we matched it by construction. Lesson: a model can nail the mean and still be wrong — PPCs on the right statistic reveal it.

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
section: Bayes factors — and why to distrust them
text:
Bayes factors — and why to distrust them
A Bayes factor treats the model itself as a parameter and compares two models by the ratio of their marginal likelihoods (the “evidence”) (Lambert 2018):

\text{BF}_{1,0} = \frac{p(\text{data}\mid M_1)}{p(\text{data}\mid M_0)} .

Lambert reads it on Jeffreys’ scale: 1–3 weak, 3–10 moderate, 10–100 strong, >100 “decisive” evidence for M_1 (Lambert 2018). But the weaknesses are severe (Lambert 2018):

Intractability. The marginal likelihood is the same high-dimensional integral that defeats exact Bayesian inference (Lambert 2018).
Prior sensitivity (Jeffreys–Lindley paradox). BFs are extremely sensitive to the prior even when the posterior is not — and a very diffuse prior will always unfairly favour the simpler model (Lambert 2018).
Undefined under improper priors. The arbitrary normalising constant does not cancel, so a researcher can change the answer at will (Lambert 2018).

The practical upshot: prefer predictive criteria below for routine model comparison; reserve Bayes factors for cases with genuinely informative, proper priors.

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
text:
Information criteria: estimating out-of-sample fit
Information criteria estimate out-of-sample predictive performance from in-sample data, correcting the bias of using the data twice (Lambert 2018):

Deviance = -2\ln L — the raw badness-of-fit (Lambert 2018).
DIC (Deviance Information Criterion) — a Bayesian AIC using the posterior-mean estimate, penalised by the effective number of parameters p_D = 2\operatorname{Var}_{\text{posterior}}(\ln p(y\mid\theta)) (Lambert 2018).
WAIC (Widely Applicable Information Criterion) — the fully Bayesian, pointwise criterion: it treats each data point separately and uses the full posterior (not a point estimate), with a penalty equal to the sum of the posterior variances of the pointwise log-likelihood (Lambert 2018). WAIC is the modern default.

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
text:
Cross-validation: LOO and PSIS-LOO
Cross-validation is the “ideal measure” of predictive accuracy — it uses a genuinely held-out point (Lambert 2018). Leave-one-out (LOO-CV) trains on n-1 points and tests on the one left out, repeated for all n (Lambert 2018). The target is the elpd — the expected log pointwise predictive density, the distance between the model’s predictions and the true data-generating distribution (Lambert 2018). Refitting n times is expensive, so Lambert recommends PSIS-LOO (Pareto-Smoothed Importance Sampling), which approximates LOO from a single posterior fit using importance weights (Lambert 2018). (Its Pareto-k diagnostic flags points where the approximation is unreliable.)

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
section: Occam’s razor and the bias–variance balance
text:
Occam’s razor and the bias–variance balance
Model comparison is a balance between fitting this sample and generalising to the next (Lambert 2018):

Bayesian Occam’s razor. A complex model must spread its probability mass thinly over many possible datasets, so it is “punished” for wasting probability on outcomes that don’t occur — simpler models that concentrate mass on what did happen are automatically favoured (Lambert 2018).
Bias–variance. Complex models can eliminate bias but inflate variance, making predictions hypersensitive to the training sample (Lambert 2018) — the bias–variance trade-off in Bayesian dress.
Overfitting = cheating. Lambert’s image: overfitting is “stealing the answers before a test” — it performs perfectly on old data and “fails miserably on new data” (Lambert 2018).







TipA practical hierarchy



Always run posterior predictive checks — they catch misfit no number can. For comparing models, prefer LOO-CV / WAIC (predictive accuracy). Treat Bayes factors with suspicion unless your priors are proper and meaningful. And let Occam break ties toward the simpler model. (Lambert 2018)

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Skipping PPCs. A converged chain on a misspecified model gives precise, confident, wrong answers — the check is non-optional. (Lambert 2018)
Selecting by Bayes factor under vague priors. Jeffreys–Lindley means the diffuse prior decides; you’ll systematically over-favour the null/simple model. (Lambert 2018)
Computing a Bayes factor with improper priors. The result is arbitrary — don’t report it. (Lambert 2018)
Reading WAIC/LOO differences without their standard error. Small elpd gaps are within noise; compare the difference to its SE before declaring a winner. (Lambert 2018)
Choosing the model with the best in-sample fit. That rewards overfitting; judge by out-of-sample prediction. (Lambert 2018)

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — posterior predictive checks (the two-step simulation, graphical and test-statistic comparison with min/max/proportion-of-zeros, the Bayesian p-value ≈ 0.5 ideal, PPCs as “best friend”) and the prior predictive; Bayes factors as the marginal-likelihood ratio, Jeffreys’ scale, and the three weaknesses (intractability, prior sensitivity / Jeffreys–Lindley, undefined under improper priors); deviance, DIC with p_D=2\operatorname{Var}_{\text{post}}(\ln p), and WAIC (pointwise, fully Bayesian); LOO-CV, the elpd, and PSIS-LOO; Bayesian Occam’s razor (complex models spread mass thin and get punished), the bias–variance balance, and overfitting as “cheating on a test.”

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Bayesian p-value, defined. For a test statistic T, p_B = \Pr\!\big(T(y^{\text{rep}},\theta) \ge T(y,\theta)\mid y\big), averaged over the posterior of \theta and the replicated y^{\text{rep}}. Unlike a frequentist p-value it is not uniform under the null — but extreme values (≈0 or 1) still flag the statistics the model fails to reproduce.
WAIC. With pointwise log predictive density \text{lppd} = \sum_i \log \mathbb{E}_{\text{post}}[p(y_i\mid\theta)] and penalty p_{\text{WAIC}} = \sum_i \operatorname{Var}_{\text{post}}(\log p(y_i\mid\theta)), \text{WAIC} = -2(\text{lppd} - p_{\text{WAIC}}). Both terms are computed directly from posterior draws — no point estimate, no plug-in — which is what makes it “fully Bayesian.”
elpd and LOO. The leave-one-out estimate is \text{elpd}_{\text{loo}} = \sum_i \log p(y_i\mid y_{-i}), where p(y_i\mid y_{-i}) is the predictive density for point i from a fit excluding it. PSIS approximates each p(y_i\mid y_{-i}) by reweighting the full-fit posterior draws with importance weights \propto 1/p(y_i\mid\theta^{(s)}), Pareto-smoothed for stability.
The Bayesian Occam factor. The evidence factorises as (best-fit likelihood) × (Occam factor), where the Occam factor \approx posterior width / prior width < 1 — a model with many parameters or wide priors pays a larger penalty because it spread its prior predictive mass over more datasets. This is the automatic parsimony Bayes factors encode (and also why they depend so heavily on prior width).

title: Bayesian Model Checking & Comparison
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Bayesian machinery (the marginal likelihood / evidence), MCMC in depth and hierarchical models (the models being compared), and the bias–variance trade-off.
Mirrors: cross-validation and AIC/BIC on the predictive side — WAIC/LOO are their fully-Bayesian counterparts.
Closes: the Bayesian Inference sub-track of Part II.

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
text: An A/B test is a randomized experiment in digital dress: split users at random between the old version (A) and the new (B), compare one pre-chosen metric, and let the hypothesis test tell you whether the difference is more than chance (Bruce et al. 2020). It is the workhorse of the modern web. But it has a hidden cost: while you run it, half your users keep seeing the losing version — and you only learn about two options. Multi-armed bandits attack that waste head-on, turning the experiment itself into a profit-seeking machine that shifts traffic toward the winner as it learns. This entry covers both: the discipline of a clean test, and the explore–exploit logic that sometimes beats testing entirely.

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
text:
Why it matters
Almost every product decision at scale — a headline, a price, a button colour, a recommendation — is settled by an online experiment. Done right, A/B testing is the cleanest causal evidence you will ever get cheaply: randomization does all the work the Furious Five strain to approximate. Done wrong — peeking until significant, fishing for a metric, stopping early — it manufactures false discoveries at industrial rates. And in many business settings the classic fixed test is simply the wrong tool: when the aim is to maximise reward, not to publish an effect, a bandit reaches the best option faster and bleeds fewer customers on the way. Knowing which regime you are in is the skill.

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
section: The A/B test: a randomized experiment in digital dress
text:
The A/B test: a randomized experiment in digital dress
Three disciplines make an A/B test trustworthy (Bruce et al. 2020):

A control group. The treatment group sees the change; the control sees the standard version. Without a control there is no assurance that “other things are equal” — the control is exposed to the same time-of-day, seasonality, and traffic mix, so any difference between the groups can’t be blamed on those (Bruce et al. 2020).
Random assignment. Subjects are split at random, so any group difference is due to the treatment or to the luck of the draw — never to systematic bias in who got what (Bruce et al. 2020). This is the same logic that powers causal inference.
A single, pre-specified metric. Choose the test statistic — conversion rate, revenue per visitor, session length — before the test. Picking the winning metric afterwards is data snooping, and it guarantees you find “significance” somewhere (Bruce et al. 2020).

The analysis is then ordinary inference: a two-proportion test or — increasingly — the permutation and bootstrap machinery that gauges chance variation without leaning on a formula (Bruce et al. 2020). The null hypothesis is the skeptic’s baseline, “the difference is just chance”; a small p-value is evidence against it (Bruce et al. 2020).

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
section: Design it before you run it
text:
Design it before you run it
The single most important decision happens before any data arrives: how big must the test be? Bruce & Bruce frame it as four interlocking quantities — fix any three and the fourth follows (Bruce et al. 2020):



Quantity
What it is




Effect size
the smallest improvement you care to detect (e.g. a 10% lift in conversions)


Significance α
your false-positive threshold (usually 5%) — a Type I error


Power 1-\beta
the chance of catching a real effect (convention: 80%); missing it is a Type II error


Sample size n
how many users you must collect



The lever that hurts is effect size: the smaller the effect you want to detect, the dramatically larger the sample you need (Bruce et al. 2020). Computing the required n in advance is what stops you from running an underpowered test that was doomed to be inconclusive — a full treatment lives in statistical power & sample size. Fixing n ahead of time also disarms the most common online-experiment sin, below.






WarningThe peeking problem (optional stopping)



The tempting move — watch the dashboard and stop the moment it crosses significance — quietly destroys the test (Bruce et al. 2020). Every peek is another chance for noise to wander past the threshold, so repeated looks inflate the false-positive rate far above the nominal 5% (check a test enough times and a null difference will eventually look “significant”). The fixes are exactly two: commit to a pre-computed sample size and analyse once, or use a sequential method designed to allow continuous monitoring. Eyeballing and stopping is neither. This is optional stopping, the same bug behind the replication crisis and a cousin of the multiple-testing problem.

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
section: From testing to optimising: explore vs exploit
text:
From testing to optimising: explore vs exploit
A fixed A/B test answers a static question — “is B better than A?” — and to answer it cleanly it keeps sending half the traffic to the loser right up to the end, even once the data strongly hint at a winner (Bruce et al. 2020). In a business that is pure waste, and it scales badly: classic tests handle two arms, but you often have five headlines or ten prices to try at once (Bruce et al. 2020).
Reframe the problem and the waste disappears. Picture a row of slot machines — “one-armed bandits” — each with an unknown payout rate; you want to maximise your total winnings across many pulls (Bruce et al. 2020). Every pull forces the central dilemma:

Exploit — play the arm that looks best so far, to cash in now.
Explore — try other arms, to learn whether one is secretly better.

Pure exploitation can lock onto a false early leader; pure exploration is just a never-ending A/B test. A good multi-armed bandit algorithm balances the two, steering traffic toward the apparent winner while still sampling the rest enough to stay honest (Bruce et al. 2020).

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
text:
Two bandit algorithms
Epsilon-greedy — the simplest balance (Bruce et al. 2020):

Draw a random number between 0 and 1.
If it is below ε, explore: pick an arm at random.
Otherwise (≥ ε), exploit: serve the arm with the best response rate so far.

The knob ε tunes the trade-off: ε = 1 is a plain A/B test (always explore), ε = 0 is pure greed (never experiment again, so it can get stuck on a false leader) (Bruce et al. 2020). A small ε (say 0.1) keeps a trickle of exploration alive.
Thompson sampling — the Bayesian bandit, and usually the better one (Bruce et al. 2020). Keep a posterior belief about each arm’s success rate — a Beta distribution, updated with every win and loss. At each step, draw one sample from each arm’s posterior and play whichever sample is highest (Bruce et al. 2020). Arms that are probably good get played often; arms that are merely uncertain still get tried (their wide posteriors occasionally sample high) — so exploration is automatic and self-extinguishing as evidence accumulates. The objective has shifted from cleanly measuring a difference to minimising regret — the cumulative reward lost by not having played the best arm all along (Bruce et al. 2020).


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
p = np.array([0.030, 0.050, 0.045, 0.055, 0.040])   # true conversion rates
K = p.size; best = p.max(); best_arm = int(p.argmax())
T, runs, eps = 1200, 150, 0.10

def argmax_random(v):                # argmax with random tie-break
    m = np.flatnonzero(v == v.max())
    return int(m[0] if m.size == 1 else rng.choice(m))

def simulate(kind):
    regret = np.zeros(T); share = np.zeros(T)
    for _ in range(runs):
        succ = np.zeros(K); fail = np.zeros(K); cum = 0.0
        for t in range(T):
            if kind == "ab":                                   # fixed: equal allocation
                arm = t % K
            elif kind == "eps":                                # epsilon-greedy
                if rng.random() < eps:
                    arm = int(rng.integers(K))
                else:
                    arm = argmax_random((succ + 1) / (succ + fail + 2))   # Laplace-smoothed rate
            else:                                              # Thompson sampling
                arm = int(np.argmax(rng.beta(1 + succ, 1 + fail)))
            reward = 1.0 if rng.random() < p[arm] else 0.0
            if reward: succ[arm] += 1
            else:      fail[arm] += 1
            cum += best - p[arm]
            regret[t] += cum
            share[t]  += (arm == best_arm)
    return regret / runs, share / runs

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.6, 4.3))
styles = [("ab", "fixed A/B (equal split)", "#888888"),
          ("eps", "epsilon-greedy (ε=0.1)", "#d97706"),
          ("ts", "Thompson sampling", "#1f4e79")]
steps = np.arange(1, T + 1)
for kind, label, c in styles:
    reg, sh = simulate(kind)
    axL.plot(steps, reg, color=c, lw=2, label=label)
    axR.plot(steps, sh, color=c, lw=2, label=label)

axL.set_xlabel("users (time)"); axL.set_ylabel("cumulative regret (conversions lost)")
axL.set_title("Regret: the cost of still exploring"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
axR.axhline(1 / K, color="#888888", ls=":", lw=1)
axR.set_xlabel("users (time)"); axR.set_ylabel("share of traffic to the best arm")
axR.set_title("Where the traffic goes"); axR.set_ylim(0, 1.0)
axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Why adaptive beats fixed, simulated by hand over many runs. Five ‘arms’ (think headlines) have true conversion rates 3.0–5.5%; the best is arm 4 at 5.5%. LEFT: cumulative regret — total conversions lost versus an oracle that always played the best arm. A fixed A/B test (equal split throughout) keeps sending 4/5 of traffic to inferior arms, so its regret grows in a straight line forever. Epsilon-greedy (ε=0.1) and Thompson sampling bend sharply: once they identify the winner they stop wasting pulls, and regret nearly flattens. RIGHT: the share of traffic sent to the BEST arm — fixed A/B is pinned at 1/5 = 20% by design, while the bandits climb toward 90–97%. Thompson edges out epsilon-greedy by exploring smarter. The price (see text): the bandits’ biased sampling makes a clean, unbiased effect estimate much harder to recover.

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
text:
Which one should you run?









Fixed A/B test
Multi-armed bandit




Goal
measure the effect cleanly
maximise reward while learning


Allocation
equal, fixed in advance
adaptive, shifts to the winner


Arms
usually 2
scales to many (Bruce et al. 2020)


Best for
a decision you must defend; an unbiased effect size; regulated / scientific settings
web, marketing, e-commerce; rapid optimisation; short-lived content (Bruce et al. 2020)


The catch
wastes traffic on the loser; only two options
biased sampling makes a clean, unbiased effect estimate and p-value hard to recover (Bruce et al. 2020)



The honest summary (Bruce et al. 2020): bandits get you to the best option faster and cheaper, but because they deliberately sample toward the winner, they sacrifice clean inference — you can no longer read off an unbiased difference or a textbook p-value. Choose the fixed test when you need to know and defend the effect; choose the bandit when you need to win and move on.

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Peeking and early stopping. Repeated looks inflate false positives; fix n in advance or use a proper sequential design. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Choosing the metric after the fact. A single statistic, pre-specified — anything else is fishing. (Bruce et al. 2020)
No control group. Comparing “after” to “before” without a concurrent control confounds the change with time, seasonality, and traffic shifts. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Running underpowered. Too small an n for the effect you care about is a Type II error waiting to happen — money spent on an inconclusive result. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Reading a bandit like an A/B test. Its adaptive, biased allocation means the naïve difference in rates is not an unbiased treatment effect; don’t quote it as one. (Bruce et al. 2020)
ε = 0 greed. Switching off exploration entirely can lock you onto a false early leader forever. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce, Bruce & Gedeck, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the A/B test as a randomized experiment (control vs treatment, random assignment, a single pre-specified metric, the role of the control group); hypothesis testing and significance (null hypothesis, p-values, α, Type I/II errors, one- vs two-tailed); power and sample size as four interlocking quantities and why to fix n in advance; the peeking/“torturing the data” hazard; multi-armed bandits (the slot-machine metaphor, explore-vs-exploit, the inefficiency of fixed tests); the epsilon-greedy algorithm step by step and the meaning of ε = 0 and ε = 1; Thompson sampling as a Bayesian (Beta) bandit; regret minimisation and scalability to many arms; and the bandit-vs-A/B trade-off (faster optimisation vs cleaner inference).

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Sample size for two proportions. To detect a lift from p_A to p_B (with \delta=p_B-p_A) at significance \alpha and power 1-\beta, the per-arm sample size is 
n \;\approx\; \frac{\bigl(z_{1-\alpha/2}+z_{1-\beta}\bigr)^2\,\bigl[p_A(1-p_A)+p_B(1-p_B)\bigr]}{\delta^{2}} .
 Because n\propto 1/\delta^2, halving the effect you want to detect quadruples the users you need — the quantitative form of Bruce & Bruce’s “smaller effect size → much larger sample.” A handy rule of thumb for \alpha=0.05, 80\% power is n\approx 16\,\bar p(1-\bar p)/\delta^2 per arm.
Regret. With arms of mean reward p_k and best arm p^{*}=\max_k p_k, choosing arm a_t at step t incurs cumulative regret 
R_T \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T}\bigl(p^{*}-p_{a_t}\bigr)\;=\;T p^{*}-\sum_t p_{a_t}.
 A fixed equal-allocation test pulls each arm T/K times, so its regret grows linearly, R_T=\tfrac{T}{K}\sum_k (p^{*}-p_k)=\Theta(T) — the straight line in the figure. Good bandits achieve sub-linear regret, R_T=O(\log T): the per-user waste tends to zero, which is why their curves flatten.
Thompson sampling is Beta–Bernoulli. With a \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_0,\beta_0) prior on an arm’s success rate, observing s successes and f failures gives the conjugate posterior \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_0+s,\ \beta_0+f). At each step you draw \theta_k\sim\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_0+s_k,\beta_0+f_k) for every arm and play \arg\max_k\theta_k. Arms with high posteriors are usually picked (exploit); arms with wide posteriors occasionally sample high (explore) — so exploration is automatic and fades exactly as fast as the evidence sharpens.

title: A/B Testing & Multi-Armed Bandits
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing and comparing groups (the two-proportion test under the hood), statistical power (the sample-size calculation), and resampling (how A/B results are often analysed).
Guards against: optional stopping and p-hacking and the multiple-testing inflation that peeking is a special case of.
Bayesian engine: Thompson sampling is Bayesian updating with a Beta–Bernoulli conjugate posterior, used for decisions rather than estimation.
Causally: an A/B test is a randomized experiment — the cleanest identification strategy there is.

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text: You rarely study a random variable raw. You log skewed income, you add up item scores into a total, you ask a computer to simulate a distribution. Each move transforms one random variable into another, and each follows a precise rule. Learn the three — change of variables, convolution, and the probability integral transform — and you can track chance through almost any manipulation you will perform.

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text:
Why it matters
Three everyday operations are all secretly the same mathematical question — if I know the distribution of X, what is the distribution of the thing I built from it?

You transform a variable: take logs of income, square a deviation, standardise a score.
You sum variables: total a survey battery, aggregate daily sales, pool independent trials.
You simulate: every Monte Carlo draw a computer makes rests on one transformation theorem.

This entry supplies the rules. It also closes a loop left open in the random-variables entry (how a function of X gets its distribution) and lays the mechanical groundwork beneath the Central Limit Theorem: the CLT is, after all, a statement about what happens when you keep summing.

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
section: Transforming a single variable: change of variables
text:
Transforming a single variable: change of variables
Suppose Y = g(X) for a strictly monotonic g, and you know X’s density f_X. The density of Y follows the change-of-variables formula (Ross 2018):

f_Y(y) = f_X\!\big(g^{-1}(y)\big)\,\left|\frac{d}{dy}\,g^{-1}(y)\right|.
