The first factor relocates the probability; the second — the derivative (Jacobian) — rescales it.






TipWhy the derivative factor must be there



A density is probability per unit length, so it must respond to how the transformation stretches or compresses the axis. If g spreads X’s values over a wider range of Y, the same total probability is smeared over more room, so the density must be thinner there; if g compresses, the density piles up thicker. The derivative is exactly that stretch factor, and it is what keeps the total area equal to 1. (Ross 2018)








NoteRoss’s worked example: Y = Xⁿ



Let X be uniform on (0,1) and Y = X^n. Work through the cdf (always the safe route): 
F_Y(y) = P\{X^n \le y\} = P\{X \le y^{1/n}\} = y^{1/n}, \qquad 0 \le y \le 1,
 since X is uniform. Differentiate to get the density: 
f_Y(y) = \frac{1}{n}\,y^{\frac1n - 1}, \qquad 0 \le y \le 1.
 A flat distribution, cubed, becomes one piled up near zero — the transformation reshaped the chances. (Ross 2018)

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text:
The log-normal: taming the tail
The most important transformation in social science is the logarithm, and the distribution it produces has a name. A positive variable Y is log-normal if \log Y is Normal. (Ross 2018) This is the workhorse model for income, wealth, and prices — quantities that are strictly positive and heavily right-skewed, with a long tail of the very rich. (Ross 2018) The log pulls in those extreme values, compressing the tail until the distribution becomes the symmetric bell that so many methods assume. (Ross 2018)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
# log-income ~ Normal(mean 10.5, sd 0.6); income = exp(that) -> log-normal
log_income = rng.normal(10.5, 0.6, 20000)
income = np.exp(log_income)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.5))
axL.hist(income, bins=80, range=(0, 200000), color="#9a6a2f", alpha=0.85)
axL.set_title("Income (log-normal): a long right tail")
axL.set_xlabel("income ($)"); axL.set_yticks([])
axR.hist(log_income, bins=60, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85)
axR.set_title("log(income): a symmetric bell")
axR.set_xlabel("log income"); axR.set_yticks([])
fig.suptitle("Why social scientists log skewed positive quantities", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The log transform tames a long tail. Left: simulated incomes are sharply right-skewed (a few very high earners stretch the axis). Right: the natural log of the same incomes is a clean symmetric bell — which is precisely why economists model and analyse log-income.





This is why “log income,” “log wage,” and “log GDP” are everywhere in empirical work: the transformation buys symmetry, stabilises variance, and lets the Normal-based toolkit apply to data that, raw, would violate its assumptions.

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
section: The probability integral transform: the engine of simulation
text:
The probability integral transform: the engine of simulation
A small theorem with enormous reach. If X has a continuous cdf F, then the transformed variable

U = F(X) \;\sim\; \text{Uniform}(0,1).

Feeding any continuous variable through its own cdf flattens it to a uniform. (Ross 2018) Run it backward and you get the trick that powers all of computational statistics — inverse-transform sampling: if U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1), then X = F^{-1}(U) has distribution F. (Ross 2018)






TipHow a computer draws from any distribution



This is the foundation of Monte Carlo simulation (and of the MCMC you met in the Bayesian sub-chapter). To sample from a Normal, an Exponential, anything: the machine generates a plain uniform random number between 0 and 1, then passes it through the inverse cdf of the target distribution. Every simulated dataset in this book was born from a uniform draw and a F^{-1}. (Ross 2018)

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text:
Summing independent variables: convolution
When you add two independent variables, Z = X + Y, the distribution of the sum is their convolution — sum (or integrate) over every way the parts can combine to the total. (Ross 2018)

P\{Z=z\} = \sum_k P\{X=k\}\,P\{Y=z-k\} \quad\text{(discrete)}, \qquad
f_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(z-y)\,f_Y(y)\,dy \quad\text{(continuous)}.

The intuition is direct: to land on a total z, X can take any value k provided Y supplies the remainder z-k; sum the probabilities of all such pairs. (Ross 2018)

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
section: Reproductive results: families that stay in the family
text:
Reproductive results: families that stay in the family
Convolution is laborious in general, but several distributions are closed under addition — the sum of independents stays in the same family, with parameters that simply combine. (Ross 2018) These are worth memorising; they save constant work.








Sum of independent…
…is distributed as
Parameters combine by




Normals \mathcal N(\mu_i,\sigma_i^2)
Normal
means add, variances add: \mathcal N(\sum\mu_i, \sum\sigma_i^2)


Poissons \text{Pois}(\lambda_i)
Poisson
rates add: \text{Pois}(\sum\lambda_i)


Exponentials \text{Exp}(\lambda) (n of them)
Gamma / Erlang
\text{Gamma}(n,\lambda)


Binomials \text{Bin}(n_i,p) (same p)
Binomial
trials add: \text{Bin}(\sum n_i, p)



The Normal case is the quiet hero behind the standard error: averaging Normal data keeps it Normal, with variance \sigma^2/n. The exponential-to-Gamma result is the bridge to waiting-time and survival models. (Ross 2018)

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text:
The punchline: sums become Normal
The closure results above are special — they need a specific starting family. The Central Limit Theorem is the miracle that needs none: add up enough independent pieces from almost any distribution and the sum is approximately Normal, the original shape — skew, gaps, lumps — dissolving into the bell as more terms accumulate. (Ross 2018) Convolution is the machinery; the CLT is what that machinery converges to. This is the deep reason aggregate social quantities — totals, averages, indices built from many small contributions — are so reliably bell-shaped, and why the Normal-based methods of Part II work as well as they do.

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting the Jacobian. Transforming a density without the derivative factor breaks the total-probability-equals-1 rule and gives a non-distribution. The stretch factor is not optional. (Ross 2018)
Back-transforming a mean naïvely. Because E[g(X)] \ne g(E[X]) (Jensen), the mean of log-income is not the log of mean income. Exponentiating a log-scale average understates the arithmetic mean — a routine error in reporting.
Convolving dependent variables. The convolution formula and the “variances add” rule both require independence. With correlated terms a covariance appears (variance of a sum).
Assuming every sum is Normal at small n. The CLT is asymptotic; for few terms or heavily skewed pieces the sum can still be far from Normal. Closure results are exact, the CLT is approximate — don’t confuse them. (Ross 2018)

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the change-of-variables formula and the Jacobian as a rescaling factor (the Y=X^n example, f_Y(y)=\tfrac1n y^{1/n-1}); the log-normal as the model for income and skewed positive quantities; the probability integral transform U=F(X)\sim\text{U}(0,1) and inverse-transform sampling for simulation; the convolution formula for sums; the reproductive results for Normal, Poisson, exponential-to-Gamma, and binomial; the CLT as the universal limit of sums.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — convolutions of independent variables and the build-up toward the Normal as terms accumulate.

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Change of variables, derived. For strictly increasing g, the cdf of Y=g(X) is F_Y(y)=P\{g(X)\le y\}=P\{X\le g^{-1}(y)\}=F_X(g^{-1}(y)). Differentiate with the chain rule: f_Y(y)=f_X(g^{-1}(y))\cdot \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y). For decreasing g the inequality flips and the derivative is negative, so the absolute value appears, giving the general formula. The Y=X^n case: g^{-1}(y)=y^{1/n}, \frac{d}{dy}y^{1/n}=\frac1n y^{1/n-1}, and with f_X\equiv1 on (0,1) this is f_Y(y)=\tfrac1n y^{1/n-1}.
Probability integral transform. For continuous strictly-increasing F, P\{U\le u\}=P\{F(X)\le u\}=P\{X\le F^{-1}(u)\}=F(F^{-1}(u))=u for u\in(0,1) — the cdf of a Uniform(0,1). Hence U=F(X) is uniform, and inverting, F^{-1}(U)\sim F.
Convolution, derived (continuous). F_Z(z)=P\{X+Y\le z\}=\iint_{x+y\le z} f_X(x)f_Y(y)\,dx\,dy. Fix y and integrate x up to z-y: F_Z(z)=\int f_Y(y)F_X(z-y)\,dy; differentiate in z to get f_Z(z)=\int f_X(z-y)f_Y(y)\,dy.
Sum of independent Normals. Quickest via moment generating functions: M_{X+Y}(t)=M_X(t)M_Y(t) for independents, and \exp(\mu_X t+\tfrac12\sigma_X^2 t^2)\exp(\mu_Y t+\tfrac12\sigma_Y^2 t^2)
=\exp((\mu_X+\mu_Y)t+\tfrac12(\sigma_X^2+\sigma_Y^2)t^2) — the MGF of \mathcal N(\mu_X+\mu_Y,
\sigma_X^2+\sigma_Y^2). The same product-of-MGFs trick proves the Poisson and binomial closure results.

title: Transformations & Sums of Random Variables
text:
Connections

Builds on: random variables (the cdf route to a transformed distribution), the common distributions (the Normal, Poisson, exponential, and their log-normal/Gamma relatives), and expectation & variance (means and variances of sums).
Leads to: the Law of Large Numbers and the Central Limit Theorem (the limit of sums), and the simulation behind every bootstrap and MCMC in the book.
Pays off in: log-linear regression (logging skewed regressors and outcomes) and survival/duration models built on the exponential–Gamma family.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text: Before you can compute a probability you must say what could happen (the sample space) and which happenings you care about (events). Events are sets, so the language of probability is the language of sets: union, intersection, complement. On top of that, three short axioms fix the rules — and almost every formula you’ll ever use is squeezed out of them.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Why it matters
This is the entry that turns probability from a vibe into a system. The three interpretations in the previous entry disagreed about meaning; they all agree about these rules. Andrey Kolmogorov’s 1933 axioms are the small, shared foundation that lets classical, frequentist, and Bayesian reasoning coexist and compute identically.
The payoff is leverage. You will not memorise the complement rule, the addition rule, or inclusion–exclusion as separate facts — you will derive them, in two lines each, from three axioms. Internalising that “everything follows from the axioms” is what makes the rest of the chapter feel inevitable instead of arbitrary.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
section: The objects: sample space and events
text:
The objects: sample space and events
The sample space is the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment — written S in Ross, \Omega in Grinstead & Snell (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997). An event is any subset of the sample space: a collection of outcomes you’ve decided to group together (Ross 2018).
That second definition is the quiet key: an event is a set. “The die shows an even number” is the set \{2,4,6\}. “The respondent is satisfied or very satisfied” is a set of two response categories. Once events are sets, combining them is set algebra.









Operation
Notation
Meaning
“Occurs when…”




Union
E \cup F
outcomes in E, in F, or both
either E or F happens


Intersection
E \cap F (or EF)
outcomes in both E and F
both happen


Complement
E^{c}
outcomes in S not in E
E does not happen



Two events are mutually exclusive (or disjoint) if they cannot both occur — their intersection is the empty event, EF = \varnothing (Ross 2018).

Worked example (Ross). Roll two dice. Let E = “the sum is 7” and F = “the sum is 6.” A single roll can’t sum to both 6 and 7, so EF = \varnothing: these events are mutually exclusive (Ross 2018).







TipMutually exclusive ≠ independent



These two ideas get confused constantly, so plant the flag now. Mutually exclusive means the events can’t co-occur (one happening rules the other out). Independent (next entry) means one event’s occurrence tells you nothing about the other’s odds. They are nearly opposites: if two events with positive probability are mutually exclusive, then learning one occurred tells you the other definitely didn’t — that’s the strongest possible dependence, not independence.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
The three axioms
Kolmogorov’s axioms are the entire rulebook. For events in a sample space S (Ross 2018):

Non-negativity & scale: every event has a probability between 0 and 1, 0 \le P(E) \le 1.
Total mass: the sample space is certain, P(S) = 1 — something in S must happen.
Additivity: for any sequence of mutually exclusive events E_1, E_2, \dots, the probability of their union is the sum of their probabilities, 
P\!\left(\bigcup_i E_i\right) = \sum_i P(E_i).


That’s it. Read them as common sense: probabilities are fractions of a whole (axioms 1–2), and non-overlapping chances add up (axiom 3). The word “mutually exclusive” in axiom 3 is the load-bearing fine print — it’s why the general addition rule below needs a correction term.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
What follows immediately
Each of these is a short consequence of the axioms, not a new assumption (Ross 2018):

Complement rule. Since E and E^c are mutually exclusive and together fill S, axioms 2–3 give P(E) + P(E^c) = 1, so P(E^c) = 1 - P(E). (Often the fastest route to an answer: the chance of “at least one” is 1 minus the chance of “none.”)
The impossible event. P(\varnothing) = 0 — the empty set has no outcomes to contribute.
Monotonicity. If E \subset F (every way E happens is also a way F happens), then P(E) \le P(F). A bigger set can’t be less likely.
Addition rule (inclusion–exclusion for two events). For any two events — overlapping or not — 
P(E \cup F) = P(E) + P(F) - P(EF).
 You subtract P(EF) because adding P(E) and P(F) counts the overlap twice (Ross 2018).


Worked example (Ross). Toss two fair coins; the four outcomes HH, HT, TH, TT are equally likely. Let E = “heads on the first coin” = \{HH, HT\} and F = “heads on the second” =
\{HH, TH\}. Then P(E) = P(F) = \tfrac12, but E and F overlap at HH, so 
P(E \cup F) = \tfrac12 + \tfrac12 - \tfrac14 = \tfrac34 .
 The naïve \tfrac12 + \tfrac12 = 1 is wrong precisely because it double-counts HH (Ross 2018).

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Adding probabilities of overlapping events. P(E \cup F) = P(E) + P(F) holds only when the events are mutually exclusive. Forgetting the -P(EF) correction is the most common arithmetic error in the chapter.
Confusing “mutually exclusive” with “independent.” They are different — often opposite — ideas (see the callout above). Independence is about information, exclusivity about co-occurrence.
Mis-specifying the sample space. Half of all probability errors are really errors about S. The Chevalier de Méré (previous entry) lost money because he counted the wrong outcomes, not because he did the wrong division. Define S explicitly before you compute.
Forgetting the complement trick. Many “at least one” problems are far easier as 1 - P(\text{none}). Reaching for inclusion–exclusion when the complement is one step away wastes effort and invites mistakes.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — sample space S and events as subsets; union, intersection, complement, and mutually exclusive events (the two-dice sum-6/sum-7 example); Kolmogorov’s three axioms; the complement rule, P(\varnothing)=0, monotonicity, and the two-event addition rule (the two-coins \tfrac34 example).
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the sample space as \Omega and the set-theoretic framing of events; the axiomatic foundation shared across interpretations.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Write down S first, every time. The discipline of listing what can happen catches most errors before they start, and makes “equally likely” assumptions explicit rather than smuggled.
Reach for the complement on “at least one” problems. P(\text{at least one}) =
1 - P(\text{none}) is the highest-leverage trick in elementary probability.
Keep a mental Venn diagram. Picturing events as overlapping regions makes the -P(EF) correction obvious and stops you double-counting.
Sanity-check against the axioms. If a calculation ever yields a probability below 0, above 1, or summing past 1 over a partition, you’ve broken an axiom — stop and find the error.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Complement rule, from the axioms. E and E^c are mutually exclusive and E \cup E^c = S. By axiom 3, P(E) + P(E^c) = P(S), and by axiom 2, P(S) = 1. Hence P(E^c) = 1 - P(E). Setting E = S gives P(\varnothing) = 1 - P(S) = 0.
Monotonicity. If E \subset F, write F as the disjoint union F = E \cup (F \cap E^c). Axiom 3 gives P(F) = P(E) + P(F \cap E^c) \ge P(E), since the second term is \ge 0 by axiom 1.
Addition rule (two events). Decompose each set into disjoint pieces: 
E \cup F = (E \cap F^c) \cup (E \cap F) \cup (E^c \cap F),
 a union of three mutually exclusive events. Applying axiom 3 to this union, and also to E = (E \cap F^c) \cup (E \cap F) and F = (E^c \cap F) \cup (E \cap F), then eliminating the piece probabilities, yields 
P(E \cup F) = P(E) + P(F) - P(E \cap F).

Inclusion–exclusion (three events). The same bookkeeping extended one level: 
P(E \cup F \cup G) = P(E)+P(F)+P(G) - P(EF) - P(EG) - P(FG) + P(EFG).
 The alternating pattern — add singles, subtract pairs, add the triple — continues for any number of events, and is the general inclusion–exclusion principle.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Connections

Builds on: What probability is — the axioms are the rulebook all three interpretations share.
Leads to: Counting — when outcomes are equally likely, probabilities are ratios of set sizes, so computing them is counting.
Pays off in: Conditional probability & independence, where the addition rule meets its multiplicative cousin, and everywhere a probability is manipulated thereafter.

title: Moment Generating Functions
text: A distribution has infinitely many features — mean, variance, skew, every higher moment. The moment generating function packs them all into a single function M(t)=\mathbb E[e^{tX}], a “convenient bookkeeping device.” Differentiate it to read off any moment; recognise its form to identify the distribution; and — its killer feature — multiply two MGFs to get the distribution of a sum, turning convolution from a slog into a one-liner and handing you the cleanest proof of the Central Limit Theorem.

title: Moment Generating Functions
text:
Why it matters
Two operations are everywhere in statistics and both are awkward head-on: computing a distribution’s moments, and finding the distribution of a sum of random variables. The MGF makes both mechanical. It’s the tool behind the closure properties you rely on (“the sum of Normals is Normal”) and the engine of the limit theorems — a small piece of machinery with outsized reach. (Ross 2018)

title: Moment Generating Functions
text:
The definition, and why “moment generating”
The MGF of a random variable X is the expected value of e^{tX}: (Ross 2018) 
M(t) = \mathbb E\!\left[e^{tX}\right],
 a sum over a pmf (discrete) or an integral over a density (continuous). The name comes from a small miracle: differentiate M and set t=0 to generate the moments. The n-th derivative gives the n-th moment, (Ross 2018) 
M^{(n)}(0) = \mathbb E[X^n].
 So M'(0)=\mathbb E[X], M''(0)=\mathbb E[X^2], and the variance \sigma^2 = M''(0)-[M'(0)]^2 falls out far more easily than computing it from the definition. (Ross 2018) (The reason: e^{tX}=\sum_k
\frac{t^k}{k!}X^k, so M(t) is a Taylor series whose coefficients are the moments.)

title: Moment Generating Functions
section: Uniqueness: the MGF is a fingerprint
text:
Uniqueness: the MGF is a fingerprint
The deep result: the MGF uniquely determines the distribution. (Ross 2018) If two variables have the same MGF, they have the same distribution — so you can identify a distribution just by recognising the form of its MGF, without ever touching its density. This is what makes the next property a proof technique rather than a curiosity.

title: Moment Generating Functions
section: The killer app: sums become products
text:
The killer app: sums become products
Finding the distribution of a sum is normally a “rather complicated” convolution. (Ross 2018) The MGF collapses it: for independent X and Y, 
M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t)\,M_Y(t).
 The MGF of a sum is the product of the MGFs. (Ross 2018) (Because \mathbb E[e^{t(X+Y)}]=\mathbb E[e^{tX}e^{tY}]=\mathbb E[e^{tX}]\mathbb E[e^{tY}] by independence.) Combine this with uniqueness and you get closure theorems for free: the sum of independent Normals is Normal, of independent Poissons is Poisson, of independent Binomials (same p) is Binomial — each proved by multiplying MGFs and recognising the result. (Ross 2018)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Convolve the Uniform(0,1) density with itself n-1 times on a fine grid
dx = 0.01
base = np.ones(int(1/dx))            # Uniform(0,1) density (height 1 on [0,1])
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(10.0, 2.9), sharey=False)
dens = base.copy()
for i, n in enumerate([1, 2, 3, 8]):
    d = base.copy()
    for _ in range(n - 1):
        d = np.convolve(d, base) * dx     # convolution = MGF product
    x = np.arange(len(d)) * dx
    axes[i].fill_between(x, d, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85)
    axes[i].set_title(f"sum of {n} uniform{'s' if n>1 else ''}", fontsize=9.5)
    axes[i].set_yticks([])
fig.suptitle("Adding independent variables (multiplying MGFs) → the Normal", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Sums become products, and the product marches to a bell. Each panel is the distribution of a sum of n independent Uniform(0,1) variables (the Irwin–Hall distribution), computed by repeated convolution — exactly what ‘multiply the MGFs’ encodes. One uniform is flat; add a few and the shape is already nearly Normal. This is the MGF machinery behind both the closure properties and the Central Limit Theorem.

title: Moment Generating Functions
text:
Proving the Central Limit Theorem
The MGF is “the key to the proof” of the CLT. (Ross 2018) The argument: write the MGF of a standardized sum of n independent variables, and show that as n\to\infty it converges to e^{t^2/2} — which is exactly the MGF of the standard Normal. By uniqueness, the distribution must therefore converge to Normal. (Ross 2018) That is the entire CLT in three moves: standardize, take the MGF, watch it become the Gaussian’s. The bell isn’t an accident; it is what the product of many MGFs is forced to become.

title: Moment Generating Functions
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Assuming the MGF always exists. \mathbb E[e^{tX}] can be infinite (heavy tails — the Cauchy has no MGF). When it fails, use the always-defined characteristic function \mathbb E[e^{itX}] instead. (Ross 2018)
Multiplying MGFs of dependent variables. M_{X+Y}=M_X M_Y needs independence; without it the product rule fails. (Ross 2018)
Forgetting to evaluate at t=0. Moments are M^{(n)}(0), not M^{(n)}(t) — the derivatives are taken, then set t=0. (Ross 2018)
Confusing the MGF with the distribution. The MGF encodes the distribution; you still recognise the family from its form (uniqueness), not read probabilities off M directly.

title: Moment Generating Functions
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the MGF M(t)=\mathbb E[e^{tX}] as a “bookkeeping device”; generating moments by differentiation (M^{(n)}(0)=\mathbb E[X^n], variance made easy); the uniqueness theorem; the sum → product property and closure of the Normal/Poisson/Binomial families under independent sums; and MGFs as “the key to the proof” of the CLT (standardized-sum MGF \to e^{t^2/2}).
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — generating functions / MGFs for sums and the limit theorems.

title: Moment Generating Functions
text:
Connections

Builds on: expectation & variance (the moments it generates) and transformations & sums (the convolution it simplifies).
Proves: the Central Limit Theorem and the closure properties of common distributions.
Cousin of: the characteristic function (its always-convergent complex sibling), used when the MGF diverges.

title: Location, Spread & Shape
text: Before any model, you describe. A single variable is summarised by three things: a centre (the typical value), a spread (how much values vary), and a shape (symmetry, tails, peaks). The recurring theme is robustness — some summaries (mean, standard deviation) are hijacked by a single outlier, while others (median, IQR) shrug it off. Knowing which is which is the difference between a summary that informs and one that misleads.

title: Location, Spread & Shape
text:
Why it matters
This is the empirical mirror of the probability sub-chapter. There, distributions had theoretical means and variances; here, real data has estimated ones — and real data is messy, skewed, and dotted with outliers. Choosing the wrong summary is one of the most common and consequential mistakes in applied work: report a mean income and a few billionaires make the “typical” person look rich; report a median and you see the actual middle. Every chart, table, and model downstream rests on summaries chosen here, so choosing them well — matched to the data’s shape and to its measurement level — is foundational craft.

title: Location, Spread & Shape
section: Measures of location: where is the centre?
text:
Measures of location: where is the centre?
A measure of location pins down a “typical” value (Bruce et al. 2020).

Mean — the arithmetic average, \bar x = \tfrac1n\sum x_i. The familiar choice, and the right one for roughly symmetric data without extremes (Bruce et al. 2020).
Weighted mean — \bar x_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}, used when some observations should count more (or less): when groups are unequally represented in the sample, or when some measurements are intrinsically noisier and should be down-weighted (Bruce et al. 2020).
Median — the middle value of the sorted data. The better choice for skewed data or when outliers are present (household incomes are the canonical case) (Bruce et al. 2020).
Trimmed mean — the average after dropping a fixed number p of the most extreme values at each end, \dfrac{1}{n-2p}\sum_{i=p+1}^{n-p} x_{(i)}. A compromise between mean and median; Olympic diving and gymnastics scores use it so a single rogue judge can’t swing the result (Bruce et al. 2020).
Mode — the most frequent value. Mainly a summary for categorical data, where “average” has no meaning (Bruce et al. 2020).







TipRobustness: the one idea that organises this whole entry



A statistic is robust (or resistant) if a few extreme values barely change it. The mean is not robust: every point enters the sum, so one giant value drags it (Bruce et al. 2020). The median is robust: it depends only on the position of the middle observation, and an outlier at the end of the sorted list can’t move the middle (Bruce et al. 2020). The trimmed mean is robust because it deletes the extremes before averaging. This single distinction — does one weird value wreck the summary? — recurs for spread (below) and decides which numbers to trust on real, messy data.



Worked example (Bruce & Bruce). Across U.S. state populations, the mean is 6,162,876 but the median is only 4,436,370 (Bruce et al. 2020). The mean is much larger because a few highly populous states (California, Texas) inflate it; the median reports the genuine middle. The gap between them is itself a signal — of skew, which the shape section makes precise.







NoteBill Gates walks into a bar (Wheelan)



Charles Wheelan’s Naked Statistics makes the same point unforgettable. Picture ten people in a Seattle bar each earning $35,000 — mean income $35,000. Then Bill Gates walks in (with, in Wheelan’s telling, a talking parrot on his shoulder). If Gates earns $1 billion, the mean income of the bar leaps to about $91 million — yet not one of the original drinkers is a cent richer. (Wheelan 2013) Calling it a bar full of multimillionaires would be “statistically correct and grossly misleading.” It is why, Wheelan warns, “average income in America is not equal to the income of the average American” — to see the typical case on skewed data, you want the median. (Wheelan 2013)

title: Location, Spread & Shape
section: Measures of spread: how much does it scatter?
text:
Measures of spread: how much does it scatter?
Spread asks whether values cluster tightly or range widely (Bruce et al. 2020). The same robust/non-robust split applies.








Measure
Definition
Robust?




Range
\max - \min
✗ — defined by the two extremes


Variance s^2
\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i - \bar x)^2
✗ — squared deviations magnify outliers


Standard deviation s
\sqrt{s^2}
✗ — same, in original units


Mean absolute deviation
\tfrac1n\sum |x_i - \bar x|
✗ — still anchored to the mean


Median absolute deviation (MAD)
\operatorname{median}(|x_i - m|), m= median
✓ — robust


Interquartile range (IQR)
Q_{75} - Q_{25}
✓ — robust



The standard deviation is the default partner of the mean (and the quantity behind the standard error and CLT), but it is especially outlier-sensitive because it squares deviations — one extreme point, squared, dominates the sum (Bruce et al. 2020). When data is messy, the robust pair — median for location, IQR or MAD for spread — is the safer summary (Bruce et al. 2020).






NoteWhy divide by n − 1?



The sample variance divides by n-1, not n. Intuitively, you’ve “spent” one degree of freedom estimating the mean \bar x from the same data, so the deviations are slightly too small; dividing by n-1 corrects the bias, making s^2 an unbiased estimate of the true variance. The appendix makes this precise.

title: Location, Spread & Shape
text:
Shape: symmetry, tails, and peaks
Two distributions can share a mean and a standard deviation yet look completely different — shape is the missing third summary (Bruce et al. 2020; Albers 2017).
Skewness is asymmetry (Bruce et al. 2020):

Right (positive) skew — a long tail toward high values; a handful of large observations pull the mean above the median (\bar x > m). Income, city sizes, and insurance claims are classically right-skewed.
Left (negative) skew — a long tail toward low values; the median sits above the mean (m > \bar x).

The mean-versus-median comparison is therefore a quick skew detector: mean > median signals right skew, mean < median signals left skew, and the state-population example above (\bar x \gg m) is textbook right skew.
Modality counts the peaks (Albers 2017):

Unimodal — a single peak.
Bimodal (or multimodal) — two or more peaks, often a tell that the data secretly mixes two populations (e.g. heights pooled across men and women) (Albers 2017). A bimodal histogram is a standing invitation to disaggregate.


Worked example (Albers). Two typing methods are compared by mean alone — Method A at 42 wpm, Method B at 40 wpm — which would crown A the winner. But graphing the distributions reveals they have opposite skews, so their practical implications differ: judged on the full shape rather than the mean, the lower-mean method can be the better choice (Albers 2017). The lesson is the entry’s thesis: a mean without a shape can point you the wrong way.



Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
data = rng.lognormal(mean=10.2, sigma=0.9, size=5000)   # right-skewed, income-like

mean, median = data.mean(), np.median(data)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.hist(data, bins=80, range=(0, np.quantile(data, 0.99)),
        color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85)
ax.axvline(median, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2, label=f"median = {median:,.0f}")
ax.axvline(mean, color="#b5446e", lw=2, ls="--", label=f"mean = {mean:,.0f}")
ax.set_title("Right skew: the mean chases the tail, the median holds the middle")
ax.set_xlabel("value (e.g. income)"); ax.set_yticks([])
ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A right-skewed sample (like incomes). The median (solid) marks the typical value; the mean (dashed) is dragged rightward by the long tail of large values. One robust summary, one not.

title: Location, Spread & Shape
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting a mean for skewed data. With incomes, claims, or city sizes, the mean overstates the “typical” value. Use the median, and show both so the skew is visible.
Trusting the standard deviation on outlier-prone data. Because it squares deviations, one extreme point can dominate it. Reach for the IQR or MAD when robustness matters.
Summarising shape away. Mean and SD alone hide skew and bimodality. Always pair summaries with a histogram or boxplot (next entry).
Averaging the wrong measurement level. A mean of ordinal codes or nominal categories is meaningless — match the statistic to the measurement level (mode for nominal, median for ordinal).
Ignoring a bimodal peak. Two peaks usually mean two populations; a single summary of the pooled data describes neither.

title: Location, Spread & Shape
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — estimates of location (mean, weighted mean, median, trimmed mean, mode) and the robustness distinction; the state-population mean (6,162,876) vs median (4,436,370); estimates of variability (range, variance, SD, mean absolute deviation, MAD, IQR) and which are robust; skewness via mean-vs-median.
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — descriptive framing; modality (unimodal/bimodal as a sign of mixed populations); the two-typing-methods example showing why the mean alone can mislead.
Wheelan, Naked Statistics — the Bill-Gates-walks-into-a-bar illustration of how a mean becomes “statistically correct and grossly misleading” on skewed data, and why “average income in America is not equal to the income of the average American.”

title: Location, Spread & Shape
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Default to median + IQR for messy data, mean + SD for clean symmetric data. Let the shape decide, not habit.
Always report mean and median together. Their gap is a free, instant read on skew.
Treat one outlier as a question, not a nuisance. If the mean and median disagree sharply, ask why before “fixing” anything — the outlier may be the most interesting datum.
Plot before you summarise. A five-minute histogram catches skew and bimodality that no single number reveals — the bridge to the EDA entry.

title: Location, Spread & Shape
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why the mean is the least-squares centre. The value c minimising \sum_i (x_i - c)^2 is c = \bar x (differentiate and set to zero). The value minimising \sum_i |x_i - c| is the median. So the mean is the centre under squared loss, the median under absolute loss — and squaring is exactly what makes the mean chase outliers.
The n-1 correction (Bessel’s correction). With \mu unknown and estimated by \bar x, 
E\!\left[\sum_i (x_i - \bar x)^2\right] = (n-1)\sigma^2,
 because the deviations are taken about the sample mean, which itself was fit to the data. Dividing by n-1 (not n) therefore makes E[s^2] = \sigma^2 — an unbiased estimator. The lost degree of freedom is the one used to estimate \bar x.
MAD as a robust σ-estimate. For Normally distributed data, \sigma \approx 1.4826 \times
\operatorname{MAD}; the constant rescales the median absolute deviation so it matches the standard deviation under Normality while retaining robustness against outliers.
Percentiles. The p-th percentile is a value below which (roughly) p\% of the data falls; Q_{25}, Q_{50}=\text{median}, Q_{75} are the quartiles, and \text{IQR} = Q_{75} - Q_{25} spans the middle half — robust because it ignores the outer quarters entirely.

title: Location, Spread & Shape
text:
Connections

Builds on: expectation & variance (the theoretical centre and spread these summaries estimate) and the Overture’s measurement levels (which statistic is valid).
Leads to: exploratory data analysis (visualising location, spread, and shape) and messy real data (where robustness earns its keep).
Pays off in: all of inference — the sample mean and standard deviation are the raw material of the CLT and every estimate built on it.

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text: How much probability can sit far from the mean if all you know is the mean — or the mean and variance? Markov and Chebyshev answer with bounds that hold for every distribution, no Normality required. They’re the scaffolding that proves the Law of Large Numbers, and they force a useful distinction: a sequence of random quantities can “converge” in several different senses, and telling them apart is what separates the weak law from the strong law from the CLT.

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text:
Why it matters
Most results assume a distribution; these two assume almost nothing. Markov needs only a mean, Chebyshev only a mean and variance — so they give you guarantees on any data, however weird its shape. (Ross 2018) That distribution-free quality makes them both a practical tool (a worst-case tail bound when you can’t assume Normality) and the theoretical engine behind the limit theorems that justify all of inference.

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text:
Markov’s inequality
For a non-negative random variable X and any a>0: (Ross 2018) 
\Pr\{X \ge a\} \le \frac{\mathbb E[X]}{a}.
 The intuition: if you only know the average of a non-negative quantity, you can limit how much can sit far above it. (Ross 2018) If average weekly production is 50 items, the chance it exceeds 75 is at most 50/75 = 2/3. (Ross 2018) The proof is one line: let I=1 if X\ge a and 0 otherwise; since X\ge 0, I \le X/a, so taking expectations \mathbb E[I]=\Pr\{X\ge a\}\le \mathbb E[X]/a. (Ross 2018)

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text:
Chebyshev’s inequality
Apply Markov to the non-negative variable (X-\mu)^2 and you get the more useful Chebyshev bound, for any variable with finite mean \mu and variance \sigma^2, and any k>0: (Ross 2018) 
\Pr\{|X-\mu| \ge k\} \le \frac{\sigma^2}{k^2}.
 Stated in standard deviations, the chance of landing more than k SDs from the mean is at most 1/k^2 — for any distribution. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) So at least 75% of any distribution lies within 2 SDs, at least 89% within 3. Example: with \mathbb E[X]=50 and \operatorname{Var}(X)=25, the probability of falling outside 40–60 (k=10) is at most 25/100 = 1/4 — we’re “at least .75” sure we land inside. (Ross 2018)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

k = np.linspace(1, 4, 300)
chebyshev = 1.0 / k**2

# actual two-sided Normal tail P(|Z| >= k), via a hand erf (no scipy)
def erf(x):
    t = 1.0 / (1.0 + 0.3275911 * np.abs(x))
    y = 1.0 - (((((1.061405429*t - 1.453152027)*t) + 1.421413741)*t - 0.284496736)*t + 0.254829592)*t*np.exp(-x*x)
    return np.sign(x) * y
normal_tail = 1.0 - erf(k / np.sqrt(2))   # P(|Z| >= k)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.plot(k, chebyshev, color="#c0392b", lw=2.6, label=r"Chebyshev bound  $1/k^2$ (any distribution)")
ax.plot(k, normal_tail, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6, label=r"actual Normal tail  $P(|Z|\geq k)$")
ax.fill_between(k, normal_tail, chebyshev, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.08)
ax.set_xlabel("k (standard deviations from the mean)"); ax.set_ylabel("probability in the tails")
ax.set_title("A guarantee for every distribution — at the cost of slack")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Chebyshev is universal but loose. The bound (red) caps the probability of being more than k standard deviations from the mean at 1/k² for ANY distribution. The actual tail probability for a Normal (navy) is far smaller — Chebyshev’s price for assuming nothing about the shape. Its power is the guarantee that holds no matter how strange the distribution; its weakness is the slack you see here.

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text:
Chebyshev proves the Weak Law
The payoff: Chebyshev gives the Weak Law of Large Numbers in two lines. (Ross 2018) For i.i.d. X_1,\dots,X_n with mean \mu and variance \sigma^2, the sample mean \bar X_n has mean \mu and variance \sigma^2/n. Plug that into Chebyshev: 
\Pr\{|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\} \le \frac{\sigma^2}{n\,\varepsilon^2} \xrightarrow{\ n\to\infty\ } 0.
 The variance of the average shrinks like 1/n, so the probability of any fixed-size deviation vanishes. (Ross 2018) That is the WLLN, and the bound shows why it’s true: averaging crushes variance.

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text:
Modes of convergence
The proof above is “convergence in probability,” and it’s worth naming the three senses in which random sequences settle down — they’re exactly what distinguish the limit theorems: (Ross 2018)

Convergence in probability (the Weak Law): for any \varepsilon, \Pr(|\bar X_n-\mu|\ge\varepsilon)\to 0. For a specified large n, the average is probably near \mu. But it does not rule out the average jumping far from \mu infinitely often, just at ever-rarer intervals. (Ross 2018)
Almost-sure convergence (the Strong Law): \Pr(\lim_{n\to\infty}\bar X_n = \mu)=1. The whole sequence converges and stays converged; the far-jumps happen only a finite number of times. This is strictly stronger than the weak law. (Ross 2018)
Convergence in distribution (the CLT): the weakest sense — the shape of a (standardized) quantity approaches a fixed form (the Normal), even though the individual values don’t converge to any constant. (Ross 2018)

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
section: From the Law of Large Numbers to the CLT
text:
From the Law of Large Numbers to the CLT
These three pin down the division of labour between the two great limit theorems: (Ross 2018)

The LLN says where the average goes — to the constant \mu — “swamping” outliers without compensating for them.
The CLT says how it gets there — the fluctuations around \mu, rescaled by \sqrt n, are approximately Normal, whatever the original distribution. (Ross 2018)

And the CLT is sharper than Chebyshev when you can use it. Ross’s example: to measure a distance to within .5 light-years with 95% confidence, Chebyshev demands about 320 observations, but the CLT’s Normal approximation shows just 62 suffice. (Ross 2018) The universal bound is safe; the distributional one is efficient — and knowing which you’re entitled to is the skill.

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Expecting Chebyshev to be tight. It’s a worst-case bound over all distributions; for nice ones the real tail is far smaller. Use it for guarantees, not estimates. (Ross 2018)
Applying Markov to a variable that can be negative. Markov needs X\ge 0; apply it to |X| or (X-\mu)^2 instead. (Ross 2018)
Conflating the weak and strong laws. Convergence in probability allows infinitely many (rare) far excursions; almost-sure convergence forbids them. (Ross 2018)
Reading the CLT as “the data become Normal.” It’s the sampling distribution of the average that goes Normal (convergence in distribution), not the raw data. (Ross 2018)

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — Markov \Pr\{X\ge a\}\le \mathbb E[X]/a (indicator proof, the 50/75 example); Chebyshev \Pr\{|X-\mu|\ge k\}\le \sigma^2/k^2 (via Markov on (X-\mu)^2, the 40–60 example); the Chebyshev proof of the WLLN (\sigma^2/n\varepsilon^2); convergence in probability vs almost-sure (weak vs strong law, the “infinitely often” distinction) and in distribution; the LLN-vs-CLT division of labour and the 320-vs-62 observations comparison.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — Chebyshev as “deviation of more than k SDs is \le 1/k^2”; simulation views of the laws.

title: Inequalities & Modes of Convergence
text:
Connections

Builds on: expectation & variance (the only inputs these bounds need) and random variables.
Proves: the Law of Large Numbers (Chebyshev → WLLN) and frames the Central Limit Theorem (convergence in distribution).
Reappears in: any distribution-free guarantee — concentration bounds, the bootstrap’s justification, and worst-case risk arguments in ML.

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
text: Numbers do not speak for themselves. A drug can help every subgroup yet appear to harm the whole; a 1% match can mean 99% guilt or near-innocence; switching doors can double your odds. These famous paradoxes are not parlour tricks — they are the clearest demonstrations that probability alone is not enough, and that deciding what to condition on is a causal judgment, not a statistical one.
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
text:
Why it matters
Every paradox here is a trap a working analyst can fall into with real data: aggregating when you should stratify (or vice versa), confusing a conditional with its reverse, or reading a correlation manufactured by your own sample selection. They are the intuition pumps behind Bayes’ theorem, confounding and colliders, and the whole case for causal reasoning in the social sciences.
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
text:
Simpson’s paradox: the great reversal
Simpson’s paradox is when an association that holds in the whole population reverses in every subpopulation — a puzzle about confounding that “statistics alone cannot solve” (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018).
Pearl’s worked example — a fictional heart-attack drug (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018):



Group
Control heart-attack rate
Drug rate
Drug better?




Women
5%
7.5%
✗ worse


Men
30%
40%
✗ worse


Everyone (aggregated)
22%
18%
✓ better?!



The drug looks worse for women and worse for men, yet better overall. Why? Unequal subgroup sizes plus a lurking confounder: women (lower baseline risk) were much more likely to take the drug, so the aggregate “drug group” was disproportionately low-risk, mixing the drug’s effect with gender’s (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018).
Pearl’s central point: only a causal model resolves it (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018). The numbers cannot tell you which table to trust:

If the third variable is a confounder (like gender, a cause of both taking the drug and the outcome), you must adjust — trust the partitioned data (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018).
If the third variable is a mediator (e.g. blood pressure that the drug lowers on the way to helping), you must not adjust — adjusting would mask the drug’s real benefit (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018).

Same data, opposite correct answers, decided entirely by the causal story.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
def group(x_lo, x_hi, intercept, n):
    x = rng.uniform(x_lo, x_hi, n)
    y = intercept - 1.0*x + rng.normal(0, 0.6, n)   # within-group slope = -1
    return x, y

xA, yA = group(1, 5, 10, 120)     # group A: low x, lower band
xB, yB = group(5, 9, 16, 120)     # group B: high x, higher band  -> positive aggregate

x_all = np.concatenate([xA, xB]); y_all = np.concatenate([yA, yB])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.3))
for x, y, c, lab in [(xA, yA, "#1f4e79", "group A"), (xB, yB, "#d98a29", "group B")]:
    ax.scatter(x, y, s=14, color=c, alpha=0.55, label=lab)
    b1, b0 = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)
    xs = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 20)
    ax.plot(xs, b0+b1*xs, "--", color=c, lw=2)
b1, b0 = np.polyfit(x_all, y_all, 1)
xs = np.linspace(1, 9, 30)
ax.plot(xs, b0+b1*xs, "-", color="black", lw=2.6, label=f"overall fit (slope +{b1:.2f})")
ax.text(2, 4.2, "within groups: slope −1.0", fontsize=9, color="#444")
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("Simpson's paradox: within-group ↓ but aggregate ↑")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Simpson’s paradox in continuous form. Within each group (blue, orange) the relationship between x and y is clearly negative (dashed within-group fits). But the groups are offset — the orange group sits up and to the right — so the overall regression through all the points (solid black) slopes positively. Aggregate and subgroup trends point in opposite directions. Which is ‘the’ relationship? The data can’t say; only knowing whether the grouping variable is a confounder (stratify) or a mediator (don’t) resolves it.
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
section: Monty Hall: the host’s informed action
text:
Monty Hall: the host’s informed action
Three doors: a car behind one, goats behind two (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018). You pick Door 1; the host — who knows where the car is — opens a different door (say Door 3) revealing a goat, and offers a switch to Door 2. Should you? Yes — switching wins 2/3 of the time (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018).
Intuition screams 50/50 (two doors left), but that’s wrong: your initial pick had only a 1/3 chance, and the host’s action is not random — he must open a goat door and cannot open yours, so his choice “pushes” the remaining 2/3 probability onto the other closed door (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018). Pearl frames the host’s opened door as a collider — it is caused by both your choice and the car’s location — so conditioning on it induces a dependence between your door and the prize, making the other door twice as likely (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018).
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
text:
The prosecutor’s fallacy: base-rate neglect
The prosecutor’s fallacy is the transposed conditional — confusing P(\text{evidence}\mid
\text{innocent}) with P(\text{innocent}\mid\text{evidence}) (Wheelan 2013).
The example: a crime-scene blood type found in 1% of the population (Wheelan 2013). The prosecutor argues: “only a 1% chance an innocent person matches, so 99% chance the defendant is guilty.” Wrong. In a city of 800,000, about 8,000 people match — so the defendant, absent other evidence, is 1 of 8,000 suspects, and the probability of guilt is tiny (Wheelan 2013). The lesson is pure Bayes: you must start from the base rate (prior) and update; ignoring it yields nonsense (Wheelan 2013).
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
section: Berkson’s paradox: collider bias by selection
text:
Berkson’s paradox: collider bias by selection
Berkson’s paradox shows how conditioning on a common effect manufactures a spurious association between unrelated causes (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018). Wheelan and Pearl’s dating example: why do attractive people seem to be “jerks”? If your dating pool admits anyone who is either very attractive or very nice (conditioning on the common effect “someone you’d date”), then mean-and-unattractive people are excluded — leaving a negative correlation between looks and personality that doesn’t exist in the population (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018). The hospital version: two unrelated diseases look correlated among admitted patients, because people with neither aren’t in the hospital (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018).
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
section: The thread: conditioning is a causal act
text:
The thread: conditioning is a causal act
All four share one moral. Whether you should condition on a variable — stratify, control, select — depends on its causal role:

Confounder (common cause) → do condition (Simpson’s: adjust for gender).
Mediator (on the causal path) → don’t condition (Simpson’s: don’t adjust for blood pressure).
Collider (common effect) → don’t condition; doing so creates bias (Monty Hall, Berkson’s).

No amount of data tells you which is which — that knowledge comes from a causal model. This is the Book of Why’s thesis in miniature.
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Always trusting the aggregate (or always the subgroups). Simpson’s shows either can be right — decide by the causal role of the grouping variable. (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018)
Treating the host’s door (or any collider) as uninformative. Conditioning on a common effect changes the probabilities; ignore that and you misjudge Monty Hall and selection-biased samples. (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018)
Transposing the conditional. P(\text{match}\mid\text{innocent})\ne P(\text{innocent}\mid\text{match}); always bring in the base rate. (Wheelan 2013)
Reading correlations in a selected sample as real. Berkson’s bias is everywhere data is filtered on an outcome (hospitalised, admitted, hired, dated). (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018)
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Pearl & Mackenzie, The Book of Why — Simpson’s paradox (the heart-drug 5%/7.5%, 30%/40%, 22%/18% reversal; confounder + unequal sizes; resolution only via a causal model — adjust for a confounder, not a mediator); Monty Hall (1/3 vs 2/3, the host’s informed action, the collider framing); Berkson’s paradox / collider bias (the attractive-jerks dating pool and the hospital example).
Wheelan, Naked Statistics — the prosecutor’s fallacy / transposed conditional and base-rate neglect (the 1%-blood-type, 8,000-suspects example).
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Simpson’s algebra. The aggregate rate is a size-weighted average of subgroup rates. With drug-group women heavily outnumbering drug-group men (and women low-risk), the drug average \approx w\cdot(\text{low women rate}) + (1-w)\cdot(\text{men rate}) puts large weight w on the low value; the control average reverses the weights, putting more weight on high-risk men. Different weights on the same two risk levels flip the comparison — even though, level-for-level, the drug is worse. The reversal is a weighting artefact; the fix is to compare like with like (the causal adjustment).
Monty Hall by Bayes. Let the car be behind Door 1, 2, or 3 with prior 1/3 each; you pick Door 1. The host opens Door 3. P(\text{host opens 3}\mid \text{car in 1})=1/2, P(\cdot\mid\text{car in 2})=1, P(\cdot\mid\text{car in 3})=0. By Bayes, P(\text{car in 2}\mid\text{opens 3}) =
\dfrac{1\cdot\frac13}{\frac12\cdot\frac13 + 1\cdot\frac13} = \dfrac{1/3}{1/6 + 1/3} = \dfrac{1/3}{1/2} = \tfrac{2}{3}, versus P(\text{car in 1}\mid\text{opens 3})=1/3. Switch.
Prosecutor by Bayes. With prior guilt P(G)=1/800{,}000 (one true culprit) and a match that is certain if guilty, P(M\mid G)=1, and P(M\mid \bar G)=0.01: P(G\mid M)=\frac{1\cdot\frac1{800{,}000}}{1\cdot\frac1{800{,}000} + 0.01\cdot\frac{799{,}999}{800{,}000}}
\approx \frac{1}{8001} — about 1/8000, not 0.99. The likelihood is not the posterior.
Collider bias, formally. If A\to C\leftarrow B with A\perp B marginally, then conditioning on the collider C generally makes A\not\perp B\mid C — an induced dependence. Berkson’s “you’d date them” and Monty’s “opened door” are both C.
crumbs: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor

title: Famous Paradoxes: Simpson, Monty Hall & the Prosecutor
text:
Connections

Builds on: conditional probability and Bayes’ theorem (Monty Hall, the prosecutor) — the machinery these puzzles exercise.
Leads to: DAGs, confounders & colliders (the causal grammar that resolves all four) and what regression can’t tell you.
Echoes: the Bayesian worldview (base rates as priors) and the selection-bias cautions throughout Part V.
