crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
The discrete stories
Bernoulli — the atom. A single trial with two outcomes, “success” (1) and “failure” (0) (Ross 2018). It’s the simplest possible random variable and the building block of everything below. Bruce & Bruce note that binary outcomes — buy/don’t buy, fraud/not fraud, click/no click — sit at the heart of business analytics (Bruce et al. 2020).
Binomial — counting successes. Add up n independent Bernoulli trials, each with the same success probability p, and you get the number of successes (Ross 2018). Its mean is np and variance np(1-p) — note variance is largest at p=0.5 (maximum uncertainty) and vanishes at p=0 or 1.

Worked examples. Ross: in 5 fair coin flips, P(\text{exactly 2 heads}) = \binom{5}{2}
(\tfrac12)^5 = 10/32. A quality-control case: screws defective with p = 0.01 — the probability a 10-screw package needs replacement (more than 1 defective) is \approx 0.004 (Ross 2018). Bruce: with a 2% conversion rate, the chance of 0 sales in 200 clicks (Bruce et al. 2020).

Poisson — rare events in an interval. Count events that occur at a constant average rate \lambda over a fixed span of time or space, each independent of the last (Ross 2018). Its signature: mean equals variance, both \lambda.

Worked examples. Ross: typographical errors on a page (\lambda = 0.5), or alpha particles emitted by a radioactive source (\lambda = 3.2) (Ross 2018). Bruce: queuing and capacity — how much server capacity to handle web traffic arriving in 5-second windows (Bruce et al. 2020).

Geometric — waiting for the first success. How many independent trials until success first strikes (Ross 2018). It is memoryless (the discrete version): past failures don’t make success any more “due” — a direct echo of the gambler’s fallacy.

Worked examples. Ross: the expected number of fair-die rolls to get a “1” is 1/p = 6; drawing balls from an urn of N white and M black until the first black (Ross 2018).
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
The continuous stories
Uniform — featureless ignorance. Every value in (\alpha,\beta) equally likely; the density is flat (Ross 2018). The model for “could be anywhere in this range.”

Worked example (Ross). A passenger arrives at a bus stop where buses come every 15 minutes; if their arrival is uniform between 7:00 and 7:30, the waiting-time probabilities are areas of rectangles (Ross 2018).

Normal — the sum of many small effects. The bell curve, symmetric about \mu, with spread \sigma. Its story is the deep one: a quantity shaped by many small, independent, additive influences tends to be Normal (Ross 2018) — which is precisely the Central Limit Theorem, and why the Normal is the most important distribution in statistics. The 68–95–99.7 rule is worth memorising: about 68% of the mass lies within \pm 1\sigma of the mean, 95% within \pm 2\sigma, and 99.7% within \pm 3\sigma (Ross 2018).

Worked examples. Ross: the heights of 25-year-old men, \mu = 71 inches, \sigma = 2.5 (Ross 2018). Bruce: regression errors and body temperatures (Bruce et al. 2020).

Exponential — time until an event. The waiting time to the next event in a Poisson process (Ross 2018). Its defining trait is the memoryless property: the chance the event happens in the next hour is the same no matter how long you’ve already waited — the distribution doesn’t age (Ross 2018).

Worked examples. Ross: time until the next earthquake, or until someone dials a wrong number (Ross 2018). Bruce: time between website visits, or until an electronic component fails (Bruce et al. 2020).
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Two approximations that connect them
The distributions aren’t isolated — they bleed into one another in limits, which is both beautiful and practically useful.
Binomial → Poisson (rare events). When n is large and p small with np = \lambda moderate, the Binomial converges to the Poisson (Ross 2018). The engine is the limit (1 - \lambda/n)^n \to e^{-\lambda}. Use it when you know the rate \lambda but can’t pin down n and p separately — exactly the situation for “rare events at a known average rate.”
Binomial → Normal (De Moivre–Laplace). When n is large, the Binomial is approximately Normal with mean np and variance np(1-p) (Ross 2018). Ross’s rule of thumb: trust it once np(1-p) \ge 10. Because the Normal is continuous and the Binomial is discrete, apply a continuity correction — treat P\{X = 20\} as P\{19.5 < X < 20.5\} (Ross 2018; Bruce et al. 2020). This is a special case of the CLT (a Binomial is a sum of Bernoullis), and a preview of why the bell shows up everywhere.


Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import comb

def binom_pmf(n, p):
    ks = np.arange(0, n + 1)
    pmf = np.array([comb(n, int(k)) * p**k * (1 - p)**(n - k) for k in ks])
    return ks, pmf

p = 0.5
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11, 3.2))
for ax, n in zip(axes, [5, 15, 50]):
    ks, pmf = binom_pmf(n, p)
    ax.bar(ks, pmf, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.8, width=0.9)
    mu, sigma = n * p, np.sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))
    xs = np.linspace(0, n, 300)
    normal = np.exp(-0.5 * ((xs - mu) / sigma) ** 2) / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
    ax.plot(xs, normal, color="#b5446e", lw=2)
    ax.set_title(f"n = {n}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_yticks([])
fig.suptitle("A Binomial becomes a bell — De Moivre–Laplace in action", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The Binomial (bars) and its Normal approximation (curve) for p = 0.5 as n grows. By n = 50 the match is excellent — the De Moivre–Laplace theorem, and a concrete glimpse of the CLT.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
section: The crucial caveat: real data isn’t Normal
text:
The crucial caveat: real data isn’t Normal
Bruce & Bruce sound a warning that the rest of this book takes seriously: the Normal distribution is iconic, but raw data in the real world is rarely Normal (Bruce et al. 2020). Incomes, city sizes, firm revenues, financial returns, social-media activity — these are long-tailed, with extreme “black swan” values far more likely than a bell predicts (Bruce et al. 2020). Modelling such data as Normal systematically understates the risk of the very outliers that dominate outcomes.






WarningThe Normal is for averages, not raw data



Reconcile this with the previous section: the CLT promises that averages and sums tend to Normal, not that your individual observations are. A histogram of incomes is sharply right-skewed; a histogram of mean incomes across many samples is bell-shaped. Confusing the two is the single most common modelling error in applied work — see the CLT article’s pitfalls.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Assuming Normality for raw data. Especially for money, counts, and durations, which are skewed or long-tailed. Plot the data first.
Using the Normal approximation when np(1-p) is small. For rare events (p tiny), reach for the Poisson, not the Normal — the bell approximation is poor in the tail you care about.
Forgetting the continuity correction. Approximating a discrete Binomial by a continuous Normal without the \pm 0.5 adjustment biases small-count probabilities.
Misreading the Poisson’s independence assumption. Events must occur at a constant rate, independently. Clustered events (contagion, bursts) violate it and need richer models.
Trusting the memoryless property where it doesn’t hold. Human waiting times (a bus on a schedule, a machine that wears out) are often not memoryless — the Exponential assumes no aging, which real systems frequently break.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — all seven distributions with their stories, pmfs/pdfs, means, and variances; the worked examples (5 coins → 10/32; defective screws ≈ 0.004; typos \lambda=0.5 and alpha particles \lambda=3.2; expected 6 die rolls; bus-stop uniform; men’s heights \mu=71,\sigma=2.5; earthquakes/wrong numbers); the 68–95–99.7 rule; the Binomial→Poisson limit via (1-\lambda/n)^n\to e^{-\lambda}; the De Moivre–Laplace Binomial→Normal approximation with the np(1-p)\ge 10 rule and continuity correction.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — which distributions matter in practice (Binomial for business analytics; Poisson/Exponential for queuing and simulation); the conversion-rate and server-traffic examples; and the central warning that real data is long-tailed, not Normal.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Identify the story, then the distribution. Two outcomes → Bernoulli; counting them → Binomial; rare counts per interval → Poisson; waiting → Geometric/Exponential; many small additive effects → Normal. The story picks the model.
Plot before you assume. A five-minute histogram tells you whether “Normal” is a fantasy. For money, counts, and durations, expect skew and long tails.
Match the approximation to the regime. Large n, moderate p → Normal; large n, tiny p → Poisson. Don’t Normal-approximate rare events.
Remember mean = variance flags a Poisson. If a count’s variance roughly equals its mean, Poisson is a reasonable start; if variance far exceeds the mean (overdispersion), it isn’t.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Bernoulli moments. E[X] = 1\cdot p + 0\cdot(1-p) = p; E[X^2] = p, so \operatorname{Var}(X) = p - p^2 = p(1-p).
Binomial via Bernoulli sums. Writing X = \sum_{k=1}^n B_k with independent Bernoulli(p) B_k, linearity gives E[X] = np, and independence (variances add) gives \operatorname{Var}(X) = np(1-p) — no need to sum the pmf directly.
Binomial → Poisson. With \lambda = np fixed as n\to\infty, 
\binom{n}{i}p^i(1-p)^{n-i} = \frac{n!}{i!(n-i)!}\Big(\frac{\lambda}{n}\Big)^i\Big(1-\frac{\lambda}{n}\Big)^{n-i}
\;\longrightarrow\; \frac{\lambda^i e^{-\lambda}}{i!},
 using \big(1-\tfrac{\lambda}{n}\big)^n \to e^{-\lambda} and \tfrac{n!}{(n-i)!\,n^i}\to 1.
Poisson moments. E[X] = \sum_{i\ge 0} i\,\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^i}{i!} = \lambda; a similar sum gives E[X(X-1)] = \lambda^2, so \operatorname{Var}(X) = \lambda^2 + \lambda -
\lambda^2 = \lambda — mean and variance coincide.
Exponential memorylessness. P\{X > s+t \mid X > s\} = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda
s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = P\{X > t\} — the past is forgotten. The geometric satisfies the discrete analogue.
Normal moments and the 68–95–99.7 rule follow from \int \phi = 1 and Gaussian integration; the empirical rule is \Phi(1)-\Phi(-1)\approx 0.6826, \Phi(2)-\Phi(-2)\approx 0.9544, \Phi(3)-\Phi(-3)\approx 0.9974.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Connections

Builds on: Random variables (pmfs and pdfs) and expectation & variance (each distribution’s signature moments).
Leads to: the Normal as the limit of sums (the CLT), and to proportions, counts, and duration models throughout inference and beyond.
Echoes: the counting entry — the Binomial’s \binom{n}{i} is exactly the combination you learned to compute there.
Beyond the bell curve: power laws & scaling — heavy-tailed, scale-free distributions where the mean is no longer “typical” and the variance can diverge.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text: Almost every interesting question in social science is conditional: not “what’s the chance of poverty?” but “what’s the chance of poverty given no high-school diploma?” Conditioning means shrinking the world to the cases where one thing is true and re-measuring inside it. Independence is the special — and often wrongly assumed — case where the shrinking changes nothing.

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text:
Why it matters
Conditional probability is the mathematical seed of the entire book. “Controlling for,” “holding constant,” “the effect of X on Y among people with the same Z” — every one of these is, underneath, a statement about a conditional distribution. Regression estimates a conditional mean; causal inference is a careful argument about which conditional comparisons are fair. If you understand P(A \mid B) deeply, the rest of the manual is elaboration.
Independence matters for the opposite reason: it is the assumption that makes probabilities multiply, and it is the assumption most often invoked without warrant. Knowing exactly what it claims — and how it differs from “mutually exclusive” — prevents a whole family of errors, from the gambler’s fallacy to overconfident standard errors.

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
section: Conditional probability: restricting the sample space
text:
Conditional probability: restricting the sample space
To condition on B is to assume B happened and ask about A inside that smaller world. Any outcome outside B now has probability 0; outcomes in A count only if they also lie in B (the intersection AB) (Ross 2018). Rescaling so the new world B has total probability 1 gives the definition:

P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(AB)}{P(B)}, \qquad P(B) > 0.


Worked example (Ross). Roll two dice. You’re told the first die is a 4 (event F). That discards the 30 outcomes starting with 1, 2, 3, 5, or 6, leaving 6 equally likely ones: (4,1) through (4,6). Let E = “the sum is 6.” Inside the restricted world only (4,2) works, so P(E \mid F) = 1/6 (Ross 2018).

Rearranging the definition gives the multiplication rule — often more useful than the definition itself, because real problems unfold in stages:

P(AB) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B).

“The chance both happen = the chance the first happens, times the chance the second happens given the first.” Chained across several stages (draw a card, then another, then another), it becomes the workhorse for sequential experiments (Ross 2018).






TipConditioning is the whole game



When a later chapter says it “controls for income,” picture this entry: it is restricting attention to people at the same income and re-measuring the relationship inside that slice. The power — and the peril — of all observational social science lives in which variable you condition on. (Conditioning on the wrong one can manufacture bias; see the cast of characters and Part V.)

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text:
Independence: when conditioning changes nothing
Two events are independent when knowing one occurred leaves the other’s probability untouched (Ross 2018). Equivalently, either of:

P(A \mid B) = P(A) \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad P(AB) = P(A)\,P(B).

The second form — probabilities multiply — is the one you’ll use constantly, because it’s symmetric and survives even when P(B)=0 edge cases bite. Independence is what licenses “and → multiply” for separate random events: the chance of heads then heads is \tfrac12 \times \tfrac12 = \tfrac14 because the tosses are independent.






WarningIndependent is not the same as mutually exclusive



This confusion is so common it deserves its own warning (Ross 2018). Mutually exclusive events with positive probability are the opposite of independent. If A and B are disjoint, then learning A occurred tells you B definitely did not — that’s perfect information, i.e. maximal dependence. Formally, P(B \mid A) = 0 \ne P(B). Disjoint events can’t co-occur; independent events carry no information about each other. They are different ideas, and for positive-probability events, never both true at once.



The most expensive assumption in statistics
Some things really are independent — successive fair coin flips, well-separated measurement errors. But independence is assumed far more often than it is checked, and Ross flags this directly: many quantities people treat as independent are not (Ross 2018). The cost is real. Standard errors, p-values, and the Central Limit Theorem lean on independent (or weakly dependent) observations; when data are clustered — students within schools, repeated measures on the same person, neighbours sharing a shock — pretending they’re independent makes you far more certain than you’ve earned.

The gambler’s fallacy (Albers). After eight heads in a row, the ninth toss is not more likely to be tails. The tosses are independent, so the ninth is still 50/50 — the coin has no memory of the streak (Albers 2017). The fallacy is exactly a failure to apply independence; it returns, formalised, in the Law of Large Numbers.

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text:
The law of total probability
Often you can’t get P(E) directly, but you can get it inside each of several scenarios. If F_1, F_2, \dots, F_n partition the sample space (mutually exclusive, and together exhaustive), then E is split across them and its probability is the weighted average of the conditionals (Ross 2018):

P(E) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \mid F_i)\,P(F_i).

Read it as: average the chance of E within each scenario, weighting each scenario by how likely it is. This “condition on which case you’re in, then average” move is one of the most useful in all of probability — and it is the denominator of Bayes’ theorem, the very next entry.

Quick illustration. Suppose 60% of voters are urban (turnout 70%) and 40% rural (turnout 55%). Overall turnout is 0.70(0.60) + 0.55(0.40) = 0.42 + 0.22 = 0.64 — a weighted average of the two conditional turnouts, not a naïve midpoint.

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Transposing the conditional. P(A \mid B) and P(B \mid A) are different numbers. “Most rioters were young men” does not mean “most young men riot.” (This is the prosecutor’s fallacy, dissected in the Bayes entry.)
Assuming independence by default. Multiplying probabilities you haven’t earned the right to multiply is the structural error behind overconfident results. Ask “could one of these carry information about the other?” before invoking P(AB)=P(A)P(B).
Confusing mutually exclusive with independent. Disjoint events are maximally dependent, not independent (see the warning above).
The gambler’s fallacy and its inverse. Streaks don’t make the opposite “due” (gambler’s fallacy), and they also don’t prove the coin is rigged after only a few tosses (its hot-hand-style inverse). Independence means each trial is fresh.
Ignoring clustering. Treating clustered or repeated-measure observations as independent understates uncertainty — a quiet but serious error that later chapters fix with clustered standard errors and multilevel models.

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — conditional probability as a restricted sample space and the formula P(A\mid B)=P(AB)/P(B) (the two-dice “first die is 4” example); the multiplication rule; independence as P(AB)=P(A)P(B); the warning that independence is often wrongly assumed; mutually-exclusive-≠-independent; the law of total probability.
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — the gambler’s fallacy as a misuse of independence (eight heads then a “due” tail).

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Always ask “conditional on what?” Naming the conditioning set turns vague claims into precise ones and exposes hidden assumptions about who’s being compared.
Say the multiplication rule out loud. “Chance of both = chance of the first × chance of the second given the first” handles sequential problems that look hard when attacked head-on.
Earn your independence. Before multiplying, articulate why the events carry no information about each other. If you can’t, don’t multiply.
Watch for clusters. Repeated measures, geographic grouping, and shared shocks all break independence; flag them early so the right standard errors get used later.

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Conditional probability is itself a probability. Fix B with P(B)>0 and define Q(A) = P(A\mid B) = P(AB)/P(B). Then Q satisfies all three axioms: Q(A)\ge 0; Q(S) = P(SB)/P(B) = P(B)/P(B) = 1; and for disjoint A_1,A_2,\dots, additivity of P on the disjoint sets A_iB gives additivity of Q. So every theorem about probabilities applies verbatim to conditional probabilities.
The multiplication (chain) rule, general form. 
P(A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n) = P(A_1)\,P(A_2\mid A_1)\,P(A_3\mid A_1 A_2)\cdots P(A_n\mid A_1\cdots A_{n-1}).

Law of total probability, derivation. If \{F_i\} partitions S, then E = \bigcup_i (E F_i), a union of mutually exclusive events. By axiom 3 and the multiplication rule, 
P(E) = \sum_i P(E F_i) = \sum_i P(E\mid F_i)\,P(F_i).

Independence of several events is stronger than pairwise independence: A,B,C are (mutually) independent only if every subset multiplies, including P(ABC)=P(A)P(B)P(C). Pairwise independence does not imply this — a standard cautionary example, and a reason to state independence assumptions carefully.

title: Conditional Probability & Independence
text:
Connections

Builds on: Sample spaces & the axioms — conditioning restricts S; the conditional measure still obeys the axioms.
Leads directly to: Bayes’ theorem — total probability supplies its denominator, and conditioning supplies its whole logic.
Pays off in: regression (a conditional mean) and causal inference (which conditional comparisons are fair), with the independence assumption underwriting the CLT and every standard error.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text: One variable at a time only gets you so far; the interesting questions are about pairs — education and income, temperature and crime, treatment and outcome. Covariance and correlation measure whether two variables move together. But they measure only linear co-movement, they can be zero for variables that are tightly related, and — the warning that echoes through the rest of this book — a correlation, however strong, is not a cause.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
Why it matters
This entry is a hinge. Backward, it completes the probability machinery: the variance-of-a-sum formula had a covariance term, and here it finally gets defined. Forward, it is the gateway to all of applied social science. Regression is, at its core, structured correlation; the entire causal-inference summit exists because correlation alone cannot establish cause. The single most repeated mistake in empirical work — reading an association as a mechanism — is born exactly here, so this is where we inoculate against it.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
section: Two variables at once: joint, marginal, conditional
text:
Two variables at once: joint, marginal, conditional
When you track two random variables together, three distributions describe them (Ross 2018):

Joint distribution. The probability of value-pairs at once: the joint cdf F(a,b) = P\{X \le a,\, Y \le b\}, with a joint pmf p(x,y) = P\{X=x, Y=y\} (discrete) or a joint density f(x,y) whose volume over a region gives probability (continuous). It holds all the information about how X and Y relate.
Marginal distribution. Each variable on its own, recovered by summing (or integrating) out the other — collapsing the joint down onto one axis. The margin is what you’d see ignoring the partner variable.
Conditional distribution. The distribution of one variable given a specific value of the other — p(x \mid y) = p(x,y)/p_Y(y) — the same conditioning idea, now between numeric variables. This is the object regression will model: the conditional mean of Y given X.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
Covariance: do they move together?
Covariance summarises the direction of co-movement (Ross 2018): 
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]\,E[Y].

Read it through the signs: it is positive when X being above its mean tends to coincide with Y being above its mean (they rise together), and negative when one tends to be high while the other is low (they move oppositely) (Ross 2018). Zero covariance means no linear tendency either way.
A key fact, and the one that closed the variance-of-a-sum loop: if X and Y are independent, then \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = 0, because independence makes E[XY] = E[X]E[Y] (Ross 2018). (The converse fails — see below.)
The trouble with covariance is units. Measure height in inches versus feet and the covariance of height-and-weight changes by a factor of 12, though nothing about the relationship did (Ross 2018). Its sign is meaningful; its magnitude, on its own, is not.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
Correlation: covariance made unitless
Correlation (Pearson’s \rho) fixes the units problem by standardising — dividing covariance by each variable’s standard deviation (Ross 2018): 
\rho(X,Y) = \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \,\sigma_Y}.

This rescaling forces \rho into the interval [-1, +1] — a consequence of the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality (Ross 2018). Now the magnitude means something:

\rho = +1: a perfect positive linear relationship (points fall exactly on an upward line).
\rho = -1: a perfect negative linear relationship.
\rho \approx 0: no linear association.

The word linear is doing enormous, easily-missed work — as the next section shows.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
The trap: zero correlation ≠ independence
Independence implies zero correlation. The reverse is false (Ross 2018). Correlation sees only the linear part of a relationship and is blind to everything else.






WarningThe canonical counterexample



Let X take values -1, 0, 1 each with probability 1/3, and set Y = X^2 (Ross 2018). Then Y is a perfect, deterministic function of X — knowing X tells you Y exactly, the strongest dependence possible. Yet 
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = E[X^3] - E[X]\,E[X^2] = 0 - 0\cdot\tfrac23 = 0,
 so \rho = 0. The relationship is entirely non-linear (a U-shape), and correlation, which only measures linear trend, reports “nothing here.” A correlation of zero means no straight-line association — not “no relationship.”




Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
x = rng.uniform(-3, 3, size=400)
y = x**2 + rng.normal(0, 0.6, size=400)   # clear nonlinear dependence
r = np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=12, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.6)
ax.set_title(f"Strong dependence, yet correlation r = {r:.2f}")
ax.set_xlabel("X"); ax.set_ylabel("Y = X² + noise")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two variables with a strong, obvious relationship (Y rises with the square of X) but a sample correlation near zero. Correlation measures straight-line co-movement only; the U-shape is invisible to it.





The practical lesson: always plot the data. A near-zero correlation can hide a U-shape, a cycle, or a threshold — relationships that matter enormously and that a single coefficient erases.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
section: The big one: correlation is not causation
text:
The big one: correlation is not causation
Even a strong, genuinely linear correlation does not establish that one variable causes the other. The usual culprit is a lurking (confounding) variable that drives both, producing a spurious correlation (Bruce et al. 2020). The sources offer a rogues’ gallery:

Ice cream and shark attacks rise together at the beach — but ice cream doesn’t summon sharks. Temperature is the lurking variable: heat drives both beach attendance (more swimmers, more shark encounters) and ice-cream sales (Bruce et al. 2020).
Marriages and deaths across U.S. states correlate strongly — a regression of deaths on marriages gives a coefficient of 0.888. Marriage is not lethal; state population drives both (bigger states have more of everything) (Bruce et al. 2020).
Nicolas Cage films and pool drownings show a 66.6% correlation over 1999–2009 — pure coincidence surfaced by data mining across enough unrelated series (Bruce et al. 2020).
Bathrooms in the home and student achievement correlate — not because plumbing teaches, but because both track parental economic status (Bruce et al. 2020).







TipWhy this is the seed of the whole book



Each example has the same shape: X and Y correlate because a third variable Z causes both (recall the confounders from the Overture). Correlation measures the association; it cannot tell you whether it came from X \to Y, Y \to X, or Z \to X and Z \to Y. Sorting those apart is impossible with correlation alone — which is the entire reason causal inference exists, and why randomisation (which severs the link to every Z) is so prized.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading correlation as causation. The headline error of empirical social science. An association is a question, not an answer — ask “what third variable could produce both?”
Trusting \rho = 0 as ‘no relationship.’ It means no linear relationship. Plot the data; U-shapes and cycles hide in plain sight.
Comparing raw covariances. Covariance magnitude is unit-dependent and meaningless across variables; standardise to correlation before comparing strengths.
Mining for correlations. Test enough pairs and chance will hand you a striking, meaningless one (Nicolas Cage). Correlations found by trawling need far more skepticism than ones predicted in advance.
Forgetting correlation is symmetric. \rho(X,Y) = \rho(Y,X) carries no direction; it cannot even hint which variable came first, let alone which caused which.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — joint, marginal, and conditional distributions; covariance \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[XY]-E[X]E[Y], its sign intuition, and independence ⟹ zero covariance; correlation as standardised covariance, bounded in [-1,1] by Cauchy–Schwarz; the Y=X^2 counterexample showing zero correlation with perfect dependence.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — correlation captures only linear association; correlation-is-not-causation and spurious correlation via lurking variables (ice cream/sharks; marriages/deaths with coefficient 0.888; Nicolas Cage/drownings at 66.6%; bathrooms/achievement and parental status).

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Plot first, correlate second. A scatterplot reveals non-linearity, clusters, and outliers that a single coefficient hides. Never report \rho without having looked.
For every correlation, name a candidate confounder. Forcing yourself to propose the lurking Z is the fastest cure for causal over-reach.
Standardise before comparing. Use correlation, not covariance, when asking “which relationship is stronger” — covariance units make raw comparison meaningless.
Distrust mined correlations. A relationship you went looking for across many variables deserves far more doubt than one your theory predicted beforehand.

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Covariance, bilinearity, and the variance link. Expanding the definition, \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = E[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)] = E[XY]-E[X]E[Y], and \operatorname{Cov}(X,X) = \operatorname{Var}(X). Covariance is bilinear: \operatorname{Cov}(aX+b,\,cY+d) = ac\,\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y), which is why \operatorname{Var}(X+Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X)+\operatorname{Var}(Y)+2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y).
Why |\rho| \le 1 (Cauchy–Schwarz). For any random variables, \big(E[UV]\big)^2 \le E[U^2]\,E[V^2]. Set U = X-\mu_X, V = Y-\mu_Y: 
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)^2 \le \operatorname{Var}(X)\operatorname{Var}(Y)
\;\Longrightarrow\; \rho^2 \le 1.
 Equality (so \rho = \pm 1) holds exactly when Y is an affine function of X, i.e. the points lie on a straight line — the precise sense in which correlation measures linearity.
The counterexample, computed. X \in \{-1,0,1\} each w.p. 1/3, Y = X^2. Then E[X]=0, E[X^2]=2/3, E[XY]=E[X^3]=0, so \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0 and \rho=0, despite Y being a deterministic function of X. Zero correlation, total dependence.
Independence vs uncorrelatedness. Independence ⟹ E[XY]=E[X]E[Y] ⟹ \rho=0. The converse needs the whole joint distribution to factor, p(x,y)=p_X(x)p_Y(y) — far stronger than a single number vanishing. (One exception worth knowing: for a jointly Normal pair, zero correlation does imply independence.)

title: Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation
text:
Connections

Builds on: Expectation & variance (covariance completes the variance-of-a-sum formula) and conditional probability (the conditional distribution).
Leads to: regression, which models the conditional mean and is “correlation with a direction and a slope,” and to the causal-inference summit, which exists precisely because correlation cannot deliver causation.
Echoes: the Overture’s cast of characters — confounders and spurious relationships, now made quantitative.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text: Before a single hypothesis is tested, you look. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the discipline of summarising and graphing data to build intuition, spot errors, find outliers, and check whether the data even fits the methods you planned. A histogram or boxplot routinely reveals in seconds what a table of numbers hides — skew, gaps, two-populations, a data-entry typo. EDA is not a preliminary to the “real” analysis; it is the step that decides whether the real analysis is valid.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text:
Why it matters
The previous entry reduced a variable to a few numbers; this one insists you also see it, because numbers alone deceive. A mean and standard deviation are identical for a clean bell and for a skewed mess with an outlier — but a glance at the picture is not. EDA is where you catch the data-entry error, notice the bimodal split, and discover that your “Normal” variable is a long-tailed monster that will break the tests ahead. Every later chapter assumes you looked first. Skipping EDA is how confident analyses quietly go wrong.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
section: The EDA philosophy: look before you model
text:
The EDA philosophy: look before you model
EDA is younger than most of statistics. For its first century the field was almost entirely about inference — using small samples to draw conclusions about large populations (Bruce et al. 2020). In 1962 John Tukey called for a reformation in his paper The Future of Data Analysis, arguing that inference should be only one component of a broader practice; he established the discipline fully in his 1977 classic Exploratory Data Analysis (Bruce et al. 2020).
The creed is simple: the most important first step in any data project is to look at the data (Bruce et al. 2020). Summarise it, graph it, and you gain intuition you can get no other way. Albers makes the practical case: EDA surfaces patterns the human eye cannot see in raw numbers, flags errors like data-entry mistakes, locates outliers, and reveals whether the distribution (Normal? skewed?) actually meets the assumptions of the test you intended to run (Albers 2017). That last function — checking assumptions before committing to a method — is what makes EDA a safeguard, not a formality.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text:
Percentiles and the five-number summary
A percentile is a value below which a given percentage of the data falls — the p-th percentile has p\% of values at or beneath it (Bruce et al. 2020). A quantile is the same idea indexed by fractions (the 0.8 quantile is the 80th percentile). Percentiles are inherently robust: they describe positions in the sorted data, untouched by how extreme the extremes are.
The five-number summary captures a distribution’s location and spread in five robust values (Bruce et al. 2020): 
\text{Min} \;\le\; Q_1\,(25\text{th}) \;\le\; \text{Median}\,(50\text{th}) \;\le\; Q_3\,(75\text{th}) \;\le\; \text{Max}.


Worked example (Bruce & Bruce). State murder rates: the 5th percentile is 1.6 per 100,000, the median is 4.0, and the 95th percentile is 6.51 (Bruce et al. 2020). Three numbers already sketch the whole distribution’s centre and reach.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text:
The boxplot
Tukey’s boxplot (box-and-whisker) turns the five-number summary into a picture (Bruce et al. 2020):

The box spans Q_1 to Q_3 — the middle 50% of the data, i.e. the IQR; the line inside marks the median.
The whiskers extend to the furthest points within 1.5 \times \text{IQR} of the box (the common R convention); Albers notes other variants reach to the absolute min/max or to one standard deviation (Bruce et al. 2020; Albers 2017).
Points beyond the whiskers are drawn individually as outliers (Bruce et al. 2020).


Worked example (Bruce & Bruce). A boxplot of state populations sits with its median near 4.4 million, the box skewed toward the bottom (small states vary little), while a few giants like California float up top as individual outlier points (Bruce et al. 2020). The asymmetry is the skew, made visible.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
section: Histograms, frequency tables, and density plots
text:
Histograms, frequency tables, and density plots
To see a numeric variable’s whole distribution (Bruce et al. 2020):

A frequency table tallies counts into equal-width bins.
A histogram plots that table — bins on the x-axis, counts on the y-axis, bars contiguous (no gaps), with empty bins shown so you can see where data is missing (Bruce et al. 2020).
A density plot is a smoothed histogram (a kernel density estimate), drawn as a continuous curve with the y-axis as a proportion rather than a raw count (Bruce et al. 2020).


Worked example (Bruce & Bruce). A 10-bin histogram of state populations puts 24 states in the smallest bin and 14 in the next, while the largest bin — holding only California — is separated from the rest by several empty bins, visually screaming “extreme value” (Bruce et al. 2020).



Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
data = rng.lognormal(mean=1.0, sigma=0.8, size=2000)   # right-skewed

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 3.8),
                         gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [2.4, 1]})

axes[0].hist(data, bins=50, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85)
axes[0].set_title("Histogram: shape and the long tail")
axes[0].set_xlabel("value"); axes[0].set_yticks([])

bp = axes[1].boxplot(data, vert=True, widths=0.5, patch_artist=True)
bp["boxes"][0].set(facecolor="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.6)
bp["medians"][0].set(color="#b5446e", linewidth=2)
axes[1].set_title("Boxplot: five-number summary")
axes[1].set_xticks([])

fig.suptitle("Same data, two lenses — and the skew is obvious in both", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two views of the same right-skewed variable. The histogram (left) shows the shape and the long right tail; the boxplot (right) compresses it to the five-number summary, with the median line low in a bottom-heavy box and the tail’s extremes flagged as individual outlier points.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text:
Categorical and binary data
Numeric summaries don’t apply to categories; you summarise them differently (Bruce et al. 2020):

Proportions — the share of records in each category — and the mode, the most frequent category, are the basic summaries.
Bar charts display them, with gaps between bars to signal the categories are discrete and mutually exclusive — the deliberate visual opposite of a histogram’s touching bars (Bruce et al. 2020).
When categories map to numbers (price tiers, say), the expected value acts as a weighted mean, the category values weighted by their probabilities (Bruce et al. 2020) — the empirical echo of expectation.


Worked example (Bruce & Bruce). Causes of Dallas/Fort Worth airport delays: Carrier 23.02%, ATC 30.40%, Weather 4.03%, Security 0.12% — with the mode, inbound-aircraft delays, at 42.43% (Bruce et al. 2020). Proportions and a mode tell the whole categorical story.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
section: Two or more variables at once
text:
Two or more variables at once
To explore relationships (Bruce et al. 2020):

The scatterplot is the standard view of two numeric variables.
The correlation coefficient r \in [-1, +1] measures linear strength and direction (the covariance/correlation entry, now as an EDA tool); a correlation matrix tabulates it for every pair at once — a fast scan for structure.
For large data, where a scatterplot becomes an ink blot: hexagonal binning shades hexagons by point density, contour plots draw density like a topographic map, and contingency tables cross-tabulate two categorical variables with row/column percentages (Bruce et al. 2020).


Worked examples (Bruce & Bruce). A scatterplot of daily returns for AT&T and Verizon shows a strong positive correlation — the stocks move in tandem. A hexbin plot of home size versus tax-assessed value reveals a main peak and a distinct secondary cluster of high-value, small-size homes — structure a raw scatterplot would have buried (Bruce et al. 2020).

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Modelling before looking. Jumping to a test without a histogram is how skew, outliers, and bimodality silently break assumptions. Always plot first.
Confusing a bar chart with a histogram. Touching bars = a binned numeric variable; gapped bars = categorical. The spacing carries meaning.
Over-binning or under-binning a histogram. Too few bins hide structure; too many turn it to noise. Try several bin widths before believing a shape.
Trusting one summary plot for big, dense data. An overplotted scatter looks like a blob; reach for hexbin or contours to recover the density.
Treating outliers flagged by a boxplot as errors. The 1.5\timesIQR rule is a flag, not a verdict — investigate before deleting (next entry).

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the Tukey history of EDA and “look at the data first”; percentiles/quantiles and the five-number summary (murder rates 1.6 / 4.0 / 6.51); the boxplot (1.5\timesIQR whiskers; state-population example, median ~4.4M); frequency tables, histograms (10-bin state-population example, California isolated), and density plots; categorical/binary summaries (proportions, mode, bar charts, expected value; DFW delay percentages, mode 42.43%); scatterplots, correlation and correlation matrices, hexagonal binning, contour plots, contingency tables (AT&T/Verizon; home size vs tax value).
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — EDA reveals patterns the eye misses in raw numbers, flags data-entry errors and outliers, and checks whether the distribution fits the intended test; boxplot whisker variations.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Make a histogram and a boxplot your reflex. Two plots, thirty seconds, and most data-quality surprises surface before they cost you.
Read the boxplot for skew. A median line low in the box with a long upper whisker and high outliers is right skew at a glance.
Vary the bin width. Shapes that survive several binnings are real; shapes that don’t are artefacts.
Use a correlation matrix as a map, not a verdict. It points to relationships worth a closer scatterplot — but remember it only sees linear association (P8).

title: Exploratory Data Analysis & Visual Summaries
text:
Connections

Builds on: location, spread & shape (the summaries these plots visualise) and covariance & correlation (now an exploratory tool).
Leads to: messy real data — EDA is precisely how you find the mess.
Pays off in: inference (EDA checks the assumptions tests rely on) and every modelling chapter, where “look at the data first” never stops being the first step.

title: I · Foundations: Probability & Data
text: The bedrock. Before inference, before regression, before causality, you need a feel for randomness and the objects we use to tame it — and a feel for real data, which is messier than any textbook’s. The aim is not rigour for its own sake but fluency: enough probability and data sense that later ideas feel inevitable rather than arbitrary.

title: I · Foundations: Probability & Data
text:
Probability
The full sub-chapter, a stand-alone textbook on probability, runs in one rising arc from “what a probability means” to “why averages stabilise”:

title: I · Foundations: Probability & Data
text:
Describing Data
The empirical mirror of the probability sub-chapter: from the theory of distributions to the practice of summarising and looking at real data before any modelling.



Entry

title: I · Foundations: Probability & Data
text:
Where this leads
Into Part II · Inference: once you accept that a statistic has a sampling distribution, confidence intervals and hypothesis tests become careful ways of reading that distribution. The bridge article — Sampling Distributions & the CLT — already lives there.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text: Average enough independent draws and the average stops wandering: it homes in on the true mean. That is the Law of Large Numbers, and it is what makes the frequentist interpretation of probability true rather than merely assumed. But it works by swamping past deviations, not by compensating for them — which is exactly why the gambler’s fallacy is a fallacy.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Why it matters
This is the theorem that closes the chapter’s loop. Way back in the interpretations entry, we said the frequentist view defines probability as a long-run frequency, and flagged that taking this as a definition is awkward. The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) repays that debt: starting only from the axioms, it proves that long-run frequencies converge to probabilities. The frequentist picture is a result, not an assumption.
It also seats the Central Limit Theorem. The LLN says where the sample average is heading (the mean); the CLT says what shape the cloud of averages takes on the way there (a bell). Together they are the theoretical engine of all of inference — and the LLN, properly understood, is also the definitive cure for one of the most stubborn errors about chance.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
The law, weak and strong
Let X_1, X_2, \dots be independent draws from the same distribution with mean \mu. Form the running average \bar X_n = \tfrac1n \sum_{i=1}^n X_i. The LLN says \bar X_n \to \mu — in two precise senses (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997):

Weak law (WLLN). For any margin \varepsilon > 0, the probability that the average is more than \varepsilon from the mean goes to zero as n \to \infty: 
P\big(|\bar X_n - \mu| > \varepsilon\big) \longrightarrow 0.
 For a specific, large n, the average is very likely close to \mu.
Strong law (SLLN). With probability 1, the entire sequence of averages converges to \mu. Stronger than the weak law: it guarantees the average gets close and stays close for all larger n, not just that any single large n is probably fine (Ross 2018).


Simulation (Grinstead & Snell). Toss a fair coin and watch the proportion of heads. At 20 tosses they got 5 heads (0.25); at 1,000 tosses, 494 heads (0.494); at 10,000 tosses, 5,039 heads (0.5039) — marching toward the theoretical 0.5 (Grinstead and Snell 1997). A fair die behaves the same way: the running average of rolls converges to E[X] = 3.5 (Grinstead and Snell 1997).



Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 10_000
flips = rng.integers(0, 2, size=n)          # 1 = heads
heads_cumsum = np.cumsum(flips)
trials = np.arange(1, n + 1)

proportion = heads_cumsum / trials
abs_dev = np.abs(heads_cumsum - trials / 2)  # |heads - n/2|

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 3.6))

axes[0].plot(trials, proportion, color="#1f3a5f", lw=1)
axes[0].axhline(0.5, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.4)
axes[0].set_xscale("log")
axes[0].set_title("Proportion of heads → 0.5")
axes[0].set_xlabel("number of tosses (log scale)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("proportion heads")
axes[0].set_ylim(0, 1)

axes[1].plot(trials, abs_dev, color="#9a6a2f", lw=1)
axes[1].set_title("Absolute deviation |heads − n/2| grows")
axes[1].set_xlabel("number of tosses")
axes[1].set_ylabel("|heads − n/2|")

fig.suptitle("The average settles while the raw gap widens", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: 10,000 fair-coin tosses. Left: the proportion of heads converges to 0.5 (the Law of Large Numbers). Right: yet the absolute deviation |heads − n/2| tends to grow. Averages stabilise by swamping early deviations, not by cancelling them.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
section: The tools behind the proof: Markov and Chebyshev
text:
The tools behind the proof: Markov and Chebyshev
Two inequalities bound probabilities using only a mean (Markov) or a mean and variance (Chebyshev) — remarkable because they hold for any distribution with those moments (Ross 2018).
Markov’s inequality. For a non-negative random variable, the chance it reaches a high value a is capped by its mean over a: 
P\{X \ge a\} \le \frac{E[X]}{a}.


Ross’s example. A factory averages 50 items a week. Then P\{X \ge 75\} \le 50/75 = 2/3 — the best you can say knowing only the mean (Ross 2018).

Chebyshev’s inequality. Bounds deviation from the mean using the variance: 
P\{|X - \mu| \ge k\} \le \frac{\sigma^2}{k^2}.


Ross’s example. If that factory’s production has variance 25, then production lands within 10 of the mean (between 40 and 60) with probability at least 1 - 25/10^2 = 0.75 (Ross 2018).







TipHow Chebyshev proves the weak law — in one line



Apply Chebyshev to the average \bar X_n. Its mean is still \mu, but its variance is \sigma^2/n (the variance-of-a-sum result). So 
P\{|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\} \le \frac{\sigma^2/n}{\varepsilon^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n\varepsilon^2} \longrightarrow 0.
 The averaging shrinks the variance, which strangles the deviation probability. That’s the entire weak law (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997).

title: The Law of Large Numbers
section: The crucial distinction: swamping, not compensating
text:
The crucial distinction: swamping, not compensating
Here is where almost everyone’s intuition goes wrong, and where the LLN earns its keep.






WarningThe gambler’s fallacy



After eight heads in a row, a tail is not “due.” The flips are independent, so the ninth is still exactly 50/50 — the coin has no memory and no debt to repay (Albers 2017).


So if the average converges to 0.5, how does it get there without tails “catching up”? By swamping, not compensating (Grinstead and Snell 1997). Suppose after some flips you’re “ahead” by 100 extra heads. The LLN does not summon 100 extra tails to cancel them. Instead, as you flip millions more times, that fixed surplus of 100 becomes a vanishing fraction of the total. The deviation isn’t erased — it’s diluted.
This produces a genuinely surprising fact:

The average stabilises while the raw gap grows. The proportion S_n/n settles toward 0.5, but the absolute deviation — the literal (heads - n/2) — tends to grow with n. Off by 10 heads in 100 flips is a 10% error; off by 500 heads in 1,000,000 flips is a larger number but only a 0.05% error (Grinstead and Snell 1997). The right panel of the figure above shows exactly this: the gap widens even as the ratio converges.

That second fact is not a footnote — it’s the seed of the CLT. The absolute deviation grows like \sqrt{n}, which is precisely why the standard error scales as \sigma/\sqrt{n} and why the sampling distribution has the width it does.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
section: What the LLN buys, and what it doesn’t
text:
What the LLN buys, and what it doesn’t
It justifies frequentist probability. Apply the strong law to an event’s indicator (1 if it happens, 0 if not): its long-run relative frequency converges, with probability 1, to P(E) (Ross 2018). This is the theorem that makes “probability = long-run frequency” consistent with the axioms rather than a separate article of faith — the promissory note from the first entry, paid in full.
It pairs with the CLT. The LLN gives the destination (the average converges to a point); the CLT gives the travel pattern (the averages scatter Normally around that point, with a width that shrinks like 1/\sqrt n) (Ross 2018). The LLN says the variance of the average vanishes; the CLT says how much probability sits within any given distance for finite n.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




The gambler’s fallacy. Believing past outcomes make future ones “due.” Independence means each trial is fresh; the law works by dilution, not by debt collection.
Confusing the average with the absolute count. The proportion converges; the raw gap (heads minus n/2) typically grows. Expecting the literal counts to equalise is the fallacy in another costume.
Expecting the LLN at small n. It is an asymptotic statement. Twenty tosses can sit far from 50% (Grinstead & Snell’s run hit 25%); “the law of small numbers” — trusting tiny samples — is a classic error the inference chapters attack directly.
Assuming convergence without finite variance. Heavy-tailed distributions (no finite variance) can break the usual guarantees — the same caveat that makes the CLT fail for long tails.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the LLN via coin-toss simulation (20 → 5 heads; 1,000 → 494; 10,000 → 5,039, proportion 0.5039) and dice averaging toward 3.5; the swamping-not-compensating intuition and the average-stabilises-while-absolute-deviation-grows point; Chebyshev as the proof tool for the weak law.
Ross, A First Course in Probability — weak vs strong law; Markov’s inequality (factory averaging 50, P\{X\ge75\}\le 2/3) and Chebyshev’s inequality (variance 25 → within 10 with probability \ge 0.75); the Chebyshev proof of the WLLN; the strong law as the justification of frequentist probability; the LLN-vs-CLT division of labour.
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — the gambler’s fallacy as the misreading of independence (eight heads then a “due” tail).

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Trust averages, distrust streaks. The LLN governs the long-run average, never the next trial. Any “it’s due” reasoning is the gambler’s fallacy.
Remember “swamping, not compensating.” Deviations don’t get cancelled; they get diluted. Keeping this straight immunises you against a whole family of chance errors.
Respect small samples. Convergence is asymptotic; at small n, averages can stray far. Don’t read a stable-looking mean off twenty data points.
Use Chebyshev for a quick, assumption-light bound. When you know only a mean and variance, it gives a guaranteed (if loose) probability bound for any distribution — handy as a sanity check before invoking Normality.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Markov’s inequality. For X \ge 0 and a > 0, let \mathbf{1}_{\{X \ge a\}} be the indicator of the event \{X \ge a\}. Since X \ge 0, we have X \ge a\,\mathbf{1}_{\{X \ge a\}} (it holds with equality-or-more when X \ge a, and trivially when X < a). Taking expectations, E[X] \ge a\,P\{X \ge a\}, hence P\{X \ge a\} \le E[X]/a.
Chebyshev’s inequality. Apply Markov to the non-negative variable (X-\mu)^2 with threshold k^2: 
P\{|X-\mu| \ge k\} = P\{(X-\mu)^2 \ge k^2\} \le \frac{E[(X-\mu)^2]}{k^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{k^2}.

Weak law of large numbers. With \bar X_n = \tfrac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i, independence gives E[\bar X_n] = \mu and \operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n. Chebyshev then yields, for any \varepsilon > 0, 
P\{|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\} \le \frac{\sigma^2}{n\varepsilon^2} \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{} 0.
 This is convergence in probability. The strong law strengthens it to convergence almost surely (P\{\lim_n \bar X_n = \mu\} = 1); its proof is harder and needs more than the second moment, but the conclusion is the one that licenses defining probability as a long-run frequency.
Why the absolute deviation grows. For fair coins, S_n - n/2 has standard deviation \tfrac12\sqrt n, so the typical absolute gap grows like \sqrt n even as the proportion gap |S_n/n - \tfrac12| shrinks like 1/\sqrt n. The \sqrt n is the same one that runs through the standard error and the CLT.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Connections

Builds on: Expectation & variance (the \sigma^2/n variance of the average) and independence (which the law and the fallacy both hinge on).
Pays off the promise of: What probability is — the frequentist interpretation, now proved consistent.
Leads directly to: the flagship Sampling Distributions & the CLT, which adds the shape to the LLN’s destination and opens Part II.

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text: Everything so far assumed tidy numbers. Real data arrives with missing values, outliers, mislabelled types, units silently mixed, and tails far heavier than any bell curve. Garbage in, garbage out: no model, however sophisticated, rescues conclusions built on bad data. This entry is the reality check that closes Part I — what to expect, how to diagnose it, and why the unglamorous work of structuring and cleaning data is as decisive as the analysis it precedes.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Why it matters
A common, costly belief among beginners is that analysis is the hard part and the data “is just there.” The opposite is closer to the truth: experienced researchers spend the majority of their time getting data into shape, because the cleanest regression in the world is worthless if it runs on a column where someone typed 500 for 50. Albers puts it bluntly — a well-designed study with bad data is still a failure (Albers 2017). This entry connects the chapter’s theory to the field’s practice, and it is where the robust statistics of D1 and the diagnostic plots of D2 finally earn their keep.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Data types and structure
Raw inputs — text, sensor streams, logs — must first be turned into structured data (Bruce et al. 2020). The standard structure is rectangular data, the data frame: a two-dimensional table where rows are records (observations, cases) and columns are features (variables, attributes) (Bruce et al. 2020). Almost every tool you’ll use expects this shape.
Within it, every variable has a type, and the type dictates what is legal to do with it (Bruce et al. 2020; Albers 2017):

Numeric — continuous (any value in a range, e.g. wind speed) or discrete (integer counts, e.g. number of complaints).
Categorical — a fixed set of values: nominal (unordered, e.g. department names), binary (two values, e.g. yes/no), or ordinal (ordered but with unknown gaps, e.g. a 1–5 Likert item).







TipType decides what’s valid — the Overture’s measurement levels, operationalised



Knowing the type isn’t bookkeeping; it determines which statistics and plots even mean something (Bruce et al. 2020). A scatterplot illuminates two numeric variables but collapses into an uninformative “stack” of points for ordinal data. An arithmetic mean is meaningless for nominal data — the “average” of Accounting and Shipping is nothing at all (Bruce et al. 2020). This is the measurement-levels lesson from the Overture, now as a daily practical constraint: match the method to the type, or compute nonsense precisely.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Missing data
Missingness is a fact of social-science life — participants refuse questions, drop out over time (attrition), or a researcher mis-records a value (Albers 2017). Why data is missing matters enormously:

Missing Completely At Random (MCAR) — no systematic pattern; the gaps are unrelated to anything. The benign case (Albers 2017).
Missing Not At Random (MNAR) — the missingness is systematic and informative. If high-income respondents disproportionately refuse to report income, the holes themselves are biased, creating hidden rival explanations for whatever you find (Albers 2017).

Two common fixes, both with risks (Albers 2017):

Listwise deletion — drop any record with a missing value. Simple, but it shrinks the sample and, if data is MNAR, leaves a non-representative remainder.
Imputation — fill the gaps. Mean substitution (insert the average) is widely criticised because it artificially shrinks variance and dampens correlations with other variables; regression imputation predicts the missing value from other variables and is usually better (Albers 2017).


Worked example (Albers). In a birth-weight study, 197 of 1,388 observations were dropped because parental-education data was missing — those cases simply fell out of the regression (Albers 2017). If the missingness was related to the outcome, that deletion quietly biased the result.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Outliers
An outlier is an observation lying an abnormal distance from the rest (Bruce et al. 2020). Standard detectors: values more than 3 standard deviations from the mean, or the boxplot’s 1.5 × IQR rule beyond the quartiles (Bruce et al. 2020; Albers 2017).
The temptation is to delete them. Resist it. Outliers matter doubly because OLS regression is acutely sensitive to them — least squares minimises squared residuals, so one extreme point carries enormous weight (Bruce et al. 2020). But Albers warns against blind deletion: an outlier may be a genuine anomaly worth studying, a sign of a bimodal mix of two populations, or just the tail of a long-tailed distribution — not an error (Albers 2017).

Worked example (Albers). In a typing-speed study where most scores cluster near 150 wpm, a single value of 54 wpm drags the group mean down to 133.67; delete it and the mean jumps to 149.6 — a materially different conclusion from one decision (Albers 2017). That single point deserves investigation, not a reflexive delete key.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Long-tailed distributions
The Normal curve is iconic, and — as P7 warned — a poor default for raw data (Bruce et al. 2020). Real variables are routinely skewed (asymmetric) or heavy-tailed (high kurtosis: a propensity for extreme values) (Bruce et al. 2020).
The stakes are captured by the black swan idea: extreme, anomalous events — market crashes, viral cascades, mega-claims — occur far more often than a Normal model predicts (Bruce et al. 2020). Tukey’s own observation is the useful mental model: data is often “normal in the middle” but with much longer tails than the bell allows (Bruce et al. 2020). Pricing risk, or any decision dominated by extremes, off a Normal assumption systematically understates the danger.

Worked example (Bruce & Bruce). A QQ-plot of Netflix (NFLX) stock returns bends sharply away from the straight Normal reference line at both ends — concrete evidence that extreme gains and losses strike far more often than Normality anticipates (Bruce et al. 2020).



Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
data = rng.standard_t(df=3, size=2000)          # heavy-tailed (Student-t, 3 df)
data = (data - data.mean()) / data.std()

# Theoretical Normal quantiles vs sorted standardized data.
# inv_cdf is the standard-normal inverse CDF (no scipy needed).
n = data.size
probs = (np.arange(1, n + 1) - 0.5) / n
nd = NormalDist()
theo = np.array([nd.inv_cdf(p) for p in probs])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4.2))
ax.scatter(theo, np.sort(data), s=10, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.6)
lim = [-4, 4]
ax.plot(lim, lim, color="#b5446e", lw=1.8)
ax.set_xlim(lim); ax.set_ylim(-8, 8)
ax.set_xlabel("Normal theoretical quantiles")
ax.set_ylabel("observed quantiles (standardized)")
ax.set_title("QQ-plot: tails peel off the Normal line")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A QQ-plot of heavy-tailed (Student-t) data against the Normal. Points track the line through the middle but peel away at both ends — the signature of fat tails, exactly as in Bruce & Bruce’s Netflix-returns example. ‘Normal in the middle, long in the tails.’
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Data cleaning and data quality
The governing principle is GIGO — garbage in, garbage out: even the most powerful algorithm cannot salvage valid findings from invalid data (Albers 2017). The usual culprits (Albers 2017):

Data-entry errors — typing 500 for 50.
Measurement errors — poorly calibrated instruments.
Bad units — mixing meters and kilometres in one column.

These are not cosmetic. Messy data threatens both internal validity (did the variable really cause the effect?) and external validity (do the results generalise?) — the credibility criteria from the Overture (Albers 2017).

Worked example (Albers). In a mindfulness study, a variable’s standard deviation (9.9) exceeded its mean (8.6) — a red flag. Chasing it down revealed a data-entry error: a score of 50 had been recorded as 500, single-handedly inflating the variance (Albers 2017). Note the method: a robust summary check from D1 caught the error — EDA and clean data are the same discipline.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Deleting cases with missing data without asking why. If the data is MNAR, listwise deletion bakes the bias in. Diagnose the missingness mechanism before dropping anything.
Mean-imputing blindly. It shrinks variance and weakens correlations — flattering your precision while distorting relationships.
Reflexively removing outliers. They may be the real signal — an anomaly, a second population, or a fat tail. Investigate first; document any removal.
Assuming Normality for raw data. Heavy tails are the rule, not the exception, for money, counts, and durations; a QQ-plot reveals them in seconds.
Skipping the units/entry sanity checks. SD larger than a positive mean, impossible values, suspiciously round numbers — these red flags catch the errors that wreck conclusions.
Treating cleaning as menial. It is where most real errors are made or caught. Garbage in, garbage out — no model fixes it downstream.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — rectangular data / data frames (records × features); numeric (continuous/discrete) vs categorical (nominal/binary/ordinal) and why type constrains valid methods (scatterplot vs ordinal; mean of nominal); outlier detection (3 SD, 1.5×IQR) and OLS’s sensitivity; long-tailed distributions, kurtosis, black swans, Tukey’s “normal in the middle,” and the Netflix QQ-plot.
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — missing data (MCAR vs MNAR; listwise deletion and imputation risks; mean substitution shrinking variance; the 197/1,388 birth-weight example); outliers as possible anomalies/bimodality/long tails and the danger of blind deletion (the 54-vs-150 wpm example); GIGO, data-entry/measurement/unit errors; validity threats; the mindfulness SD-9.9-vs-mean-8.6 data-entry catch.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Budget real time for cleaning. Plan for it to dominate the project; it usually does, and it’s where validity is won or lost.
Diagnose missingness before handling it. Ask whether gaps could relate to the outcome (MNAR); if so, deletion and naïve imputation both bias the result.
Investigate every outlier before touching it. Decide — and document — whether it’s an error, an anomaly, or a real fat tail. Never silently delete.
Run cheap sanity checks. SD vs mean, min/max, value counts, and a QQ-plot catch a surprising share of fatal errors in minutes — the same robust summaries from D1.
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Messy Real Data: What to Expect
text:
Connections

Builds on: location, spread & shape (robust summaries that flag bad data) and EDA (how you see the mess), plus the Overture’s measurement levels and credibility criteria.
Echoes: the long-tail warning from P7 — now with QQ-plots and black swans.
Leads to: inference (measurement error widens uncertainty), regression (OLS’s outlier sensitivity), and the tyranny of metrics (when a flawed measure is trusted anyway).
crumbs: Messy Real Data: What to Expect

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text: A random variable is not a variable in the algebra sense and not really random — it’s a function that attaches a number to each outcome of an experiment. That small reframing is what turns probability into something you can average, add, and plot. Two species cover everything you need: discrete variables you can count, and continuous ones you can only measure.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Why it matters
Up to now we’ve talked about events — sets of outcomes. But almost every quantity a social scientist studies is a number: a test score, an income, a count of protests, a vote share. The random variable is the bridge: it lets us stop describing outcomes one set at a time and start summarising them with a single distribution, then compress that distribution into an expectation and a variance. Every histogram you’ll ever draw, every “the average effect was…”, every regression coefficient is downstream of this definition.
The discrete/continuous split also previews a practical fork: discrete data invite counts and proportions, continuous data invite densities and areas, and the two call for different tools throughout the book.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
What a random variable is
Formally, a random variable is a real-valued function defined on the sample space — it maps each outcome to a number (Ross 2018). The motivation is honest convenience: we are usually more interested in a function of the outcome than the raw outcome itself. Roll two dice and you rarely care about the ordered pair (4,2); you care about the sum, 6. “Sum of the dice” is a random variable (Ross 2018).
The two species:

Discrete — takes at most a countable set of values. The number of heads in ten flips, the count of yes-responses in a survey, the number of children in a household. You can list the possible values (Ross 2018).
Continuous — takes values in an uncountable continuum. A person’s exact weight, the lifetime of a device, a precise income or waiting time. You can measure it to arbitrary precision but never enumerate the possibilities (Ross 2018).

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
section: Describing the distribution: pmf, pdf, cdf
text:
Describing the distribution: pmf, pdf, cdf
How you assign probability depends on the species.
Discrete → probability mass function (pmf). The pmf gives the probability of each value, p(a) = P\{X = a\}, and these masses must sum to 1 over all possible values (Ross 2018). Picture a bar at each value whose height is its probability.
Continuous → probability density function (pdf). Here probability is an area. A density f(x) is a curve such that the probability X lands in a region B is the area under f over B (Ross 2018): 
P\{X \in B\} = \int_B f(x)\,dx, \qquad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1.







TipWhy P(X = x) = 0 for continuous variables



For a continuous variable, the probability of any exact value is zero — the “width” of a single point is zero, so the area above it is zero (Ross 2018). This sounds paradoxical (some value does occur!) but it’s the right bookkeeping: for continuous quantities, only intervals carry probability. “The chance someone weighs exactly 70.000… kg” is 0; “the chance they weigh between 69.5 and 70.5 kg” is positive. The density f(x) is not a probability — it’s probability per unit length, which is why it can exceed 1.


Both → cumulative distribution function (cdf). The cdf works for any random variable: 
F(b) = P\{X \le b\}.
 It accumulates probability from the left. For a discrete variable it’s a step function (jumping at each value by that value’s mass); for a continuous variable it’s a smooth curve, and the pdf is exactly its derivative, f(x) = F'(x) (Ross 2018). The cdf is the one description that unifies the two species — handy whenever you need “the probability of at most” or want to compare distributions on equal footing.









Discrete
Continuous




Building block
pmf p(a) = P\{X=a\}
pdf f(x) (a density, not a probability)


Probability of a point
can be positive
always 0


Probability of a set
\sum_{a \in B} p(a)
\int_B f(x)\,dx (an area)


cdf F(b)=P\{X\le b\}
step function
smooth; f = F'


Total
\sum p(a) = 1
\int f = 1

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading a density as a probability. f(x) can be greater than 1; it is probability per unit, and only becomes a probability once integrated over an interval. Heights of a pdf are not chances.
Asking for P(X = x) on continuous data. It’s always 0. Rephrase as an interval, or use the cdf for “at most”/“at least.”
Forcing continuous data into the discrete mold (or vice versa). Counts (protests, children) are genuinely discrete; binning them or modelling them as Normal can mislead, especially for rare counts (see the Poisson).
Forgetting the distribution must sum/integrate to 1. A proposed pmf or pdf that doesn’t normalise isn’t a valid distribution — a quick, error-catching sanity check.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — random variable as a real-valued function on the sample space (the “sum of two dice” motivation); discrete (countable) vs continuous (uncountable) variables; pmf and its summing to 1; pdf with P\{X\in B\}=\int_B f; P(X=x)=0 for continuous variables; the cdf F(b)=P\{X\le b\} as a step function (discrete) and the pdf as the derivative of the cdf (continuous).

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Name the random variable explicitly. Writing “Let X = number of yes-responses” forces you to fix the support and the species before computing anything.
Decide discrete vs continuous early. It selects your whole toolkit — masses vs densities, sums vs integrals, bar charts vs histograms.
Use the cdf for “at least / at most” questions. It sidesteps fiddly summation and is the natural object for quantiles and percentiles later.
Always check it normalises. If your masses don’t sum to 1 (or your density doesn’t integrate to 1), something is wrong before you’ve even started.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Properties of the cdf. For any random variable, F is (i) non-decreasing, (ii) right- continuous, with (iii) \lim_{b\to-\infty}F(b)=0 and \lim_{b\to\infty}F(b)=1. Probabilities of intervals come straight from it: 
P\{a < X \le b\} = F(b) - F(a).

Discrete cdf as a sum of jumps. F(b) = \sum_{a \le b} p(a); the jump at each value a is exactly p(a) = F(a) - F(a^-), recovering the pmf from the cdf.
Continuous cdf and pdf. F(b) = \int_{-\infty}^{b} f(x)\,dx, so by the fundamental theorem of calculus f(x) = F'(x) wherever F is differentiable. The probability of an exact point is P\{X=x\} = F(x) - F(x^-) = 0 because F is continuous.
Functions of a random variable. If Y = g(X), its distribution can be found from X’s — but remarkably, you don’t need Y’s distribution to get its mean: that’s the job of LOTUS in the next entry.

title: Random Variables (Discrete & Continuous)
text:
Connections

Builds on: Sample spaces & the axioms — a random variable is a function on the sample space, and its probabilities inherit the axioms.
Leads to: Expectation & variance — once you have a distribution, you summarise it by a centre and a spread.
Pays off in: the common distributions (named pmfs and pdfs) and the sampling distribution of a statistic, which is itself the distribution of a random variable.

title: Simulation & Monte Carlo
text: Most real probability questions have no closed-form answer. Monte Carlo methods sidestep the algebra: generate random draws, and let the Law of Large Numbers turn their average into the answer. Lambert calls it “abandoning brute force” — instead of grinding out an integral, you sample and count. It is how you estimate an intractable probability, integrate in a thousand dimensions, build a bootstrap sampling distribution, and — not least — see the limit theorems happen on screen.

title: Simulation & Monte Carlo
text:
Why it matters
Simulation is both a computational tool (approximate what you can’t derive) and a way of thinking. Lambert stresses it’s the most direct route to internalising the “repeated sample and sampling distribution” concepts at the heart of statistics: when you can simulate a process a million times, the abstract idea of a sampling distribution becomes something you can literally plot. (Lambert 2018) His image: lost in dense fog, you take random steps and record where you are and how high — and from enough random samples you build a reliable map of the mountain. (Lambert 2018) Sampling is how you map a landscape you can’t see all at once.

title: Simulation & Monte Carlo
text:
Generating random variables
Everything rests on turning a stream of uniform random numbers into draws from the distribution you want. Two workhorse methods: (Ross 2018)

Inverse-transform. The basic technique: to draw from a distribution with CDF F, generate U\sim
\text{Uniform}(0,1) and set X = F^{-1}(U). (Ross 2018) (This is the probability integral transform run backwards.) An exponential variate, for instance, is X=-\ln(U)/\lambda. (Ross 2018) Clean when you can invert F.
Rejection sampling. When F^{-1} is intractable, draw from an easy proposal g(x) and accept the draw with probability proportional to f(x)/g(x), rejecting the rest. (Lambert 2018) It always works for a density you can evaluate, but Lambert warns it can be “pretty wasteful” — many draws thrown away — which is exactly the inefficiency that motivates MCMC for hard, high-dimensional targets. (Lambert 2018)

title: Simulation & Monte Carlo
text:
Monte Carlo integration
The central trick: an integral is an expectation, and an expectation is an average you can sample. To approximate \mathbb E[g(X)]=\int g(x)f(x)\,dx, draw X_1,\dots,X_n from f and take the sample mean: (Ross 2018) 
\mathbb E[g(X)] \approx \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n g(X_i).
 The Law of Large Numbers guarantees this converges to the true value, and the CLT tells you the error shrinks like 1/\sqrt{n}.
