Below, two estimators chase the same true mean (\mu = 1, marked). One is the ordinary sample mean — dead-centred but wide. The other shrinks it halfway to zero — visibly biased (shifted left) but far tighter. Watch which one ends up closer to the truth more often.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
mu, sigma, n = 1.0, 3.0, 10
reps = 50_000

samples = rng.normal(mu, sigma, size=(reps, n))
xbar   = samples.mean(axis=1)      # unbiased estimator
shrink = 0.5 * xbar                # biased toward 0, lower variance

def mse(est):  return np.mean((est - mu) ** 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.6))
bins = np.linspace(-3, 5, 80)
ax.hist(xbar,   bins=bins, density=True, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.75,
        label=f"Sample mean  X̄   (bias 0,  MSE ≈ {mse(xbar):.2f})")
ax.hist(shrink, bins=bins, density=True, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85,
        label=f"Shrinkage  ½·X̄   (bias −0.5, MSE ≈ {mse(shrink):.2f})")
ax.axvline(mu, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.8, label="True mean μ = 1")
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("Estimated value")
ax.set_title("Unbiased isn't always best — total error is what counts")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two estimators of the same mean μ = 1 (dashed line), over 50,000 samples of n = 10. The sample mean (grey) is unbiased but wide; the shrinkage estimator ½·X̄ (blue) is biased toward 0 yet tighter — and here it wins on total error (MSE), the sum of variance and squared bias.





The shrinkage estimator is wrong on average — its cloud is centred at 0.5, not 1 — yet because it wobbles so much less, its squared misses are smaller on the whole. When the signal is weak relative to the noise, a confident-but-slightly-biased rule beats an honest-but-jittery one. This is not a trick; it is the logic behind ridge regression, James–Stein estimation, and every regularised model in Part IV.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: Two more properties for large samples
text:
Two more properties for large samples
Consistency is the minimal demand we place on any serious estimator: as the sample grows without bound, the estimator’s whole distribution should collapse onto the true value. (Wooldridge 2019) Where unbiasedness is about averaging over many small samples, consistency is about one sample getting huge. The two can come apart: the sample standard deviation S is a consistent estimator of \sigma yet is biased in small samples — because the expectation of a square root is not the square root of an expectation, taking \sqrt{\,\cdot\,} bends the estimate. (Wooldridge 2019) An estimator that isn’t even consistent is hard to defend at all.
Efficiency ranks the survivors. Among unbiased estimators, the efficient one is the minimum-variance one — it extracts the most precision from the same data. The gold standard is the Minimum-Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE). (Ross 2018) In the regression setting this becomes the celebrated Gauss–Markov theorem: under its assumptions, Ordinary Least Squares is BLUE — the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator, “best” meaning smallest variance — a result we cash in fully in Part III. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: Where estimators come from: two recipes
text:
Where estimators come from: two recipes
So far we have judged estimators. Where do they come from? Two general recipes generate most of them.
Method of moments (MoM) — the intuitive one. A population moment (the mean \mu, the variance \sigma^2) is some function of the parameter you want. So replace each population moment with its sample counterpart and solve. The population mean’s sample counterpart is the sample average, which is exactly why \bar X is the method-of-moments estimator of \mu. (Wooldridge 2019) Simple, general, and the historical starting point.
Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) — the powerful one, and the workhorse of modern modelling. Fix a probability model, observe your data, then choose the parameter values that make the data you actually saw as probable as possible. (Ross 2018) Lambert frames the logic as an inversion: from the effects (data) we reason backward to the most likely cause (parameter). (Lambert 2018)






TipMLE in one coin flip



Flip a coin 10 times, see 7 heads. What bias p best explains that? The likelihood of 7 heads is \binom{10}{7}p^7(1-p)^3; maximise it over p and the answer is exactly \hat p = 7/10 = 0.7. The maximum-likelihood estimate is just the sample proportion — the value that makes “7 heads out of 10” the least surprising thing that could have happened. (Ross 2018; Lambert 2018)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: Why divide by n-1? Degrees of freedom
text:
Why divide by n-1? Degrees of freedom
The most-asked question in introductory statistics: why is the sample variance

S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X)^2

divided by n-1 and not n? Because dividing by n systematically underestimates the true variance — it is biased. (Wooldridge 2019) The intuition is degrees of freedom. To measure spread you must first compute the sample mean \bar X from the very same data; that step spends one piece of information and imposes a constraint (the deviations X_i - \bar X must sum to zero). Once n-1 of the deviations are known, the last is forced. More tellingly: each X_i sits closer to its own sample mean \bar X than to the unseen true mean \mu, so the squared deviations come out too small. Dividing by the smaller n-1 inflates them by exactly the right amount to make \mathbb{E}[S^2] = \sigma^2. (Wooldridge 2019) (A formal proof is in the appendix.)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Judging the estimate instead of the estimator. A single estimate is one draw from a distribution; it can be unlucky even when the procedure is excellent. Evaluate the rule’s long-run behaviour, not whether this one number “looks right.” (Wooldridge 2019)
Worshipping unbiasedness. Unbiased estimators can have ruinous variance. A slightly biased estimator with far lower variance has lower MSE and is genuinely better — the entire premise of regularisation. (Ross 2018; Wooldridge 2019)
Confusing standard deviation with standard error. \sigma (and its estimate S) describes the data’s spread; the standard error \sigma/\sqrt n describes the estimator’s spread. Same confusion that haunts the CLT entry; same cost.
Assuming consistency implies unbiasedness (or vice versa). They are different limits. S is consistent but biased in small samples; some estimators are unbiased yet inconsistent. Check the one you actually need. (Wooldridge 2019)
Forgetting the model behind MLE. Maximum likelihood is only as good as the distribution you assumed. Choose the wrong model and you efficiently estimate the wrong thing. (Ross 2018)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — estimator vs estimate and hat notation; unbiasedness and the formal bias definition; the \bar X-is-unbiased result; MSE = variance + bias² and the “always guess 0” trivial estimator; consistency as the minimal requirement; S consistent but small-sample biased; method of moments; Gauss–Markov / BLUE; the n-1 degrees-of-freedom argument.
Ross, A First Course in Probability — the estimator as a random variable; the shrinkage estimator with smaller MSE; efficiency and the minimum-variance unbiased estimator; maximum likelihood and the coin-bias example.
Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — MLE as inversion from effects (data) back to likely causes (parameters); the bridge to Bayesian inference.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — sampling-distribution intuition underlying estimator behaviour.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

The MSE decomposition. Add and subtract \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] inside the square:

\operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta) = \mathbb{E}\big[(\hat\theta - \theta)^2\big]
= \mathbb{E}\Big[\big((\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}\hat\theta) + (\mathbb{E}\hat\theta - \theta)\big)^2\Big].

Expand. The cross term is 2(\mathbb{E}\hat\theta - \theta)\,\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}\hat\theta] = 0 because \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}\hat\theta] = 0. What survives is

\operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\big[(\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}\hat\theta)^2\big]}_{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)}
+ \underbrace{(\mathbb{E}\hat\theta - \theta)^2}_{\operatorname{Bias}^2}.

Why n-1 is unbiased. With X_i i.i.d. (\mathbb{E}X_i=\mu, \operatorname{Var}X_i=\sigma^2), use the algebraic identity \sum_i (X_i-\bar X)^2 = \sum_i (X_i-\mu)^2 - n(\bar X-\mu)^2. Take expectations: \mathbb{E}\sum_i(X_i-\mu)^2 = n\sigma^2, and \mathbb{E}[(\bar X-\mu)^2]=\operatorname{Var}(\bar X)=\sigma^2/n, so

\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_i (X_i-\bar X)^2\right] = n\sigma^2 - n\cdot\frac{\sigma^2}{n} = (n-1)\sigma^2 .

Dividing by n-1 — not n — therefore gives \mathbb{E}[S^2]=\sigma^2. The “lost” \sigma^2/n is exactly the degree of freedom consumed by estimating \mu with \bar X.
MLE of a coin’s bias. The log-likelihood for k heads in n flips is \ell(p) = k\log p + (n-k)\log(1-p) + \text{const}. Differentiate and set to zero: \ell'(p) = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{n-k}{1-p} = 0 \Rightarrow \hat p = k/n. For k=7, n=10, \hat p = 0.7 — the sample proportion is the maximum-likelihood estimate. The second derivative is negative, confirming a maximum.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
Connections

Builds on: sampling distributions & the CLT (an estimator has a sampling distribution; that is what bias and variance describe), expectation & variance, and sampling design (a good estimator on a bad sample is still biased).
Leads to: confidence intervals (a point estimate plus a measured margin) and hypothesis testing (is the estimate surprising under a null?). The MSE decomposition is the seed of the bias–variance trade-off in Part IV.
Reappears in: every regression coefficient (OLS is BLUE by Gauss–Markov) and every causal estimate, each of which is an estimator to be judged by exactly these criteria.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text: A hypothesis test asks one disciplined question: if nothing were going on, how surprising would my data be? The p-value measures that surprise. It is a genuinely useful number wrapped in a thick fog of misunderstanding — and clearing the fog is most of what it takes to use it well.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Why it matters
Null hypothesis significance testing (NHST) is the most widely used statistical procedure in all of science. (Bruce et al. 2020) It is also the most widely abused, and the gap between those two facts is the engine of the replication crisis. The trouble is rarely the arithmetic; it is that the p-value answers a narrow, conditional question while researchers crave a broad, unconditional one — and the slippage between the two has corrupted whole literatures.
So this entry does two things at once: it builds the testing machinery honestly, and it polices the single most consequential misreading in quantitative research. Get the definition of a p-value exactly right and most of the downstream sins become visible.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
The logic: proof by contradiction
A hypothesis test is an argument by contradiction. (Bruce et al. 2020) You set up two rival claims:

The null hypothesis H_0 — the “nothing special is happening” baseline: no effect, no difference, no relationship. It exists to be falsified. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
The alternative hypothesis H_1 — the research hypothesis, the effect you suspect is real.

You then assume the null is true and ask whether the data you actually collected is wildly inconsistent with that assumption. If the data would be bizarrely unlikely under “no effect,” you reject the null and take that as evidence for the alternative. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) The asymmetry is deliberate and important: you never prove the null, you only fail to reject it — exactly as a court returns “not guilty,” not “innocent.”

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
section: The test statistic: a surprise meter
text:
The test statistic: a surprise meter
How do you measure “inconsistent with the null”? You compress the data into a single test statistic — one number summarising how far your result sits from what the null predicts. (Bruce et al. 2020) Think of it as a surprise meter: the larger its magnitude, the more your data departs from the null’s expectation, and the more the null looks like the wrong model for your world. (Bruce et al. 2020) A test statistic of 0.3 says “business as usual”; a 4.0 says “something is off.”

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
The p-value, defined exactly
Here is the definition, and every word earns its place:

The p-value is the probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true. (Bruce et al. 2020)

It is the tail area — the probability that pure chance, with no real effect operating, would produce a result as striking as yours or more so. A small p-value means your data sits far out in the tail where the null rarely ventures, so the null becomes hard to believe.

The p-value as an area
That “tail area” is literal. Below is the sampling distribution of the test statistic under the null — the bell of results you’d see if nothing were going on. The observed statistic lands at \pm 2.1, and the p-value is simply the shaded area beyond it in both tails.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

nd = NormalDist()
z_obs = 2.1
x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 600)
y = np.array([nd.pdf(v) for v in x])
p_two = 2 * (1 - nd.cdf(z_obs))   # two-tailed p-value ≈ 0.0357

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.6))
ax.plot(x, y, color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.8)
ax.fill_between(x, y, where=(x >=  z_obs), color="#c0392b", alpha=0.75)
ax.fill_between(x, y, where=(x <= -z_obs), color="#c0392b", alpha=0.75)
ax.axvline( z_obs, color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, ls="--")
ax.axvline(-z_obs, color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, ls="--")
ax.annotate(f"observed = +2.1", xy=(z_obs, nd.pdf(z_obs)), xytext=(2.4, 0.22),
            fontsize=9, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"))
ax.set_title(f"p-value = shaded tail area ≈ {p_two:.3f}")
ax.set_xlabel("Test statistic (standardized), under the null")
ax.set_yticks([])
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The p-value is a tail area. Under the null, the test statistic follows the bell shown. An observed value of 2.1 (two-tailed) cuts off the shaded tails — their combined area, here about 0.036, is the p-value: the chance of a result this extreme if nothing were going on.





If that shaded area is smaller than your pre-set threshold, you reject the null. The picture also makes the definition’s conditional vivid: the entire bell is drawn assuming the null is true. The p-value lives inside that assumption — which is exactly why it cannot, by itself, tell you whether the assumption holds.


What the p-value is not
This is the most important list in Part II. The p-value is: (Reinhart 2015)

NOT the probability that the null is true. It is computed assuming the null; it cannot turn around and report the null’s probability. (\Pr(\text{data}\mid H_0) is not \Pr(H_0\mid\text{data}) — flipping them is the transposed-conditional error, the same blunder as the base-rate fallacy.) (Reinhart 2015)
NOT the probability your result was a fluke. Same transposition: it gives the odds of the data if the null holds, not the odds favouring the null given the data. (Reinhart 2015)
NOT a measure of effect size. A microscopic, meaningless effect can yield p<0.001 if n is huge. Small p \ne big effect. (Bruce et al. 2020)
NOT the probability of replication, and a non-significant p>0.05 does NOT mean “no effect” — it may just mean the study was underpowered to detect one. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
section: The decision rule: α and the two errors
text:
The decision rule: α and the two errors
To turn the continuous p-value into a decision, you fix a threshold in advance — the significance level \alpha, conventionally 0.05 in social science. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) \alpha is a pre-committed error budget: it is the rate at which you are willing to reject a true null and cry wolf. Choosing it before seeing the data is what keeps the procedure honest.
Because the verdict is probabilistic, two mistakes are possible: (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)









H_0 true
H_0 false




Reject H_0
Type I error (false positive), rate \alpha
Correct — power = 1-\beta


Fail to reject
Correct
Type II error (false negative), rate \beta



A Type I error cries wolf — declaring a drug works when it doesn’t. A Type II error misses a real wolf — failing to notice a drug that genuinely works. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) They trade off: tightening \alpha to 0.01 cuts false positives but raises the false-negative rate \beta, costing you power — the subject of the next entry. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
One-tailed vs two-tailed
A two-tailed test asks “is there an effect in either direction?” and splits the rejection region across both tails (the case drawn above). A one-tailed test stakes everything on a direction chosen in advance (“B beats A”) and puts the whole rejection region in one tail, making it easier to reach significance there. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) The catch is non-negotiable: you must choose the direction before seeing the data. Switching to a one-tailed test after peeking, because it drags you under p<0.05, is a textbook form of p-hacking. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
The workhorses: z- and t-tests
The z-test applies when the population standard deviation \sigma is known — rare outside standardized-test norming. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The t-test is what you actually use: \sigma is unknown, so you estimate it with the sample s, and the statistic follows Student’s t-distribution with its uncertainty-absorbing fat tails. (Bruce et al. 2020) The one-sample form is

t = \frac{\bar y - \mu_0}{\operatorname{se}(\bar y)} = \frac{\bar y - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt n},

the observed gap measured in standard errors. It comes in three flavours: (Bruce et al. 2020)

One-sample — compare a sample mean to a fixed benchmark. Does this class’s mean score differ from the district average of 105?
Two-sample (independent) — compare the means of two separate groups, with  t = \frac{\bar X_1 - \bar X_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}}.  Did the treatment group outscore the control group?
Paired (dependent) — compare the same units at two times, testing the mean of the differences. Did participants’ weight change from pre-test to post-test? Pairing strips out person-to-person variation and is far more powerful when it applies.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Statistical vs practical significance
The deepest trap in the whole apparatus: a result can be statistically significant yet practically trivial. (Bruce et al. 2020) Statistical significance is a binary yes/no about whether p<\alpha; practical (substantive) significance is about the magnitude and real-world importance of the effect. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) Wooldridge’s example: a study of baseball salaries might find a statistically significant effect of “runs per year” (p<0.05), but if each extra run is worth only $10, the effect is economically meaningless. (Wooldridge 2019) With a large enough n, everything becomes significant; the question that matters is how big, not whether. Always report and interpret the effect size, not just the verdict.






NoteThe effect size is the scientific question (Kaplan)



Daniel Kaplan makes this the organizing principle of Lessons in Statistical Thinking: the job of statistics is to estimate how much — the effect size — because that magnitude is what actually informs a decision or a policy, whereas a significance verdict does not. (Kaplan 2024) He is blunt that the test was never the scientific question, quoting the biostatistician Frank Harrell: the “complacency … in statistics education has resulted in [the mis-]belief that null hypothesis significance testing ever answered the scientific question.” (Kaplan 2024) Kaplan’s reform is the one this entry keeps arriving at — report a magnitude with its uncertainty and ask what level of belief or plausibility it warrants, rather than collapsing the evidence into a binary “significant / not.” This reframes statistics as accounting for variation in service of judgement, not a verdict machine. (Kaplan 2024)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
The duality with confidence intervals
Testing and interval estimation are the same logic in two costumes. (Wooldridge 2019) The bridge:

If the null value falls outside the 95% confidence interval, you reject H_0 at the 5% level.

So the scrap-rate interval [-2.28,\,-0.02] — which excludes zero — is a rejection of “no effect” at 5%. (Wooldridge 2019) The interval is strictly more informative than the test: it reports the whole range of effect sizes you can’t reject, not just a pass/fail. This is why a growing chorus urges reporting intervals and effect sizes over bare p-values — a reform we take up in the replication-crisis entry.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Reading p as “the probability the null is true” (or “the probability it’s a fluke”). The cardinal error — a transposed conditional. The p-value lives entirely inside the assumption that the null holds. (Reinhart 2015)
Reading a big p-value as proof of no effect. p>0.05 can mean “no effect” or “too little data to see one.” Absence of evidence isn’t evidence of absence — check the power. (Reinhart 2015)
Confusing statistical with practical significance. A trivially small effect is “significant” at large n; a large, important effect can be “non-significant” at small n. The verdict is not the magnitude. (Bruce et al. 2020; Wooldridge 2019)
Choosing one- vs two-tailed after seeing the data, or nudging \alpha to fit the result. Both are p-hacking; both inflate false positives far above the nominal 5%. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating p = 0.049 and p = 0.051 as categorically different. The 0.05 line is a convention, not a law of nature. A p-value is continuous evidence, not a cliff edge. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — NHST as proof by contradiction; the test statistic as a surprise meter; the exact p-value definition; small-p-from-large-n; z- vs t-tests and the three t-test forms.
Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — the catalogue of what a p-value is not (transposed conditional; not effect size; not replication probability); non-significance ≠ no effect; one- vs two-tailed switching and threshold-fiddling as p-hacking; the arbitrariness of 0.05.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative & Statistical Research Methods — H_0/H_1 and the falsification logic; \alpha as a pre-committed error rate; Type I/II errors and their trade-off; statistical vs practical significance.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the baseball-salary example of statistical-but-not- economic significance; the CI–test duality and the scrap-rate interval.
Kaplan, Lessons in Statistical Thinking — effect size as the quantity that actually answers the scientific question (the Frank Harrell critique of NHST), and the reframing of inference as establishing a level of belief or plausibility in a magnitude rather than a binary verdict.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

The one-sample t-test. Under H_0: \mu = \mu_0, with X_i i.i.d. and \sigma estimated by s, the statistic

T = \frac{\bar X - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt n}

follows a t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom (exactly, if the X_i are Normal; approximately otherwise, by the CLT). The two-sided p-value is p = 2\,\Pr(t_{n-1} \ge |T_\text{obs}|).
Two-sample (Welch) form. For independent groups with possibly unequal variances,

T = \frac{\bar X_1 - \bar X_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}},

with Welch–Satterthwaite degrees of freedom. The denominator is the standard error of the difference — which is why two overlapping single-group intervals can still yield a significant T: the SE of a difference is not the sum of the two separate SEs (see the overlap fallacy).
Paired form. Reduce to one sample by differencing: let D_i = X_i^{\text{post}} -
X_i^{\text{pre}} and run a one-sample test of \bar D against 0. Because \operatorname{Var}(D) =
\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\operatorname{Cov}(X^{\text{pre}}, X^{\text{post}}) and the within-person correlation is usually positive, pairing shrinks the standard error — the source of its extra power.
Why the CI–test duality holds. The 95% interval is exactly the set of null values \mu_0 that would not be rejected at the 5% level: |{\bar X - \mu_0}|/(s/\sqrt n) \le t_{n-1,0.975} rearranges to \mu_0 \in [\bar X \pm t_{n-1,0.975}\, s/\sqrt n]. Reject \iff \mu_0 outside the interval.

title: Hypothesis Testing & p-values
text:
Connections

Builds on: confidence intervals (its dual), estimation (the standard error in the denominator), sampling distributions & the CLT (the null distribution of the statistic), and the base-rate fallacy (the transposed conditional that wrecks p-value interpretation).
Leads to: statistical power (the Type II / \beta side of the table) and the replication crisis (what happens when testing is abused at scale).
Reappears as: the t- and F-statistics on every regression coefficient, and the significance tests behind every causal estimate.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text: You have data and a model with unknown knobs. Maximum likelihood turns the knobs to whatever setting makes the data you actually observed the most probable. That single principle — “choose the parameters that make the observed sample most likely” — generates the estimators for logistic regression, Poisson regression, mixed models, and far beyond, and it comes with a built-in measure of its own precision. Once you see it, a huge swath of statistics stops being a list of formulas and becomes one move applied again and again.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Why it matters
OLS handles linear models, but the moment the outcome is binary (logit/probit), a count (Poisson), or censored (Tobit), least squares is “infeasible” or “notably inconsistent.” (Wooldridge 2019) Maximum likelihood is the general estimation framework that covers all of them — and most of the models you’ll meet are “fit by maximum likelihood” under the hood. Understanding it once explains the standard errors, the asymptotics, and the link to Bayes everywhere else.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
The likelihood: probability, read backwards
Start with the likelihood function, \Pr(\text{data}\mid\theta). A probability distribution fixes the parameters \theta and asks how likely various data are. The likelihood reverses the view: it holds the data fixed (the sample you actually got) and lets the parameters vary, asking — for each candidate \theta — how probable would this data have been? (Lambert 2018) Maximum likelihood then picks the \hat\theta that “maximizes the likelihood of achieving the observed data sample” — the value that makes the sample “most likely.” (Moffatt 2016)






NoteWorked intuition: a biased coin



Draw 10 times and see 7 successes, 3 failures. If the success probability is p, the likelihood of that exact sample is 
L(p) = p^7(1-p)^3.
 Take logs, differentiate, set to zero, and the maximiser is \hat p = 0.7 — exactly the observed proportion. (Moffatt 2016) That is maximum likelihood’s sanity check: the parameter that makes the data most probable is the one that matches what you saw. (Lambert 2018)

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Why the log-likelihood
In practice you almost always maximise the log-likelihood, \ell(\theta)=\log L(\theta), for three reasons: (Moffatt 2016)

Sums beat products. With independent observations the likelihood is a product of densities; the log turns it into a sum, which is far easier to differentiate. (Wooldridge 2019)
Numerical stability. A product of many small probabilities is a number “extremely close to zero” — a very flat function a computer struggles to maximise. The log “stretches it vertically,” exposing the peak. (Moffatt 2016)
Same answer. Because \log is monotonically increasing, the \theta that maximises \ell is the identical \theta that maximises L. (Moffatt 2016)







TipMLE contains OLS



For a Normal model, the log-likelihood is a constant minus \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_i (y_i-\beta x_i)^2. So maximising the likelihood is exactly minimising the sum of squared residuals — the MLE of a Normal mean is the OLS estimator. (Wooldridge 2019) Least squares is maximum likelihood wearing a Gaussian coat.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
section: The shape of the likelihood is your precision
text:
The shape of the likelihood is your precision
Maximum likelihood hands you uncertainty for free, through the curvature of the log-likelihood at its peak: (Moffatt 2016)

The score is the gradient \partial\ell/\partial\theta — the direction that would raise the likelihood; it is zero at the MLE.
The Fisher information is the curvature (negative second derivative) at the peak. The memorable picture: a strongly peaked likelihood means “only a small range of parameters could have generated that data” — high confidence; a gently peaked, flat one means low confidence. (Moffatt 2016)

The standard error follows directly: the variance of the MLE is the inverse of the Fisher information — so peakedness dictates precision. (Moffatt 2016)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

p = np.linspace(0.01, 0.99, 400)

def loglik(n, phat):
    k = phat * n
    ll = k * np.log(p) + (n - k) * np.log(1 - p)
    return ll - ll.max()          # rescale so the peak sits at 0 (compare shapes)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.plot(p, loglik(10, 0.7),  color="#9bb4d4", lw=2.4, label="n = 10  (flat peak → imprecise)")
ax.plot(p, loglik(200, 0.7), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6, label="n = 200 (sharp peak → precise)")
ax.axvline(0.7, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.3)
ax.text(0.705, -6.5, r"$\hat p = 0.7$", color="#c0392b", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylim(-8, 0.5)
ax.set_xlabel("candidate parameter  $p$"); ax.set_ylabel("log-likelihood (peak set to 0)")
ax.set_title("Maximum likelihood: the peak is the estimate, its sharpness the precision")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="lower center")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The likelihood peaks at the observed proportion, and its sharpness is your precision. Both curves are coin-flip log-likelihoods with the SAME 70% success rate, but n=10 (light) vs n=200 (dark), each rescaled to its own peak. More data → a far sharper peak at p=0.7 → larger Fisher information → a smaller standard error. The width of the peak is, literally, your uncertainty.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
section: Why statisticians trust it: the asymptotic properties
text:
Why statisticians trust it: the asymptotic properties
Maximum likelihood is the default because of its “superior statistical properties” as the sample grows: (Wooldridge 2019)

Consistency — as n\to\infty, \hat\theta converges to the true value. Wooldridge calls this the “minimal requirement” for a useful estimator, quoting Clive Granger: “If you can’t get it right as n goes to infinity, you shouldn’t be in this business.” (Wooldridge 2019)
Asymptotic normality — even when the data aren’t Normal, the sampling distribution of the MLE becomes approximately Normal in large samples (so the usual z/t machinery applies). (Wooldridge 2019)
Asymptotic efficiency — among consistent estimators, the MLE has the smallest possible variance as n\to\infty, attaining the Cramér–Rao lower bound (the theoretical floor for an unbiased estimator’s variance). (Wooldridge 2019)
Invariance — if \hat\theta is the MLE of \theta, then g(\hat\theta) is the MLE of g(\theta) for any continuous g — so you can estimate odds ratios, elasticities, or probabilities without re-deriving anything. (Moffatt 2016)

These are large-sample guarantees; in small samples the MLE can be biased (a reason the bootstrap and exact methods still matter).

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
The Bayesian neighbour
Maximum likelihood and Bayesian estimation share the same likelihood but treat \theta differently. (Lambert 2018) MLE is frequentist: it treats the parameter as a “static, unwavering constant” and reports its single peak. A Bayesian treats \theta as a random variable and uses the likelihood to compute a full posterior — a whole distribution of belief. The bridge is the MAP (maximum a posteriori) estimate, the mode of the posterior — and Lambert’s clean identity: MLE is exactly the MAP estimate under a flat (uniform) prior. (Lambert 2018) So “just maximise the likelihood” is the Bayesian answer when you bring no prior information — which is also why regularization (a penalty) is MAP with an informative prior.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading large-sample properties as small-sample truth. Consistency/efficiency are n\to\infty guarantees; small-sample MLEs can be biased. (Wooldridge 2019)
Maximising the raw likelihood numerically. It underflows to ~0 and is too flat; always work on the log scale. (Moffatt 2016)
Forgetting MLE supplies its own SEs. The inverse Fisher information is the variance; a flat peak means a big standard error you must report, not hide. (Moffatt 2016)
Using OLS where MLE is required. For binary, count, or censored outcomes, least squares is infeasible or inconsistent — switch to the likelihood. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — MLE for nonlinear models (logit/probit, Poisson, Tobit) where OLS is infeasible/inconsistent; the Normal log-likelihood reducing to least squares; the asymptotic properties — consistency (the Granger “minimal requirement” quote), asymptotic normality, efficiency / Cramér–Rao.
Moffatt, Experimetrics — the likelihood as “make the observed sample most likely”; the coin/urn worked example (\hat p=0.7); log-likelihood (sums vs products, the “very flat function” / vertical stretch, monotonicity); the score, Fisher information as curvature (“strongly vs gently peaked”), and SEs as the inverse information; invariance; the experimental-design “chicken-and-egg.”
Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — likelihood as probability of data viewed as a function of \theta; the MLE vs MAP vs full posterior contrast and the identity MLE = MAP under a flat prior.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The estimator. With independent data, \ell(\theta)=\sum_i \log f(y_i;\theta). The MLE solves the score equation \nabla_\theta \ell(\hat\theta)=0. Under regularity conditions, 
\sqrt{n}\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)\ \xrightarrow{d}\ \mathcal N\!\big(0,\ \mathcal I(\theta_0)^{-1}\big),
 where \mathcal I(\theta)=-\mathbb E\big[\partial^2\ell/\partial\theta\,\partial\theta^\top\big] is the Fisher information — the curvature. The asymptotic variance \mathcal I^{-1} is the Cramér–Rao bound, so the MLE is efficient; in practice the SE is \sqrt{[\hat{\mathcal I}^{-1}]_{jj}}.
Coin, in full. \ell(p)=k\log p+(n-k)\log(1-p) with k successes. Setting \ell'(p)=k/p-(n-k)/(1-p)=0 gives \hat p=k/n; the second derivative gives \mathcal I(p)=n/[p(1-p)], so \operatorname{Var}(\hat p)\approx p(1-p)/n — the familiar binomial standard error, derived from curvature.
MLE ⊂ MAP. The posterior mode maximises \ell(\theta)+\log \pi(\theta). A flat prior makes \log\pi constant, so the MAP equals the MLE; an informative prior adds a penalty — the link to ridge/lasso.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Connections

Builds on: estimation (MLE is the workhorse estimator), common distributions (the densities you take logs of), and the likelihood in Bayes.
Powers: logit & probit, GLMs, count models, survival/Cox, and mixed models — all fit by maximum likelihood.
Contrasts with: the Bayesian posterior (MLE = MAP under a flat prior) and is penalised into regularization.

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text: Every real dataset has missing values, and the software default — delete any row with a hole — can be a “grave threat” to your conclusions. Filling holes with the column mean is worse: it shrinks variance, flattens relationships, and pretends a guess is a fact. The honest approach has two parts: diagnose why the data is missing (the mechanism), then use multiple imputation — fill the holes several times, with noise, and combine — so the final uncertainty includes the fact that you were guessing.

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
Why it matters
Missingness is not a nuisance to be cleared before “the real analysis” — it is part of the analysis. Get it wrong and you bias estimates, lose power, and report confidence intervals that are too narrow. Get it right and you recover most of what careless deletion throws away, with honest uncertainty. This entry sits in inference because it is fundamentally about propagating the uncertainty of what you didn’t observe.

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
The three mechanisms
What you’re allowed to do depends entirely on why values are missing (Murphy 2012; Gerber and Green 2012):

MCAR — Missing Completely At Random. Missingness is independent of both observed and unobserved data — “unpredictable, no systematic pattern” (Gerber and Green 2012). The observed data is a random subsample, so estimates stay unbiased (you just lose power) (Gerber and Green 2012).
MAR — Missing At Random. Missingness depends only on observed data, not the missing value itself (Murphy 2012). Example: men report income less often, but conditional on gender it’s random. Under MAR you can recover unbiased estimates by conditioning on the relevant covariates (Murphy 2012).
MNAR — Missing Not At Random. Missingness depends on the unobserved value itself — the “worst type” because it is informative (very high or very low earners skipping the income question) (Gerber and Green 2012). Standard fixes fail; the missingness pattern is itself a rival explanation (Gerber and Green 2012).

The crux: MCAR and MAR are handle-able; MNAR needs a model of the missingness (or honest caveats). Most methods, including multiple imputation, assume MAR.

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
Why the naive fixes fail


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
n = 200
x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
y_full = 1.0 + 0.8*x + rng.normal(0, 1.5, n)
# MAR: probability of missingness rises with x (depends on OBSERVED x, not y)
p_miss = 1/(1 + np.exp(-(x - 6)))
miss = rng.random(n) < p_miss
obs = ~miss

def slope(xx, yy):
    return np.polyfit(xx, yy, 1)[0]

# mean imputation
y_mean = y_full.copy(); y_mean[miss] = y_full[obs].mean()
# single MI draw: regress y~x on observed, impute = prediction + noise
b1, b0 = np.polyfit(x[obs], y_full[obs], 1)
resid_sd = np.std(y_full[obs] - (b0 + b1*x[obs]), ddof=2)
y_mi = y_full.copy()
y_mi[miss] = b0 + b1*x[miss] + rng.normal(0, resid_sd, miss.sum())

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4.0), sharey=True)
xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 30)
for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.plot(xs, 1.0 + 0.8*xs, "k--", lw=1.4, alpha=0.7)
    ax.scatter(x[obs], y_full[obs], s=12, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.5, label="observed")
    ax.set_xlabel("x")
axL.scatter(x[miss], y_mean[miss], s=18, color="#c0392b", label="mean-imputed")
axL.set_title(f"Mean imputation  (slope {slope(x, y_mean):.2f})"); axL.set_ylabel("y")
axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
axR.scatter(x[miss], y_mi[miss], s=18, color="#1f8a4c", label="MI-imputed (1 draw)")
axR.set_title(f"Multiple imputation  (slope {slope(x, y_mi):.2f})")
axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.suptitle(f"True slope = 0.80.  Mean imputation flattens it; MI preserves it.", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two ways to fill the holes, on the same MAR data (y is missing more often when x is large). Grey points are observed; the dashed line is the true relationship. Left — MEAN imputation places every missing y at the observed mean (red), a horizontal stripe that sits off the trend and FLATTENS the estimated slope (the correlation is attenuated, the variance shrunk). Right — a single MULTIPLE-IMPUTATION draw fills each hole from the predictive distribution (regression line + random noise, green), preserving both the relationship and the spread. Mean imputation fakes certainty by inventing data on the mean; MI injects the uncertainty back.






Complete-case (listwise) deletion — the software default — drops any row with a single missing value (Gerber and Green 2012). It is a “grave threat” if data aren’t MCAR (the survivors are unrepresentative), and it shrinks the effective sample, inflating standard errors and sacrificing power (Gerber and Green 2012).
Single / mean imputation replaces a hole with the average (Murphy 2012). It artificially reduces variance, lowers correlations, and — fatally — “understates uncertainty” by treating a guess as a known value (Murphy 2012). The left panel above is this failure made visible.

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
Multiple imputation (Rubin)
Multiple imputation (MI) fixes single imputation’s lie by admitting we don’t know the missing values (Murphy 2012):

Create M completed datasets. In each, draw the missing entries from a posterior predictive distribution p(x_{\text{hidden}}\mid x_{\text{visible}},\theta) that includes random noise to reflect uncertainty (Murphy 2012).
Analyse each separately with standard methods (e.g. OLS), giving M estimates and M standard errors (Murphy 2012).
Combine with Rubin’s rules (Murphy 2012):

Point estimate = the average of the M estimates.
Total variance = the within-imputation variance (average of the M variances — ordinary sampling error) plus (1+\tfrac1M)\times the between-imputation variance (the variance of the M estimates). That second term is the magic: it explicitly captures the extra uncertainty created by the missingness itself (Murphy 2012).


This is why MI’s confidence intervals are honestly wider than mean imputation’s — they remember you were guessing.

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
MICE: chained equations
The workhorse algorithm is MICE (Multivariate Imputation by Chained Equations) (Murphy 2012). It handles many missing variables of mixed types by specifying a regression for each variable conditional on all the others, then cycling through the variables — replacing each one’s missing values with draws from its model — until the imputations converge to a stable joint distribution (Murphy 2012).

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
Practical guidance

Include auxiliary variables. Put variables that predict missingness into the imputation model even if they won’t be in the final analysis — it makes the MAR assumption more plausible (Murphy 2012).
Impute, then transform. Do logs/√ after imputation (or build them into the imputation model) so you don’t distort relationships between variables (Murphy 2012).
Use enough imputations. The old advice of M=5 is superseded — modern computing makes M=20+ cheap and gives stable between-imputation variance (Murphy 2012).
Report a sensitivity analysis. Because MAR can never be proven, it’s good practice to report both the complete-case and imputed results and check they agree (Gerber and Green 2012).

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Deleting rows by default. Complete-case analysis is biased unless MCAR and always wastes power; justify it or impute. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Mean (single) imputation. It fakes certainty and attenuates relationships — never use it for inference. (Murphy 2012)
Imputing the outcome and then “finding” effects. Including the analysis variables but omitting key predictors of missingness undercuts the MAR assumption. (Murphy 2012)
Assuming MAR when MNAR is plausible. If the value itself drives its missingness (income, symptom severity), standard MI is biased — model the missingness or caveat heavily. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Reporting MI point estimates with naive SEs. You must use Rubin’s rules; the between-imputation variance is the whole point. (Murphy 2012)

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Murphy, Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective — the MAR/MNAR definitions; mean imputation’s variance shrinkage and understated uncertainty; multiple imputation as M noisy draws from the posterior predictive; Rubin’s combining rules (average estimate; within + (1+1/M)·between variance); MICE / chained equations; and the auxiliary-variable, impute-then-transform, and M guidance.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments (and Martin & Bridgmon) — MCAR as a random subsample; complete-case deletion as a “grave threat” and power loss; MNAR as informative missingness; and reporting both complete-case and imputed results as sensitivity analysis.

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Rubin’s rules, formally. With per-imputation estimates \hat\theta_m and variances U_m (m=1,\dots,M):

\bar\theta = \frac{1}{M}\sum_m \hat\theta_m,\qquad
\bar U = \frac{1}{M}\sum_m U_m \ \text{(within)},\qquad
B = \frac{1}{M-1}\sum_m (\hat\theta_m-\bar\theta)^2 \ \text{(between)}.

The total variance is T = \bar U + \big(1+\tfrac1M\big)B. As B\to0 (the imputations agree, little missing-data uncertainty) T\to\bar U, the ordinary variance; as B grows, the intervals widen. The (1+1/M) factor corrects for using a finite number of imputations. Inference uses a t-distribution with Rubin’s degrees of freedom \nu = (M-1)\big(1+\bar U/((1+1/M)B)\big)^2.
Why mean imputation shrinks variance. Replacing n_{\text{mis}} values with the constant \bar x adds points with zero deviation from the mean, so the sample variance \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2 is computed with extra zero terms in the numerator but a larger denominator — it is biased downward, and any correlation with another variable is attenuated toward zero. MI avoids this by imputing draws with the right residual spread.

title: Missing Data & Multiple Imputation
text:
Connections

Builds on: sampling & survey design (nonresponse is missingness) and the bootstrap / Bayesian computation (MI draws from a predictive distribution, a Bayesian idea).
Complements: the Dataframes track’s missing-values mechanics — this entry is the statistical theory behind those operations.
Reappears in: any regression or causal estimate built on incomplete data.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text: The replication crisis is not a story about fraud. It is what happens when honest researchers, pushed by real incentives, use the tools of the previous six entries slightly wrong — flexibly, selectively, repeatedly — and a publication system rewards exactly that. The mathematics of why the literature fills with false positives is the natural capstone of this whole sub-chapter.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Why it matters
Across psychology, medicine, and economics, findings that everyone “knew” to be true have collapsed when independent labs tried to reproduce them. (Reinhart 2015) The unsettling part is the diagnosis: this is driven less by fraud than by the systemic misuse of statistical tools and the misaligned incentives of publishing. (Reinhart 2015) Every mechanism below is something a well-meaning researcher can do without ever feeling dishonest — which is precisely what makes it dangerous, and what makes understanding it a core competence rather than an ethics footnote.
This entry gathers the threads of Part II — p-values, power, confidence intervals, and the base-rate logic of Bayes — into one rigorous account of how science goes wrong at scale, and how it is being fixed.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: Researcher degrees of freedom: the garden of forking paths
text:
Researcher degrees of freedom: the garden of forking paths
Hand the same dataset to the same question, and a researcher still faces a thicket of small, individually defensible choices: which outliers to drop, which covariates to control for, how to code a borderline response, where to bin a continuous variable. Gelman and Loken named the result the garden of forking paths. (Reinhart 2015) Each fork looks reasonable in isolation; taken together they grant enormous researcher degrees of freedom — enough latitude to steer an analysis, even unconsciously, toward whatever comes out significant. Simulations show that flexible outlier handling and covariate selection alone can swing an effect size by a factor of two — routinely enough to drag a null result across the p<0.05 line. (Reinhart 2015)
The deep point: you do not need to cheat to exploit the forking paths. You only need to make each choice after glancing at the data and pick, at each fork, the path that looks a little better.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: P-hacking: torture the data until it confesses
text:
P-hacking: torture the data until it confesses
When that flexibility is turned, deliberately or not, toward chasing significance, it has a name: p-hacking (or data dredging) — running analysis after analysis until a p-value drops below 0.05, then reporting only the winners. (Bruce et al. 2020) The folk wisdom is exact: “torture the data long enough, and it will confess.” (Reinhart 2015)






NoteProving the Beatles make you younger



In a deliberately outlandish demonstration, researchers “showed” that listening to the Beatles’ When I’m Sixty-Four made participants literally younger in chronological age. They got there purely through flexible analysis — trying combinations of covariates and outcome variables until a significant result surfaced. The absurd conclusion was the point: the same freedom can manufacture a plausible-sounding effect just as easily, and you’d never know. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Optional stopping: the peeking problem
A special, seductive form of p-hacking concerns when you stop collecting data. Optional stopping means watching results arrive and halting the moment significance appears. (Reinhart 2015) It feels rational — why gather more once you’ve “found it”? — but it quietly wrecks the error rate. The \alpha=0.05 guarantee assumes a single test at a fixed sample size; peek after every participant and you take many bites at the apple, so the chance of eventually crossing p<0.05 by pure luck climbs far above 5%. (Reinhart 2015) Combine flexible analyses and flexible stopping and simulations put the false-positive rate above 50% for a single dataset — worse than a coin flip. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Multiple comparisons: the jelly-bean problem
The cleanest way to see false positives accumulate is to test many things at once. Each test at \alpha=0.05 has a 1-in-20 chance of a false alarm, so testing twenty near-guarantees one.






TipThe green jelly beans



Reinhart’s parable: test whether jelly beans cause acne and find nothing. So you test each colour — 20 of them. Nineteen show nothing; the green one hits p<0.05. Headline: “Green Jelly Beans Linked to Acne!” But you ran 20 tests; finding one false positive at the 5% level is exactly what chance predicts. (Reinhart 2015)


The arithmetic is brutal. For n independent tests, the probability of at least one false positive is

P(\text{at least one false positive}) = 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{n},

which reaches 99% by n = 100. (Reinhart 2015) And n runs into the thousands more often than you’d think — most memorably when researchers slid a dead Atlantic salmon into an fMRI scanner, showed it photographs, and ran thousands of uncorrected per-voxel tests. They found “significant” brain activity. In a dead fish. (Reinhart 2015)
The fixes adjust for the number of tests:

Bonferroni correction — divide \alpha by the number of tests (0.05/n). Simple and strict; it controls the chance of any false positive but costs power. (Reinhart 2015)
False discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg) — controls the proportion of significant results that are false alarms rather than the chance of any, retaining far more power when you expect some real effects among many tests. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Publication bias: the file drawer
Even flawless individual studies produce a broken literature if which studies get published depends on their results. Publication bias is journals’ preference for “exciting,” significant findings; the null results get filed away. (Reinhart 2015) So the published record is a biased sample of all studies run — selection bias, returned from the sampling entry to haunt science itself. If 20 labs run the same true-null study, the one that gets lucky at p<0.05 is the one the world sees; the 19 nulls vanish.






NoteWhen the file drawer was opened



When the suppressed trial data for the antidepressant reboxetine was finally unearthed, the “highly significant” benefit from the published studies evaporated: across all trials, the drug was no better than placebo. The published subset had told a confident, and false, story. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: Why most published findings can be false
text:
Why most published findings can be false
Put p-hacking, low power, and publication bias together and you get John Ioannidis’s famous claim, and it is just the base-rate fallacy wearing a lab coat. The chance that a significant finding is true — its positive predictive value (PPV) — depends on three things: the prior probability the hypothesis is true, the study’s power, and \alpha. (Reinhart 2015)






ImportantThe base-rate fallacy, in science



Suppose a field tests 100 hypotheses of which only 10 are true (a 10% base rate). With 80% power and \alpha=0.05:

True positives: 0.80 \times 10 = 8.
False positives: 0.05 \times 90 \approx 5.

So of the 13 “significant” findings, 5 — nearly 40% — are flukes. (Reinhart 2015) And that’s the optimistic case: drop power, add p-hacking, and the false share climbs past half.


This is the exact structure of the rare-disease Bayes calculation: a test can be individually reliable yet mostly wrong when the thing it screens for is rare. Significant findings are no different.

Watch the predictive value collapse
The PPV is governed by one formula, and plotting it is sobering. As the prior probability of true hypotheses falls — as a field reaches for more surprising, less likely effects — the share of “discoveries” that are real drops, fast, and low power drops it faster.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

alpha = 0.05
prior = np.linspace(0.005, 0.6, 400)

def ppv(power):
    tp = power * prior
    fp = alpha * (1 - prior)
    return tp / (tp + fp)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.8))
ax.plot(prior, ppv(0.80), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.0, label="80% power")
ax.plot(prior, ppv(0.35), color="#d98a29", lw=2.0, label="35% power (underpowered field)")
ax.axhline(0.5, color="#9aa0a6", ls=":", lw=1.2)
ax.text(0.45, 0.52, "coin flip", color="#9aa0a6", fontsize=8)

# Ioannidis's 10% base-rate example at 80% power
p0 = 0.80 * 0.10 / (0.80 * 0.10 + alpha * 0.90)
ax.scatter([0.10], [p0], color="#c0392b", zorder=5)
ax.annotate(f"10% base rate → PPV ≈ {p0:.2f}", xy=(0.10, p0), xytext=(0.16, 0.45),
            fontsize=9, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"))

ax.set_xlabel("Base rate: fraction of tested hypotheses that are true")
ax.set_ylabel("P(finding is true | significant)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title("Most 'discoveries' are false when the truth is rare")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The probability a significant finding is actually true (PPV), as a function of how often the tested hypotheses are true (the base rate), at α = 0.05. Even at 80% power, a field chasing unlikely hypotheses (low base rate) sees most of its ‘discoveries’ turn out false. Low power (35%) pushes the crossover further right. The dot marks Ioannidis’s 10%-base-rate example: PPV ≈ 0.64.





The lesson is structural, not moral: a field that prizes surprising results (low base rate) and tolerates underpowered studies (low power) will publish mostly false positives even if every individual researcher is honest. Reform has to change the system, not just scold the scientists.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
HARKing: the story told backwards
One more degree of freedom deserves its own name. HARKing — Hypothesizing After the Results are Known — is presenting a hypothesis cooked up after seeing the data as though it had been predicted in advance. (Reinhart 2015) It weaponises a deep human reflex: our talent for spinning a plausible story around any pattern that catches the eye. The fingerprint is exploratory research dressed as confirmatory — and it dissolves the entire logic of the p-value, which only means anything when the hypothesis is fixed before the data.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
The fixes: a new rigour
The encouraging half of the story is that the social sciences are responding, and the reforms map directly onto the diseases above: (Reinhart 2015; Bruce et al. 2020)

Pre-registration and registered reports — commit to the hypothesis and the full analysis plan before seeing the data; with registered reports the journal accepts the study on its design, before the results exist. This closes the garden of forking paths, p-hacking, optional stopping, and HARKing in one stroke, and defangs publication bias by deciding to publish before the result is known. (Reinhart 2015)
Replication — independent teams rerun studies; a finding earns trust by holding up, not by appearing once. (Reinhart 2015)
The ASA statement on p-values — the American Statistical Association’s six principles, formally warning that a p-value is not the probability a hypothesis is true and not a measure of an effect’s importance — the misreadings from the testing entry, now official. (Reinhart 2015)
Estimation over testing — report effect sizes and confidence intervals instead of bare significance verdicts, so readers see how big and how certain, not just whether. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Believing only fraudsters p-hack. The forking paths are walked by honest people making defensible choices. Good intentions are no protection; only pre-committed procedures are. (Reinhart 2015)
Running many tests and celebrating the one that’s significant. With 20 tests, one false positive is expected. Correct for multiplicity (Bonferroni or FDR) or pre-specify your primary outcome. (Reinhart 2015)
Trusting a literature of significant results. Publication bias means the published record overstates effects and hides the nulls. A single significant paper is weak evidence; a registered replication is strong. (Reinhart 2015)
Forgetting the base rate. A significant result for a surprising hypothesis is more likely a fluke than a discovery — exactly the Bayesian point. (Reinhart 2015)
Stopping data collection when it “looks significant.” Fix your sample size in advance (via a power analysis) or use a method designed for sequential peeking. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — researcher degrees of freedom and the garden of forking paths (Gelman & Loken); p-hacking and the Beatles example; optional stopping and the >50% false-positive simulations; the jelly-bean parable, the 1-(1-0.05)^n formula, and the dead salmon; Bonferroni; publication bias, the file drawer, and reboxetine; the PPV / Ioannidis base-rate argument; HARKing; pre-registration, replication, the ASA statement, and estimation over testing.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — p-hacking / data dredging; the false discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg) as a higher-power alternative to Bonferroni; the push toward effect sizes and intervals.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Family-wise error from multiple tests. For m independent true nulls each tested at level \alpha, the probability of no false positive is (1-\alpha)^m, so the family-wise error rate is

\text{FWER} = 1 - (1-\alpha)^m .

At \alpha = 0.05: m=10 \Rightarrow 0.40; m=100 \Rightarrow 0.99. Bonferroni tests each at \alpha/m, since 1-(1-\alpha/m)^m \le \alpha keeps the FWER at or below \alpha.
Positive predictive value. Let \pi be the prior probability a tested hypothesis is true, 1-\beta the power, \alpha the level. Among many tests,

\text{PPV} = \Pr(\text{true}\mid \text{significant})
= \frac{(1-\beta)\,\pi}{(1-\beta)\,\pi + \alpha\,(1-\pi)} .

With \pi=0.10, power 0.80, \alpha=0.05: \text{PPV} = \frac{0.08}{0.08 + 0.045} = 0.64 — so 36% of significant findings are false, matching the “5 of 13” count once the 0.05\times90 = 4.5 false positives are rounded. As \pi\to 0 or power falls, \text{PPV}\to 0. This is identically the rare-disease Bayes formula with power playing the role of sensitivity and \alpha the role of the false-positive rate.
False discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg). Order the m p-values p_{(1)}\le\dots\le p_{(m)}; find the largest k with p_{(k)} \le \frac{k}{m}q and reject H_{(1)},\dots,H_{(k)}. This bounds the expected proportion of false discoveries at q — controlling a fraction rather than a probability, which is why it keeps more power than Bonferroni.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing & p-values (every abuse here is a p-value misused), statistical power (underpowered studies feed truth inflation and low PPV), confidence intervals (estimation as the reform), and the base-rate fallacy (the skeleton of the PPV argument).
Leads to: the Bayesian sub-chapter (one response to p-value abuse is to reason about hypotheses’ probabilities directly), and to the tyranny of metrics in Part VI (what happens when “significant result” becomes a target to be gamed).
Closes: the Classical Toolkit. You now have the whole frequentist apparatus and a clear-eyed view of how it fails — the prerequisite for using it responsibly.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text: Every claim about a population rests on a sample, and every sample rests on how it was drawn. Get the drawing right — give every unit a known, non-zero chance of selection — and a few hundred people can speak for millions. Get it wrong, and ten million ballots can point to the loser.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
Why it matters
The Central Limit Theorem told you that the average of a sample behaves predictably — if the sample is a fair draw from the population. That “if” is the whole of survey design. Inference is a machine for turning a sample into a claim about a population, and like any machine it obeys garbage-in, garbage-out: no amount of clever statistics downstream can repair a sample that was biased the moment it was selected.
This is the least mathematical chapter in Part II and the one practitioners most often skip — to their cost. The famous disasters of empirical social science are almost never failures of arithmetic. They are failures of sampling: the right calculation performed on the wrong people. So before estimators, confidence intervals, and tests, we ask the prior question: who is in your data, and how did they get there?

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: The core trio: population, sample, frame
text:
The core trio: population, sample, frame
Three ideas, constantly confused, must be kept apart. (Bryman 2016)

The population is the universe of units you want to draw conclusions about. “Units” need not be people — they can be nations, cities, firms, hospital records, even suicide notes. The population is defined by your research question, not by convenience.
The sample is the subset you actually study.
The sampling frame is the concrete list of population units from which you draw — the registrar’s roster, the phone book, the voter file. The frame is the population as the world actually hands it to you, and the gap between frame and population is where much trouble lives.







NoteA worked picture



Your population is all 10,000 students at a university. Your sampling frame is the registrar’s digital list of enrolled students. You randomly select 450 names from that list — those 450 people are your sample. If the registrar’s list silently omits part-time students, your frame no longer matches your population, and every downstream number inherits that flaw. (Bryman 2016)

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: Probability sampling: the only road to inference
text:
Probability sampling: the only road to inference
The dividing line in all of survey design is whether selection is random. In probability sampling, every unit has a known, non-zero probability of being selected. (Bryman 2016) This is not a stylistic preference — it is the precondition that makes the rest of the book legal. Only when selection probabilities are known can you compute a standard error and attach a margin of error to your estimate. (Bruce et al. 2020) Randomness is what lets a sample be, in Bryman’s phrase, a “population in miniature.”
There are four workhorses, in rising order of sophistication.
Simple random sampling (SRS). Every unit has an equal chance; assign the frame numbers 1 to N and draw n of them with a random number generator. It is the conceptual baseline — mechanical, unbiased, assumption-free. Its cost is practical: you need a complete, enumerated list of the population, which for dispersed or hidden populations may not exist. (Bryman 2016)
Systematic sampling. Order the frame and take every k-th unit after a random start. For a frame of N = 9{,}000 and a desired n = 450, the interval is k = 20; pick a random start between 1 and 20 — say 16 — then take units 16, 36, 56, \dots It is easier to execute than SRS and needs no random draw per unit. The one hazard: if the list has a hidden periodicity that lines up with your interval (every 20th apartment is a corner unit; the roster alternates male/female), the regularity quietly biases the sample. (Bryman 2016)
Stratified random sampling. Divide the population into strata — subgroups defined by some characteristic (faculty, gender, region) — and sample randomly within each. With proportional allocation you draw from each stratum in proportion to its size: if Humanities is 20% of a university and you want n = 450, you draw exactly 90 Humanities students. (Bryman 2016) The payoff is real and worth understanding: stratifying guarantees the subgroups are represented in the right proportions instead of leaving it to luck, and in doing so it removes the between-stratum variation from your estimate’s error. A stratified estimate is never less precise than SRS, and is often dramatically more precise — we will see exactly how much below. The price is information: you must know each unit’s stratum before you sample. (Bryman 2016) Proportional allocation is not the only option: Neyman (optimal) allocation sharpens the estimate further by sampling more heavily from strata that are larger or more internally variable (n_h\propto N_h\sigma_h) — covered, with the rest of the design-based estimation machinery, in survey weights & complex-survey estimation. (Bui 2026)
Cluster and multistage sampling. Sample whole clusters — naturally occurring groupings — rather than individuals. For a national student survey you might randomly select 10 universities (clusters), then randomly sample 500 students within each. (Bryman 2016) Clustering exists for one reason: cost. Interviewing 5,000 students at 10 sites is enormously cheaper than chasing 5,000 students scattered across 200 campuses. But there is no free lunch: units within a cluster tend to resemble each other (students at one university share a catchment, a culture, a curriculum), so each new unit carries less fresh information than an independent draw would. The result is larger standard errors for a given n — the so-called design effect. (Bruce et al. 2020)









Method
Core idea
Buys you
Costs you




Simple random
Equal chance for all
Cleanest baseline; no assumptions
A complete enumerated frame


Systematic
Every k-th after random start
Easy execution
Fails on hidden periodicity


Stratified
Random within subgroups
Guaranteed representation; lower variance
Must know strata in advance


Cluster / multistage
Sample groups, then units
Big cost savings on dispersed populations
Higher variance (design effect)




See the gain from stratifying
The claim that stratification reduces the variance of your estimate is not hand-waving — it is visible. Below, the same population is sampled two ways for the same total n = 100. The population has three strata with quite different means (think income across three regions). Simple random sampling lets the number you happen to draw from each region vary by luck, and that luck sloshes around in your estimate. Proportional stratification nails the regional mix down, so only the small within-region noise remains.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)

# Three strata: weights, means, and a common within-stratum SD.
weights = np.array([0.5, 0.3, 0.2])
mus     = np.array([40.0, 60.0, 80.0])
sd      = 5.0
true_mean = float(weights @ mus)          # 0.5*40 + 0.3*60 + 0.2*80 = 54

n_total = 100
n_reps  = 30_000

# --- Simple random sampling: stratum membership is itself random ---
choice = rng.choice(3, size=(n_reps, n_total), p=weights)
srs_vals = rng.normal(mus[choice], sd)
srs_means = srs_vals.mean(axis=1)

# --- Stratified proportional allocation: fixed 50 / 30 / 20 ---
a = rng.normal(mus[0], sd, size=(n_reps, 50))
b = rng.normal(mus[1], sd, size=(n_reps, 30))
c = rng.normal(mus[2], sd, size=(n_reps, 20))
strat_means = (a.sum(1) + b.sum(1) + c.sum(1)) / n_total

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.6))
bins = np.linspace(48, 60, 70)
ax.hist(srs_means,   bins=bins, density=True, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.75,
        label=f"Simple random  (SE ≈ {srs_means.std():.2f})")
ax.hist(strat_means, bins=bins, density=True, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85,
        label=f"Stratified      (SE ≈ {strat_means.std():.2f})")
ax.axvline(true_mean, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.8, label="True mean = 54")
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("Estimated population mean")
ax.set_title("Same n, same population — stratifying sharpens the estimate")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two ways to estimate one population mean (true value 54, dashed line) with n = 100. Simple random sampling (grey) scatters widely because the stratum mix varies by chance; proportional stratified sampling (blue) is over three times tighter because it removes the between-stratum variation.





Both estimators are unbiased — both clouds centre on the truth, 54. But the stratified estimator is roughly three times tighter. That difference is free: it costs nothing but knowing each unit’s region before you draw. This is the single most useful design lesson in sampling — when you have strata that differ, use them.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
Non-probability sampling: convenient, and uninferable
The opposite of probability sampling is selection by judgement or availability, where some units have no chance of selection and the probabilities are unknown. The common forms: (Bryman 2016)

Convenience sampling — whoever is easiest to reach (the undergraduates in your 9 a.m. lecture, the shoppers who walk past).
Snowball sampling — start with a few relevant people and ask them to nominate others; useful for hidden populations (undocumented workers, niche subcultures) where no frame exists.
Quota sampling — fill pre-set category targets non-randomly (e.g., “5 Asian women aged 25–34”) until each quota is met.

These designs have legitimate uses — exploratory work, qualitative depth, hard-to-frame populations. But they share a disqualifying flaw for inference: because the probability of selection is unknown, you cannot compute a standard error, and without a standard error you cannot say how far your sample statistic might be from the population truth. (Bruce et al. 2020) A convenience sample can describe itself perfectly and tell you nothing certain about anyone else. Generalisation requires randomness; judgement cannot substitute for it.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: Representativeness, and the two kinds of error
text:
Representativeness, and the two kinds of error
A sample is representative when it mirrors the population — the “population in miniature.” The engine of representativeness is random selection, which has no opinion about whom it picks and so introduces no systematic tilt. (Bryman 2016) When a sample is unrepresentative, the findings simply do not generalise beyond the people in it.
It helps enormously to split a survey’s total error into two species, because they have different cures. (Bruce et al. 2020)

Sampling error is the unavoidable discrepancy between a sample and its population that arises purely because you looked at a subset. It is the bounce the CLT describes; it exists even in a flawless random sample, and it shrinks predictably as n grows. This is the “honest” error — quantifiable, and the thing confidence intervals measure.
Non-sampling error is everything else: bad question wording, interviewer effects, data-entry mistakes, coverage gaps, non-response. It does not shrink with n — a bigger biased sample is just a more confidently wrong one — and it is invisible to your standard error. This is the dangerous error, because the statistics give you no warning of it.

The whole moral of this chapter lives in that contrast: statistics manages sampling error; only design manages non-sampling error.

The three biases that ruin samples
Non-sampling error usually arrives as one of three biases — systematic, directional errors baked in by the selection or measurement process. (Bruce et al. 2020)

Selection bias — the sample is selected in a way related to the thing you are measuring. In Gerber & Green’s framing, the danger of non-random selection is that receiving the treatment is correlated with the outcome: healthier people self-select into exercise programs, so a naïve comparison credits the program with their pre-existing health. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Coverage / frame bias — the frame fails to list part of the target population, so those people had zero chance from the start (the part-time students missing from the registrar’s list). (Bruce et al. 2020)
Non-response bias — selected units who decline or can’t be reached differ systematically from those who respond. If wealthier households are likelier to refuse a survey about income, the recorded average is biased downward — and collecting more responses from the same cooperative group does not fix it. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: Sample size: why a thousand can speak for millions
text:
Sample size: why a thousand can speak for millions
Here is the result that surprises every newcomer and underlies the entire polling industry: the precision of an estimate depends on the absolute sample size n, not on the population size N. (Bruce et al. 2020) A well-drawn random sample of about 1,000 people pins down a national proportion to roughly a \pm 3\% margin — and that margin is the same whether the country has 100,000 people or 100 million.
The intuition: each random draw is an independent measurement of the population, and the information you accumulate is governed by how many measurements you take, not by how large the thing you’re measuring is. (The population size matters only when your sample is a sizeable fraction of it — the finite-population correction in the appendix — which for national surveys it essentially never is.) The same draw also reveals the law of diminishing returns: because sampling error falls like 1/\sqrt{n}, each additional thousand respondents buys less precision than the last. Doubling a poll from 1,000 to 2,000 does not halve the margin of error; it shrinks it by only about 30%. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: A cautionary tale: the Literary Digest, 1936
text:
A cautionary tale: the Literary Digest, 1936
No story makes the point more bluntly. In 1936 the magazine Literary Digest mailed straw-poll ballots to ten million people, drawn from telephone directories and automobile registration lists, and got over two million back. On that mountain of data it confidently predicted a landslide for Alf Landon. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Franklin Roosevelt won 46 states.
The frames — phone and car owners in 1936 — skewed sharply toward the affluent, who favoured Landon; that was coverage bias. And the people motivated to mail back an unsolicited ballot differed from those who didn’t; that was non-response bias. Ten million biased responses are still biased. Meanwhile George Gallup, using a scientifically representative sample of around 2,000, called the election correctly. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The lesson is the founding creed of survey design, and it directly contradicts the instinct of the big-data age: how you sample beats how much you sample. A small representative sample crushes a huge biased one.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Believing a bigger sample fixes bias. Size only shrinks sampling error. Bias — selection, coverage, non-response — is untouched by n and is the actual cause of the famous failures. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Confusing the frame with the population. Your inference is only ever valid for the frame you drew from. If the frame omits a group, your conclusions silently omit them too — and no statistic will flag it. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Reporting a margin of error for a non-probability sample. Convenience, quota, and snowball samples have no defined selection probabilities, so a “±3%” attached to them is fiction. The margin of error is a property of random sampling. (Bruce et al. 2020)
