title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text:
The bridge to causality
Mastering ’Metrics frames regression as a way of making “other things equal.” That framing is the hinge between description and Causal Inference: a coefficient is only an effect if you have an argument for why the comparison is fair.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text:
Where this leads
Onward to Part IV · Predictive Modelling — the computational turn, where the goal shifts from explaining to predicting, and the same regression machinery is judged by a completely different standard.

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text: Half the outcomes social scientists care about are binary: employed or not, approved or denied, voted or stayed home. You can run OLS on a 0/1 variable — and sometimes should — but a line that keeps climbing will eventually predict probabilities above 1 and below 0. Logit and probit fix that by fitting an S-shaped curve trapped between 0 and 1, at the cost of coefficients you must work harder to interpret.
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text:
Why it matters
A great many dependent variables are limited — they can only take certain values, the simplest case being binary. (Wooldridge 2019) Labour-force participation, loan approval, turnout, purchase. The regression machinery you’ve built still applies, but the binary outcome forces three choices — the easy-but-flawed linear model, or the bounded-but-nonlinear logit/probit — and a new interpretive discipline (the coefficients aren’t what you think). This is also the gateway to the classification models of Part IV: logistic regression is the workhorse of predictive modelling, and it’s born here.
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text:
The linear probability model
The linear probability model (LPM) is just OLS with a 0/1 outcome. (Wooldridge 2019) Its interpretation is unusually clean: since E[y\mid x] = P(y=1\mid x) for a binary y, each coefficient is the change in the probability of “success” for a one-unit change in x_j:

P(y=1\mid x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k,
\qquad \Delta P(y=1\mid x) = \beta_j\,\Delta x_j.

That directness is the appeal — coefficients are marginal effects, no transformation needed, and it estimates in one OLS call. (Wooldridge 2019) But it has three problems: (Wooldridge 2019)

Nonsensical predictions. A line doesn’t stop at 0 or 1, so for extreme x it predicts probabilities below 0 or above 1 — impossible.
Built-in heteroskedasticity. For a binary outcome, \operatorname{Var}(y\mid x) =
P(y=1\mid x)\,[1 - P(y=1\mid x)] — the variance depends on x, so MLR.5 fails by construction and you must use robust standard errors.
Unrealistic constant effects. The LPM says x_j moves the probability by the same amount everywhere. In a labour-force model it would claim the first child and the fourth child each cut the probability of working by the same 26.2 points — intuitively wrong, since the effect should shrink as the probability nears the floor. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
section: Logit and probit: bending the line into an S
text:
Logit and probit: bending the line into an S
To respect the [0, 1] boundary, logit and probit pass the linear index through an S-shaped (sigmoid) function G: (Wooldridge 2019)

P(y=1\mid x) = G(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k), \qquad 0 < G < 1.


Logit uses the logistic CDF, G(z) = \dfrac{e^z}{1 + e^z}.
Probit uses the standard Normal CDF, G(z) = \Phi(z).

A compelling way to motivate both is the latent-variable story: imagine an unobserved propensity y^* = \beta_0 + x\beta + e (the underlying tendency to “do it”), and you observe y = 1 only when that propensity crosses a threshold, y^* > 0. (Wooldridge 2019; Moffatt 2016) The S-curve falls out, and it has exactly the right shape: steepest in the middle (near P=0.5) and flattening toward the 0 and 1 boundaries — encoding the realism the LPM lacked, that a push matters most for the undecided and barely moves the near-certain. (Wooldridge 2019)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
n = 200
x = rng.uniform(-4, 4, n)
p = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(0.2 + 1.1 * x)))     # true logistic probabilities
y = (rng.uniform(size=n) < p).astype(float)

# LPM by OLS
b1 = np.cov(x, y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(x)
b0 = y.mean() - b1 * x.mean()
xs = np.linspace(-5.5, 5.5, 200)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.0))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=14, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.5, zorder=2)
ax.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color="#c0392b", lw=2.2, label="Linear probability model")
ax.plot(xs, 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(0.2 + 1.1 * xs))), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6, label="Logit (S-curve)")
ax.axhline(0, color="#999", lw=0.8, ls=":"); ax.axhline(1, color="#999", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.fill_between(xs, 1, 1.25, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.06)
ax.fill_between(xs, -0.25, 0, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.06)
ax.set_ylim(-0.25, 1.25)
ax.set_xlabel("predictor x"); ax.set_ylabel("P(y = 1)")
ax.set_title("LPM escapes [0,1]; the logistic S-curve respects it")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, frameon=False, loc="center right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A binary outcome (dots at 0 and 1) against a predictor. The linear probability model (red) fits a straight line that escapes the [0,1] box — predicting impossible probabilities at the extremes. The logistic curve (blue) stays inside the box, steep in the middle and flat at the edges, where the LPM’s constant slope is least believable.
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text:
Odds and odds ratios (logit)
Logit has a special gift: while the probability is nonlinear in x, the log-odds is perfectly linear. (Wooldridge 2019) The odds are the ratio of success to failure, and their log is the linear index:

\frac{P(y=1)}{P(y=0)} = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots}, \qquad
\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k.

So e^{\beta_j} is the odds ratio — the multiplicative factor by which the odds change per one-unit rise in x_j. (Wooldridge 2019) If e^{\beta_j} = 2.57, a one-unit increase multiplies the odds by 2.57 — a 157% rise in the odds. (Odds, note, not probability — a distinction that trips up many readers.)
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
section: Marginal effects: the coefficient isn’t the effect
text:
Marginal effects: the coefficient isn’t the effect
The crucial interpretive trap: in logit and probit, the coefficients are not the marginal effects on probability. (Wooldridge 2019) They give the sign and significance, but the actual change in probability depends on where you sit on the S-curve — big in the steep middle, tiny in the flat tails. The partial effect of a continuous x_j is g(\beta_0 + x\beta)\,\beta_j, where g is the density (the slope of the S). (Wooldridge 2019) Two standard summaries: (Wooldridge 2019)

Marginal effect at the mean (MEM): evaluate the formula at the average person, \bar x.
Average marginal effect (AME): compute each person’s marginal effect and average them — usually preferred, since it reflects the whole sample’s diversity rather than a possibly-atypical “average” person.

This is why logit/probit output is reported as marginal effects, not raw coefficients.
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text:
Estimation: maximum likelihood, not OLS
Because logit and probit are nonlinear in the parameters, OLS doesn’t apply; they are fit by maximum likelihood instead. (Wooldridge 2019) MLE chooses the parameters that make the observed pattern of 0s and 1s most probable under the assumed (logistic or Normal) model — the same “what parameter best explains my data?” logic from estimation, now doing the heavy lifting. It yields estimates that are consistent (homing in on the truth as n grows), asymptotically Normal (their sampling distribution approaches a Normal in large samples, by the CLT — so the usual t- and z-style inference applies), and efficient — and it automatically handles the binary heteroskedasticity. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
section: Which to use, and how they compare
text:
Which to use, and how they compare
In practice all three usually tell the same story about signs and significance. (Wooldridge 2019) Guidance:

LPM is a fine starting point and quick approximation, especially when fitted probabilities sit near 0.5 (away from the boundaries). (Wooldridge 2019)
Logit/probit when probabilities approach 0 or 1, or when you need predictions guaranteed inside [0, 1]. (Wooldridge 2019)
Rough conversion: multiply probit coefficients by ~1.6 to compare with logit; divide logit by ~4 (probit by ~2.5) to compare with LPM marginal effects. (Wooldridge 2019)







NoteMarried women’s labour-force participation (Mroz, n = 753)



The marginal effect of a year of education on the probability of working is remarkably stable across methods: LPM 0.038, logit ≈0.040, probit ≈0.039 — about a 3.8–4.0 percentage-point increase per year. Where they diverge is at the extremes: a child under six lowers the probability of working by 26.2 points in the LPM but 33.4 points for an average woman under probit — the nonlinear “penalty” the S-curve captures and the straight line flattens. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading logit/probit coefficients as probability effects. They aren’t — compute marginal effects (AME preferred). Only LPM coefficients are direct probability changes. (Wooldridge 2019)
Confusing odds ratios with probability changes. e^{\beta}=2.57 means the odds multiply by 2.57, not the probability. At p=0.5 that’s a big move; near p=0.95 it’s small. (Wooldridge 2019)
Forgetting robust SEs in the LPM. Binary outcomes are inherently heteroskedastic (p(1-p)), so the LPM always needs robust standard errors. (Wooldridge 2019)
Over-trusting LPM predictions near the boundary. When fitted probabilities approach 0 or 1, the LPM can predict impossibilities; switch to logit/probit. (Wooldridge 2019)
Treating a binary-outcome coefficient as causal. Everything from the causal entry still applies — a precise logit coefficient on an endogenous regressor estimates a biased effect.
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the LPM, its probability interpretation, and its three problems (out-of-range predictions, inherent heteroskedasticity p(1-p), constant effects — the 26.2-point child example); logit (logistic CDF) and probit (Normal CDF) and the bounded S-curve; odds, log-odds, and the odds ratio e^{\beta}; marginal effects (MEM vs AME) and why coefficients aren’t effects; MLE; LPM-vs-logit-vs-probit comparison and conversion factors; the Mroz labour-force example (n=753; education ≈3.8–4.0 points; child-under-6 −26.2 LPM vs −33.4 probit).
Moffatt, Experimetrics — the latent-variable / index-function formulation y^*=x\beta+e, y=\mathbf 1\{y^*>0\}, for binary choice.
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

LPM heteroskedasticity. For y\in\{0,1\} with p=P(y=1\mid x), y is Bernoulli, so \operatorname{Var}(y\mid x)=p(1-p)=(\beta_0+x\beta)(1-\beta_0-x\beta) — a function of x. Hence the homoskedasticity assumption fails identically, and robust SEs are mandatory.
Latent variable → S-curve. With y^*=x\beta+e and y=\mathbf 1\{y^*>0\}, P(y=1\mid x)=P(e>-x\beta)=1-F(-x\beta)=F(x\beta) for a symmetric error CDF F. Take F=\Phi for probit (Normal e) or the logistic CDF for logit. The threshold-crossing is the S-curve.
Marginal effect. Differentiate P(y=1\mid x)=G(x\beta): \partial P/\partial x_j = g(x\beta)\,\beta_j, where g=G' is the density. Because g peaks at the centre and vanishes in the tails, the same \beta_j produces a large probability effect near P=0.5 and a negligible one near 0 or 1.
Log-likelihood (logit). \ell(\beta)=\sum_i\big[y_i\log G(x_i\beta)+(1-y_i)\log(1-G(x_i\beta))\big]; maximising it (no closed form — solved numerically) gives the MLE, the binary-data analogue of the coin-bias MLE.
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit
text:
Connections

Builds on: multiple regression (the linear index inside G), the common distributions (the logistic and Normal CDFs), maximum likelihood (the estimator), and diagnostics (the built-in heteroskedasticity).
Leads to: logistic regression as classification in Part IV — the same model, judged by out-of-sample prediction rather than coefficient interpretation.
Inherits: every caveat of regression and causality — a binary outcome doesn’t make a coefficient causal.
crumbs: Limited Dependent Variables: LPM, Logit & Probit

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text: You can’t randomize the world. Richer districts have both smaller classes and better labs, so a raw comparison of class sizes confounds the two. Multiple regression is how observational researchers approximate “all else equal” — adding control variables to hold confounders constant and isolate the one relationship they care about. It is the workhorse, and its central idea is simpler than its reputation.

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
Why it matters
The simple regression slope folds every other influence into the error term u — and if any of those influences correlates with your x, the slope is contaminated. (Wooldridge 2019) Multiple regression pulls the big confounders out of u and into the model, so each coefficient can be read ceteris paribus — holding the others fixed. This is the move that lets you ask “the effect of education at the same level of ability,” or “spending at the same level of poverty.” (Angrist and Pischke 2015) Master it and most applied empirical work becomes legible; misunderstand it and you will read confounded slopes as effects.

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
section: The model and the ceteris paribus reading
text:
The model and the ceteris paribus reading
The multiple linear regression model adds regressors: (Wooldridge 2019)

y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k + u.

The whole payoff is in the interpretation of each slope:

\beta_j is the change in y for a one-unit increase in x_j, holding all the other regressors fixed. (Wooldridge 2019)

That “holding fixed” is the ceteris paribus clause, and it is why regression matters in the social sciences. (Wooldridge 2019) You cannot literally freeze the world to test class size, because the schools with small classes differ in a dozen other ways. (Angrist and Pischke 2015) But you can statistically compare observations identical on every included dimension — schools with the same budget, students with the same prior scores — and that comparison is what each coefficient reports. This is how researchers make “other things equal” claims from data that was never an experiment. (Angrist and Pischke 2015)

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
How regression “holds constant”: partialling out
What does the machinery actually do to hold things fixed? The Frisch–Waugh–Lovell result — “regression anatomy” — gives the exact, beautiful answer. Any multiple-regression coefficient \hat\beta_1 can be reconstructed in three steps: (Wooldridge 2019; Angrist and Pischke 2015)

Cleanse the regressor. Regress x_1 on all the other controls and keep the residuals \tilde x_1 — the part of x_1 that the controls can’t explain.
Cleanse the outcome. Regress y on the same controls and keep its residuals \tilde y.
Link the leftovers. Regress \tilde y on \tilde x_1. That slope is exactly \hat\beta_1.

The lesson is profound: a multiple-regression coefficient uses only the variation in x_1 that is orthogonal to the other controls. (Wooldridge 2019) It throws away any part of x_1 that moves together with the controls — that’s precisely what “holding them constant” means.

See partialling-out at work
Below, school spending and test scores both depend on a confounder (district poverty): poorer districts spend less and score lower. The naive scatter (left) shows a steep slope — but much of it is poverty masquerading as a spending effect. The partial-regression plot (right), after residualising both variables on poverty, reveals the flatter true slope — spending’s effect among districts with the same poverty level.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(8)
n = 200
poverty = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)                       # the confounder Z
spend   = 8 - 3 * poverty + rng.normal(0, 0.6, n)    # poorer districts spend less
true_beta = 2.0
scores  = 40 + true_beta * spend - 25 * poverty + rng.normal(0, 2.5, n)

def ols_slope(x, y):
    return np.cov(x, y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(x)
def resid_on(z, v):                                  # residual of v after regressing on z
    b = ols_slope(z, v); a = v.mean() - b * z.mean()
    return v - (a + b * z)

naive = ols_slope(spend, scores)
sx, sy = resid_on(poverty, spend), resid_on(poverty, scores)
partial = ols_slope(sx, sy)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 4.0))
axL.scatter(spend, scores, s=14, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6)
xs = np.linspace(spend.min(), spend.max(), 40)
axL.plot(xs, scores.mean() + naive * (xs - spend.mean()), color="#c0392b", lw=2.4)
axL.set_title(f"Raw: slope ≈ {naive:.1f}  (confounded)")
axL.set_xlabel("school spending"); axL.set_ylabel("test score")

axR.scatter(sx, sy, s=14, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.6)
xr = np.linspace(sx.min(), sx.max(), 40)
axR.plot(xr, sy.mean() + partial * (xr - sx.mean()), color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4)
axR.set_title(f"Poverty held constant: slope ≈ {partial:.1f}  (true ≈ {true_beta})")
axR.set_xlabel("spending (poverty partialled out)")
axR.set_ylabel("score (poverty partialled out)")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Holding a confounder constant. Left: the raw slope of scores on spending is steep — but poorer districts both spend less and score lower, inflating it. Right: after partialling poverty out of both variables (Frisch–Waugh–Lovell), the true slope is much flatter. This is exactly what the multiple-regression coefficient computes — mirroring Wooldridge’s MEAP93 drop from 11.16 to 7.75.

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
Adjustment: accounting for variation
There is a second, complementary way to say what “holding constant” does — Daniel Kaplan’s framing in Lessons in Statistical Thinking, and a useful one because it carries straight into the causal chapters. Kaplan calls this move adjustment, and sets it inside a bigger idea: accounting for variation. (Kaplan 2024) Variation is the raw material of statistics — “without variation there would be no need for statistical thinking” — and a model’s job is to split it into a signal (the part a model function explains) and noise (the leftover). (Kaplan 2024)
In those terms, a confounder is variation in the wrong column. District poverty makes spending and scores move together; left in the error term, that shared variation gets misread as a spending effect. To adjust for poverty is to move its variation out of the noise and into the explained part of the model — so the spending coefficient is computed only from the variation poverty can’t account for. This is the same residual-variation story as partialling out above, and it is exactly why adding a relevant covariate raises R^2 (just below): R^2 is the fraction of variation the model now accounts for. (Kaplan 2024)
Hold onto the word adjustment: it is the hinge of the DAG chapter. Adjusting for the right covariate blocks a confounding path (good control); adjusting for the wrong one — a mediator or a collider — opens a spurious one (the bad controls below). “Accounting for variation” and “closing a backdoor” are one act seen from two sides. (Kaplan 2024)

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
Omitted-variable bias
The flip side of “holding constant” is what happens when you fail to. Leave out a variable that (a) affects y and (b) correlates with an included regressor, and its influence gets forced into that regressor’s coefficient — omitted-variable bias. (Wooldridge 2019) The formula is worth memorising because it also tells you the direction: (Angrist and Pischke 2015)

\underbrace{\beta_{\text{short}}}_{\text{omit the control}} =
\underbrace{\beta_{\text{long}}}_{\text{true effect}} + \;\pi\,\gamma,

where \gamma is the omitted variable’s effect on y (in the full model) and \pi is the slope from regressing the omitted variable on the included one. So:

\text{sign of bias} = \text{sign}(\gamma)\times\text{sign}(\pi).







NoteAbility bias in the returns to schooling



Regress wage on education but omit ability. Ability raises wages (\gamma > 0) and is positively correlated with education (\pi > 0). So the bias is (+)\times(+) = (+): the simple-regression education coefficient is biased upward — it overstates the true return to schooling, because some of what looks like a payoff to education is really a payoff to the ability that tends to come with it. (Angrist and Pischke 2015) This single example is why “the return to education” was argued about for decades.

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
Good controls, bad controls
Adding a control changes another coefficient whenever the new variable correlates with both that regressor and the outcome. (Angrist and Pischke 2015) But not all controls are good:

Good controls are predetermined — fixed before the treatment (demographics, pre-treatment test scores, baseline characteristics). They remove confounding and, as a bonus, soak up error variance and sharpen your estimate. (Angrist and Pischke 2015)
Bad controls are themselves outcomes of the treatment. In a wage regression, controlling for occupation is a trap: education helps determine your occupation, so holding occupation fixed closes off one of the very channels through which education raises wages — you’ve thrown away part of the effect you wanted to measure. (Angrist and Pischke 2015) “Control for everything” is not the rule; control for the right things.

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
section: A fully worked example: school spending and test scores
text:
A fully worked example: school spending and test scores
Wooldridge’s MEAP93 data, 408 Michigan high schools, math pass rate on spending. (Wooldridge 2019) First the simple regression:

\widehat{\text{math10}} = -69.34 + 11.16\,\log(\text{expend}).

Taken at face value, a 10% spending increase buys about a 1.12-point rise in pass rates. But high-spending schools sit in wealthier districts — classic omitted-variable territory. Add poverty (free-lunch eligibility, lnchprg) and enrollment: (Wooldridge 2019)

\widehat{\text{math10}} = -23.14 + 7.75\,\log(\text{expend}) - 0.324\,\text{lnchprg} - 1.26\,\log(\text{enroll}).

Holding poverty and enrollment constant, the spending effect drops from 11.16 to 7.75. The OVB logic explains the direction exactly: poverty is negatively correlated with spending (\pi < 0) and negatively affects scores (\gamma < 0), and (-)\times(-) = (+) — so the simple regression was biased upward. Money mattered less than the naive number implied, once you compared schools at the same poverty level. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
section: Goodness of fit with many regressors: adjusted R²
text:
Goodness of fit with many regressors: adjusted R²
A trap waits in the R^2 once you have several regressors: adding any regressor — even a useless, random one — never lowers R^2, and almost always raises it. (Wooldridge 2019) OLS can always exploit a new variable’s chance correlation with the residuals to nudge the fit up, so R^2 rewards kitchen-sink models and is useless for deciding whether a variable belongs. The adjusted R^2 (\bar R^2) fixes this by penalising extra regressors, dividing each sum of squares by its degrees of freedom: (Wooldridge 2019)

\bar R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}/(n-k-1)}{\text{SST}/(n-1)} = 1 - (1 - R^2)\,\frac{n-1}{n-k-1}.

Adding a regressor lowers SSR (good for \bar R^2) but also spends a degree of freedom (bad). So \bar R^2 rises only if the new variable improves the fit by more than chance would predict — it can and does fall when you add junk. This makes \bar R^2 a rough tool for comparing models with different numbers of regressors, where raw R^2 would always favour the bigger one. Two cautions: a higher \bar R^2 is not a licence to believe a model is correctly specified or causal (the whole next several entries), and it should only be compared across models with the same dependent variable. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




“Control for everything.” Bad controls (post-treatment variables) introduce bias rather than remove it. Adding regressors is not automatically safer — it can close the channel you’re studying. (Angrist and Pischke 2015)
Reading a controlled coefficient as definitively causal. Holding observed confounders fixed does nothing about unobserved ones (ability is still in u). Selection-on-observables is an assumption, not a guarantee — the causal entry presses this. (Wooldridge 2019)
Forgetting OVB has a direction. You can often sign the bias before collecting more data (\text{sign}(\gamma)\,\text{sign}(\pi)) — a powerful sanity check on whether your estimate is too high or too low. (Angrist and Pischke 2015)
Confusing “holding constant” with “the data has constant values.” Partialling-out uses only the residual variation; if a regressor barely varies once controls are in, its coefficient is imprecise — the multicollinearity issue of diagnostics.
Interpreting each coefficient in isolation from its set. A coefficient’s meaning is “holding these specific controls fixed.” Change the controls and you change what the number means.

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the multiple-regression model and the ceteris-paribus interpretation; partialling-out / Frisch–Waugh–Lovell; the omitted-variable-bias formula and direction-of-bias reasoning; the MEAP93 school-spending example (simple 11.16 → multiple 7.75 after adding poverty and enrollment, n=408).
Angrist & Pischke, Mastering ’Metrics / Mostly Harmless — “other things equal” as the engine of observational social science; regression anatomy; ability bias in the returns to schooling; the good-controls vs bad-controls distinction (occupation as a bad control for education).
Kaplan, Lessons in Statistical Thinking — “holding constant” recast as adjustment inside the broader frame of accounting for variation: a model splits variation into signal (the model function) and noise, a confounder is shared variation misread as an effect, and adjusting for it moves that variation into the explained part — which is what R^2 measures. The word adjustment is the through-line to the DAG chapter’s good-vs-bad controls.

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The partialling-out formula. For y = \beta_0+\beta_1 x_1+\beta_2 x_2+u, the FWL theorem gives

\hat\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_i \tilde x_{1i}\,y_i}{\sum_i \tilde x_{1i}^2},

where \tilde x_{1i} are the residuals from regressing x_1 on the other regressors. Only the variation in x_1 orthogonal to the controls enters — the algebraic meaning of “holding the others fixed.”
Omitted-variable bias, derived. True model y=\beta_0+\beta_1x_1+\beta_2x_2+u; you instead run the short regression of y on x_1 alone. Then

E[\tilde\beta_1] = \beta_1 + \beta_2\,\delta_1,
\qquad \delta_1 = \frac{\widehat{\operatorname{Cov}}(x_1,x_2)}{\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(x_1)},

with \delta_1 the slope of x_2 on x_1. In the notation above \gamma=\beta_2, \pi=\delta_1, so \text{bias}=\pi\gamma and its sign is \text{sign}(\beta_2)\,\text{sign}(\delta_1).
Bad controls, sketched. If x_2 is itself caused by x_1 (post-treatment), then conditioning on x_2 blocks the indirect path x_1\to x_2\to y, so \hat\beta_1 no longer captures the total effect of x_1 — and can even pick up collider bias. Predetermined controls don’t have this problem because x_1 cannot have caused them.

title: Multiple Regression & “Holding Constant”
text:
Connections

Builds on: simple linear regression (one regressor → many) and conditional expectation (each coefficient is a slice of the CEF).
Leads to: the Gauss–Markov assumptions (what makes these coefficients unbiased), inference (their standard errors and tests), and — decisively — what regression can and can’t tell you (OVB and bad controls revisited as the threats to a causal reading).
Underwrites: the control strategy (“selection on observables”) in the causal summit, and its limits.

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text: You can now fit, interpret, and stress-test a regression. The last and hardest question is the one that matters most: when is a coefficient a cause, not just a correlation with controls? The honest answer is humbling — almost never automatically. Regression estimates a conditional average; turning that into a causal effect takes an assumption that data alone can’t verify, and usually a research design. This entry is the bridge to the summit.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Why it matters
It is dangerously easy to run a regression, add some controls, and announce “the effect of X on Y.” But holding things constant only neutralises the confounders you observed and included. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The unobserved ones — ability, motivation, health, a thousand others — sit in the error term, and if they correlate with your regressor, the coefficient is biased no matter how clean the output looks. This entry assembles the threats into one clear-eyed account of regression’s causal limits, and explains why credible causal claims need more than a longer control list — they need the methods of Part V.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
The one condition: exogeneity
Strip everything away and a single condition decides whether a slope is causal: the regressor must be exogenous, E[u\mid x]=0 — equivalently, treatment is as good as randomly assigned once you condition on the controls (the conditional independence or selection-on-observables assumption). (Angrist and Pischke 2009; Wooldridge 2019) When it holds, the regression line is the causal conditional expectation function. When it fails, regression still faithfully estimates the best linear approximation to the CEF — but the CEF is descriptive, not causal. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Regression is an extraordinary describing machine; whether its description is also an explanation is a question about the world, not the math.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Threat 1 — omitted variables (the central one)
The dominant threat, revisited from multiple regression: leave out a confounder and its influence loads onto your coefficient. The logic is the “short = long + bias” identity — the coefficient you get omitting a control equals the true effect plus (\text{effect of omitted on }y)\times(\text{its relation to the included regressor}). (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The classic case is ability bias: regress wages on education without ability, and since ability raises wages and correlates with schooling, the education coefficient is biased upward. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
The uncomfortable truth: you can never be certain you’ve controlled for everything. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) There is no test that confirms exogeneity; the unobserved confounder is, by definition, unobserved. This is why “I controlled for a lot of stuff” is an argument, not a proof — and why more controls buy credibility, never certainty.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
section: Threat 2 — bad controls (when a control hurts)
text:
Threat 2 — bad controls (when a control hurts)
The instinct “control for everything” is wrong, and dangerously so. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) A bad control is a variable that is itself an outcome of the treatment — a post-treatment variable. Conditioning on it doesn’t remove confounding; it introduces bias. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)






NoteOccupation, the textbook bad control



Study the effect of education on wages, and it’s tempting to control for occupation. Don’t: education causes occupation (more schooling opens better jobs), so holding occupation fixed closes off one of the very channels through which education raises wages. You’d be measuring only the within-occupation effect and throwing away the part that flows through getting a better job — biasing the total effect, usually toward zero. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The rule: good controls are predetermined (fixed before treatment); post-treatment variables are bad controls.



Collider bias: how conditioning creates correlation
The subtlest version: conditioning on a common consequence of two variables manufactures an association that isn’t there. This is collider (or selection) bias. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The picture below is the canonical example: talent and beauty are uncorrelated in the population, but among people selected on their sum (say, working actors, hired for one or the other), they appear negatively correlated — condition on the collider and a spurious relationship springs into being.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
n = 2000
talent = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
beauty = rng.normal(0, 1, n)          # independent of talent in the population
hired = (talent + beauty) > 1.6       # selected on a common consequence (a collider)

r_all = np.corrcoef(talent, beauty)[0, 1]
r_sel = np.corrcoef(talent[hired], beauty[hired])[0, 1]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.4))
ax.scatter(talent[~hired], beauty[~hired], s=8, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.5,
           label=f"everyone (r = {r_all:+.2f})")
ax.scatter(talent[hired], beauty[hired], s=16, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.8,
           label=f"hired (selected) (r = {r_sel:+.2f})")
# trend line within the selected group
b = np.cov(talent[hired], beauty[hired], bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(talent[hired])
xs = np.linspace(talent[hired].min(), talent[hired].max(), 30)
ax.plot(xs, beauty[hired].mean() + b * (xs - talent[hired].mean()),
        color="#c0392b", lw=2.2)
ax.set_xlabel("talent"); ax.set_ylabel("beauty")
ax.set_title("Conditioning on a collider invents a correlation")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Collider/selection bias. In the full population (grey), talent and beauty are independent — no relationship. But the ‘hired’ subsample (blue), selected because talent + beauty is high, shows a spurious negative correlation: among the selected, more of one implies less of the other. Conditioning on a common consequence manufactures an association — which is exactly what a bad (post-treatment) control can do.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Threat 3 — reverse causality
Regression coefficients are symmetric; the data don’t know which way the arrow points. Reverse causality (simultaneity) is when y also causes x. (Wooldridge 2019) More police and more crime appear together — but high-crime cities hire more police, so the naive regression confuses “police cause crime” with “crime causes police.” Likewise income and health: wealth buys health, and health enables earning. When x and y co-determine each other, OLS estimates a tangle of both directions, not the effect you want. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Threat 4 — measurement error and attenuation
If your regressor is measured with noise — self-reported income, a proxy for ability, a mismeasured treatment — the coefficient is biased, and in a predictable direction. Under classical measurement error, the bias is attenuation: the coefficient is pulled toward zero. (Wooldridge 2019) The intuition: noise in x adds variation that carries no signal about y, so the estimated slope is diluted — a real effect looks weaker than it is. Mismeasure the outcome and you mostly lose precision; mismeasure the regressor and you bias the effect downward.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
section: The verdict: you need a design, not just controls
text:
The verdict: you need a design, not just controls
Put the threats together and the lesson is stark. Adding control variables cannot, by itself, deliver a credible causal effect — it addresses observed confounders while leaving unobserved confounding, reverse causality, and measurement error untouched, and it can create bias through bad controls. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Selection-on-observables is an assumption you must argue for, not a box the software ticks.
What does deliver credibility is a research design that makes exogeneity defensible by construction: (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

Randomized experiments — random assignment forces X \perp u, the gold standard.
Instrumental variables — a lever that moves X but affects Y only through X.
Natural experiments — regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences — where nature or policy supplies as-good-as-random variation.

This is the hinge of the whole book. Regression is the engine every one of those designs runs on — but the design is what licenses the causal claim. Part V is the study of building that license.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




“I controlled for confounders, so it’s causal.” Only for the confounders you observed. Unobserved ones remain, and there’s no test for exogeneity. Controls build credibility, not proof. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Controlling for everything available. Post-treatment (bad) controls and colliders add bias. Control for predetermined variables; never for outcomes of the treatment. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Ignoring the direction of causation. If y could plausibly cause x, the coefficient blends both directions. Ask which way the arrows run before interpreting. (Wooldridge 2019)
Trusting a precise coefficient on a noisy regressor. Measurement error attenuates toward zero; a small “insignificant” effect may be a real effect, diluted. (Wooldridge 2019)
Believing a tiny standard error rescues a biased estimate. Inference quantifies sampling noise, not specification error. A precise estimate of the wrong number is still the wrong number. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Angrist & Pischke, Mastering ’Metrics / Mostly Harmless Econometrics — a coefficient is causal only under selection-on-observables / conditional independence; the “short = long + bias” OVB logic and ability bias; bad controls (post-treatment variables; occupation in the education–wage regression) and the good-controls-are-predetermined rule; selection/collider bias from conditioning on a common consequence; and the case that credible causal inference needs a design (randomization, IV, natural experiments), with regression as the engine.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — exogeneity E[u\mid x]=0 as the causal condition; endogeneity from omitted variables, simultaneity / reverse causality (police–crime, income–health), and measurement-error attenuation (classical error in a regressor biases toward zero).
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

When OLS is causal. Writing the structural model y=\beta x + u (the data-generating equation whose \beta is the true causal effect, as opposed to a merely descriptive fit), OLS estimates \hat\beta \xrightarrow{p} \beta + \dfrac{\operatorname{Cov}(x,u)}{\operatorname{Var}(x)}, where \xrightarrow{p} denotes the large-sample (probability) limit — the value OLS converges to as n\to\infty. The second term — the bias — vanishes iff \operatorname{Cov}(x,u)=0, i.e. exogeneity. Every threat below is a reason \operatorname{Cov}(x,u)\neq 0.
Attenuation, derived. Let the true regressor be x^* but you observe x=x^*+e with classical error (e independent of x^* and u, variance \sigma_e^2). Then (writing \operatorname{plim}, the probability limit, for that same large-sample value) 
\operatorname{plim}\hat\beta = \beta\cdot\frac{\sigma_{x^*}^2}{\sigma_{x^*}^2+\sigma_e^2} = \beta\cdot\lambda,
\qquad 0<\lambda<1,
 so the estimate is the truth times a reliability ratio \lambda<1 — shrunk toward zero. The noisier the measure, the smaller \lambda, the worse the attenuation.
Why a collider induces dependence. If C = x + y + \varepsilon is a common consequence and x,y are marginally independent, then conditioning on C=c ties them: y \approx c - x - \varepsilon, so within a stratum of C, higher x forces lower y — a negative conditional correlation from a zero marginal one. Controlling for a post-treatment collider does exactly this.
Simultaneity bias. With y=\beta x+u and x=\gamma y+v, solving the system makes \operatorname{Cov}(x,u)\neq0, so OLS is inconsistent — the coefficient mixes \beta and \gamma. The fix is an instrument that shifts x without touching u — the subject of Part V.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Connections

Builds on: multiple regression (OVB and good/bad controls), the Gauss–Markov assumptions (exogeneity and its failure modes), conditional expectation (regression as the best-linear-approximation to the CEF, which is descriptive), and correlation is not causation (now formalised).
Leads to: the entire causal-inference summit — potential outcomes, randomized experiments, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and difference-in-differences — each a strategy for making exogeneity credible.
Closes: Part III. You can fit and interpret regressions and judge when their coefficients deserve a causal reading — the prerequisite for everything that follows.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text: Correlation tells you whether two things move together; regression tells you by how much, with an equation. Fit a line through a scatter so that it misses the points by as little as possible, and you have the single most-used tool in empirical social science — and, it turns out, an estimate of the conditional expectation you met in Part I.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Why it matters
Almost every empirical claim in the social sciences is, under the hood, a regression coefficient: the return to a year of schooling, the effect of class size on test scores, the gender wage gap. Where correlation gives a single unitless number, regression gives a formula — \hat y = \hat\beta_0 +
\hat\beta_1 x — whose slope you can read in real units and whose machinery extends, in the next entries, to holding other things constant. (Wooldridge 2019) This entry builds the one-predictor case completely: what the line means, how it’s chosen, and how to tell whether it fits.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
The model
The simple linear regression model describes a relationship in the population: (Wooldridge 2019)

y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + u.


y is the dependent (outcome, explained) variable; x is the independent (regressor, explanatory) variable. (Wooldridge 2019)
\beta_0 is the intercept — the expected y when x=0.
\beta_1 is the slope, the parameter of interest: the ceteris paribus effect of x on y.
u is the error term — everything else that affects y but isn’t x (all the unobserved factors). (Wooldridge 2019)

Interpreting the slope is the whole game: a one-unit increase in x is associated with a \beta_1 change in y, on average — formally, holding the unobservables fixed (\Delta u = 0), \Delta y = \beta_1 \Delta x. (Wooldridge 2019)






TipRegression is the conditional expectation function



This connects straight back to conditional expectation. Under the zero conditional mean assumption — the unobservables average out to zero at every value of x, E[u\mid x]=0 — the regression line is the CEF: (Wooldridge 2019)

E[y \mid x] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x.

So the regression line threads the average value of y for every slice of x. That is why regression is the best predictor machinery made practical — and why E[u\mid x]=0 (which we scrutinise in R3) is the assumption that does all the work.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Ordinary least squares
We never see the population line; we estimate it from a sample of n points. Ordinary least squares (OLS) picks the line that makes the total squared miss as small as possible — it chooses \hat\beta_0, \hat\beta_1 to minimise the sum of squared residuals: (Wooldridge 2019)

\min_{\hat\beta_0,\hat\beta_1} \sum_{i=1}^n \big(y_i - \hat\beta_0 - \hat\beta_1 x_i\big)^2.

Why squared? Squaring makes every miss positive (so they can’t cancel), and penalises big misses disproportionately. Solving this minimisation (calculus in the appendix) gives two beautifully interpretable formulas: (Wooldridge 2019)

\hat\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_i (x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\sum_i (x_i-\bar x)^2}
= \frac{\widehat{\operatorname{Cov}}(x,y)}{\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(x)},
\qquad
\hat\beta_0 = \bar y - \hat\beta_1 \bar x.

The slope is just the covariance of x and y divided by the variance of x — co-movement, rescaled by how much x itself varies. The intercept then sits wherever it must for the line to pass through the average point (\bar x, \bar y).

Least squares, made visible
The picture below is the method: OLS slides and tilts a line until the sum of the squared vertical gaps (the residuals, in red) is as small as possible. No other line through this cloud makes that total smaller.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 70
educ = rng.integers(6, 19, n).astype(float)            # years of schooling
wage = -0.90 + 0.54 * educ + rng.normal(0, 1.6, n)     # WAGE1-style relationship

# OLS by the covariance/variance formula
b1 = np.cov(educ, wage, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(educ)
b0 = wage.mean() - b1 * educ.mean()
fit = b0 + b1 * educ

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.vlines(educ, np.minimum(wage, fit), np.maximum(wage, fit),
          color="#c0392b", lw=0.9, alpha=0.7)          # residuals
ax.scatter(educ, wage, s=22, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.8, zorder=3)
xs = np.linspace(6, 18, 50)
ax.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4,
        label=f"OLS:  wage = {b0:.2f} + {b1:.2f}·educ")
ax.set_xlabel("years of education"); ax.set_ylabel("hourly wage ($)")
ax.set_title("OLS minimises the total squared residual (red)")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Ordinary least squares on simulated wage–education data. The blue line minimises the total squared length of the red residual segments — the vertical gaps between each point and the line. Its slope (≈ 0.54) says each extra year of schooling predicts about 54 cents more per hour, echoing Wooldridge’s WAGE1 estimate.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
section: Fitted values, residuals, and OLS’s built-in properties
text:
Fitted values, residuals, and OLS’s built-in properties
For each observation the line gives a fitted value \hat y_i = \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1 x_i (the prediction) and a residual \hat u_i = y_i - \hat y_i (the miss). (Wooldridge 2019) By construction, OLS guarantees three algebraic facts that hold for any dataset: (Wooldridge 2019)

The residuals sum to zero, \sum_i \hat u_i = 0 — positive and negative misses cancel exactly.
The residuals are uncorrelated with x — the line has wrung all the linear information out of x; whatever is left is, by definition, unrelated to it.
The line passes through (\bar x, \bar y) — plug in the average x and you get the average y.

These aren’t assumptions to check; they’re identities baked into the least-squares solution.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
section: Goodness of fit: R² and the standard error
text:
Goodness of fit: R² and the standard error
How much of y’s variation did the line actually capture? Decompose the total variation exactly as ANOVA does — the same between/within split: (Wooldridge 2019)

\underbrace{\sum_i (y_i-\bar y)^2}_{\text{SST (total)}} =
\underbrace{\sum_i (\hat y_i-\bar y)^2}_{\text{SSE (explained)}} +
\underbrace{\sum_i \hat u_i^2}_{\text{SSR (residual)}}.

The coefficient of determination is the explained share: (Wooldridge 2019)

R^2 = \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} \in [0,1].

An R^2 of 0.60 means x explains 60% of the sample variation in y. (Wooldridge 2019) Two facts worth carrying: in simple regression R^2 is exactly the square of the correlation coefficient, R^2 = r_{xy}^2 — regression and correlation measuring the same association two ways; and the standard error of the regression, \text{SER} = \sqrt{\text{SSR}/(n-2)}, estimates the typical vertical distance of points from the line, in the units of y. (Wooldridge 2019) (Low R^2 is not failure: in social data, individual behaviour is noisy, and a small-but-precise slope can still be the answer you want.)
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
section: A fully worked example: wage and education
text:
A fully worked example: wage and education
Wooldridge’s canonical case, estimated on n = 526 workers (the WAGE1 data): (Wooldridge 2019)

\widehat{\text{wage}} = -0.90 + 0.54\,\cdot\,\text{educ}, \qquad R^2 = 0.171.


Slope 0.54: each additional year of schooling predicts about 54 cents more per hour. Put comparatively (the honest framing): comparing two groups one year of schooling apart, the more-schooled group earns about $0.54/hour more on average. (Wooldridge 2019)
Intercept -0.90: literally, zero education predicts a negative wage — nonsensical, a reminder that the intercept is often just a mathematical anchor, not a meaningful x=0 value. (Wooldridge 2019)
R^2 = 0.171: education explains about 17% of the variation in wages. The other 83% lives in u — ability, experience, luck, industry — which is exactly why we move to multiple regression next. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading the slope as causal. \hat\beta_1 is an association unless E[u\mid x]=0 holds — and with one regressor it rarely does (ability is in u and correlates with education). The causal reading waits on much more. (Wooldridge 2019)
Over-interpreting the intercept. \hat\beta_0 is the fitted value at x=0, often outside the data’s range and meaningless (the -0.90 wage). Don’t force a story onto it. (Wooldridge 2019)
Worshipping R^2. A high R^2 doesn’t make a model correct or causal, and a low R^2 doesn’t make a slope wrong. Social-behaviour models routinely have small R^2 and useful coefficients. (Wooldridge 2019)
Extrapolating beyond the data. The line is fitted where the points are; predictions far outside the observed x range are unsupported.
Forgetting the squared-miss objective. OLS minimises squared residuals, so it is sensitive to outliers — one extreme point can swing the line.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the model y=\beta_0+\beta_1x+u and the meaning of each term; the slope as the ceteris-paribus effect and the CEF link under E[u\mid x]=0; OLS as minimising the SSR; the slope =\widehat{\operatorname{Cov}}(x,y)/\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(x) and intercept formulas; fitted values, residuals, and the three algebraic properties; SST=SSE+SSR, R^2, R^2=r_{xy}^2, and the SER; the WAGE1 example (\widehat{\text{wage}}=-0.90+0.54\,\text{educ}, R^2=0.171, n=526).
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — regression as fitting an equation (vs correlation’s single number); fitted values and residuals in the predictive idiom.
Angrist & Pischke, Mastering ’Metrics — the comparative (“two groups one unit apart”) framing of a slope, foreshadowing the causal reading.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Deriving the OLS estimators. Minimise S(\hat\beta_0,\hat\beta_1)=\sum_i(y_i-\hat\beta_0-\hat\beta_1 x_i)^2. The first-order conditions (set partial derivatives to zero) are the normal equations:

\sum_i (y_i-\hat\beta_0-\hat\beta_1 x_i)=0, \qquad \sum_i x_i(y_i-\hat\beta_0-\hat\beta_1 x_i)=0.

The first gives \hat\beta_0=\bar y-\hat\beta_1\bar x (the line through the means). Substituting into the second and simplifying gives \hat\beta_1=\dfrac{\sum_i(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\sum_i(x_i-\bar x)^2}. The two normal equations are the algebraic properties “residuals sum to zero” and “residuals uncorrelated with x.”
Why R^2=r_{xy}^2 in simple regression. With \hat y_i-\bar y=\hat\beta_1(x_i-\bar x), \text{SSE}=\hat\beta_1^2\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2. Insert \hat\beta_1=S_{xy}/S_{xx}: \text{SSE}=S_{xy}^2/S_{xx}, so R^2=\text{SSE}/\text{SST}=S_{xy}^2/(S_{xx}S_{yy})=r_{xy}^2.
The SER and degrees of freedom. \text{SER}=\sqrt{\text{SSR}/(n-2)}; the n-2 is the two degrees of freedom spent estimating \hat\beta_0 and \hat\beta_1, making \text{SER}^2 an unbiased estimate of \operatorname{Var}(u).
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Connections

Builds on: conditional expectation (the regression line as the CEF / best predictor), covariance & correlation (the slope is Cov/Var; R^2=r^2), and estimation (OLS is an estimator).
Leads to: multiple regression (adding regressors to hold things constant and shrink u), and inference for coefficients (standard errors and tests on \hat\beta_1).
Mirrors: the ANOVA decomposition (SST = SSE + SSR is the between/within split) and the law of total variance.

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text: Many social-science outcomes are durations: time until re-arrest, until a job ends, until a degree is finished, until a marriage dissolves. Two things make these special. The clock can’t run backward (time is non-negative), and — the defining complication — when the study ends, some events haven’t happened yet. That’s censoring, and it quietly wrecks ordinary regression. Survival analysis is the regression family built to handle it: the Kaplan–Meier curve describes it, and Cox proportional hazards models the effect of covariates on the rate at which events occur.

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
Why it matters
Logistic regression extended OLS to binary outcomes; survival analysis extends it to time-to-event outcomes — and it’s indispensable because the alternative fails badly. The reason is censoring, a feature of almost every longitudinal study, and the methods here are the standard tools of event-history / duration analysis across sociology, political science, criminology, and economics. (Wooldridge 2019; James et al. 2021) If you study how long something takes, this is your toolkit.

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
The problem: censoring
The outcome is a duration T — but for some units the event simply hasn’t occurred by the time the study ends. This is right-censoring: you don’t know T, only that T>t (the parolee wasn’t re-arrested during the study, the employee was still employed at the last follow-up). (James et al. 2021)
Why not just run OLS on the durations? Both naive fixes are biased: (Wooldridge 2019)

Drop the censored cases → the remaining sample is non-random (you keep only the fast events), biasing everything.
Treat the censoring time as the event time → you record durations that are too short, shrinking coefficient estimates toward zero. (Wooldridge 2019)

Censored observations carry real information — this unit lasted at least this long — and survival methods are built to use that information correctly rather than discard or distort it.

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
Two functions: survival and hazard
Survival analysis describes a duration distribution through two interrelated functions: (James et al. 2021)

The survival function S(t)=\Pr(T>t) — the probability of “surviving” (no event yet) past time t. It starts at 1 and steps downward.
The hazard function h(t) — the instantaneous rate of the event at time t, given survival up to t: among those still at risk at t, how fast are events happening right now?

They are two views of the same distribution, linked by 
h(t)=\frac{f(t)}{S(t)}=\frac{f(t)}{1-F(t)},
 the event density divided by the share still at risk. (Wooldridge 2019) The hazard is the natural object for modelling covariate effects — it asks how a predictor changes the moment-to-moment risk of the event.

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
Kaplan–Meier: describing survival with censoring
The Kaplan–Meier (KM) estimator is the nonparametric, assumption-light estimate of S(t) that handles censoring correctly. (James et al. 2021) It builds S(t) as a step function, taking the product, over each observed event time, of the conditional probability of surviving that instant: at event time t_j with d_j events among n_j units still at risk, 
\hat S(t)=\prod_{t_j\le t}\Big(1-\frac{d_j}{n_j}\Big).
 The trick is the risk set n_j: a censored unit stays counted as “at risk” until the moment it is censored, then drops out — so its partial information is used without pretending its event was observed. (James et al. 2021) To compare groups (treated vs control, high vs low skill), the log-rank test asks whether two KM curves differ by more than chance. (James et al. 2021) The figure shows the canonical picture.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)

def make_group(rate, cens_rate, n):
    t_event = rng.exponential(1 / rate, n)
    t_cens = rng.exponential(1 / cens_rate, n)
    obs = np.minimum(t_event, t_cens)
    event = (t_event <= t_cens).astype(int)     # 1 = event observed, 0 = censored
    return obs, event

def kaplan_meier(obs, event):
    order = np.argsort(obs); obs, event = obs[order], event[order]
    times = np.unique(obs)
    n = len(obs); surv = 1.0
    xs, ys = [0.0], [1.0]
    for t in times:
        at_risk = np.sum(obs >= t)
        d = np.sum((obs == t) & (event == 1))
        if d > 0:
            surv *= (1 - d / at_risk)
        xs += [t, t]; ys += [ys[-1], surv]       # step down at event time
    return np.array(xs), np.array(ys)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
for rate, cr, color, name in [(0.7, 0.25, "#c0392b", "Group A (higher hazard)"),
                              (0.35, 0.25, "#1f3a5f", "Group B (lower hazard)")]:
    obs, event = make_group(rate, cr, 80)
    xs, ys = kaplan_meier(obs, event)
    ax.step(xs, ys, where="post", color=color, lw=2.2, label=name)
    cens = obs[event == 0]
    # ticks at censoring times, placed on the curve height
    for c in cens:
        h = ys[np.searchsorted(xs, c, side="right") - 1]
        ax.plot(c, h, marker="|", color=color, ms=7, mew=1.4)

ax.set_xlabel("time t"); ax.set_ylabel("Ŝ(t) = P(survive past t)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.02); ax.set_xlim(0, 8)
ax.set_title("Kaplan–Meier survival curves (ticks = censored)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Kaplan–Meier survival curves for two groups, computed from censored data. Each curve steps down at observed event times; vertical ticks mark censored units (event not yet observed — they leave the risk set without a step). Group B’s curve lies above Group A’s: B ‘survives’ longer (lower hazard). A log-rank test formalises whether the gap is more than chance. The estimator uses censored units correctly — counting them at risk until censoring — which is exactly what ordinary regression cannot do.

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
Cox proportional hazards regression
KM describes; the Cox proportional-hazards model explains — the workhorse regression for survival data. (James et al. 2021) It models the hazard for unit i as a shared baseline hazard times an exponential function of covariates: 
h_i(t\mid X)=h_0(t)\,\exp(\beta_1 X_{i1}+\dots+\beta_p X_{ip}).
 Its genius is being semiparametric: the baseline hazard h_0(t) is left completely unspecified — you make no assumption about the shape of time-dependence — yet you can still estimate the \beta’s (via a partial likelihood that cancels h_0). (James et al. 2021)
Coefficients are read as hazard ratios, \text{HR}=\exp(\beta): (Wooldridge 2019)

\beta>0 \Rightarrow \text{HR}>1: the covariate raises the hazard — the event comes sooner (shorter duration).
\beta<0 \Rightarrow \text{HR}<1: it lowers the hazard — the event comes later (longer survival).

An HR of 0.91 means each unit of the predictor cuts the moment-to-moment risk by about 9%.

The proportional-hazards assumption
The “PH” is a real assumption: a covariate multiplies the hazard by the same constant factor at every time — the HR is constant over time. (Wooldridge 2019) If a treatment helps early but fades (non-proportional), the model is misspecified. Check it (e.g. Schoenfeld residuals / cox.zph); if it fails, stratify on the offending variable or interact it with time. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
section: The social-science bridge, and the caution
text:
The social-science bridge, and the caution
These are the duration / event-history models of applied social science. (Wooldridge 2019)






NoteDuration models in the wild




Recidivism. In Wooldridge’s study of North Carolina inmates (time until re-arrest), more prior convictions significantly shortens the duration until the next arrest — a higher hazard. (Wooldridge 2019)
Unemployment & worker’s comp. Duration models are standard for unemployment spells and policy effects: a Kentucky benefit increase lengthened the time high-earners stayed on workers’ compensation by roughly 19%. (Wooldridge 2019)



And the discipline this Part has hammered applies here too: a hazard ratio is associational. Unless the covariate was randomly assigned, interpret it with exactly the caution owed any OLS coefficient — unobserved confounding and selection bias don’t vanish because the outcome is a duration. (Wooldridge 2019) Cox regression is a superb description of risk over time; turning that into a cause still needs a design.

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Ignoring censoring. Dropping censored units biases via selection; coding them as events biases toward zero. Use survival methods that count them at risk until censoring. (Wooldridge 2019)
Reading the proportional-hazards model without checking PH. If a covariate’s effect changes over time, the constant-HR model is wrong; test it and stratify/interact if needed. (Wooldridge 2019)
Misreading hazard ratios. \text{HR}>1 means the event comes sooner (shorter survival), not “more of a good thing”; mind the direction. (Wooldridge 2019)
Treating a hazard ratio as causal. It’s an association unless a design supports causation — the same rule as every other coefficient in this Part. (Wooldridge 2019)
Informative censoring. KM and Cox assume censoring is unrelated to the event risk; if people drop out because they’re about to fail, the estimates are biased. (James et al. 2021)

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — duration/event-history models; why OLS fails on censored data (selection from dropping; attenuation from coding censored as events); the hazard h(t)=f(t)/[1-F(t)]; Cox hazard ratios and the proportional-hazards assumption (and fixes); the recidivism and unemployment / Kentucky workers’-comp (~19%) examples; hazard ratios as associational.
James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 11) — censoring; the survival function S(t)=\Pr(T>t) and hazard; the Kaplan–Meier estimator (product over event times, the risk set) and the log-rank test; the Cox proportional-hazards model with unspecified baseline hazard h_0(t).

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Survival, hazard, and their identity. With density f, CDF F, and S=1-F: 
h(t)=\frac{f(t)}{S(t)}=-\frac{d}{dt}\log S(t)\;\Longrightarrow\; S(t)=\exp\!\Big(-\!\int_0^t h(u)\,du\Big).
 The integral \int_0^t h is the cumulative hazard H(t); survival decays as e^{-H(t)}. A constant hazard h(t)=\lambda gives the exponential duration S(t)=e^{-\lambda t} — the memoryless baseline.
Kaplan–Meier as a product-limit estimator. Order the distinct event times t_1<t_2<\cdots. At t_j, with d_j events and n_j at risk, the conditional survival of that instant is estimated by 1-d_j/n_j, and survival is the running product \hat S(t)=\prod_{t_j\le t}(1-d_j/n_j). Censored units inflate n_j at every time up to their censoring, then leave — using their partial information without bias. It is the nonparametric MLE of S under non-informative censoring.
Cox partial likelihood. At each event time, the probability that the unit who failed was the one who failed, given the risk set \mathcal R(t), is 
\frac{\exp(\beta^\top X_{i})}{\sum_{j\in\mathcal R(t)}\exp(\beta^\top X_{j})}.
 The baseline hazard h_0(t) cancels from numerator and denominator, so maximising the product of these terms estimates \beta without ever specifying h_0 — the semiparametric magic. The proportional-hazards assumption is exactly that h_i(t)/h_j(t)=\exp(\beta^\top(X_i-X_j)) is constant in t; Schoenfeld residuals test whether it drifts with time.

title: Survival & Event-History Analysis
text:
Connections

Builds on: limited dependent variables (regression for non-continuous outcomes; MLE), common distributions (the exponential/Weibull durations and the hazard), and functional form (the log-duration and exponential-hazard links).
Leads to: what regression can’t tell you — hazard ratios are associational, and a causal duration effect needs a design.
Reappears in: applied field work on recidivism, unemployment, turnover, and program duration — wherever the question is how long until.

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text: Every predictive method faces the same tug-of-war. Too rigid and it misses the signal (bias); too flexible and it chases the noise (variance). The expected error on new data is literally the sum of those two plus an irreducible floor — and that sum is minimised not at maximum flexibility but somewhere in the middle. Finding that middle is what predictive modelling is.

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
Why it matters
The last entry showed the U-shaped test-error curve. This one explains why it’s U-shaped, and the answer — the bias–variance decomposition — is the most important single idea in statistical learning. (James et al. 2021) It is the seed planted back in estimation (MSE = variance + bias²) now grown into the organising principle of an entire field: every method choice, every tuning parameter, every regularization penalty is, underneath, a position on this trade-off. Understand it and the rest of Part IV is commentary.

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
Flexibility, overfitting, underfitting
Flexibility is the range of shapes a method can take to estimate the unknown f. (James et al. 2021) Too little or too much, and you fail — in opposite ways:

Underfitting (high bias). The model is too rigid to capture the signal — fitting a straight line to a curved truth. It misses the same way in every sample. (James et al. 2021)
Overfitting (high variance). The model is so flexible it follows the training data too closely, mistaking that sample’s noise for signal. It nails the data it trained on and fails miserably on new data. (James et al. 2021)

The art is the flexibility between these — enough to catch the signal, not so much as to memorise the noise.

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
The decomposition
ISL makes it exact. For a test point x_0, the expected squared prediction error splits into three non-negative pieces: (James et al. 2021)

\mathbb{E}\big[(y_0 - \hat f(x_0))^2\big]
= \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}\big(\hat f(x_0)\big)}_{\text{variance}}
+ \underbrace{\big[\operatorname{Bias}(\hat f(x_0))\big]^2}_{\text{bias}^2}
+ \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\epsilon)}_{\text{irreducible}}.

Each term has a clean meaning: (James et al. 2021)

Bias — the error from approximating a complex reality with a simpler model. Assume linearity when the world is curved and you build in a systematic miss. High for rigid models.
Variance — how much \hat f would change if you re-estimated it on a different training set. A stable method barely moves; a flexible one pivots wildly to chase each sample’s points. High for flexible models.
Irreducible error — the noise \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon) that X simply cannot predict — the floor no model beats. (James et al. 2021)

Because all three are non-negative, the irreducible term is an absolute lower bound: the best possible test MSE is \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon), achieved only with zero bias and zero variance — generally impossible.

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
The trade-off and the U-shape
Here is the tension. As flexibility rises, bias falls but variance rises — a tug-of-war. (James et al. 2021) And it plays out in two acts:

Early on, added flexibility cuts bias faster than it adds variance, so total test MSE falls.
Eventually, bias is nearly gone and there’s little left to gain, while variance keeps climbing as the model fits noise — so total MSE rises.

The result is the U-shaped test-error curve, and the optimal model sits at the bottom of the U, where bias² + variance is smallest. (James et al. 2021) This is also exactly why training error misleads: training error tracks bias down but is blind to variance, so it falls forever while test error turns back up.

Watch the trade-off decompose
The figure below runs the decomposition by brute force: fit polynomials of each flexibility to hundreds of fresh training sets, then measure, at held-out points, how much the predictions miss on average (bias²) and how much they scatter across training sets (variance). Bias falls, variance rises, and their sum — plus the irreducible floor — traces the familiar U.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
def f_true(x):  return np.sin(1.5 * x)
sigma = 0.4
x_grid = np.linspace(-2, 2, 40)                 # test points
truth = f_true(x_grid)

degrees = range(1, 11)
n_train, reps = 25, 300
bias2, variance = [], []
for d in degrees:
    preds = np.empty((reps, x_grid.size))
    for r in range(reps):
        xt = rng.uniform(-2, 2, n_train)
        yt = f_true(xt) + rng.normal(0, sigma, n_train)
        preds[r] = np.polyval(np.polyfit(xt, yt, d), x_grid)
    mean_pred = preds.mean(axis=0)
    bias2.append(np.mean((mean_pred - truth) ** 2))
    variance.append(np.mean(preds.var(axis=0)))

bias2, variance = np.array(bias2), np.array(variance)
total = bias2 + variance + sigma ** 2
best = list(degrees)[int(np.argmin(total))]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.0))
ax.plot(list(degrees), bias2, "o-", color="#d98a29", lw=2, label="bias²")
ax.plot(list(degrees), variance, "o-", color="#1f3a5f", lw=2, label="variance")
ax.plot(list(degrees), total, "o-", color="#111", lw=2.4, label="total test MSE")
ax.axhline(sigma ** 2, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.4, label="irreducible error")
ax.axvline(best, color="#1f8a4c", ls=":", lw=1.6)
ax.text(best + 0.15, total.max() * 0.8, f"sweet spot\n(degree {best})", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
ax.set_xlabel("model flexibility (polynomial degree)"); ax.set_ylabel("error")
ax.set_title("Bias falls, variance rises, total error is U-shaped")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The bias–variance decomposition by Monte Carlo. Across 300 training sets, bias² (orange) falls as flexibility grows, variance (blue) rises, and total test error (black) — their sum plus the irreducible floor (grey dashed) — is U-shaped. The minimum (green) is the sweet spot: not the most flexible model, but the one balancing the two errors.

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
It depends on the truth
A subtlety worth carrying: where the sweet spot sits depends on how complex reality actually is. ISL’s three simulated cases make the point: (James et al. 2021)

When the truth is nearly linear, a rigid linear model is already near-best — extra flexibility only adds variance, so test error rises almost immediately. (ISL Fig. 2.10.)
When the truth is mildly non-linear, an intermediate flexibility wins; both the rigid line (too biased) and the wiggly spline (too variable) lose. (ISL Fig. 2.9.)
When the truth is highly non-linear, flexibility pays off for a long way — bias reduction is huge — and the optimum is far more flexible before variance finally bites. (ISL Fig. 2.11.)

There is no universally best flexibility; it must be learned from the data, which is precisely the job of cross-validation. (James et al. 2021)

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Chasing the lowest training error. Training error tracks bias only; driving it to zero maximises variance and overfits. The target is the bottom of the test-error U. (James et al. 2021)
Believing more flexible is always better. It lowers bias but raises variance; past the sweet spot it makes predictions worse. Flexibility is a dial to tune, not a quantity to maximise. (James et al. 2021)
Ignoring that the optimum depends on the unknown truth. You can’t pick flexibility from theory alone; estimate test error (CV) and let the data choose. (James et al. 2021)
Forgetting the irreducible floor. No model beats \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon). A test error near the floor means you’re done, not that you need a fancier model. (James et al. 2021)
Reading low variance as “good.” A rigid model has low variance and high bias — confidently wrong. Both terms matter; only their sum does. (James et al. 2021)

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — flexibility, underfitting (high bias) and overfitting (high variance); the exact decomposition \mathbb{E}[(y_0-\hat f(x_0))^2] = \operatorname{Var}(\hat f(x_0)) + [\operatorname{Bias}(\hat f(x_0))]^2 + \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon); the bias and variance intuitions (simpler-model error vs sensitivity to the training set); the bias-down / variance-up tug-of-war and the U-shaped test MSE; training error always decreasing; and the three Chapter-2 simulated cases (nearly-linear, mildly non-linear, highly non-linear) where the optimal flexibility differs.

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Deriving the decomposition. Let y_0=f(x_0)+\epsilon with \mathbb{E}[\epsilon]=0, \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon)=\sigma^2, \epsilon\perp\hat f. Add and subtract \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x_0)]:

\mathbb{E}[(y_0-\hat f(x_0))^2]
= \mathbb{E}\big[(f(x_0)+\epsilon-\hat f(x_0))^2\big].

Expand; the \epsilon cross-terms vanish (mean zero, independent), leaving \sigma^2 + \mathbb{E}[(f(x_0)-\hat f(x_0))^2]. Now split the second term with \bar f \equiv \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x_0)]:

\mathbb{E}[(f-\hat f)^2] = \underbrace{(f-\bar f)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2}
+ \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\hat f-\bar f)^2]}_{\text{Var}(\hat f)},

its cross-term zero because \mathbb{E}[\hat f-\bar f]=0. Hence the three-way split. This is the same bias–variance identity as for a single estimator, now applied to the prediction \hat f(x_0) and averaged over training sets.
Why flexibility trades them. Intuitively, a model class with more free parameters can sit closer to any target (bias \downarrow) but is pinned down less firmly by n fixed observations, so it swings more across resamples (variance \uparrow). Roughly, for many methods variance grows with the effective number of parameters p like \sigma^2 p/n, while bias shrinks as the class enriches — their sum is minimised at finite p.

title: The Bias–Variance Trade-off
text:
Connections

Builds on: estimation (the MSE = variance + bias² identity, the seed) and explaining vs predicting (the U-shaped test error this explains).
Leads to: classification (the same trade-off for categorical outcomes, where the irreducible floor becomes the Bayes error rate), cross-validation (how to find the sweet spot from data), regularization (deliberately adding bias to slash variance), and trees & forests (a high-variance method tamed by ensembling).
Reappears in: causal machine learning, where regularised, cross-fitted nuisance estimates trade bias for variance in service of an unbiased effect.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text: Spurious regression warned: never regress one I(1) series on another. Cointegration is the principled exception. When several non-stationary series are tied together by a common stochastic trend, a particular linear combination of them is stationary — and the levels regression recovers a genuine long-run equilibrium, not a fluke. The companion idea, the error-correction model, says the short run is the system pulling itself back toward that equilibrium whenever it strays.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
Why it matters
Many economic relationships are equilibrium statements: consumption and income, spot and futures prices, short and long interest rates. Each series wanders (is I(1)), yet theory says they can’t drift apart forever. Cointegration makes that intuition testable and gives the only legitimate way to estimate a relationship in levels among non-stationary variables (Enders 2014).
