It is also the bridge from description to dynamics: the error-correction form separates the long-run relationship from the short-run adjustment toward it.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
The core idea
Two or more I(1) series are cointegrated if some linear combination of them is stationary (I(0)) (Enders 2014). The series individually meander with no fixed mean, but they share a common stochastic trend, so they never drift arbitrarily far apart — that bounded gap is the long-run equilibrium (Enders 2014). Formally, for a vector \mathbf{x}_t of I(1) variables, cointegration holds if there is a cointegrating vector \boldsymbol{\beta} with \boldsymbol{\beta}'\mathbf{x}_t stationary (Enders 2014).
The textbook picture is the drunk and her dog: both wander unpredictably (two random walks), but a leash keeps the distance between them bounded — that bounded distance is the stationary combination (Enders 2014).


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
T = 240
x = np.cumsum(rng.normal(0, 1, T))     # the drunk: a random walk (I(1))

# the leash: a stationary AR(1) deviation
e = np.empty(T); e[0] = 0.0
for t in range(1, T):
    e[t] = 0.75 * e[t-1] + rng.normal(0, 0.8)
y = x + e                              # the dog: follows X, bounded slack -> Y is I(1)
spread = y - x                         # = e, stationary (cointegrating combination)

fig, (axT, axB) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(7.4, 5.0), sharex=True,
                               gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": [2, 1]})
axT.plot(x, color="#1f4e79", lw=1.6, label="X  (random walk — the drunk)")
axT.plot(y, color="#c0392b", lw=1.6, alpha=0.85, label="Y = X + wobble (the dog)")
axT.set_title("Two I(1) series sharing a common stochastic trend")
axT.set_ylabel("level"); axT.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="upper left")

axB.plot(spread, color="#3f8f6f", lw=1.4)
axB.axhline(spread.mean(), color="#444", ls="--", lw=1.2)
axB.fill_between(range(T), spread.mean() - 2*spread.std(), spread.mean() + 2*spread.std(),
                 color="#3f8f6f", alpha=0.10)
axB.set_title("Their spread  Y − X  is stationary — the 'leash'")
axB.set_xlabel("time"); axB.set_ylabel("spread")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Cointegration, by construction. Top: a random walk X (the ‘drunk’) and Y = X + a stationary wobble (the ‘dog’). Each is I(1) — neither has a fixed mean, both wander off. Yet they wander together. Bottom: their spread Y − X is stationary, hovering in a bounded band around a constant (the ‘leash’). That stationary combination is what cointegration means — and a regression of Y on X in levels recovers the genuine long-run relationship, not a spurious one.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
Rescuing the spurious regression
Recall that regressing two independent I(1) series gives a fake high R^2 and huge t-stats. If the variables are cointegrated, that regression is no longer spurious — it consistently estimates the meaningful long-run equilibrium toward which the system converges (Enders 2014). Cointegration is precisely the condition that separates a real levels relationship from a coincidence of two wandering trends.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
The Engle–Granger two-step method
Engle & Granger (1987) gave a simple recipe (Enders 2014):

Pretest and regress. Confirm every series is I(1), then estimate the long-run equilibrium by OLS in levels: y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 z_t + e_t.
Test the residuals. Collect \hat e_t and test it for a unit root with an ADF-type regression \Delta \hat e_t = a_1 \hat e_{t-1} + \sum \beta_i \Delta \hat e_{t-i} + \epsilon_t. If \hat e_t is stationary, the series are cointegrated.







WarningDon’t use ordinary Dickey–Fuller critical values here



Because OLS chose \beta to minimise the residual variance, the residuals are “prejudiced toward looking stationary.” You must use special critical values (Phillips–Ouliaris, or Enders’ Table C), not the standard DF table (Enders 2014).


Weaknesses. Engle–Granger finds only one cointegrating vector even when more exist; it is sensitive to normalisation (which variable goes on the left-hand side can flip the verdict); and it carries small-sample bias (Enders 2014). These are exactly what Johansen’s method fixes.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
The error-correction model
The Granger Representation Theorem is the deep result: for I(1) variables, cointegration and error-correction are equivalent representations (Enders 2014). If the series are cointegrated, their short-run changes must respond to the equilibrium error — last period’s gap from the long-run relationship:

\Delta \mathbf{x}_t = \pi_0 + \pi\,\mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^p \pi_i\,\Delta\mathbf{x}_{t-i} + \epsilon_t .

The coefficient \pi (often \alpha) is the speed of adjustment: it governs how quickly the variables return to equilibrium after a shock (Enders 2014). A large |\alpha| means fast correction; \alpha = 0 means that variable doesn’t adjust (it’s weakly exogenous — the long-run forcing variable). The ECM is the best of both worlds: a long-run equilibrium and a short-run dynamic in one estimable equation, all in stationary (\Delta) terms so standard inference works.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
The Johansen methodology
Johansen (1988) recast cointegration as a maximum-likelihood, VAR-based problem — sidestepping Engle–Granger’s normalisation issue and allowing multiple cointegrating vectors (Enders 2014). The key object is the rank r of the matrix \pi in the VECM, which counts the cointegrating relations (Enders 2014):







\operatorname{rank}(\pi)
Meaning




r = 0
No cointegration (model the differences as a VAR)


0 < r < n
r cointegrating vectors (estimate a VECM)


r = n
All variables already stationary (a VAR in levels is fine)



Two likelihood-ratio statistics built from the estimated characteristic roots \lambda test the rank (Enders 2014):

\lambda_{\text{trace}} — null: at most r cointegrating vectors.
\lambda_{\max} — null: exactly r, against r+1.

The system is then estimated as a VECM (vector error-correction model), with \pi factored as \pi = \alpha\boldsymbol{\beta}': \boldsymbol{\beta} holds the cointegrating vectors (the long-run relations) and \alpha the speed-of-adjustment weights (how each equation responds) (Enders 2014).

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using standard DF critical values in Engle–Granger step 2. The residuals are biased toward stationarity; use Phillips–Ouliaris/Enders Table C. (Enders 2014)
Trusting Engle–Granger with more than two variables. It can miss extra cointegrating vectors and is normalisation-sensitive; use Johansen. (Enders 2014)
Running a plain VAR on cointegrated I(1) data. Differencing away a true cointegrating relation loses the long-run information — fit a VECM instead. (Enders 2014)
Over-reading a single speed-of-adjustment sign. \alpha must have the right sign and be significant to be “error-correcting”; a wrong-signed \alpha signals a misspecified or non-cointegrated system. (Enders 2014)
Claiming cointegration ⇒ causation. A long-run equilibrium constrains the variables; it does not, by itself, establish causal direction. (Enders 2014)

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Enders, Applied Econometric Time Series — cointegration as a stationary linear combination of I(1) series sharing a common stochastic trend; the cointegrating vector and the drunk-and-dog leash; how cointegration rescues the levels regression from spuriousness; the Engle–Granger two-step method, the need for special (non-DF) critical values because OLS residuals are “prejudiced toward stationarity,” and its weaknesses (one vector, normalisation-sensitivity, small-sample bias); the Granger Representation Theorem and the error-correction model with the speed-of-adjustment coefficient; and the Johansen ML/VAR approach — the rank of \pi, the \lambda_{\text{trace}} and \lambda_{\max} statistics, and the VECM with \pi=\alpha\beta'.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why \pi = \alpha\beta' encodes cointegration. In the VECM \Delta\mathbf{x}_t = \pi\mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \cdots + \epsilon_t, the term \pi\mathbf{x}_{t-1} must be stationary (everything else is a difference of I(1), hence I(0)). If \pi had full rank n, \mathbf{x}_{t-1} itself would be stationary — contradiction. If \pi=0, there’s no level relationship. The interesting case is reduced rank 0<r<n: then \pi=\alpha\beta' with \alpha,\beta of dimension n\times r, and \beta'\mathbf{x}_{t-1} is the r stationary cointegrating combinations, premultiplied by the adjustment loadings \alpha. The rank of \pi literally counts the long-run relations.
The error-correction intuition in scalars. With long-run relation y_t = \beta z_t and equilibrium error \hat e_{t-1} = y_{t-1} - \beta z_{t-1}, the ECM \Delta y_t = \alpha\,\hat e_{t-1} + (\text{short-run } \Delta\text{ terms}) + \epsilon_t requires \alpha < 0: if y overshot last period (\hat e_{t-1}>0), it is pushed down this period. The half-life of a deviation is \ln(0.5)/\ln(1+\alpha).
Engle–Granger’s “prejudice.” OLS picks \hat\beta to minimise \sum \hat e_t^2; among all linear combinations it finds the most stationary-looking one. Hence even under the null of no cointegration the residuals look “too stationary,” shifting the unit-root test’s distribution left — the reason ordinary DF critical values over-reject and bespoke tables are needed.

title: Cointegration & Error-Correction Models
text:
Connections

Builds on: unit roots (every series must be confirmed I(1) first) and VAR (the VECM is a VAR for cointegrated data).
Resolves: the spurious-regression prohibition — cointegration is the licensed exception.
Relates to: causal inference — a long-run equilibrium disciplines but does not by itself identify causal direction.

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text: Everything so far asked why: estimate an effect, defend it, attach a standard error. Predictive modelling asks a different question — what next? — and keeps score completely differently. A coefficient that’s wrong-but-significant fails the first goal; a black box that forecasts perfectly fails the second’s interpretability but wins its accuracy. Knowing which game you’re playing is the whole of this chapter.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Why it matters
The same fitted regression can be a triumph or a disaster depending on what you wanted from it, and conflating the two goals is the source of endless confusion across academia and industry. (James et al. 2021) Social science has traditionally lived in the explanatory culture — theory, hypotheses, interpretable effects. The predictive culture — machine learning — optimises something else entirely, and judges success on data the model has never seen. This entry draws the line cleanly, because every method in Part IV (cross-validation, regularization, trees) only makes sense once you accept the predictive scorecard, and because modern causal inference now borrows these tools.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Two goals









Explanatory (statistical / inferential)
Predictive (machine learning)




Asks
Why? — the mechanism
What next? — the forecast


Wants
unbiased, interpretable effects with valid uncertainty (p-values, CIs)
the most accurate prediction on new data


Will trade away
predictive accuracy, flexibility
interpretability, unbiasedness — happily treats the model as a black box


A “failure” is
a biased or uninterpretable coefficient
poor accuracy on unseen data



(James et al. 2021; Jacobucci et al. 2023) The point is not that one is better — it’s that they answer different questions and reward different models. A wrong-but-significant coefficient is a failure for explanation even if it predicts; a black box is a success for prediction even if no coefficient means anything. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)






NoteTwo goals, or three?



Llaudet & Imai split the purposes of data analysis into three: measurement, prediction, and explanation (Llaudet and Imai 2022). The two cultures on this page are the latter pair — explanation (the causal why) and prediction (the what next) — while measurement (describing how much, how many) is the quieter third job that Parts I–II handle. Same divide, one extra category; the discipline is the same — never mistake a tool built for one job as fit for another. See what quantitative social science does.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
The statistical-learning frame: Y = f(X) + \epsilon
ISL gives the unifying notation. Assume the inputs X (features, predictors) relate to the output Y (the response) through (James et al. 2021)

Y = f(X) + \epsilon,

where f is the systematic information X carries about Y, and \epsilon is a random error, independent of X with mean zero. All of statistical learning is estimating f from training data to get \hat f — and there are exactly two reasons to do it: (James et al. 2021)

Prediction — produce a guess \hat Y = \hat f(X) for new observations. Here \hat f can be a black box; you only care that \hat Y is close to Y.
Inference — understand the form of f: which predictors matter, in what direction, linearly or not. Here you must be able to read \hat f.

This single fork — predict with \hat f vs interpret \hat f — is the two-cultures divide, formalised. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Reducible vs irreducible error
Even a perfect estimate of f can’t predict perfectly, and ISL’s decomposition says why. The expected prediction error splits into two pieces: (James et al. 2021)

Reducible error — because \hat f \neq f. Better methods and more data shrink it; in principle it can go to zero. This is the part we work on.
Irreducible error — the \epsilon itself. Since Y depends on \epsilon, and \epsilon is by definition unpredictable from X, this error never goes away. It is an absolute ceiling on accuracy. (James et al. 2021)

Why does \epsilon exist? It holds the unmeasured and unmeasurable — a patient’s mood that day, a shopper’s whim, all the causes you didn’t (or couldn’t) record. (James et al. 2021) No algorithm, however clever, predicts what it has no information about.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
The in-sample / out-of-sample paradox
Here is the crux of why a great explanatory model can be a terrible predictor. (James et al. 2021)

In-sample fit measures performance on the same data used to train — the R^2 and MSE you’ve optimised all through Part III.
Out-of-sample accuracy measures performance on a held-out test set (or via cross-validation) — data the model has never seen.

The two can diverge sharply through overfitting: a flexible model can fit the training data perfectly by memorising its noise — the random idiosyncrasies of those particular cases — rather than the signal f. (James et al. 2021)






TipLambert’s exam analogy



Overfitting is like cheating on a test by memorising the answer key beforehand: the student aces that exam and flunks the next one. (James et al. 2021) A model that has memorised its training data looks brilliant in-sample and fails the moment it meets new data — which is the only test that counts for prediction.



Watch it happen: training error always falls, test error doesn’t
Fit polynomials of rising flexibility to the same noisy data. The training error falls monotonically — more flexibility always fits the seen data better. But the test error is U-shaped: it improves, bottoms out at a sweet spot, then climbs as the model starts fitting noise. That gap is overfitting.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
def f_true(x):  return np.sin(2.2 * x) + 0.4 * x          # smooth signal

x_tr = np.sort(rng.uniform(-3, 3, 30))
y_tr = f_true(x_tr) + rng.normal(0, 0.45, x_tr.size)
x_te = np.sort(rng.uniform(-3, 3, 1000))
y_te = f_true(x_te) + rng.normal(0, 0.45, x_te.size)

degrees = range(1, 14)
train_mse, test_mse = [], []
for d in degrees:
    coef = np.polyfit(x_tr, y_tr, d)
    train_mse.append(np.mean((y_tr - np.polyval(coef, x_tr)) ** 2))
    test_mse.append(np.mean((y_te - np.polyval(coef, x_te)) ** 2))

train_mse, test_mse = np.array(train_mse), np.array(test_mse)
best = degrees[int(np.argmin(test_mse))]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.0))
ax.plot(list(degrees), train_mse, "o-", color="#9aa0a6", lw=2, label="training error (in-sample)")
ax.plot(list(degrees), test_mse, "o-", color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2, label="test error (out-of-sample)")
ax.axvline(best, color="#1f8a4c", ls="--", lw=1.5)
ax.text(best + 0.2, test_mse.max() * 0.7, f"sweet spot\n(degree {best})", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
ax.axvspan(best + 0.5, 13.5, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.06)
ax.text(10.5, test_mse.max() * 0.9, "overfitting", color="#c0392b", fontsize=9, ha="center")
ax.set_xlabel("model flexibility (polynomial degree)"); ax.set_ylabel("mean squared error")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_title("Training error always falls; test error is U-shaped")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper center")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Training vs test error as model flexibility grows. The training error (grey) falls forever — a flexible enough model memorises the data it was shown. The test error (blue), on data never seen, is U-shaped: it falls, hits a minimum at moderate flexibility, then rises as the model fits noise. The minimum marks the best predictor; everything to its right is overfitting.





The lesson that organises the whole chapter: in-sample fit is not the goal — it can be made perfect and meaningless. The goal is the bottom of that blue curve, and finding it is what bias–variance and cross-validation are for.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
section: Flexibility vs interpretability: no free lunch
text:
Flexibility vs interpretability: no free lunch
A second trade-off runs through everything: as flexibility rises, interpretability falls. (James et al. 2021)

Restrictive, interpretable methods — linear regression, the Lasso — can only bend so far, but you can read exactly how each predictor acts. Ideal for inference.
Flexible, opaque methods — boosting, random forests, neural networks — fit wildly non-linear relationships and often predict better, but produce an \hat f so complex that no single predictor’s role is legible. Ideal for prediction. (James et al. 2021)

There is no method that is both maximally flexible and maximally interpretable — choosing where to sit on that frontier is choosing your culture. (We return to it in interpretability.)
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Breiman’s two cultures
Leo Breiman named the divide. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

The data-modelling culture assumes Nature follows a specific stochastic model (a linear regression, say) and estimates its parameters. This has dominated social science.
The algorithmic-modelling culture treats Nature’s mechanism as unknown and uses algorithms to find whatever function maps X to Y best, judged by prediction.

For social scientists this is more than a technical choice — it’s a methodological shift. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Used well, predictive tools let researchers test whether existing theories actually hold up out of sample, discover heterogeneous treatment effects (how an intervention helps different people differently), and — because out-of-sample validation is built in — provide a principled guard against the p-hacking and fishing that plague the explanatory culture. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) The cultures are complements, not enemies — which is exactly why this part sits between regression and the causal summit.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Judging a predictive model by in-sample R^2. Training fit can be made perfect by overfitting; it says nothing about new data. Evaluate out-of-sample, always. (James et al. 2021)
Judging an explanatory model by predictive accuracy (or vice versa). They optimise different things. A model can predict well with biased, uninterpretable coefficients — useless for a causal question — and an unbiased causal model can predict poorly. Pick the scorecard first. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Forgetting the irreducible error. No model beats the \epsilon ceiling; chasing zero error guarantees overfitting. Some of the world is genuinely unpredictable from your features. (James et al. 2021)
Assuming flexible = better. More flexibility lowers bias but raises variance and destroys interpretability; the right amount is a choice, not “as much as possible.” (James et al. 2021)
Treating a black box as an explanation. A forest can predict default beautifully and tell you nothing trustworthy about why — reading mechanism off a predictive model is a category error. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — the Y=f(X)+\epsilon frame; estimating f for prediction vs inference; reducible vs irreducible error and the accuracy ceiling; in-sample vs out-of-sample and overfitting; the flexibility–interpretability trade-off (restrictive/interpretable vs flexible/opaque methods); the exam-cheating analogy for overfitting.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, Machine Learning for Social & Behavioral Research — the explanatory vs predictive cultures for researchers; Breiman’s data-modelling vs algorithmic-modelling cultures; using ML to test theory out-of-sample, find heterogeneous effects, and resist p-hacking.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the applied train/test framing of predictive accuracy.
Llaudet & Imai, Data Analysis for Social Science — the three purposes of data analysis (measurement, prediction, explanation); this entry’s two cultures are its prediction and explanation.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The reducible/irreducible split. For a fixed \hat f and test point X=x_0, with Y=f(x_0)+\epsilon,

\mathbb{E}\big[(Y-\hat f(x_0))^2\big]
= \underbrace{\big(f(x_0)-\hat f(x_0)\big)^2}_{\text{reducible}}
+ \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\epsilon)}_{\text{irreducible}} .

The cross term vanishes because \epsilon has mean zero and is independent of \hat f(x_0). No choice of \hat f touches \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon) — hence the ceiling. Averaging the reducible part over training sets is what produces the bias–variance decomposition of the next entry.
Why training error understates test error. Training error is \frac1n\sum_i (y_i-\hat f(x_i))^2 evaluated at the same points used to choose \hat f, so \hat
f is tuned to those \epsilon_i. A flexible enough \hat f can drive it to 0 by interpolating the noise, while test error — evaluated at independent points with fresh \epsilon — rises. The gap is the optimism of the training error, and it grows with flexibility.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Connections

Builds on: estimation (the MSE = variance + bias² seed, now the organising principle), simple regression (R^2 as in-sample fit), and the replication crisis (out-of-sample validation as a discipline against overfitting/p-hacking).
Leads to: the bias–variance trade-off (the U-shape, formalised), cross-validation (estimating test error honestly), and regularization (buying lower variance with a little bias).
Bridges to: causal inference, where ML predictions of nuisance functions power modern estimators, and the ML Track.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text: The flexible methods that predict best are the hardest to read, and social science cares about why. This entry navigates that tension: the accuracy–interpretability frontier, the tools that partly open a black box (importance, partial dependence, SHAP), the iron rule that interpretable is not causal, and the genuinely exciting part — how machine learning, used carefully, comes back to serve the causal questions of Part V.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Why it matters
This entry closes the predictive sub-chapter by confronting its central discomfort for researchers: the most accurate models are black boxes, and a discipline built on explanation cannot simply accept “it predicts well, trust me.” (Jacobucci et al. 2023) So we do two things. First, survey the tools that make a complex model partly legible. Second — and more importantly — show that the right response isn’t to reject ML but to bend it back toward causation: ML is now a power tool inside modern causal inference, not a rival to it. This is the bridge from prediction to the summit.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
The accuracy–interpretability frontier
The trade-off from the opening entry, now made central: as model flexibility rises, interpretability falls. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Restrictive, transparent — linear and logistic regression, the Lasso: each coefficient is a ceteris paribus effect you can hand a policymaker. Limited in shape, but legible.
Flexible, opaque — random forests, boosting, neural nets: they bend to complex non-linearities and high-order interactions and often predict far better, but their input–output map runs through thousands of parameters that defy human explanation. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

methods = [
    ("Subset selection / Lasso", 0.92, 0.22),
    ("Linear / logistic regression", 0.96, 0.32),
    ("Generalized additive models", 0.70, 0.50),
    ("Decision trees", 0.80, 0.45),
    ("Bagging / random forests", 0.34, 0.74),
    ("Boosting", 0.28, 0.80),
    ("Neural nets / SVM", 0.15, 0.95),
]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.6))
for name, interp, flex in methods:
    ax.scatter(interp, flex, s=70, color="#1f3a5f", zorder=3)
    right = interp > 0.55                                   # label points inward
    ax.annotate(name, (interp, flex), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(-8 if right else 8, 4), fontsize=8.5,
                ha="right" if right else "left")
ax.annotate("", xy=(0.12, 0.97), xytext=(0.97, 0.20),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.4))
ax.text(0.45, 0.50, "the frontier:\nno model is both", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8.5, rotation=-34)
ax.set_xlim(0.0, 1.05)                         # interpretability increasing rightward (ISL convention)
ax.set_ylim(0.1, 1.05)
ax.set_xlabel("Interpretability  →"); ax.set_ylabel("Flexibility / predictive accuracy  →")
ax.set_title("You choose where to sit — there's no free lunch")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The accuracy–interpretability frontier (after ISL). Methods trade off: linear/logistic regression and the Lasso are highly interpretable but inflexible; random forests, boosting, and neural nets are flexible and accurate but opaque. Social science has clustered in the lower-right (interpretable); predictive modelling pushes up-left. There is no method in the top-right corner.





Social science has historically clustered at the interpretable end, because a model that predicts perfectly but explains nothing is of limited value for theory-building. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) But that needn’t mean abandoning flexible models — only learning to interrogate them.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Opening the box: interpretable ML
A toolkit has grown up to extract human-readable insight from complex models. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Variable (feature) importance — a heuristic ranking of which predictors drive the model, via Gini/RSS reduction or permutation. Answers “which features matter?”
Partial dependence plots (PDP) — show how the prediction changes as one feature varies, with all others averaged out. They reveal the shape of a learned relationship — linear, S-shaped, threshold — even inside a forest. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
SHAP and LIME — local explanations of a single prediction. SHAP (Shapley Additive exPlanations) attributes a prediction to its features by their exact additive contributions, naming the “key drivers” of this case; LIME fits a simple linear model locally around one point to see what mattered there. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) They answer “why did the model predict this for this person?”

These make a black box accountable — but accountable about its predictions, not about the world.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
The iron rule: interpretable ≠ causal
The most important caveat in the chapter. A feature-importance ranking or a partial-dependence plot is descriptive and predictive — never, by itself, a causal effect. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) It shows how the model uses information to guess, not how the world works. A predictor can rank high because it’s a proxy, a collider, or a confounded correlate.
Causation still requires an identification strategy — randomization, instruments, a credible design — to strip out selection bias. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) As Chernozhukov puts it: however impressive a model looks, its outputs carry no causal interpretation unless the data satisfy conditions like conditional exogeneity / unconfoundedness — the very assumptions Part III hammered. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Accuracy is not identification.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
ML comes back to causation
Here is the exciting turn. Rather than competing with causal inference, modern ML has become a power tool inside it. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

High-dimensional controls. With thousands of potential confounders, you can’t hand-pick controls. Use the Lasso to systematically select which ones are needed to make a “other things equal” comparison credible. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE). Instead of one average effect, causal forests (a causal adaptation of random forests) and BART automate the hunt for interactions — discovering for whom a treatment works best. Crucially, they are “honest”: they use separate subsamples for choosing the splits and for estimating effects, so they don’t manufacture illusory interactions (the garden of forking paths, defused by design). (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Double / debiased machine learning (DML). Use ML to flexibly learn the nuisance parts — how the outcome and the treatment each depend on the controls — then partial them out (an FWL-style residualisation) and estimate the treatment effect on what’s left. This keeps the final effect estimate valid for inference, dodging the bias that arises from plugging ML directly into a coefficient. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Prediction-policy problems. Some policy questions are genuinely predictive, not causal: targeting interventions to those most at risk — of suicide, of loan default, of dropping out. Here a good black-box predictor is exactly what’s wanted. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

This is the substance of the causal summit’s modern wing — and why prediction sits between regression and causation in this book.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Risks: bias, fishing, and gaming
Power brings hazards social scientists must manage. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Encoded bias. Models learn the prejudices in their training data — text embeddings absorb gender and racial bias from the corpus — and can propagate or amplify them. Audit and correct. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Overfitting and fishing. Flexible models excel at mistaking noise for signal; without cross-validation and a sacred test set, “discoveries” won’t replicate. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Gaming (Goodhart’s law). As The Tyranny of Metrics warns, when a measure becomes a target, it stops being a good measure — people game the predictor. Combined with the duty to report everything (every algorithm, transformation, and tuning choice) to prevent p-hacking, this is the transparency the next part demands. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading importance or a PDP as a causal effect. They explain the model, not the world. Causal claims need identification, not a high feature ranking. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Rejecting ML wholesale because it’s a black box. Interpretable-ML tools and causal-ML methods (DML, causal forests) make flexible models genuinely useful to researchers. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Plugging ML predictions straight into a regression coefficient. Naïvely using ML for inference biases the effect; DML’s cross-fitting and orthogonalisation exist precisely to fix this. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Trusting a fair-looking model on biased data. ML faithfully reproduces the discrimination in its training data. Fairness must be checked, not assumed. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Optimising a deployed metric and forgetting Goodhart. Once people know the predictor, they game its inputs; a model that worked in validation can rot in deployment. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, Machine Learning for Social & Behavioral Research — the accuracy–interpretability trade-off and why social science resists black boxes; interpretable-ML tools (feature importance, partial dependence, SHAP, LIME); interpretable ≠ causal and the need for identification (citing Chernozhukov on unconfoundedness); ML for causal questions (high-dimensional Lasso controls, causal forests / BART for honest heterogeneous effects, double/debiased ML, prediction-policy problems); the risks of encoded bias, overfitting/fishing, and metric-gaming.
James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — the flexibility–interpretability frontier and the placement of methods along it.
Angrist & Pischke — identification (randomization, IV) as the source of causal claims that ML accuracy cannot supply.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Partial dependence, defined. The partial dependence of the prediction on feature x_S averages the fitted model over the empirical distribution of the other features x_C:

\widehat{\text{PD}}_S(x_S) = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \hat f\big(x_S,\, x_{C,i}\big).

Sweeping x_S traces the marginal shape the model learned — interpretable, but still a property of \hat f, not a causal dose–response.
Why naïve ML biases an effect (the DML idea). For Y=\theta D + g(X)+\epsilon, D=m(X)+v, regressing Y on D with an ML estimate \hat g plugged in carries the regularisation bias of \hat g into \hat\theta. DML instead forms residuals \tilde Y = Y-\hat g(X) and \tilde D = D-\hat m(X) (each fit on a separate fold — cross-fitting) and regresses \tilde Y on \tilde D. The resulting

\hat\theta = \frac{\sum_i \tilde D_i \tilde Y_i}{\sum_i \tilde D_i^2}

is Neyman-orthogonal: first-order insensitive to errors in \hat g,\hat m, so it stays \sqrt n-consistent and supports valid confidence intervals. It is exactly Frisch–Waugh–Lovell partialling-out, with ML doing the partialling and sample-splitting protecting the inference.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Connections

Builds on: explaining vs predicting (the two cultures), trees & forests (the black boxes being interpreted), regularization (Lasso for high-dimensional control selection), and regression & causality (identification, which ML cannot replace).
Leads to: causal inference — causal forests, double ML, and high-dimensional controls are its modern, ML-powered wing — and to the tyranny of metrics (Goodhart, gaming, transparency).
Closes: the predictive paradigm sub-chapter; next, the dedicated time-series treatment.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text: Everything so far assumed observations were a random sample — order irrelevant, each draw independent. Time series breaks both: the data come in order, and today is correlated with yesterday. That single change voids the i.i.d. machinery, introduces a spectacular trap (spurious regression), and forces a new central concept — stationarity — before any forecasting can begin.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Why it matters
GDP, inflation, public opinion, crime rates, stock prices — vast tracts of social data arrive as time series, and the tools of the previous chapters can mislead badly when applied to them. (Enders 2014) The defining feature is temporal ordering: in a cross-section the order of rows is meaningless, but in a time series the sequence is everything, because the past affects the future. (Wooldridge 2019) This breaks the i.i.d. assumption under all of Parts II–IV, and it opens a notorious trap — regressing two unrelated trending series yields a beautiful, entirely fake result. This entry builds the foundations (dependence, stationarity, unit roots) that the forecasting entry then turns into models.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
section: What a time series is made of
text:
What a time series is made of
A series is conventionally decomposed into four components: (Enders 2014)

Trend — long-run direction (real GDP’s steady climb).
Seasonality — regular fluctuations within a year (December retail, spring housing starts).
Cycles — periodic swings longer than a year (the business cycle).
Irregular / random — the unpredictable noise left after the rest.

But the deeper feature is dependence: today’s GDP is highly correlated with yesterday’s, so the observations are emphatically not independent. (Wooldridge 2019) Quantifying that dependence is the first task.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Autocorrelation and the ACF
Autocorrelation (serial correlation) is the correlation of a series with its own past. The autocorrelation function (ACF) reports it at each lag s: (Enders 2014)

\rho_s = \frac{\gamma_s}{\gamma_0},

where \gamma_s = \operatorname{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-s}) is the autocovariance at lag s and \gamma_0 = \operatorname{Var}(y_t). Plotting \rho_s against s gives the correlogram, the basic diagnostic of a series’ memory. For a well-behaved (stationary) series the correlogram decays toward zero as lags grow — the distant past eventually stops mattering. (Enders 2014)

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
section: Stationarity: the stability a series needs
text:
Stationarity: the stability a series needs
For inference and forecasting to be possible at all, the process must be stable over time — stationary. The working definition is weak (covariance) stationarity: (Enders 2014)

\mathbb{E}[y_t] = \mu, \qquad \operatorname{Var}(y_t) = \sigma^2, \qquad
\operatorname{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-s}) = \gamma_s \text{ (depends only on the lag } s, \text{ not on } t).

In words: constant mean, constant variance, and an autocovariance that depends only on the gap between observations, not on when you look. (Enders 2014) Why insist on this? Because if a process changes its basic nature at every instant, a single historical path tells you nothing about the future. (Enders 2014) Stationarity is precisely what lets the law of large numbers and the CLT apply to time averages — restoring the ground the i.i.d. assumption used to provide.
The atom of it all is white noise: a sequence \{\epsilon_t\} with mean zero, constant variance \sigma^2, and zero autocorrelation at every non-zero lag. (Enders 2014) White noise is the purest stationary series and the building block of every model in the next entry.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
section: The random walk and the unit root
text:
The random walk and the unit root
The most important non-stationary process — and the one social data so often resembles — is the random walk: (Enders 2014)

y_t = y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t.

Each value is last period’s plus a fresh shock. Embed it in y_t = \rho_1 y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t; the random walk is the knife-edge case \rho_1 = 1, a unit root. (Enders 2014) Two consequences make it treacherous:

The variance grows with time: \operatorname{Var}(y_t) = t\sigma^2. The series has no tendency to revert to any mean — it meanders wherever the shocks push it. (Enders 2014)
Shocks are permanent. In a stationary series a shock fades; in a random walk every shock shifts all future values forever. (Enders 2014)

Add a constant and you get a random walk with drift, y_t = a_0 + y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t, combining a deterministic trend with the stochastic wandering. (Enders 2014) To test for a unit root, the Dickey–Fuller test regresses the change on the lagged level, \Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \cdots; failing to reject \gamma = 0 means a unit root is present. (Enders 2014)

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
The trap: spurious regression
Here is the warning that makes this entry essential. Regress one trending, non-stationary series on another, completely unrelated one, and you get a deceptively high R^2 and wildly significant t-statistics — pointing to a strong relationship that does not exist. (Wooldridge 2019) Both series wander under their own stochastic trends, and by sheer coincidence they appear to move together. First diagnosed by Granger and Newbold (1974), this is spurious regression, and its lesson is blunt: in time-series levels, a high R^2 is often a sign of trouble, not of a good fit. (Enders 2014)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
T = 200
y = np.cumsum(rng.normal(0, 1, T))      # random walk 1
x = np.cumsum(rng.normal(0, 1, T))      # random walk 2 — INDEPENDENT of y

# regress y on x
b1 = np.cov(x, y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(x)
b0 = y.mean() - b1 * x.mean()
fit = b0 + b1 * x
ss_res = np.sum((y - fit) ** 2); ss_tot = np.sum((y - y.mean()) ** 2)
r2 = 1 - ss_res / ss_tot
se = np.sqrt(ss_res / (T - 2) / np.sum((x - x.mean()) ** 2))
tstat = b1 / se

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.8))
axL.plot(y, color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.6, label="series Y (random walk)")
axL.plot(x, color="#d98a29", lw=1.6, label="series X (random walk)")
axL.set_title("Two independent random walks"); axL.set_xlabel("time")
axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)

axR.scatter(x, y, s=12, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6)
xs = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 30)
axR.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color="#c0392b", lw=2.2)
axR.set_title(f"Regress Y on X:  R² = {r2:.2f},  t = {tstat:.1f}")
axR.set_xlabel("X"); axR.set_ylabel("Y")
fig.suptitle("Spurious regression: a strong 'relationship' that isn't there", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Spurious regression. Left: two completely independent random walks — neither has any relationship to the other. Right: regress one on the other and OLS reports a high R² and a hugely ‘significant’ t-statistic. The relationship is pure coincidence of two wandering trends. In time-series levels, a high R² is a warning, not a triumph.





A “t of 10” and an “R^2 of 0.7” between two series that are, by construction, unrelated — that is the whole danger in one picture. Standard inference simply does not work on non-stationary levels.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
The fix: differencing and integration
The cure for a unit root is differencing. The first difference \Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} of a random walk is — by construction — just \epsilon_t, i.e. white noise, which is stationary. (Enders 2014) More generally, a series is integrated of order d, written I(d), if it must be differenced d times to become stationary: (Enders 2014)

I(0): already stationary (white noise, a stationary AR series).
I(1): one unit root — a random walk. Most highly persistent economic series (interest rates, the S&P 500) are treated as I(1). (Enders 2014)

A series made stationary by differencing is difference-stationary. Once differenced to stationarity, the honest forecasting models of the next entry — the “I” in ARIMA — apply. (Enders 2014)

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Running OLS on non-stationary levels. Spurious regression gives fake high R^2 and huge t-stats between unrelated series. Test for stationarity (Dickey–Fuller) and difference first. (Wooldridge 2019)
Treating a high R^2 as success in time series. In levels it often signals shared trends, not a real relationship — the opposite of the cross-sectional intuition. (Enders 2014)
Assuming shocks fade. In an I(1) series shocks are permanent; “it’ll revert to trend” is false for a random walk. (Enders 2014)
Ignoring autocorrelation. Serially correlated errors make ordinary standard errors wrong (too small), inflating significance — the time-series cousin of clustering. (Wooldridge 2019)
Over-differencing. Differencing an already-stationary series injects an artificial MA unit root and needless noise. Difference only as much as a unit-root test demands. (Enders 2014)

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Enders, Applied Econometric Time Series — temporal ordering and the trend/seasonality/cycle/ irregular decomposition; the ACF \rho_s=\gamma_s/\gamma_0 and the correlogram; weak (covariance) stationarity and why it underwrites inference; white noise; the random walk, the unit root (\rho_1=1), growing variance t\sigma^2, permanent shocks, drift, and the Dickey–Fuller test; spurious regression (Granger–Newbold 1974); differencing, I(d) integration, and difference-stationarity.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — time series vs cross-section and the breakdown of i.i.d.; serial correlation and its effect on inference; the spurious-regression warning.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why a random walk’s variance grows. Iterating y_t=y_{t-1}+\epsilon_t from y_0 gives y_t = y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^t \epsilon_i. With i.i.d. shocks of variance \sigma^2, \operatorname{Var}(y_t)=t\sigma^2 — unbounded in t, so no stationary variance exists. The autocovariance \operatorname{Cov}(y_t,y_{t-s})=(t-s)\sigma^2 depends on t, not just s — violating stationarity directly.
Shocks are permanent. \partial y_{t+h}/\partial \epsilon_t = 1 for every horizon h\ge 0 (the shock enters the running sum and never leaves), versus a stationary AR(1) where the effect is \rho_1^h \to 0. Permanence is the signature of a unit root.
Why spurious regression happens. Regressing one I(1) series on an independent I(1) series, the OLS t-statistic does not converge to a standard normal; it diverges with the sample size, so “significance” grows with T no matter what. The fix — differencing to I(0), or cointegration when a genuine long-run relation exists — restores standard asymptotics. Two I(1) series are cointegrated when some linear combination of them is itself I(0)/stationary: each wanders without bound, yet they wander together, tethered by a long-run equilibrium (the textbook case is a drunk and her dog — both random walks, but never far apart). When that holds, a regression in levels is no longer spurious but estimates the genuine equilibrium relation.
First-differencing a random walk. \Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} = \epsilon_t, which is white noise: mean 0, variance \sigma^2, zero autocorrelation — stationary by inspection. Hence a random walk is I(1).

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Connections

Builds on: covariance & correlation (autocorrelation is correlation with the past), the LLN & CLT (which stationarity restores for time averages), and regression diagnostics (serial correlation as a cousin of heteroskedasticity/clustering).
Leads to: forecasting with ARIMA — AR, MA, and the “I” that this entry’s differencing supplies.
Warns: any regression on time-series levels, where spurious results lurk, and connects to predictive evaluation (time-aware splits, not random folds).
Contrast: nonlinear dynamics & chaos — irregular series need not be stochastic; deterministic chaos produces aperiodic paths from a fixed rule, and sets a hard prediction horizon no model can beat.

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text: Spurious regression makes the question unavoidable: is this series stationary, or does it have a unit root? Get it right and standard inference returns; get it wrong and you either chase phantom trends or destroy real ones. But the tests that decide are non-standard (the usual t-table doesn’t apply), directional (which hypothesis is the null matters), and famously low-powered (they often can’t tell a near-unit-root from a unit root). This entry is the careful machinery behind a deceptively simple “to difference or not.”

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
Why it matters
The previous entry introduced the unit root and the one-line Dickey–Fuller test. Real data force three complications it glossed over. First, economic errors are serially correlated, so the basic test is biased. Second, the choice between trend-stationary and difference-stationary changes your long-run forecasts entirely — and the wrong “fix” injects new pathologies. Third, the tests are weak enough that a confident verdict often isn’t available. Knowing how the tests work — and how they fail — is what separates mechanical differencing from judgement.

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
The Augmented Dickey–Fuller test
The basic Dickey–Fuller regression \Delta y_t = a_0 + \gamma y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t assumes \epsilon_t is white noise. Most economic series violate that — and if the residuals are correlated, \gamma and its standard error are poorly estimated (Enders 2014). The fix is to augment the regression with lagged differences of y, which “soak up” the serial correlation until the remaining error is white noise (Enders 2014):

\Delta y_t = a_0 + \gamma\,y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=2}^{p}\beta_i\,\Delta y_{t-i+1} + \epsilon_t .

The null of a unit root is \gamma = 0 (since \gamma = \rho_1 - 1); the test statistic is the t-ratio on \gamma, but read against special critical values (below).
The three test regressions. Which deterministic terms to include is a real decision, because the critical values differ for each (Enders 2014):








Specification
Regression
Statistic




Pure random walk (no constant)
\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum \beta_i \Delta y_{t-i+1} + \epsilon_t
\tau


With drift (constant)
\Delta y_t = a_0 + \gamma y_{t-1} + \cdots
\tau_\mu


With drift & linear trend
\Delta y_t = a_0 + \gamma y_{t-1} + a_2 t + \cdots
\tau_\tau



Choosing among them is crucial and asymmetric: including unnecessary deterministic regressors reduces power, but omitting a needed one (e.g. a trend in a trending series) gives a test with zero power as the sample grows (Enders 2014). Enders’ advice: plot the series first, match the specification to what you see, and use a general-to-specific strategy — start generously lagged and prune insignificant lags (Enders 2014).

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
Why the t-table doesn’t apply
You cannot read the ADF statistic against the normal or t distribution, because their theory assumes stationary variables (Enders 2014). Under the null, the series’ variance grows without bound, the OLS estimator of \rho is biased below unity, and the statistic does not converge to a standard normal (Enders 2014). Its true distribution — the Dickey–Fuller distribution — is obtained by Monte Carlo simulation and is skewed left: for the drift case the statistic centres near -1.53, not 0, so the 5% critical value sits around -2.9 rather than the familiar -1.65 (Enders 2014). Using the wrong table massively over-rejects.

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
section: KPSS: testing stationarity as the null
text:
KPSS: testing stationarity as the null
ADF puts the unit root on trial (null = non-stationary). The KPSS test (Kwiatkowski–Phillips–Schmidt–Shin) reverses the burden of proof: its null is stationarity, the alternative a unit root (Enders 2014). Because both tests are low-powered, they are best used together, as confirmatory analysis (Enders 2014):








ADF result
KPSS result
Conclusion




Fails to reject unit root
Rejects stationarity
Strong evidence of a unit root


Rejects unit root
Fails to reject stationarity
Strong evidence of stationarity


Both fail to reject (or both reject)
—
Data “not sufficiently informative”



That last row is the honest one: sometimes the series simply cannot adjudicate between the two, and the right answer is “we can’t tell.”

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
Phillips–Perron: the nonparametric route
The Phillips–Perron (PP) test attacks the same serial-correlation problem differently. Instead of adding lagged differences, it runs the basic DF regression and applies a nonparametric correction to the statistic itself, based on the residuals (Enders 2014). PP was once favoured for series with large negative MA components, but Enders notes it generally does not perform as well as the ADF test with a modified-AIC lag choice (Enders 2014).

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
section: Two ways to be non-stationary: TS vs DS
text:
Two ways to be non-stationary: TS vs DS
A trending series can be non-stationary in two fundamentally different ways, and distinguishing them is vital for long-run forecasting (Enders 2014):

Difference-stationary (DS). An I(1) series with a stochastic trend; stationary only after differencing (y_t - y_{t-1}). Shocks are permanent.
Trend-stationary (TS). Stationary once a deterministic trend is removed (y_t - a_0 - a_2 t). Shocks are transitory — the series returns to its trend line.

The mis-specification costs run both ways (Enders 2014):

Differencing a TS series (over-differencing) injects a non-invertible (unit) root into the MA part of the model — a needless pathology that wrecks estimation.
Detrending a DS series fails to remove the stochastic trend, leaving the series still non-stationary.

You must diagnose which kind you have; you cannot safely apply both fixes.

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
The low-power problem
Here is the practical warning. Unit-root tests have low power: when a series is genuinely stationary but its largest root is close to 1, they routinely fail to reject the false unit-root null (Enders 2014). Enders’ example is sobering — with 100 observations and a true AR coefficient of 0.95, the ADF test correctly rejects a unit root only 12.7% of the time (Enders 2014). A “fail to reject” is therefore weak evidence: it may mean a unit root, or merely a persistent stationary series the test can’t resolve.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
T, n_sim = 100, 600
crit_5 = -2.89                      # DF τ_μ 5% critical value, n≈100
rhos = np.array([1.00, 0.99, 0.97, 0.95, 0.90, 0.85, 0.80, 0.70])

def df_tau(y):
    """Drift-form Dickey–Fuller t-stat on gamma, OLS by hand."""
    dy = np.diff(y)                 # Delta y_t
    ylag = y[:-1]                   # y_{t-1}
    X = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(ylag), ylag])   # [const, level]
    beta, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(X, dy, rcond=None)
    resid = dy - X @ beta
    s2 = resid @ resid / (len(dy) - 2)
    covb = s2 * np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X)
    return beta[1] / np.sqrt(covb[1, 1])            # gamma / se(gamma)

power = []
for rho in rhos:
    rejects = 0
    for _ in range(n_sim):
        y = np.empty(T); y[0] = 0.0
        e = rng.normal(0, 1, T)
        for t in range(1, T):
            y[t] = rho * y[t-1] + e[t]
        if df_tau(y) < crit_5:
            rejects += 1
    power.append(rejects / n_sim)
power = np.array(power)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.plot(rhos, power * 100, "o-", color="#1f4e79", lw=2.2, ms=6)
ax.axhline(5, color="#9aa0a6", ls=":", lw=1.2)
ax.text(0.70, 7, "test size ≈ 5%", color="#6b7280", fontsize=8.5)
ax.axvline(1.0, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.3)
ax.text(0.985, 80, "true unit root", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8.5, rotation=90, va="top")
i95 = int(np.where(rhos == 0.95)[0][0])
ax.annotate(f"ρ=0.95: rejects only {power[i95]*100:.0f}%",
            xy=(0.95, power[i95]*100), xytext=(0.86, 55),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"), fontsize=8.5, color="#c0392b")
ax.set_xlabel("true AR(1) coefficient  ρ  (1 = unit root)")
ax.set_ylabel("% of samples rejecting the unit-root null")
ax.set_title("ADF power collapses near a unit root (T = 100)")
ax.invert_xaxis(); ax.set_ylim(0, 100)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The low power of the ADF test, simulated by hand. For each true AR(1) coefficient ρ we generate 600 series of length 100, run the drift-form Dickey–Fuller regression Δy = a₀ + γ·y₋₁ + ε by hand, and reject the unit-root null when the τ-statistic falls below the 5% critical value (≈ −2.89). At ρ = 1 the rejection rate is the test’s size (~5%); as ρ falls the test should reject, but power climbs only slowly — near ρ = 0.95 it is dismal (Enders reports ~12.7%). ‘Fail to reject’ is weak evidence: a near-unit-root stationary series looks just like a unit root.

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
Structural breaks: the Perron critique
The low-power problem has a famous twist. Perron (1989) argued that many macroeconomic series are really trend-stationary with a few structural breaks, not unit-root processes (Enders 2014). The mechanism: a single permanent shift in the mean makes a stationary series look non-stationary to an ADF test, biasing it toward not rejecting the unit root (Enders 2014). Perron’s remedy is to build the known break date into the test regression with dummy variables for the pulse (DP, a one-period spike) and level (DL, a permanent shift) changes (Enders 2014):

y_t = a_0 + a_1 y_{t-1} + a_2 t + \mu_1 DP + \mu_2 DL + \epsilon_t .

The lesson generalises: what looks like a unit root may be a stable process with a break — always ask whether a regime change, not a stochastic trend, is doing the work.

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
Seasonal unit roots and SARIMA
Seasonality comes in two flavours: deterministic (captured by seasonal dummies) or stochastic (requiring seasonal differencing) (Enders 2014). For quarterly data the seasonal difference is \Delta_4 y_t = y_t - y_{t-4} (Enders 2014). The HEGY test (Hylleberg et al. 1990) distinguishes non-seasonal, semi-annual, and seasonal unit roots (Enders 2014). A model with both regular and seasonal structure is a multiplicative SARIMA, written \text{ARIMA}(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s — the uppercase (P,D,Q) are the seasonal AR, differencing, and MA orders and s the seasonal period (Enders 2014).

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading the ADF statistic off the t-table. The distribution is non-standard and left-skewed; use Dickey–Fuller critical values, or you will massively over-reject. (Enders 2014)
Omitting a needed trend term. A trending series tested without a trend regressor yields a test with zero power asymptotically. Match the specification to a plot of the data. (Enders 2014)
Treating “fail to reject” as proof of a unit root. With low power, non-rejection is weak evidence; confirm with KPSS, and accept “not informative” when that’s the truth. (Enders 2014)
Over-differencing a trend-stationary series. It plants a non-invertible MA unit root; difference only when the data are difference-stationary. (Enders 2014)
Ignoring structural breaks. A break can manufacture a spurious unit root (Perron); test for the break rather than blindly differencing. (Enders 2014)

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Enders, Applied Econometric Time Series — the ADF test and why lagged differences are added (whitening serially correlated errors); the three test regressions (\tau, \tau_\mu, \tau_\tau) and the asymmetric cost of including/omitting deterministic terms; the non-standard DF distribution (Monte-Carlo critical values, left-skew, \approx -1.53 centring); the KPSS stationarity-null test and confirmatory ADF+KPSS analysis (incl. “not sufficiently informative”); Phillips–Perron as a nonparametric alternative; trend- vs difference-stationary and the over-/under-differencing pathologies; the low-power result (12.7% at \rho=0.95, T=100); the Perron structural-break critique and its dummy-augmented regression; seasonal differencing, HEGY, and SARIMA (p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s notation.

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why \gamma = 0 is the unit-root null. Start from y_t = \rho y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t and subtract y_{t-1}: \Delta y_t = (\rho-1)y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t. A unit root \rho = 1 is exactly \gamma = 0; stationarity (|\rho|<1) is \gamma < 0. The one-sided alternative \gamma < 0 is why the DF rejection region is the left tail.
Why OLS is biased downward. Under \rho = 1 the regressor y_{t-1} is itself I(1), so the usual \sqrt T-asymptotics fail; \hat\rho converges at rate T (super-consistency) to a non-normal functional of Brownian motion, with a mean below 1 in finite samples — the Dickey–Fuller distribution. Its quantiles must be tabulated by simulation.
ADF lag choice. Choose p (number of augmenting lags) large enough that \epsilon_t is white noise — checked with the Ljung–Box test — but no larger, since extra lags cost power. Information criteria (AIC, or Enders’ preferred MAIC) or general-to-specific pruning both work.
The HEGY idea. Seasonal differencing \Delta_s = 1 - L^s factors into (1-L) and seasonal-frequency roots; HEGY tests each factor separately, so you difference only at the frequencies that actually carry a unit root — avoiding the over-differencing \Delta_s would force everywhere.

title: Unit Roots & Stationarity Testing
text:
Connections

Builds on: time-series fundamentals (unit roots, the random walk, differencing, white noise) and hypothesis testing (null, power, critical values — here all made non-standard).
Feeds: ARIMA (the test decides the “d”), and is the gateway to cointegration (which asks whether several I(1) series share a trend).
Echoes: statistical power — the low-power problem is a power problem, with unusually high stakes.

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text: ARIMA and VAR model the conditional mean. But for asset returns the mean is nearly unforecastable while the variance is strikingly predictable: calm and turbulent periods cluster. ARCH/GARCH models seize that regularity by letting today’s variance depend on yesterday’s shocks — turning “risk” from a constant nuisance into a modelled, forecastable quantity. This is the foundation of modern financial econometrics and risk management.

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
Why it matters
Classical regression assumes homoskedasticity — constant error variance (Enders 2014). For returns that assumption is simply false, and not randomly so: volatility arrives in clusters. Engle’s insight (1982, a Nobel-winning one) was that the variance could be given its own equation and forecast like a mean. The payoff is everywhere risk is priced: option valuation, Value-at-Risk, portfolio choice, and any social-science series — exchange rates, inflation, polls — where uncertainty itself ebbs and flows.

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
Volatility clustering and conditional variance
The motivating stylised fact: “large changes tend to be followed by large changes, of either sign, and small changes by small changes” — volatility clustering (Enders 2014). The key is a distinction:

Unconditional (long-run) variance — constant. Over the whole sample, the series has one variance.
Conditional variance h_t — the variance at time t given past information — time-varying and forecastable (Enders 2014).

A series with constant unconditional but time-varying conditional variance is conditionally heteroskedastic. Homoskedasticity fails for returns because rational agents use all available information — including recent volatility — to assess current risk (Enders 2014).

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
The ARCH(q) model
Engle (1982) showed you can model the mean and the variance simultaneously (Enders 2014). Write the error as

\epsilon_t = v_t\sqrt{h_t}, \qquad v_t \sim \text{white noise},\ \operatorname{Var}(v_t)=1,

so h_t is the conditional variance. The ARCH(q) variance equation makes it a function of past squared shocks (Enders 2014):

h_t = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \alpha_2 \epsilon_{t-2}^2 + \cdots + \alpha_q \epsilon_{t-q}^2 .

The mean equation (say an ARMA, y_t = a_0 + a_1 y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t) and the variance equation work in tandem: a large shock in the mean equation raises the conditional variance for subsequent periods — exactly the clustering mechanism (Enders 2014).
Testing for ARCH effects. The standard diagnostic is the McLeod–Li (1983) LM test: regress the squared residuals on a constant and q of their own lags; a significant TR^2 statistic (\sim \chi^2_q) signals ARCH errors (Enders 2014).

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
The GARCH(p,q) model
Bollerslev (1986) generalised ARCH by letting the conditional variance depend on its own past values — an ARMA for the variance (Enders 2014):

h_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{q}\alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j h_{t-j}.

GARCH(1,1) dominates practice — h_t = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1\epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 h_{t-1} — because it is extremely parsimonious, mimicking a very high-order ARCH with just three parameters (Enders 2014).

Persistence condition. For a finite, stable unconditional variance you need \sum\alpha_i + \sum\beta_j < 1 (Enders 2014).
IGARCH and long memory. If \alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 1, the model is Integrated GARCH (IGARCH): the unconditional variance is infinite, volatility shocks are permanent, and today’s variance is the best forecast of all future variances (Enders 2014). Empirically \alpha_1+\beta_1 is often just below 1 — volatility is highly persistent.



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(20)
a0, a1, b1 = 0.05, 0.10, 0.85          # persistence a1+b1 = 0.95
T = 600
h = np.empty(T); eps = np.empty(T)
h[0] = a0 / (1 - a1 - b1)              # start at the unconditional variance
for t in range(T):
    eps[t] = rng.normal(0, 1) * np.sqrt(h[t])
    if t + 1 < T:
        h[t+1] = a0 + a1 * eps[t]**2 + b1 * h[t]

fig, (axT, axB) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(7.6, 5.0), sharex=True,
                               gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": [2, 1]})
axT.plot(eps, color="#1f4e79", lw=0.8)
axT.axhline(0, color="#999", lw=0.6)
axT.set_title("GARCH(1,1) returns: volatility clusters (α₁+β₁ = 0.95)")
axT.set_ylabel("return  εₜ")

axB.plot(np.sqrt(h), color="#c0392b", lw=1.2)
uncond_sd = np.sqrt(a0 / (1 - a1 - b1))
axB.axhline(uncond_sd, color="#444", ls="--", lw=1.1)
axB.text(8, uncond_sd*1.05, "unconditional SD", fontsize=8, color="#444")
axB.set_title("Conditional volatility  √hₜ  (the forecastable part)")
axB.set_xlabel("time"); axB.set_ylabel("√hₜ")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A GARCH(1,1) process simulated by hand (α₀=0.05, α₁=0.10, β₁=0.85, so persistence α₁+β₁=0.95). Top: the returns εₜ — note the clusters, stretches of violent swings interleaved with calm, exactly the ‘large changes follow large changes’ stylised fact. Bottom: the conditional standard deviation √hₜ that generated them, rising after big shocks and decaying slowly back. The mean is unforecastable noise; the variance is highly structured and predictable — which is the whole point of GARCH.

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
Estimation by maximum likelihood
ARCH/GARCH models are non-linear and cannot be estimated by OLS (Enders 2014). They are fit by maximum likelihood (usually assuming conditional normality), maximising the log-likelihood over the mean and variance parameters simultaneously, with a non-linear search algorithm (BFGS, simplex) to find the peak (Enders 2014).

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
Asymmetry: the leverage effect
Another stylised fact: the leverage effect — bad news (negative shocks) raises volatility more than good news of the same size (Enders 2014). Symmetric GARCH (which uses \epsilon_{t-1}^2, blind to sign) misses this, so two extensions add asymmetry:

TARCH / GJR-GARCH — add a dummy d_{t-1} = 1 when \epsilon_{t-1} < 0: h_t = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1\epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \lambda_1 d_{t-1}\epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 h_{t-1}. If \lambda_1 > 0, negative shocks hit harder (Enders 2014).
EGARCH (Nelson 1991) — model \ln h_t, which keeps h_t > 0 automatically (no non-negativity constraints on the coefficients) and captures asymmetry through standardised shocks (Enders 2014).

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
section: ARCH-M: when risk enters the mean
text:
ARCH-M: when risk enters the mean
The ARCH-in-Mean (ARCH-M) model lets the conditional mean depend on the conditional variance (Enders 2014) — the econometric form of “no risk premium without risk”:

y_t = \beta + \delta h_t + \epsilon_t, \qquad \delta > 0 .

Risk-averse investors demand a higher expected return when risk is high, so a shock raises h_t, which raises the required risk premium \delta h_t (Enders 2014). It closes the loop between the two equations — variance feeds the mean.

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Modelling variance before the mean is right. ARCH tests on the residuals of a misspecified mean equation can falsely signal ARCH; get the mean model right first. (Enders 2014)
Forgetting the persistence constraint. If \sum\alpha+\sum\beta \ge 1 the unconditional variance is infinite (IGARCH); check it before trusting long-horizon variance forecasts. (Enders 2014)
Using symmetric GARCH on equity returns. The leverage effect is real; a symmetric model understates the volatility surge after bad news — use GJR/EGARCH. (Enders 2014)
Estimating with OLS. These are non-linear; OLS is invalid — use MLE. (Enders 2014)
Assuming normality blindly. Returns are fat-tailed even after GARCH; consider a t-distributed conditional density (Enders notes the normal is the usual but not mandatory choice).

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Enders, Applied Econometric Time Series — homoskedasticity’s failure for returns and volatility clustering; the conditional vs unconditional variance distinction; Engle’s ARCH(q) (\epsilon_t=v_t\sqrt{h_t}, the squared-shock variance equation, mean/variance interaction) and the McLeod–Li LM test; Bollerslev’s GARCH(p,q), the dominance and parsimony of GARCH(1,1), the persistence condition \sum\alpha+\sum\beta<1 and IGARCH (infinite unconditional variance, permanent shocks); MLE estimation via non-linear search; the leverage effect with TARCH/GJR (the d_{t-1} dummy) and EGARCH (modelling \ln h_t); and ARCH-M with the risk-premium term \delta h_t.

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Unconditional variance of GARCH(1,1). Take expectations of h_t = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1\epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 h_{t-1}. Since \mathbb{E}[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] = \mathbb{E}[h_{t-1}] = \sigma^2 in the stationary state, \sigma^2 = \alpha_0 + (\alpha_1+\beta_1)\sigma^2, giving

\sigma^2 = \frac{\alpha_0}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1}.

This is positive and finite iff \alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1 — the persistence condition. At \alpha_1+\beta_1 = 1 the denominator is zero: the unconditional variance diverges (IGARCH).
Why GARCH(1,1) ≈ ARCH(∞). Recursively substitute h_{t-1}: h_t = \alpha_0(1+\beta_1+\beta_1^2+\cdots) + \alpha_1\sum_{k\ge 0}\beta_1^{k}\epsilon_{t-1-k}^2. The single \beta_1 encodes a geometrically declining weight on all past squared shocks — an infinite ARCH captured by one extra parameter. That is the source of its parsimony.
Volatility forecasting. The s-step-ahead forecast of the conditional variance reverts to the unconditional level: \mathbb{E}_t[h_{t+s}] = \sigma^2 + (\alpha_1+\beta_1)^{s}(h_{t+1}-\sigma^2), decaying at rate (\alpha_1+\beta_1). Near 1 (high persistence) the decay is slow — calm and storm both last.

title: Volatility: ARCH & GARCH Models
text:
Connections

Builds on: ARIMA (the mean equation), regression diagnostics (heteroskedasticity — here modelled rather than corrected), and maximum likelihood (estimation).
Completes: the Enders time-series sub-track — fundamentals → unit roots → ARIMA → VAR → cointegration → volatility.
Relates to: risk and uncertainty quantification, and the bias–variance theme — here the variance itself is the object of prediction.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text: Every design in this Part rests on one untestable assumption — CIA, exclusion, continuity, parallel trends. The mature applied researcher exploits the fact that these assumptions fail in different ways: combine two designs and you can survive the failure of either; run several and a conclusion is believable only when independent strategies — each with its own flaws — agree. This is the orchestration the Furious Five were building toward: not picking a weapon, but commanding the arsenal.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Why it matters
A single design is a single point of failure. (Cunningham 2021) The credibility revolution’s mature phase is triangulation: because matching’s selection-on-observables, IV’s exclusion, RD’s continuity, and DiD’s parallel trends are logically independent bets, evidence that converges across them is far harder to dismiss than any one estimate. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) This entry is the synthesis the chapter has been pointing at — and the causal-design cousin of mixed-methods triangulation in Part VI: many imperfect lenses, trusted where they coincide.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Two ways to combine
There are two distinct moves, and it’s worth keeping them separate: (Cunningham 2021)

Stacking — strengthen identification. Layer designs so the combined estimator needs a weaker assumption than either alone (matching and DiD; an outcome model and a propensity model).
Triangulating — test robustness. Run designs side by side; if independent strategies disagree, at least one assumption is failing, and you learn which story is fragile.

The rest of the entry is the standard combinations, then the ethic that ties them together.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
section: Bracketing: when two biased designs bound the truth
text:
Bracketing: when two biased designs bound the truth
The most elegant case: two designs biased in opposite directions sandwich the real effect. Angrist & Pischke’s bracketing result pairs fixed effects with a lagged-dependent-variable (Lagged-DV) model: (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

Fixed effects assumes selection is on a time-invariant unobserved trait \alpha_i — and tends to over-control, biasing one way.
Lagged-DV assumes selection is on the past outcome Y_{i,t-1} — and tends to under-control, biasing the other way.

Because they get selection wrong from opposite sides, the true effect is typically bounded between them. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Report both: if they’re close, you’ve bracketed the answer to a narrow range; if they’re far apart, the data can’t yet pin it down — itself an honest finding. The figure shows this, plus the broader triangulation picture.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

truth = 1.0
# (label, estimate, std error)  — illustrative
rows = [
    ("Naive comparison",      2.05, 0.10),
    ("Fixed effects (upper)", 1.28, 0.12),
    ("Lagged-DV (lower)",     0.74, 0.12),
    ("Matching + DiD",        1.05, 0.14),
    ("IV (LATE)",             0.98, 0.20),
    ("Triple-difference",     1.08, 0.16),
    ("Doubly robust",         1.02, 0.11),
]
labels = [r[0] for r in rows]
est = np.array([r[1] for r in rows])
se = np.array([r[2] for r in rows])
y = np.arange(len(rows))[::-1]
colors = ["#c0392b", "#1f3a5f", "#1f3a5f", "#1f8a4c", "#1f8a4c", "#1f8a4c", "#1f8a4c"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.3))
# bracket band between FE and Lagged-DV
ax.axvspan(0.74, 1.28, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.07)
ax.axvline(truth, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="true effect")
ax.errorbar(est, y, xerr=1.96 * se, fmt="o", color="none", ecolor="#666", capsize=3, zorder=2)
for xi, yi, c in zip(est, y, colors):
    ax.plot(xi, yi, "o", color=c, ms=8, zorder=3)
ax.text(0.755, len(rows)-0.5, "FE / Lagged-DV bracket", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=8)
ax.set_yticks(y); ax.set_yticklabels(labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("estimated treatment effect")
ax.set_title("Different designs, different biases — believe the convergence")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Bracketing and triangulation. A forest plot of the same effect estimated many ways. The naive comparison (red) is badly biased. Fixed effects and Lagged-DV bias from opposite directions and BRACKET the truth (green line) between them (shaded band). The credible designs — matching, IV, DiD, triple-difference, doubly-robust — cluster tightly around the truth: independent strategies with different flaws converging is what makes a causal claim believable.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Doubly robust estimation (AIPW)
The cleanest stacking result marries the two selection-on-observables tools — an outcome-regression model and a propensity-score model — into a single doubly robust (DR) estimator, usually Augmented Inverse-Propensity Weighting (AIPW). (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Its near-magical property: the estimate is consistent if either working model is correctly specified — you get two shots at the truth and only need to hit one. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) This is exactly the engine under double/debiased ML: use flexible ML for both nuisance functions, lean on Neyman-orthogonality so small errors in each don’t bite, and cross-fit to kill overfitting. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Doubly robust is the formal reason DML is trustworthy — it tolerates getting one half wrong.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Matching + DiD: conditional parallel trends
Parallel trends is a strong, population-wide bet. Matching + DiD (“conditional DiD”) weakens it: match treated and control units on pre-treatment covariates first, then difference. (Cunningham 2021) Now you need only conditional parallel trends — trends parallel within cells of X, not for the whole population. (Cunningham 2021) Mechanically, matching is preprocessing to balance covariates and prune incomparable units; the DiD runs on the matched sample. It’s a clean stack: matching handles the level differences in observables, DiD differences out the fixed unobservables, and the remaining assumption is the most defensible version of parallel trends.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
