text:
Triple differences (DDD)
What if you fear a confounding trend that DiD can’t remove — a shock hitting the treated group (not just the treated units) at treatment time? Add a third difference. (Cunningham 2021) Difference-in-difference-in-differences (DDD) introduces a within-group control — an unaffected subgroup in the same place and time — to net out group-specific shocks.






NoteGruber’s maternity mandate (the textbook DDD)



Gruber (1994) studied state maternity-benefit mandates’ effect on wages. A plain DiD (affected states vs others, before vs after) would be confounded by any state-level economic trend coinciding with the law. The fix: a third difference — compare the wages of those targeted by the mandate (women of childbearing age) to an untargeted group (men and older women) within the same states. The triple interaction nets out both general state trends and national shifts for the targeted demographic, isolating the mandate’s effect. (Cunningham 2021)


The DDD effect is the coefficient on the triple interaction (\text{group}\times\text{time}\times\text{treated}) — one more difference, one fewer thing to assume away.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
RD + DiD, and synthetic DiD
Two more combinations from the modern frontier: (Cunningham 2021; Baker et al. 2025)

Difference-in-discontinuities fuses RD with DiD: compare the jump at a cutoff across time periods, so anything else that changes discontinuously at the threshold (and would bias a plain RD) differences out. (Cunningham 2021)
Synthetic DiD (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021) blends synthetic control with DiD: use synthetic-control weights to build a well-matched comparison and the DiD double difference with unit and time fixed effects — gaining robustness and sidestepping the staggered-adoption “forbidden comparisons.” (Baker et al. 2025)

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
The credibility ethic
Underneath all of it is a stance: the researcher as skeptical judge of their own claim. (Cunningham 2021) Two habits express it:

Robustness checks. Show the result survives reasonable changes — different bandwidths in RD, alternative instruments, added state-specific trends in DiD. A finding that evaporates under a defensible tweak was never solid. (Cunningham 2021)
Independent agreement. A conclusion is most trustworthy when independent designs, each with different flaws, converge. (Cunningham 2021) Lee’s (2008) incumbency RD earned its credibility partly because the effect showed the predicted pattern across multiple correlated outcomes — internal triangulation that an artefact would be unlikely to fake. (Cunningham 2021)

The lesson that closes the design toolkit: don’t ask “which design?” — ask “do my designs agree, and where do they disagree?”

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Combining two bad designs. Stacking strengthens identification only if each part is plausible; garbage plus garbage is still garbage. (Cunningham 2021)
Reporting only the design that “worked.” Cherry-picking among strategies is p-hacking in a new costume. Report the set, and explain disagreements. (Cunningham 2021)
Reading a tight bracket as proof. FE and Lagged-DV bound the effect only under their respective selection stories; agreement is reassuring, not conclusive. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Assuming doubly robust means assumption-free. AIPW/DML still need unconfoundedness and overlap; it buys robustness to model specification, not to a broken design. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Conditional parallel trends taken for granted. Matching + DiD relaxes parallel trends but doesn’t abolish it — defend the conditional version. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, The Mixtape — designs rest on different untestable assumptions; matching + DiD (conditional parallel trends); triple differences and Gruber (1994); difference-in-discontinuities; the skeptical-judge credibility ethic, robustness checks, and independent-agreement (Lee 2008 across correlated outcomes).
Angrist & Pischke — the bracketing property of fixed effects vs lagged-dependent-variable models (opposite selection assumptions bound the effect).
Chernozhukov et al. — doubly robust / AIPW (consistent if either the outcome or propensity model is correct) and its realisation as double/debiased ML with cross-fitting.
DiD Practitioner’s Guide — synthetic DiD (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021) combining synthetic-control weighting with DiD and robustness to staggered-adoption forbidden comparisons.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Doubly robust / AIPW. With propensity \hat e(X) and outcome models \hat\mu_d(X)=\hat{\mathbb
E}[Y\mid D=d,X], the AIPW estimate of the ATE is 
\hat\tau_{\text{DR}}=\frac1n\sum_i\Big[\hat\mu_1(X_i)-\hat\mu_0(X_i)
+\frac{D_i\,(Y_i-\hat\mu_1(X_i))}{\hat e(X_i)}
-\frac{(1-D_i)(Y_i-\hat\mu_0(X_i))}{1-\hat e(X_i)}\Big].
 If the outcome models are right, the augmentation terms have mean zero and the regression part is consistent; if instead the propensity is right, the weighting part is consistent and the regression errors cancel. Either suffices — the double robustness. The estimating equation is Neyman-orthogonal, which is why DML plugs ML into \hat e,\hat\mu and keeps \sqrt n-inference.
Triple differences. With group g\in\{targeted, untargeted\}, state s, time t, the saturated model’s effect is the triple interaction 
Y_{gst}=\dots+\beta_{\text{DDD}}\,(\text{Targeted}_g\times\text{Post}_t\times\text{Treated}_s)+\varepsilon,
 which differences out (i) state \times time shocks common to both groups and (ii) group \times time shifts common to all states — identifying \beta_{\text{DDD}} under a weaker parallel-trends assumption than 2×2 DiD (the two ordinary DiDs would have been biased by the same nuisance trend, which the third difference removes).
Why bracketing works. If true selection mixes a time-invariant component and a past-outcome component, FE (controlling only the former) and Lagged-DV (controlling only the latter) misattribute the other component in opposite directions, so \hat\beta_{\text{Lagged-DV}}\le\beta\le\hat\beta_{\text{FE}} (or the reverse) — the truth is sandwiched. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Connections

Builds on: all of the Furious Five — matching, IV, RD, DiD, panel/FE — plus synthetic control and causal ML / DML (doubly robust).
Leads to: sensitivity analysis & external validity (the other half of credibility — how fragile, how generalisable) and the field-realities ethic of triangulation and honest robustness.
Synthesises: the design toolkit — from “what is an effect?” to orchestrating many imperfect designs into one credible claim.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text: A DAG is a diagram of what causes what, drawn before you touch the data. Its power is that it turns the murky question “which variables should I control for?” into a mechanical rule. Confounders open backdoor paths that must be closed; colliders are dams that you must not open. The same graph that justifies a control exposes the controls that would manufacture bias — Part III’s good-vs-bad controls, now derivable rather than memorised.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Why it matters
Randomization is the gold standard, but most questions can’t be randomized — you can’t assign people a gender, a recession, or a war. Then you must reason about confounding explicitly, and a directed acyclic graph is the tool for it: a transparent statement of your causal assumptions that makes the consequences for estimation derivable. (Cunningham 2021) DAGs are how Cunningham bridges theory and econometrics — and they settle, with a rule instead of an argument, the question that ended Part III: which controls help, and which ones hurt?

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: The intuitive picture: causal influence and adjustment
text:
The intuitive picture: causal influence and adjustment
Before the formal machinery, the plain-language version — which is how Daniel Kaplan introduces DAGs in Lessons in Statistical Thinking. A DAG is a network of causal influence: each arrow carries influence from cause to effect, and “acyclic” keeps that influence flowing one way, so the reasoning is a clean chain of deduction rather than a circular argument. (Kaplan 2024) A confounder is then easy to see — it is a variable that sends influence down two arrows at once, manufacturing a non-causal association between your explanatory variable and your response and so disguising the true effect size. (Kaplan 2024)
The cure is adjustment — and it is literally the same move as holding a confounder constant in regression. To adjust for a covariate is to account for the variation it injects, blocking the non-causal flow so that what remains is the signal you want, separated from the noise. (Kaplan 2024) That is the bridge worth holding onto: “accounting for variation” in Part III and “closing a backdoor” here are one act seen from two sides. What the rest of this entry adds is the precision Kaplan’s intuition can’t supply on its own — exactly which covariates to adjust for, and the cases where adjusting creates bias instead of removing it.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Reading the graph
A DAG has three ingredients: (Cunningham 2021)

Nodes are random variables.
Directed edges (arrows) are direct causal effects: D\to Y means D causes Y. The absence of an arrow is the strongest claim of all — a firm theoretical commitment that no direct effect exists.
Acyclic means no feedback loops: causation flows one way. Reverse causality isn’t drawn as a cycle but by splitting the variable across time (Y_1, Y_2), so the arrows still point forward.

Behind the picture sits a structural causal model — a set of equations in which each variable is generated by its parents (the nodes with arrows into it) plus a random shock. (Cunningham 2021) The graph is the qualitative skeleton of that data-generating process; drawing it forces you to make your assumptions visible and falsifiable.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: Three structures, and how association flows
text:
Three structures, and how association flows
Every DAG is built from three elementary configurations, and association (statistical correlation, causal or not) flows through them differently: (Cunningham 2021)

The chain / mediator D\to M\to Y. D affects Y only through M. Association flows along the chain — that flow is the causal effect.
The fork / confounder D\leftarrow Z\to Y. A common cause Z drives both. This creates a non-causal association between D and Y: they move together because they share an origin, not because one causes the other.
The collider D\to C\leftarrow Y. Two arrows collide into a common effect. Here D and Y are independent — until you condition on C.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Paths, backdoors, and the dam
A path is any sequence of edges connecting D and Y, ignoring arrow direction. The one you want is the direct/causal path D\to Y. The troublemakers are backdoor paths — any path that leaves D through an incoming arrow, like D\leftarrow Z\to Y. (Cunningham 2021) A backdoor path is a pipe through which confounding flows, manufacturing correlation that isn’t cause.
Whether a path transmits association depends on what’s on it — the intuition of d-separation: (Cunningham 2021)

A path through a confounder (a fork) is open: association flows. Conditioning on that confounder blocks it.
A path through a collider is naturally blocked. The collider is a dam: it stops the flow — unless you condition on it (or its descendant), which opens the dam and lets spurious association through.

This is the crucial asymmetry. Conditioning closes forks but opens colliders. Controlling is not free; it changes which pipes are open.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: The backdoor criterion: the rule for controls
text:
The backdoor criterion: the rule for controls
Putting it together gives the backdoor criterion, the formal rule for what to condition on: a set of variables identifies the causal effect of D on Y if it (Cunningham 2021)

closes every backdoor path (block each one by conditioning on a non-collider along it), and
opens no new path (never condition on a collider or a descendant of one).

Satisfy it and the leftover association between D and Y is purely causal. This is selection-on-observables stated as graph surgery: the “controls” you need are exactly the ones that shut the backdoors, and not one variable more.


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
pos = {"D": (0.15, 0.30), "Y": (0.85, 0.30), "Z": (0.50, 0.82), "C": (0.50, 0.04)}
for name, (x, y) in pos.items():
    color = {"D": "#1f3a5f", "Y": "#1f3a5f", "Z": "#c0392b", "C": "#d98a29"}[name]
    ax.add_patch(plt.Circle((x, y), 0.055, color=color, zorder=3))
    ax.text(x, y, name, color="white", ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", zorder=4)

def arrow(a, b, color, style="-|>", lw=2.0, rad=0.0):
    ax.annotate("", xy=pos[b], xytext=pos[a],
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle=style, color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=16, shrinkB=16,
                                connectionstyle=f"arc3,rad={rad}"), zorder=2)

arrow("D", "Y", "#1f8a4c", lw=2.8)      # causal effect of interest
arrow("Z", "D", "#c0392b")               # confounder -> D
arrow("Z", "Y", "#c0392b")               # confounder -> Y  (backdoor path D<-Z->Y)
arrow("D", "C", "#d98a29")               # D -> collider
arrow("Y", "C", "#d98a29")               # Y -> collider

ax.text(0.50, 0.40, "causal effect", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=9, ha="center")
ax.text(0.50, 0.62, "backdoor path (close it: condition on Z)", color="#c0392b",
        fontsize=8.5, ha="center")
ax.text(0.50, 0.13, "collider — a dam (do NOT condition on C)", color="#b9791f",
        fontsize=8.5, ha="center")
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.0); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("Confounder vs collider: what to control for")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A DAG with both a confounder and a collider. The causal effect of interest is D → Y (green). Z is a confounder opening the backdoor path D ← Z → Y (red), which you must close by conditioning on Z. C is a collider (D → C ← Y): it sits on no backdoor path and is naturally blocked, so conditioning on it would OPEN a spurious path and manufacture bias. The backdoor criterion here: control for Z, never for C.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: Good vs bad controls, now derivable
text:
Good vs bad controls, now derivable
The graph delivers Part III’s control rules as theorems: (Cunningham 2021)

Conditioning on a mediator M in D\to M\to Y blocks the very causal flow you want, stripping out the indirect effect and under-stating the total impact. This is over-control — the occupation-in-the-wage-regression mistake, now visibly a blocked chain.
Conditioning on a collider C opens a closed path and invents association between variables that were independent. This is selection/collider bias — the gravest control error.


Mediation: when the mechanism is the question
Sometimes you want the path through M — you ask not just whether D affects Y but why and how much through which channel. That is mediation analysis, which decomposes the total effect into a direct effect (D\to Y, not through M) and an indirect effect (the part flowing D\to M\to Y). (Gerber and Green 2012) The DAG makes the stakes precise, and they cut two ways:

To estimate the total effect, never condition on the mediator — that is the over-control above, blocking the indirect channel and biasing the total toward zero. (Cunningham 2021)
To estimate the direct/indirect split, you must reason about M — but naively “controlling for M” does not cleanly identify the direct effect, because M is a post-treatment variable and is typically a collider linking D to the unobserved causes of Y. Conditioning on it reopens a backdoor and manufactures bias.

This is why Gerber & Green warn that mediation is one of the most misused techniques in social science: the intuitive “regress Y on D and M” (Baron–Kenny-style) silently assumes the mediator is itself unconfounded with the outcome — an assumption as strong as, and separate from, the one that justified the experiment on D. (Gerber and Green 2012) Credible mechanism work needs experimental manipulation of the mediator itself, or explicit, defended assumptions about M’s confounders — not a third variable bolted into the regression. (Gerber and Green 2012) The graph turns a tempting shortcut into a visible warning.






NoteTwo colliders worth memorising (Cunningham)



Google and the gender pay gap. Google argued that once you control for occupation (plus location, tenure, role), the gender gap disappears. But occupation is a collider: discrimination affects which jobs women get (\text{gender}\to\text{occupation}) and so does unobserved ability (\text{ability}\to\text{occupation}). Conditioning on occupation opens a path between gender and ability and can mask real discrimination. The “control” that made the gap vanish is exactly the one the backdoor criterion forbids. (Cunningham 2021)
The movie-star collider (Cunningham, crediting Gabriel Rossman). Why do film stars seem to be either gorgeous or talented, rarely both? In the population, beauty and talent are independent. But stardom is a collider: \text{beauty}\to\text{star}\leftarrow\text{talent}. Look only at stars (condition on the collider) and a spurious negative correlation appears — an untalented star must be beautiful, a plain star must be talented. Pure selection bias, manufactured by conditioning. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: Intervention and the front door: Pearl’s do-operator
text:
Intervention and the front door: Pearl’s do-operator
Cunningham’s backdoor language and Judea Pearl’s are two dialects of one theory; Pearl’s Primer supplies the piece that makes “cause” a verb. To intervene is written with the do-operator, do(X=x), and it is sharply different from merely observing X=x. Conditioning on a value “narrows our focus” to the cases where X happened to equal x — it changes your perception. Intervening “changes the system”: you reach in and set X, overriding whatever normally produces it. (Pearl et al. 2016) Formally, do(x) is graph surgery — erase every arrow pointing into X, which “emancipates it from all other influences,” so any remaining association with Y is genuinely causal. (Pearl et al. 2016) The backdoor criterion is precisely the condition under which the surgical \Pr(Y\mid do(x)) can be rewritten as ordinary, estimable conditional probabilities — seeing used to compute doing. (Pearl et al. 2016)

When every backdoor is blocked for you: the front-door
Sometimes there is an incurable unobserved confounder U between X and Y — no backdoor set exists, because the variable you’d need to adjust for is unmeasured. Pearl’s front-door criterion is the escape: if the effect of X on Y runs entirely through an observed mediator Z that is itself shielded from the confounder, you can still identify the effect — without any data on U. (Pearl et al. 2016)
His example is the 1950s smoking debate. The tobacco industry posited a “smoking gene” U that caused both the craving to smoke (X) and cancer (Y), which would make the observed link spurious. Pearl’s move: measure tar deposits Z, on the assumptions that smoking causes cancer only through tar (X\to Z\to Y) and the gene does not affect tar. Then estimate X\to Z and Z\to Y (adjusting for X), and chain them together. (Pearl et al. 2016) You have deconfounded U using only observed variables — what Pearl calls an “utter miracle.” (Pearl et al. 2016) The front door is rarer in practice than the backdoor (its “effect flows only through Z” assumption is demanding), but it is the proof that a credible mechanism can rescue identification where adjustment alone is helpless.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




“Control for everything you have.” Each control can close a backdoor or open a collider. Conditioning is surgery, not insurance. (Cunningham 2021)
Conditioning on a collider (or its descendant). It creates association from nothing — the subtlest bias in applied work, and invisible to R^2 or significance. (Cunningham 2021)
Controlling for a mediator when you want the total effect. You block the channel you meant to measure and under-state the effect. (Cunningham 2021)
Drawing the DAG to fit the result. The graph encodes assumptions; its missing arrows are claims. Draw it from theory, before estimation, and defend the absences. (Cunningham 2021)
Believing a DAG removes unobserved confounding. It only tells you what to condition on given the assumed structure; an omitted common cause you didn’t draw is still a backdoor left open. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, Causal Inference: The Mixtape — DAGs as a causal language bridging theory and econometrics; nodes/arrows, the meaning of a missing arrow, acyclicity, and the underlying structural causal model; the three structures — chain/mediator D\to M\to Y, fork/confounder D\leftarrow Z\to Y, collider D\to C\leftarrow Y; paths, backdoor paths, open vs blocked and colliders as dams (d-separation); the backdoor criterion; over-control from conditioning on a mediator and selection bias from conditioning on a collider; the Google/occupation gender-gap example and the movie-star (beauty → star ← talent) collider.
Kaplan, Lessons in Statistical Thinking — the intuitive on-ramp: a DAG as a network of causal influence, acyclicity as one-way deductive reasoning; a confounder as a variable that injects non-causal association; and adjustment (covariate adjustment) as the act of accounting for variation that blocks the confounding flow — the same move as regression’s “holding constant,” seen on the graph.
Pearl, Glymour & Jewell, Causal Inference in Statistics: A Primer — the do-operator do(x) and intervention as graph surgery (erase arrows into X); intervening (“changes the system”) vs conditioning (“narrows our focus”); the back-door criterion stated formally; and the front-door criterion with the smoking → tar → cancer example that deconfounds an unmeasured “smoking gene” — the “utter miracle.”

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The backdoor adjustment formula. If a set Z satisfies the backdoor criterion for D\to Y, the causal effect is identified by conditioning then averaging over Z: 
\Pr(Y\mid \operatorname{do}(D=d)) = \sum_{z}\Pr(Y\mid D=d, Z=z)\,\Pr(Z=z),
 where \operatorname{do}(D=d) denotes setting D by intervention (not merely observing it). In means, \text{ATE}=\mathbb E_Z\big[\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1,Z]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0,Z]\big] — exactly the subclassification estimator of the next entry. The criterion is what licenses writing the causal \operatorname{do}-probability as ordinary conditional probabilities you can estimate.
Why a collider induces dependence (algebra). Let D\perp Y marginally and C=\alpha D+\beta
Y+\varepsilon. Then \operatorname{Cov}(D,Y)=0, but conditional on C=c we have \beta Y \approx c-\alpha D-\varepsilon, so within a stratum of C a higher D forces a lower Y — a nonzero conditional covariance created purely by conditioning. This is the same algebra as the collider figure in Part III, now located on the graph.
Acyclicity and solvability. Acyclicity guarantees the structural equations can be ordered topologically and solved recursively (each variable from its parents), so the joint distribution factorises as \prod_i \Pr(X_i\mid \text{parents}(X_i)) — the Markov factorisation that makes d-separation read off the graph correspond to real conditional independence in the data.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Connections

Builds on: regression and causality (good/bad controls and collider bias, now derived) and potential outcomes (the backdoor adjustment is the conditional version of the selection-bias fix).
Leads to: matching & propensity scores (estimating the backdoor adjustment when the criterion holds) and the quasi-experimental designs (IV, RD, DiD) for when it doesn’t — when an unblockable backdoor remains.
Reappears in: the IV graph (an instrument Z\to D\to Y with no Z\to Y arrow) and the bad-control warnings throughout Part V.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text: The summit of the whole manual, and the centre of gravity of the source library. The question is deceptively simple — what is the effect of X on Y? — and answering it honestly requires a framework for imagining the world that didn’t happen, plus the design and computational tools built in every part before this one.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
The ladder of causation
Judea Pearl gives the whole summit its organizing picture: the Ladder of Causation, a three-rung hierarchy of the questions you can ask, each strictly above the last. (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018)

Association — seeing. “What if I see…?” Find regularities in data: a customer who buys toothpaste also buys floss. This is the rung of correlation, ordinary statistics, and “model-blind” machine learning — it can predict, but it cannot say why. (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018) Everything in Parts I–IV lives here.
Intervention — doing. “What if I do…?” Predict the effect of an action that changes the world — raise the price, administer the drug. Pearl’s line: “seeing smoke tells us a totally different story about the likelihood of fire than making smoke.” (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018) This Part is the climb to rung 2.
Counterfactuals — imagining. “What if I had done…?” and “Why?” Reason about the world that didn’t happen for a specific unit — comparing reality to a “fictitious and contradictory world.” This, Pearl argues, is what most distinguishes human reasoning from a pattern-matcher. (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018)

The rungs are a one-way gate: “you cannot answer a question on rung two… using only data collected from rung one.” (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018) No amount of correlation becomes causation on its own, because “data are profoundly dumb about causal relationships” — they need a push from a causal model (a DAG, a potential-outcomes assumption, or a design). That push is exactly what this Part supplies. Read the rest of the summit as the engineering of the climb from rung 1 to rungs 2 and 3.






TipLevel



graduate. The deepest theme: Cunningham’s Mixtape, Gerber & Green, Mostly Harmless, Mastering ’Metrics, Panel Data and Experimetrics all converge here.

The “Furious Five”
The applied core, as Angrist & Pischke frame it: regression/matching adjustment, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences (with panel fixed effects as their backbone). Each is a different argument for why a comparison is fair — a different way of approximating the experiment you wish you could have run.
