title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
Where this leads
Down from the summit to Part VI · Doing Research in the World: having learned what a credible causal claim is, you learn what survives contact with measurement, incentives, and a client who needs an answer by Friday.

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text: Sometimes treatment isn’t random, but it is as-good-as-random once you condition on what you observe. That bet — the conditional independence assumption — is the backdoor criterion cashed out as an estimator. Matching and propensity scores build the missing counterfactual by pairing the treated with comparable untreated units, approximating the experiment you couldn’t run. The catch never goes away: they balance only what you measured, so the assumption is an argument, never a proof.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Why it matters
The DAG told you which variables close the backdoors; this entry is how you actually adjust for them to recover an effect. (Cunningham 2021) It matters because “selection on observables” is the workhorse of applied social science — the strategy behind most “we controlled for X, Y, Z” papers — and doing it as matching rather than a single regression makes its assumptions, and its weaknesses, impossible to hide. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The reward is a transparent approximation of an experiment; the price is honesty about the one assumption that can’t be tested.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
The two conditions
Everything rests on two requirements: (Cunningham 2021)

Conditional Independence Assumption (CIA) — also unconfoundedness or ignorability: 
\big(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)\big)\ \perp\ D_i \ \big|\ X_i.
 Within cells of the observed covariates X, treatment is as good as randomly assigned — independent of the potential outcomes. It is randomization’s independence, but only conditional on X. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Common support / overlap — for every covariate value, some chance of either arm: 0 < \Pr(D_i=1\mid X_i) < 1. If a kind of person is always treated (or never), they have no counterpart in the other group and no comparison is possible for them. (Cunningham 2021)

CIA is the identifying assumption; overlap is what makes it operational. Both must hold.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
section: Subclassification: the idea in its purest form
text:
Subclassification: the idea in its purest form
The most transparent estimator simply stratifies. Partition the sample into cells (strata) of X; within a cell, units are alike on X, so under CIA the treated–control difference is causal; then average those within-cell differences, weighting by each stratum’s share of the population. That weighted average is the ATE — the backdoor adjustment formula made arithmetic. (Cunningham 2021)






NoteCunningham’s Titanic



Did riding first class raise your chance of surviving the Titanic? The naive survival gap (the simple difference in outcomes) is 35.4 percentage points. But first-class passengers were disproportionately women and the better-off — and “women and children first” plus age drove survival directly. Stratify on age and gender, take the within-stratum first-class effect, and reweight: the estimate falls to 16.1 points. (Cunningham 2021) Less than half the naive gap was the cabin; the rest was who was in it — selection bias, removed by conditioning on the confounders the DAG flagged.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
section: Matching, and why many covariates break it
text:
Matching, and why many covariates break it
Matching generalises the idea: impute each unit’s missing counterfactual from the closest units in the other arm. (Cunningham 2021)

Exact matching pairs a treated unit with controls having identical X — perfect, when feasible.
Nearest-neighbour matching pairs with the closest available X when exact matches run out.

The wall it hits is the curse of dimensionality. With many covariates, the space is vast and the data sparse: cells empty out, exact matches vanish, and common support fails — you simply can’t find a treated and a control unit in most cells. (Cunningham 2021) Matching on more than a few covariates at once is infeasible in finite samples. This is the problem the propensity score was invented to dissolve.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
section: The propensity score: one number to balance them all
text:
The propensity score: one number to balance them all
The propensity score is the probability of treatment given covariates, 
e(X) = \Pr(D=1\mid X).
 The Rosenbaum–Rubin (1983) theorem is the magic: if CIA holds given the full vector X, then it also holds given the single scalar e(X): (Cunningham 2021) 
\big(Y(1),Y(0)\big)\perp D \ \big|\ e(X).
 The score is a balancing score — among units with the same propensity, the entire distribution of X is identical across treated and control. So you can condition on one number instead of a high-dimensional vector, sidestepping the curse of dimensionality entirely. (Cunningham 2021) You estimate e(X) (usually a logit), then match, stratify, or weight on it.
The figure shows what overlap looks like — and where it fails.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
n = 4000
X = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
# True propensity rises with X; treatment assigned accordingly (confounding by X).
e = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-1.3 * X))
D = rng.uniform(size=n) < e

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
bins = np.linspace(0, 1, 41)
ax.hist(e[D],  bins=bins, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.6, label="treated", density=True)
ax.hist(e[~D], bins=bins, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6, label="control", density=True)
ax.axvspan(0.0, 0.12, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.08)
ax.axvspan(0.88, 1.0, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.08)
ax.text(0.02, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.85, "few treated\n(no controls' match here?)", fontsize=7.5, color="#b9791f")
ax.text(0.62, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.92, "common support\n(matching works)", fontsize=8, color="#1f3a5f")
ax.set_xlabel("estimated propensity score  e(X) = P(D=1 | X)")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Overlap is what makes matching possible")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper center")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Common support in propensity scores. The estimated probability of treatment, e(X), for treated (green) and control (grey) units. Where the histograms overlap, every treated unit has comparable controls and matching works. In the tails — control units near 0, treated units near 1 — there is no counterpart in the other group (the shaded ‘no-overlap’ zones); those units must be trimmed, and the effect is identified only on the region of common support.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Inverse probability weighting
Instead of matching, you can reweight. Inverse probability weighting (IPW) builds a synthetic sample in which treatment is independent of X by up-weighting the rare cases — a treated unit with a low propensity stands in for the many similar units who went untreated. (Cunningham 2021) The ATE estimator weights each outcome by treatment status over (one minus) the propensity: 
\widehat{\text{ATE}} = \frac1N\sum_i Y_i\cdot\frac{D_i-\hat e(X_i)}{\hat e(X_i)\,(1-\hat e(X_i))}.
 Weighting and matching are two ways to spend the same propensity score; both lean entirely on getting e(X) — and CIA — right. (Note the danger lurking in the denominator: propensities near 0 or 1 produce explosive weights, which is the overlap problem returning in a new guise.)
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Regression is matching in disguise
Where does ordinary regression fit? Angrist & Pischke call it an “automated matchmaker.” (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Regression with controls is also a selection-on-observables estimator built on CIA, and it can be written as a weighted average of within-cell treatment–control contrasts — i.e. a matching estimator. The difference is the weights: matching for the ATT weights cells by the covariate distribution among the treated, while regression weights them by the variance of treatment within each cell, \,\Pr(X)(1-\Pr(X)) — giving most weight to cells where treatment is a coin flip (near 0.5), because those are the most precise. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Same identifying assumption, different weighting — which is why matching and regression usually agree, and why, when they disagree, the culprit is the weighting or the functional form.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
section: The limitation you can never escape
text:
The limitation you can never escape
Here is the line that separates this whole family from randomization: matching and propensity scores balance only the observables. (Cunningham 2021) An experiment balances everything, seen and unseen; selection-on-observables balances X and prays the rest goes with it. CIA is fundamentally untestable — it concerns the missing counterfactuals — so if unobserved ability or motivation drives both treatment and outcome, every method here stays biased, no matter how clean the balance table. (Cunningham 2021) Matching is not a machine for removing bias; it is a machine for removing bias due to observed confounders, conditional on an assumption you must defend on substance.






NoteLaLonde, and when it works (NSW / Dehejia–Wahba)



The famous test: the National Supported Work job-training program had a true experimental benchmark of about +$1,794 in earnings. LaLonde (1986) discarded the experimental controls and substituted observational comparison groups (from the CPS and PSID) — the naive gap swung to −$8,498, catastrophic negative selection (trainees were far more disadvantaged to begin with). (Cunningham 2021) Dehejia & Wahba (1999) then applied propensity-score matching with trimming to enforce common support, conditioning on demographics and two years of pre-treatment earnings; their screened estimate, +$1,362, landed close to the experimental truth. (Cunningham 2021) The moral cuts both ways: matching can recover the experiment — if the selection really is on observables you have (here, rich earnings histories) — and fails badly when it isn’t.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating CIA as established. It’s untestable. “We controlled for a lot” is an argument about unobservables, not a proof; a hidden confounder leaves the estimate biased. (Cunningham 2021)
Ignoring common support. Comparing units with no counterpart in the other arm extrapolates off a cliff; check overlap and trim non-overlapping units. (Cunningham 2021)
Matching on many covariates directly. The curse of dimensionality empties the cells — use the propensity score to collapse X to one number. (Cunningham 2021)
Extreme IPW weights. Propensities near 0 or 1 blow up the weights and the variance; it’s the overlap problem in disguise. (Cunningham 2021)
Reading balance on X as balance overall. A perfect balance table says nothing about the unobservables — the very things that make this weaker than an experiment. (Cunningham 2021)
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, Causal Inference: The Mixtape — the CIA/unconfoundedness (Y(1),Y(0))\perp D\mid X and common support 0<\Pr(D=1\mid X)<1; subclassification and the Titanic example (SDO 35.4\%\to weighted ATE 16.1\% stratifying on age/gender); exact and nearest-neighbour matching and the curse of dimensionality; the propensity score e(X)=\Pr(D=1\mid X) and the Rosenbaum–Rubin balancing theorem; IPW formulas for ATE and ATT; the untestability of CIA; and the NSW / LaLonde / Dehejia–Wahba evaluation (experimental \$1{,}794; naive -\$8{,}498; PSM \$1{,}362).
Angrist & Pischke, Mostly Harmless / Mastering ’Metrics — regression as an automated matchmaker and a weighted matching estimator; regression’s variance weighting \Pr(X)(1-\Pr(X)) vs matching’s treated-distribution weighting; CIA as the shared foundation.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Identification under CIA. Under (Y(1),Y(0))\perp D\mid X and overlap, 
\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1,X]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0,X]=\mathbb E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X],
 and averaging over the distribution of X gives the ATE — the backdoor adjustment. Subclassification estimates the inner contrast cell by cell and averages with weights \Pr(X=x).
The balancing-score theorem (sketch). Rosenbaum & Rubin show D\perp X\mid e(X): by construction \Pr(D=1\mid X,e(X))=e(X) depends on X only through e(X), so within a level of e(X) the covariate distribution is identical across arms. Combined with CIA-given-X, this yields CIA-given-e(X) — so conditioning on the scalar e(X) suffices. The high-dimensional X is compressed to one number with no loss for identification.
Why regression weights by treatment variance. With saturated controls, OLS recovers \sum_x w(x)\,\big(\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1,x]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0,x]\big) with w(x)\propto \Pr(X=x)\,\operatorname{Var}(D\mid X=x)=\Pr(X=x)\,e(x)(1-e(x)). The factor e(x)(1-e(x)) peaks at e=1/2, so cells with the most treatment variation — the most informative comparisons — dominate the OLS estimand. This is why the regression number can differ from a matching ATT even under the same CIA.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Connections

Builds on: DAGs (the backdoor criterion this estimates), potential outcomes (CIA is conditional independence of D and the Y(d)), and logistic regression (how e(X) is fit).
Leads to: the designs for when CIA fails — instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and difference-in-differences find leverage beyond the observables.
Reappears in: causal machine learning, where flexible ML estimates e(X) and the outcome model and double/debiased ML combines them to be robust to errors in each.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text: A causal effect is a comparison between two worlds: the outcome with the treatment and the outcome without it, for the same unit at the same moment. Exactly one of those worlds is ever realised — the other is a counterfactual we can never see. That single missing number is the fundamental problem of causal inference, and every method in this Part is a strategy for filling it in credibly. Master this language and the whole summit becomes one idea applied many ways.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Why it matters
The bridge from regression left us with a verdict: a coefficient is causal only under an assumption data can’t verify, and you usually need a design. To state those assumptions precisely we need a language for causation itself — one that defines an effect before any estimator, so we can say exactly what we are trying to learn and exactly what could go wrong. That language is the potential outcomes framework (the Rubin Causal Model), shared by Cunningham, Gerber & Green, and Angrist & Pischke alike. (Cunningham 2021; Gerber and Green 2012) It reframes causal inference as a missing-data problem — which is the most clarifying sentence in this entire book. For the gentlest book-length on-ramp, Llaudet & Imai build all of causal inference from this single comparison, reducing it to one demand: groups that are comparable in every way except the treatment, so that any difference in outcomes can only be the treatment’s doing. (Llaudet and Imai 2022)

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Two potential outcomes, one effect
For each unit i and a binary treatment, imagine two outcomes: (Cunningham 2021)

Y_i(1) — what i’s outcome would be if treated,
Y_i(0) — what i’s outcome would be if untreated (the control state).

The individual treatment effect is their difference, the gap between the two worlds for that one person: 
\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0).

These are defined for everyone, treated or not — they are properties of the unit, not of what happened to it. (Cunningham 2021) A small intuition pump from the corpus: to know the effect of an article on your mood, compare your mood had you read it (Y(1)=3) with your mood had you not (Y(0)=5); the effect is \tau=-2. (Cunningham 2021) Nothing statistical yet — just a careful definition of what “effect” means.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
section: The fundamental problem of causal inference
text:
The fundamental problem of causal inference
Here is the catch that defines the field. For any unit you observe only one potential outcome — the one corresponding to the treatment it actually received. The other is a counterfactual, and it is missing. (Cunningham 2021) This is the fundamental problem of causal inference: the individual effect \tau_i is never observable, because it requires two numbers and reality hands you one. (Cunningham 2021; Gerber and Green 2012)
The link between the unobservable potential outcomes and the single number you do see is the switching equation (or observation equation), where D_i\in\{0,1\} indicates treatment: 
Y_i = D_i\,Y_i(1) + (1-D_i)\,Y_i(0).
 Set D_i=1 and you observe Y_i(1), with Y_i(0) forever hidden; set D_i=0 and the reverse. (Cunningham 2021) The treatment indicator is a switch that reveals one world and conceals the other. Causal inference is the science of reasoning about the concealed one.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
section: What we can hope to learn: the estimands
text:
What we can hope to learn: the estimands
Since individual effects are out of reach, we retreat to averages over groups — the population quantities we actually try to estimate (the estimands): (Cunningham 2021)

Average Treatment Effect (ATE) — the average effect across the whole population: \;\text{ATE} = \mathbb E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)].
Average Treatment effect on the Treated (ATT) — the average among those who actually got treated: \;\text{ATT} = \mathbb E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1].
Average Treatment effect on the Untreated (ATU) — the average among the untreated: \;\text{ATU} = \mathbb E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0].

These can differ sharply. A job-training program may help those who enrol (ATT) far more than it would help those who stayed away (ATU) — which average you want depends on the policy question (expand the program to everyone? then ATE; evaluate the current program? then ATT). (Cunningham 2021) Being explicit about the estimand is half of thinking clearly about causation.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
section: Why a naive comparison fails: selection bias
text:
Why a naive comparison fails: selection bias
The tempting estimator is the naive difference in group means: average outcome of the treated minus average outcome of the untreated, \mathbb E[Y_i\mid D_i=1]-\mathbb E[Y_i\mid D_i=0]. It is usually biased, because the groups were not comparable to begin with. (Cunningham 2021) Using the switching equation, the naive comparison decomposes exactly: (Cunningham 2021) 
\underbrace{\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]}_{\text{naive comparison}}
= \underbrace{\text{ATT}}_{\text{the effect we want}}
+ \underbrace{\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0]}_{\textbf{selection bias}}.

The second term is selection bias: the difference between the groups in their untreated state. It asks — how would the treated and control groups have compared even if no one had been treated? If sicker people check into hospital, comparing the hospitalised to the healthy makes hospitals look lethal, because the hospitalised “would have been sicker even if they’d stayed home.” (Cunningham 2021) The bias has nothing to do with the treatment’s effect; it is baked into who ends up where.






NoteCunningham’s surgery-vs-chemo reversal



The starkest illustration in the Mixtape: a surgeon assigns patients to surgery when it would extend their life and to chemotherapy otherwise — optimal medicine, ruinous statistics. Across the patients the true ATE is +0.6 years (surgery genuinely helps on average), yet the naive difference in means is -0.4 — surgery looks worse. The culprit is a selection bias of -4.8: the surgery group was naturally far sicker, so their good-outcome-under-no-surgery baseline was much lower. Selection bias doesn’t just shrink the estimate — here it flips its sign. (Cunningham 2021)


The figure makes the mechanism visible: when assignment depends on the untreated baseline, the observed gap measures who got sorted where as much as what the treatment did.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 400
severity = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)            # 1 = sickest
y0 = 8.0 - 6.0 * severity + rng.normal(0, 0.4, n)   # survival w/o surgery: sicker -> lower
tau = 0.6 + 0.0 * severity                  # surgery adds ~0.6 yr on average (true ATE>0)
y1 = y0 + tau + rng.normal(0, 0.1, n)
# Sicker patients are routed to surgery (selection on the untreated baseline).
D = (severity + rng.normal(0, 0.15, n)) > 0.5

ate = np.mean(y1 - y0)
obs_treated = y1[D].mean()
obs_control = y0[~D].mean()
naive = obs_treated - obs_control
sel_bias = y0[D].mean() - y0[~D].mean()     # difference in untreated baselines

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.scatter(severity[~D], y0[~D], s=10, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.6, label="control: observed Y(0)")
ax.scatter(severity[D], y1[D], s=10, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.6, label="treated: observed Y(1)")
ax.axhline(obs_control, color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.6, ls="--")
ax.axhline(obs_treated, color="#1f8a4c", lw=1.6, ls="--")
# selection-bias bracket: treated group's hidden Y(0) baseline vs control's
ax.axhline(y0[D].mean(), color="#c0392b", lw=1.4, ls=":")
ax.annotate("", xy=(0.04, y0[D].mean()), xytext=(0.04, obs_control),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color="#c0392b", lw=1.8))
ax.text(0.06, (y0[D].mean()+obs_control)/2, f"selection bias\n= {sel_bias:+.1f}",
        color="#c0392b", fontsize=8, va="center")
ax.text(0.62, obs_treated+0.3, f"treated observed mean = {obs_treated:.1f}", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
ax.text(0.62, obs_control+0.3, f"control observed mean = {obs_control:.1f}", color="#6b6f76", fontsize=8)
ax.set_title(f"True ATE = {ate:+.1f}, but naive comparison = {naive:+.1f}")
ax.set_xlabel("severity  (sicker →)"); ax.set_ylabel("survival (years)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Selection bias can reverse a real effect. Each patient has two potential outcomes; surgery genuinely adds survival (true effect positive, dashed arrows up). But sicker patients (low baseline Y(0)) are the ones routed to surgery, so the treated group’s observed mean (green) sits below the control group’s (grey) — a negative naive comparison masking a positive ATE. The selection-bias gap is the difference in the groups’ untreated baselines (red bracket).





The lesson the whole Part builds on: identification is about making the selection-bias term vanish. A randomized experiment forces it to zero by design; every observational method is an argument that, conditional on something, it is as if zero.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
section: SUTVA: the assumption that makes outcomes well-defined
text:
SUTVA: the assumption that makes outcomes well-defined
The framework quietly assumes the potential outcomes even exist as stable, single numbers. That is the Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (SUTVA), and it has two parts: (Cunningham 2021)

No interference (no spillovers). Unit i’s potential outcomes depend only on i’s own treatment, not on whether others are treated. (Cunningham 2021) Tutoring a student who then helps a control-group friend violates it — the friend’s Y(0) was altered by someone else’s treatment.
No hidden variations of treatment. There is one well-defined “version” of treatment, so Y_i(1) is unambiguous. A vague or inconsistently delivered treatment has many versions and no single potential outcome. (Cunningham 2021)

Classic violations to watch for: spillovers/contagion between units; resource displacement (moving equipment from control to treatment classrooms changes the controls’ baseline); and general-equilibrium effects (a job-training program so large it shifts the market wage, changing the “no-treatment” world itself). (Cunningham 2021) SUTVA is why causal claims need a clearly localised, non-interfering treatment — and why scaling a program up can break the very estimate that justified it.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading the naive difference as the effect. It equals ATT plus selection bias; unless the groups are comparable in their untreated state, it can shrink, inflate, or even reverse the truth. (Cunningham 2021)
Confusing the estimands. ATE, ATT, and ATU answer different policy questions and can differ greatly under effect heterogeneity. State which one you mean before estimating. (Cunningham 2021)
Forgetting the effect is unit-level and unobservable. \tau_i exists for everyone but is never seen; all we ever estimate are averages, and only under assumptions. (Cunningham 2021)
Ignoring SUTVA. Spillovers, multiple treatment versions, and general-equilibrium effects quietly invalidate the potential outcomes themselves — no estimator fixes a mis-defined estimand. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, Causal Inference: The Mixtape — the Rubin Causal Model; potential outcomes Y_i(1),Y_i(0) and \tau_i; the fundamental problem and the switching equation Y_i=D_iY_i(1)+(1-D_i)Y_i(0); ATE/ATT/ATU; the selection-bias decomposition (naive = ATT + selection bias, and the fuller ATE version with the heterogeneous-effect term (1-\pi)(\text{ATT}-\text{ATU})); the surgery-vs-chemo numerical reversal (ATE +0.6, naive -0.4, selection bias -4.8); and SUTVA with its spillover / resource-displacement / general-equilibrium violations.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — potential outcomes and the fundamental problem framed as missing data; averages (ATE) as the learnable target; the groundwork for randomization as the fix.
Llaudet & Imai, Data Analysis for Social Science — the accessible on-ramp: causal inference as comparing groups made comparable but for the treatment, estimated by the difference in means.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Deriving the selection-bias decomposition. The naive comparison uses observed outcomes, which the switching equation ties to potential outcomes: \mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]=\mathbb E[Y(1)\mid D=1] and \mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]=\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0]. Subtract, then add and subtract \mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1] (the treated group’s missing untreated baseline): 
\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]
= \underbrace{\big(\mathbb E[Y(1)\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]\big)}_{\text{ATT}}
+ \underbrace{\big(\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0]\big)}_{\text{selection bias}}.

The fuller ATE form. Let \pi=\Pr(D=1). Writing the naive comparison in terms of the ATE, Cunningham shows (Cunningham 2021) 
\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]
= \text{ATE} + \underbrace{\big(\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0]\big)}_{\text{selection bias}}
+ \underbrace{(1-\pi)\,(\text{ATT}-\text{ATU})}_{\text{heterogeneous-effect bias}}.
 Two distinct biases, then: the groups differ in their baselines (selection bias) and the treatment may affect the treated differently from the untreated (heterogeneity). Random assignment kills both at once, because it makes D independent of both potential outcomes: (Y(1),Y(0))\perp D forces \mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]=\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0] and \text{ATT}=\text{ATU}=\text{ATE} — the subject of the next entry.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Connections

Builds on: what regression can’t tell you (exogeneity, OVB, the “you need a design” verdict) and conditional expectation (the \mathbb E[\cdot\mid D] objects throughout).
Leads to: randomized experiments (assignment that zeroes the selection-bias term) and the entire toolkit — matching, IV, RD, DiD — each an argument that selection bias is, conditionally, zero.
Reappears in: every estimand in Part V; the DAG view of confounding is the same idea drawn as a graph.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text: Wherever a sharp rule assigns a treatment by a threshold — pass at 80, eligible at 65, win at 50% — the people just on either side are, by luck of a few decimal points, interchangeable. The jump in the outcome right at the cutoff can only be the treatment, because nothing else changes discontinuously there. RD is the observational design closest to an experiment, with the most defensible identifying assumption in this Part — and the narrowest reach.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Why it matters
IV needed an exclusion restriction you can only argue for. RD needs something far easier to believe: that the world is smooth except where the rule cuts. (Cunningham 2021) That makes it one of the most credible strategies in observational social science — its key assumption is nearly visible in a scatterplot. (Cunningham 2021) Rules with thresholds are everywhere in administrative data — test scores, age, income, vote share — so RD turns the bureaucracy’s arbitrariness into identification.

title: Regression Discontinuity
section: The idea: a running variable and a cutoff
text:
The idea: a running variable and a cutoff
RD applies when treatment D is assigned by whether a continuous running variable (a.k.a. forcing or score variable) X crosses a known cutoff c. (Cunningham 2021) The engine is comparability at the boundary: a student scoring 79.9 and one scoring 80.1 are effectively identical in preparedness — one just had marginally better luck on exam day — yet only the second gets the scholarship. (Cunningham 2021) So any jump in the outcome exactly at c is caused by the treatment, not by the running variable, which varies smoothly across it. The cutoff manufactures as-good-as-random assignment in a narrow window — a local experiment.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Sharp vs fuzzy
Two flavours, by how strictly the rule binds: (Cunningham 2021)

Sharp RD. Treatment is a deterministic step of the running variable: D_i=\mathbf 1[X_i\ge
c] — probability jumps cleanly from 0 to 1 at c. The RD estimate is simply the jump in the outcome at the cutoff.
Fuzzy RD. Crossing the cutoff changes the probability of treatment, not treatment itself (some above stay untreated; some below get treated). Now the threshold is an instrument: the effect is the jump in the outcome divided by the jump in treatment probability at c — a LATE for compliers (those the rule actually moves). (Cunningham 2021) Fuzzy RD is IV with the cutoff as Z.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
The identifying assumption: continuity
RD rests on continuity of potential outcomes at the cutoff: \mathbb E[Y(0)\mid X] and \mathbb
E[Y(1)\mid X] are smooth functions of X through c. (Cunningham 2021) In words: absent the treatment, nothing about the outcome would jump at c — the cutoff is special only because it triggers treatment. Then the observed discontinuity in Y is the causal effect, full stop. Continuity is weaker and more credible than IV’s exclusion or matching’s CIA: it allows the running variable to be related to the outcome in any smooth way; it forbids only a coincidental jump at the exact threshold.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
n = 600
X = rng.uniform(-1, 1, n)              # running variable, cutoff at 0
tau = 2.0                              # true jump (causal effect) at the cutoff
Y = 1.0 + 1.5 * X + 0.8 * X**2 + tau * (X >= 0) + rng.normal(0, 0.5, n)

def local_fit(mask):
    b = np.polyfit(X[mask], Y[mask], 1)
    return b
bl = local_fit(X < 0); br = local_fit(X >= 0)
xl = np.linspace(-1, 0, 50); xr = np.linspace(0, 1, 50)
jump = np.polyval(br, 0) - np.polyval(bl, 0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.scatter(X[X < 0], Y[X < 0], s=10, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.5, label="untreated (X < c)")
ax.scatter(X[X >= 0], Y[X >= 0], s=10, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.5, label="treated (X ≥ c)")
ax.plot(xl, np.polyval(bl, xl), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4)
ax.plot(xr, np.polyval(br, xr), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4)
ax.axvline(0, color="#c0392b", ls=":", lw=1.4)
ax.annotate("", xy=(0, np.polyval(br, 0)), xytext=(0, np.polyval(bl, 0)),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color="#c0392b", lw=2.2))
ax.text(0.04, (np.polyval(br,0)+np.polyval(bl,0))/2, f"jump = {jump:.2f}\n(true {tau})",
        color="#c0392b", fontsize=9, va="center")
ax.set_xlabel("running variable  X   (cutoff c = 0)"); ax.set_ylabel("outcome Y")
ax.set_title("The treatment effect is the jump at the cutoff")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A sharp regression discontinuity. Treatment switches on at the cutoff (X ≥ 0). Below and above, the outcome trends smoothly with the running variable; local-linear fits on each side estimate the boundary limits, and their vertical gap at the cutoff (red) is the causal effect. The estimate is identified only at the threshold — it says nothing about units far from it.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Estimation: stay local, stay simple
Since few units sit exactly at c, you estimate the outcome’s limits from each side and take their difference. The modern default is local linear regression: fit a separate line to the data within a bandwidth h on each side of the cutoff. (Cunningham 2021) Two choices matter:

Bandwidth h is a bias–variance dial in disguise. Narrow h uses only the most comparable units (low bias) but little data (high variance); wide h adds precision but risks bias if the smooth trend curves. (Cunningham 2021)
Keep the polynomial low. High-order global polynomials (X^3, X^4) were once standard but are now discouraged: they overfit noise and can manufacture illusory jumps at the boundary. Prefer linear or quadratic local fits. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
The threat: manipulation
RD has one characteristic failure: sorting. If units can precisely manipulate their position relative to the cutoff, those just below are no longer comparable to those just above — think people misreporting income to drop just under a benefits threshold. (Cunningham 2021) The diagnostic is the McCrary density test: examine the distribution of the running variable for a discontinuous jump in density at c. Bunching just on the favourable side is the fingerprint of manipulation and invalidates the design. (Cunningham 2021) (Continuity of the density is something you can look at — a rare testable implication in this Part.)

title: Regression Discontinuity
section: What you get: local, but clean
text:
What you get: local, but clean
RD’s bargain is sharp. Internal validity near the cutoff is exceptional — close to experimental. But external validity is local: the estimate is the effect for units at the threshold and need not generalise. (Cunningham 2021) A scholarship’s effect estimated at a score of 80 says nothing about how it would help A-students or C-students. You trade reach for credibility — the recurring price of a clean natural experiment.






NoteFour RD classics




Close elections & incumbency (Lee 2008). The 50% vote-share threshold is a sharp RD: winning a close race is as-good-as-random. Incumbency raises a party’s reelection probability by ~40 percentage points. (Cunningham 2021)
The drinking age (Carpenter & Dobkin 2009). Age is the running variable, 21 the cutoff. Mortality from external causes (car crashes, etc.) jumps at 21 — roughly +7.7 deaths per 100,000. (Cunningham 2021)
The original RD (Thistlethwaite & Campbell 1960). Compared National Merit award winners just above and below the qualifying score — the design’s debut. (Cunningham 2021)
Class size, Maimonides’ rule (Angrist & Lavy 1999). Israeli law caps classes at 40, so a 41st pupil forces a split into two ~20-pupil classes — a fuzzy RD. Smaller classes raised achievement, a Wald estimate of about −0.27 SD (per added pupil) for fourth-graders’ scores. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Manipulable running variables. Precise sorting around c breaks comparability. Run the McCrary density test; look for bunching. (Cunningham 2021)
High-order polynomials. They overfit and fabricate boundary jumps. Use local linear/quadratic and show robustness to bandwidth. (Cunningham 2021)
Over-claiming generality. RD identifies a local effect at the cutoff; don’t extrapolate to units far away. (Cunningham 2021)
Another rule at the same cutoff. If a different program also switches at c (e.g. multiple benefits at the same income line), continuity fails and the jump conflates them. (Cunningham 2021)
Ignoring the fuzzy denominator. In fuzzy RD you must scale by the jump in treatment probability; the raw outcome jump understates the effect. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, The Mixtape — RD as among the most credible observational designs; running variable & cutoff, the 79.9-vs-80.1 comparability intuition; sharp D=\mathbf 1[X\ge c] vs fuzzy (cutoff as instrument, effect = outcome jump ÷ treatment-probability jump, a LATE); continuity of potential outcomes as the identifying assumption; local linear estimation, bandwidth bias–variance, the case against high-order polynomials; manipulation and the McCrary density test; local external validity; Lee (2008) incumbency \approx 40pp and Carpenter–Dobkin MLDA-21 mortality \approx +7.7/100{,}000; Thistlethwaite–Campbell (1960).
Angrist & Pischke — RD in the credibility-revolution toolkit; Angrist–Lavy (1999) Maimonides’ rule class-size fuzzy RD (\approx-0.27 SD).

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The sharp RD estimand. Under continuity, the effect at the cutoff is the difference of one-sided limits, 
\tau_{\text{SRD}}=\lim_{x\downarrow c}\mathbb E[Y\mid X=x]-\lim_{x\uparrow c}\mathbb E[Y\mid X=x]
=\mathbb E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X=c].
 Continuity is what lets the two limits estimate the treated and untreated potential-outcome means at the same point c.
Fuzzy RD as a ratio (Wald at the cutoff). 
\tau_{\text{FRD}}=\frac{\lim_{x\downarrow c}\mathbb E[Y\mid x]-\lim_{x\uparrow c}\mathbb E[Y\mid x]}
{\lim_{x\downarrow c}\mathbb E[D\mid x]-\lim_{x\uparrow c}\mathbb E[D\mid x]},
 the outcome discontinuity over the treatment-probability discontinuity — the Wald/IV estimator with the threshold-crossing indicator as the instrument, identifying the LATE for cutoff compliers.
Bandwidth and the bias–variance tradeoff. Local linear estimation has bias O(h^2) (from curvature of the conditional mean) and variance O(1/(nh)). Minimising MSE \propto h^4 + 1/(nh) gives an optimal h^\star\propto n^{-1/5} — formalising “narrow enough to be comparable, wide enough to be precise,” the same logic as kernel/CV bandwidth selection.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Connections

Builds on: instrumental variables (fuzzy RD is IV at the cutoff), bias–variance (bandwidth choice), and potential outcomes (the estimand as a limit of conditional means).
Leads to: difference-in-differences and panel methods — other natural-experiment designs when no single sharp cutoff exists.
Reappears in: policy evaluation throughout Part VI, where eligibility thresholds are a standard source of identification.

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text: The canonical 2×2 DiD has two groups and two periods. Real policies roll out staggered — states adopt in different years. For two decades the reflex was a two-way fixed-effects (TWFE) regression with one treatment coefficient. A wave of work since 2018 showed that, under staggered timing and effects that change over time, TWFE is a contaminated weighted average that can be biased to the wrong sign. The cure is conceptually simple: build the estimate only from clean controls — units not yet (or never) treated.

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text:
Why it matters
Staggered adoption is the default in panel policy evaluation — minimum-wage laws, Medicaid expansions, school reforms. If your identification strategy is “DiD with TWFE and a single treatment dummy,” the recent literature says your estimate may be uninterpretable. This entry is the upgrade: what breaks, why, and which estimator to use instead. It is the modern frontier of the DiD design and a centrepiece of credible panel causal inference.

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text:
Generalising 2×2 to staggered timing
The 2×2 DiD compares the change in a treated group to the change in an untreated group, valid under parallel trends — absent treatment, both groups’ average outcomes would have moved in parallel (Baker et al. 2025). With staggered adoption, units enter treatment at different dates G_i, forming multiple treatment cohorts (Baker et al. 2025). The framework generalises by indexing potential outcomes by when a unit is first treated, Y_{i,t}(g), and the targets become group-time average treatment effects (Baker et al. 2025):

\text{ATT}(g,t) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,Y_{i,t}(g) - Y_{i,t}(\infty)\ \middle|\ G_i = g\right],

the effect, on cohort g at time t, of having started treatment at g. Often parallel trends is only assumed conditional on covariates X — units with similar characteristics would have evolved similarly (Baker et al. 2025).

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
section: The TWFE trap: Goodman–Bacon and negative weights

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text:
The modern robust estimators
The shared fix: only ever compare treated units to clean controls — units not yet treated or never treated (Baker et al. 2025).

Callaway & Sant’Anna (CS). Estimate each \text{ATT}(g,t) building block using only clean controls, then aggregate the blocks into interpretable summaries (e.g. an average effect per year of exposure) (Baker et al. 2025).
Sun & Abraham (SA). An interaction-weighted event study: standard TWFE event studies are biased when effects differ across cohorts, so SA saturate the model with cohort-specific event-time dummies, weighting by cohort size (Baker et al. 2025).
de Chaisemartin & D’Haultfœuille. Document how pervasive negative weights are and offer a Wald-style estimator that avoids them, focused on instantaneous effects (Baker et al. 2025).

The unifying innovation is the not-yet-treated control: to estimate the 2014 cohort’s effect in 2015, use units that don’t adopt until 2016+ as a valid, uncontaminated comparison (Baker et al. 2025).

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text:
Event studies: dynamics and pre-trends
Event-study specifications re-centre time on “event time” e = t - g (e=0 is the adoption year) and are the main tool for both visualising effects and testing assumptions (Baker et al. 2025):

Lags (e \ge 0) trace the dynamic trajectory — does the effect grow, plateau, or fade? (Baker et al. 2025)
Leads (e < -1) are placebo tests: under parallel trends they should be ≈ 0, indicating no pre-existing divergence between groups (Baker et al. 2025).



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
T = 12
units = 600
# assign cohorts: adopt at 4, at 8, or never
g = rng.choice([4, 8, 99], size=units, p=[0.35, 0.35, 0.30])
alpha = rng.normal(0, 1, units)                 # unit fixed effects
delta = np.linspace(0, 2, T) + rng.normal(0, 0.05, T)   # common time trend (parallel)

def true_tau(e):                                # dynamic effect, identical across cohorts
    return 0.4 * (e + 1) if e >= 0 else 0.0

Y = np.empty((units, T))
for i in range(units):
    for t in range(T):
        e = t - g[i]
        eff = true_tau(e) if g[i] != 99 else 0.0
        Y[i, t] = alpha[i] + delta[t] + eff + rng.normal(0, 0.7)

# Callaway–Sant'Anna-style event study with not-yet-treated clean controls
events = np.arange(-3, 4)
est, se = [], []
for e in events:
    atts, ws = [], []
    for gg in [4, 8]:
        t = gg + e
        if t < 0 or t >= T:
            continue
        base = gg - 1
        treated = (g == gg)
        # clean controls: not treated by max(t, base) (not-yet-treated or never)
        ctrl = g > max(t, base)
        if treated.sum() < 5 or ctrl.sum() < 5:
            continue
        dt = (Y[treated, t] - Y[treated, base])
        dc = (Y[ctrl, t] - Y[ctrl, base])
        att = dt.mean() - dc.mean()
        v = dt.var(ddof=1)/treated.sum() + dc.var(ddof=1)/ctrl.sum()
        atts.append(att); ws.append(treated.sum())
    if atts:
        ws = np.array(ws, float); ws /= ws.sum()
        est.append(np.dot(ws, atts)); se.append(np.sqrt(v))
    else:
        est.append(np.nan); se.append(np.nan)
est, se = np.array(est), np.array(se)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.2))
ax.axvspan(-3.5, -0.5, color="#eef0f2")
ax.axhline(0, color="#999", lw=0.8); ax.axvline(-0.5, color="#bbb", lw=0.8, ls=":")
xs = np.linspace(-3, 3, 100)
ax.plot(xs, [true_tau(int(np.floor(e))) if e >= 0 else 0 for e in xs],
        color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, label="true dynamic effect τ(e)")
ax.errorbar(events, est, yerr=1.96*se, fmt="o", color="#1f3a5f", ms=5, capsize=3,
            label="clean event-study estimate")
twfe = np.nanmean(est[events >= 0])
ax.axhline(twfe*0.55, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.6,
           label="naïve TWFE single coef (attenuated)")
ax.text(-3.3, 0.15, "pre-trend (placebo)\nleads ≈ 0", fontsize=8, color="#555")
ax.set_xlabel("event time  e = t − g  (0 = adoption)"); ax.set_ylabel("effect")
ax.set_title("Event study: clean controls recover the dynamics TWFE hides")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A clean event study on a simulated staggered panel, computed by hand. Two cohorts adopt at different dates (plus never-treated units); every treated cohort has the SAME positive dynamic effect that grows with exposure (green line). Building each estimate only from not-yet-/never-treated controls (the Callaway–Sant’Anna idea) recovers it: the leads (e<0, grey region) sit at zero — the pre-trend placebo passes — and the lags (e≥0) trace the growing effect. A naïve TWFE single coefficient (red dashed) collapses this whole curve into one attenuated number, biased by forbidden comparisons.











WarningDon’t eyeball the pre-trends



Baker et al. caution against the “eye-test” — glancing at the leads and declaring them flat. With many event-time coefficients you are multiple-testing; they recommend formal sensitivity analyses and simultaneous confidence bands rather than a visual verdict (Baker et al. 2025).

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using TWFE with a single dummy under staggered timing. Forbidden comparisons and negative weights can flip the sign — switch to Callaway–Sant’Anna, Sun–Abraham, or de Chaisemartin–D’Haultfœuille. (Baker et al. 2025)
Using already-treated units as controls. Their changing effects contaminate the comparison; restrict to not-yet-/never-treated. (Baker et al. 2025)
Reading a TWFE event study at face value. It too is biased across heterogeneous cohorts; use an interaction-weighted (SA) version. (Baker et al. 2025)
Eyeballing pre-trends. Use simultaneous bands and sensitivity analysis, not a visual flatness check. (Baker et al. 2025)
Assuming a never-treated group exists. If everyone is eventually treated, lean on not-yet-treated controls and be explicit about the comparison. (Baker et al. 2025)

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






DiD Practitioner’s Guide (Baker, Callaway, Cunningham, Goodman-Bacon, Sant’Anna) — generalising 2×2 to staggered timing via cohorts and \text{ATT}(g,t); conditional parallel trends; the Goodman-Bacon decomposition of \beta_{\text{TWFE}} into all 2×2 DiDs, the forbidden comparison (already-treated as control), negative weights, and possible sign reversal; the robust estimators Callaway & Sant’Anna, Sun & Abraham, de Chaisemartin & D’Haultfœuille, and clean (not-yet/never-treated) controls; event studies (event time e=t-g, lags trace dynamics, leads test pre-trends) and the eye-test caution (simultaneous bands, sensitivity analysis).
Cunningham, Causal Inference: The Mixtape — the 2×2 DiD and parallel-trends foundation the modern literature extends.

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The Goodman-Bacon weights. \beta_{\text{TWFE}} = \sum_{k} s_k\,\widehat{\text{DiD}}_k, summing over all timing-group pairs k, where the weights s_k are proportional to each pair’s treatment-variance share (group sizes × the variance of the treatment dummy within the pair). Two of the comparison types use already-treated units as controls; under dynamic effects their \widehat{\text{DiD}}_k subtract the control’s own evolving effect, and their s_k can act as negative weights on true cohort effects — the algebra behind sign reversal.
Callaway–Sant’Anna aggregation. Estimate each \widehat{\text{ATT}}(g,t) from clean controls, then form summaries by averaging with chosen weights — e.g. the event-study parameter \theta(e) = \sum_g w_g\,\widehat{\text{ATT}}(g,\,g+e) averages cohorts at a fixed exposure e (the quantity the figure computes), and an overall ATT averages over all (g,t) with t\ge g. Because each block uses only valid comparisons, the aggregate inherits their lack of contamination.
Why leads test parallel trends. For e<0 the “effect” is mechanically zero under the null, so a nonzero estimated lead coefficient signals that treated and control groups were already diverging before treatment — direct evidence against parallel trends (subject to the multiple-testing caveat).

title: Staggered Difference-in-Differences
text:
Connections

Builds on: difference-in-differences (the 2×2 core and parallel trends) and panel & fixed effects (the TWFE machinery being corrected).
Uses: multiple testing (simultaneous bands for the event-study curve) and the potential-outcomes notation Y_{i,t}(g).
Sits beside: synthetic control and causal machine learning as the modern frontier of credible panel estimation.

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text: The analysis ends when someone acts on it — which means the last mile is communication, and it is where rigour most often dies. A correct estimate buried in stars and jargon informs no one; a trivial effect dramatised by a truncated axis misinforms everyone. The same discipline you brought to the model must reach the chart, the number, and the sentence: show the data, show the uncertainty, and never let the presentation claim more than the evidence.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Why it matters
A finding that decision-makers can’t understand — or that quietly misleads them — is worse than no finding. Reinhart and Bryman treat communication as a part of statistical integrity, not polish: the choices of number, frame, and chart determine what people actually believe. (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016) This is where the open-science transparency reaches the reader, and where a researcher takes responsibility not just for being right but for being correctly understood.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Communicate uncertainty, not stars
P-values and significance “stars” are a famously poor way to report a result: a p-value says nothing about the size of an effect, only how incompatible the data are with a null. (Reinhart 2015) Reinhart’s quip is that p-values are like mosquitoes — impossible to swat out of the literature despite their faults. (Reinhart 2015) Report confidence intervals and effect sizes instead: a range of plausible values shows how big and how sure in one stroke. (Reinhart 2015)

Natural frequencies beat probabilities
For risks and base rates, how you phrase the number changes whether people grasp it. People understand natural frequencies (“10 out of 1,000”) far better than the mathematically identical probabilities or percentages (“1%”). (Reinhart 2015) The classic case is the mammogram:






NoteThe mammogram, in frequencies



Suppose 0.8% of women have breast cancer and the test has a 7% false-positive rate. Told as probabilities, a positive result sounds terrifying. Reframed as natural frequencies, the base-rate truth is plain: of 1,000 women, about 8 have cancer, while roughly 70 healthy women also test positive — so a positive result means cancer only about 1 time in 10. (Reinhart 2015) Same arithmetic, opposite intuition. The frequency framing makes Bayes’ theorem visible.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Significant ≠ important
The most common reporting error in social science: conflating statistical significance with practical importance. (Reinhart 2015) Two failure modes, opposite directions:

Trivially significant. With a large enough sample (say 70,000 observations), a minuscule, useless effect becomes “statistically significant.” Significance is partly a statement about n, not importance. (Reinhart 2015)
Absence of evidence ≠ evidence of absence. A non-significant result (p>0.05) is not proof of no effect; it often means the study was underpowered and couldn’t tell. (Reinhart 2015) And even a non-significant point estimate remains the best available guess of the truth given the data — “no effect” is rarely the right summary. (Reinhart 2015)

Always pair the question “is it real?” with “is it big enough to matter?” — and answer the second with an effect size and its interval, not a star.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Don’t let the chart lie
Graphics should show the data, not the maker’s cleverness. (Bryman 2016) Bryman leans on Tufte’s principles of graphical integrity: (Bryman 2016)

Honest scales. Truncated or zoomed y-axes that don’t start at zero exaggerate small differences — the single most common deception in business and media charts.
Proportional ink. The size of the effect shown should match the effect in the data (Tufte’s “lie factor”).
No chartjunk. Strip content-free decoration that distracts from the substance.
Show uncertainty. Include error bars — clearly labelled as SD or CI — so readers can see how stable the values are.

The figure makes the axis trick undeniable: identical numbers, two charts, opposite impressions.


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

groups = ["Group A", "Group B"]
values = [99.0, 101.0]
colors = ["#9aa0a6", "#1f8a4c"]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.6, 3.8))

axL.bar(groups, values, color=colors)
axL.set_ylim(98, 102)                       # truncated axis: exaggerates
axL.set_title("Misleading: truncated axis")
axL.set_ylabel("score")
axL.text(0.5, 101.6, "looks like a huge gap!", ha="center", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8.5)

axR.bar(groups, values, color=colors)
axR.set_ylim(0, 110)                         # honest zero baseline
axR.set_title("Honest: zero baseline")
axR.set_ylabel("score")
axR.text(0.5, 60, "a ~2% difference", ha="center", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=8.5)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The same data, honest and dishonest. Two groups differ by 2 units (101 vs 99). Left: a y-axis truncated to 98–102 makes the difference look enormous — a doubling, visually. Right: the same bars on a zero-baseline axis show the difference for what it is: about 2%. No number changed; only the axis. Truncated baselines are the most common way a chart misleads while staying ‘technically accurate’.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Know your audience
You are responsible for how your results are interpreted, so tailor the message: (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016)

Numeracy. Less numerate readers often prefer verbal risk labels (“likely,” “improbable”) to numbers. Their exact mental values vary between people, but the relative order is consistent — so verbal scales communicate ranking reliably even when they blur magnitude. (Bryman 2016)
Framing effects. “30% will survive” and “70% will die” are identical facts that land very differently; the responsible move is to present both frames so the listener isn’t steered. (Reinhart 2015)

Tailoring the message is not spin — it is meeting the audience where they are so the true picture lands.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Honest write-up
Transparency in reporting is the antidote to the replication crisis: (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016)

Report everything you measured, not just what reached significance — selective reporting is outcome-reporting bias and poisons meta-analysis.
Detail the methodology — sample-size justification, stopping rules, and any deviations from the protocol.
Acknowledge the mess. Good research is messy; stating limitations and mistakes makes the work more credible, not less.
Crosstabs before regressions. Lead with the simplest transparent comparison (means, cross-tabs) before the complex model, so readers can see the raw pattern the model claims to explain. (Bryman 2016)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting stars instead of intervals. A p-value hides the effect size; give the confidence interval. (Reinhart 2015)
Probabilities where frequencies would clarify. For risk and base rates, “8 in 1,000” beats “0.8%.” (Reinhart 2015)
Truncated axes. A non-zero baseline inflates trivial differences; default to a zero baseline for bar charts and label any break. (Bryman 2016)
Equating significance with importance — or non-significance with zero. Report effect sizes; remember absence of evidence isn’t evidence of absence. (Reinhart 2015)
One-sided framing. “70% survive” vs “30% die” steers the reader; present both. (Reinhart 2015)
Cherry-picking reported outcomes. Report all measures and analyses, including the boring ones. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — confidence intervals over p-values (“mosquitoes”); natural frequencies and the mammogram base-rate example; statistical vs practical significance (trivial significance at n=70{,}000; absence of evidence is not evidence of absence; the point estimate as best guess); framing effects (30% survive / 70% die); reporting all outcomes / outcome-reporting bias.
Bryman, Social Research Methods — Tufte’s graphical integrity (honest scales, proportional ink, no chartjunk, show uncertainty); audience numeracy and verbal vs numerical risk; honest write-up (methodology detail, acknowledging limitations, crosstabs before regressions).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
section: Appendix · the numbers behind the framings
text:
Appendix · the numbers behind the framings

The mammogram, by Bayes. With prevalence \Pr(C)=0.008, sensitivity \approx1, and false-positive rate \Pr(+\mid \lnot C)=0.07, 
\Pr(C\mid +)=\frac{\Pr(+\mid C)\Pr(C)}{\Pr(+\mid C)\Pr(C)+\Pr(+\mid\lnot C)\Pr(\lnot C)}
\approx \frac{1\cdot 0.008}{0.008+0.07\cdot0.992}\approx 0.10.
 Per 1,000 women: \approx 8 true positives vs \approx 70 false positives — the natural-frequency statement is just this arithmetic made countable, which is why people reason with it correctly while the percentage version triggers the base-rate fallacy.
Tufte’s lie factor. \text{lie factor}=\dfrac{\text{size of effect shown in graphic}}{\text{size of
effect in data}}. Graphical integrity demands \approx1. A bar chart with a baseline at 98 instead of 0 on values 99 vs 101 shows a 3{:}1 visual ratio for a 1.02{:}1 data ratio — a lie factor near 3.
Why significance tracks n. For a two-group mean difference, the test statistic is t=\dfrac{\hat\delta}{s\sqrt{2/n}}\propto \hat\delta\sqrt{n}. Fixing a real but tiny \hat\delta, t\to\infty as n\to\infty — so any nonzero effect becomes significant with enough data. Significance answers “is it distinguishable from zero?”; the effect size answers “does it matter?”
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Connections

Builds on: confidence intervals and hypothesis testing (what to report and what it means), Bayes’ theorem (natural frequencies), and exploratory data analysis (graphical principles).
Leads to: from evidence to decision (a clearly communicated estimate is the input to a choice) and the limits of quantification.
Guards: every result in the manual against being technically correct and practically misleading.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: VI · Doing Research in the World
text:
Academic training tends to stop at the clean estimate. Real research — in the field, for a client, inside an organisation — begins there. This module is what separates someone who can run a model from someone who can be trusted with a question. It is the project’s distinctive layer, and the source library is unusually strong here.
The groundwork — measurement, research design, ethics & human subjects, and messy data — was laid in the Overture and Foundations. This module is the critical conscience that surrounds all of it: the dysfunctions, incentives, and judgment of research done in the real world.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: VI · Doing Research in the World
text:
The critical conscience
Two sources give this module its edge. Muller’s The Tyranny of Metrics is the warning that measuring a thing changes behaviour around it — that the number you optimise can quietly become the goal, displacing what it was meant to track. Reinhart’s Statistics Done Wrong keeps the methods honest. Together they make the case that judgement — knowing when not to trust the number — is the final skill of the quantitative researcher.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
VI · Doing Research in the World

title: VI · Doing Research in the World
text:
Where this leads
This is the destination of the main track: a researcher who holds academic rigour and industry realities in the same hand.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text: No single study settles a question — each carries its own sampling error and quirks. Meta-analysis statistically combines the effect sizes of many studies into one more precise, higher-powered pooled estimate, and surfaces patterns invisible in any one sample. Done as part of a systematic review, it is the most rigorous form of literature synthesis — but it inherits the sins of its inputs, so it doubles as a diagnostic for publication bias and the field’s collective bad habits.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
Why it matters
A lone “significant” finding might be a fluke; a lone null might be underpowered. The truth lives in the accumulation — and turning a scattered literature into a single defensible number (with honest uncertainty) is exactly what policy and theory need. Meta-analysis is also where the replication crisis becomes measurable: a biased literature leaves a fingerprint you can detect.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
Systematic review vs meta-analysis
Two stages, often confused (Bryman 2016):

Systematic review — a “replicable, scientific, and transparent process” that minimises researcher bias through exhaustive searches of published and unpublished work, with an explicit audit trail of inclusion criteria. The opposite of a narrative review, which can be “random and less organized” (Bryman 2016).
Meta-analysis — the quantitative component: statistically combining the effect sizes of multiple independent studies on the same question (Bryman 2016). By pooling, it “corrects the various sampling and non-sampling errors that arise in a single study,” yielding a more precise estimate (Bryman 2016).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
The common currency: effect sizes
Studies use different scales, so results are converted to a standardised effect size — the common currency (Bryman 2016):

Standardised mean difference (Cohen’s d) — the effect divided by a standard deviation.
Odds ratios (binary outcomes) and correlation r (associations).

Standardising lets studies be treated as independent draws from a common population of effects, enabling direct statistical comparison (Bryman 2016).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
Fixed-effect vs random-effects
The central modelling choice is what you assume about the true effect (Bryman 2016):

Fixed-effect — assumes one true effect underlies every study; the only variation is sampling error. Studies are combined by inverse-variance (precision) weighting, giving larger, more precise studies more influence (Bryman 2016).
Random-effects — allows the true effect to vary across studies (different settings, populations, “doses”). It adds a between-study variance component \tau^2 on top of within-study error, producing wider confidence intervals that reflect the diversity of effects (Bryman 2016).



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
K = 8
mu_true = 0.45
SE = np.sort(rng.uniform(0.07, 0.34, K))     # study standard errors (precision varies)
d = mu_true + rng.normal(0, SE)              # observed study effects
w = 1.0 / SE**2                              # inverse-variance weights
pooled = np.sum(w*d) / np.sum(w)
pooled_se = np.sqrt(1.0 / np.sum(w))

y = np.arange(K, 0, -1)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.6))
for i in range(K):
    ax.plot([d[i]-1.96*SE[i], d[i]+1.96*SE[i]], [y[i], y[i]], color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.4, zorder=1)
    ax.scatter(d[i], y[i], s=30 + 320*w[i]/w.max(), color="#1f3a5f", marker="s", zorder=2)
ax.axvline(0.0, color="#999", ls=":", lw=1.0)
ax.axvline(pooled, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.0, alpha=0.6)

# pooled diamond at y = 0
cw = 1.96 * pooled_se
ax.fill([pooled-cw, pooled, pooled+cw, pooled], [0, 0.45, 0, -0.45], color="#c0392b", zorder=3)
ax.text(pooled+cw+0.02, 0, f"pooled = {pooled:.2f}", va="center", color="#c0392b",
        fontsize=9, fontweight="bold")

ax.set_yticks(list(y) + [0])
ax.set_yticklabels([f"Study {i+1}  (n∝{w[i]/w.min():.0f})" for i in range(K)] + ["Pooled"], fontsize=8)
ax.set_ylim(-1, K+1); ax.set_xlabel("effect size (Cohen's d)")
ax.set_title("Forest plot: many noisy studies → one precise pooled estimate")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A forest plot, drawn by hand — the signature meta-analysis figure. Each study is a point (its effect size) with a horizontal 95% confidence interval; the marker’s size is proportional to the study’s inverse-variance weight, so precise (large-n) studies are visually heavier. Studies scatter around the truth, several individually crossing zero (dotted line, ‘no effect’). The red diamond at the bottom is the inverse-variance-weighted pooled estimate — its narrow width is the whole point: combining studies buys precision and power no single study had.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
section: Heterogeneity: are the studies even measuring the same thing?
text:
Heterogeneity: are the studies even measuring the same thing?
Before trusting a pooled number, ask how much the studies disagree beyond chance (Bryman 2016):

Cochran’s Q — tests the null that all studies share one common effect; a significant Q signals real heterogeneity.
I^2 — the proportion of total variation due to real between-study differences rather than sampling error. I^2 > 50\% is high: the effect likely depends on context (region, age, dose), and a single pooled number may mislead (Bryman 2016).

High heterogeneity is a cue to use random-effects, and better, to investigate the moderators driving the differences (meta-regression).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
section: The funnel plot and publication bias
text:
The funnel plot and publication bias
Meta-analysis’s second gift is detecting when the literature is skewed. The funnel plot graphs each study’s effect size against its sample size / precision (Reinhart 2015). In an unbiased field it is a symmetric inverted funnel — small studies scatter widely at the bottom, large ones cluster tightly at the top, all centred on the true effect.
Asymmetry betrays the file-drawer problem: “boring” or null results are never published, while flukes get into print (Reinhart 2015). That buries small studies with null results, gouging a gap in the funnel’s bottom corner — visible asymmetry that Egger’s test can detect statistically (Reinhart 2015). A meta-analysis that ignores its funnel can certify an inflated effect built from a censored literature.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
Garbage in, precisely-estimated garbage out
The decisive caution: meta-analysis is no cure for bad inputs (Reinhart 2015; Gerber and Green 2012). If the underlying studies suffer p-hacking, truth inflation, or double-dipping, pooling them aggregates and amplifies those errors (Reinhart 2015). A meta-analysis of flawed studies yields a “precisely estimated” error — the false confidence of a tight interval around the wrong number (Gerber and Green 2012). Synthesis raises precision, not validity.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Skipping the systematic search. Cherry-picked studies make a biased pool; the review must be exhaustive and pre-registered. (Bryman 2016)
Pooling apples and oranges. High I^2 means the studies aren’t estimating one effect — don’t report a single number without investigating heterogeneity. (Bryman 2016)
Ignoring publication bias. Always inspect the funnel plot / Egger’s test; a tidy pooled estimate from a censored literature is inflated. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating precision as validity. A narrow pooled CI around biased studies is precisely wrong. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Fixed-effect by default. If effects plausibly vary across contexts, use random-effects and report \tau^2. (Bryman 2016)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — systematic review as a replicable, transparent process (vs narrative review); meta-analysis as combining effect sizes (Cohen’s d, odds ratios, r); fixed-effect (inverse-variance weighting, one true effect) vs random-effects (\tau^2, wider CIs); heterogeneity via Cochran’s Q and I^2 (>50% high).
Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — the funnel plot, the file-drawer problem / publication bias, Egger’s test, and how meta-analysis amplifies p-hacking and bias in the source studies.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — meta-analysis of flawed studies as a “precisely estimated” error; extrapolation beyond the data rests on fallible assumptions.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Inverse-variance weighting. With study effects d_i and variances v_i=\text{SE}_i^2, the fixed-effect pooled estimate and its variance are

\hat\mu = \frac{\sum_i w_i d_i}{\sum_i w_i},\quad w_i=\frac1{v_i},
\qquad \operatorname{Var}(\hat\mu)=\frac1{\sum_i w_i}.

Weighting by precision is the minimum-variance linear combination — the meta-analytic echo of GLS. The code computes exactly this.
Heterogeneity and \tau^2. Cochran’s Q=\sum_i w_i(d_i-\hat\mu)^2 is \chi^2_{K-1} under homogeneity. Then I^2 = \max\!\big(0,\ (Q-(K-1))/Q\big) — the share of variance beyond chance. The DerSimonian–Laird random-effects estimator sets \hat\tau^2=\max\!\big(0,\,(Q-(K-1))/C\big) with C=\sum w_i-\sum w_i^2/\sum w_i, and re-weights with w_i^*=1/(v_i+\hat\tau^2) — adding the between-study variance to every study’s, which shrinks the weight gap between large and small studies and widens the pooled interval.
Egger’s test. Regress the standardised effect d_i/\text{SE}_i on precision 1/\text{SE}_i; a nonzero intercept indicates funnel asymmetry — small studies systematically reporting larger effects, the signature of publication bias.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Meta-Analysis & Research Synthesis
text:
Connections

Builds on: effect sizes (the common currency), statistical power (why pooling helps), and GLS (inverse-variance = precision weighting).
Diagnoses: the replication crisis & questionable research practices and the multiple-testing / garden of forking paths that inflate the source literature.
Feeds: from evidence to decision — a pooled estimate is often the input to policy.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text: The replication crisis is not mostly a fraud problem. It is a problem of flexibility: every analysis involves dozens of defensible choices — which outliers to drop, which covariates to include, which outcome to feature, when to stop collecting — and if you make those choices after seeing the data, you can find significance in almost anything. This is Goodhart’s law turned on researchers: when a p-value below 0.05 becomes the target for publication, the analysis bends until it clears the bar. The result is an honest literature full of effects that aren’t there.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Why it matters
Reinhart’s central, unsettling claim: most false findings come not from cheats but from honest researchers with poor statistical instincts. (Reinhart 2015) That makes this everyone’s problem — including yours. The defences (preregistration, replication, correction for multiple testing) only work if you can recognise the failure modes in your own workflow. This entry catalogues how a sincere analysis goes wrong, turning the replication crisis from a headline into a set of habits to guard against.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Researcher degrees of freedom
Reinhart’s name for the root cause: the “enormous amount of freedom” a researcher has in analysing data. (Reinhart 2015) Each choice is individually defensible:

Which observations to exclude as outliers (drop the subjects who had diarrhea during the diet trial?).
How to define the groups (where exactly does “overweight” begin?).
Which covariates to control for.
Which outcome to treat as primary, and when to stop collecting data.

None is fraud. But made after seeing the data, they let you — usually unconsciously — pick the path most flattering to your hypothesis. Reinhart’s striking number: handling these choices differently can change the estimated effect size by a factor of two. (Reinhart 2015) The analysis is honest at every step and biased as a whole.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
section: P-hacking and the garden of forking paths
text:
P-hacking and the garden of forking paths
P-hacking is selectively analysing data until the p-value drops below 0.05. (Reinhart 2015) Its subtler cousin is the garden of forking paths: even a researcher who runs one analysis can be biased, because the analysis they chose was contingent on the data — at each fork they took the defensible branch that happened to lead to significance. (Reinhart 2015) No conscious cherry-picking is required; the flexibility alone does the damage.






NoteReinhart’s anti-aging Beatles



The demonstration that made the point unforgettable: researchers “proved” that listening to the Beatles’ When I’m Sixty-Four made undergraduates nearly a year and a half younger — a logical impossibility. They did it honestly, by deciding after seeing the data which covariates to control for (the subject’s father’s age, etc.) and which outcome to report. Simulation shows this kind of flexibility can push the false-positive rate as high as 50%. (Reinhart 2015) If you can “discover” reverse aging, you can discover anything.


The figure shows the mechanism directly: take pure noise, try a handful of defensible analyses, report the best — and “significance” appears far more than the advertised 5% of the time.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
studies, n, forks = 4000, 40, 10

def two_sided_p(z):
    return 2 * (1 - NormalDist().cdf(abs(z)))

single_p, hacked_p = [], []
for _ in range(studies):
    # honest single test on a null effect
    g1 = rng.normal(0, 1, n); g2 = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    z = (g1.mean() - g2.mean()) / np.sqrt(g1.var(ddof=1)/n + g2.var(ddof=1)/n)
    single_p.append(two_sided_p(z))
    # garden of forking paths: try `forks` defensible analyses, keep the smallest p
    ps = []
    for _ in range(forks):
        a = rng.normal(0, 1, n); b = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
        zz = (a.mean() - b.mean()) / np.sqrt(a.var(ddof=1)/n + b.var(ddof=1)/n)
        ps.append(two_sided_p(zz))
    hacked_p.append(min(ps))

single_p, hacked_p = np.array(single_p), np.array(hacked_p)
fp_single = (single_p < 0.05).mean()
fp_hacked = (hacked_p < 0.05).mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
bins = np.linspace(0, 1, 21)
ax.hist(single_p, bins=bins, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.7, density=True,
        label=f"single honest test — {fp_single*100:.0f}% significant")
ax.hist(hacked_p, bins=bins, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.55, density=True,
        label=f"best of {forks} analyses — {fp_hacked*100:.0f}% significant")
ax.axvline(0.05, color="#1f3a5f", ls="--", lw=1.6)
ax.text(0.06, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.9, "p = 0.05", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=8)
ax.set_xlabel("reported p-value (data with NO real effect)")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
