ax.set_title("Flexibility manufactures significance from noise")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The garden of forking paths. Each study here is pure noise — there is no real effect. An honest single test (grey) gives p-values that are uniform, crossing 0.05 exactly 5% of the time. But an analyst who tries 10 defensible analyses per study and reports the smallest p-value (red) finds ‘significance’ in roughly 40% of studies — eight times the nominal rate. The flexibility, not dishonesty, manufactures the false positives.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Multiple comparisons
The most quantifiable QRP: run enough tests and false positives are guaranteed. (Reinhart 2015) Test 20 independent true-null hypotheses at \alpha=0.05, and the chance of at least one false positive is 1-(1-0.05)^{20}\approx \mathbf{64\%}. (Reinhart 2015) This is the jelly-bean problem: test 20 colours of jelly bean for a link to acne and you’ll “find” one — a famous comic, and a real research pathology. (Reinhart 2015) The corrections: (Reinhart 2015)

Bonferroni — divide the threshold by the number of tests (\alpha/n); controls the chance of any false positive (family-wise error), at the cost of power.
Benjamini–Hochberg — controls the false discovery rate (the expected fraction of significant results that are false), less conservative and often more sensible when many tests are genuinely exploratory.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
HARKing and circular analysis
HARKing — Hypothesising After Results are Known — is dressing up a pattern you found in the data as a hypothesis you predicted. (Reinhart 2015) It is a form of circular analysis or “double-dipping”: using the same data to both generate and test a hypothesis. The canonical case is neuroimaging — select a brain “region of interest” because it lit up to a stimulus, then run a detailed test on that same region, and you’ve guaranteed an inflated effect: you selected for the strongest random noise, then “confirmed” it. (Reinhart 2015) Discovery and confirmation must use different data.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Optional stopping
Optional stopping is peeking at the data as it accumulates and stopping the moment p<0.05. (Reinhart 2015) Because the p-value random-walks as n grows, it will dip below 0.05 by chance eventually even under the null — and if you stop there, you bank a false positive. Testing after every subject and stopping at significance massively inflates the error rate. The clinical signature: medical trials stopped early for benefit exaggerate their effects by an average of 29% versus trials that ran to completion. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
section: Publication bias and the file drawer
text:
Publication bias and the file drawer
Even a literature of honest, correct studies can lie in aggregate, because of what gets published. Eye-catching significant results are far more publishable than null findings — the file-drawer problem — so the published record is a biased sample of all results. (Reinhart 2015) Two cases: (Reinhart 2015)

TP53 and cancer. A meta-analysis of 18 published studies showed a highly significant correlation; add the unpublished data and the effect vanished.
Reboxetine. Pfizer’s antidepressant looked effective in the published trials — while studies comprising three times more data, showing it didn’t work, went unmentioned.

This is why meta-analysis must explicitly probe for publication bias: pooling a biased sample faithfully reproduces the bias.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
section: Truth inflation and the winner’s curse
text:
Truth inflation and the winner’s curse
The final twist, and the link back to statistical power: truth inflation (a Type M, or magnitude, error). (Reinhart 2015) An underpowered study has high sampling variability, so it only reaches significance when it draws a lucky, large-effect sample. The published estimate is therefore systematically too big — the winner’s curse. (Reinhart 2015) Hence the Proteus phenomenon: in fast-moving fields the earliest published effects are the most extreme, and follow-ups shrink them — and higher-impact journals, chasing splashier findings, may overstate effects more. (Reinhart 2015) A significant result from a small study is not just possibly false; if true, it is probably exaggerated.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Reading other people’s research
Every practice above is also a checklist for consuming research — you spend more time reading studies than producing them, and the same flaws hide in others’ work. (Reinhart 2015) Martin & Bridgmon offer a disciplined five-step way to evaluate a paper before trusting its headline: (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

Gain a general overview — what is the study about, and what does it claim?
Ascertain the rationale — why was it done; what gap or question motivates it?
Identify the theoretical framework — what assumptions and prior theory underpin the design?
Identify the setting — who, where, when; the sample and context that bound the result.
Determine the nature of the research — design, methods, and analysis: is the inference licensed by the design? (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

Two habits sharpen the read. First, return to the research question as your anchor — it is easy to get lost in charts and jargon, so keep asking whether each result actually bears on the question posed. (Reinhart 2015) Second, look for the mundane failure modes this Part catalogues: an uncorrected multiple-comparisons fishing expedition, a subgroup that smells of HARKing, a small “significant” study with an implausibly large effect, a non-reproducible analysis with no shared data or code, sloppy code errors like mislabelled columns. (Reinhart 2015) Critical consumption is not cynicism — it is applying to others’ work the same scrutiny you owe your own.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Deciding the analysis after seeing the data. Outlier rules, covariates, subgroups, and the primary outcome should be fixed in advance; otherwise you walk the garden of forking paths. (Reinhart 2015)
Running many tests and reporting the winners. Correct for multiplicity (Bonferroni / FDR) and report all the tests you ran, not just the significant ones. (Reinhart 2015)
Testing a hypothesis on the data that generated it. HARKing/double-dipping inflates effects; split discovery from confirmation. (Reinhart 2015)
Peeking and stopping at significance. Optional stopping inflates false positives and effect sizes; fix the sample size or use a proper sequential design. (Reinhart 2015)
Trusting a small significant study’s effect size. Truth inflation means it’s likely overstated; expect regression toward a smaller true effect. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — false results from honest researchers via researcher degrees of freedom (outliers/groups/covariates; effect sizes off by 2×); p-hacking and the garden of forking paths (the When I’m Sixty-Four study; false-positive rates to 50%); multiple comparisons (1-0.95^{20}\approx64\%; jelly beans; Bonferroni and Benjamini–Hochberg FDR); HARKing / circular analysis / double-dipping (neuroimaging ROI); optional stopping (early- stopped trials exaggerate ~29%); publication bias / file drawer (TP53; Reboxetine); truth inflation / Type M / winner’s curse and the Proteus phenomenon; the consumer’s habit of returning to the research question and spotting these failure modes in others’ work.
Martin & Bridgmon — the five-step process for critically evaluating a study (overview, rationale, theoretical framework, setting, nature of the research).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Family-wise error. For m independent true-null tests at level \alpha, the probability of at least one false rejection is 
\text{FWER}=1-(1-\alpha)^m,
 \to1 as m grows (64\% at m=20,\alpha=0.05). Bonferroni uses \alpha/m per test, bounding FWER \le\alpha. Benjamini–Hochberg instead orders the p-values p_{(1)}\le\dots\le p_{(m)} and rejects the largest k with p_{(k)}\le \frac{k}{m}\alpha, controlling the expected false discovery proportion at \alpha — more powerful when many alternatives are true.
Optional stopping inflates \alpha. Under the null the test statistic behaves like a random walk; by the law of the iterated logarithm it crosses any fixed boundary infinitely often as n\to\infty, so \Pr(\exists\,n: p_n<0.05)\to1. Stopping at the first crossing therefore rejects with probability far above 0.05.
Truth inflation (Type M). Condition the estimate \hat\theta\sim\mathcal N(\theta,\sigma^2) on significance |\hat\theta|>1.96\,\sigma. When power is low (\theta/\sigma small), the conditional mean \mathbb E[\hat\theta\mid \text{significant}] greatly exceeds \theta — the published effect is inflated, by a factor that grows as power falls. This is the formal winner’s curse, and the reason power protects magnitude, not just detection.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Connections

Builds on: the replication crisis (the symptom), hypothesis testing and power (the machinery being abused), and the tyranny of metrics (the p<0.05 target as a Goodhart trap).
Leads to: open & reproducible science — preregistration, replication, and sharing as the structural fixes for every practice here.
Reappears in: communicating findings (significant ≠ important) and from evidence to decision (discounting inflated literatures).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text: Everything before this taught you to measure well. This entry is the warning that measuring is never neutral. The instant a number carries stakes — pay, promotion, ranking, funding — the people being measured become self-conscious actors who optimise the number, not the goal beneath it. Test scores rise while learning stagnates; survival rates climb because surgeons turn away the sick. The metric improves; the world it represented does not. Knowing when not to trust a number is the final skill of the quantitative researcher.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Why it matters
The whole manual has been a machine for producing trustworthy numbers. Muller’s The Tyranny of Metrics supplies the conscience: a number that drives behaviour changes the behaviour it measures, often perversely. (Muller 2018) This is not a niche caution — it is the central risk of applied quantitative work, where your estimate doesn’t sit in a journal but becomes a target someone is paid to hit. The researcher who can build a metric and foresee how it will be gamed is the one who can be trusted with a real decision.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Metric fixation
Muller names the disease metric fixation: “the seemingly irresistible pressure to measure performance, to publicize it, and to reward it.” (Muller 2018) It rests on three beliefs: (Muller 2018)

Replace judgment with numbers — that standardised, comparable metrics can and should substitute for the judgment of experienced practitioners.
Accountability = transparency — that making the numbers public guarantees institutions serve their purpose.
Motivate by extrinsic reward — that people work best when pay and penalties are tied to measured performance (pay-for-performance, league tables).

Each belief is plausible; together they license replacing professional judgment with a dashboard. The rest of the entry is what goes wrong when you do.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Goodhart’s law and Campbell’s law
Two “laws” name the mechanism: (Muller 2018)

Goodhart’s law — “when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.”
Campbell’s law — “the more any quantitative social indicator is used for social decision-making, the more subject it will be to corruption pressures and the more apt it will be to distort and corrupt the social processes it is intended to monitor.”

The engine under both: people are self-conscious actors. Attach stakes to a measurement and they respond by skewing the data to maximise the score, whether or not the underlying reality improves. (Muller 2018) A measure passively observed and the same measure optimised against are different objects — the act of targeting corrupts the signal. The figure shows the signature: a proxy that faithfully tracks the goal until the day it becomes a target, then peels away.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
t = np.arange(0, 24)
target_start = 12
# Before targeting, proxy and true goal move together; after, proxy is gamed up, goal drifts down.
true_goal = 50 + 1.2 * t + rng.normal(0, 1.0, t.size)
true_goal[target_start:] = true_goal[target_start-1] - 0.8 * (t[target_start:] - t[target_start-1]) \
                           + rng.normal(0, 1.0, t.size - target_start)
proxy = true_goal.copy()
proxy[target_start:] = true_goal[target_start-1] + 3.2 * (t[target_start:] - t[target_start-1]) \
                       + rng.normal(0, 1.0, t.size - target_start)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.plot(t, proxy, "o-", color="#c0392b", lw=2.2, ms=3, label="measured proxy (the metric)")
ax.plot(t, true_goal, "o-", color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, ms=3, label="true goal (what we care about)")
ax.axvline(target_start - 0.5, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.4)
ax.text(target_start - 0.3, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.96, "metric becomes a target", fontsize=8, color="#555")
ax.fill_between(t[target_start:], true_goal[target_start:], proxy[target_start:],
                color="#c0392b", alpha=0.10)
ax.text(18, 70, "the gap =\nGoodhart corruption", fontsize=8.5, color="#b03a2e", ha="center")
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("performance")
ax.set_title("A measure tracks the goal — until it becomes a target")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Goodhart’s law in action. Before the metric carries stakes (left of the dashed line), the measured proxy tracks the true goal it was meant to capture. Once it becomes a target — tied to pay, ranking, or funding — effort flows into the proxy itself: it keeps climbing (gamed) while the true outcome stalls or declines. The gap that opens after targeting is the corruption Goodhart and Campbell describe.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
How metrics get gamed
When judgement is reduced to a narrow score, practitioners “juke the stats.” Muller catalogues the recurring tactics: (Muller 2018)

Teaching to the test. Effort shifts from the institution’s true purpose to whatever lifts the metric — narrowing a curriculum to the tested subjects and test-taking strategy.
Creaming / cherry-picking. Avoid the hard cases that might dent your success rate. Cardiac surgeons facing public scorecards refer the sickest patients elsewhere — improving their mortality numbers by not treating the people who most need treatment.
Lowering standards. Hit the target by making it easier — inflating graduation rates by making courses easier to pass.
Outright gaming and fraud. Misclassify or falsify: police downgrading felonies to misdemeanours (or thefts to “lost property”) so they don’t count against headline crime.
Short-termism. Sacrifice long-term investment (R&D, training) for an immediate numerical bump.
Goal displacement. The deepest pathology — the metric means replace the organisational ends they were meant to serve. Hitting the number becomes the mission.







NoteMuller’s case files




Education (No Child Left Behind). High-stakes testing narrowed curricula to math and English and, in several cities, produced systematic cheating — teachers altering answer sheets. (Muller 2018)
Healthcare (surgical scorecards). Public mortality rankings drove case-selection bias: surgeons declined high-risk patients to protect their stats. (Muller 2018)
Policing (Compstat / FBI reports). Pressure to “reduce crime” led to reclassifying offences downward and chasing easy street arrests over hard long-term cases. (Muller 2018)
The military (Vietnam body counts). McNamara’s reliance on body counts as a success metric produced fabricated numbers — and soldiers risking their lives to find corpses to hit the target. (Muller 2018)
Universities (rankings). League tables push schools toward glossy marketing and revenue-maximising programs over their educational mission. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
section: Surrogate outcomes: measuring the easy thing
text:
Surrogate outcomes: measuring the easy thing
A quieter failure: metric fixation pushes you to measure what is easy to count, not what matters. Organisations track inputs (money spent) or processes (workshops held) because the real outcomes are complex or delayed. (Muller 2018) Muller’s barb is worth memorising: “the ease of measuring may be inversely proportional to the significance of what is measured.” (Muller 2018) This is the field cousin of the surrogate-outcome and proxy-variable problems — only here the substitution is driven by managerial convenience, not statistical necessity.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
The costs
Metric fixation is not free, even when it isn’t gamed: (Muller 2018)

A data arms race. Effort migrates from frontline work to documenting it — more managers and analysts, producers spending their hours on reports instead of the job.
Discouraged innovation. Real innovation needs experiments that might fail; metrics punish failure, so people retreat to safe, established, measurable goals.
Demoralisation. Professionals resent the loss of autonomy and a scoreboard that conflicts with their vocational ethos — driving the most creative people out.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
The balanced verdict
Muller is not anti-measurement — he is anti-fixation. (Muller 2018) Metrics are a valuable complement to judgment, never a replacement for it. They work best when they are: (Muller 2018)

Internal and diagnostic — used by practitioners themselves to spot outliers and refine practice, not as a public cudgel.
Low-stakes — not the sole basis of pay or promotion, so the incentive to game is weak.
Bottom-up — designed with the people measured, who then trust the metric’s validity.

The mature stance the whole book has built toward: a number is an input to judgment, not a substitute for it. The skill is holding both — rigorous measurement and the wisdom to know its limits.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Attaching high stakes to a single number. Goodhart guarantees it will be gamed; the tighter the incentive, the faster the corruption. (Muller 2018)
Measuring the proxy and forgetting the goal. Optimise a surrogate and you can improve it while the real outcome decays — verify the proxy still tracks the thing you care about. (Muller 2018)
Counting inputs/processes as if they were outcomes. Workshops held is not learning gained; spend follows ease of measurement, not importance. (Muller 2018)
Ignoring gaming when designing a metric. If you can’t say how people will game it, you haven’t finished designing it. (Muller 2018)
Replacing professional judgment wholesale. Metrics inform judgment; they don’t substitute for the experience that knows which cases the number misreads. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Muller, The Tyranny of Metrics — metric fixation (replace judgment / accountability-as- transparency / extrinsic reward); Goodhart’s and Campbell’s laws and the self-conscious-actor mechanism; the gaming taxonomy (teaching to the test, creaming, lowering standards, fraud, short-termism, goal displacement); surrogate/proxy outcomes and “ease of measuring inversely proportional to significance”; the costs (data arms race, discouraged innovation, demoralisation); the balanced verdict (metrics as complement — internal, low-stakes, bottom-up); and the case files: NCLB, surgical scorecards, Compstat policing, Vietnam body counts, university rankings.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Appendix · the mechanism

Goodhart formalised. Let the true goal be G and a proxy M=G+\eta with measurement slack \eta an agent can influence at cost. Passively, \eta\approx0 and \operatorname{Corr}(M,G)\approx1 — the proxy is informative. Introduce a reward R(M) for high M. A rational agent now invests effort to raise \eta (gaming) up to where marginal reward equals marginal cost, independently of G. The observed M rises, but \operatorname{Corr}(M,G) collapses — the very act of using M for control destroys its correlation with G. The proxy was a good measure because no one was optimising it; the optimisation is what breaks it.
Why surrogates drift. A surrogate S validated to satisfy \mathbb E[G\mid S]=f(S) under observational conditions need not preserve that relation under intervention on S — exactly the \operatorname{do} vs conditioning distinction. Optimising S intervenes on it, and the conditional relation that licensed S as a stand-in can fail — the managerial twin of reward hacking.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Connections

Builds on: concepts & measurement (validity and operationalisation — now under incentive pressure), the replication crisis (incentives distorting science), and proxy/surrogate variables.
Leads to: questionable research practices (Goodhart applied to researchers chasing significance) and from evidence to decision (using numbers without being ruled by them).
Reappears in: the limits of quantification, the book’s closing case for judgment.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
text: Take a row of cells, each on or off. Give every cell one dumb rule: look at yourself and your two neighbours, and switch on or off accordingly. Update everyone simultaneously, then again, and again. That is a cellular automaton — about the simplest system imaginable. And yet, depending on the rule, it can die to a blank screen, settle into stripes, boil into chaos, or grow intricate moving structures that turn out to be capable of any computation a computer can do. Cellular automata are the cleanest demonstration of the central claim of complexity science: simple local rules can generate unlimited global complexity.
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
text:
Why it matters
Cellular automata (CA) strip the idea of emergence down to its bones. There is no randomness, no hidden machinery, no central plan — just a local rule applied everywhere at once — and complexity still pours out (Mitchell 2009). That makes CA the proving ground for the field’s foundational intuition and the source of its sharpest concepts: Wolfram’s classification of dynamical behaviour, the discovery that simple rules can be computationally universal, and the edge of chaos — the idea that the capacity for complex computation lives precisely at the boundary between order and disorder (Sayama 2015; Mitchell 2009). Every agent-based model and every “simple rules, rich behaviour” argument traces back here.
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
section: Wolfram’s elementary automata and the four classes
text:
Wolfram’s elementary automata and the four classes
The simplest CA — Stephen Wolfram’s elementary rules — use a 1-D line, two states, and the three-cell neighbourhood (left, self, right). There are 2^3=8 possible neighbourhood patterns, and a rule assigns an output to each, so there are exactly 2^8 = 256 elementary rules, numbered 0–255 by reading the eight outputs as a binary number (Mitchell 2009). We can draw the entire history of one as a space-time diagram: space across, time down.
Running all 256, Wolfram found their behaviour falls into just four classes (Mitchell 2009):

Class 1 — fixed. Everything collapses to a single uniform state.
Class 2 — periodic. Settles into stable or simply repeating patterns (stripes, blocks).
Class 3 — chaotic. Aperiodic, random-looking noise (e.g. Rule 30, used as a randomness source).
Class 4 — complex. Localised structures that move and interact in intricate ways — neither frozen nor random. Rule 110 is the exemplar, and it was proven capable of universal computation (Mitchell 2009).

The figure builds the space-time diagrams of four rules, one per class, entirely by hand.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def elementary_ca(rule, width, steps):
    """Run an elementary (1-D, 2-state, 3-neighbour) CA from a single ON cell."""
    table = np.array([(rule >> i) & 1 for i in range(8)], dtype=np.uint8)
    state = np.zeros(width, dtype=np.uint8)
    state[width // 2] = 1
    grid = np.empty((steps, width), dtype=np.uint8)
    for t in range(steps):
        grid[t] = state
        left  = np.roll(state, 1)
        right = np.roll(state, -1)
        idx = (left << 2) | (state << 1) | right     # neighbourhood -> 0..7
        state = table[idx]
    return grid

width, steps = 201, 100
rules = [(250, "Rule 250 · Class 1–2 (ordered)"),
         (90,  "Rule 90 · nested fractal"),
         (30,  "Rule 30 · Class 3 (chaotic)"),
         (110, "Rule 110 · Class 4 (complex, universal)")]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(9.4, 6.4))
for ax, (rule, title) in zip(axes.ravel(), rules):
    ax.imshow(elementary_ca(rule, width, steps), cmap="binary",
              interpolation="nearest", aspect="auto")
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=9)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
fig.suptitle("Elementary cellular automata: same machinery, four behaviours", fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Wolfram’s four classes, each grown by hand from a single ON cell at the top, with time running downward. Rule 250 (Class 1/2) is utterly regular — a predictable triangle. Rule 90 produces a nested fractal — the Sierpiński triangle — order with structure at every scale. Rule 30 (Class 3) erupts into chaos: deterministic, yet its centre column is random enough to seed random-number generators. Rule 110 (Class 4) is the fascinating in-between — neither periodic nor chaotic but a shifting traffic of localised structures (particles) colliding and interacting; this regime is rich enough to be Turing-complete. Same trivial machinery, four utterly different worlds — set only by the rule.
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
text:
Conway’s Game of Life
The most celebrated CA is Conway’s Game of Life, a 2-D automaton whose cells live or die by counting live neighbours (Sayama 2015):

Birth — a dead cell with exactly three live neighbours becomes alive.
Survival — a live cell with two or three live neighbours stays alive.
Death — otherwise it dies, of loneliness (fewer than two) or overcrowding (more than three).

From these three lines emerges a whole bestiary: still lifes (stable blocks), oscillators (blinkers that cycle), and — most famously — gliders: small patterns that walk across the grid, propagating information (Sayama 2015). There are even glider guns that emit gliders forever. Because you can build logic gates and memory out of colliding gliders, the Game of Life is Turing-complete: a universal computer hiding inside a three-rule toy (Sayama 2015). (A glider is just five cells in a stepped shape that returns to itself, shifted one square diagonally, every four ticks — a “particle” of pure pattern.)
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
text:
The edge of chaos
Why are Class-4 rules like Rule 110 and systems like the Game of Life special? Christopher Langton’s answer is the edge of chaos (Mitchell 2009). Tune a parameter \lambda that measures how “active” a rule is — from frozen order at one end to full chaos at the other — and the complex, computation-capable behaviour appears in a narrow band at the phase transition between them (Mitchell 2009). The intuition is compelling: real computation needs both storage (stable structures to hold information — an ordered trait) and transmission (moving signals to process it — a dynamic trait). Only at the edge of chaos can a system do both at once (Mitchell 2009). It is a recurring theme of complexity science — and the through-line connecting CA back to self-organised criticality and complex adaptive systems, all of which prize that fertile boundary between rigidity and noise.
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading global intent into local rules. A glider is not “trying” to move and Life is not “trying” to compute; behaviour is the parallel application of a local rule, nothing more. (Sayama 2015)
Sequential updating. Updating cells one at a time instead of all at once from the same snapshot changes the dynamics — synchronous parallelism is part of the model. (Sayama 2015)
Expecting the rule to reveal the behaviour. You generally cannot read a rule and predict its class; for Class-4 rules there is no shortcut but to run it (a consequence of computational universality). (Mitchell 2009)
Treating “edge of chaos” as a precise law. It is a powerful organising idea, not a sharp, universally-located threshold; its exact form depends on the system. (Mitchell 2009)
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Sayama, Introduction to the Modeling and Analysis of Complex Systems — the formal CA setup (grid, states, neighbourhood, local parallel rule); Conway’s Game of Life (birth/survival/death, gliders, glider guns, Turing-completeness).
Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — Wolfram’s elementary CA and the four classes, Rule 110 and universal computation, and Langton’s edge of chaos at the order–chaos phase transition.
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Counting elementary rules. A neighbourhood is a 3-bit pattern (\text{left},\text{self},\text{right}), so there are 2^3=8 neighbourhoods. A rule assigns one output bit to each, giving 2^{8}=256 rules. The rule number is the 8-bit output string read as a binary integer — e.g. “Rule 110” =01101110_2 specifies the outputs for neighbourhoods 111,110,\dots,000. The update is s_i^{t+1}=R\!\left(s_{i-1}^{t},s_i^{t},s_{i+1}^{t}\right), applied to every i from the same time-t snapshot.
Langton’s \lambda. For a CA with k states, let n of the k^{(\text{neighbourhood size})} rule-table entries map to a chosen “quiescent” state; then \lambda = 1-\tfrac{n}{k^{\dots}} is the fraction of active transitions. Sweeping \lambda from 0 (all quiescent, Class 1) to its maximum (Class 3 chaos) passes through an intermediate value where Class-4 complexity — and the longest transients / largest correlation lengths — concentrate: the edge of chaos.
Universality. Rule 110 and the Game of Life are computationally universal: one can encode an arbitrary program and its input as an initial configuration whose evolution carries out the computation. A corollary is undecidability — no general algorithm can predict their long-run behaviour faster than running them, so “what will this do?” has, in general, no shortcut.
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos
text:
Connections

Builds on: emergence & self-organisation (CA are emergence in its purest form) and nonlinear dynamics & chaos (Class-3 chaos, Class-4 complexity).
Relates to: agent-based modelling (a CA is an ABM with cells fixed in a lattice) and complex adaptive systems (both prize the edge of chaos).
Closes the loop with: self-organised criticality and power laws & scaling — different routes to the same destination, the productive boundary between order and randomness.
crumbs: Cellular Automata & the Edge of Chaos

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text: Networks aren’t uniform — they clump. A community is a set of nodes more densely connected to each other than to the rest, and finding communities reveals the social circles, political factions, topic clusters, and echo chambers that organise a system (Menczer et al. 2020). The standard tool is modularity (a score for any proposed grouping) maximised by the Louvain algorithm — and the clumping itself is usually driven by homophily, the pull of like toward like.

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
Why it matters
Community structure is where networks meet the social questions this manual cares about: polarisation, segregation, filter bubbles, in-group/out-group dynamics. Detecting it lets you classify nodes by role — core members versus boundary/bridge nodes — and turns “is this network polarised?” into a measurable quantity.

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
What a community is
A community (module, cluster) is a group of nodes connected more densely internally than to the outside (Menczer et al. 2020). Socially they are friendship circles, liberal-vs-conservative blog blocs, web topic clusters, and echo chambers of like-minded exposure (Menczer et al. 2020). Knowing the partition lets you separate core members from boundary nodes that broker between groups (Menczer et al. 2020).

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
Modularity: scoring a partition
Modularity Q measures how much denser the within-community links are than expected by chance (Menczer et al. 2020). “By chance” means a configuration model — a randomised network preserving each node’s degree but rewiring at random (Menczer et al. 2020). For an undirected network:

Q = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i,j}\Big[A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}\Big]\,\delta(c_i, c_j),

with m edges, degrees k_i, and \delta(c_i,c_j)=1 when i,j share a community (Menczer et al. 2020). The bracket is observed minus expected internal connection; a high Q means internal links far exceed random fluctuation — a good partition (Menczer et al. 2020).

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
Louvain: finding the partition
Maximising Q over all partitions is intractable, so the Louvain method greedily approximates it — and is parameter-free and scales to millions of nodes (Menczer et al. 2020):

Local optimisation. Start every node in its own community; repeatedly move each node into the neighbouring community that most increases Q (\Delta Q) until no move helps (Menczer et al. 2020).
Aggregation. Collapse each community into a super-node, building a smaller meta-network whose edge weights sum the underlying links (Menczer et al. 2020).
Recurse the two phases on the meta-network until Q can’t improve (Menczer et al. 2020).



Show the figure code (modularity + Louvain local-moving, by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
sizes = [13, 13, 13]; N = sum(sizes)
label_true = np.concatenate([[g]*s for g, s in enumerate(sizes)])
A = np.zeros((N, N), int)
for i in range(N):
    for j in range(i+1, N):
        p = 0.45 if label_true[i] == label_true[j] else 0.02
        if rng.random() < p: A[i, j] = A[j, i] = 1
k = A.sum(1); m = A.sum() / 2; adj = [np.where(A[i])[0] for i in range(N)]

def modularity(comm):
    Q = 0.0
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(N):
            if comm[i] == comm[j]:
                Q += A[i, j] - k[i]*k[j]/(2*m)
    return Q/(2*m)

# Louvain phase 1: local moving
comm = np.arange(N)
improved = True
while improved:
    improved = False
    for i in rng.permutation(N):
        nbr_comms = set(comm[adj[i]]) | {comm[i]}
        best, bestgain = comm[i], 0.0
        for c in nbr_comms:
            kin = sum(1 for nb in adj[i] if comm[nb] == c)          # i's edges into c
            sigtot = k[(comm == c)].sum() - (k[i] if comm[i] == c else 0)
            gain = kin - k[i]*sigtot/(2*m)                          # ∝ ΔQ
            if gain > bestgain + 1e-9: best, bestgain = c, gain
        if best != comm[i]: comm[i] = best; improved = True

# relabel communities 0..K-1
_, comm = np.unique(comm, return_inverse=True)
Q_det = modularity(comm)
Q_rand = modularity(rng.integers(0, comm.max()+1, N))

# layout: clusters apart, jitter within
ctr = {c: (np.cos(2*np.pi*c/(comm.max()+1)), np.sin(2*np.pi*c/(comm.max()+1))) for c in range(comm.max()+1)}
pos = np.array([ctr[comm[i]] for i in range(N)]) + rng.normal(0, 0.18, (N, 2))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.6))
for i in range(N):
    for j in adj[i]:
        if j > i: ax.plot(*zip(pos[i], pos[j]), color="#dde1e5", lw=0.5, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(pos[:,0], pos[:,1], c=comm, cmap="Set1", s=90, zorder=2, edgecolor="white")
ax.set_title(f"Louvain recovers the communities:  Q = {Q_det:.2f}  (random partition Q = {Q_rand:.2f})")
ax.axis("off")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Community detection by hand. A network is planted with three groups (dense within, sparse between), then the Louvain local-moving step — repeatedly moving each node to the neighbouring community that most raises modularity — recovers them from scratch (every node starts in its own community). Node colour = detected community; the layout places communities apart. The recovered partition scores modularity Q ≈ 0.6, versus ≈ 0 for a random partition of the same network: modularity quantifies how much more clustered the structure is than chance.

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
Homophily and assortativity
Why do communities form? Largely homophily — the tendency to connect with similar others (“birds of a feather”) (Menczer et al. 2020). The technical measure is assortativity: the correlation between the attributes of connected nodes (Barabási 2016):

Assortative mixing — similar connects to similar (hubs to hubs). Most human social networks are assortative (Barabási 2016).
Disassortative mixing — dissimilar connects (hubs to peripheral nodes), common in biological and technological networks (Barabási 2016).

The social consequence is direct: homophily and assortative mixing naturally produce segregated clusters, and with them polarisation and filter bubbles (Menczer et al. 2020) — the network-structural root of the Schelling-style segregation the Dynamics cluster will simulate.

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
Cautions






WarningCommunity detection is genuinely hard




Resolution limit. Modularity optimisation can’t see small communities — it merges any whose total links fall below \sqrt{2m} into larger ones (Menczer et al. 2020).
Ill-posed. There is no unique definition of a community, so different algorithms give different answers (Menczer et al. 2020).
Degeneracy. Large networks have many distinct partitions with near-optimal Q, so a single “true” community structure may not exist (Menczer et al. 2020).

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reifying the partition. Treat the output as a useful grouping, not the truth — run multiple algorithms/seeds and compare. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Ignoring the resolution limit. If you need fine structure, use multi-resolution methods, not vanilla modularity. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Reading communities as causal groups. Homophily (selection) and influence both produce clusters; the partition alone can’t separate them. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Comparing Q across networks. Q depends on size and density; don’t rank different networks by raw modularity. (Barabási 2016)

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Menczer, Fortunato & Davis, A First Course in Network Science — communities as denser-within groups (factions, echo chambers); modularity Q=\frac{1}{2m}\sum_{ij}[A_{ij}-k_ik_j/2m]\delta(c_i,c_j) vs the configuration null; the Louvain local-moving + aggregation algorithm; homophily; and the resolution limit, ill-posedness, and degeneracy cautions.
Barabási, Network Science — assortativity (assortative vs disassortative mixing) and the size/density dependence of modularity.

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why k_ik_j/2m is “expected.” In the configuration model, a stub of node i connects to a stub of node j with probability \propto k_j; summing over i’s k_i stubs gives an expected k_ik_j/2m edges between i and j. Modularity sums (actual - expected) over within-community pairs, so Q>0 means communities have more internal edges than a degree-matched random graph.
The local-moving gain (the code). Moving isolated node i into community C changes modularity by \Delta Q \propto k_{i,C} - \frac{k_i\,\Sigma_{\text{tot}}(C)}{2m}, where k_{i,C} is i’s edge count into C and \Sigma_{\text{tot}}(C) the total degree of C. Greedily picking the C that maximises this is one Louvain sweep — exactly the loop above. Aggregation then repeats it on the community graph, giving Louvain’s near-linear scaling.
Resolution limit, sketched. Because the null term scales with the whole network’s 2m, two small but clearly separate modules can look like noise relative to the global expectation and get merged once their internal links drop below \sim\sqrt{2m} — a global yardstick applied to local structure.

title: Community Detection & Homophily
text:
Connections

Builds on: network structure & metrics (density, clustering) and centrality (boundary/bridge nodes between communities).
Relates to: factor analysis / spectral methods (spectral community detection is the same eigen-idea) and unsupervised clustering.
Leads to: scale-free networks and spreading on networks — community structure shapes how things diffuse.

title: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner’s Wisdoms
text: Meadows closes Thinking in Systems not with a control manual but with a posture. Because systems are self-organising and have their own internal logic, we cannot “optimise” them like a machine — but we can listen and “dance” with them through humble, adaptive intervention (Meadows 2008). Her final chapter is a set of practitioner wisdoms — the operating ethic for anyone who designs the metrics, policies, and incentives that act on complex social systems.
crumbs: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner's Wisdoms

title: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner’s Wisdoms
text:
Why it matters
A quantitative social scientist is, professionally, a system intervenor — every metric, model, and policy recommendation perturbs a complex system. Meadows’ wisdoms are the antidote to the engineer’s reflex (find the control knob, optimise the number) that the rest of this track has shown to backfire. They are also the humane bookend to the tyranny of metrics.
crumbs: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner's Wisdoms

title: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner’s Wisdoms
text:
The spirit: humility over control
The shift is from imposing your will to a posture of humility and learning (Meadows 2008). You can’t fully model a system you’re embedded in, so you intervene by trial and error, watch the response, and adjust — you dance, you don’t drive.
crumbs: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner's Wisdoms

title: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner’s Wisdoms
text:
The wisdoms
Meadows’ fifteen guidelines, grouped by what they ask of you (Meadows 2008):
Understand before you act.

Get the beat of the system — before disturbing it, watch its behaviour and history over time, not just its events, to learn its inherent patterns.
Expose your mental models to the open air — every model is a simplified map; make your assumptions explicit and testable so others can challenge them.
Stay humble, stay a learner — no one grasps all of a system’s relationships; admit ignorance and keep intervening by trial and error.
Celebrate complexity — systems are “wonderfully complex”; that messiness is their resilience, so don’t strip away the diversity that gives them strength.
Defy the disciplines — systems ignore academic boundaries, so be interdisciplinary and pull insight from any field.

Honour information and the whole.

Honour, respect, and distribute information — information holds systems together; most failures are information failures, so never distort, withhold, or delay it.
Use language with care — language is itself a model; use it to reflect interconnection, not to reduce the world to isolated parts.
Go for the good of the whole — serve the larger system’s goal; avoid suboptimisation, where a part’s narrow goal undermines the whole.
Listen to the wisdom of the system — respect the self-repairing, self-organising mechanisms a system has evolved before you override them.
Locate responsibility within the system — look to how the structure causes the behaviour rather than blaming external actors; restructure rules and interconnections instead of hunting a control knob.

Design for dynamics.

Make feedback policies for feedback systems — design adaptive, self-correcting policies, not rigid rules, so the policy can manoeuvre as the system changes.
Expand time horizons — stocks adjust slowly; short-termism produces fixes that fail, while a long view accounts for delays and momentum.

Keep the right goal.

Pay attention to what is important, not just what is quantifiable — resist metric fixation, where measurable numbers crowd out intangible but essential qualities (purpose, ethics, resilience).
Expand the boundary of caring — the system boundary is a choice; widen it to include more people, ecosystems, and future generations so your interventions don’t dump surprises on others.
Don’t erode the goal of goodness — systems drift to low performance when past failures lower current standards; keep goals high and absolute to prevent a race to the bottom.



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

T = 160
t = np.arange(T)
target = 50 + 25*np.sin(t/18) + 0.15*t            # the moving optimum (cycles + drift)

fixed = np.full(T, target[0])                     # rule set once at t=0, never updated

adaptive = np.empty(T); adaptive[0] = target[0]
for k in range(1, T):
    adaptive[k] = adaptive[k-1] + 0.35*(target[k] - adaptive[k-1])   # policy = a feedback loop

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.2))
ax.plot(t, target, color="#888", ls="--", lw=1.6, label="moving optimum")
ax.plot(t, fixed, color="#c0392b", lw=2.0, label="fixed policy (set once) → drifts off")
ax.plot(t, adaptive, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, label="adaptive feedback policy → tracks")
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("level")
ax.set_title("A rule that doesn't adapt falls behind a system that moves")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: ‘Make feedback policies for feedback systems’, simulated. The system’s optimal level (grey dashed) drifts and cycles over time. A FIXED policy (red) — a rule set once and held — was right at t=0 but drifts ever further from the moving target. An ADAPTIVE feedback policy (green) watches the gap each step and corrects toward it, so it tracks the target through the change. Meadows’ point in one picture: in a system that moves, a rigid rule guarantees growing error; only a policy that is itself a feedback loop keeps up.











TipThe two wisdoms this manual keeps returning to



“Pay attention to what is important, not just what is quantifiable” and “don’t erode the goal of goodness” are the systems-thinking statement of the tyranny of metrics and Goodhart’s law. A quantitative researcher, of all people, must hold them: your job is to measure — and therefore yours is the responsibility not to let the measurable crowd out the mission.
crumbs: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner's Wisdoms

title: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner’s Wisdoms
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Driving instead of dancing. Treating a self-organising system as a machine to optimise invites the traps; intervene humbly and watch the response. (Meadows 2008)
Hunting the control knob. Blaming an external actor instead of the structure misdirects effort; fix the rules and interconnections. (Meadows 2008)
Rigid policy in a moving system. A rule set once and defended drifts ever further from the target; build the feedback into the policy. (Meadows 2008)
Metric fixation. Letting the quantifiable crowd out the important erodes the very goal the metric served. (Meadows 2008)
Short time horizons. Ignoring delays and stock momentum produces fixes that fail. (Meadows 2008)
crumbs: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner's Wisdoms

title: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner’s Wisdoms
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Meadows, Thinking in Systems (“Living in a World of Systems” / “Dancing with Systems”) — the spirit of humility over control, and the fifteen wisdoms: get the beat; expose mental models; honour/distribute information; use language with care; attend to the important not just the quantifiable; make feedback policies for feedback systems; go for the good of the whole; listen to the wisdom of the system; locate responsibility within; stay humble/a learner; celebrate complexity; expand time horizons; defy the disciplines; expand the boundary of caring; don’t erode the goal of goodness.
crumbs: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner's Wisdoms

title: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner’s Wisdoms
text:
Appendix · the dynamics

Fixed vs feedback policy. Let the optimum be a moving target g_t and the policy set a level a_t. A fixed policy holds a_t = g_0, so its tracking error |g_t - g_0| grows without bound as g_t drifts. A feedback policy is itself a balancing loop a_t = a_{t-1} + k\,(g_t - a_{t-1}); for 0<k<1 its error satisfies e_t = (1-k)e_{t-1} + (g_t - g_{t-1}), a stable filter that stays bounded and tracks g_t up to a lag set by k. (Crank k too high in the presence of a perception delay, though, and you re-create the overshoot/oscillation of the previous entry — adaptivity and delay interact.) The moral is structural: matching the form of the policy (a loop) to the form of the problem (a moving, feedback-driven system) is what “dancing” means operationally.
crumbs: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner's Wisdoms

title: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner’s Wisdoms
text:
Connections

Builds on: why systems work (self-organisation and the good of the whole), why systems surprise us (humility follows from bounded rationality and delays), and systems traps & leverage points (the wisdoms are the antidotes to the traps).
Culminates: the Systems Thinking pillar of the Complexity Science track.
Echoes: the tyranny of metrics and from evidence to decision — the ethic of acting wisely on imperfect quantitative knowledge.
crumbs: Dancing with Systems: The Practitioner's Wisdoms

title: Graphs: The Language of Connection
text: A network strips a system down to its connective tissue: nodes (the components) and edges (their interactions). That deliberate simplification is powerful — friendship, collaboration, citation, trade, and contagion are all the same object, a graph, and once you write it as an adjacency matrix every structural question becomes a matter of counting and linear algebra. This entry is the vocabulary the rest of the Network Analysis sub-track is built on.

title: Graphs: The Language of Connection
text:
Why it matters
Social science is dense with relational data — who trusts whom, which firms trade, which papers cite which — and the variables-in-a-table mindset throws the relationships away. The graph keeps them, and turns “how is this system wired?” into precise, computable questions. It is also a structure you’ve already met elsewhere in this manual: causal DAGs and SEM path diagrams are graphs.

title: Graphs: The Language of Connection
text:
What a network is
A network (graph) is a set of nodes (vertices) and the edges (links) between them (Menczer et al. 2020). The edges come in flavours that change everything downstream (Menczer et al. 2020; Barabási 2016):

Undirected vs directed. Undirected edges are mutual (a friendship, a power line); directed edges point from a source to a target (a hyperlink, a phone call, “follows” on social media).
Unweighted vs weighted. Unweighted edges are binary (present/absent); weighted edges carry an intensity — trade volume between countries, messages exchanged between people.
Bipartite. Two distinct node types with edges only between the types — actors and the films they appeared in, authors and papers, voters and bills. Projecting a bipartite graph onto one type yields a co-affiliation network (co-stars, co-authors).

Typical social-science graphs: friendship (Facebook), collaboration (co-authorship), citation (papers citing papers), trade (countries) (Menczer et al. 2020).

title: Graphs: The Language of Connection
text:
The adjacency matrix and degree
The workhorse representation is the adjacency matrix A: an N\times N matrix with A_{ij}=1 if an edge joins nodes i and j, and 0 otherwise (Barabási 2016). (For an undirected graph A is symmetric; for a weighted graph A_{ij} holds the weight.) The matrix is the graph — and it makes the graph computable.
The first quantity it gives is the degree k_i, the number of edges at node i — simply the row (or column) sum (Menczer et al. 2020):

k_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} A_{ij}.

For directed graphs the degree splits into in-degree k_i^{\text{in}} (incoming) and out-degree k_i^{\text{out}} (outgoing) — column and row sums respectively (Menczer et al. 2020). Aggregating over all nodes gives the degree distribution p_k — the probability a random node has degree k (Barabási 2016) — the single most informative summary of a network’s structure, and the star of the scale-free entry.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

labels = ["A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F", "G"]
edges = [(0,1),(0,2),(0,3),(0,4),(1,2),(2,3),(3,4),(4,5),(1,6)]   # A is a hub
N = len(labels)
A = np.zeros((N, N), int)
for i, j in edges:
    A[i, j] = A[j, i] = 1
deg = A.sum(1)

ang = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, N, endpoint=False) + np.pi/2
pos = np.c_[np.cos(ang), np.sin(ang)]

fig, (axG, axM) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2), gridspec_kw={"width_ratios":[1.1,1]})
for i, j in edges:
    axG.plot(*zip(pos[i], pos[j]), color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.4, zorder=1)
axG.scatter(pos[:,0], pos[:,1], s=600 + 220*deg, color="#1f4e79", zorder=2)
for k, (x, y) in enumerate(pos):
    axG.text(x, y, f"{labels[k]}\n(k={deg[k]})", ha="center", va="center", color="white",
             fontsize=8.5, fontweight="bold", zorder=3)
axG.set_title("A network"); axG.axis("off"); axG.set_xlim(-1.4,1.4); axG.set_ylim(-1.4,1.4)

axM.imshow(A, cmap="Blues", vmin=0, vmax=1)
axM.set_xticks(range(N)); axM.set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=8)
axM.set_yticks(range(N)); axM.set_yticklabels(labels, fontsize=8)
for i in range(N):
    for j in range(N):
        axM.text(j, i, A[i,j], ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7,
                 color="white" if A[i,j] else "#bbb")
axM.set_title("its adjacency matrix A")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A small undirected social network (left) and its adjacency matrix (right), built by hand. Each node is drawn on a circle; an edge {i,j} lights up cells A[i,j] and A[j,i] (the matrix is symmetric for an undirected graph). A node’s degree is its row sum — the dark cells in its row — so node A (4 edges) is the hub and node F (1 edge) is a pendant. Everything the sub-track measures — paths, clustering, centrality, communities — is computed from this matrix.

title: Graphs: The Language of Connection
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Ignoring directedness. Treating a “follows” or citation graph as undirected discards real information (influence flows one way). Match the graph type to the relation. (Barabási 2016)
Throwing away weights. Binarising a weighted network (trade, messaging) loses intensity that often carries the signal. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Projecting a bipartite graph carelessly. A co-authorship projection inflates clustering (every paper becomes a clique); account for it. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Confusing the matrix’s zeros. A_{ij}=0 means “no observed edge,” which in sampled social data may mean “unobserved,” not “no relationship.” (Menczer et al. 2020)

title: Graphs: The Language of Connection
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Menczer, Fortunato & Davis, A First Course in Network Science — nodes/edges; directed/undirected, weighted, bipartite types with social examples; the degree k_i=\sum_j A_{ij}, in/out-degree, and the degree distribution p_k.
Barabási, Network Science — the adjacency matrix A (A_{ij}=1 for an edge), symmetry for undirected graphs, and the degree distribution as the network’s defining structural summary.

title: Graphs: The Language of Connection
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The adjacency matrix is a computational engine. Beyond storing edges, A encodes paths: the (i,j) entry of A^k counts the number of walks of length k from i to j. So \operatorname{tr}(A^2)/2 = number of edges, and \operatorname{tr}(A^3)/6 = number of triangles — the raw material for the clustering coefficient. Many network metrics are spectral: the leading eigenvector of A is eigenvector centrality, and the eigenvalues govern spreading thresholds.
Sparsity. Real networks are sparse: the number of edges L \ll \binom{N}{2}, so A is mostly zeros. In practice one stores an adjacency list (each node’s neighbours) rather than the full N\times N matrix — O(N+L) memory instead of O(N^2) — which is what makes BFS and the other algorithms scale.

title: Graphs: The Language of Connection
text:
Connections

Builds on: linear algebra (the adjacency matrix) — and connects to structures you’ve met: causal DAGs (directed graphs), SEM path diagrams, and relational data (a graph stored as tables).
Leads to: network structure & metrics (paths, density, clustering), centrality, and the network models (Erdős–Rényi, small-world, scale-free).

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text: With the adjacency matrix in hand, a small vocabulary of metrics characterises a network’s shape: distances (how many hops separate nodes), components (what’s reachable from what), density (how many of the possible edges exist), and clustering (how tightly neighbourhoods close into triangles). All of them are computed by walking the graph — the breadth-first search that ripples outward from a node.

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
Why it matters
These metrics turn “what does this network look like?” into defensible numbers — and the combinations are diagnostic: short distances plus high clustering is the signature of a small world; a single giant component signals an integrated system. They are the measurements every later entry builds on.

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
Paths, distance, diameter
A path is a sequence of edges; its length is the number of edges (Menczer et al. 2020). The distance (shortest path) between two nodes is the minimum number of edges connecting them (Menczer et al. 2020). Two summaries follow (Barabási 2016):

Diameter d_{\max} — the largest shortest-path distance between any pair: the network’s “width.”
Average path length \langle\ell\rangle = \frac{1}{N(N-1)}\sum_{i\ne j}\ell_{ij} — the typical separation. Famously small in social networks (the “six degrees” phenomenon).

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
Components and the giant component
A connected component is a maximal set of nodes all reachable from one another (Menczer et al. 2020). Real networks typically have one giant component holding the vast majority of nodes, plus small fragments and isolates (Barabási 2016). Distances are only defined within a component, so most analysis runs on the giant component.

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
Density
Density is the fraction of possible edges that actually exist (Menczer et al. 2020):

d = \frac{L}{L_{\max}} = \frac{2L}{N(N-1)},

with L edges and N nodes. The headline fact: real networks are sparse, d \ll 1 — a person has hundreds of friends, not millions (Barabási 2016). Sparsity is what makes networks computable (store an adjacency list, not the full matrix).

title: Network Structure & Metrics
section: Clustering: do your friends know each other?
text:
Clustering: do your friends know each other?
The clustering coefficient measures how much a node’s neighbourhood closes into triangles (Menczer et al. 2020). The local clustering of node i is the fraction of its neighbour-pairs that are themselves connected (Menczer et al. 2020):

C_i = \frac{2\,\tau(i)}{k_i(k_i-1)},

where \tau(i) is the number of triangles through i and k_i its degree. The network’s average clustering \langle C\rangle averages C_i over all nodes (Menczer et al. 2020). High clustering is the fingerprint of triadic closure — “friends of friends are likely to be friends” — and of tightly knit communities (Menczer et al. 2020).


Show the figure code (BFS + metrics by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import deque

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
N = 70
pos = rng.random((N, 2))
Dm = np.sqrt(((pos[:, None, :] - pos[None, :, :])**2).sum(-1))
A = ((Dm < 0.21) & (Dm > 0)).astype(int)
adj = [np.where(A[i])[0] for i in range(N)]

def bfs(src):
    dist = np.full(N, -1); dist[src] = 0; q = deque([src])
    while q:
        u = q.popleft()
        for v in adj[u]:
            if dist[v] < 0:
                dist[v] = dist[u] + 1; q.append(v)
    return dist

# connected components -> giant
seen = np.zeros(N, bool); comps = []
for i in range(N):
    if not seen[i]:
        m = np.where(bfs(i) >= 0)[0]; seen[m] = True; comps.append(m)
giant = max(comps, key=len)
src = giant[np.argmin(pos[giant, 0])]               # a left-side source for nice ripples
dist = bfs(src)

# all-pairs shortest paths within the giant component
alld = []
for s in giant:
    d = bfs(s)
    alld += [d[t] for t in giant if t > s]
alld = np.array(alld)
diam, avg_l = alld.max(), alld.mean()

ng = len(giant); sub = A[np.ix_(giant, giant)]; L = sub.sum() // 2
density = 2*L / (ng*(ng-1))
Cs = []
for i in giant:
    nb = [v for v in adj[i] if v in set(giant)]; k = len(nb)
    if k < 2: continue
    tri = sum(A[a, b] for x, a in enumerate(nb) for b in nb[x+1:])
    Cs.append(2*tri / (k*(k-1)))
avgC = np.mean(Cs)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 4.3), gridspec_kw={"width_ratios":[1.25,1]})
for i in range(N):
    for j in adj[i]:
        if j > i: axL.plot(*zip(pos[i], pos[j]), color="#d4d8dc", lw=0.6, zorder=1)
out = dist < 0
axL.scatter(pos[out,0], pos[out,1], s=25, color="#cfcfcf", zorder=2)
sc = axL.scatter(pos[~out,0], pos[~out,1], c=dist[~out], s=45, cmap="viridis", zorder=3)
axL.scatter(*pos[src], s=140, color="#c0392b", edgecolor="white", zorder=4)
axL.set_title("BFS ripples: hop-distance from the source"); axL.axis("off")
fig.colorbar(sc, ax=axL, shrink=0.8, label="distance (hops)")

axR.hist(alld, bins=range(1, diam+2), color="#1f4e79", alpha=0.8, align="left", rwidth=0.85)
axR.axvline(avg_l, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2, label=f"⟨ℓ⟩ = {avg_l:.2f}")
axR.axvline(diam, color="#c0392b", lw=2, ls="--", label=f"diameter = {diam}")
axR.set_xlabel("shortest-path length"); axR.set_ylabel("# of node pairs")
axR.set_title(f"density = {density:.3f},  ⟨C⟩ = {avgC:.2f}"); axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Walking a network with breadth-first search, by hand. The graph is a random geometric network (nodes linked if they’re close), drawn at its true coordinates. Left: BFS from the red source colours every node by its hop-distance — the ‘ripples’ spreading outward layer by layer, exactly how BFS finds shortest paths. Grey nodes are outside the source’s connected component. Right: the distribution of all-pairs shortest paths in the giant component, with the average path length ⟨ℓ⟩ and the diameter (the longest shortest path) marked. The density and average clustering are computed from the same adjacency matrix.

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
How they’re computed: breadth-first search
All the distance-based metrics come from breadth-first search (BFS) (Menczer et al. 2020). From a source, BFS explores the network layer by layer — neighbours, then neighbours-of-neighbours — “like ripples spreading in a pond” (Menczer et al. 2020). It finds shortest paths and connected components in one sweep; to get all-pairs distances (for diameter and \langle\ell\rangle) you run BFS from every node (Menczer et al. 2020).

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Computing distances across components. Nodes in different components have infinite distance; restrict path metrics to the giant component (or report reachability). (Menczer et al. 2020)
Comparing densities across sizes. Density falls mechanically as N grows for a fixed average degree; compare average degree, not raw density, across networks of different size. (Barabási 2016)
Clustering on tiny-degree nodes. C_i is undefined for k_i<2; handle those before averaging. (Menczer et al. 2020)
All-pairs BFS on huge graphs. It is O(N(N+L)) — fine for thousands of nodes, infeasible for millions; use sampling or approximate diameters at scale. (Menczer et al. 2020)
Reading high clustering as causal. Triadic closure is one generator of triangles; so is a bipartite projection or homophily. (Menczer et al. 2020)

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Menczer, Fortunato & Davis, A First Course in Network Science — paths, shortest-path distance, connected components; density d=2L/[N(N-1)]; the clustering coefficient C_i=2\tau(i)/[k_i(k_i-1)] and average clustering, with triadic closure; and BFS (“ripples in a pond”) as the engine for shortest paths, components, and all-pairs distances.
Barabási, Network Science — diameter and average path length; the giant component; and the sparsity of real networks (d\ll1).

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

BFS correctness and cost. BFS visits nodes in non-decreasing distance from the source, so the first time it reaches a node it does so by a shortest path — correctness follows from processing the frontier in FIFO order. Each node and edge is touched once, giving O(N+L) per source and O(N(N+L)) for all-pairs.
Counting triangles with the matrix. The number of triangles through i is \tau(i)=\tfrac12\sum_{j,k}A_{ij}A_{jk}A_{ki}, i.e. \tfrac12 (A^3)_{ii}. So local clustering is C_i=(A^3)_{ii}/[k_i(k_i-1)] and the total triangle count is \tfrac16\operatorname{tr}(A^3) — the adjacency-matrix-powers identity at work.
Small-world preview. A random graph of the same size has short paths (\langle\ell\rangle\sim\ln N/\ln\langle k\rangle) but low clustering; a lattice has high clustering but long paths. Real social networks have both short paths and high clustering — the puzzle the Watts–Strogatz model resolves.

title: Network Structure & Metrics
text:
Connections

Builds on: graphs & the adjacency matrix (everything here is a matrix computation) and BFS / algorithms.
Leads to: centrality (importance, also distance-based), community detection (dense subgroups), and the small-world model (short paths + high clustering together).

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text: Pick a person at random and you can guess their height to within a few inches — there is a typical human, and nobody is ten times the average. Now pick a city, a word, or a personal fortune at random: the “typical” value tells you almost nothing, because a handful of cases are thousands of times larger than the median. That is a power law — p(x)\propto x^{-\alpha} — and it describes a startling number of social and natural systems. Its defining feature is what it lacks: a characteristic scale. The bell curve has a centre of gravity; the power law has none.

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text:
Why it matters
If you assume a bell curve where a power law rules, you will be blindsided. Bell-curve thinking says extreme events are so rare they can be ignored — the hundred-year flood, the market crash, the runaway bestseller. Power-law thinking says the extremes are where the action is: they are rare, but not nearly rare enough to ignore, and they can dominate the total (Mitchell 2009). Wealth, city sizes, web traffic, word usage, earthquake energy, and the casualties of wars all live in this regime. Getting the shape of the tail wrong is the difference between a risk model that survives and one that detonates.

title: Power Laws & Scaling
section: Two kinds of randomness: the bell curve vs the heavy tail
text:
Two kinds of randomness: the bell curve vs the heavy tail
The normal (bell) distribution has a characteristic scale: values cluster around the mean, and the probability of an outlier dies off exponentially fast. There is a representative member — the “average” — and deviations more than a few standard deviations out are effectively impossible. Human height, measurement error, and sums of many small independent effects look like this (Mitchell 2009).
A power-law distribution is scale-free: there is no representative member (Barabási 2016). Zoom in on the small values or out to the large ones and the distribution looks the same — self-similar across scales. Its tail is heavy (or “fat”): extreme events, while still rare, are many orders of magnitude more likely than a bell curve would ever predict (Mitchell 2009). The practical upshot is unsettling: in a scale-free system with exponent 2<\alpha<3, the mean is finite but the variance diverges — fluctuations are so large that the average is “meaningless as a scale” (Barabási 2016).

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text:
What a power law is
A power law says the probability of an observation of size x falls off as a power of x:

p(x) \;\propto\; x^{-\alpha},

with \alpha the exponent (typically between 2 and 3 for the things social scientists study). Take logs of both sides and the magic appears:

\log p(x) \;=\; \text{const} - \alpha\,\log x .

On a log-log plot, a power law is a straight line with slope -\alpha (Mitchell 2009). That is the eyeball test, and the source of the term scaling: multiply x by a factor and p(x) changes by a fixed ratio, no matter where you started — the hallmark of having no special scale.

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text:
Where they show up
Power laws turn up across wildly different domains (Mitchell 2009; Barabási 2016):

Zipf’s law — the frequency of words in a language and the sizes of cities: rank a city r-th by population and its size is roughly proportional to 1/r.
The Pareto principle — wealth and income (the “80/20 rule”): a small fraction of people hold most of the wealth.
Earthquakes — the Gutenberg–Richter law relating magnitude to frequency.
Networks — the number of links to a web page or the connections of a hub (a scale-free network; see scale-free networks).
Catastrophes — the casualties of wars and the sizes of forest fires.



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)

def survival(x):
    """Empirical P(X > x): sort ascending, plot complementary rank."""
    xs = np.sort(x)
    n = xs.size
    ccdf = 1.0 - np.arange(1, n + 1) / n
    return xs, ccdf

# --- power-law sample via inverse transform: P(X>x)=(x/xmin)^-(a-1) ---
def sample_powerlaw(n, alpha, xmin=1.0):
    u = rng.random(n)
    return xmin * (1.0 - u) ** (-1.0 / (alpha - 1.0))

n = 20000
pl   = sample_powerlaw(n, alpha=2.5, xmin=1.0)
# normal with a matched median (~1), folded to positive support
nrm  = np.abs(rng.normal(1.0, 0.35, n))
logn = rng.lognormal(mean=0.0, sigma=1.3, size=n)   # heavy-ish but NOT power law

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.3))

for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_yscale("log")
    ax.set_xlabel("value  x"); ax.set_ylabel("P(X > x)")

xs, cc = survival(pl);  axL.plot(xs, cc, color="#1f4e79", lw=2, label="power law (α=2.5)")
xs, cc = survival(nrm); axL.plot(xs, cc, color="#888888", lw=2, label="normal (bell curve)")
axL.set_ylim(1e-4, 1.5); axL.set_xlim(0.2, 1e3)
axL.set_title("Heavy tail vs thin tail")
axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower left")
axL.text(20, 0.2, "extremes\nrare but\nnot negligible", color="#1f4e79", fontsize=8)

xs, cc = survival(pl);   axR.plot(xs, cc, color="#1f4e79", lw=2, label="true power law")
xs, cc = survival(logn); axR.plot(xs, cc, color="#d97706", lw=2, label="lognormal (impostor)")
axR.set_ylim(1e-4, 1.5); axR.set_xlim(0.2, 1e3)
axR.set_title("Why 'looks straight' isn't proof")
axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower left")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Heavy tails, and the trap of seeing them everywhere. Both panels plot the survival function P(X > x) — the chance of exceeding x — on log-log axes, computed by hand from samples. LEFT: a power law (blue) is a straight line; its tail decays so slowly that values 100× the median remain perfectly possible. A normal distribution (grey) with the same median falls off a cliff — beyond a few scales, exceedances are effectively impossible. That gap IS the heavy tail. RIGHT: the over-claiming caution. A lognormal sample (orange) is NOT a power law, yet over two or three decades it looks deceptively straight — only at the far tail does it curve away from the true power law (blue). Eyeballing a ‘straight-ish’ log-log line is not proof; rigorous fitting (Clauset–Shalizi–Newman) is required.

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text:
The over-claiming caution
Because a straight line on a log-log plot is so easy to want to see, power laws are among the most over-claimed patterns in science (Mitchell 2009). Many distributions that look like power laws over a decade or two are really lognormal, stretched-exponential, or some other heavy tail (Barabási 2016) — and a lognormal can masquerade as a straight line across a surprising range before it curves away (right panel above).
The discipline here is non-negotiable: a power law is a statistical hypothesis, not a doodle. Rigorous fitting and goodness-of-fit testing — the Clauset–Shalizi–Newman methodology of maximum-likelihood estimation of \alpha, a principled lower cutoff x_{\min}, and a likelihood-ratio test against alternatives like the lognormal — is what separates a real claim from wishful log-log eyeballing (Mitchell 2009). The mechanism matters too: a power law is interesting because it hints at an underlying generative process (preferential attachment, self-organised criticality), but only once the statistics actually hold up.

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting a mean as if it’s typical. In a heavy-tailed sample the mean is dragged around by the largest few values and the variance may be effectively infinite; the median and the tail exponent describe the system, the mean does not. (Barabási 2016)
Bell-curve risk models. Assuming normal tails systematically under-prices catastrophe — the crash that “should happen once in millennia” arrives every decade. (Mitchell 2009)
Declaring a power law from a straight-ish line. Two or three decades that look linear are not proof; fit it properly and test against a lognormal before you claim it. (Mitchell 2009)
Confusing scale-free with structureless. “No characteristic scale” does not mean “no structure” — it means the same structure repeats at every scale. (Barabási 2016)

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — power laws as scale-free, heavy-tailed distributions p(x)\propto x^{-\alpha}; the contrast with the bell curve’s characteristic scale; the log-log straight line; the canonical examples (Zipf, Pareto, earthquakes, web links); and the strong over-claiming caution with the Clauset–Shalizi–Newman testing discipline.
Barabási, Network Science — the scale-free property and the divergence of the variance for 2<\alpha<3 (“the mean is meaningless as a scale”); power-law degree distributions in real networks; and the warning that many apparent power laws are lognormal.

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Normalisation and the cutoff. A power law cannot hold all the way to x=0 (the density would not integrate). One writes it for x\ge x_{\min}: 
p(x)=\frac{\alpha-1}{x_{\min}}\left(\frac{x}{x_{\min}}\right)^{-\alpha},\qquad x\ge x_{\min},
 which integrates to 1 for \alpha>1. The survival function is the clean object to plot: 
P(X>x)=\left(\frac{x}{x_{\min}}\right)^{-(\alpha-1)},
 a straight line of slope -(\alpha-1) on log-log axes — which is exactly the inverse-transform used to generate the sample in the figure: x=x_{\min}(1-u)^{-1/(\alpha-1)} for u\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1).
Divergent moments. The k-th moment is \langle x^k\rangle\propto\int_{x_{\min}}^{\infty} x^{k-\alpha}\,dx, which diverges unless \alpha>k+1. So the mean is finite only for \alpha>2, and the variance only for \alpha>3. In the common social-science regime 2<\alpha<3 the mean exists but the variance is infinite — formal justification for distrusting the average.
ML estimation (Clauset–Shalizi–Newman). For continuous data above x_{\min}, the maximum-likelihood exponent is 
\hat\alpha = 1 + n\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln\frac{x_i}{x_{\min}}\right]^{-1},
 with x_{\min} chosen to minimise the Kolmogorov–Smirnov distance between data and fit, and the whole power-law hypothesis then tested against alternatives (e.g. lognormal) by a likelihood-ratio test. A small slope estimate alone proves nothing.

title: Power Laws & Scaling
text:
Connections

Builds on: nonlinear dynamics & chaos (systems poised between order and randomness) and scale-free networks (where a power-law degree distribution comes from preferential attachment).
