Generated by: self-organised criticality — a mechanism that produces power-law–distributed events (avalanches) without any tuning.
Cross-checks: any heavy-tailed modelling claim — fit and test the tail; don’t assume a bell curve.

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
text: Erdős–Rényi and Watts–Strogatz both produce networks where every node has roughly the same degree. Real networks don’t: a handful of hubs dominate while most nodes are sparsely connected — a power-law degree distribution. Barabási & Albert (1999) showed two simple dynamics generate it: networks grow, and new nodes attach preferentially to the already-popular. The result is scale-free structure, with profound consequences for robustness, spreading, and fragility.

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
text:
Why it matters
Hubs change everything: how short the network is to cross, how epidemics spread, what breaks it. The Internet, the web, citation networks, protein interactions, and trade are all hub-dominated — so a model that explains where hubs come from is foundational. It is also a clean story of emergence through a dynamical rule, the bridge to the Dynamics cluster.

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
section: Two ingredients — and you need both
text:
Two ingredients — and you need both
Barabási & Albert identified two mechanisms (Barabási 2016):

Growth. Real networks are not fixed — they expand over time as new nodes join (new papers, new web pages, new people) (Barabási 2016).
Preferential attachment. A new node links not at random but preferentially to well-connected nodes: the probability of attaching to node i is proportional to its degree, \Pi(k_i) = k_i/\sum_j k_j (Barabási 2016). This is “the rich get richer” — the Matthew effect / cumulative advantage (Barabási 2016).

Both are essential (Barabási 2016):

Growth alone (new nodes attaching randomly) gives an exponential distribution — no hubs.
Preferential attachment alone (fixed N) eventually makes a complete graph.

Only growth + linear preferential attachment yields a stationary power law.

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
text:
The power law and “scale-free”
The combination produces a power-law degree distribution (Barabási 2016):

p_k \sim k^{-\gamma}, \qquad \gamma = 3 \text{ (the BA exponent)}.

Scale-free means no characteristic scale: unlike ER, where the average degree is typical, here the mean is not representative — for \gamma<3 the second moment \langle k^2\rangle diverges, so fluctuations around the mean can be arbitrarily large (Barabási 2016). A few massive hubs coexist with a vast majority of tiny-degree nodes — a structure forbidden by ER’s peaked Poisson (Barabási 2016).


Show the figure code (growing a BA network by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
N, m = 4000, 2

# Barabási–Albert by hand: growth + preferential attachment
deg = np.zeros(N, int)
repeated = []                          # node appears once per edge-endpoint (∝ degree)
for i in range(m+1):                   # seed: small clique
    for j in range(i+1, m+1):
        deg[i]+=1; deg[j]+=1; repeated += [i, j]
for new in range(m+1, N):
    targets = set()
    while len(targets) < m:
        targets.add(repeated[rng.integers(len(repeated))])    # preferential pick
    for t in targets:
        deg[new]+=1; deg[t]+=1; repeated += [new, t]
deg_ba = deg

# Erdős–Rényi with the same average degree (~2m)
kbar = deg_ba.mean()
deg_er = rng.poisson(kbar, N)          # ER degree ≈ Poisson(<k>)

def dist(d):
    vals, cnt = np.unique(d[d > 0], return_counts=True)
    return vals, cnt/cnt.sum()

kb, pb = dist(deg_ba); ke, pe = dist(deg_er)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.3))
ax.loglog(ke, pe, "s", color="#d98a29", ms=5, label="Erdős–Rényi (Poisson, peaked)")
ax.loglog(kb, pb, "o", color="#1f4e79", ms=5, label="Barabási–Albert (power law, hubs)")
ks = np.array([3, 200]); ax.loglog(ks, 0.7*ks**(-3.0), "--", color="#555", lw=1.2, label="slope −3 reference")
ax.set_xlabel("degree  k"); ax.set_ylabel("p(k)")
ax.set_title(f"Degree distributions: hub (max k={deg_ba.max()}) vs no hub (max k={deg_er.max()})")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Why scale-free is different, on a log–log plot. A Barabási–Albert network is grown by hand (each new node attaches to 2 existing nodes, chosen with probability ∝ their degree) and its degree distribution (blue) is compared to an Erdős–Rényi graph of the same average degree (orange). ER is peaked: nearly every node sits near the mean, and nothing exceeds ~3× it — a straight cliff. BA is a straight LINE on log–log: a power law spanning two decades, with a heavy tail of hubs (the most-connected node has 50–100× the average). Same number of edges, utterly different architecture — the mean is meaningless for BA.

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
text:
Ultra-small worlds
Random networks are “small worlds”; scale-free networks are ultra-small. For 2<\gamma<3 the hubs act as bridges, shrinking the average distance to scale as \ln\ln N rather than \ln N (Barabási 2016) — so even enormous networks are essentially a couple of hops across, via the hubs.

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
section: Robust and fragile: the Achilles’ heel
text:
Robust and fragile: the Achilles’ heel
The hub structure creates a stark robustness–fragility trade-off (Barabási 2016):

Robust to random failure. Since almost every node is low-degree, a random removal (a router glitch) almost certainly hits an unimportant node — the giant component survives (Barabási 2016).
Fragile to targeted attack. Removing a few hubs rapidly fragments the network into isolated pieces — its Achilles’ heel (Barabási 2016).

The consequences are concrete (Barabási 2016): the Internet shrugs off random failures but is vulnerable to coordinated attacks on backbone hubs; and on a scale-free network there is no epidemic threshold — a disease can persist however low its infectiousness, because the hubs reliably catch and rebroadcast it (the subject of spreading on networks).

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Claiming a power law from a straight-ish log–log plot. Power laws are over-diagnosed — lognormal and other heavy tails look similar; fit and test properly (Clauset–Shalizi–Newman). (Barabási 2016) (Full treatment in the Dynamics cluster’s power-laws entry.)
Using the mean degree as typical. For scale-free networks the mean is not representative and the variance can diverge — summarise the whole distribution. (Barabási 2016)
Assuming preferential attachment is the only generator. Fitness, copying, and optimisation also produce power laws; the mechanism isn’t identified by the distribution alone. (Barabási 2016)
Forgetting growth. Preferential attachment on a fixed network does not give a power law. (Barabási 2016)

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Barabási, Network Science — growth + preferential attachment (\Pi(k_i)\propto k_i, the Matthew effect) and that both are required; the power law p_k\sim k^{-\gamma} with \gamma=3 and the scale-free (no-characteristic-scale, diverging \langle k^2\rangle) meaning; hubs vs the Poisson; the ultra-small-world (\ln\ln N); and the robustness–fragility trade-off (random failure vs hub attack) with the Internet and no-epidemic-threshold implications.

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why \gamma=3 (continuum sketch). Add one node with m links per step; node i’s degree grows as \frac{dk_i}{dt}=m\,\frac{k_i}{\sum_j k_j}=m\,\frac{k_i}{2mt}=\frac{k_i}{2t}, so k_i(t)=m\sqrt{t/t_i} where t_i is i’s birth time. Older nodes (t_i small) end up with the highest degree — first-mover advantage. Inverting and differentiating the distribution of birth times gives p_k\sim k^{-3}. Linear preferential attachment is exactly what makes the exponent 3; sublinear gives a stretched exponential, superlinear a single dominant hub.
Why no epidemic threshold. The epidemic threshold on a network scales as \lambda_c \sim \langle k\rangle/\langle k^2\rangle. For scale-free networks with \gamma\le3, \langle k^2\rangle\to\infty, so \lambda_c\to0 — any positive infectiousness can spread. Hubs are the mechanism: they are quickly infected and then expose enormous numbers of others.

title: Scale-Free Networks: Barabási–Albert & Preferential Attachment
text:
Connections

Builds on: Erdős–Rényi (the peaked Poisson it overturns) and graphs & degree (the degree distribution p_k).
Leads to: spreading on networks (hubs as superspreaders; no threshold) and the Dynamics cluster’s power-laws & criticality entry (heavy tails done rigorously).
Echoes: preferential attachment ≈ cumulative advantage — the “success-to-the-successful” systems trap in network form.

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
text: Erdős–Rényi gives short paths but no clustering; a regular lattice gives clustering but long paths. Real social networks have both — your friends know each other (high clustering) and a stranger is six handshakes away (short paths). Watts & Strogatz (1998) showed the resolution is almost embarrassingly simple: take a clustered lattice and rewire a tiny fraction of edges at random. Those few shortcuts collapse the distances while leaving the clustering nearly intact.

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
text:
Why it matters
The “small-world” property — short global paths plus dense local clustering — is one of the most robust empirical facts about social networks, and it governs how fast information, disease, and behaviour spread. WS is the minimal model that produces it, and the lesson generalises: a little randomness goes a very long way in a structured system.

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
text:
The puzzle
Two reference points bracket the problem (Barabási 2016):

A regular ring lattice (each node tied to its nearest neighbours) has high clustering but path lengths that grow linearly with N — a big, “large” world.
A random graph has short (logarithmic) paths but negligible clustering.

Real networks want the short paths of the random graph and the clustering of the lattice at once.

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
section: The model: order plus a pinch of randomness
text:
The model: order plus a pinch of randomness
Watts–Strogatz interpolates between them (Barabási 2016):

Start from a ring lattice — each node connected to its k nearest neighbours (high clustering, long paths).
Rewire each edge with probability p — detach one end and reconnect it to a node chosen uniformly at random from the whole network.

Each rewired edge is a shortcut linking otherwise-distant regions (Barabási 2016).


Show the figure code (clustering & path length vs rewiring, by hand)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import deque

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
N, half = 100, 3            # ring lattice: each node tied to 3 neighbours each side (degree 6)

def ring():
    A = np.zeros((N, N), int)
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(1, half+1):
            A[i, (i+j) % N] = A[(i+j) % N, i] = 1
    return A

def rewire(A, p):
    A = A.copy()
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(1, half+1):
            nb = (i+j) % N
            if A[i, nb] and rng.random() < p:
                A[i, nb] = A[nb, i] = 0
                cand = np.where((A[i] == 0))[0]; cand = cand[(cand != i)]
                if len(cand):
                    t = rng.choice(cand); A[i, t] = A[t, i] = 1
                else:
                    A[i, nb] = A[nb, i] = 1
    return A

def clustering(A):
    adj = [np.where(A[i])[0] for i in range(N)]; Cs = []
    for i in range(N):
        nb = adj[i]; k = len(nb)
        if k < 2: continue
        tri = sum(A[a, b] for x, a in enumerate(nb) for b in nb[x+1:])
        Cs.append(2*tri/(k*(k-1)))
    return np.mean(Cs)

def avg_path(A):
    adj = [np.where(A[i])[0] for i in range(N)]; tot = cnt = 0
    for s in range(N):
        d = np.full(N, -1); d[s] = 0; q = deque([s])
        while q:
            u = q.popleft()
            for v in adj[u]:
                if d[v] < 0: d[v] = d[u]+1; q.append(v)
        dd = d[d > 0]; tot += dd.sum(); cnt += len(dd)
    return tot/cnt

A0 = ring(); C0, L0 = clustering(A0), avg_path(A0)
ps = np.logspace(-3, 0, 14)
Cr, Lr = [], []
for p in ps:
    cs, ls = [], []
    for _ in range(6):
        A = rewire(A0, p); cs.append(clustering(A)); ls.append(avg_path(A))
    Cr.append(np.mean(cs)/C0); Lr.append(np.mean(ls)/L0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.semilogx(ps, Cr, "o-", color="#d98a29", lw=2, label="C(p)/C(0)  — clustering (stays high)")
ax.semilogx(ps, Lr, "s-", color="#1f4e79", lw=2, label="L(p)/L(0)  — path length (falls fast)")
ax.axvspan(0.004, 0.1, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.08)
ax.text(0.012, 0.45, "small-world\nregime", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8.5, ha="center")
ax.set_xlabel("rewiring probability  p"); ax.set_ylabel("value relative to the ring lattice")
ax.set_title("A few random shortcuts: short paths, clustering intact")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False); ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The Watts–Strogatz small-world regime, computed by hand. Starting from a ring lattice (p=0), we rewire each edge with probability p and measure two things relative to the lattice: the average clustering C(p)/C(0) and the average path length L(p)/L(0) (both averaged over several realisations; log-scale p). The key is the GAP: even a tiny rewiring (p≈0.01) collapses the path length toward its random-graph value (blue falls fast), while the clustering stays almost untouched (orange stays high). In that wide intermediate band the network is a ‘small world’ — locally clustered yet globally short. Six degrees of separation needs only a few random shortcuts.

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
text:
From order to randomness
As p sweeps from 0 to 1 (Barabási 2016):

p=0 (regular): a pure lattice — high clustering, long paths.
intermediate p (small-world): short paths and high clustering — the regime real social networks inhabit, and where “six degrees of separation” lives (Barabási 2016).
p=1 (random): every edge rewired — an Erdős–Rényi graph, short paths but low clustering.

The striking part is how early the path length drops: a rewiring of just p\approx 0.01 already makes the world small, because each shortcut helps many pairs at once (Barabási 2016).

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
section: The limitation that opened the next door
text:
The limitation that opened the next door
WS captures clustering and short paths, but its degree distribution stays nearly uniform — it still cannot generate hubs (Barabási 2016). That missing ingredient — the heavy-tailed, hub-dominated degree distribution of real networks — is exactly what the Barabási–Albert scale-free model was built to explain (Barabási 2016).

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading WS as a model of degree. It explains clustering + short paths, not hubs; its degrees are near-uniform. Use scale-free models for hub structure. (Barabási 2016)
Thinking you need many shortcuts. A tiny rewiring already shrinks the world; the effect is highly nonlinear in p. (Barabási 2016)
Confusing ‘small-world’ with ‘random’. A random graph is not a small world — it lacks the clustering. The small world is the intermediate regime. (Barabási 2016)

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Barabási, Network Science (with Menczer) — the small-world property as short paths + high clustering; the ring-lattice → random-rewiring construction; how even p\approx0.01 collapses the path length while clustering persists (“six degrees”); the p:0\to1 regimes (lattice → small-world → ER); and the limitation (no hubs) that motivated the scale-free model.

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why path length drops so fast. In the ring, reaching a node d steps away costs \sim d/k hops, so L\sim N/k — linear in N. A single random shortcut creates a “wormhole” that, once you’re near one end, lets you jump across the whole ring; with pNk/2 shortcuts the typical distance falls to L\sim \ln N/\ln k, the random-graph scaling. Because one shortcut shortens paths for many pairs, the collapse begins at vanishingly small p — the hallmark of the model.
Why clustering survives. Rewiring touches only a fraction p of edges, so a node keeps \approx(1-p) of its lattice triangles; clustering degrades roughly as C(p)\approx C(0)(1-p)^3 — slowly for small p. The gap between the fast-falling L(p) and the slow-falling C(p) is the small-world regime.

title: Small Worlds: The Watts–Strogatz Model
text:
Connections

Builds on: network structure & metrics (clustering and path length) and Erdős–Rényi (the p=1 limit, and the short-path benchmark).
Leads to: scale-free networks (the hubs WS can’t make) and spreading on networks (short paths accelerate diffusion).
Echoes: Milgram’s “six degrees” experiment and the everyday surprise of the small world.

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text: Every estimate in this book isolates one effect: what does X do to Y, all else equal? But the world you act on is a system — stocks, flows, and feedback loops that respond to your intervention and often push back. Donella Meadows’ Thinking in Systems is the lens for that: it explains why policies provoke resistance, why problems persist despite our efforts, and why the highest-leverage place to intervene is almost never the one our instincts reach for. It is the structural complement to everything you’ve learned about estimating effects.
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Why it matters
A causal estimate is a statement about a nudge to one variable. A policy is a sustained shove against a living structure that adapts. (Meadows 2008) The reason good interventions disappoint — and the reason a metric gets gamed the moment it becomes a target — is that you are not acting on an inert quantity but on a network of feedback loops that responds. Meadows’ core warning is blunt: “the system causes its own behavior.” (Meadows 2008) The quantitative researcher who can estimate an effect and read the system it lives in is the one whose recommendations survive contact with reality.
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
What a system is
A system is not a heap of parts; it is “an interconnected set of elements coherently organized in a way that achieves something.” It has three components, in rising order of importance and falling order of visibility: (Meadows 2008)

Elements — the tangible parts you notice first (a university’s students, faculty, buildings).
Interconnections — the relationships that bind them, usually flows of information or signals (admission standards, grading rules, the chemical messages in a tree).
Purpose / function — the “most crucial but least obvious” part. (Meadows 2008)

The deep move is how you find the purpose: “purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals.” (Meadows 2008) Swap every player on a team and the system is barely changed; change its purpose — a university shifting from “disseminating knowledge” to “making a profit” — and it transforms, even with the same people in the same buildings. Meadows’ compressed version, and a razor for cutting through any mission statement: “the purpose of a system is what it does.” (Meadows 2008)
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Stocks and flows
The building blocks. A stock is an accumulation — water in a bathtub, a population, a bank balance, a “reserve of good will.” A flow is the action that fills or drains it (the tap and the plughole). (Meadows 2008) Two properties make stocks the heart of system behaviour:

A stock is “the memory of the history of changing flows within the system.” (Meadows 2008) It carries the past forward; you read history in the level, not the moment.
Stocks decouple inflow from outflow. Because the bathtub buffers, the two flows need not match instant to instant — an oil company keeps running even when refinery output doesn’t equal the rate cars burn fuel. (Meadows 2008) Stocks act as delays, buffers, and shock absorbers — and those delays are exactly what makes systems hard to steer.
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Feedback loops
A feedback loop is “a closed chain of causal connections” from a stock, through a flow, back to the stock. (Meadows 2008) There are only two kinds, and almost all dynamics are their interplay:

Balancing (stabilizing) loops are goal-seeking: they oppose whatever direction of change is imposed, holding a stock near a target. A thermostat returning a room to its setpoint; a hot coffee cooling to room temperature. (Meadows 2008)
Reinforcing (amplifying) loops are self-multiplying: the more there is in the stock, the faster it grows — exponential growth or runaway collapse. An interest-bearing account; a breeding population of rabbits. (Meadows 2008)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

T = 60
# Reinforcing loop: stock grows in proportion to itself (dS = r*S dt)
r = 0.06
reinforcing = np.empty(T); reinforcing[0] = 2.0
for t in range(1, T):
    reinforcing[t] = reinforcing[t-1] + r * reinforcing[t-1]

# Balancing loop: stock seeks a goal (dS = k*(goal - S) dt)
goal, k = 8.0, 0.12
balancing = np.empty(T); balancing[0] = 0.5
for t in range(1, T):
    balancing[t] = balancing[t-1] + k * (goal - balancing[t-1])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.plot(reinforcing, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4, label="reinforcing loop (amplifies)")
ax.plot(balancing, color="#3f8f6f", lw=2.4, label="balancing loop (goal-seeking)")
ax.axhline(goal, color="#3f8f6f", ls="--", lw=1.2, alpha=0.7)
ax.text(T*0.62, goal + 0.4, "balancing loop's goal", color="#3f8f6f", fontsize=8.5)
ax.set_ylim(0, 16)
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("stock level")
ax.set_title("Reinforcing vs balancing feedback")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The two feedback loops, simulated. A reinforcing loop (navy) feeds on itself — the fuller the stock, the faster it grows — producing exponential lift-off. A balancing loop (green) is goal-seeking: it closes the gap to a target (dashed) and settles, opposing any push away from it. Real systems are tugs-of-war between the two; which dominates, and the delays in between, set the behaviour you observe.
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
section: The system causes its own behaviour
text:
The system causes its own behaviour
Here is the insight that reorders how you read evidence. We instinctively blame events — a single bad number, a single villain. Meadows insists we climb from events to behaviour (trends over time) to structure (the arrangement of stocks, flows, and loops). (Meadows 2008) “The system causes its own behavior.” A political leader doesn’t cause a recession; “ups and downs are inherent in the structure of the market economy.” (Meadows 2008) Persistent ills — poverty, war — endure because they are “intrinsically systems problems,” generated by structures that recreate them regardless of who occupies the roles. (Meadows 2008)
This is why “systems fool us”: they behave beautifully for a while, then surprise us with terrible results. (Meadows 2008) And the surprise is rarely anyone’s malice. Under bounded rationality, every actor behaves “dutifully and rationally” on the local information they can see, yet the aggregate is “a perfectly terrible result.” (Meadows 2008) No bad guys required — just a structure that makes sensible local choices add up to collective failure.
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
section: Leverage points — and why we push them backwards
text:
Leverage points — and why we push them backwards
A leverage point is “a place in a system where a small shift… can produce big changes in everything.” (Meadows 2008) The famous, humbling observation: people are often good at intuiting where the leverage points are — and then push them in the wrong direction. (Meadows 2008) The hierarchy explains why our instincts mislead. The interventions that feel powerful — changing a parameter (swap the leader, tweak a number, adjust a tax rate) — are usually the weakest. (Meadows 2008) The strongest leverage sits where we rarely look: in the interconnections, the goals, and the paradigm — the system’s purpose. Change the rules and the purpose and you change everything; change the people at the top and the structure quietly reasserts itself.
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
section: What this means for the quantitative researcher
text:
What this means for the quantitative researcher
Systems thinking does not contradict the causal toolkit — it frames it. Three consequences for applied work:

Policy resistance is predictable. A balancing loop will oppose your intervention; the system pushes back toward its prior state. An effect estimated in a trial can be neutralised at scale by feedback the trial never exercised — a structural reason external validity fails.
Goodhart’s law is a feedback loop. “When a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure” is precisely a system responding to the stock you chose to optimise. Metric fixation is systems-blindness with a dashboard. (Meadows 2008)
Look for structure, not villains. When a result disappoints or an organisation misbehaves, the systems question — what structure makes this behaviour the natural output? — beats the search for someone to blame, and points at the high-leverage fix. This is the “feel for the whole” that no metric supplies. (Meadows 2008)
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating a stock like a flow. A one-time payment (flow) doesn’t fix a depleted reserve (stock) that took years to drain; mind the difference, and the delays. (Meadows 2008)
Blaming the event, missing the structure. Reacting to each data point in isolation keeps you forever fire-fighting symptoms a feedback loop keeps regenerating. Climb to behaviour and structure. (Meadows 2008)
Pulling the obvious lever. Changing a parameter (the number, the person at the top) feels decisive and usually changes little; the goals and rules are where the leverage hides. (Meadows 2008)
Pushing leverage the wrong way. Even when you find the leverage point, intuition often has the sign backwards — e.g. relieving a pressure that was actually holding the system in check. Test the direction. (Meadows 2008)
Mistaking stated purpose for real purpose. Read behaviour, not rhetoric: “the purpose of a system is what it does.” A mission statement is not evidence. (Meadows 2008)
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Meadows, Thinking in Systems: A Primer — a system as elements + interconnections + purpose, with purpose “deduced from behavior, not rhetoric” and “the purpose of a system is what it does”; stocks (the “memory of the history of changing flows”) and flows, stocks as buffers/delays that decouple inflow and outflow; feedback loops — balancing (goal-seeking: thermostat, cooling coffee) vs reinforcing (amplifying: interest, rabbits); “the system causes its own behavior” and the events → behavior → structure ladder; bounded rationality (“dutifully and rationally” → “a perfectly terrible result”); leverage points, the claim that people push them in the wrong direction, and the hierarchy in which parameters are weak and goals/paradigms are strong; and “systems fool us” / “you can’t see the forest for the trees.”
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The stock–flow integral. A stock is the accumulation of net flow over time: 
S(t)=S(0)+\int_0^t \big[\text{inflow}(\tau)-\text{outflow}(\tau)\big]\,d\tau,
 the continuous statement of “the level is the memory of past flows.” Its discrete cousin is S_{t}=S_{t-1}+(\text{in}-\text{out}) — the update rule in the figure.
Why the two loops give exponential vs goal-seeking curves. A reinforcing loop makes net flow proportional to the stock, \dot S = rS, whose solution S(t)=S_0 e^{rt} is exponential — runaway growth (or, with r<0, collapse). A balancing loop makes net flow proportional to the gap from a goal G, \dot S = k(G-S), whose solution S(t)=G-(G-S_0)e^{-kt} decays toward G and stays there — goal-seeking stability. Every system trajectory is a superposition of these two motifs, shaped by the delays that the stocks impose.
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Connections

Builds on: the tyranny of metrics (Goodhart’s law as a feedback loop) and from evidence to decision (the “feel for the whole” a single estimate lacks).
Reframes: randomized experiments and external validity — why an effect can be absorbed by feedback the study never exercised (policy resistance).
Leads to: the limits of quantification — structure, purpose, and the whole are exactly what numbers struggle to capture.
crumbs: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: What Is Complexity Science?
text: Reductionism — the engine of most of this manual — says understand the parts and you understand the whole. Complexity science is the discovery that for a vast class of systems this is false in an interesting way. A brain is not an intelligent neuron; a market is not a rational super-trader; a traffic jam is not a slow car. The organised behaviour we care about — thought, prices, congestion, segregation, epidemics, fads — is emergent: it lives in the interactions among the parts, not in any part, and it cannot be read off from them in isolation. Complexity science is the study of that: how local interaction, with no central control, generates global order.

title: What Is Complexity Science?
text:
Why it matters
For a social scientist this is not an exotic corner of science — it is the default condition of your subject matter. Societies, economies, organisations, and cultures are made of many heterogeneous, adapting agents interacting through networks. The clean reductive tools elsewhere in this manual — isolate a relationship, hold the rest constant, estimate one effect — remain indispensable, but they go quiet exactly where behaviour is emergent and nonlinear. Complexity science supplies the missing half: the concepts and computational methods for systems whose whole is genuinely more than the sum of its parts (Mitchell 2009; Miller and Page 2007).

title: What Is Complexity Science?
text:
The recurring signatures
Across wildly different systems, the same fingerprints appear (Mitchell 2009):

Emergence — global patterns (a flock, a price, a convention) that exist only at the collective level. “More is different.”
Self-organisation — that order arising without a designer or central controller, from local rules alone (Meadows 2008).
Nonlinearity — effects not proportional to causes; small triggers can have huge consequences, and feedback loops amplify or damp.
Networks of interaction — the parts are coupled through a structure of connections that shapes everything that flows across it.
Adaptation — the agents learn and change, so the system co-evolves and rarely settles to a static equilibrium (Miller and Page 2007).
Sensitivity and limited prediction — deterministic rules can still be unforecastable (chaos), and rare giant events (power-law tails) are intrinsic, not anomalies.

A single image captures the spirit: a phase transition, where turning one knob a little produces a sudden, qualitative change in the whole.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)

def cluster_labels(occ):
    """Label connected clusters of occupied sites (4-neighbour) via union-find."""
    L = occ.shape[0]
    par = np.arange(L * L)
    def find(x):
        root = x
        while par[root] != root:
            root = par[root]
        while par[x] != root:          # path compression
            par[x], x = root, par[x]
        return root
    def union(a, b):
        ra, rb = find(a), find(b)
        if ra != rb:
            par[ra] = rb
    for i in range(L):
        for j in range(L):
            if not occ[i, j]:
                continue
            c = i * L + j
            if i + 1 < L and occ[i + 1, j]:
                union(c, c + L)
            if j + 1 < L and occ[i, j + 1]:
                union(c, c + 1)
    lab = -np.ones(L * L, dtype=int)
    flat = occ.reshape(-1)
    for k in range(L * L):
        if flat[k]:
            lab[k] = find(k)
    return lab.reshape(L, L)

def largest_fraction(occ):
    lab = cluster_labels(occ)
    occ_labels = lab[lab >= 0]
    if occ_labels.size == 0:
        return 0.0, lab, -1
    vals, counts = np.unique(occ_labels, return_counts=True)
    big = vals[counts.argmax()]
    return counts.max() / occ.size, lab, big

# --- order parameter vs density ---
L = 50
ps = np.linspace(0.40, 0.80, 33)
trials = 4
order = np.array([
    np.mean([largest_fraction(rng.random((L, L)) < p)[0] for _ in range(trials)])
    for p in ps
])
p_c = 0.5927  # site-percolation threshold, square lattice

# --- snapshots either side of threshold ---
def snapshot(p):
    occ = rng.random((L, L)) < p
    frac, lab, big = largest_fraction(occ)
    img = np.zeros((L, L))          # 0 = empty
    img[occ] = 1                    # 1 = occupied (grey)
    img[lab == big] = 2             # 2 = largest cluster (blue)
    return img

lo, hi = snapshot(0.55), snapshot(0.62)

fig, (axC, axL, axR) = plt.subplots(
    1, 3, figsize=(10.2, 3.6), gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [1.7, 1, 1]})

axC.plot(ps, order, "o-", color="#1f4e79", ms=4)
axC.axvline(p_c, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1)
axC.text(p_c + 0.005, 0.05, "p_c ≈ 0.59", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axC.set_xlabel("density  p"); axC.set_ylabel("largest-cluster fraction")
axC.set_title("A sharp phase transition")

from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap
cmap = ListedColormap(["#f5f5f5", "#bbbbbb", "#1f4e79"])
for ax, img, ttl in [(axL, lo, "p = 0.55  (below)"), (axR, hi, "p = 0.62  (above)")]:
    ax.imshow(img, cmap=cmap, vmin=0, vmax=2, interpolation="nearest")
    ax.set_title(ttl, fontsize=9); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Emergence as a phase transition, built by hand (site percolation). Fill a 50×50 grid by switching on each cell independently with probability p, and ask: is there one big connected cluster that spans the system? LEFT: the order parameter — the fraction of the grid in its largest connected cluster — stays near zero, then rises SHARPLY through a critical density p_c≈0.59. Below it the world is a dust of disconnected fragments; above it a system-spanning giant cluster suddenly exists. MIDDLE & RIGHT: snapshots either side of the threshold — below p_c the largest cluster (blue) is a small island in a sea of fragments (grey); just above, it abruptly connects edge to edge. Nothing about a single cell changed; the collective crossed a tipping point. This is emergence, criticality, and the giant component all in one picture.

title: What Is Complexity Science?
section: The three disciplines of this track
text:
The three disciplines of this track
Complexity science is a stance shared across fields; to practise it quantitatively you need three complementary toolkits, which organise the rest of this track:








Discipline
Role
What it provides




Systems Thinking (the mindset)
Learn to see wholes
Stocks, flows, feedback, delays, leverage points (Meadows 2008)


Network Analysis (the tool)
Measure the connections
Graphs, centrality, communities, network models (Menczer et al. 2020; Barabási 2016)


Complexity Theory (the dynamics)
Explain the behaviour
Emergence, chaos, power laws, criticality, adaptation, cellular automata (Mitchell 2009; Sayama 2015)



The arc is structure → measurement → dynamics: the mindset tells you to look at the whole, networks let you measure its connective tissue, and complexity theory explains how it moves. The Dynamics sub-track that follows is the payoff — emergence, agent-based models, chaos, power laws, self-organised criticality, complex adaptive systems, and cellular automata — each a different lens on the same astonishing fact you just saw on screen.

title: What Is Complexity Science?
section: The QSS stance: quantitative, not metaphorical
text:
The QSS stance: quantitative, not metaphorical






TipDiscipline over poetry



“Complexity” attracts hand-waving — everything is connected, the whole is more than its parts, expect the unexpected. This track refuses to stop there. Every idea is tied to something measurable, computable, or simulable: a metric (clustering, betweenness, modularity), an algorithm (BFS, preferential attachment, a genetic algorithm), or a simulation (a sandpile, a Schelling grid, percolation) — each with one runnable figure built by hand. And where the field over-claims — “everything is a power law”, a precise “edge of chaos” — we say so plainly (Mitchell 2009).

title: What Is Complexity Science?
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Mistaking a slogan for an explanation. “It’s emergent” is the start of an inquiry, not the end; the job is to show which local rules produce which global pattern. (Mitchell 2009)
Reaching for a central controller. The defining move of the field is that order arises without one — looking for the hidden planner misses the point. (Meadows 2008)
Expecting equilibrium. Adaptive, co-evolving systems often have no fixed resting point; a single representative agent at equilibrium can be the wrong model entirely. (Miller and Page 2007)
Abandoning rigour for romance. Complexity is a licence for better models, not for vague ones — keep every claim anchored to a measurement or a simulation.

title: What Is Complexity Science?
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Mitchell, Complexity: A Guided Tour — the unifying definition of complexity science and its signatures (emergence, nonlinearity, chaos, power laws, the edge of chaos); the “more is different” thesis and the over-claiming cautions.
Miller & Page, Complex Adaptive Systems — heterogeneous adaptive agents, no central control, emergent macro behaviour, and co-evolution away from equilibrium.
Meadows, Thinking in Systems — self-organisation without a designer; the systems-thinking mindset of stocks, flows, and feedback.
Menczer/Barabási & Sayama — the network and computational-modelling toolkits that make the stance quantitative.

title: What Is Complexity Science?
text:
Connections

Read next: emergence & self-organisation — the first formal lens of the Dynamics sub-track.
The three pillars: systems thinking (mindset), graphs (tool), and the dynamics entries that follow.
Reuses: the manual’s simulation & Monte Carlo machinery, now pointed at emergence rather than estimation.

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text: Stocks, flows and feedback explain how a system behaves. This entry asks the deeper question: why do good systems keep working — surviving shocks, growing more capable, staying organised — and how do we wreck that without noticing? Donella Meadows’ answer is three properties: resilience, self-organisation, and hierarchy — each a source of robustness, and each routinely traded away for a short-term gain in efficiency or control.
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text:
Why it matters
A quantitative researcher who designs metrics, incentives, or policies is intervening in systems — and the most common failure is to optimise one number so hard that the system loses the redundancy, adaptability, or coherence that kept it alive. Knowing what makes systems robust is what stops a well-meant optimisation from engineering fragility.
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text:
Resilience: the capacity to recover
Meadows defines resilience as a system’s ability to survive and recover within a variable environment (Meadows 2008). It comes from a rich structure of many feedback loops that can restore the system after a large perturbation — and especially from “meta-feedback loops”: loops that can learn, repair, and restructure the other loops themselves (Meadows 2008).
The danger is the one most relevant to optimisation: resilience is routinely sacrificed for short-term productivity or stability (Meadows 2008). A hyper-“efficient” system — a monoculture crop is Meadows’ example — produces more in the short run but has stripped out the redundant pathways needed to survive a shock (Meadows 2008). Efficiency and resilience are in tension; the lean system is the brittle one.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
G, T = 100.0, 200
shock = (rng.uniform(0, 8, T) * (rng.random(T) < 0.15))   # ongoing small disturbances
shock[120] += 75                                          # one big perturbation

def resilient(shock):
    S = np.empty(T); S[0] = G
    for t in range(1, T):
        s = S[t-1] + 0.12*(G - S[t-1]) - shock[t]
        S[t] = max(s, 40.0)            # redundant backup loop = a floor it can't fall through
    return S

def efficient(shock):
    S = np.empty(T); S[0] = G
    for t in range(1, T):
        if S[t-1] < 35:                # past the threshold, no redundancy left -> collapse
            S[t] = S[t-1] * 0.92
        else:
            S[t] = max(0.0, S[t-1] + 0.30*(G - S[t-1]) - shock[t])
    return S

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.2))
ax.plot(efficient(shock), color="#c0392b", lw=2.0, label="'efficient' (fast, no redundancy)")
ax.plot(resilient(shock), color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, label="resilient (redundant meta-loop)")
ax.axvline(120, color="#888", ls=":", lw=1.2); ax.text(122, 90, "big shock", fontsize=8.5, color="#555")
ax.axhline(35, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
ax.text(2, 30, "collapse threshold", fontsize=8, color="#c0392b")
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("stock"); ax.set_ylim(0, 110)
ax.set_title("Efficiency buys calm-time performance; resilience survives the shock")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="lower left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Resilience vs efficiency, simulated. Both systems hold a stock near its goal (100) against ongoing small shocks, then take one large shock at t=120. The ‘efficient’ system (red) restores faster in calm times (a stronger single feedback) but has shed its redundant pathways — once the big shock pushes it past a threshold, it has no backup and collapses. The resilient system (green) restores more slowly but its redundant ‘meta-loop’ sets a floor it cannot fall through, so it absorbs the shock and recovers. Optimising for the calm-time number is exactly what builds in the fragility.
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text:
Self-organisation: building your own complexity
Self-organisation is a system’s capacity to make its own structure more complex — to learn, diversify, and evolve from simple organising rules, with no central blueprint (Meadows 2008). Meadows calls it a form of “collective intelligence” that arises without central intent (Meadows 2008). It is also one of the highest leverage points: a system that can rewrite its own structure can adapt to almost anything, which is why suppressing self-organisation (in the name of order or predictability) is so costly (Meadows 2008). This property is the bridge to the emergence of the Dynamics cluster — order from simple local rules.
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text:
Hierarchy: managing complexity in layers
Complex systems are typically arranged in hierarchies — layers where subsystems serve the needs of larger systems (Meadows 2008). Hierarchy is an information-management device: it reduces the information load on the whole, because each subsystem mainly coordinates its own internal parts, not the entire system (Meadows 2008).






WarningSuboptimisation — the failure mode of hierarchy



A hierarchy’s purpose is to serve its subsystems and the whole. It fails through suboptimisation: when a subsystem’s goal comes to dominate the whole system’s goal (Meadows 2008). Meadows’ example: a professor who optimises only publishing papers (a sub-goal) at the expense of the university’s mission to educate (the whole’s goal) makes the system dysfunctional (Meadows 2008). This is the structural cousin of Goodhart’s law / seeking the wrong goal and the tyranny of metrics: a local metric, maximised, starves the purpose it was meant to serve.
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Optimising away resilience. Cutting “redundant” capacity raises the calm-time number and removes the pathways that survive a shock — fragility is the invisible cost of efficiency. (Meadows 2008)
Suppressing self-organisation for control. A system that can’t restructure itself can’t adapt; rigid top-down order trades away the highest form of robustness. (Meadows 2008)
Rewarding a subsystem’s goal. Metrics that reward a part (publications, a department’s KPI) over the whole’s purpose drive suboptimisation. (Meadows 2008)
Confusing efficiency with health. A lean, fast, single-pathway system looks better right up until the perturbation it can’t absorb. (Meadows 2008)
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Meadows, Thinking in Systems — resilience as survival/recovery from many feedback loops and meta-feedback loops, and its sacrifice for short-term productivity (the monoculture); self-organisation as building one’s own complexity from simple rules (“collective intelligence,” a top leverage point); hierarchy as layered subsystems that reduce information load, and suboptimisation (the publishing professor) as its failure mode.
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text:
Appendix · the dynamics

The resilience model. Both systems are a stock with restoring feedback S_{t+1}=S_t+r(G-S_t)-\text{shock}_t. The “efficient” system uses a larger r (faster calm-time recovery) but is single-pathway: below a threshold the restoring term flips to decay S_{t+1}=0.92\,S_t (a reinforcing collapse loop). The resilient system uses a smaller r but adds a redundant backup — a hard floor S_t\ge 40 standing in for a meta-loop that activates under stress. The lesson is structural: the parameter that looks best in calm operation (r large) is the one that, without redundancy, makes the large shock fatal.
Why efficiency and resilience trade off. Redundancy is, by definition, capacity that is idle in normal operation — so any metric that scores only normal operation scores redundancy as waste and prunes it. The pruned system is Pareto-better on the calm-time metric and strictly worse on the rare-shock metric; the trade-off is invisible exactly until it matters.
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy
text:
Connections

Builds on: thinking in systems (feedback loops — resilience is redundant loops) and systems traps & leverage points (self-organisation and goals are top leverage points; suboptimisation ≈ seeking the wrong goal).
Leads to: why systems surprise us (the cognitive blind spots) and the emergence / self-organisation of the Dynamics cluster.
Echoes: the tyranny of metrics — suboptimisation is Goodhart’s law inside a hierarchy.
crumbs: Why Systems Work: Resilience, Self-Organisation & Hierarchy

title: Dates & Times
text: Dates look easy and are not. They have to reconcile two physical facts — the Earth spinning and orbiting — with “a whole raft of geopolitical phenomena”: months of unequal length, time zones, leap years, daylight saving. (Wickham et al. 2023) Get them wrong and your “events per week” is silently off by an hour or a day. The lubridate package gives dates a sane, consistent toolkit: parse them, pull them apart, round them, and do arithmetic that respects the calendar’s weirdness.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Why dates are hard
The book is unusually candid: dates and times are difficult because they must square the Earth’s rotation and orbit with human inventions — months, time zones, and daylight saving time. (Wickham et al. 2023) Every hard case (a “day” that is 23 or 25 hours long under DST, a February that is 28 or 29) comes from that collision. Treat a date as “just a number” and the calendar will eventually betray you.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Three types
R distinguishes three things, by precision: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Date — a calendar day, printed in a tibble as <date>.
Time — a time within a day (handled by the hms package).
Date-time — a date and a time that pins down a single instant (POSIXct), printed as <dttm>.

Reach for the simplest type that captures what you need; a date-time carries time-zone baggage a plain date does not.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Parsing: from text to dates
Real dates arrive as strings in a dozen formats. lubridate’s helpers parse them once you name the order of the components — ymd(), mdy(), dmy() — and you append _hms/_hm for date-times: (Wickham et al. 2023)
library(tidyverse)

ymd("2024-01-31")        # year-month-day
mdy("January 31, 2024")  # month-day-year  (same instant, messier string)
dmy("31/1/24")           # day-month-year
ymd_hms("2024-01-31 14:05:00")   # a full date-time
When the pieces live in separate columns (year, month, day, hour…), make_datetime() assembles them — the book’s flights example builds a departure date-time from five columns. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Dates & Times
Dates & Times
text:
Time spans: durations, periods, intervals
Arithmetic on dates needs three different notions of “how long,” and choosing wrong is a classic bug: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Durations — an exact number of seconds. ddays(1) is always 86,400 seconds, even across a DST boundary. Use them for physical elapsed time.
Periods — human units (weeks, months) with no fixed length in seconds; they “just work” across DST and leap years. Adding months(1) lands you on the same day next month, however many days that took.
Intervals — a duration with a start point, so it is precise even for human units (how many days in this specific month).

The DST trap: add a duration of one day to a date crossing the spring-forward boundary and you land an hour off; add a period of one day and you land on the right calendar day. Match the type to the question. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Dates & Times
text:
Time zones
R uses the IANA names of the form "<continent>/<city>" (e.g. "America/New_York") — not the ambiguous “EST”. (Wickham et al. 2023) Two operations you must not confuse: with_tz() changes how an instant is displayed (same moment, different clock), while force_tz() relabels an instant whose zone was recorded wrong (different moment). (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Dates & Times
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Durations vs periods across DST. ddays(1) (exactly 86,400s) and days(1) (one calendar day) differ on DST days. Use periods for human “a day later,” durations for elapsed physical time. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Parsing with the wrong component order. 01/02/03 is three different dates depending on locale; pick ymd/mdy/dmy deliberately. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Confusing with_tz() and force_tz(). One re-displays the same instant; the other changes which instant you mean. Mixing them corrupts timestamps. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Using a date-time when a date will do. Time-zone handling adds bugs you don’t need if the time of day is irrelevant. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Dates & Times
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — why dates are hard (rotation + orbit vs “geopolitical phenomena”, DST, leap years); the three types (<date>, time/hms, date-time/POSIXct <dttm>); lubridate parsing (ymd/mdy/dmy, _hms, make_datetime from columns); accessors (year, month, mday, yday, wday, hour), update(), and rounding (floor_date/round_date/ceiling_date); durations (fixed seconds, ddays), periods (human units), intervals; and IANA time zones with with_tz vs force_tz.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Connections

Builds on: strings & regex (dates usually arrive as text to be parsed) and the grammar of manipulation (mutate the parsed date, group_by a rounded one).
Leads to: time-series fundamentals — once observations are time-stamped and regularly bucketed, they become a series you can model and forecast.
Reappears in: panel & longitudinal data, event studies, and any analysis with a when.

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text: Social-science data is overwhelmingly categorical: party ID, religion, Likert responses, “Refused.” Stored as plain strings, categories sort alphabetically (so “Apr” comes before “Jan”) and silently absorb typos. A factor fixes both — it pins a variable to a known set of levels in an order you control. The forcats package then lets you reorder and recode those levels, which is most of what makes a categorical plot or table readable.

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
Why factors exist
A factor is R’s structure for a variable with “a fixed and known set of possible values.” (Wickham et al. 2023) Recording categories as raw strings has two failure modes the book names directly: strings “don’t sort in a useful way” (month names land alphabetically — Apr, Aug, Dec…), and there is “no protection against typos” — "Jam" for "Jan" slips through unnoticed. (Wickham et al. 2023) A factor solves both by declaring the levels up front.
library(tidyverse)

month_levels <- c("Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun",
                  "Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec")
x <- c("Dec","Apr","Jam","Mar")
factor(x, levels = month_levels)
#> [1] Dec  Apr  <NA> Mar      <- "Jam" wasn't a valid level, so it became NA (the typo is caught)
#> Levels: Jan Feb Mar ... Dec  <- and they now sort in calendar order, not alphabetically
factor() maps the data onto the declared levels; any value not in the set becomes NA (so typos surface as missing rather than passing silently). (Wickham et al. 2023) Omit the levels and R falls back to alphabetical order — rarely what you want. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
The forcats verbs
The point of forcats (an anagram of factors) is to change the order or the values of levels, which is where most categorical-data work actually lives: (Wickham et al. 2023)







Verb
What it does




fct_reorder()
reorder levels by another (numeric) variable — sort religions by mean TV hours to reveal the pattern


fct_relevel()
hand-move specific levels to the front (keep a principled order, but pull "Not applicable" first)


fct_infreq() / fct_rev()
order by frequency (and reverse it) — the standard tidy-up for bar charts


fct_recode()
rename levels by hand for clearer, consistent labels


fct_collapse()
bundle many old levels into a few new ones (various party labels → rep, dem, ind)


fct_lump()
automatically lump the smallest groups into "Other" to de-clutter a plot or table



The highest-value one is fct_reorder(): a categorical axis sorted by the value you’re plotting turns a jumbled bar chart into an instantly readable ranking. (Wickham et al. 2023)
# Reorder 'relig' by the mean of tvhours so the chart reads top-to-bottom by magnitude
gss_cat |>
  group_by(relig) |>
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = tvhours, y = fct_reorder(relig, tvhours))) +
  geom_point()






NoteThe gss_cat example



The book uses the General Social Survey sample (gss_cat) — real survey data, with the real headaches: thousands of categories, and non-substantive levels like “Refused” and “No answer” that you must decide how to handle. (Wickham et al. 2023) This is exactly the categorical mess a quantitative social scientist faces, which is why factors are a survey-research staple, not a programming nicety.

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Letting categories sort alphabetically. Months, Likert scales, and income brackets have a meaningful order; declare it in the levels or your tables and plots mislead. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Not catching invalid levels. Values outside the declared set become NA — useful, but check, or a miscoded category vanishes silently. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Plotting unordered categories. A bar chart in alphabetical order hides the pattern; fct_reorder() / fct_infreq() make it visible. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Carrying dozens of tiny categories. They clutter and overfit visuals; fct_lump() into “Other” keeps the signal. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — factors for variables with a fixed known set of values; why strings fail (no useful sort, no typo protection); factor() and levels (invalid values → NA, default alphabetical order); the forcats verbs fct_reorder, fct_relevel, fct_infreq/fct_rev, fct_recode, fct_collapse, fct_lump; and the gss_cat (General Social Survey) examples with “Refused”/“No answer” levels.

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
Connections

Builds on: strings & regex (clean the text, then make it a factor) and tidy data.
Leads to: the grammar of graphics (fct_reorder is how a categorical axis becomes a readable ranking) and regression (factor levels become the dummy variables of a model).
Reappears in: all survey analysis — party, religion, education bands, Likert items are factors.

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text: Nothing happens until the data is in R — “if you can’t get your data into R, you can’t do data science on it!” (Wickham et al. 2023) Import is “routine and boring” 80% of the time and “weird and frustrating” the other 20%, and that 20% is where analyses silently go wrong: a column guessed as text, a decimal comma mistaken for a thousands separator. This entry is the readr toolkit for reading flat files correctly, and the one habit — pinning column types — that makes import reproducible.
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Why it matters
Import is the first station of the workflow and a real technical hurdle to clear before the creative work begins. (Wickham et al. 2023) Getting it wrong corrupts everything downstream; the goal is to read the file once, correctly, reproducibly.
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
readr and the tibble
The readr package gives a consistent family for turning flat files into data frames: read_csv() (comma-delimited), read_csv2() (semicolon-delimited — common where the comma is the decimal mark), read_tsv() (tab-delimited). (Wickham et al. 2023) They produce a tibble — a “slightly tweaked data frame that works better in the tidyverse,” with a refined print method showing the first 10 rows and each column’s type (<dbl>, <int>, <chr>) under its name. (Wickham et al. 2023)
library(tidyverse)
students <- read_csv("students.csv")
#> Rows: 6 Columns: 5
#> ── Column specification ──────────────
#> chr (3): name, favourite.food, mealPlan
#> dbl (2): id, age          <- readr GUESSED these types
By default the first line supplies the column names; tweak with skip = n (drop leading metadata) or comment = "#" (drop comment lines). With no header, col_names = FALSE auto-names X1…Xn, or pass a character vector to set them. (Wickham et al. 2023)






WarningNever trust the type-guesser blindly



readr guesses each column’s type from the first 1000 rows. (Wickham et al. 2023) That heuristic fails when the early rows are unrepresentative — all integers at the top, doubles later; blanks before the real values. The fix the authors insist on for reproducible code: always supply col_types explicitly with helpers like col_double(), col_date(), col_factor(). When parsing does fail, problems() returns a tibble pinpointing exactly which cells and why. (Wickham et al. 2023)


read_csv("data.csv", col_types = cols(
  id   = col_integer(),
  date = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
  rate = col_double()
))
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
readr vs base R’s read.csv
The readr functions are “~10× faster,” show a progress bar on big files, and avoid base R’s frustrations — they don’t silently convert strings to factors and don’t mangle column names. (Wickham et al. 2023) Prefer them.
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Writing data back
write_csv() / write_tsv() save to disk — but CSV is plain text and loses type metadata, so a factor or date round-trips as a string. To preserve types exactly, write_rds() uses R’s binary format. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Many files at once
To read a folder of CSVs into one tibble, collect the paths into a vector and apply read_csv() to each with a map() (or a for loop), then bind the results — the natural job for iteration. (Wickham et al. 2023)
files <- list.files("data/", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
all   <- map(files, read_csv) |> list_rbind()
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Accepting guessed column types. The 1000-row heuristic misreads unrepresentative headers; pin col_types for anything you’ll rerun. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Wrong delimiter / decimal mark. European CSVs use ; and decimal commas — read_csv2(), not read_csv(). (Wickham et al. 2023)
Round-tripping types through CSV. Factors and dates degrade to text; use write_rds() to keep them. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Reaching for base read.csv. Slower, factor-coercing, name-munging; read_csv() avoids all three. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — import as the essential first step (“if you can’t get your data into R…”); readr (read_csv/read_csv2/read_tsv), the tibble and its print method; header handling (skip, comment, col_names); type guessing from the first 1000 rows and the rule to set col_types (with problems() to diagnose); read_csv vs read.csv (≈10× faster, no factor coercion / name munging); write_csv vs write_rds (type preservation); and reading many files with map().
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Connections

Leads to: beyond flat files (spreadsheets, databases, JSON, the web), tidy data (the next station), and iteration (reading many files).
Pairs with: strings & regex (parsing is where text becomes typed columns) and the workflow (the import box).
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text: The companion to writing functions is iteration: applying that function to many inputs — every column, every file, every group. The tidyverse’s answer is functional: across() to do the same thing to many columns, and the purrr map() family to apply a function to each element of a list and get a typed result back. The win over a for-loop is readability — you say what to do, not how to store the results.

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Why it matters
Iteration “reduces duplication when you need to perform the same operation on multiple inputs.” (Wickham et al. 2023) It’s the difference between thirty near-identical pasted lines (one per column) and a single expression that can’t drift out of sync. Combined with functions, it’s how an analysis stays short, correct, and reproducible as it scales.

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
section: across(): the same thing to many columns
text:
across(): the same thing to many columns
Inside a mutate() or summarize(), across() applies a function to many columns at once — “do the same thing to many columns.” (Wickham et al. 2023) It replaces the older mutate_each()/summarize_each() precursors.
# Summarise the mean of EVERY numeric column, missing values removed — one line, any width
df |>
  summarize(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)))

# Rescale several named columns with our own function, in place
df |>
  mutate(across(c(height, weight, age), rescale01))

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
The purrr map() family
map() applies a function to each element of a vector or list and returns a result — the functional replacement for a for-loop. (Wickham et al. 2023) Its variants differ only in the type they return, which keeps your output predictable: (Wickham et al. 2023)



Function
Returns




map()
a list


map_dbl()
a double vector


map_int()
an integer vector


map_chr()
a character vector



map_dbl(mtcars, mean)          # mean of each column -> a named numeric vector
A canonical use is reading many files: store the paths in a vector and map(paths, read_csv) to apply read_csv() to each, then bind. (Wickham et al. 2023)
files <- list.files("data/", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
data  <- map(files, read_csv) |> list_rbind()

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Why functional iteration beats loops
For-loops are explicit but verbose: they demand “bookkeeping code” — pre-allocating the output vector, tracking the index — that must be duplicated for every loop. (Wickham et al. 2023) map() hides that machinery, with three consequences the book highlights: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Readability — map functions focus on the operation; loops focus on the mechanics of storing results.
Composability — they break a list-manipulation problem into independent “bite-sized pieces” that chain with the pipe.
Predictable output — the _dbl/_chr/_int suffix guarantees the return type, so a wrong-typed result fails loudly instead of silently.







NoteFunctional, not anti-loop



The point isn’t that loops are forbidden — it’s that the repetitive bookkeeping a loop forces you to re-write is exactly the part that hides bugs. A map() says “this operation, over these inputs” and lets the package handle the rest. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Pasting a block once per column. That’s the iteration trigger; use across() so a change happens once. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Using map() when you want a vector. map() returns a list; reach for map_dbl/map_chr/map_int to get a typed vector. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Re-deriving for-loop bookkeeping. Pre-allocation and indexing are error-prone and repetitive; a functional hides them. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Silent type drift. A loop building a result can quietly change type; the typed map_* variants catch it. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — iteration to reduce duplication; across() (“do the same thing to many columns”) inside mutate/summarize; the purrr map() family (map → list, map_dbl/map_int/map_chr → typed vectors) applying a function to each element; reading many files with map(paths, read_csv); and why functional iteration beats for-loops (loops need duplicated “bookkeeping code”; maps are more readable, composable, and type-safe).

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Connections

Builds on: writing functions (you iterate a function) and the grammar of manipulation (across() lives inside the verbs).
Pairs with: importing data (read a whole folder with map) and beyond flat files (process many pages/tables).
Enables: reproducible pipelines — the same operation over every input, expressed once.

title: Missing Values
text: Real data has holes. How you handle them quietly decides your results — drop the wrong rows and you bias the sample; ignore an NA and a whole summary collapses to NA. R is deliberately strict here: a missing value is unknown, and the unknown is contagious. This entry covers what NA means, the crucial split between explicit and implicit missingness, and the tools to handle each.

title: Missing Values
text:
Why it matters
Missingness is not a formatting nuisance; it is a substantive feature of the data with its own statistics. Where the holes are, and why, can bias every estimate downstream — the same selection logic that breaks censored survival data or an experiment with attrition. R forces you to confront it rather than letting it pass silently, which is a feature, not an annoyance. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Missing Values
text:
NA is contagious
In R, NA represents an unknown value. (Wickham et al. 2023) And because it’s unknown, almost any operation touching it is also unknown — “NA is contagious.” (Wickham et al. 2023)
NA > 5      # NA  — is an unknown number bigger than 5? unknown.
10 == NA    # NA  — does 10 equal an unknown? unknown.
NA == NA    # NA  — are two unknowns equal? STILL unknown (the confusing one)
That last line is the trap: NA == NA is NA, not TRUE. So you can never test for missingness with == NA; you must use is.na(). (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Missing Values
text:
Explicit vs implicit missingness
A subtle and important distinction: (Wickham et al. 2023)

An explicit missing value is “the presence of an absence” — a cell flagged NA. You can see it.
An implicit missing value is “the absence of a presence” — a row that simply isn’t in the table. A respondent-quarter with no record; a product-month with no sales row. You can’t see it, and it’s the more dangerous kind because a naive count treats “no row” as if the case didn’t exist.

Much of careful data work is making implicit missings explicit so you can decide what to do with them.

title: Missing Values
text:
Tools
Summaries propagate NA by default — mean(), sum(), median() return NA if any input is missing, which is R insisting you make a choice. Pass na.rm = TRUE to drop missings before computing. (Wickham et al. 2023) For wrangling:







Tool
What it does




is.na(x)
the only correct test for missingness (logical vector)


coalesce(x, y, …)
take the first non-missing value across several vectors — fill from a backup


tidyr::complete()
generate all combinations of given columns, inserting explicit NA rows where they were implicitly missing


tidyr::fill()
last observation carried forward — fill a gap with the most recent non-missing value


str_replace_na()
turn NA into the literal string "NA" for display



# Make implicit missings explicit, then carry the last value forward
stocks |>
  complete(year, quarter) |>     # every year x quarter now has a row (NA where absent)
  fill(price)                    # fill the new gaps with the prior price






Warningna.rm = TRUE is a decision, not a default



Silently dropping missings changes which units you’re averaging over. If the missingness is related to the outcome (the unhappiest customers don’t respond), na.rm = TRUE gives a biased answer that looks clean. The honest move is to know why values are missing before you remove them — exactly the selection concern at the heart of causal inference. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Missing Values
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Testing x == NA. Always NA; use is.na(). (Wickham et al. 2023)
A summary that mysteriously returns NA. One missing input poisoned it; add na.rm = TRUE — but only after deciding that dropping is appropriate. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Ignoring implicit missingness. “No row” is invisible to is.na(); use complete() to surface the gaps before they bias a count. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Filling blindly with fill(). Last-observation-carried-forward assumes the value persisted; that’s a modelling assumption, not a fact. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Missing Values
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — NA as an unknown value and “NA is contagious” (NA > 5, 10 == NA, and the trap that NA == NA is NA, so use is.na()); explicit (“presence of an absence”) vs implicit (“absence of a presence”) missingness; summaries propagating NA and the na.rm argument; and the wrangling tools coalesce, complete (implicit → explicit), and fill (last observation carried forward).

title: Missing Values
text:
Connections

Builds on: logical vectors & summaries (is.na() is a logical test; na.rm governs summaries) and tidy data (implicit missing = a row that should exist).
Leads to: the statistical treatment of missingness — messy real data and the selection logic of survival/censoring and attrition.
Underwrites: every honest analysis — why data is missing is a substantive question, not a cleaning detail.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text: An analysis is finished only when someone can act on it — and trust it. Quarto is the tool R for Data Science uses to close that loop: a “unified authoring framework” that weaves prose, code, and results into one source file, so the report regenerates every figure and number from the data each time it’s rendered. (Wickham et al. 2023) The narrative can never silently fall out of sync with the computation, because the computation is the document.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Why it matters
This is the communicate station of the workflow — “absolutely critical,” the authors say, because an insight no one can follow changes nothing. (Wickham et al. 2023) Quarto is also the practical face of open and reproducible science: a copy-pasted screenshot of a result is a claim; a Quarto document that rebuilds the result from data and code is evidence. (This very encyclopedia is built in Quarto.)

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Literate programming
Quarto descends from Donald Knuth’s literate programming: the document’s spine is a narrative that explains the what and why of an analysis, with the code that performs it woven alongside. (Wickham et al. 2023) It “integrates your work into a document with commentary.” When you render (the old word is knit), a tool (knitr for R) executes the code into a markdown file of results, which pandoc then turns into the final output. (Wickham et al. 2023) You write thinking and computing together; the machine assembles the artefact.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Anatomy of a .qmd file
A Quarto document has three parts: (Wickham et al. 2023)

YAML header — between triple-dash --- fences at the very top; controls document-wide settings (title, date, output format):

---
title: "Penguin report"
format: html
---

Markdown narrative — the prose spine, written in “a lightweight set of conventions for formatting plain-text files”: # for headings, **bold**, _italics_. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Executable code chunks — a fenced block opening with the language in braces, {r} (or {python}), carrying #| options, then code, and closing with a bare fence. When rendered, the code runs and its output is inserted into the document. A chunk that draws a captioned boxplot would set a label, an echo option, and a fig-cap, then call ggplot(...) + geom_boxplot() — and the report shows the plot, regenerated from the data, not a pasted image. (Wickham et al. 2023)


Chunk options
Each chunk’s behaviour is tuned by options (in the #| header): (Wickham et al. 2023)







Option
Effect




echo
show the source code? (false → show the result but hide the code)


eval
run the code? (false → display it, don’t execute)


include
false → run the code but show neither code nor output


warning / message
suppress warnings/messages from the rendered output


fig-cap
caption a figure (and float it in some formats)


label
a unique name — used for graphic filenames and easy navigation/cross-referencing

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
One source, many formats
The payoff of separating content from format: a single .qmd renders to a “vast array” of outputs without rewriting. (Wickham et al. 2023)

Documents — HTML (the default), PDF (via LaTeX), MS Word.
Presentations — revealjs / Beamer slides.
Bigger structures — dashboards, whole websites, and books (the bookdown lineage).

Write once; deliver as a web page for a colleague, a Word doc for a journal, slides for a meeting.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Why reproducibility is the point
The authors call this “the right way to communicate data analysis” for concrete reasons: (Wickham et al. 2023)

In sync. Because every number and figure regenerates from the data on each render, the narrative can’t drift from the computation — no stale copy-pasted table.
A definitive record. The document is the analysis; it’s auditable and re-runnable, sparing you the “long-term agony” of finding a result in your workspace but having forgotten the code that made it.
Efficient. Caching (cache: true) saves long-running chunks and recomputes only when their code changes.
For future-you. It lets you “record your thoughts as you go,” so a collaborator — or you in six months — can reconstruct why each choice was made.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Pasting results instead of computing them. A static screenshot breaks the moment the data updates; let the chunk regenerate it. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Relying on a hidden workspace. If a number depends on objects not created in the document, the report isn’t reproducible. Keep the analysis self-contained. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Showing code your audience doesn’t want. Use echo: false for an executive report; keep it visible for a methods appendix. Match the output to the reader. (Wickham et al. 2023)
