Forgetting cache invalidation. Caching speeds renders but can serve stale results if you change data the chunk reads without changing its code. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — Quarto as a “unified authoring framework” weaving prose + code + results; literate programming (Knuth) and the render/knit pipeline (knitr → markdown → pandoc); the three components (YAML header, markdown narrative, executable {r} chunks) and chunk options (echo, eval, include, warning/message, fig-cap, label); the many output formats (HTML/PDF/Word, slides, dashboards, websites, books) from one source; and the reproducibility case — narrative stays in sync, a definitive, re-runnable record, caching, and documenting rationale for “future-you.”

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Connections

Builds on: the data-science workflow (this is the communicate station) and everything upstream — the report renders your transformed and visualized data.
Pairs with: open & reproducible science (the why) and communicating quantitative findings (honest presentation).
Realises: the whole track — tidy, transform, visualize, and now communicate, reproducibly.

title: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game
text: Before any single technique, R for Data Science hands you a map of the whole game: a repeatable pipeline that turns raw data into “understanding, insight, and knowledge.” Every method elsewhere in this book lives at one station on that map. Knowing the map keeps you oriented — you always know which step you’re on, which you’ve skipped, and why the analysis feels stuck.
crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game
text:
Why it matters
Beginners imagine data science as “run the model.” Practitioners know the model is a small, late step sitting on a mountain of unglamorous work that decides whether it means anything. Wickham & Grolemund’s contribution is to make that mountain legible — to give the whole process a shape you can hold in your head and return to. (Wickham et al. 2023) Internalise it and you stop thrashing: a confusing result becomes “I’m stuck at tidy,” not “data science is hard.”
crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game
text:
The map
The book organises everything around a single diagram of the process. (Wickham et al. 2023)


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import FancyBboxPatch

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.2, 3.4))

def box(x, y, w, h, label, color):
    ax.add_patch(FancyBboxPatch((x, y), w, h, boxstyle="round,pad=0.02,rounding_size=0.04",
                                 fc=color, ec="#1f3a5f", lw=1.4, zorder=2))
    ax.text(x + w/2, y + h/2, label, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold", color="#1c2733", zorder=3)

def arrow(x1, y1, x2, y2):
    ax.annotate("", xy=(x2, y2), xytext=(x1, y1),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color="#5a6b7a", lw=1.8))

box(0.02, 0.40, 0.14, 0.18, "Import", "#dce7f0")
box(0.20, 0.40, 0.14, 0.18, "Tidy",   "#dce7f0")
# the understanding loop
box(0.44, 0.66, 0.16, 0.18, "Visualize", "#e7f0e9")
box(0.44, 0.40, 0.16, 0.18, "Transform", "#e7f0e9")
box(0.44, 0.14, 0.16, 0.18, "Model",     "#e7f0e9")
box(0.72, 0.40, 0.17, 0.18, "Communicate", "#f3e9da")

arrow(0.16, 0.49, 0.20, 0.49)
arrow(0.34, 0.49, 0.43, 0.55)
arrow(0.34, 0.49, 0.43, 0.49)
arrow(0.34, 0.49, 0.43, 0.43)
# loop arrows among the three
arrow(0.52, 0.66, 0.52, 0.58)
arrow(0.52, 0.40, 0.52, 0.32)
arrow(0.60, 0.49, 0.72, 0.49)

ax.text(0.27, 0.30, "wrangling", ha="center", fontsize=8.5, style="italic", color="#9a6a2f")
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.19, 0.36), 0.16, 0.26, fill=False, ls="--",
                            ec="#9a6a2f", lw=1.2, zorder=1))
ax.text(0.52, 0.92, "understanding", ha="center", fontsize=8.5, style="italic", color="#3f8f6f")
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.42, 0.10), 0.20, 0.78, fill=False, ls="--",
                            ec="#3f8f6f", lw=1.2, zorder=1))

ax.set_xlim(0, 0.92); ax.set_ylim(0.05, 1.0); ax.axis("off")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The data-science workflow from R for Data Science. Import and Tidy get the data into R and into a consistent shape; the heart is an iterative loop of Transform ⇄ Visualize ⇄ Model (the ‘understanding’ cycle you spin many times); Communicate delivers the result to other humans. Programming underlies every step. Tidy + Transform together are ‘wrangling.’
crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game
section: The six steps, and the thinking each demands
text:
The six steps, and the thinking each demands
Import. Get the data out of a file, database, or API and into R. Trivial-sounding, occasionally maddening, and strictly first: as the authors put it, “if you can’t get your data into R, you can’t do data science on it!” (Wickham et al. 2023)
Tidy. Store the data “in a consistent form that matches the semantics of the dataset with the way it is stored.” (Wickham et al. 2023) One row per observation, one column per variable — the subject of the next entry. Tidying pays off because a consistent structure lets you spend your energy on the question, not the format.
Transform. Narrow to the observations of interest, create new variables from existing ones, and compute summaries. (Wickham et al. 2023) This is the grammar of manipulation — the verbs you apply to a tidy table.
Visualize. A “fundamentally human activity.” A good plot “will show you things that you did not expect, or raise new questions” — and sometimes hint that “you’re asking the wrong question.” (Wickham et al. 2023) Visualisation is how the data surprises you.
Model. Mathematical and computational tools, complementary to visualisation: they scale far better than a human brain. But the authors issue a sharp caution — a model “cannot fundamentally surprise you,” because it cannot question its own assumptions. (Wickham et al. 2023) Models answer the question you posed; only you (often via a plot) can notice it was the wrong question.
Communicate. “Absolutely critical.” (Wickham et al. 2023) An insight no one understands changes nothing; the analysis isn’t finished until someone else can act on it — the concern of Part VI.






NoteWrangling, and the understanding loop



Two groupings are worth naming. Tidy + Transform is “wrangling” — so named because getting data into a workable form “often feels like a fight!” (Wickham et al. 2023) And Transform ⇄ Visualize ⇄ Model is the understanding loop: you rarely run it once. You transform, plot, fit, notice something off, and circle back — many times — before anything is ready to communicate. (Wickham et al. 2023) Underneath every box sits programming, the tool that runs the whole cycle.
crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game
text:
What the map is not
It is not a one-way conveyor belt. The arrows into the understanding loop go both ways for a reason: a plot sends you back to wrangle a column you thought was clean; a model sends you back to engineer a feature. (Wickham et al. 2023) The discipline is knowing which station you’re at, so that “the analysis is broken” sharpens into a locatable problem — bad import, untidy shape, wrong transform, misleading plot, misspecified model, or unclear write-up.
crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Jumping to model before tidy. A model fit on an untidy or unclean table estimates something precise about the wrong numbers. The mountain comes before the summit. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Skipping visualize. If you never plot, you forfeit the one step that can surprise you — and you let a model’s silent assumptions stand unchecked. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Treating wrangling as menial. It is the majority of the work and the part most determinative of whether the result is trustworthy; it deserves the same care as the analysis. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Forgetting communicate. A correct analysis nobody can follow has zero effect on the world. The last step is not optional. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science — data science as turning raw data into “understanding, insight, and knowledge”; the whole-game workflow diagram (import → tidy → transform ⇄ visualize ⇄ model → communicate); “if you can’t get your data into R, you can’t do data science on it!”; tidy + transform as “wrangling” (“often feels like a fight!”); visualisation as a “fundamentally human activity” that surprises you; the caution that a model “cannot fundamentally surprise you”; and communicate as “absolutely critical.”
crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game
text:
Connections

Leads to: tidy data (the tidy station), the grammar of manipulation (transform), and the grammar of graphics (visualize).
Pairs with: exploratory data analysis (the visualize loop in statistical language) and messy real data (why import and tidy are so much work).
Closes at: communicating quantitative findings — the final station, made into its own discipline in Part VI.
crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
text: There are countless ways to arrange the same numbers in a table, and only one that every tool in the ecosystem is built to expect. Tidy data is that one shape: each variable a column, each observation a row, each value a cell. Get your data tidy and the rest of analysis stops being a fight; leave it untidy and you spend your day reshaping instead of thinking.
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
text:
Why it matters
A dataset’s meaning and its layout are two different things, and beginners conflate them. The same study can be stored a dozen ways — values spread across columns, variables crammed into one column, observations split across tables. Wickham & Grolemund’s insight is that committing to one layout pays compounding dividends: every package in the tidyverse (and most models in this book) assumes it, so a tidy table lets you “focus your struggle on the questions you care about, not fighting to get the data into the right form.” (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
text:
The three rules
A dataset is tidy if it satisfies three interlocking rules: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Each variable must have its own column.
Each observation must have its own row.
Each value must have its own cell.



Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.0))

def table(ax, title, col_labels, rows, highlight_cols=None, title_color="#1c2733"):
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10.5, color=title_color, fontweight="bold")
    ax.axis("off")
    t = ax.table(cellText=rows, colLabels=col_labels, loc="center", cellLoc="center")
    t.auto_set_font_size(False); t.set_fontsize(9); t.scale(1, 1.5)
    for j in range(len(col_labels)):
        c = t[0, j]; c.set_facecolor("#1f3a5f"); c.set_text_props(color="white", fontweight="bold")
        if highlight_cols and j in highlight_cols:
            c.set_facecolor("#9a6a2f")

# untidy: years as columns
table(axL, "Untidy: headers are values", ["country", "1999", "2000"],
      [["A", "745", "2666"], ["B", "37737", "80488"]], highlight_cols=[1, 2],
      title_color="#9a6a2f")
# tidy
table(axR, "Tidy: headers are variables", ["country", "year", "cases"],
      [["A", "1999", "745"], ["A", "2000", "2666"],
       ["B", "1999", "37737"], ["B", "2000", "80488"]], title_color="#3f8f6f")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The three rules of tidy data. Left: an untidy layout — year is spread across two column headers (the headers are values, not variable names), so a single observation is split across cells and ‘year’ has no column. Right: the tidy version — one column per variable (country, year, cases), one row per observation, one value per cell. Same numbers, a shape every tool understands.





The rules are not arbitrary aesthetics; they line up with how computers and the tidyverse vectorise work — operations run down columns, and “one observation per row” is exactly what a model, a group-by, or a plot iterates over. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
text:
The Tolstoy rule
Why does messy data feel endlessly varied while tidy data feels boringly uniform? The authors answer with a borrowed line from Anna Karenina: (Wickham et al. 2023)

“Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way. Tidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way.”

This is the whole argument in one sentence. There is essentially one tidy shape, so once you know it you can learn a single set of tools and apply them everywhere. Messiness, by contrast, is infinite — values in headers, multiple variables jammed in one column, one observation smeared across rows — so ad-hoc fixes never generalise. Investing in the one tidy form is what makes your skills transferable across every future dataset. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
text:
Tidying: pivoting
Real data arrives untidy, so tidying is a routine first move, and it usually reduces to pivoting — and the two common problems each have a direction: (Wickham et al. 2023)

A variable is spread across column headers (like 1999 and 2000 above). The headers are values of a variable (year), not variable names. Pivot longer: gather those columns into a name column and a value column.

library(tidyverse)

# values trapped in the column names -> gather them into rows
table_untidy |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(`1999`, `2000`),
               names_to  = "year",
               values_to = "cases")

One observation is split across several rows (e.g. cases and population for the same country-year sit on different rows). Pivot wider: spread the name/value pair back into one column each, reuniting the observation onto a single row. (Wickham et al. 2023)

table_long |>
  pivot_wider(names_from  = type,    # "cases" / "population"
              values_from = count)
Pivoting longer and wider are inverses; together they reshape almost any real table into the one tidy form the rest of your tools expect.
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
section: Why tidy is the foundation of this book
text:
Why tidy is the foundation of this book
Tidy data is the silent precondition of nearly every method in this encyclopedia. A regression wants one row per observation and one column per predictor; an exploratory plot maps tidy columns to visual properties; a causal design needs treatment, outcome, and covariates as clean, separate columns. (Wickham et al. 2023) When a modelling function throws a baffling error, the cause is very often an untidy input — a variable hiding in the headers, or two variables sharing a cell.
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Column headers that are values, not names. 2019, 2020, 2021 as separate columns means year is a variable with no column. Pivot longer. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Two variables in one cell. A rate cell holding "745/19987" packs cases and population together; separate them before you can compute anything. (Wickham et al. 2023)
One observation across multiple rows. If a single country-year’s cases and population sit on different rows, group-bys and joins misbehave. Pivot wider. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Tidying for the machine and forgetting the human. Tidy is the right shape for computing; a final table for readers may deliberately be untidy (wide, with year columns) because it’s easier to scan. The two audiences want different shapes. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science — the formal definition of tidy data and its three rules (each variable a column, each observation a row, each value a cell); the Tolstoy analogy (“tidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way”); tidy data as what lets you “focus on the questions you care about, not fighting to get the data into the right form”; and pivoting longer / wider as the core tidying operations.
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation
text:
Connections

Builds on: the data-science workflow (tidy is the second station) and messy real data (the rectangular data frame and why real data isn’t tidy on arrival).
Leads to: the grammar of data manipulation — the verbs all assume a tidy table — and relational data & joins (tidy tables linked by keys).
Underwrites: every modelling Part — regression onward silently requires one row per observation.
crumbs: Tidy Data: One Row, One Observation

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text: Linear regression assumes a straight line; the world is rarely straight. The flexible methods of this entry — splines, local regression, and generalized additive models — let each predictor’s effect curve freely, while keeping the model interpretable: you can still plot what each variable does, holding the others fixed. This is the sweet spot between a rigid line and a black-box forest — flexibility you can read.

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
Why it matters
Most flexible ML buys accuracy by becoming opaque. Splines and GAMs are the exception: they capture genuine non-linearity and stay legible, which is exactly what a social scientist needs when the shape of an effect is the finding (wages rise with age, then fall). (James et al. 2021) They also generalise the ad-hoc tricks of functional form — logs, quadratics, dummies — into a principled toolkit for learning the shape from data.

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
section: The simple extensions, and why they fall short
text:
The simple extensions, and why they fall short
Two first attempts, both limited: (James et al. 2021)

Polynomial regression adds powers (\text{age},\text{age}^2,\text{age}^3,\dots). Easy, but non-local: a wiggle in one region flexes the whole curve, and high-degree polynomials go wild at the boundaries, producing absurd tail predictions. (James et al. 2021)
Step functions bin X into regions and fit a constant in each (dummy variables). Simple, but unless the breakpoints are natural they miss trends within a bin — a single “age 20–40” bin hides the steady wage rise inside it. (James et al. 2021)

Splines fix both by being piecewise and smooth.

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
Regression splines
A regression spline fits separate low-degree polynomials over regions divided by knots, stitched together with smoothness constraints. (James et al. 2021)

A cubic spline is piecewise cubic, constrained so the curve and its first and second derivatives are continuous at every knot — no kinks, no jumps, visually seamless. (James et al. 2021)
Flexibility = knots. More knots ⇒ more wiggle. A cubic spline with K knots uses K+4 degrees of freedom. (James et al. 2021)
Natural splines fix the boundary problem: ordinary splines have high variance at the tails, so a natural spline forces the curve to be linear beyond the boundary knots, giving stable, trustworthy tail estimates. (James et al. 2021)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
n = 120
x = np.sort(rng.uniform(0, 1, n))
f_true = lambda t: np.sin(2.2 * np.pi * t) * (t < 0.7) + (0.5 - 0.8 * (t - 0.7)) * (t >= 0.7)
y = f_true(x) + rng.normal(0, 0.25, n)

# Natural cubic spline via a truncated-power basis with linear tail constraints.
knots = np.quantile(x, [0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8])
def trunc(t, k):
    d = (t - k); return np.where(d > 0, d**3, 0.0)
def nat_basis(t):
    # natural cubic spline basis (Hastie et al. form): linear + truncated-cubic differences
    K = knots; last = K[-1]
    cols = [np.ones_like(t), t]
    dK = trunc(t, K[-2]) - trunc(t, last)
    for k in K[:-1]:
        cols.append((trunc(t, k) - trunc(t, last)) / (last - k) - dK / (last - K[-2]))
    return np.column_stack(cols)

B = nat_basis(x); coef, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(B, y, rcond=None)
xs = np.linspace(0, 1, 300)
spline = nat_basis(xs) @ coef
poly = np.polyval(np.polyfit(x, y, 10), xs)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=10, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.6, label="data")
ax.plot(xs, f_true(xs), color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="true function")
ax.plot(xs, poly, color="#d98a29", lw=1.8, label="degree-10 polynomial")
ax.plot(xs, spline, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4, label="natural cubic spline")
for k in knots: ax.axvline(k, color="#9aa0a6", ls=":", lw=0.8)
ax.set_ylim(-1.8, 1.8)
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("Splines bend smoothly; polynomials thrash at the edges")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A natural cubic spline vs a global polynomial. Both fit the same curved, noisy data. The degree-10 polynomial (orange) tracks the middle adequately but swings wildly at the boundaries — its non-local nature amplifies edge noise. The natural cubic spline (blue), piecewise-cubic between knots (dashed verticals) and linear beyond the outer knots, follows the true curve (green) smoothly and stays stable at the tails. Flexibility you can trust.

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
Smoothing splines
Regression splines make you choose knots. Smoothing splines sidestep that with a “loss + penalty” formulation — fit every point but penalise wiggliness: (James et al. 2021) 
\min_g \ \sum_{i=1}^n \big(y_i-g(x_i)\big)^2 \;+\; \lambda \int g''(t)^2\,dt .
 The roughness penalty \int g''(t)^2 dt measures total curvature; \lambda dials the trade-off between fitting the data and staying smooth. (James et al. 2021) As \lambda runs from 0 to \infty, the effective degrees of freedom fall from n (interpolate every point) to 2 (a straight line) — so \lambda is a continuous flexibility knob. It’s chosen by leave-one-out cross-validation, which (as in the CV entry) has an efficient single-pass formula. (James et al. 2021)

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
Local regression (LOESS)
Local regression fits the curve at a target point x_0 using only nearby observations, weighted by distance — closest points count most. (James et al. 2021) The key knob is the span s, the fraction of data in each local neighbourhood: a small span gives a wiggly, very local fit; a large span approaches a global line. (James et al. 2021) It’s the KNN idea applied to regression — smooth, intuitive, and a natural way to let the data speak locally.

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
Generalized additive models (GAMs)
The payoff: extend all of this to many predictors while keeping interpretability, via a generalized additive model: (James et al. 2021) 
y_i=\beta_0+f_1(x_{i1})+f_2(x_{i2})+\dots+f_p(x_{ip})+\epsilon_i ,
 each f_j a smooth function (spline, LOESS) learned from data. The magic is additivity: because the effects add, you can examine each f_j separately — plot the (non-linear) effect of one predictor holding the others fixed, exactly the ceteris-paribus reading of a regression coefficient, now curved. (James et al. 2021) GAMs handle classification too, via a logit link (e.g. \Pr(\text{wage}>250\text{k})). (James et al. 2021)
The cost of additivity is its blind spot: a GAM misses interactions between predictors unless you add them by hand (an x_j\times x_k term or a bivariate smooth). (James et al. 2021) It sits one notch below trees/forests in flexibility (which find interactions automatically) and well above them in interpretability — the GAM’s reason for being.






NoteISL’s Wage GAM



Predicting wages from year, age, education: a smoothing spline for year (4 df), a smoothing spline for age (5 df), and a step function for the categorical education. The fitted effects read like a story: wage rises with age until ~60, then declines; rises slowly but steadily across 2003–2009; and climbs with education. (James et al. 2021) Three legible curves — non-linear, yet as interpretable as a table of coefficients.

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




High-degree global polynomials. They thrash at the boundaries; prefer splines (and natural splines for stable tails). (James et al. 2021)
Too many knots / too small \lambda or span. Over-flexible fits chase noise; tune flexibility (df, \lambda, span) by cross-validation. (James et al. 2021)
Forgetting GAM additivity. No interactions unless you add them; if effects depend on combinations, a GAM will quietly miss them. (James et al. 2021)
Reading a smooth effect as causal. A GAM’s f_j is a flexible conditional shape — still a correlation with controls, not an effect. (James et al. 2021)
Over-interpreting wiggles at the data’s edges. Sparse-tail regions have high variance; natural splines and honest uncertainty bands matter most there. (James et al. 2021)

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 7) — polynomial regression (non-local, wild boundaries) and step functions (binning); regression splines (knots; cubic splines with continuous first/second derivatives; natural splines linear beyond boundary knots; K knots =K+4 df); smoothing splines (RSS +\lambda\int g''^2, effective df from n to 2, LOOCV); local regression / LOESS (distance weights, the span); GAMs (y=\beta_0+\sum_j f_j(x_j), additive interpretability, logit for classification, missing interactions); the Wage GAM (year 4 df, age 5 df, education step function; wage peaks near 60).

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Truncated-power spline basis. A cubic spline with knots \xi_1,\dots,\xi_K can be written as a linear model in the basis \{1,x,x^2,x^3,(x-\xi_1)_+^3,\dots,(x-\xi_K)_+^3\} where (t)_+=\max(t,0). Each truncated term adds curvature only past its knot, which is what makes the fit piecewise while continuity of g,g',g'' is automatic (the cubic truncation has two vanishing derivatives at the knot). Fitting is then ordinary least squares on these K+4 columns — flexibility with the linear model’s machinery intact.
The smoothing-spline solution. Remarkably, the minimiser of \sum_i (y_i-g(x_i))^2+\lambda\int g''(t)^2dt over all functions is a natural cubic spline with knots at every unique x_i — an n-dimensional problem with a finite, closed-form answer. The fit is linear in y: \hat g=S_\lambda y for a smoother matrix S_\lambda, and the effective degrees of freedom are \operatorname{trace}(S_\lambda), decreasing in \lambda. LOOCV has the leverage shortcut \text{CV}=\frac1n\sum_i\big(\frac{y_i-\hat g(x_i)}{1-S_{\lambda,ii}}\big)^2 — the same leverage-corrected residual as for linear models.
GAM fitting (backfitting). With \hat f_j smooth, GAMs are fit by backfitting: cycle through predictors, each time smoothing the partial residual y-\beta_0-\sum_{k\ne j}\hat f_k(x_k) against x_j to update \hat f_j, until convergence — a coordinate-descent that reduces a p-dimensional smooth to a sequence of one-dimensional ones.

title: Beyond Linearity: Splines & GAMs
text:
Connections

Builds on: functional form (logs/quadratics generalised), bias–variance (df/\lambda/span as flexibility), and cross-validation (tuning).
Leads to: support vector machines (non-linearity via kernels) and neural networks (non-linearity via learned features) — the same goal by other means, trading away the GAM’s legibility.
Bridges back: a flexible f_j is still descriptive; the shape is a conditional association, not a cause.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text: Part IV kept naming neural networks as the far, opaque end of the flexibility–interpretability frontier. Here is what’s inside the box. A neuron is a weighted sum passed through a nonlinear switch; stack thousands across layers and the network learns its own features — edges, then shapes, then faces — instead of you engineering them. That representation-learning is why deep nets conquered vision and language. It is also why, on a small social-science table, they usually have nothing to offer a random forest doesn’t.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Why it matters
Deep learning is the most consequential ML development of the century, and the one most surrounded by hype. (James et al. 2021) A social scientist needs to understand it well enough to (a) use it when the data really is unstructured (text, images, audio) and (b) resist it when a linear model or forest would do as well and explain itself. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) This entry demystifies the architecture and the training — and draws the honest line about when it actually wins.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
The neuron
The atom of a network is the neuron: take inputs Z, form a weighted sum plus a bias, and pass it through a nonlinear activation function \sigma: (James et al. 2021) 
X_m=\sigma\big(\alpha_{m0}+\alpha_m^\top Z\big).
 That nonlinearity is non-negotiable: without it, stacking layers just composes linear maps into one linear map, and the whole network collapses to a regression. (James et al. 2021) The two activations to know: (James et al. 2021)

Sigmoid \sigma(v)=1/(1+e^{-v}) — the logistic squash to (0,1), classic but prone to vanishing gradients.
ReLU \sigma(v)=\max(0,v) — the modern default: cheap, and effective in deep networks. Each ReLU is a hinge, and a sum of hinges builds a piecewise-linear curve (the figure).

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Feed-forward networks: features, learned
A feed-forward network stacks hidden layers between input and output. (James et al. 2021) The key idea: each hidden layer’s units are learned features — “constructed regressors,” representations, embeddings — built automatically from the raw inputs rather than hand-crafted. (James et al. 2021) The output layer reads off the final representation: linear for regression, softmax for classification (probabilities over mutually exclusive classes). (James et al. 2021)
Two facts frame the power: (James et al. 2021)

Universal approximation. A single hidden layer with enough units can approximate any reasonable function arbitrarily well (Kuan & White, 1994). Flexibility is not the question — control is.
Depth buys hierarchy. Width adds raw flexibility, but depth lets the network compose simple patterns into complex ones — early layers detect edges, later layers assemble them into objects. Hierarchical features are what make deep learning more than wide learning. (James et al. 2021)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.8, 3.9))

# ---- Left: network schematic ----
layers = [3, 5, 2]
xpos = [0.1, 0.5, 0.9]
for li, (n, x) in enumerate(zip(layers, xpos)):
    ys = np.linspace(0.15, 0.85, n)
    for y in ys:
        c = {0: "#1f3a5f", 1: "#d98a29", 2: "#1f8a4c"}[li]
        axL.add_patch(plt.Circle((x, y), 0.045, color=c, zorder=3))
    if li > 0:
        prev_ys = np.linspace(0.15, 0.85, layers[li-1])
        for y in ys:
            for py in prev_ys:
                axL.plot([xpos[li-1], x], [py, y], color="#cfd3d8", lw=0.6, zorder=1)
axL.text(0.1, 0.95, "inputs", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#1f3a5f")
axL.text(0.5, 0.95, "hidden\n(learned features)", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#b9791f")
axL.text(0.9, 0.95, "output", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#1f7a44")
axL.set_xlim(0, 1); axL.set_ylim(0.05, 1.05); axL.axis("off")
axL.set_title("Feed-forward network")

# ---- Right: a tiny ReLU net trained on 1-D data ----
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 120); ytrue = np.sin(1.2 * x) + 0.3 * x
y = ytrue + rng.normal(0, 0.15, x.size)
Xin = x[:, None]
H = 16
W1 = rng.normal(0, 1.0, (1, H)); b1 = np.zeros(H)
W2 = rng.normal(0, 0.5, (H, 1)); b2 = 0.0
lr = 0.005
for step in range(4000):
    Z = Xin @ W1 + b1; Hd = np.maximum(0, Z)            # ReLU
    yhat = (Hd @ W2 + b2).ravel()
    err = (yhat - y) / x.size
    gW2 = Hd.T @ err[:, None]; gb2 = err.sum()
    dH = (err[:, None] @ W2.T) * (Z > 0)
    gW1 = Xin.T @ dH; gb1 = dH.sum(0)
    W2 -= lr * gW2; b2 -= lr * gb2; W1 -= lr * gW1; b1 -= lr * gb1

Zf = x[:, None] @ W1 + b1; fit = (np.maximum(0, Zf) @ W2 + b2).ravel()
axR.scatter(x, y, s=8, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.6)
axR.plot(x, ytrue, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="true function")
axR.plot(x, fit, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2, label=f"{H}-unit ReLU net")
axR.set_title("Learned fit (sum of ReLU hinges)")
axR.set_xlabel("x"); axR.set_ylabel("y"); axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Left: a feed-forward network — inputs feed a hidden layer (each unit a weighted sum + activation), whose learned features feed the output. Right: a small ReLU network trained on noisy nonlinear data. Each hidden ReLU contributes a ‘hinge’; summed at the output they compose a smooth-looking piecewise-linear fit (blue) of the true curve (green) — universal approximation in miniature.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Training: gradient descent and backpropagation
Training means finding the weights that minimise a loss (penalised least squares for regression, cross-entropy for classification). (James et al. 2021) The machinery: (James et al. 2021)

Gradient descent nudges every weight downhill along the loss gradient; the learning rate \eta sets the step size (too big diverges, too small crawls).
Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) computes the gradient on small minibatches instead of the whole dataset — essential at scale; one pass through all batches is an epoch.
Backpropagation is the efficient chain rule: it propagates the error backward through the layers to get the gradient with respect to every parameter in one sweep. (James et al. 2021)
Non-convexity. The loss surface has local minima and saddle points — and remarkably, the noise in SGD’s minibatch sampling helps the optimiser skitter past them. (James et al. 2021)

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
section: Regularization: taming a model with too many parameters
text:
Regularization: taming a model with too many parameters
A network can have more parameters than observations, so overfitting is the central danger. (James et al. 2021) The countermeasures: (James et al. 2021)

Weight decay — an \ell_2/ridge penalty shrinking weights toward zero.
Dropout — randomly zero out a fraction of units each training step, forcing redundant, robust pathways rather than fragile co-adaptations. (James et al. 2021)
Early stopping — watch a validation set and stop when out-of-sample error starts to rise. (James et al. 2021)
Data augmentation — manufacture new training examples by perturbing existing ones (rotate/crop an image), enlarging effective n.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Specialised architectures (conceptually)
Beyond the plain feed-forward net: (James et al. 2021)

Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) — for images: weight sharing (the same small filter slides across the image) and pooling tame pixel dimensionality; deep variants like ResNet use skip connections so gradients survive many layers.
Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) — for sequences (text, audio): they carry a state across steps, like a nonlinear autoregression; LSTMs stabilise the information flow.
Transformers — today’s state of the art for language (BERT, and the architecture behind large language models): self-attention weights every part of a sequence against every other, regardless of distance. (James et al. 2021)

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
When neural nets actually win
The practical verdict, and the reason this track keeps ML separate: neural nets are not always better. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) They shine on unstructured data — images, text, audio — where features can’t be hand-engineered, and they need very large n to train. (James et al. 2021) On the small, tabular datasets typical of social science, a well-specified linear model or random forest often matches or beats an ad-hoc network — with far more interpretability. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)






NoteThe numbers tell the story




Big, unstructured data: in a hedonic price study with 10 million observations, a deep network hit test R^2\approx90\%, beating random forests (80%) and linear models (70%). (James et al. 2021)
Small, tabular data: on the Wage data, plain OLS had a test MSE of 0.228 while a simple neural net trailed at 0.249. (James et al. 2021)

Same method, opposite verdicts — set entirely by sample size and data type. Reach for a net when you have millions of unstructured examples; reach for a regression when you have a survey.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




A neural net on small tabular data. It rarely beats a tuned forest or regression and forfeits interpretability; match the tool to n and data type. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Skipping regularization. With more parameters than data, weight decay / dropout / early stopping are not optional. (James et al. 2021)
A bad learning rate. Too large diverges, too small never converges; it’s the first thing to tune. (James et al. 2021)
Reading weights as effects. A network’s millions of weights are uninterpretable and not causal; use interpretability tools, and never confuse them with explanation. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Believing depth always helps. Past a point it adds parameters and overfitting, not signal; validate. (James et al. 2021)

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 10) — the neuron X_m=\sigma(\alpha_m^\top Z), essential nonlinearity, sigmoid and ReLU; feed-forward nets, hidden layers as learned representations, softmax output, universal approximation (Kuan & White 1994), depth/hierarchy; SGD, minibatches/epochs, backpropagation, learning rate, non-convexity; weight decay, dropout, early stopping; CNNs (weight sharing, pooling, ResNet skip connections), RNNs/LSTMs, Transformers; when NNs win (10M-obs hedonic R^2 90% vs RF 80% vs linear 70%; Wage OLS MSE 0.228 vs NN 0.249).
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang — the social-science reality: on small tabular data, simpler models often match neural nets, and weights are not explanations.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Forward pass (one hidden layer). With inputs Z\in\mathbb R^p, hidden weights A\in\mathbb
R^{H\times p}, output weights w: 
H_m=\sigma\big((AZ)_m+a_m\big),\qquad \hat Y=w^\top H + b .
 Stacking L such layers, \hat Y is a depth-L composition of (linear \to nonlinear) maps — flexible because of the repeated \sigma.
Backpropagation. For loss \mathcal L, the chain rule gives, layer by layer from the output, \delta^{(L)}=\nabla_{\hat Y}\mathcal L and \delta^{(\ell)}=(W^{(\ell+1)\top}\delta^{(\ell+1)})\odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)}), with parameter gradients \partial\mathcal L/\partial W^{(\ell)}=\delta^{(\ell)} (h^{(\ell-1)})^\top. One backward sweep yields all gradients in O(\text{#params}) — the efficiency that makes training feasible. For ReLU, \sigma'(z)=\mathbf 1[z>0], so gradients flow only through active units (and the figure’s fit is exactly piecewise-linear).
SGD update. \theta \leftarrow \theta-\eta\,\widehat{\nabla_\theta\mathcal L}, the gradient estimated on a minibatch. The minibatch noise is a feature: it perturbs the trajectory enough to escape sharp local minima and saddle points, often landing in flatter, better-generalising basins.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Connections

Builds on: logistic regression (a one-neuron sigmoid/softmax classifier), regularization (weight decay, early stopping), bias–variance (overfitting), and the accuracy–interpretability frontier (the opaque extreme).
Leads to: unsupervised learning (autoencoders/embeddings are representation learning without labels) and the social-science workflow of ML for social science.
Bridges back: a network is the ultimate predictive black box — superb at \hat Y, mute about why; its weights are not effects.

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text: Logistic regression draws the boundary between classes directly. The generative classifiers take the opposite route: model the distribution of the predictors within each class — what a defaulter’s finances look like, what a non-defaulter’s look like — attach the class priors, and invert with Bayes’ theorem to get the probability of each class. The payoff is stability where logistic regression wobbles: well-separated classes, small samples, and many classes. LDA, QDA, and Naive Bayes are three points on this generative spectrum.
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
Why it matters
Classification so far meant logistic regression and KNN. The generative classifiers complete the toolkit — and they’re not merely historical: LDA is often more stable than logistic regression exactly where the latter fails, and Naive Bayes thrives in the high-dimensional, p>n settings (text, genomics) where modelling a full covariance is hopeless. (James et al. 2021) They also make the Bayes classifier concrete: each is an attempt to approximate that gold standard by plugging estimates into Bayes’ theorem.
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
Generative vs discriminative
The fork in the road: (James et al. 2021)

Discriminative (logistic regression) models \Pr(Y=k\mid X) directly — straight to the boundary.
Generative (LDA/QDA/Naive Bayes) models the density of X within each class, f_k(x)=\Pr(X=x\mid Y=k), together with the priors \pi_k (each class’s overall share), then uses Bayes’ theorem to flip them into the posterior: 
\Pr(Y=k\mid X=x)=\frac{\pi_k\,f_k(x)}{\sum_{l=1}^K \pi_l\,f_l(x)} .


It is called generative because, knowing each f_k, you could in principle generate synthetic examples of each class. (James et al. 2021) The three methods differ only in how they model f_k.

The probabilistic perspective
This fork is the doorway to Kevin Murphy’s larger thesis in Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective: that machine learning is best seen not as a “cookbook” of ad-hoc recipes but as “a unified view of the field through the lens of probabilistic modeling and inference.” (Murphy 2012) Every method in this track is, underneath, a way of estimating a probability — supervised learning is conditional density estimation, p(y\mid x). (Murphy 2012) Seen this way, the generative/discriminative split is a genuine modelling choice with real trade-offs Murphy spells out: (Murphy 2012)

Generative models specify how the data were made — the joint p(x,y) via class-conditionals p(x\mid y) and priors p(y). They are often easy to fit (counting and averaging), and — their signature advantage — they handle missing features (marginalise them out) and unlabelled data (semi-supervised learning), because they model x itself. (Murphy 2012)
Discriminative models fit the posterior p(y\mid x) directly. Murphy’s verdict: they are usually more accurate, because their “job” is easier — they never have to model the complicated distribution of the features x — and they permit arbitrary feature preprocessing (basis expansions). (Murphy 2012)

The same lens reframes regularization: a MAP estimate is just penalised MLE, with the log-prior acting as the penalty, and plain MLE’s overfitting shows up as the “zero-count problem” — an unseen category assigned probability zero. (Murphy 2012) And it gives a principled Occam’s razor: a complex model must “spread its probability mass thinly” over the many datasets it could explain, so a simpler model that fits is preferred automatically — Bayesian model selection. (Murphy 2012) Two of Murphy’s borrowed maxims sit behind the whole track: “there is no universally best model” (the No Free Lunch theorem) and George Box’s “all models are wrong, but some models are useful.” (Murphy 2012)
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
Linear discriminant analysis (LDA)
The oldest statistical classifier (R. A. Fisher, 1936). (James et al. 2021) LDA assumes that within each class the predictors are multivariate normal, N(\mu_k,\Sigma), with class-specific means \mu_k but — the key restriction — a common covariance matrix \Sigma shared across all classes. (James et al. 2021) Plugging Gaussian densities into Bayes’ theorem and taking logs, an observation is assigned to the class with the largest discriminant function: 
\delta_k(x)=x^\top\Sigma^{-1}\mu_k-\tfrac12\mu_k^\top\Sigma^{-1}\mu_k+\log\pi_k .
 Because x enters only linearly, the boundaries \{\delta_k=\delta_l\} are lines (or hyperplanes) — hence linear discriminant analysis. (James et al. 2021) With the true parameters this is the Bayes classifier; LDA approximates it by substituting sample means, priors, and a pooled covariance. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
Quadratic discriminant analysis (QDA)
QDA relaxes the one strong assumption: let each class have its own covariance \Sigma_k. (James et al. 2021) Now x enters the discriminant quadratically, so the boundaries curve — QDA can carve shapes LDA cannot. The cost is a classic bias–variance trade: (James et al. 2021)

LDA estimates far fewer parameters (one shared \Sigma) → lower variance, higher bias if the covariances really differ. Best for small training sets.
QDA estimates a separate covariance per class — K\frac{p(p+1)}{2} parameters → lower bias, higher variance. Worth it only when the training set is large (or the classes’ shapes genuinely differ). (James et al. 2021)

The figure shows the difference directly: a straight LDA boundary vs a curved QDA one on classes with unequal spreads.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
# Class 0: compact; class 1: elongated and rotated — different covariances.
mu0 = np.array([-1.2, 0.0]); C0 = np.array([[0.5, 0.0], [0.0, 0.5]])
mu1 = np.array([1.4, 0.6]);  C1 = np.array([[1.6, 0.9], [0.9, 0.8]])
n = 250
X0 = rng.multivariate_normal(mu0, C0, n); X1 = rng.multivariate_normal(mu1, C1, n)

def gauss_loglik(x, mu, C):
    Ci = np.linalg.inv(C); d = x - mu
    return -0.5 * np.einsum("...i,ij,...j->...", d, Ci, d) - 0.5 * np.log(np.linalg.det(C))

# estimates
m0, m1 = X0.mean(0), X1.mean(0)
S0, S1 = np.cov(X0.T), np.cov(X1.T)
Spool = 0.5 * (S0 + S1)                     # LDA: shared covariance
gx, gy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-4, 5, 300), np.linspace(-3.5, 4, 300))
grid = np.c_[gx.ravel(), gy.ravel()]
lda = (gauss_loglik(grid, m1, Spool) - gauss_loglik(grid, m0, Spool)).reshape(gx.shape)
qda = (gauss_loglik(grid, m1, S1) - gauss_loglik(grid, m0, S0)).reshape(gx.shape)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.4))
ax.scatter(X0[:, 0], X0[:, 1], s=10, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.5, label="class 0")
ax.scatter(X1[:, 0], X1[:, 1], s=10, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.5, label="class 1")
ax.contour(gx, gy, lda, levels=[0], colors="#c0392b", linewidths=2.4)
ax.contour(gx, gy, qda, levels=[0], colors="#1f6fb2", linewidths=2.4, linestyles="--")
ax.plot([], [], color="#c0392b", lw=2.4, label="LDA boundary (linear)")
ax.plot([], [], color="#1f6fb2", lw=2.4, ls="--", label="QDA boundary (quadratic)")
ax.set_xlabel("x₁"); ax.set_ylabel("x₂")
ax.set_title("Shared vs class-specific covariance")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: LDA vs QDA. Two classes with different covariance shapes (green is tighter, navy is elongated). LDA (red) assumes a shared covariance and can only draw a straight boundary — it misreads the differing spreads. QDA (blue) estimates each class’s covariance and bends the boundary into a curve that tracks the real shapes. LDA is steadier on small data; QDA is more accurate when the shapes truly differ and n is large.
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
Naive Bayes
Naive Bayes simplifies the generative problem to the extreme: assume the predictors are conditionally independent within each class, so the joint density factorises into a product of one-dimensional densities: (James et al. 2021) 
f_k(x)=f_{k1}(x_1)\times f_{k2}(x_2)\times\cdots\times f_{kp}(x_p) .
 The independence assumption is almost always false — income and education are correlated among voters — yet Naive Bayes works remarkably well in practice, because the drastic parameter reduction slashes variance. (James et al. 2021) You never estimate a p\times p covariance (impossible when p>n); you estimate p separate univariate densities. That is why Naive Bayes is a staple of text classification and other high-dimensional tasks. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
section: When to prefer a generative classifier
text:
When to prefer a generative classifier
LDA especially earns its place over logistic regression in three situations: (James et al. 2021)

Well-separated classes. When the classes barely overlap, logistic regression’s coefficients become unstable (they run off to infinity); LDA stays well-behaved.
Small n with roughly normal predictors. LDA’s stronger assumptions buy stability and accuracy when data is scarce and approximately Gaussian.
More than two classes. LDA handles K>2 naturally and efficiently, where multinomial logistic regression is less commonly used. (James et al. 2021)







NoteISL’s classifiers in action




Default (predict credit-card default from balance and student status). At the default 0.5 threshold, LDA classified 99.8% of non-defaulters correctly but missed 75.7% of actual defaulters — a vivid reminder (from classification) that on imbalanced data you must move the threshold to the cost of the errors you care about. (James et al. 2021)
Smarket (predict the market Up/Down). Here QDA won — ~60% held-out accuracy — its quadratic flexibility capturing structure the linear methods missed. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




QDA on small data. Its per-class covariances are variance-prone; prefer LDA unless n is large or the class shapes clearly differ. (James et al. 2021)
Trusting the normality assumption blindly. LDA/QDA assume Gaussian predictors; badly non-normal or categorical features may favour Naive Bayes or logistic regression. (James et al. 2021)
Reading Naive Bayes’ independence as literal truth. It’s a deliberately wrong, useful simplification — fine for prediction, not a claim about the world. (James et al. 2021)
Default 0.5 threshold on imbalanced classes. As the Default example shows, it misses the rare class; tune the threshold to the costs. (James et al. 2021)
Treating a discriminant coefficient as a causal effect. These are predictive classifiers; the weights are not effects. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 4) — generative vs discriminative; the Bayes- theorem posterior \Pr(Y=k\mid x)=\pi_k f_k(x)/\sum_l \pi_l f_l(x); LDA (Fisher 1936; multivariate normal, shared \Sigma, the discriminant \delta_k, linear boundaries, approximating the Bayes classifier); QDA (class-specific \Sigma_k, quadratic boundaries, the LDA–QDA bias–variance trade and parameter counts); Naive Bayes (conditional independence, density factorisation, high- dimensional power); when to prefer generative methods (well-separated classes, small n, K>2); the Default (99.8% / 75.7%-missed at 0.5) and Smarket (QDA ~60%) examples.
Bruce & Bruce — discriminant analysis and Naive Bayes in the applied data-science idiom.
Murphy, Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective — ML as a unified probabilistic inference problem (not a cookbook); supervised learning as conditional density estimation p(y\mid x); the generative vs discriminative trade-offs (generative handles missing/unlabelled data; discriminative usually more accurate “because its job is easier”); MAP as regularized MLE and the zero-count problem; Bayesian Occam’s razor (probability mass spread thinly); and the maxims No Free Lunch (“no universally best model”) and Box (“all models are wrong, but some are useful”).
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

From Gaussian density to the LDA discriminant. With f_k(x)=\frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2}|\Sigma|^{1/2}}
\exp(-\tfrac12(x-\mu_k)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu_k)) and a shared \Sigma, take \log[\pi_k f_k(x)] and drop terms common to all classes (the x^\top\Sigma^{-1}x term cancels because \Sigma is shared): 
\delta_k(x)=x^\top\Sigma^{-1}\mu_k-\tfrac12\mu_k^\top\Sigma^{-1}\mu_k+\log\pi_k ,
 linear in x. For QDA, \Sigma_k differs by class, so the x^\top\Sigma_k^{-1}x term survives, leaving a quadratic discriminant — the source of curved boundaries.
Parameter counts (the bias–variance ledger). LDA estimates K mean vectors plus one shared \Sigma (\tfrac{p(p+1)}2 entries); QDA estimates K separate \Sigma_k, i.e. K\cdot\tfrac{p(p+1)}2 — growing fast in p, hence QDA’s variance and its need for large n.
Naive Bayes as diagonal QDA. Assuming within-class independence makes each class covariance diagonal, \Sigma_k=\operatorname{diag}(\sigma_{k1}^2,\dots,\sigma_{kp}^2), so f_k factorises into univariate densities and the parameter count drops from O(p^2) to O(p) per class — the reason it survives high dimensions where full LDA/QDA cannot.
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes
text:
Connections

Builds on: Bayes’ theorem (the inversion at its heart), logistic regression (the discriminative alternative), classification & the Bayes classifier (what these approximate, and the threshold lesson), and common distributions (the Gaussian assumption).
Leads to: support vector machines (another classifier, geometric rather than probabilistic — and the one whose Heart example compares against LDA).
Bridges back: generative classifiers model \Pr(X\mid Y) to predict Y — still prediction, not explanation; the discriminant weights are not effects.
crumbs: Generative Classifiers: LDA, QDA & Naive Bayes

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text: k-means clustering forces every point into exactly one group and assumes spherical clusters. The Gaussian mixture model (GMM) is its principled generalisation: the data are a weighted blend of Gaussians, each point gets a soft membership (a probability of belonging to each cluster), and the clusters can be elliptical. Fitting it requires the EM algorithm — an elegant alternation between guessing the hidden labels and re-estimating the parameters — that turns out to solve a vast class of latent-variable and missing-data problems far beyond clustering.

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
Why it matters
Mixtures are the natural model whenever a population is a blend of unseen subgroups — latent classes of voters, patient phenotypes, topics in text. And EM is the workhorse behind them, behind factor analysis, behind hidden Markov models, and behind principled missing-data handling. Understanding EM once unlocks all of them.

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
The Gaussian mixture model
A mixture model represents a population as a weighted sum of K base distributions (Murphy 2012). In a GMM each component k is a multivariate Normal with its own mean \mu_k and covariance \Sigma_k (Murphy 2012). A discrete latent variable z_i \in \{1,\dots,K\} records each point’s hidden cluster assignment, with prior the mixing weights \pi_k (0\le\pi_k\le1, \sum_k\pi_k=1) (Murphy 2012). The density of an observation is the convex combination (Murphy 2012):

p(x_i\mid\theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k\,\mathcal N(x_i\mid\mu_k,\Sigma_k).

The payoff over k-means is soft clustering: rather than a hard label, the GMM gives the posterior probability p(z_i=k\mid x_i,\theta) that point i belongs to cluster k — its responsibility r_{ik} (Murphy 2012).

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
section: Why direct maximum likelihood is hard
text:
Why direct maximum likelihood is hard
You’d like to just maximise the log-likelihood, but the latent z blocks you (Murphy 2012):

\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\log\!\Big[\sum_{z_i} p(x_i,z_i\mid\theta)\Big].

The log cannot be pushed inside the sum over the hidden labels, so the terms won’t separate (Murphy 2012). Worse, the surface is non-convex and multimodal, with label-switching unidentifiability — permuting the cluster labels gives the identical likelihood (Murphy 2012). Direct optimisation is a mess.

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
The EM algorithm
EM sidesteps the log-sum by alternating two steps that each have closed forms, climbing to a local optimum (Murphy 2012).
E-step (Expectation). Using the current parameters, compute the responsibilities — the posterior membership of every point in every cluster (Murphy 2012):

r_{ik} = \frac{\pi_k\,\mathcal N(x_i\mid\mu_k,\Sigma_k)}{\sum_{k'=1}^{K}\pi_{k'}\,\mathcal N(x_i\mid\mu_{k'},\Sigma_{k'})}.

M-step (Maximisation). Update each parameter as a responsibility-weighted MLE, with r_k=\sum_i r_{ik} the soft count in cluster k (Murphy 2012):

\pi_k = \frac{r_k}{N},\qquad
\mu_k = \frac{\sum_i r_{ik}\,x_i}{r_k},\qquad
\Sigma_k = \frac{\sum_i r_{ik}\,(x_i-\mu_k)(x_i-\mu_k)^\top}{r_k}.

Two guarantees and one caveat (Murphy 2012):

Monotonicity. Every iteration increases (or holds) the observed-data log-likelihood — EM never goes downhill.
Local optima. It converges to a local optimum or saddle, so initialisation matters; use multiple random restarts or initialise from k-means.



Show the EM code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
# true 1-D mixture: three Gaussians
N = 900
z = rng.choice(3, size=N, p=[0.3, 0.45, 0.25])
mus_true = np.array([-3.0, 1.0, 5.0]); sds_true = np.array([1.0, 0.8, 1.2])
x = rng.normal(mus_true[z], sds_true[z])

def normpdf(x, m, s):
    return np.exp(-0.5*((x-m)/s)**2) / (s*np.sqrt(2*np.pi))

K = 3
mu = np.quantile(x, [0.2, 0.5, 0.8]).astype(float)   # init
sd = np.full(K, x.std()); pi = np.full(K, 1/K)
loglik = []
for it in range(25):
    # E-step: responsibilities (N x K)
    comp = np.stack([pi[k]*normpdf(x, mu[k], sd[k]) for k in range(K)], axis=1)
    tot = comp.sum(axis=1)
    loglik.append(np.sum(np.log(tot)))
    r = comp / tot[:, None]
    # M-step: weighted MLEs
    rk = r.sum(axis=0)
    pi = rk / N
    mu = (r * x[:, None]).sum(axis=0) / rk
    sd = np.sqrt((r * (x[:, None] - mu)**2).sum(axis=0) / rk)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.8),
                               gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [1.6, 1]})
grid = np.linspace(x.min()-1, x.max()+1, 400)
axL.hist(x, bins=45, density=True, color="#dde4ea", edgecolor="white")
colors = ["#1f4e79", "#c0392b", "#1f8a4c"]
total = np.zeros_like(grid)
for k in range(K):
    dens = pi[k]*normpdf(grid, mu[k], sd[k]); total += dens
    axL.plot(grid, dens, "--", color=colors[k], lw=1.6)
axL.plot(grid, total, color="#222", lw=2.2, label="fitted mixture")
axL.set_title("GMM fit (3 components)"); axL.set_xlabel("x"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)

axR.plot(range(1, len(loglik)+1), loglik, "o-", color="#1f4e79", ms=3.5, lw=1.6)
axR.set_title("Log-likelihood ↑ every step"); axR.set_xlabel("EM iteration")
axR.set_ylabel("observed-data log-likelihood")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Fitting a 3-component Gaussian mixture by hand with EM. Left: the data histogram with the three fitted component densities (dashed) and their weighted sum (solid) after 25 EM iterations — the mixture recovers the underlying subgroups, giving each point a soft membership rather than a hard label. Right: the observed-data log-likelihood at each iteration — monotonically increasing and quickly plateauing, exactly EM’s guarantee. The E-step (responsibilities) and M-step (weighted means/variances/weights) are coded directly; no library does the fitting.

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
k-means is “hard EM”
Murphy frames k-means as a “hard EM” special case of GMM-EM (Murphy 2012). Assume equal mixing weights (\pi_k=1/K) and fixed spherical covariances (\Sigma_k=\sigma^2 I); then take the small-variance limit \sigma^2\to0 (Murphy 2012). The soft responsibilities collapse to hard 0/1 assignments (each point to its nearest centroid), and the two steps become exactly k-means: the E-step assigns points to the nearest centre, the M-step recomputes centres as the mean of assigned points (Murphy 2012). k-means is a GMM with the uncertainty switched off.

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
EM beyond mixtures
EM is far more general than clustering (Murphy 2012):

Missing data — e.g. fitting a multivariate Normal with missing entries (the missing values are the latent variables).
Latent-variable models — factor analysis and, for sequences, hidden Markov models (where EM is the Baum–Welch algorithm).

Choosing K is a model-selection problem (Murphy 2012). Unlike k-means reconstruction error (which always falls as K grows), a GMM’s validation negative log-likelihood is U-shaped — it eventually rises as the model overfits — so you can pick K at the minimum, or use BIC / variational Bayes to penalise complexity (Murphy 2012).

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Trusting a single EM run. It finds a local optimum; use several random restarts (or a k-means init) and keep the best log-likelihood. (Murphy 2012)
Variance collapse. A component can latch onto one point and shrink its variance toward zero, sending the likelihood to infinity — regularise the covariance (a small ridge) or use priors. (Murphy 2012)
Picking K by training likelihood. It only ever improves with K; judge on a validation set or by BIC. (Murphy 2012)
Forgetting label-switching. Cluster labels are arbitrary; don’t over-interpret which index is which across runs. (Murphy 2012)
Using a GMM when clusters aren’t roughly Gaussian. The model imposes elliptical components; very non-Gaussian shapes need a different density. (Murphy 2012)

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Murphy, Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective — the GMM as a weighted sum of Gaussians with mixing weights \pi_k, latent assignment z_i, and soft-clustering responsibilities; the log-sum difficulty and non-convex, label-switching likelihood; the EM algorithm with the E-step responsibility formula and the M-step responsibility-weighted updates for \pi_k,\mu_k,\Sigma_k; monotonic likelihood increase and convergence to a local optimum (restarts/initialisation); k-means as hard EM (equal weights, spherical \Sigma, \sigma^2\to0); EM’s broader use for missing data, factor analysis, and HMMs (Baum–Welch); and choosing K by validation likelihood / BIC.

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why EM climbs the likelihood. EM maximises a lower bound. For any distribution q(z) over the latent labels, Jensen’s inequality gives \ell(\theta) \ge \sum_i \mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p(x_i,z_i\mid\theta)] + H(q) \equiv \mathcal L(q,\theta), the ELBO. The E-step sets q(z_i)=p(z_i\mid x_i,\theta^{\text{old}}) (the responsibilities), which makes the bound tight (\mathcal L=\ell). The M-step maximises \mathcal L over \theta. Since the E-step touches the bound to \ell and the M-step only raises it, \ell cannot decrease — the monotonicity guarantee.
The M-step updates are weighted MLEs. Maximising the expected complete-data log-likelihood \sum_i\sum_k r_{ik}\big[\log\pi_k + \log\mathcal N(x_i\mid\mu_k,\Sigma_k)\big] over each parameter (with the \sum_k\pi_k=1 Lagrange constraint) yields exactly the soft-count formulas above — every point contributes to every cluster, weighted by its responsibility.
The k-means limit. With \Sigma_k=\sigma^2 I, the responsibility r_{ik}\propto \exp(-\|x_i-\mu_k\|^2/2\sigma^2); as \sigma^2\to0 the softmax becomes an argmax, so r_{ik}\in\{0,1\} picks the nearest centroid — the hard E-step of k-means.

title: Mixture Models & the EM Algorithm
text:
Connections

Builds on: unsupervised learning / k-means (the hard-clustering special case), common distributions (the Gaussian components), and maximum likelihood (what the M-step maximises).
Generalises to: factor analysis and hidden Markov models, both fit by EM.
Compares with: hierarchical Bayesian models — a fully Bayesian mixture puts priors on \pi,\mu,\Sigma and samples instead of point-maximising.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text: Regularization was one answer to “too many predictors”: shrink them. This entry covers the other two. Subset selection keeps a discrete handful and drops the rest. Dimension reduction replaces the predictors with a few linear combinations that capture most of what matters. All three are the bias–variance trade-off in another costume — trading a little bias for a large cut in variance — and all become essential once predictors outnumber observations.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Why it matters
Ordinary least squares with many predictors predicts poorly and reads opaquely: variance explodes and no coefficient is trustworthy. (James et al. 2021) Before the flexible methods of this track, you need the classical toolkit for taming dimensionality — the discrete (subset selection), the continuous (shrinkage), and the derived (dimension reduction). It is also where you meet the p>n world head-on, the regime that makes regularization not a refinement but a necessity. (James et al. 2021)

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Subset selection
The direct idea: keep only the predictors most related to Y and run OLS on those. (James et al. 2021)

Best-subset selection fits OLS for every combination of predictors and, for each size k, keeps the lowest-RSS model. Thorough but 2^p models — infeasible past ~30 predictors. (James et al. 2021)
Forward stepwise starts from the null model and adds the single best predictor at each step.
Backward stepwise starts from the full model and drops the least useful — but cannot run when p>n (there’s no full model to start from). (James et al. 2021)


You can’t choose on training error
Critically, training RSS and R^2 cannot pick the final model — they improve monotonically with every added predictor, so they always point to the biggest model and straight into overfitting. (James et al. 2021) You need a criterion that penalises size, estimating test error: (James et al. 2021)

C_p=\tfrac1n\big(\text{RSS}+2d\hat\sigma^2\big),\quad
\text{BIC}=\tfrac1n\big(\text{RSS}+\log(n)\,d\hat\sigma^2\big),\quad
\bar R^2=1-\frac{\text{RSS}/(n-d-1)}{\text{TSS}/(n-1)} .

\text{AIC} is proportional to C_p for least squares; BIC’s \log n penalty is heavier than C_p’s factor of 2 (whenever n>7), so BIC favours smaller models. (James et al. 2021) Higher adjusted R^2 is better; lower C_p/BIC is better. Or skip the formulas entirely and estimate test error directly with a validation set or k-fold CV — fewer assumptions, and now the default. (James et al. 2021) The figure shows the core lesson: training error slides down forever while held-out error is U-shaped.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n, p, k_true = 120, 14, 5
X = rng.normal(0, 1, (n, p))
beta = np.zeros(p); beta[:k_true] = rng.uniform(1.0, 2.0, k_true)
y = X @ beta + rng.normal(0, 1.5, n)
# train/validation split
tr, va = slice(0, 80), slice(80, n)

def ols_fit_predict(cols, Xtr, ytr, Xva):
    A = np.c_[np.ones(Xtr.shape[0]), Xtr[:, cols]]
    coef, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(A, ytr, rcond=None)
    yhat_tr = A @ coef
    Av = np.c_[np.ones(Xva.shape[0]), Xva[:, cols]]
    return yhat_tr, Av @ coef

chosen, remaining = [], list(range(p))
train_r2, val_mse, bic = [], [], []
ytr, yva = y[tr], y[va]
for step in range(1, p + 1):
    best = None
    for c in remaining:                       # forward step: add the predictor that best cuts train RSS
        cols = chosen + [c]
        yhat_tr, _ = ols_fit_predict(cols, X[tr], ytr, X[va])
        rss = np.sum((ytr - yhat_tr) ** 2)
        if best is None or rss < best[0]:
            best = (rss, c)
    chosen.append(best[1]); remaining.remove(best[1])
    yhat_tr, yhat_va = ols_fit_predict(chosen, X[tr], ytr, X[va])
    rss = np.sum((ytr - yhat_tr) ** 2)
    train_r2.append(1 - rss / np.sum((ytr - ytr.mean()) ** 2))
    val_mse.append(np.mean((yva - yhat_va) ** 2))
    d = len(chosen); sigma2 = rss / (len(ytr) - d - 1)
    bic.append((rss + np.log(len(ytr)) * d * sigma2) / len(ytr))

sizes = np.arange(1, p + 1)
val_min = sizes[int(np.argmin(val_mse))]; bic_min = sizes[int(np.argmin(bic))]

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax1.plot(sizes, train_r2, "o-", color="#9aa0a6", lw=2, label="training R² (left)")
ax2.plot(sizes, val_mse, "o-", color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2, label="validation MSE (right)")
ax2.axvline(val_min, color="#1f8a4c", ls=":", lw=1.6)
ax2.axvline(bic_min, color="#d98a29", ls="--", lw=1.4)
ax1.axvline(k_true, color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, alpha=0.5)
ax1.text(k_true + 0.1, 0.2, f"true size = {k_true}", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
ax2.text(val_min + 0.15, max(val_mse) * 0.9, f"val min = {val_min}", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
ax1.set_xlabel("number of predictors in model")
ax1.set_ylabel("training R²", color="#6b6f76"); ax2.set_ylabel("validation MSE", color="#1f3a5f")
ax1.set_title("Training error always falls; held-out error is U-shaped")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Choosing the number of predictors. Forward stepwise selection on simulated data where only the first few predictors carry signal. Training R² (grey) rises monotonically with model size — it can never choose. Validation MSE (blue) is U-shaped: it falls as real predictors enter, then rises as noise predictors add variance. The validation minimum (green) recovers the true model size; BIC’s heavier penalty (orange dashed minimum) lands at or below it.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Three philosophies, one trade-off
It helps to see subset selection beside the shrinkage it complements: (James et al. 2021)

Subset selection — a discrete choice: a predictor is in or out. Low bias on the kept set, but the discreteness makes it high-variance (a small data change flips which subset wins).
Ridge (\ell_2) — shrinks all coefficients toward zero, none exactly to zero: keeps every predictor but tames variance. (James et al. 2021)
Lasso (\ell_1) — shrinks and zeroes out, performing selection continuously; the diamond constraint’s corners are what set coefficients exactly to zero. (James et al. 2021)

All three buy a large variance reduction for a little bias — the universal regularization bargain. (James et al. 2021)

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Dimension reduction: PCR and PLS
A different idea: don’t select among the p predictors — replace them with M<p linear combinations and regress on those. (James et al. 2021)

Principal-components regression (PCR). Take the first M principal components — the directions of maximal variance in X — and use them as predictors. PCR is unsupervised: it finds those directions without looking at Y, betting that high-variance directions in X are also the ones that predict Y. (James et al. 2021)
Partial least squares (PLS). The supervised cousin: it builds directions that explain X and correlate with Y — the first PLS component weights each predictor by its correlation with Y. (James et al. 2021) PLS can do better when the predictive directions aren’t the highest-variance ones, though in practice it often matches PCR.

For both, standardise the predictors first (mean 0, SD 1) — otherwise a variable’s measurement scale, not its information, drives the directions. (James et al. 2021) (PCA itself returns in the unsupervised entry; here it’s a regression pre-processor.)






NoteISL’s two datasets




Credit (n=400, p=11): the size-penalising criteria disagree — BIC picks 4 variables, C_p/AIC pick 6, adjusted R^2 picks 7 — a clean illustration that BIC is the most parsimonious. (James et al. 2021)
Hitters (n=263, p=19, predicting baseball salary): cross-validated Lasso selects a 7-variable model with test MSE (\approx 100,743) comparable to ridge’s — but far more interpretable for being sparse. (James et al. 2021)

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
The high-dimensional world (p>n)
Modern data routinely has more predictors than observations — genomics, text, behavioural logs. (James et al. 2021) Here ordinary least squares breaks: (James et al. 2021)

No unique solution, and coefficient variance becomes infinite.
It fits the training data perfectly (\text{RSS}=0) even when every predictor is pure noise — so training R^2=1 is meaningless, evidence of nothing.
The curse of dimensionality: test error tends to rise with each added feature unless it’s genuinely associated with Y; and extreme multicollinearity makes it impossible to say which variable is truly predictive (any one is a combination of the others). (James et al. 2021)

In this regime the less-flexible methods — Lasso, PCR, ridge — stop being optional and become the only way to get generalisable predictions. (James et al. 2021) And the hard truth: with p>n you can predict, but you usually cannot identify which predictors matter — a humbling boundary that the causal cautions of this track underline.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Selecting on training RSS/R^2. They fall monotonically and always pick the biggest model; use C_p/BIC/adjusted R^2 or cross-validation. (James et al. 2021)
Doing selection outside the CV loop. Choosing predictors on the full data then cross-validating leaks information and flatters the estimate — selection is a choice inside the loop. (James et al. 2021)
PCR/PLS without standardising. Unscaled predictors let the largest-scale variable dominate the components. (James et al. 2021)
Trusting PCR when variance ≠ signal. High-variance directions need not predict Y; PLS or supervised methods may be needed. (James et al. 2021)
Reading R^2=1 in high dimensions as success. With p>n a perfect fit is automatic and worthless. (James et al. 2021)

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 6) — best-subset and forward/backward stepwise selection (backward needs n>p); why training RSS/R^2 can’t choose; C_p, AIC, BIC, adjusted R^2 and CV; subset vs Ridge/Lasso shrinkage and the bias–variance rationale; PCR (unsupervised, max-variance directions) vs PLS (supervised), and standardising; the p>n failure of OLS, the curse of dimensionality, multicollinearity, and meaningless R^2; the Credit (BIC 4 / C_p 6 / adj-R^2 7) and Hitters (Lasso 7-var, MSE \approx 100,743) examples.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why C_p estimates test error. Training RSS underestimates test error by roughly 2d\hat\sigma^2/n (one \hat\sigma^2 of optimism per fitted parameter). C_p=\tfrac1n(\text{RSS}+2d\hat\sigma^2) adds it back, making C_p an (approximately) unbiased estimate of test MSE. BIC replaces the 2 with \log n, derived from a Bayesian model-evidence approximation, hence its stronger preference for small models and its consistency (it picks the true model as n\to\infty).
PCR as constrained ridge. Let X=UDV^\top (SVD). PCR keeps the first M right-singular directions v_1,\dots,v_M and discards the rest entirely; ridge instead down-weights direction j by d_j^2/(d_j^2+\lambda) — shrinking low-variance directions smoothly rather than truncating. PCR is the hard-threshold limit of ridge’s soft down-weighting along the principal axes — which is why both protect against the high-variance, low-eigenvalue directions that wreck OLS.
Why OLS dies at p>n. X^\top X is p\times p but has rank \le n<p, so it is singular and (X^\top X)^{-1} does not exist — infinitely many \hat\beta give \text{RSS}=0. Regularization restores a unique solution by adding a full-rank penalty (\lambda I for ridge) or restricting to M\le
n derived directions (PCR/PLS).

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Connections

Builds on: regularization (the shrinkage sibling), bias–variance (the shared rationale), cross-validation (how to choose), and multicollinearity/VIF.
Leads to: beyond linearity (flexible forms, once you’ve chosen the features) and unsupervised learning (PCA in its own right).
Bridges back: prediction with p>n can succeed while identification of which predictors matter fails — a caution sharpened in ML for social science.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text: Everything so far had a target Y to predict. Unsupervised learning removes it: you have only the features X, and the goal is to discover structure — which variables move together (PCA), which observations group together (clustering). It is exploratory, powerful, and treacherous: with no right answer to check against, the methods will happily impose patterns on data that has none. The discipline is knowing the difference between finding structure and manufacturing it.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Why it matters
Unsupervised learning is the data-reduction and pattern-discovery engine behind much of social science — constructing scales from many items, mapping high-dimensional survey or text data to a readable picture, finding latent typologies of people or places. (James et al. 2021) But it is harder and more subjective than supervised learning, precisely because there is no gold-standard Y to validate against. (James et al. 2021) Understanding both the power and the looseness is what separates honest exploration from clustering the noise.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
The unsupervised setting
What changes without a label: (James et al. 2021)

No clear goal. The aim is to “discover interesting things,” not to predict a specific output — it is exploratory analysis, not estimation.
No validation. There is no cross-validation here: without a true answer, you can’t score a clustering as “right.” (James et al. 2021)
