Sensitivity to choices. Results swing on the analyst’s decisions — scaling, distance metric, number of clusters — far more than supervised methods do. (James et al. 2021)

Treat unsupervised output as a source of hypotheses, not conclusions.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Principal component analysis
PCA finds a few derived variables — the principal components — that capture most of the information in many original features. (James et al. 2021)

The first principal component is the direction of maximal variance — geometrically, the line in p-space closest to the observations. (James et al. 2021)
Each subsequent component has the largest variance subject to being orthogonal (uncorrelated) to the previous ones. (James et al. 2021)
The weights defining a component are its loadings; the projected observation values are its scores. (James et al. 2021)
Scaling is essential: standardise each variable to mean 0, SD 1 first, or the highest-variance variable (often just an artefact of its units) dominates the components. (James et al. 2021)

How many components to keep? The proportion of variance explained (PVE) per component, read off a scree plot, where you look for an “elbow” — the point past which extra components add little. (James et al. 2021) (This is PCR’s engine, now used in its own right for visualisation and reduction.)






NotePCA in the wild




USArrests. PCA on US crime data: the first component loads roughly equally on Assault, Murder, and Rape — an overall “crime” axis — while the second isolates UrbanPop (urbanisation). Two interpretable axes from four correlated variables. (James et al. 2021)
NCI60 gene expression (6{,}830 genes \times 64 cancer cell lines). PCA compresses the thousands of genes into a 2-D picture in which cell lines of the same cancer type cluster together — high-dimensional structure made visible. (James et al. 2021)

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Clustering: k-means
Clustering partitions observations into groups that are similar within and different between. (James et al. 2021) K-means fixes the number of clusters K and minimises within-cluster variation — the sum of squared Euclidean distances to each cluster’s centroid: (James et al. 2021)

Randomly assign observations to K clusters.
Repeat until assignments stop changing: (a) recompute each centroid (the vector of feature means); (b) reassign each point to its nearest centroid. (James et al. 2021)

Two cautions baked into the method: it finds a local optimum, so run it many times from different random starts (nstart) and keep the best; and you must choose K — typically via the elbow in a plot of within-cluster variation against K. (James et al. 2021)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.8, 3.9))

# ---- Left: PCA via eigendecomposition of the covariance ----
cov = np.array([[1.0, 0.8], [0.8, 1.0]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(cov)
X = (rng.normal(size=(300, 2)) @ L.T) * np.array([1.0, 0.7])
Xc = X - X.mean(0)
C = np.cov(Xc.T)
vals, vecs = np.linalg.eigh(C)
order = np.argsort(vals)[::-1]; vals, vecs = vals[order], vecs[:, order]
axL.scatter(Xc[:, 0], Xc[:, 1], s=10, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.7)
for k, c in zip(range(2), ["#c0392b", "#d98a29"]):
    v = vecs[:, k] * np.sqrt(vals[k]) * 2.5
    axL.annotate("", xy=v, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=c, lw=2.4))
axL.text(*(vecs[:, 0]*np.sqrt(vals[0])*2.7), "PC1", color="#c0392b", fontsize=9)
axL.text(*(vecs[:, 1]*np.sqrt(vals[1])*2.9), "PC2", color="#b9791f", fontsize=9)
axL.set_title("PCA: directions of maximal variance")
axL.set_xlabel("x₁"); axL.set_ylabel("x₂"); axL.set_aspect("equal")

# ---- Right: k-means by hand (assign / recompute) ----
centers0 = np.array([[-2.5, 0], [2.5, 0.5], [0, 3.0]])
pts = np.vstack([rng.normal(c, 0.7, (60, 2)) for c in centers0])
K = 3
cent = pts[rng.choice(len(pts), K, replace=False)]
for _ in range(20):
    d = ((pts[:, None, :] - cent[None, :, :])**2).sum(2)
    lab = d.argmin(1)
    newc = np.array([pts[lab == k].mean(0) if np.any(lab == k) else cent[k] for k in range(K)])
    if np.allclose(newc, cent): break
    cent = newc
colors = ["#1f3a5f", "#1f8a4c", "#b9791f"]
for k in range(K):
    axR.scatter(pts[lab == k, 0], pts[lab == k, 1], s=12, color=colors[k], alpha=0.7)
    axR.scatter(*cent[k], marker="X", s=140, color=colors[k], edgecolors="white", lw=1.5, zorder=5)
axR.set_title("k-means clusters (K=3) with centroids")
axR.set_xlabel("x₁"); axR.set_ylabel("x₂")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Left: PCA on correlated 2-D data. The first principal component (red) points along the direction of maximal variance — the line closest to the cloud; the second (orange) is orthogonal, capturing the rest. PCA simply rotates to these axes. Right: k-means with K=3 on three blobs. Points are coloured by their assigned cluster and each centroid (✕) is the mean of its members; the algorithm iterates assign-and-recompute until the partition stabilises.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Hierarchical clustering
Hierarchical clustering avoids pre-specifying K. The agglomerative (bottom-up) version starts with every point its own cluster and repeatedly merges the two closest, recording the result as a dendrogram — a tree whose fusion heights show how dissimilar the merged groups were. (James et al. 2021) To measure distance between groups you pick a linkage: (James et al. 2021)

Complete — the maximal pairwise dissimilarity (compact, balanced clusters).
Single — the minimal (can produce straggly, “trailing” chains).
Average — the mean pairwise dissimilarity.
Centroid — distance between cluster centroids.

The payoff: one dendrogram yields any number of clusters — just cut the tree at a chosen height. (James et al. 2021) (Bruce & Bruce cluster the S&P 500: energy, financial, and consumer stocks fall into distinct subtrees — sectors emerging with no labels. (Bruce et al. 2020))

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Practical cautions
The looseness is real, and ignoring it is how unsupervised learning misleads: (James et al. 2021)

No ground truth. You cannot validate a clustering the way you validate a classifier; treat results as hypotheses, not truths. (James et al. 2021)
Clustering the noise. Algorithms always return clusters — even when none exist. A tidy partition is not evidence of real subgroups. (James et al. 2021)
Parameter sensitivity. Scaling, the distance metric, K, and the linkage can change the answer drastically — report your choices and check robustness. (James et al. 2021)

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




PCA without standardising. Unscaled variables let measurement units, not information, define the components. (James et al. 2021)
Taking clusters as real subgroups. k-means and hierarchical clustering partition any data; verify structure exists before interpreting it. (James et al. 2021)
One run of k-means. It converges to a local optimum; use many random restarts. (James et al. 2021)
Over-reading the dendrogram. Only fusion heights (not horizontal proximity) encode similarity, and the linkage choice reshapes the tree. (James et al. 2021)
Treating components or clusters as causal. They are descriptive structure in X — never effects. (James et al. 2021)

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 12) — the unsupervised setting (no Y, hard to validate, subjective); PCA (first PC = max-variance/closest line, orthogonal components, loadings & scores, PVE & scree/elbow, mandatory scaling) with USArrests and NCI60; k-means (minimise within-cluster sum of squares, the iterate-assign-recompute algorithm, local optima/nstart, elbow for K); hierarchical clustering (agglomerative, dendrogram, linkage — complete/single/average/centroid, cut the tree); the cautions (no ground truth, clustering noise, parameter sensitivity).
Bruce & Bruce — applied clustering/dimension reduction, the S&P 500 hierarchical example.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

PCA as an eigenproblem. With standardised data X (columns mean 0, SD 1) and sample covariance C=\tfrac1n X^\top X, the loadings of the m-th component are the eigenvector \phi_m of C with the m-th largest eigenvalue \lambda_m; the scores are z_{im}=\phi_m^\top x_i. Because \phi_m maximises \operatorname{Var}(X\phi)=\phi^\top C\phi subject to \|\phi\|=1 and orthogonality, the first component is the max-variance direction, and \lambda_m is the variance along it. The PVE of component m is \lambda_m/\sum_k\lambda_k. (Equivalently, via the SVD X=UDV^\top: loadings are columns of V, scores are UD, and \lambda_m=d_m^2/n.)
k-means minimises within-cluster sum of squares. The objective is 
\min_{C_1,\dots,C_K}\ \sum_{k=1}^K \sum_{i\in C_k}\|x_i-\mu_k\|^2,\qquad \mu_k=\tfrac1{|C_k|}\sum_{i\in C_k}x_i .
 The two-step algorithm is coordinate descent: step (a) fixes assignments and the optimal centroid is the cluster mean; step (b) fixes centroids and the optimal assignment is the nearest centroid. Each step weakly decreases the objective, so it converges — but to a local minimum depending on the initialisation, hence multiple restarts.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Connections

Builds on: exploratory data analysis (the exploratory spirit), correlation/covariance (what PCA decomposes), and PCR (PCA as a regression pre-processor).
Leads to: ML for social science — using PCA/clustering for measurement and typology-building, with discipline.
Bridges back: structure in X is descriptive — a principal component is not a cause, and a cluster is not a kind; the causal questions need a different toolkit.
